# Ohio Eviction and Landlord-Tenant Law

Version: 2022-06 (Sixth Edition)

## I. Eviction Action, Procedures


### A. General Background


#### 1. Statutory Remedy

R.C. Chapter 1923 establishes Ohio’s forcible entry and detainer (“FED”) action, which is also known as an eviction action. “A forcible entry and detainer action is a statutory remedy….” CS/RW Westlake Indoor Storage, L.L.C. v. Kesi, L.L.C. , No.

102535, 2015 WL 6819281, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 5, 2015). Accord Egner
v. Egner, 24 Ohio App.3d 171, 172, 493 N.E.2d 999 (11th Dist. 1985). It is a remedy unknown at common law and thus purely statutory.Fodor v. First Nat’l Supermarkets, Inc., 63 Ohio St.3d 489, 494, 589 N.E.2d 17 (1992).

#### 2. Purpose of an Eviction Action

“‘The purpose of the forcible entry and detainer statute is to provide a summary, extraordinary, and speedy method for the recovery of the possession of real estate ....’” Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jackson , 67 Ohio St.2d 129, 131, 423 N.E.2d 177
(1981) (quoting 24 Ohio Jur.2d Forcible Entry and Detainer § 2, at 455 (1957)).

Accord State ex rel. Weiss v. Hoover, 84 Ohio St.3d 530, 533, 705 N.E.2d 1227 (1999);

State ex rel. GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Callahan, 45 Ohio St.3d 51, 55, 543 N.E.2d 483 (1989).

An FED action “determines the right to immediate possession of the property and nothing else.” Seventh Urban, Inc. v. Univ. Circle Prop. Dev., Inc., 67 Ohio St.2d 19, 25 n.11, 423 N.E.2d 1070 (1981). Accord Hussain v. Sheppard, No. 14AP-686, 2015 WL 915467, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 24, 2015); Miami Valley Hous. v.

Jackson, No. 25020, 2012 WL 5382834, ¶ 5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 2, 2012);

Long v. MacDonald, No. 3-02-10, 2002 WL 31007937, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept.

9, 2002).

The ultimate issue in any FED action is solely whether the party who commenced the action has a present right to possession of the real property. Fodor v. First Nat’l Supermarkets, Inc., 63 Ohio St.3d 489, 494, 589 N.E.2d 17 (1982); Sholiton Indus.,
Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd. , No. 17480, 1999 WL 355898, at *9 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 4, 1999); Fenner v. Parkinson, 69 Ohio App.3d 210, 213, 590 N.E.2d 339 (10th
Dist. 1990); Cent. Park Place v. McDowell , 38 Ohio App.2d 29, 31, 311 N.E.2d 533 (8th Dist. 1974).

A party’s right to present possession is not necessarily based on ownership of the title to the property. For example, a lessor, as defined in R.C. 1923.01(C)(2), may bring an FED action even though the lessor is not the owner of the title to the property.

KDI Mgmt. Servs., Inc. v. Enerchem, Inc. , No. C -960587, 1997 WL 119898 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar. 19, 1997). For a discussion on the real party in interest standard in an FED action, see Section II.CC, below.

An FED action “is not an action to determine ownership of the titl e to the property.” Behrle v. Beam , 6 Ohio St.3d 41, 44, 451 N.E.2d 237 (1983). Accord Tucker v. Pfirsch, No. 2014-CA-0036, 2014 WL 3540883, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 16, 2014). “Forcible entry and detainer actions determine neither legal nor equitable title.” Brainard v. Cardwell , Nos. 75870,75896, 2000 WL 897793, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 6, 2000). When the right to present possession is based on ownership of the title and a dispute exists as to which party holds present record title, it may create jurisdictional issues in the FED action. See Section I.L.3, below.

#### 3. Parties in an Eviction Action – Not Limited to

Landlord Claims Against Tenants

##### a. Overview

“[T]he [FED] statute … does not on its face limit the class of defendants to ‘tenants’and the class of plaintiffs to ‘landlords’….” Bio Energy (Ohio), LLC v.

Phoenix Golf Links, Ltd. , No. 12AP-171, 2012 WL 4470638, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 27, 2012).

“Applying basic principles of statutory construction, there is no restrictionin R.C.

1923.01(A) upon who may bring an FED action, while under R.C. 1923.02 there is a defined class of defendants against whom it may be brought.”Id., ¶ 13. Accord CS/RW Westlake Indoor Storage, L.L.C. v. Kesi, L.L.C., No. 102535, 2015 WL 6819281, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 5, 2015).

A tenant may bring an FED action against “a landlord who is otherwise one of the permissible defendants defined in R.C. 1923.02(A),” against a competing co -tenant, against a squatter, or against others. Bio Energy, 2012 WL 4470638, ¶ 15.

A property owner may bring an FED action against any non-tenant occupier of the property who is “without color of title.” R.C. 1923.02(A)(5). E.g., Hardin v. Hardin,
No. E-18-001, 2019 WL 259092 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 1 8, 2019) (occupancy by 3 permission of the owner);Deutsche Bank Nat’l Trust Co. v. Lovette, No. 106546, 2018 WL 6266863 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 29, 2018) (tenancy at sufferance extinguished by judicial foreclosure sale); White v. Bergman, No. 15-COA-010, 2015 WL 5783088 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 2, 2015) (guest); Lee v. Wallace, 186 Ohio App.3d 18, 926 N.E.2d 328 (8th Dist. 2010) (live -in caregiver, without rent or exclusive use);

Executive Bus. Ctrs., Inc. v. TransPacific Mfg., Ltd., No. L-08-1060, 2009 WL 282383 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 6, 2009) (office service agreement); Estate of Wos v. Wos,
No. L-05-1408, 2006 WL 2383966 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 18, 2006) (temporary occupancy, with decedent’s permission but without rent).

However, the property owner in these circumstances is not required to use an FED action to recover possession of the property. Lee v. Wallace, 186 Ohio App.3d 18, 926 N.E.2d 328 (Cuyahoga Cty. 2010). Cf. Northfield Park Assocs. v. Northeast Ohio Harness, 36 Ohio App.3d 14, 521 N.E.2d 466 (8th Dist. 1987) (commercial tenancy).

In addition, by express statutory authority: a unit owners association may bring an FED action against a tenant of a unit owner, for a violation of the deed restrictions or condominium rul es, R.C. 5311.19(B)(1); 1 and, with respect to land installment contracts, the vendor may bring an FED action against the vendee in certain circumstances (R.C. 5313.07,.08), which are discussed in Section XXIII.E.2, below.

Also, R.C. 1923.02(A)(6) permits an FED action “[i]n any o ther case of the unlawful and forcible detention of lands or tenements.” One court has held that R.C. 1923.02(A)(6) is not authority for a long -term acute care hospital to bring an FED action against a patient who is ready for discharge.

Regency Hosp. of Columbus v. Thomas, No. 2016-CVG-004483 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Mar. 8, 2016).

In any FED action, a court may, pursuant to the plaintiff’s request, enter an order evicting one (or more) but not all of the occupiers of the premises. White v. Bergman,
No. 15-COA-010, 2015 WL 5783088 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 2, 2015). See also Sanders v. Favors, No. C-950304, 1995 WL 763681 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 29, 1995). In Bergman, the owner evicted his daughter’s guest but not his daughter.

1 In these FED actions, the unit owners association must bring the action in the name of the unit owner, and as the unit owner’s agent. Homeowners Ass’n at Arrow-Head Bay v. Fidoe , No.

12-MA-136, 2014 WL 1384577 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar. 31, 2014).

##### b. Applicability to Housing Cooperatives

An FED action may be used to evict a member of a housing cooperative, notwithstanding the member’s ownership interest.2 Gvozdanovic v. Woodford Corp., 139 Ohio App.3d 11, 742 N.E.2d 1145 (1st Dist. 2000). See also Murphy v. Brooklyn Acres Mut. Homes, Inc., No. 68593, 1996 WL 284856 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 30, 1996); Cent. Park Place v. McDowell, 38 Ohio App.2d 29, 311 N.E.2d 533 (8th Dist.

1974).

“A [cooperative] member is both a landlord by virtue of being an owner of the cooperative and a tenant by virtue of the proprietary lease.” Gvozdanovic, 139 Ohio App.3d at 23. See also Kohler v. Snow Vill., Inc. , 16 Ohio App.3d 350, 352 -53, 475 N.E.2d 1298 (8th Dist. 1984).

However, the member’s right of possession depends entirely uponthe terms of the proprietary lease. Gvozdanovic, 139 Ohio App.3d at 28.

Therefore, if the member breaches the proprietary lease, the cooperative may bring an FED action against the member under R.C. 1923.02(A)(6), which permits an FED action “[i]n any other case of the unlawful and forcible detention of lands or tenements.” Gvozdanovic, 139 Ohio App.3d at 28.

#### 4. Action at Law

An FED action “is one at law based upon contract.”Behrle v. Beam, 6 Ohio St.3d 41, 44, 451 N.E.2d 237 (1983). Consequently, a municipal court has jurisdiction under R.C. 1901.18(C) to hear an equity defense in an FED action. Id.; Terra Mgmt. Co. v.

Bishop, No. CA -9919, 1987 WL 5312, at *1 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 2, 1987);

Lauch v. Monning, 15 Ohio App.2d 112, 114, 239 N.E.2d 675 (1st Dist. 1968). If the FED action “is based upon a written lease, the municipal court has jurisdiction pursuant to R.C. 1901.18(C) to hear and determine all legal and equitable remedies necessary for a complete determination of the rights of the part ies to the contract.” Brown v.

Brown, No. 04 -CA-000018, 2005 WL 914490, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 14, 2005). For additional discussion on the equity defense in FED actions, see Sections II.BB and XX.C.2, below.

2 Alternatively, an ejectment action under R.C. 5303.03 may be used to evict a member of a housing cooperative. Gvozdanovic v. Woodford Corp., 139 Ohio App.3d 11, 742 N.E.2d 1145 (1st Dist. 2000).

5

### B. Application of the Ohio Rules of Civil

Procedure Civil Rule 1(C) establishes, for forcible entry and detainer (“FED”) actions and other special statutory proceedings, two alternative standards for determining which provisions of the Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure apply to the proceeding.3 The first standard is that the Civil Rules apply to FED actions (and other special statutory proceedings) except “to the extent that they would by their nature be clearly inapplicable.” Ohio R. Civ. P. 1(C). A Civil Rule, or a provision of a Civil Rule, is clearly inapplicable only “when [its] use will alter the basic statutory purpose for which 3 When a complaint includes an FED claim and a monetary claim, Civil Rule 1(C) applies to the FED claim, but generally not to the monetary claim. See Glazier v. Hall , No. CA -1076, 1994 WL 477994, at *2 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 18, 1994); Borges v. Everdry Waterproofing of Wapakoneta, Inc. , 95 Ohio App.3d 175, 177 -78, 642 N.E.2d 16 (3rd Dist.

1994). All of the Civil Rules should apply to these monetary claims. But, as a result of a few court decisions, an issue now exists on whether Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply to these monetary
claims. For a discussion on this issue, see Section I.G.3.c, below.

the special procedure was originally provided.”4 Woodman v. Tubbs Jones, 103 Ohio App.3d 577, 583, 660 N.E.2d 520 (8th Dist. 1995).Accord Robinson v. B.O.C. Group,
Gen. Motors Corp., 81 Ohio St.3d 361, 370, 691 N.E.2d 667 (1998);Tower City Props.
v. Cuyahoga County Bd. of Revisions , 49 Ohio St.3d 67, 69, 551 N.E.2d 122 (1990);

Price v. Westinghouse Elec. Corp., 70 Ohio St.2d 131, 133, 435 N.E.2d 1114 (1982).

The clearly inapplicable standard is “a rule of inclusion rather than exclusion.” Price, 70 Ohio St.2d at 132. Accord Robinson, 81 Ohio St.3d at 370; Tower City Props., 49 Ohio St.3d at 69. “[T]he Civil Rules [are] applicable to special statutory proceedings adversary in nature, unless there is good and sufficient reason not to apply the rules.” Ohio R. Civ. P. 1, Staff Notes. See also Ramsdell v. Ohio Civil Rights 4 Some court decisions erroneously hold that a conflict between a statutory provision and a Civil Rule provision per se makes the Civil Rule provision clearly inapplicable under Civil Rule 1(C).

See, e.g., State ex rel. Fowler v. Smith, 68 Ohio St.3d 357, 360, 626 N.E.2d 950 (1993); Alex- Bell Oxford Ltd. P’ship v. Woods, No. 16038, 1998 WL 289028, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 5, 1998); In re Foreclosure of Liens for Delinquent Land Taxes v. Parcels of Land, Etc. ,
No. 96-APE02-160, 1996 WL 421862, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 25, 1996). When such a conflict exists, the court is obligated under Civil Rule 1(C) to determine whether application of the Civil Rule provision would alter or frustrate the purpose of the special statutory
proceeding. See, e.g., State ex rel. Yeagley v. Harris, 68 Ohio St.3d 136, 137, 624 N.E.2d 702 (1993); State ex rel. Millington v. Weir, 60 Ohio App.2d 348, 349, 397 N.E.2d 770 (10th Dist.

1978); Talley v. Warner, 99 Ohio Misc.2d 42, 71 5 N.E.2d 635 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., 1999). In this determination, the conflict is a relevant, but not determinative, factor. Id.

If the Civil Rule provision does not alter or frustrate the purpose of the special statutory proceeding, the Civ il Rule provision applies, notwithstanding the conflicting statutory
provision. Id.

Likewise, some statutes purport to override certain Civil Rules for their special statutory
proceedings. E.g., R.C. 1925.16 (“Except as inconsistent procedures are provided in this chapter ..., all proceedings in the small claims division of a municipal court are subject to the Rules of Civil Procedure”); R.C. 3111.08(A) (parentage actions “brought pursuant to sections 3111.01 to 3111.18 of the Revised Code ... shall be gov erned by the Rules of Civil Procedure unless a different procedure is specifically provided by those sections”). If a Civil Rule applies to a special statutory proceeding under the Civil Rule 1(C) standards, any provision in the statute purporting to esta blish a procedure that is in conflict with the Civil Rule is a violation of the Supreme Court’s rule making authority under Article IV, Section 5(B) of the Ohio Constitution.

See Hiatt v. S. Health Facilities, Inc., 68 Ohio St.3d 236, 237, 626 N.E.2d 71 (1994). However, if a Civil Rule does not apply to a special statutory proceeding under the Civil Rule 1(C) standards, any provision in the statute purporting to establish a procedure that is in conflict with the Civil Rule is valid because, in such cases, the Supreme Court has acceded its constitutional rulemaking authority to the General Assembly. George Shima Buick, Inc. v. Ferencak, No. 98- L-202, 1999 WL 1313675, at *2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 17, 1999).

7 Comm’n, 56 Ohio St.3d 24, 27, 563 N.E.2d 285 (1990); Miller v. Strausbaugh, No. 4- 2000-28, 2001 WL 475332, at *1 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., May 4, 2001);Skiles v. Dearth,
No. 2000-CA-30, 2000 WL 1838747, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 15, 2000).

The determination as to which provisions of the Civil Rules apply under the clearly inapplicable standard is made on a rule-by-rule basis. Robinson, 81 Ohio St.3d at 370;

Tower City Props., 49 Ohio St.3d at 69; Price, 70 Ohio St.2d at 132.

With respect to FED actions, this determination is generally based on whether application of the Civil Rule would frustrate the summary nature of FED actions.E.g., Miele v. Ribovich, 90 Ohio St.3d 439, 441 -44, 739 N.E.2d 333 (2000); Colonial Am.
Dev. Co. v. Griffith, 48 Ohio St.3d 72, 73, 549 N.E.2d 513 (1990); State ex rel. GMS
Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Callahan, 45 Ohio St.3d 51, 54, 543 N.E.2d 483 (1989); Cuyahoga
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jackson , 67 Ohio St.2d 129, 130 -32, 423 N.E.2d 177 (1981).

The focus of the courts in these cases has been whether the application of a procedural rule “would delay the ultimateresolution of an action.” T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly, 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020-Ohio-4279, ¶ 30 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020).

Generally, the civil rules that govern the conduct of the trial apply to FED actions.

Id., ¶¶ 29-32. The reason is that, “while a forcible entry and detainer action is designed to provide an expedited outcome, nothing in the law provides for or suggests an abbreviated or summary trial.” Id., ¶ 29.

The second standard is that a Civil Rule, or a provision of a Civil Rule, applies to FED actions (and other special statutory proceedings) whenever the statute “provides for procedure by a general or specific reference to the statutes governing procedure in civil actions.” Ohio R. Civ. P. 1(C). For example, under this general or specific reference standard, Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply to FED actions filed under R.C.

1923.02(A)(6)(a),(b), because R.C. 1923.051(A)(1) requires “the service and return of the summons in the action in accordance with the Rules of Civil Procedure.” Court decisions on the applicability of specific Civil Rules to FED actions are set forth below and in the sections referenced in the Civil Rule headings below.

Civil Rules 4.1(A) and 4.6: see Section I.G.3.b, below.5 Civil Rule 6(A) (R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate): see Section I.C.3.b, below.6 Civil Rule 9(A): applicable GMS Mgmt. v. Axe , 5 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 449 N.E.2d 43 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, 1982) Civil Rule 10(C): applicable:

Siegler v. Batdorff, 63 Ohio App.2d 76, 408 N.E.2d 1383 (8th Dist.1979) Civil Rule 10(D): see Section I.E.2.b, below.

Civil Rule 13(A) (FED claim and monetary claim): see Section I.I.2, below.

Civil Rule 13(A) (FED claim only): see Section I.I.2, below.

Civil Rule 13(B): see Section I.I.1, below.

5 As revised effective March 30, 1999, and Se ptember 29, 1999, R.C. 1923.06 purports to establish procedures for service of process in FED action s, some of which are in conflict with the Civil Rules. The court decisions that are discussed in Section I.G.3.b., below, address this revised R.C. 1923.06.

Between 1987 and 1999, R.C. 1923.06(A) provided thatservice of process in FED actions shall be “as in the Rules of Civil Procedure.” Prior to 1987, R.C. 1923.06(A) provided that such service shall be “as in other cases.” Pursuant to the “general or specific reference” standard in Civil Rule 1(C), both of these former R.C. 1923.06(A) provisions made Civil Rules 4.1(A) and 4.6 applicable to FED actions. Cuyahoga River Assocs. Ltd. P’ship v. MJK Corp., No. 68673, 1996 WL 17292 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 18, 1996); Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Thompson,
No. 14428, 1990 WL 106217 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 25, 1990);Tucker v. Grigsby, No. CA- 3154, 1985 WL 4492 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 13, 1985); Real Estate Servs., Inc. v. Leigh ,
No. 77AP-130, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 8899 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 2, 1977); Burton v.

Murry, 8 Ohio Op.3d 179 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1973); Cotterman v. Fahrig, 55 Ohio App.2d 15, 378 N.E.2d 742 (2nd Dist. 1972).

6 The revisions of R.C. 1923.06(A), effective March 30, 1999, and September 29, 1999, increased to seven days the minimum time period between service of process and trial.

Therefore, the Civil Rule 6(A) provision for computing time periods of less than se ven days is no longer relevant to this R.C. 1923.06(A) time period.

Prior to these 1999 revisions, this R.C. 1923.06(A) time period was five days, the computation of which was subject to the Civil Rule 6(A) provision for time periods of less than seven day s.

Stewart v. Hughes , No. 88 -CVG-265 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., July 20, 1988); Yozwiak v.

McCabe, No. 87-CVG-630 (Ohio Mun., Girard, Aug. 19, 1987).

9 Civil Rule 13(J): see Section I.L.1, below.

Civil Rule 17(A) see Section II.CC, below.

Civil Rule 18(A): applicable:

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jackson, 67 Ohio St.2d 129, 423 N.E.2d 177 (1981) (dicta) Marvin v. Stemen, 68 Ohio App.2d 26, 426 N.E.2d 205 (6th Dist. 1980) Civil Rule 19(A): applicable:

Siegler v. Batdorff, 63 Ohio App.2d 76, 408 N.E.2d 1383 (8th Dist. 1979) Civil Rule 38(B) (time for filing a jury demand): see Section I.J.2, below.

Graham v. Pavarini, 9 Ohio App.3d 89, 458 N.E.2d 421 (8th Dist. 1983) Civil Rule 42(B): applicable:

### C. Notice to Vacate, R.C. 1923.04


#### 1. General Background


##### a. Notice for Commencing Eviction Action

Proper service of an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate the premises(“notice to vacate”) is a condition precedent to the commencement of any eviction action. Eureka Multifamily Group v. Terrell, No. L-14-1152, 2015 WL 2329168, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 15, 2015);Ebbing v. Mathis, No. CA2012-10-201, 2013 WL 2423702, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 3, 2013);Eberly v. Irons, No. 2006AP-01-0004, 2007 WL 2351644, ¶ 36 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 9, 2007); Classic A Props. v. Brown , No.

02-CA-2868, 2003 WL 22461816, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 30, 2003);Colbert
v. McLemore, No. 81961, 2003 WL 21434169, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 19, 2003); Gvozdanovic v. Woodford Corp. , 139 Ohio App.3d 11, 30, 742 N.E.2d 1145 (1st Dist. 2000); Shimko v. Marks, 91 Ohio App.3d 458, 463, 632 N.E.2d 990 (5th Dist.

1993); Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 9, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th
Dist. 1985); Voyager Vill. Ltd. v. Williams, 3 Ohio App.3d 288, 290, 444 N.E.2d 1337 (2nd Dist.1982); Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Russell, 16 Ohio Op.3d 94, 95-96 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1980);Sternberg v. Washington, 113 Ohio App. 216, 221, 177 N.E.2d 11 525 (9th Dist. 1960).8 This notice requirement is in addition to whatever notice may be necessary to effect a termination of the lease or tenancy. Gvozdanovic v. Woodford Corp. , 139 Ohio App.3d 11, 29, 742 N.E.2d 1145 (1st Dist. 2000) ; Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

McCollum, 45 Ohio App.2d 197, 200, 341 N.E.2d 857 (1st Dist. 1975).

##### b. Jurisdictional Requirement

If a landlord fails to serve a notice to vacate or serves a notice to vacate but its content or the service fails to comply with R.C. 1923.04, the trial court lacks subject - matter jurisdiction over the eviction action. Riley v. Parker , No. 109600, 2021 WL 2012263, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 20, 2021); UMH OH Buckeye II, L.L.C. v.

DeCarlo, No. 108912, 2019 WL 6606376, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 5, 2019);

Deutsche Bank Nat’l Trust Co. v. Lovette, No. 106546, 2018 WL 6266863,¶ 27 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 29, 2018); Eureka Multifamily Group v. Terrell, No. L-14-1152, 2015 WL 2329168, ¶¶ 11,17 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 15, 2015); Ebbing v. Mathis,
No. CA2012-10-201, 2013 WL 2423702, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 3, 2013);

Chinnock v. Kokinda, No. 10-CA-009863, 2011 WL 860417, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,
Mar. 14, 2011); Mularcik v. Adams, No. 03-JE-17, 2004 WL 549489, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar. 18, 2004); Classic A Props. v. Brown , No. 02 -CA-2868, 2003 WL 22461816, ¶ 24 (Ohi o App., 4th Dist., Sept. 30, 2003); Colbert v. McLemore , No.

81961, 2003 WL 21434169, ¶ 5 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 19, 2003); Youngstown
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Higgins, No. 99-CA-81, 2000 WL 1159418, at *2 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Aug. 4, 2000); Godbelt v. McClain, No. 94-CA-0066, 1995 WL 768531, at *1 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 20, 1995);MLR Props., Inc. v. Hemmelgarn, No. L-94- 349, 1995 WL 504629, at *3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 25, 1995); Gallia Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Fry, No. 93-CA-22, 1994 WL 412403, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug.

2, 1994); Lucas Metro. Hous. Auth. v. M. Shouman Supermarkets, Inc., No. L-93-244, 8 See also Simco Mgmt. Corp. v. Snyder, No. 98-CA-210, 2000 WL 309396, at *1 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar. 20, 2000); Sholiton Indus., Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd. , No. 17480, 1999 WL 355898, at *6 (Ohio App., 2 nd Dist., June 4, 1999); Greene Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Manning ,
No. 98 -CA-55, 1999 WL 76456, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 19, 1999); Godbelt v.

McClain, No. 94 -CA-0066, 1995 WL 768531, at *1 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 20, 1995);

Gallia Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Fry, No. 93-CA-22, 1994 WL 412403, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist.,
Aug. 2, 1994); Sheridan Manor Apts. v. Carter , No. 92-CA-4, 1992 WL 388539, at *1 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 22, 1992); Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Anderson , No. 1406, 1988 WL 69118, at *5 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988).

1994 WL 110844, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 31, 1994); Crigger v. Shaw, No.

E-93-62, 1994 WL 68499, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 25, 1994); Chillicothe
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Anderson , No. 1406, 1988 WL 69118, at *5 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988); Gibbes v. Freeman , No. 52745, 1987 WL 16530, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 3, 1987); Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 8-9, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985); Sternberg v. Washington, 113 Ohio App. 216, 221, 177 N.E.2d 525, (9th Dist. 1960).9 But see Sharrock v. Bond, No. 2003-CA-0102, 2004 WL 2035242 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Sept. 9, 2004) (2-1 decision) (a proper notice to vacate is an essential element of landlord’s claim, but not a requirement for subject- matter jurisdiction); Schlenk v. Cornette , No. 12416, 1991 WL 108703 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 21, 1991) (a defective notice to vacate is grounds to dismiss the eviction action but does not deprive the trial court of subject-matter jurisdiction); J & M Trailer Court v. Dissette, No. 1556, 1987 WL 16917 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 9, 1987) (the requirement to serve the notice to vacate is a jurisdictional requirement; the requirement not to file the complaint until at least three days thereafter is not a jurisdictional requirement).

##### c. Service of a Second Notice to Vacate

When a landlord serves two notices to vacate before filing an eviction complaint, the second notice to vacate waives and supersedes the first notice to vacate.See Andrus
v. Dunbar, 178 Vt. 554, 878 A.2d 245 (2005);28 Mott St. Co. v. Summit Import Corp., 64 Misc.2d 860, 316 N.Y .S.2d 259 (Civ. Ct. 1970). Therefore, the second notice to vacate renders moot any service or content defect and any waiver (via future rent
acceptance) of the first notice to vacate.

When a landlord voluntarily dismisses without prejudice a first eviction action, the landlord must serve a new notice to vacate before filing a second eviction action against 9 See also McKoy v. Bell, No. 2021-CVG-008782 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 9, 2021); Kaseman v. Maddux , No. 21 -CVG-02194 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Nov. 1, 2021);

Dte/Earthscape Land & Constr. LLC v. Marbury, Ohio Mun., Garfield Heights, July 14, 2020);

Powers v. Arnold , No. 03 -CVG-01890 (Ohio Mun., Lancaster, Aug. 26, 2003); Barnebey v.

Oliver, No. 99-CVG-00026 (Ohio Mun., Lancaster, Jan. 27, 1999); Labaki v. Carter, No. 96- CVG-00558 (Ohio Mun., Z anesville, July 1, 1996); Williams v. Mentzer , No. 94 -CVG-238 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, May 17, 1994); Oliver v. Montgomery, No. 94-CVG-0049 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, Feb. 24, 1994); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Mallett , No. 93 -CVG- 09659 (Ohio Mun., Cle veland, Hous. Div., Jan. 7, 1994); Tompkins v. Goins , No. 93 -CVG- 01097 (Ohio Mun., Zanesville, Dec. 13, 1993).

13 the tenant. Apt. Mgmt., Inc. v. Woods, No. 05-CVG-650 (Ohio Mun., Lorain, June 1, 2005). The new notice to vacate renders moot any service or content defect and any waiver (via rent acceptance) of the notice to vacate served for the first eviction action.

Morrow v. Gates, No. CA91-11-021, 1992 WL 156116, at *2 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 29, 1992).

Likewise, when a court dismisses a first eviction a ction due to the landlord’s waiver (via future rent acceptance) of the notice to vacate, the landlord must serve a new notice to vacate before filing a second eviction action against the tenant. See N.

Face Props., Inc. v. Lin , No. CA2010-09-083, 2013 WL 2423802, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 3, 2013).

Also, when a landlord serves the tenant with a second notice to vacate and the service of the second notice is subsequent to the landlord’s filing of the eviction complaint, the second notice to vacate waives the first notice to vacate. RLJ Mgmt.
Co. Inc. v. Lewis, No. 2020-CVG-33669 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., June 2, 2021). As a result, in these circumstances: (1) the landlord is attempting to rely on a notice to vacate that was not served prior the filing of the complaint; and (2) therefore, the court lacks subject-matter jurisdiction over the eviction action. Id.; DePalmo v. George, No.

88-CVG-0572 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Aug. 23, 1988). The proper disposition of the eviction action is a dismissal either by the landlord or the court, with the landlord retaining the option to file a second eviction action. Id.

#### 2. Content


##### a. Written Notice

The notice to vacate must be in writing. R.C. 1923.04(A).

##### b. Name of the Prospective FED Plaintiff

Under R.C. 1923.04(A), t he notice to vacate must be signed by or otherwise contain the name of the party who will be the plaintiff in the complaint, if an eviction action is thereafter commenced. Riley v. Parker, No. 109600, 2021 WL 2012263 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 20, 2021); UMH OH Buckeye II, L.L.C. v. DeCarlo, No. 108912, 2019 WL 6606376 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 5, 2019); Gabriel v. Lakeside Tavern of Dayton, Inc. , No. 6578, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 12894, at *5 -7 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 22, 1980); Furr v. Pat terson, No. 2021 -CVG-004661 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 22, 2021); Flipping Lake County, Ltd. v. Egan , No. 21- CVG-0305 (Ohio Mun., Euclid, May 12, 2021); 128 Fleek Newark LLC v. Hassett ,
No. 20 -CVG-02022 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Oct. 21, 2020); KMW III Ltd. v.

Falacienski, No. 2020-CVG-01333R (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Sept. 14, 2020);Tr. of Kokosing Valley Trust v. Myers , No. 13-CVG-00428 (Ohio Mun., Mt. Vernon, Aug.

19, 2013). See also Shurtz Mgmt., Inc. v. Perry , No. 93 -CA-04, 1993 WL 290001 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 26, 1993) (valid notice where the notice to vacate is signed in the name of the plaintiff corporation, without the name of an officer or agent of the plaintiff corporation); Morrow v. Gates, No. CA91-11-021, 1992 WL 156116 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 29, 1992) (valid notice where the notice to vacate is signed only by plaintiff husband but it includes the names of plaintiff husband and plaintiff wife).

If the notice to v acate does not meet this requirement, it is an invalid notice and, therefore, the court is without subject-matter jurisdiction over the eviction action. Id.

For a discussion on the persons who may be the plaintiff in an eviction action, see Section II.CC, below.

##### c. Statement to Leave – Statutory Language

The notice to vacate must “notify the adverse party to leave the premises.” R.C.

1923.04(A).

R.C. 1923.04(A) prescribes certain language10 for so notifying the tenant, when a landlord seeks to recover res idential premises. 11 The notice to vacate may not paraphrase the statutory language. Mularcik v. Adams , No. 03 -JE-17, 2004 WL 549489 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar. 18, 2004). Rather, it must include the statutory language verbatim. Id.

The notice to vacate must contain the statutory language in a conspicuous manner.

This “conspicuous manner” requirement is not defined in R.C. 1923.04(A).

Two appellate districts have relied on the Uniform Commercial Code (R.C.

10 The language required by R.C. 1923.04(A) is: “You are being asked to leave the premises.

If you do not leave, an eviction action may be initiated against you. If you are in doubt regarding your legal rights and obligations as a tenant, it is recommended that you seek legal assistance.” R.C. 1923.04(A).

11 If the prospective FED plaintiff is not seeking to recover residential premises, R.C.

1923.04(A) does not require any particular language for notifying the adverse party to leave.

For example, “[a]lthough the words ‘leave the premises’ appear in R.C. 1923.04(A), the General Assembly has not indicated that these are the only words that a comme rcial landlord can use to instruct the tenant to vacate the property; rather, R.C. 1923.04 merely requires that a landlord ‘notify the adverse party to leave the premises ***.’”Maggiore v. Kovach, 101 Ohio St.3d 184, 803 N.E.2d 790, ¶ 26 (2004).

15 1301.01(J)) definition of “conspicuous,” namely: “a term or clause ... written [so] that a reasonable person against whom it is to operate ought to have noticed it.” Chinnock
v. Kokinda, No. 10-CA-009863, 2011 WL 860417, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 14, 2011); Greene Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Manning, No. 98-CA-55, 1999 WL 76456, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 19, 1999); Adm’r of Veterans Affairs v. Jackson, 41 Ohio App.3d 274, 276, 535 N.E.2d 369 (9th Dist. 1987). See also Dayton Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Russell, 16 Ohio Op.3d 94 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1980).

Court decisions provide additional guidance for determining whether a notice to vacate contains the statutory language in a conspicuous manner.

Where the entire notice to vacate, including the statutory language, is in the same type, color, and font, the statutory language is not conspicuous. Chinnock v. Kokinda,
No. 10-CA-009863, 2011 WL 860417 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 14, 2011); Dayton
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Russell, 16 Ohio Op.3d 94 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1980);Cherry Lee Apts. v. Walcott, No. 03-CVG-00510 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Apr. 25, 2003).

Where the entire notice to vacate, including the statutory language, is in upper case, generally the statutory language is not conspicuous. Sahara Apts. v. Brown -Austin,
No. CA-9028, 1993 WL 221253 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 7, 1993); Owner’s Mgmt.
Co. v. Willis, No. WD-87-59, 1988 WL 13214 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 12, 1988) (dicta); Queen v. Blakeman , No. 07 -CVG-00808 (Ohio Mun., Gallipolis, Dec. 13, 2007); Neighbors Organized for Action in Hous. v. Smith, No. 91-CVG-11983 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 26, 1991),aff’d on other grounds, No. 62219, 1992 WL 25305 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 13, 1992); Cody v. Bettinger , No. 96 -CVG- 00965 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Jan. 3, 1997).

Where the entire notice to vacate, including the statutory language, is in upper case type, but the statutory language is set off from the body of the notice by two lines of asterisks, the statutory language is conspicuous. Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v. Willis , No.

WD-87-59, 1988 WL 13214 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 12, 1988). See also Dayton
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Westfall, No. 79-CVG-7932 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Oct. 15, 1979) (statutory language enclosed in bright border and starred).

Where the statutory language is in smaller print than the other print on the notice, but is in bold faced print and is framed with red lines forming a rectangle around it and is placed in the corner of the notice, the statutory language is conspicuous. Cf.

Shewman v. Coons , No. 89 -CA-46, 1990 WL 34814, at *5 -7 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Mar. 29, 1990) (construing the R.C. 1923.06(B) provision that requires the summons in an eviction action to include certain statutory language in a conspicuous manner).

Where the statutory language is in upper case, a nd the remainder of the notice is in “letter form” upper and lower case, generally the statutory language is conspicuous.

Sahara Apts. v. Brown-Austin, No. CA-9028, 1993 WL 221253 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 7, 1993); Adm’r of Veterans Affairs v. Jackson, 41 Ohio App.3d 274, 535 N.E.2d 369 (9th Dist. 1987).

Where the statutory language is in upper case, and the remainder of the notice is in “letter form,” but the statutory language is not set off by a border and is in smaller print than and is in the samecolor as the remainder of the notice, the statutory language is not conspicuous. Detroit Hous. Ltd. P’ship v. Spurlock, No. 03-CVG-08908 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Sept. 23, 2003).

Where the statutory language is in upper case, and the remainder of the notice is a mix of “letter form” and all upper case, the statutory language is not conspicuous.

Scioto Woods Apts. v. Ferko , No. 08 -CVG-1860 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Oct. 21, 2008).

A notice that meets the “conspicuous manner” requirement “for sighted persons is not adequate to provide notice to an individual known to be sight -impaired.” Greene
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Manning , No. 98 -CA-55, 1999 WL 76456, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 19, 1999). In these circumstances, any statutory language in a written notice is not conspicuous, if the notice is simply posted on the tenant’s door. “A landlord, knowing that a tenant isblind, cannot claim that the tenant has been properly served with statutory notice when she cannot even see the notice posted on her door.” Id.

##### d. Reason for the Eviction

A notice to vacate is not required to state the reason for the eviction. Snyder v.

Wylie, No. L -17-1019, 2017 WL 4334189 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 29, 2017);

Venetta v. Battle, No. 88AP-1044, 1990 WL 157344 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Oct. 18, 1990); N.W. Ohio Food Marts, Inc. v. Mugg , No. L -86-115, 1987 WL 7791 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 13, 1987); Gerber v. Bistor , No. 1795, 1982 WL 2806 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Oct. 27, 1982); McKoy v. Bell, No. 2021-CVG-008782 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 9, 2021); Hawkins v. Collier , No. 87 -CVG-884 (Ohio Mun., Portsmouth, Sept. 10, 1987). But see DePalmo v. George , No. 88-CVG-0572 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Aug. 23, 1988) (“although there is authority to the contrary, this court finds that due process mandates that a landlord, at least in general terms which would put the tenant on notice of th e acts complained of, specify the grounds 17 for the eviction in the three-day notice”).

However, if the notice to vacate includes a statement of the reason for the eviction, it may preclude the landlord from asserting any other reasons in the eviction action .

Merriweather v. Wilcox, No. 2021-CVG-011765 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Jan. 26, 2022); McKoy v. Bell, No. 2021-CVG-008782 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., Nov. 9, 2021); Venetta v. Staples, No. M88-CVG-12477 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 13, 1988). Cf. Milbourn v. Aska, 81 Ohio App. 79, 82, 77 N.E.2d 619 (3rd
Dist. 1946) (notice declaring forfeiture of lease).

Also, if the notice to vacate states more than one reason for the eviction and the complaint alleges some but not all of those reasons, the landlord may rely on only those reasons alleged in the complaint. M.L.R. Props., Inc. v. Baer, No. L-85-330, 1986 WL 6703 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 13, 1986).

For further discussion on eviction complaints, see Section I.E, below.

##### e. Vacation Date

A notice to vacate is not required to state a specific date by which the tenant is to vacate the premises. Andrews Sch. v. Brown, No. 10-109, 1984 WL 7259 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 21, 1984);Brandy v. Fontroy, No. 77-CVG-9209 (Ohio Mun., Dayton,
Dec. 14, 1977).

A notice to vacate is also not required to reference the three -day period that must elapse before the landlord may file the eviction action. Andrews Sch. v. Brown , No.

10-109, 1984 WL 7259 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 21, 1984).

#### 3. Time of Service


##### a. Three-Day Rule

The notice to vacate must be served at least three days before the eviction action is commenced. R.C. 1923.04(A). The case law on the computation of this three -day period is discussed in Section I.C.3.b, below.

##### b. Computation of the Three-Day Period

The three-day period is not a seventy-two-hour period. Cooper v. Aberts, No. 98- CVG-384 (Ohio Mun., Mansfield, Feb. 25, 1998).

The case law is split on whether the three -day period is to be computed in accordance with Civil Rule 6(A).

Court decisions holding that Civil Rule 6(A) is applicable to the computation of this three -day period include: Ebbing v. Mathis , No. CA2012 -10-201, 2013 WL 2423702 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 3, 2013);Hudgins v. Campbell, No. 2013-CVG- 00086 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, Mar. 11, 2013); Midtown Apts. v. Davis , 20 Ohio B. 128, 1985 WL 6044 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 1985); GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Alnazer , No.

11-CVG-02425 (Ohio Mun., Berea, Nov. 4, 2011);Phillips v. Derringer, No. 05-CVG- 000657 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Apr. 12, 2005); Vogt v. Riley, No. 04-CVG-00829 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., Nov. 17, 2004); Powers v. Arnold , No. 03 -CVG-01890 (Ohio Mun., Fairfield, Aug. 26, 2003); McCallister v. Pickrel , No. 02 -CVG-00268 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Mar. 12, 2002);Labaki v. Carter, No. 96-CVG-00558 (Ohio Mun., Zanesville, July 1, 1996); Will v. Siller , No. 90 -CVG-1248 (Ohio Mun., Painesville, Sept. 19, 1990); Bezon v. James , No. 89 -CV-G&F-1957 (Ohio Mun., Alliance, Feb. 5, 1990); Spero v. Killeen , No. 7 -89-CVG-302 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Aug. 31, 1989); Wintrow v. Smith, 32 Ohio Misc.2d 12, 513 N.E.2d 363 (Ohio Mun., Akron, 1987); Bowers v. Starcher , No. 87 -CL-254686 (Ohio Mun., Springfield, July 11, 1987); Northpointe Apts. v. Dooley, No. M85-CVG-3909 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 8, 1985).

Court decisions holding that Civil Rule 6(A) is not applicable to the computation of this three -day period include: Faqi v. Patti , Nos. L -19-1038,1107, 2020 WL 6375793 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 30, 2020); Wodzisz v. Bayes, No. 96-APE07-891, 1997 WL 142715 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 25, 1997); Fed. Prop. Mgmt. v.

Daugherty, No. 12591, 1991 WL 116640 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 28, 1991);Willis
v. Thibault , No. 9 -87-47, 1989 WL 83816 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., July 31, 1989);

Pleasant View Mobile Home Court, Inc. v. Prichard, No. 82-CVG-1881 (Ohio Mun., Canton, May 28, 1982). See also State ex rel GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Lazzaro , No.

97875, 2012 WL 3765055 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 24, 2012) (court finds petitioner made a “strong argument” for the inapplicability of Civil Rule 6(A) but denies a writ of prohibition on two grounds: an appeal is an adequate remedy at law; and petitioner is essentially seeking a declaratory judgment via the writ).

If Civil Rule 6(A) is not applicable to the computation of this three-day period, the computation is nonetheless governed by R.C. 1.14. Midtown Apts. v. Davis, 20 Ohio
B. 128, 1985 WL 6044 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 1985); Wayland v. Plymire , No. 03 - CVG-987 (Ohio Mun., Barberton, July 9, 2003). Cf. Dennis v. Coble, 18 Ohio App.

62 (6th Dist. 1923) (applying General Code § 10216). Under R.C. 1.14, this three-day period is computed by excluding the first day, including the last day, and excluding any Sunday or holiday that would otherwise be the last day.

19 Consequently, for this computation, the only significant difference between R.C.

1.14 and Civil Rule 6(A) is that, unlike Civil Rule 6(A), R.C. 1.14 does not exclude every Saturday and Sunday.

##### c. Additional Time – Specified Vacation Date in the

Notice If the notice to vacate states a date by which the tenant is to vacate the premises and it affords the tenant more time than the minimum three -day period under R.C.

1923.04(A), the landlord may not file the eviction action until after the vacation date specified in the notice. Wodzisz v. Bayes, No. 96-APE07-891, 1997 WL 142715, at *5 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 25, 1997). But see Crawford v. Norman , 46 Ohio Law
Abs. 404, 66 N.E.2d 559 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1946); Mack v. Eckerlin, 2 Ohio L.R.

441 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., 1905), aff’d, 75 Ohio St. 627 (1907).

##### d. Additional Time – Landlord Delay

Generally, R.C. 1923.04(A) does not impose any limit on the amount of time beyond three days that a landlord may delay between serving the notice to vacate and filing the eviction complaint. See, e.g. , Reese v. Webb , No. L -04-1257, 2005 WL 1009889 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 29, 2005) (76 days between service of the notice to vacate and filing of the complaint); Adm’r of Veterans Affairs v. Jackson, 41 Ohio App.3d 274, 535 N.E.2d 369 (9th Dist. 1987) (five months between service of the notice to vacate and filing of the complaint, although landlord delayed filing the complaint at tenant’s request to allow tenant to present a purchase proposal); Owner’s
Mgmt. Co. v. Johnson , No. 61691, 1992 WL 309362 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 22,
1992) (45 days between service of the notice to vacate and filing of the complaint).

One exception to this general rule applies when the eviction action is fora tenant’s violation of R.C. 5321.05(A)(9). In those cases, the landlord is required to commence the eviction action “promptly” after the service of the notice to vacate. R.C.

5321.05(C)(2), 5321.17(C).

The courts are split on whether a second exception to this general rule exists. They are split on whether a landlord’s delay, between serving the notice to vacate and filing the FED action, may constitute a waiver of the notice to vacate.

One appellate court has held: “If the tenant does not vacate the premises by the end of the three-day period, the landlord may then file the FED action as long as the action is filed within two years of the date on which the tenant was in default of the terms of the rental agreement.” Reese v. Webb, No. L-04-1257, 2005 WL 1009889, ¶ 4 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 29, 2005).

In contrast, one municipal court has held: “A landlord who, … after its service of a notice to vacate, can waive its right to pursue an eviction action if it acts inconsistently with its pursuit of the eviction….[An] action that can be inconsistent is a delay in taking action … between the service of a notice to vacate and the filing of an eviction.” CMHA v. Hawthorne, No. 2012-CVG-007158, slip op. at 1 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 30, 2012, objections overruled Nov. 30, 2012).

Irrespective of the general rule and the exceptions, in most cases, the landlord’s inability to accept future rent without waiving the notice to vacate (see Section II.C,
below) prompts the landlord to file theeviction action promptly upon the expiration of the three-day period.

##### e. Expiration of Tenancy Prerequisite

Generally, the notice to vacate may not be served until after the expiration of the tenancy.12 Crigger v. Shaw, No. E-93-62, 1994 WL 68499, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Feb. 25, 1994); FMJ Props. v. Hinton, No. 50314, 1986 WL 4396, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 10, 1986); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Owens, No. 92-CVG-5159 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 21, 1992); Lincoln Mgmt. Co. v. Ash iegbu,
No. M9312-CVG-037858 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 26, 1994);Polster v. Lewis,
No. 91-CVG-29002 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 26, 1991); Phipps v.

Cable, No. CVG -91-0099 (Ohio Mun., Portsmouth, Mar. 14, 1991); Cassidy v.

Vaccariello, No . M8907-CVG-026434 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 28, 1989).

But see Leutzey v. Hershelrode , 20 Ohio St. 334 (1870); Hile v. Besecker , 82 Ohio
App. 301, 79 N.E.2d 582 (2nd Dist. 1947); Crawford v. Norman, 46 Ohio Law Abs.

404, 66 N.E.2d 559 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1946).

Therefore, when a month-to-month tenancy is terminated under R.C. 5321.17(B), the notice to vacate may not be served until after the expiration of the R.C. 5321.17(B) 12 When a tenancy is terminated during a period for which rent has been prepaid, the expiration of tenancy requirement does not require the expiration of that rent period. 1994-N1 Ohio Assocs., L.P. v. Planet Earth Entm’t, Inc., No. 15338, 1995 WL 738416 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Dec. 15, 1995). But see Summit Mgmt. Servs. v. Gough , No. 19714, 2000 WL 1226605 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 30, 2000); Forest City Mgmt., Inc. v. McDonald , No. 2001 -CVG-2806 (Ohio Mun., Akron, May 24, 2001). As to whether the landlord’s failure to return the prepaid rent for the post-termination period may waive the notice to vacate, see Section II.C, below.

21 thirty-day period.13 Amick v. Sickles, 177 Ohio App.3d 337, 894 N.E.2d 733 (4th Dist.
2008) (dicta); Mockensturm v. Razeq, No. L-85-149, 1986 WL 1692 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 7, 1986); Knights Ctr. Corp. v. Thomas, No. 49636, 1985 WL 9039 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 23, 1985); Voyager Vill. Ltd. v. Williams , 3 Ohio App.3d 288, 444 N.E.2d 1337 (2nd Dist. 1982);Siegler v. Batdorff, 63 Ohio App.2d 76, 408 N.E.2d 1383 (8th Dist. 1979); McKoy v. Bell, No. 2021-CVG-008782 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Nov. 9, 2021); Kaseman v. Maddux , No. 21 -CVG-02194 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Nov. 1, 2021);Lincoln Mgmt. Co. v. Ashiegbu, No. M9312-CVG-037858 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 26, 1994); Ewert v. Basinger, 59 Ohio Misc. 43, 392 N.E.2d 911 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, 1978). Cf. Lucas Metro. Hous. Auth. v. M. Shouman Supermarkets, Inc., No. L-93-244, 1994 WL 110844 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 31,
1994) (commercial month -to-month tenancy); Sterling Health Care Group, Inc. v.

Laughlin, No. 92 -WD-051, 1993 WL 179509 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 28, 1993) (commercial month-to-month tenancy).

Likewise, when a tenancy is terminated for nonpayment of rent, the notice to vacate may not be served until after the expiration of any grace period. Colonial Oaks
Props. LLC v. Hurst, No. 2021-CVG-00455 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, Dec. 9, 2020); Hayes Colony, Ltd. v. Woods, No. 08-CV-2030 (Ohio Mun., Delaware, Sept. 3, 2008);Myszka
v. Bledsoe , No. 00 -CVG-08398 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Nov. 9, 2000); Williams v.

Mentzer, No. 94 -CVG-238 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, May 17, 1994). A grace period before imposing a late payment fee is a grace period for payment of the rent, unless the lease unambiguously provides otherwise. Reinhard v. Lorek, No. 95-L-028, 1995 WL 787378 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 8, 1995); Colonial Oaks Props. LLC v. Hurst ,
No. 2021 -CVG-00455 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, Dec. 9, 2020); Hayes Colony, Ltd. v.

Woods, No. 08-CV-2030 (Ohio Mun., Delaware, Sept. 3, 2008).

And, when a tenancy is terminated under R.C. 5321.11, 14 the notice to vacate may not be served until after the expiration of the R.C. 5321.11 thirty-day period. Gibbes
v. Freeman, No. 52745, 1987 WL 16530 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 3, 1987);McKoy
v. Bell, No. 2021 -CVG-008782 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div ., Nov. 9, 2021);

Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v. Brent, No. 01-CVG-1693 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Aug. 1, 2001); Avondale Hous. Action Program v. Butler , No. 88 -CV-42805 (Ohio Mun., 13 R.C. 5321.17(B) is discussed in Section I.D, below.

14 R.C. 5321.11 is discussed in Section I.D, below.

Hamilton Cty., Mar. 23, 1989). See also Crigger v. Shaw , No. E -93-62, 1994 WL 68499 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 25, 1994). But see Label & Co. v. Hyde, No. 85-C- 63, 1986 WL 9120 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Aug. 20, 1986).

For assisted housing tenancies, which require the landlord to serve a federal notice of termination, the case law is split on whether the notice to vacate may be served prior to the expiration of the tenancy.15 Court decisions holding that, in the termination of these tenancies, the notice to vacate may not be served until after the expiration of the federal notice of termination include: Owner’s Mgmt. v. Weems, No. 97-CVG-07486 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., July 28, 1997); Wills Creek Valley Apts. v. Marlatt , No. 93-CVG-00066 (Ohio Mun., Cambridge, Apr. 27, 1993); McConnelsville Assocs. v. Gladden, No. 92-CVG- 56 (Ohio County Ct., Morgan Cty., Sept. 28, 1992); Lakeview Terrace v. Smith , No.

92-CVG-8034 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 21, 1992); Cuyahoga Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Owens, No. 92-CVG-5159 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 21, 1992); Polster v. Lewis, No. 91-CVG-29002 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec.

26, 1991); Oppmann Props. v. Jackson , No. 90 -CVG-9118 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., May 29, 1990); Avondale Hous. Action Program v. Butler , No. 88 -CV- 42805 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Mar. 23, 1989); Knoll Group Mgmt. v. Brown, No.

83-CVG-9624 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Feb. 8, 1984).16 Court decisions holding that the notice to vacate and the federal notice of termination may be consolidated into one notice and, consequently upholding the service of a notice to vacate prior to the expiration of the federal notice of termination include: Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Armstrong, No. L-93-168, 1994 WL 262571 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 1994); Pataskala Green II v. Strickler , No. CA -3581, 1991 WL 122358 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 25, 1991); Arlington Square ABC Mgmt. v.

Clevenger, No. 88-CA-004447, 1989 WL 52620 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 17, 1989);

15 The federal rules permit the federal notice of termination to run concurrently with the state notice to vacate. See Section III.C.4, below. But those rules do not eliminate state requirements on when the state notice to vacate may be served. 56 Fed. Reg. 51559, 51565 (1991) (“Federal law does not affect the time when the State notice may be given” ); Kennedy v. Andover Place Apts., 203 S.W.3d . 495, 498 (Tex. App. 2006) ( the federally subsidized lease (see note 174,
above) “does not say that a HUD notice can eliminate the statestatutory [notice] requirement”).

16 Also, in Crossroads Somerset Ltd. v. Newland , 73 Ohio App.3d 20, 531 N.E.2d 327 (10th
Dist. 1987), the court held that the consolidated federal notice of termination and state notice to vacate rendered the federal notice invalid. This holding is discussed inSection III.C.4, above.

23 Sandefur Co. v. Jones , 9 Ohio App.3d 85, 458 N.E.2d 390 (10th Dist. 1982); 17 Bella Vista Apts. v. Herzner , 125 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 796 N.E.2d 593 (Ohio Mun., Clermont Cty., 2003). See also Knoll Group Mgmt. Co. v. Wolfe , Nos. 93 -CA-553,554, 1994 WL 326347 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28,1994) (permitting service of federal notice of termination during pendency of eviction action); New Boston Kiwanis Hous., Etc.
v. Sparks, No. 1957, 1992 WL 79561 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Apr. 14, 1992) (permitting a second notice of termination to cure the content defect of the first notice of termination, if the second notice is served “timely” before the case comes to trial).

#### 4. Method of Service


##### a. General Rule

R.C. 1923.04(A) specifies three alternative methods for serving a notice to vacate:
(1) certified mail, return receipt requested; (2) personal service; and (3) leaving it at the tenant’s usual place of abode or at the premises.

The third method – leaving the notice at the premises – permits the landlord to leave the notice at the premises without physical delivery to a person. Atwood v. Bare,
No. 1854, 1988 WL 117999 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 28, 1988).

Under the third method, a landlord may serve the notice by posting it on the outside of the exterior door. Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Morgan , 104 Ohio St.3d 445, 820 N.E.2d 315 (2004); Hauke v. Harris , No. 2005 -CA-000003, 2005 WL 3316338 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 8, 2005); Hall v. Kottha , No. 64965, 1994 WL 110963 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 31, 1994);Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Anderson, No.

1406, 1988 WL 69118 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988). Posting means properly securing the notice to the door – thumbtack, adhesive tape, glue, etc. – and not simply leaving it in the crack of the door. Oakwood Mgmt. Co. v. Bryant, No. 04-CVG-0057 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Mar. 17, 2004).

The service by posting does not, on its face, violate due process.Cincinnati Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Morgan, 104 Ohio St.3d 445, 820 N.E.2d 315 (2004). But, if the rental unit is located in a neighborhood where notices frequently are removed from exterior doors, an “as applied” due process claim may exist. Id.

Under the third method, a landlord also may serve the notice by inserting it through 17 In ABC Mgmt. Co. v. Gamble , No. 83AP -788, 1983 Ohio App. LEXIS 16070 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 15, 1983), the court declined a request to overrule Sandefur Co. v. Jones.

the mail slot of the exterior door. Sanders v. Favors, No. C-950304, 1995 WL 763681 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 29, 1995);Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Anderson, No.

1406, 1988 WL 69118 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988).

“Service of [the R.C. 1923.04] notice in a manner not specified in the statute is not proper [service].” Youngstown Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Higgins , No. 99 -CA-81, 2000 WL 1159418, at *2 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Aug. 4, 2000). See Manifold v. Schuster, 67 Ohio App.3d 251, 586 N.E.2d 1142 (4th Dist. 1990).

For example, regular mail service is not a permissible method of service because it is not expressly authorized under R.C. 1923.04(A). Higgins, 2001 WL 1159418, at *2; Tompkins v. Goins, No. 93-CVG-01097 (Ohio Mun., Zanesville, Dec. 13, 1993).

Nonetheless, one appellate courtpermitted regular mail service where the landlord first attempted certified mail service and it was refused, there was no evidence that the tenant failed to receive the notice to vacate via the regular mail service, and, based on the date of the regular mail service, there was sufficient time for that service to deliver the notice timely. Aultman Home for Aged Women v. Ott , No. CA -7688, 1989 WL 64055 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 5, 1989).

##### b. Deceased Resident of Manufactured Home Park

When the adverse party i n an eviction action is a deceased resident of a manufactured home park, the R.C. 1923.04(A) notice to vacate “shall be left at the premises from which the defendant is sought to be evicted and shall also be sent by ordinary mail to the following persons i f their names and addresses are known to the park operator:” (1) any executor or administrator appointed by the probate court for any estate of the adverse party; and (2) “the deceased resident’s spouse and any other members of the deceased resident’s immediate family.” R.C. 1923.04(C).

#### 5. Time and Method of Service, Drug-Related R.C.

1923.02(A)(6) Eviction In an eviction action filed under R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a), the R.C. 5321.17(C) notice of termination also constitutes the notice to vacate for R.C. 1923.04 purposes. R.C.

1923.04(B).

An R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a) eviction action is based on a tenant’s alleged violation of R.C. 5321.05(A)(9). Under R.C. 5321.05(A)(9), a tenant and the tenant’s guests are required to conduct themselves, in connection with the p remises, so as not to violate the prohibitions against drug -related activities contained in R.C. Chapters 2925 and 25 3719 (or in any municipal ordinances that are substantially similar to any section in either of those chapters).

R.C. 5321.17(C) provides tha t, if the landlord has actual knowledge of or reasonable cause to believe that the tenant (or tenant’s guest) has violated R.C.

5321.05(A)(9), the landlord shall terminate the tenancy by giving the tenant a notice of termination that “shall specify that th e tenancy or other rental agreement is terminated three days after the giving of the notice.” In these circumstances, the landlord is obligated to “promptly” commence an FED action, after service of the R.C.

5321.17(C) notice to vacate. R.C. 5321.04(A)(9 ), 5321.17(C). See also Reese v.

Webb, No. L-04-1257, 2005 WL 1009889, ¶ 5 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 29, 2005).

For R.C. 5321.17(C) purposes, the landlord has actual knowledge of or reasonable cause to believe that the tenant (or tenant’s guest) has v iolated R.C.

5321.05(A)(9) only if:

a search warrant was issued pursuant to Criminal Rule 41 or Chapter
2933. of the Revised Code; the affidavit presented to obtain the warrant named or described the tenant or person as the individual to be searched and pa rticularly described the tenant’s premises as the place to be searched, named or described one or more controlled substances to be searched for and seized, stated substantially the offense under Chapter 2925. or 3719. of the Revised Code or the substantially similar municipal ordinance that occurred in, is occurring in, or otherwise was or is connected with the tenant’s premises, and states the factual basis for the affiant’s belief that the controlled substances are located on the tenant’s premises; the wa rrant was properly executed by a law enforcement officer and any controlled substance described in the affidavit was found by that officer during the search and seizure; and, subsequent to the search and seizure, the landlord was informed by that or anothe r law enforcement officer of the fact that the tenant or person has or presently is engaged in a violation as described in this Division and it occurred in, is occurring in, or otherwise was or is connected with the tenant’s premises.

R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a)(i).

If a search warrant has not been issued, R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a)(i) does not apply.

Buckeye Mgmt. Co. v. Mason, No. 2002-CO-68, 2003 WL 22120673 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Sept. 11, 2003).

But R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a)(i ) is not the exclusive grounds for an eviction action for drug-related criminal activity. Buckeye Mgmt. Co. v. Mason , No. 2002 -CO-68, 2003 WL 22120673 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Sept. 11, 2003). The R.C. 1923.02(A)(9) grounds for an eviction action – breach of a written lease – permits an eviction action for breach of a lease provision that prohibits any drug -related criminal activity on the
premises. Id.

### D. Notices of Termination, R.C. 5321.11,

5321.17

#### 1. Introduction

In two circumstances, R.C. Chapter 5321 req uires a residential landlord to serve the tenant with a notice of termination, if the landlord seeks to terminate the tenancy.18 The first is where a landlord seeks to terminate a tenancy on the ground that the tenant has violated an R.C. 5321.05 duty (oth er than R.C. 5321.05(A)(9)) and the violation materially affects health and safety. R.C. 1923.02(A)(8), 5321.11.

The second is where a landlord seeks to terminate a month-to-month (or week-to-
week) tenancy and the tenant has not violated the rental agreement or any R.C. Chapter 5321 obligation. R.C. 5321.17(A),(B). See also Drake Townhouses, LLC v.

Woodberry, No. C -160632, 2017 WL 3168981 , ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., July 26, 2017); Eberly v. Irons, No. 2006AP-01-0004, 2007 WL 2351644, ¶ 34 (Ohio App.,5th Dist., Aug. 9, 2007). If the tenant has violated the rental agreement (e.g., failed to pay
rent) or an R.C. Chapter 5321 obligation, this notice of termination requirement is not
applicable. R.C. 5321.17(D). See also Drake Townhouses, LLC v. Woodberry, No. C- 160632, 2017 WL 3168981, ¶¶ 18-20 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., July 26, 2017); Denney v.

Carroll, No. 15 -14-10, 2015 WL 1966227 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., May 4, 2015);

Georgetown Park Apts. v. Woernley, 112 Ohio App.3d 428, 679 N.E.2d 16 (8th Dist.

1996); Gerber v. Bistor , No. 1795, 1982 WL 2806 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Oct. 27, 1982).

These notices of termination are a different type of notice than the R.C. 1923.04(A) notice to vacate, which is discussed in Section I.C, above. The notice of termination terminates or proposes to terminate the tenancy, whereas the R.C. 1923.04(A) notice to vacate is a prerequisite to the filing of an eviction action. See Gvozdanovic v.

18 In an assisted housing tenancy, the federal regulations require the landlord to serve a notice of termination, if the landlord seeks to terminate the tenancy. For a discussion on the federal notices of termination, see Sections III.A-E and Section XXIV.B.6, below.

27 Woodford Corp. , 139 Ohio App.3d 11, 30, 742 N.E.2d 1145 (1st Dist. 2000);

Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. McCollum , 45 Ohio App.2d 197, 199, 341 N.E.2d 857 (1st Dist. 1975). A landlord’s termination or proposal to terminate a tenancy is an action separate and distinct from the landlord’s filing of an eviction action. Id.

#### 2. R.C. 5321.11 Notice of Termination


##### a. Materially Affects Health and Safety, Effect of the

Limitation R.C. 5321.11, together with R.C. 1923.02(A)(8), permit a landlord to terminate a tenancy for a tenant’s violation of R.C. 5321.05, but only if the R.C. 5321.05 violation materially affects health and safety. They also make any such termination of tenancy subject to the notice and right to cure requirements that are discussed in Sections I.D.2.b-g, below.

A resulting issue is whether, despite the inapplicability of R.C. 5321.11,a landlord may terminate a tenancy for a tenant’s violation of R.C. 5321.05 if the violation does not materially affect health and safety. The case law is split.Compare Jones v. Burns,
No. R90 -CVG-2008 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 10, 1990) (terminati on not
permitted) with Starr v. Kaderly, No. 96-APG03-293, 1996 WL 465256 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Aug. 15, 1996) (termination permitted), Berkeley Park, LLC v. Pile , No.

2005-CVG-046214 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 11, 2007), and Klepetko v. Bettis,
No. 06-CVG-09092 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 11, 2006).

The better reasoned conclusion is that the landlord may not terminate the tenancy for such violations. Three factors support this conclusion.

First, R.C. 5321.11 is the only provision in R.C. Chapter 5321 that authorizes a landlord to terminate a tenancy for a tenant’s violation of R.C. 5321.05. This evidences the legislature’s intent to permit a termination of tenancy for an R.C. 5321.05 violation only if the violation materially affects health and safety.

Second, a construction of the statute that would permit a landlord to terminate a tenancy for an R.C. 5321.04 violation that does not materially affect health and safety would grant a tenant more rights – a 30 -day notice and right to cure – when the violation materially affects health and safety than when the violation does not materially affect health and safety. It is unlikely that the legislature intended the illogical consequence of granting tenants more rights for engaging in mor e harmful
behavior. When a statute is ambiguous, as in this matter, the courts are to consider “the consequences of a particular construction” (R.C. 1.49(E)) and “to presume that [a] just and reasonable result is intended” (R.C. 1.47(C)).

Third, prohibiting a termination of tenancy for R.C. 5321.05 violations that do not materially affect health and safety is consistent with the legal maxim “de minimis not curat lex,” which is discussed, with respect to residential leases, in Section II.W, below.

A relevant sub-issue is whether R.C. 1923.02(A)(9) is authority for terminating a tenancy for tenant conduct that violates R.C. 5321.05 and a rental agreement provision but does not materially affect health and safety. R.C. 1923.02(A)(9) authorizes eviction actions for breach of an obligation under a written rental agreement.

For oral periodic rental agreements, R.C. 1923.02(A)(9) is simply inapplicable and, therefore, cannot be a basis for terminating the tenancy for an R.C. 5321.05 violation that does not materially affect health and safety.

For written rental agreements, because R.C. Chapter 5321 does not permit a termination of tenancy for an R.C. 5321.05 violation that does not materially affect health and safety, R.C. 1923.02(A)(9) cannot be a basis for terminating the tenancy for such violations. R.C. 5321.06 and 5321.13(A) prohibit lease provisions that are inconsistent with R.C. Chapter 5321. As discussed in Section I.D.2.f, below, this restriction applies to terminations of tenancies for a tenant’s violation of R.C. 5321.05.

##### b. Written Notice

The R.C. 5321.11 notice of termination (“R.C. 5321.11 notice”) must be a “written notice.” R.C. 5321.11.

##### c. Statement of the Violation with Specificity

“Inherent in the opportunity to cure a default is knowledge o f what is in default.

Absent knowledge of what must be corrected in order to avoid the specified penalty, the right to cure the default becomes a meaningless guessing game.” Gallagher v.

Borden, Inc. , 84 Ohio App.3d 185, 188, 616 N.E.2d 577 (10th Dist. 1992) (lease provision notice requirement similar to the R.C. 5321.11 notice).

The R.C. 5321.11 notice must describe with specificity the acts and omissions of the tenant that constitute the alleged R.C. 5321.05 violation. R.C. 5321.11.

Examples of statements that failed to state the tenant’s acts and omissions with the requisite specificity are set forth below.
(1) “[D]ue to your interference with the right of your neighbors to the peaceful enjoyment of their premises.” Hood v. Kusior, No. 97-CVG- 29 3823 (Ohio Mun., Akron, July 23, 1997).
(2) “[N]umerous violations of conditions of lease, specifically #30.” Avondale Hous. Action Program v. Butler , No. 88 -CV-42805 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Mar. 23, 1989).
(3) The statement that the tenant performed acts that disturbed her neighbors and that the tenant created physical hazards. Burney View Apts. v.

Zeisler, No. 87-CV-17778 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Feb. 3, 1988).

##### d. Statement of a Termination Date, and the Thirty -Day

Requirement The R.C. 5321.11 notice must advise the tenant that the tenancy will terminate upon a date specified in the notice. Lovett v. Brown, No. 97-CVG-5586 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Aug. 20, 1997); Hood v. Kusior, No. 97-CVG-3823 (Ohio Mun., Akron, July 23, 1997). Cf. M.L.R. Props., Inc. v. Baer, No. L-85-330, 1986 WL 6703 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 13, 1986) (construing R.C. 3733.13 (now R.C. 4781.45) – the manufactured home park provision for a notice of termination that is essentially the same as R.C. 5321.11).

This requirement is not met if the notice uses conditional language such as “may lead to,” “may result in,” or “could result in” your eviction if you fail to comply in the
future. Green v. Sephus , No. 90 -CVG-1768-38, slip op. at 3 (Ohio M un., East Cleveland, Jan. 8, 1991).

The termination date specified in the R.C. 5321.11 notice may not be less than thirty days after the date on which the notice has been served. R.C. 5321.11.

For example, if an R.C. 5321.11 notice specifies that the tenancy will be terminated on November 30th, the notice must be served on or before October 31st. If the notice is served on November 2nd, it provides the tenant with only twenty-eight days advance notice and thus fails to comply with R.C. 5321.11. Willowood Apts. v. Page, No. 96- L-003, 1996 WL 535235, at *3 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 6, 1996).

##### e. Timely Remedy of the Violation

If the tenant remedies the R.C. 5321.05 violation before the termination date specified in the R.C. 5321.11 notice, the rental agre ement is not terminated pursuant to the notice and the tenant’s conduct that violated R.C. 5321.05 cannot be the basis for an eviction action. 19 R.C. 5321.11. See also Columbiana Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Parker, No. 03 -CO-46, 2004 WL 1533250 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 28, 2004);

Woodrose Ltd. Belmont Dev. Co. v. Debolt , No. CA -403, 1991 WL 115990 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 20, 1991);Hampshire House Apts. v. Henderson, No. 3791, 1987 WL 31705 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 24, 1987) (dicta). Cf. Leichtman v. Fike, No.

1106, 1984 WL 7338 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Apr. 27, 1984) (construing R.C. 3733.13 (now R.C. 4781.45) – the manufactured home park provision for a notice of termination that is essentially the same as R.C. 5321.11).

R.C. 5321.11 does not expressly require a statement of the right to cure in the R.C.

5321.11 notice. One trial court has held that no such requirement exists and, thereupon, rejected the magistrate’s conclusion to the contrary. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Mayson, No. 97 -CVG-1293 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, magistrate’s decision and judgment entry filed Sept. 8, 1997, order nunc pro tunc filed Sept. 29, 1997, and order filed Mar. 19, 1998).

However, the R.C. 5321.11 notice may not include language that suggests that the right to cure does not exist. Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v. Brent , No. 01 -CVG-1693 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Aug. 1, 2001). For example, the R.C. 5321.11 notice may not include the language required for an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate and state categorically that, if the t enant fails to vacate as of the specified termination date, an eviction action will be commenced.20 Id.

When the R.C. 5321.05 violation specified in the R.C. 5321.11 notice is damage to the premises, the tenant may cure the violation by paying to the landlord the amount of the landlord’s cost to repair the damage.M.L. Gabriel Commons v. King, No. 13159, 1992 WL 288781 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 14, 1992); Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Mayson, No. 97-CVG-1293 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, May 5, 1998).

A landlord’s refusal to accept such payment is a violation of the landlord’s obligations under R.C. 5321.11. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Mayson, No. 97-CVG- 19 In an eviction action, if the landlord claims that the tenancy has been terminated pursuant to an R.C. 5321.11 notice, an element of the landlord’s claim is that the tenant failed to remedy timely the R.C. 5321.05 violation. See Section II.F, below.

20 A related issue, discussed in Section I.C.3.e, above, is whether a consolidation of the R.C.

1923.04 notice to vacate and the R.C. 5321.11 notice of termination constitutes improper service of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate because it is served prior to th e expiration of the tenancy.

31 1293 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, May 5, 1998).

A tenant’s failure to make such payment may be a failur e to cure the violation.

M.L. Gabriel Commons v. King, No. 13159, 1992 WL 288781 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Oct. 14, 1992).

When the R.C. 5321.05 violation specified in the R.C. 5321.11 notice is an ongoing activity of the tenant, the tenant may cure the vi olation by no longer engaging in the improper activity.

For example, the R.C. 5321.05 violation may be the tenant causing excessive noise in her dwelling unit that disturbs the neighbors’ peaceful enjoyment. E.g., Parker v.

Fisher, 17 Ohio App.3d 103, 477 N.E.2d 654 (9th Dist. 1984). In that case, the tenant may cure the R.C. 5321.11 violation by no longer causing such noise.21 Id.

For another example, the R.C. 5321.05 violation may be continuing to put trash bags on the yard.E.g., Columbiana Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Parker, No. 03-CO-46, 2004 WL 1533250 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 28, 2004). In that case, the tenant may cure the R.C. 5321.05 violation by ceasing to put trash bags on the yard. Id.

##### f. Applicability to Written Leases

The R.C. 5321.11 notice and right to cure are applicable to the termination of a written lease, if the termination is based on conduct of the tenant that violates both the lease and R.C. 5321.05. Columbiana Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Parker , No. 03 -CO-46, 2004 WL 1533250 (Ohio App.,7th Dist., June 28, 2004);Woodrose Ltd. Belmont Dev.
Co. v. Debolt, No. CA-403, 1991 WL 115990 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 20, 1991);

Portage Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Brown, 66 Ohio App.3d 737, 586 N.E.2d 168 (11th Dist.

1990); Vandalia Apts., Ltd. v. Leonard, No. 10277, 1987 WL 15725 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Aug. 13, 1987); Northland Vill. Apts. v. Harris , No. 10156, 1987 WL 14276 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 6, 1987);Shreve Ltd. v. Seevers, No. 2026, 1987 WL 10766 21 However, one court found that the cessation did not constitute a timely cure where the tenant’s children had engaged in the improper conduct and, prior to the service of the R.C. 5321.11 notice, the landlord and tenant had had several meetings and agreements to prevent the children from continuing to cause the disturbances, which had become increasingly more serious. Fed.
Prop. Mgmt. v. Brown , No. 17424, 1999 WL 961275 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 25, 1999).

But cf. Columbiana Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Parker , No. 03 -CO-46, 2004 WL 1533250, ¶ 27 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 28, 2004) (distinguishes Brown: “In this case, the situation complained of (garbage bags in the yard) was completely under appellant’s control whereas in Brown, the situation complained of (Brown’s children, disturbing neighbors and breaking the
rules) seemed out of Brown’s control....Brown could not control her children”).

(Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 22, 1985); Sandefur Mgmt. Co. v. Wilson, 21 Ohio App.3d 160, 486 N.E.2d 1267 (10th Dist. 1985); Parker v. Fisher, 17 Ohio App.3d 103, 477 N.E.2d 654 (9th Dist. 1984); Odneal v. Wolfe, No. L-79-346, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 9841 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 13, 1980). 22 But see Colonial Am. Dev. Corp., Etc.
v. Walters, No. CA-87-8, 1987 WL 25252 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 17, 1987).

The R.C. 5321.11 notice and right to cure are not applicable to the termination of a written lease, if the termination is based on conduct of th e tenant that violates the lease but not R.C. 5321.05. Carkhuff v. Corpening , No. 2017 -A-0090, 2018 WL 4693341 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 28, 2018); Denney v. Carroll , No. 15 -14-10, 2015 WL 1966227 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., May 4, 2015); Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Fonseca, 110 Ohio App.3d 292, 673 N.E.2d 1019 (9th Dist. 1996); Georgetown Park
Apts. v. Woernley , 112 Ohio App.3d 428, 679 N.E.2d 16 (8th Dist. 1996); Portage
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Brown, 66 Ohio App.3d 737, 586 N.E.2d 168 (11th Dist. 1990);

Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Sistrunk, No. 12253, 1986 WL 2214 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,
Feb. 12, 1986); Parker v. Fisher , 17 Ohio App.3d 103, 477 N.E.2d 654 (9th Dist.

1984). See also Denhart v. Whaley , No. 88 -CV-16092 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty.,
Nov. 2, 1988) (R.C. 5321.11 does not apply to an eviction based upon a criminal conviction because the criminal act cannot conceivably be cured).

##### g. Jurisdictional Requirement

When the ground for an eviction action is a tenant’s violation of an R.C.

5321.05(A) obligation that materially affects health and safety, R.C. 1923.02(A)(8) is the source of the court’s subject-matter jurisdiction.

R.C. 1923.02(A)(8) imposes a requirement on any landlord who seeks to invoke this subject-matter jurisdiction: “Prior to the commencem ent of an action under this division, notice shall be given to the tenant and compliance secured with section 5321.11 of the Revised Code.” Therefore, in such eviction actions, the landlord’s compliance with the R.C.

5321.11 notice and cure rules is a jurisdictional requirement. See Woodrose Ltd.

22 See also Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v. Brent, No. 01-CVG-1693 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Aug.

1, 2001); Hood v. Kusior, No. 97-CVG-3823 (Ohio Mun., Akron, July 23, 1997); Williams v.

Wilson, No. 89 -CVG-2173 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Apr. 10, 1989); Yutzy v. Huebner , 14 Ohio Op.3d 440 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., 1979); Denhart v. Cooper , No. 78 -CV-35878 (Ohi o Mun., Hamilton Cty., Jan. 10, 1979); Fitzpatrick Realty v. Wilkey , No. 78 -CVG-8102 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Nov. 17, 1978).

33 Belmont Dev. Co. v. Debolt , No. CA -403, 1991 WL 115990 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 20, 1991);Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Anderson, No. 1406, 1988 WL 69118 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988); Filinger v. Harris, No. 07-CVG-01188 (Ohio Mun., Shaker Heights, Aug. 10, 2007); Walnut Hills Apts. v. Costner , 108 Ohio Misc.2d 43, 739 N.E.2d 416 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1999); Yutzy v. Huebner, 14 Ohio Op.3d 440 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., 1979).

#### 3. R.C. 5321.17 Notice of Termination


##### a. Written or Oral Notice

The R.C. 5321.17 notice of termination (“R.C. 5321.17 notice”) may be a written notice or an oral notice. Shewman v. Coons, No. 89-CA-46, 1990 WL 34814 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 29, 1990). See also Feldmann v. Ponziani, No. 14857, 1995 WL 232476 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 21, 1995).

If a month-to-month tenancy is created pursuant to a written leas e agreement and the lease requires a written thirty -day notice of termination, the requirement for a written notice is not per se enforceable. See Section VII.K.1, below, for the longstanding line of cases that “concern oral notification of termination of the lease which occurred before the written notification was required. In those cases, the tenant failed to provide written notification.” Claypool v. Dews, No. 18-CA-0925, 2018 WL 6930496, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Dec. 31, 2018). The courts hold th at, in these circumstances, to require written notification would be unconscionable under R.C.

5321.14.

Likewise, if a written lease for a fixed term (e.g., one year) requires a written thirty- day notice to prevent commencement of a month -to-month tenancy at the end of the fixed term, the requirement for a written thirty-day notice is not per se unenforceable.

Knipp v. Sadler , No. 6 -09-04, 2009 WL 2710053 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 31, 2009); Meadowbrook Dev. Corp. v. Roberts , No. 79747, 2001 WL 1654540 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 27, 2001); Ballard v. KMG Investors, Ltd. P’ship, No. 14-93-5, 1993 WL 293944 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 3, 1993); McGowan v. DM Group IX, 7 Ohio App.3d 349, 455 N.E.2d 1052 (10th Dist. 1982).

However, in either of these circumstances, if the tenant provides the landlord with a timely oral notice of termination but not a written notice, enforcement of the written notice requirement would be both hypertechnical and unconscionable. Id.; Seginak v.
