ABC Mgmt. Co., No. 3816, 1987 WL 17258 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 18, 1987).

R.C. 5321.17 does not specify the permissible methods of service for the R.C.

5321.17 notice. When a landlord elects to serve the tenant with a written R.C. 5321.17 notice, the courts are likely to permit service by any of the methods that R.C. 1923.04 permits for the notice to vacate. See, e.g., DePalmo v. George , No. 88 -CVG-0572 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Aug. 23, 1988) (written R.C. 5321.17 notices are not required to be served personally upon the tenantand may be served by leaving them at the premises).

##### b. Content Requirements

The only content requirement for an R.C. 5321.17 notice is that it must inform the tenant of the termination of the tenancy. The notice is not required to include any particular language.

However, it must “unequivocally inform” the tenant that the tenancy will be terminated as of a certain date. Gordon v. Morris, No. 2000-CA-69, 2001 WL 85797, at *5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 2001); Reith v. Skruck, 71 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 2, 653 N.E.2d 755 (Ohio Mun., Wadsworth, 1995).

##### c. Time of Service, and the Thirty-Day Requirement

The R.C. 5321.17(B) notice must be given at least thirty days before the periodic rental date. R.C. 5321.17(B). See also Risch v. Samuel , No. C -190159, 2020 WL 1488724 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar. 25, 2020);Detweiter v. Galt, No. 01-CA-34, 2001 WL 1682822 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 26, 2001); DeBenedictus v. Gialamas, No.

96-L-006, 1997 WL 51453 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Jan. 24, 1997);M. Friedman Mgmt.
Co. v. Malek, No. 93-L-022, 1993 WL 548091 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 3, 1993);

Dunning v. Green, No. F-82-12, 1983 WL 13825 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 4, 1983);

Siegler v. Batdorff, 63 Ohio App.2d 76, 408 N.E.2d 1383 (8th Dist. 1979).

This differs from a thirty-day period before the termination date (Detweiter, 2001 WL 1682822, at *1), and the difference is often significant.

The court decisions set forth below ill ustrate the R.C. 5321.17(B) notice period (i.e., thirty days before the periodic rental date). Each of these court decisions concerned a tenancy with a periodic rental date of the first day of the month, unless another date is specified.
(1) Notice served Oct ober 9, 2000, tenancy terminated November 30,
2000. Detweiter v. Galt , No. 01 -CA-34, 2001 WL 1682822 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 26, 2001).

35
(2) Notice served May 2, 1994, tenancy terminated May 31, 1994.

Feldmann v. Ponziani , No. 14857, 1995 WL 232476 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 21, 1995).
(3) Notice served December 14, 1991, tenancy terminated January 31, 1992.

### M. Friedman Mgmt. Co. v. Malek , No. 93 -L-022, 1993 WL 548091

(Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 3, 1993).
(4) Notice served January 2, 1990, tenancy terminated Ja nuary 31, 1990.

Wiltsie v. Teamor , 89 Ohio App.3d 380, 624 N.E.2d 772 (8th Dist.

1993).
(5) Notice served January 31, 1985, tenancy terminated March 31, 1985.

Mockensturm v. Razeq, No. L-85-149, 1986 WL 1692 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 7, 1986).
(6) Notice served November 15, 1976, tenancy terminated December 31,
1976. Cavucci v. Simco Mgmt. Corp., No. 77-CA-40, 1978 Ohio App.

LEXIS 9669 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Apr. 4, 1978).
(7) Periodic rental date 7th day of month, notice served February 14, 2008, tenancy terminat ed April 6, 2008. Smith v. Donaldson , No. CVG - 0800217 (Ohio Mun., Hocking Cty., Apr. 4, 2008).
(8) Notice served November 7, 2001, tenancy terminated January 1, 2002.

Tipper v. Recklaw, No. 2001-CVG-11482 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Feb. 1, 2002).
(9) Notice served Octob er 21, 1993, tenancy terminated November 30,
1993. Lincoln Mgmt. Co. v. Ashiegbu, No. M9312-CVG-037858 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 26, 1994).
(10) Notice served October 10, 1988, tenancy terminated November 30,
1988. Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. v. Russell , No. 7 -89-CVG-100 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Apr. 5, 1989).

When the month -to-month tenancy is created by a written lease, a tenant’s substantial compliance with the thirty-day requirement may be sufficient to terminate the tenancy. E.g., Claypool v.Dews, No. 18-CA-0925, 2018 WL 6930496, (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Dec. 31, 2018) (written notice delivered two days late). The Claypool decision relied on the line of cases that hold that “‘hypertechnical construction’ of a residential lease provision should not be required where a party has substantially complied with a notice provision.” Id., ¶ 13.

In determining the periodic rental date for R.C. 5321.17(B) purposes, the court should consider any established practice between the parties that allows the ten ant to pay rent late. Smith v. Mallory , No. 52982, 1987 WL 20215 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Nov. 19, 1987).

The parties to a month -to-month tenancy may not enter into or enforce any agreement, oral or written, that shortens the time period for an R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of termination. Detweiter v. Galt, No. 01-CA-34, 2001 WL 1682822, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 26, 2001) (invalid oral agreement for thirty -day notice requirement irrespective of periodic rental date); DeBenedictus v. Gialamas, No. 96 -L-006, 1997 WL 51453, at *3 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Jan. 24, 1997) (invalid written agreement for twenty-day notice requirement). Any such agreement would violate the R.C. 5321.06 prohibition against rental agreement provisions that are inconsistent with or prohibited by R.C. Chapter 5321. Id.

However, the R.C. 5321.06 prohibition does not preclude the parties, at any time, from mutually agreeing to terminate a month-to-month tenancy as of a specified date.

E.g., Tuttle v. Smith, No. 93AP-1078, 1993 WL 538309 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec.

23, 1993).

#### 4. Rent Payment After Service of the Notice of

Termination For the thirty-day period of an R.C. 5321.11 notice or an R.C. 5321.17(B) notice, the tenant is obligated to pay the rent. A failure to do so would expose the tenant to an eviction action for nonpaymen t of rent, which would eliminate the notice of termination requirement (see Section I.D.1, above) and thereby accelerate the eviction process.

The landlord has a corresponding obligation to accept any tender of such rent. A landlord may not circumvent R. C. 5321.11 or R.C. 5321.17(B) by refusing to accept rent that has been tendered timely and in full and then evicting for nonpayment of rent.

See Section II.J, below.

The landlord’s acceptance of the rent for the thirty-day period (and thus before the termination of the tenancy) does not waive the R.C. 5321.11 or 5321.17(B) notice.Cf.

Edgbarton Inv., Inc. v. Target Expediting, Inc., No. L-99-1410, 2000 WL 1713816, at *3-4 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 17, 2000) (commercial month-to-month tenancy).

However, the landlord’s acceptance of future rent for any period after the thirty - day period may waive the R.C. 5321.11 or 5321.17(B) notice. See Section II.D, below.

37

#### 5. Second Notice of Termination After Voluntary

Dismissal The case law is split on whether a landlord, who served an R.C. 5321.17(B) notice and thereafter filed and dismissed voluntarily an eviction action, may rely on that R.C.

5321.17(B) notice in a second eviction action.Compare Voyager Vill. Ltd. v. Williams, 3 Ohio App.3d 288, 293-94, 444 N.E.2d 1337 (2nd Dist. 1982) (“The fact that notice made in compliance with R.C. 5321.17(B) was involved in litigation which was dismissed is of no consequence”) with Apt. Mgmt., Inc. v. Woods , No. 05 -CVG-650 (Ohio Mun., Lorain, June 1, 2005) (“[T]his court holds as a matter of law that where an action in forcible entry and detainer is dismissed, prior to refiling a subsequent action in forcible entry and detainer, any and all notices required by either statute or the contract at issue must be re-served by the Plaintiff upon the Defendant”).

### E. Content of the Complaint


#### 1. R.C. 1923.05

Content requirements for an FED complaint are established in R.C. 1923.05, which states in relevant part:

The complaint shall particularly describe the premises so entered upon and detained, and set forth either an unlawful and forcible entry and detention, or an unlawful and forcible detention after a peaceable or lawful entry of the described premises.

R.C. 1923.05 does not require a landlord to allege in the FED complaint the existence or terms of the lease between the landlord and tenant. Steiner v. Minkowski, 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist. 1991).

R.C. 1923.05 also does not require a landlord to request in the FED complaint the issuance of a writ of restitution in order f or the landlord to secure the writ upon the entry of the FED judgment. Marysville Estates v. Bruce , No. 14 -13-12, 2013 WL 5347438, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 23, 2013). For further discussion on the writ of restitution, see Section I.O.2, below.

#### 2. Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure


##### a. Civil Rule 8(A) – Ground for Eviction Limited by

Complaint The ground for the eviction action that the landlord alleges in the complaint is the only ground on which the landlord may rely. M.L.R. Props., Inc. v. Baer , No. L-85- 330, 1986 WL 6703, at *3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 13, 1986);Boardwalk Glenville
Apts. v. Cage , No. 2007 -CVG-3065 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 13, 2007); Jackson v. Hammond , No. 2006 -CVG-31171 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., Feb. 15, 2007); Cmty. Gardens Park & Sales, Inc. v. Roe , No. M8109 -CVG- 027176 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 6, 1981); Inner City Hous. v. Sebastian, No.

81-CV-15029 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., June 29, 1981).

Consequently, if a notice to vacate or a notice of termination states more than one reason for the eviction and the FED complaint alleges some but not all of those reasons, the landlord may rely on only those reasons alleged in the complaint. M.L.R. Props.,
Inc. v. Baer , No. L -85-330, 1986 WL 6703, at *1,3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 13,
1986) (notice to vacate);Inner City Hous. v. Sebastian, No. 81-CV-15029 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., June 29, 1981) (notice of termination).

##### b. Civil Rule 10(D) – Attachment of Lease

An FED complaint must conform with the requir ements of Civil Rule 10(D).

Small v. Williams, No. 44418, 1982 WL 5247 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 25, 1982) (dicta); Point Rental Co. v. Posani, 52 Ohio App.2d 183, 368 N.E.2d 1267 (10th Dist.

1976); Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Westfall, No. 79-CVG-7932 (Ohio Mun., Dayton,
Oct. 15, 1979).

Therefore, if the tenancy and thus the eviction action is founded on a written lease, a copy of the lease must be attached to the complaint or the reason for the omission must be stated in the complaint. Id. Cf. Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Morgan, 155 Ohio App.3d 189, 194, 800 N.E.2d 64 (1st Dist. 2003), rev’d on other grounds, 104 Ohio St.3d 445, 820 N.E.2d 315 (2004) (“when a landlord such as CMHA seeks to evict a tenant based on the terms of a lease agreement, the landlord should at the very least provide the trial court with a copy of the lease”).

If only part of the lease (instead of the entire lease) is attached to the complaint, this Civil Rule 10(D) requirement is not met. Green v. Smith, No. 2014-CVG-025805 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 12, 2014).

39 Also, the landlord’s belief that the tenant has a copy of the lease is an insufficient reason for failing to attach it. Point Rental Co. v. Posani , 52 Ohio App.2d 183, 185, 368 N.E.2d 1267 (10th Dist. 1976).

“The proper procedure in attacking the failure of a plaintiff to attach a copy of a written instrument [in this case, a written lease] or to state a valid reason for his failure to attach same is to serve a motion for a definite statement, pursuant to Civ.R. 12(E).”
Id. at 186. Accord Fletcher v. Univ. Hosp. of Cleveland , 120 Ohio St.3d 167, 897 N.E.2d 147 (2008);Trabuco Homes, L.L.C. v. Brewer, No. 109595, 2021 WL 2375962 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 10, 2021); Asset Acquisitions Group, L.L.C. v. Gettis, 186 Ohio App.3d 586, 929 N.E.2d 506 (2nd Dist. 2010);Landskroner v. Landskroner, 154 Ohio App.3d 471, 797 N.E.2d 1002 (8th Dist. 2003).

Nonetheless, some trial courts have dismissed eviction actions for a landlord’s failure to attach a copy of the lease to the complaint. E.g., Davis v. Risten, No. 2010- CVG-951 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Mar. 1, 2010).

##### c. Civil Rule 11 – Signature of Pro Se Landlord

When a pro se landlord files an FED complaint, the landlord is required under Civil Rule 11 to sign the complaint. The failure of a pro se landlord to sign the complaint is grounds for the court to dismiss the complaint. E.g., Bennett v. Reynolds, No. CVG- 2100164 (Ohio Mun., Perry Cty., May 19, 2021).

##### d. Civil Rule 12(E) – More Definite Statement

Civil R ule 12(E) establishes the degree of specificity that is required for the allegations in an FED complaint.23 It permits a motion for more definite statement if the allegations are so vague and ambiguous that the tenant cannot reasonably be required to frame a responsive pleading.

Landlords often use form FED complaints that fail to comport with the specificity requirement of Civil Rule 12(E). These form complaints typically allege, with respect to the landlord’s right to possession, only that the tenant is unlawfully and forcibly detaining possession of the premises.

A tenant cannot reasonably be required to frame an answer to an FED complaint 23 Civil Rule 1(C) establishes the standard for determining whether Civil Rule 12(E) applies to eviction actions. See Section I.B, abov e. Like Civil Rule 10(D) (see Section I.E.2.b, above), Civil Rule 12(E) applies to eviction actions because it does not frustrate the summary nature of eviction proceedings.

that fails to specify the ground ( e.g., nonpayment of rent, violation of lease, violation of R.C. 5321.05, terminat ion of periodic tenancy) upon which the allegation of unlawful and forcible detention is based. Absent specification of that ground, the tenant cannot reasonably determine the elements of the landlord’s claim for possession or the potential defenses to the claim.

However, one municipal court has held that a landlord is not required to identify in the complaint the specific assisted housing program under which the landlord is
subsidized. Fed. Prop. Mgmt. Corp. v. Jackson , No. 79 -CVG-8820 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Nov. 1, 1979). The same municipal court also has held that, where an eviction action is for criminal activity, the landlord is not required to identify in the complaint the specific criminal statute or criminal offense on which the criminal activity claim is
based. DMHA v. Walker, No. 07-CVG-8591 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Nov. 30, 2007).

##### e. Civil Rule 15(B) – Amendment to Allege New Ground

for Eviction At trial, a landlord may be permitted to amend the FED complaint under Civil Rule 15(B) so as to add a new ground. See, e.g., N.W. Ohio Food Marts, Inc. v. Mugg, No.

L-86-115, 1987 WL 7791 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 13, 1987).

But, if the tenant would be prejudiced by the amendment, Civil Rule 15(B) requires the court to deny the motion to amend or to grant a continuance to enable the tenant to prepare a defense for the new ground. Id., 1987 WL 7791, at *2 (“Furthermore, once appellee was given leave to amend his complaint pursuant to Civ. R. 15(B), the case was continued, giving appellant ample time to prepare a rebuttal argument”).

##### f. Civil Rule 18(A) – Joinder of Claims

Under Civil Rule 18(A), a landlord may allege in a complaint both an eviction claim and additional, non -eviction claims that the landlord has against the tenant.

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jackson, 67 Ohio St.2d 129, 132 n.6, 423 N.E.2d 177 (1981); Mathews v. Cooper , No. 109974, 2021 WL 3560200 , ¶ 76 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 12, 2021); Bard Props. LLC v. Dalton , No. 24521, 2009 WL 2033078, ¶¶ 12,13 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 15, 2009); Marvin v. Stemen, 68 Ohio App.2d 26, 29, 426 N.E.2d 205 (6th Dist. 1980). The additional claims often are monetary claims for nonpayment of rent and/or damage to the premises. Jackson, 67 Ohio St.2d at 132;

Mathews v. Cooper, No. 109974, 2021 WL 3560200, ¶ 76 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug.

12, 2021); Maduka v. Parries, 14 Ohio App.3d 191, 470 N.E.2d 464 (8th Dist. 1984).

41 This joinder of claims is permissive, not mandatory. Mathews v. Cooper , No.

109974, 2021 WL 3560200, ¶ 76 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 12, 20 21); Marvin v.

Stemen, 68 Ohio App.2d at 29; Stewart v. McWilliams , No. 34694 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 20, 1975). Consequently, a landlord may file a complaint alleging an FED claim only and secure an FED judgment, and thereafter file a complaint alleg ing a monetary claim against the tenant for nonpayment of rent and/or damage to the
premises. Mathews v. Cooper, No. 109974, 2021 WL 3560200, ¶ 76 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 12, 2021); Stewart v. McWilliams , No. 34694, 1975 WL 183103 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 20, 1975). See also Dennis v. Morgan, 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 419, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000).

For eviction actions against a deceased resident of a manufactured home park, the legislature purports to prohibit joinder of claims for past due rent and other d amage claims under the rental agreement. R.C. 1923.081; Sub. H.B. No. 56, 127th General Assembly, Legislative Service Commission, Bill Analysis at 7 (“The act provides that a park operator may not seek a judgment for past due rent and other damages under a rental agreement against a deceased manufactured home park resident in an FE and D action but must proceed in a separate civil action for damages (R.C. 1923.081)”).

Notwithstanding this legislative intent, the unambiguous language of R.C.

1923.081 addresses consolidation of trials, and not joinder of claims.24 R.C. 1923.081 presumes that the claims have been consolidated. It permits a consolidation of the trial on these claims, unless good cause is shown. For this purpose, it specifies that good cause includes a defendant’s request to file an answer, to file a counterclaim, or to conduct discovery.

Construing R.C. 1923.081 as a joinder of claims provision renders its good cause exception illogical. A plaintiff decides whether to join claims when prepar ing the
complaint. At that stage, the plaintiff does not know whether the defendant will file an answer or counterclaim or conduct discovery and thus whether the relevant good cause exception exists.

24 When an eviction claim and a monetary claim are joined, the court may (and often does) bifurcate the trials on the respective claims under Civil Rule 42. See Section I.M. 1, below.

### F. Content of the Summons


#### 1. R.C. 1923.06

R.C. 1923.06(B) req uires the summons to contain, in a conspicuous manner, certain language, 25 if a landlord is seeking to recover residential premises in the eviction action.

Court decisions provide some guidance for determining whether a summons contains the R.C. 1923.06(B) language in a conspicuous manner. One court found the statutory language in a summons to be sufficiently conspicuous where it was “placed in the bottom left -hand corner of the page, was in small bold faced print, and was framed with red lines forming a r ectangle around the language.” Shewman v. Coons,
No. 89-CA-46, 1990 WL 34814, at *6 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 29, 1990). Because “the language was in bold print and was outlined in red,” the court found the conspicuous manner requirement to be met “des pite the fact that the [statutory] language was in smaller print than the rest of the print on the summons.” Id.

Several other court decisions have determined the analogous issue of whether an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate contains, in a conspicuous manne r, certain statutory language that is required for that notice. See Section I.C.2.c, above.

R.C. 1923.06(A) states that the summons “shall be ... in the form specified ... in this section.”26 But R.C. 1923.06 does not specify any form, other than the foregoing advisory language required by R.C. 1923.06(B).

#### 2. Civil Rule 4(B)

Civil Rule 4(B) is applicable to eviction actions because its application does not 25 The language required by R.C. 1923.06(B) is:

A complaint to evict you has been filed with this court. No person shall be evicted unless his right to possession has ended and no person shall be evicted in retaliation for the exercise of his lawful rights. If you are depositing rent with the clerk of this court you shall continue to deposit such rent until the time of the court hearing. The failure to continue to deposit such rent may result in your eviction. You may request a trial by jury. You have the right to seek legal
assistance. If you cannot afford a lawyer, you may contact your local legal aid or legal service
office. If none is available, you may contact your local bar association.

26 The revisions of R.C. 1923.06(A), effective March 30, 1999,and September 29, 1999, deleted the provision stating that the summons in an FED action “shall be ... in the form specified ... in the Rules of Civil Procedure.” 43 interfere with the summary nature of eviction proceedings. 27 Moreover, its requirements are not inconsistent with R.C. 1923.06.

In an eviction action, since the time for the tenant “to appear and defend” may be the time of trial (see Section I.H, below), Civil Rule 4(B) requires the summons to contain a statement of the date and time of trial. See Costantino v. Lyons, No. 3774, 1987 WL 31096, at *4 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 18, 1987).

### G. Service of the Summons and Complaint


#### 1. Introduction

R.C. 1923.06 establishes the time period by which service of process in an eviction action must be completed. This requirement is discussed in Section I.G.2, below.

R.C. 1923.06 purports to establish service of process rules for complaints that allege either an eviction claim only or both eviction and non-eviction claims.28 Some of these R.C. 1923.06 rules conflict with Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6. These rules and conflicts are discussed in Section I.G.3, below.

R.C. 1923.051 establishes service of process rules for drug -related R.C.

1923.02(A)(6) eviction actions. These rules are discussed in Section I.G.4, below.

#### 2. Time of Service

In an eviction action, the summons and complaint must be served at least seven days before the trial date for the eviction claim. R.C. 1923.06(A).29 Under R.C. 1.14, this seven-day time period is computed by excluding the first day and including the last day, and excluding any Sunday or holiday that would otherwise be the last day.

To ensure that there is adequate time to effect timely service of process, some courts have a policy of scheduling the trial date for the eviction claim on a specified number of days (often between 14 and 21 days) from the date on which the complaint is filed.

27 Civil Rule 1(C) establishes this standard for determining whether Civil Rule 4(B) applies to eviction actions. See Section I.B, above.

28 These R.C. 1923.06 service of process rules became effective March 30, 1999, and September 29, 1999. They supplanted the R.C. 1923.06 provision that had required service to be made “as in the Rules of Civil Procedure.” See note 6, above.

29 See note 6, above.

#### 3. Method of Service


##### a. R.C. 1923.06 Rules

The R.C. 1923.06 service of process rules purport to apply to any complaint that alleges an eviction claim. See R.C. 1923.06(A). This includes any complaint that alleges both an eviction claim and one or more non -eviction claims. See R.C.

1923.06(H)(2). The non-eviction claim is typically a monetary claim for nonpayment of rent and/or damage to the premises. See Section I.E.2.f, above.

The R.C. 1923.06 service of process rules establish a two-fold process. The rules require ordinary mail service and, at the landlord’s option, either bailiff service or certified mail, return receipt requested service.

Under the bailiff service, the bailiff makes one trip to the premises– (1) the bailiff tenders the summons and complaint to the tenant, or (2) if the bailiff cannot find the tenant, the bailiff leaves the summons and complaint with “a person of suitable age and discretion found at the premises,” or (3) if the bailiff cannot find the tenant or a person of suitable age and discretion at the premises, the bailiff posts the summons and complaint “in a conspicuous place on the subject premises.”30 R.C. 1923.06 service is deemed complete on the date that any of the following occurs: (1) bailiff service is made on the tenant, (2) bailiff service is made on another person at the premises, (3) bailiff service is made by posting, after ordinary mail service has been made, (4) ordinary mail service is made, after bailiff service has been made by posting, (5) certified mail service is mailed, if, as of the trial date for the eviction claim, the certified mail has not been returned for any reason othe r than refused or unclaimed, or (6) certified mail service is mailed, if, as of the trial date for the eviction claim, the certified mail has not been endorsed but the ordinary mail service has been mailed and has not been returned. R.C. 1923.06(G).

“Regarding [R.C. 1923.06(C) ] service by regular mail, service is ‘presumed complete when a certificate of mailing is entered in the record, provided that the ordinary mail envelope is not returned for failure of delivery.’” Timber Top Apts. v.

Klinkiewicz, No. 28860, 2018 WL 1957849, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 25, 2018) 30 Generally, a bailiff makes this “bailiff service” if the eviction action is commenced in a municipal court, and a sheriff makes this “bailiff service” if the eviction action is commenced in a court of common pleas or a county court. See R.C. 1923.06(D)(1)(a),(b). However, in any of these courts, the court may designate certain other persons to make this “bailiff service.”See R.C. 1923.06(D)(1)(c).

45 (quoting Don Ash Props. v. Dunno , No. 03AP-375, 2003 WL 22480323, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 4, 2003)).

When the R.C. 1923.06(C) ordinary mail envelope is returned to the clerk of court due to failure o f delivery, the question arises whether this ordinary mail service is subject to the Civil Rule 4.6(D) provisions on perfecting ordinary mail service . At least three court decisions have addressed this issue: Don Ash Props. v. Dunno , No.

03AP-375, 2003 WL 22480323 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 4, 2003), Showe Mgmt.
Corp. v. Cunningham, 191 Ohio App.3d 123, 944 N.E.2d 1234 (10th Dist. 2011); and G.K.G. Builders, Inc. v. Burgess , 13 N.E.2d 745, 2014 -Ohio-2431 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 6, 2014).

In Dunno, the monetary claim, but not the eviction claim, was before the court of
appeals. The court of appeals assumed arguendo, without deciding, that the R.C.

1923.06 service of process rules applied to the monetary claim. It held that the R.C.

1923.06(C) ordina ry mail service is subject to the rules in Civil Rule 4.6(D) on perfecting ordinary mail service. Thereupon, it held that, because the R.C. 1923.06(C) ordinary mail service was returned to the clerk of court due to failure of delivery, it had not been perfected.

In Cunningham, both the eviction claim and the monetary claim were before the court of appeals. The court of appeals held that, with respect to the eviction claim, the R.C. 1923.06(C) ordinary mail service is not subject to the rules in Civil Ru le 4.6(D) on perfecting ordinary mail service. Thereupon, it held that R.C. 1923.06(C) ordinary mail service is perfected for the eviction claim when the summons and complaint are placed in the mail, notwithstanding any return of this mail service to the clerk of court due to failure of delivery (as in that case). 31 It distinguished Dunno as a ruling on a monetary claim, with dicta on the eviction claim, and held that the Dunno ruling remains good law for the monetary claim.32 In Burgess, the complaint alleged only an eviction claim. The court of appeals 31 This holding raises a due process issue. See War Eagle Vill. Apts. v. Plummer, 775 N.W.2d 714 (Iowa 2009) (due process violation where FED service of process by mail deemed received by tenant when mailed). Cf. Jones v. Flowers, 547 U.S. 220 (2006) (when certified mail letter returned unclaimed, due process requires additional reasonable steps to attempt to notify owner before selling owner’s home in tax sale).

32 Consequently, the court of appeals in Cunningham, like in Dunno, did not decide whether R.C. 1923.06 service of process rules apply to the monetary claim.

held that, under Civil Rule 1(C) , service of process for an eviction claim is exempt from Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6. Relying on Cunningham, it then held that ordinary mail service was “made,” for purposes of R.C. 1923.06(G)(2), “when the clerk placed a copy of the summons and complaint in the mail and the fact of the mailing is placed on the record.” 13 N.E.2d 745, ¶ 15. Like the court in Cunningham, it held that the return of the ordinary mail service (which occurred in this case) was immaterial.

##### b. Conflict Between R.C. 1923.06 Rules and Civil Rules

4.1 and 4.6 – Eviction Claim The R.C. 1923.06 provisions that permit service of process by a combination of ordinary mail and posting conflic t with Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6. Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 permit service of process by ordinary mail, but only after service of process by certified or express mail has been attempted. See Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Thompson, No. 14428, 1990 WL 106217 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 25, 1990). Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 do not permit service of process by posting in any circumstances.

See Ohio R. Civ. P. 4.1, Staff Notes (Civil Rule 4.1 “eliminate[d] the possibility of doorknob service”).

Also, the R.C. 1923.06 provisions that permit service of process by serving “a person of suitable age and discretion found at the premises” conflict with Civil Rule 4.1(C). Civil Rule 4.1(C) permits such residence service only if the person is “then residing therein.” One issue that arises from these conflicts is whether Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply to an eviction action, notwithstanding R.C. 1923.06. If they apply to an eviction action, the R.C. 1923.06 service of process provisions that are in conflict with these Civil Rules are invalid and of no force and effect by virtue of Article IV, Section 5(B) of the Ohio Constitution. See Hiatt v. S. Health Facilities, Inc. , 68 Ohio St.3d 236, 626 N.E.2d 71 (1994). “The Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure ... must control over subsequently enacted statutes purporting to govern procedural matters.” Id. at 237.

Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply to an eviction action unless their appli cation would interfere with the summary nature of eviction proceedings. See Section I.B, above.

47 After an initial split in the early municipal court decisions, 33 the appellate courts have determined that Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 do not apply to eviction act ions. They have done so with little (if any) analysis in their decisions on whether Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 interfere with the summary nature of eviction proceedings.34 The appellate court decisions holding that Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 do not apply to an eviction action include: Vargyas v. Brasher , No. L -14-1193, 2015 WL 502854 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 6, 2015); G.K.G. Builders, Inc. v. Burgess, 13 N.E.2d 745, 2014-Ohio-2431 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 6, 2014); Machshonba v. Cleveland
Metro. Hous. Aut h., No. 96811, 2011 WL 6920728 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 29, 2011); Oak Park Mgmt. Corp. v. Via, No. 07-CA-0022, 2008 WL 2168505 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 27, 2008); Dobbins v. Kalson, No. 07AP-831, 2008 WL 307724 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 5, 2008); Amherst Vill. Mgmt. v. Vestal, No. WD-99-075, 2000 WL 1595719 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 27, 2000).

If the Supreme Court (or other appellate districts) in the future hold that Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply to an eviction action notwithstanding R.C. 1923.06, the case law on service of process in eviction actions includes the following examples of service that fail to comply with the Civil Rules.35 33 Compare McKown v. Johnson, No. 99-CVG-7561 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Nov. 1, 1999) (Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply), Boyle v. Hernandez, No. CVG-99-11252 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous.

Div., Sept. 3, 1999), and Talley v. Warner, 99 Ohio Misc.2d 42, 715 N.E. 2d 635 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., 1999) (except provision on time for ordinary mail service) with Harris
v. Hampton, No. 2005-CVG-43533 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Mar. 24, 2006) (Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 do not apply), Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v. Brent, No. 01-CVG-1693 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, July 24, 2001), Columbus Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Curry, No. M1999-CVG-035067 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 21, 2000), Chubb v. Bueno-Crespo, No. 99-CVG-01635 (Ohio Mun., Marion, Dec. 13, 1999), and Anderson v. Champer, No. 99-CVG-00424 (Ohio Mun., Marion, May 13, 1999).

34 Absent personal service or residence service, due process requires that service of process in an eviction action include mail service.See Greene v. Lindsey, 456 U.S. 444 (1982). Therefore, the issue here is whether the incremental delay, resulting from the use of certified mail instead of ordinary mail, frustrates the summary nature of an eviction action. The length of time that a post office holds undeliverable certified mail is “15 days ... unl ess the sender specifies fewer days on the piece.” U.S. Postal Service, Domestic Mail Manual Chapter D042, Conditions of Delivery, § 1.7 (incorporated by reference in 39 C.F.R. § 111.4).

35 The case law listed in the text here was decided under the former versions of R.C. 1923.06 that provided that service of process in an eviction action shall be “as in the Rules of Civil Procedure” and “as in other cases.” See note5, above.
(1) Regular mail service is invalid when regular mail service and certified mail service of the summons and compla int are served simultaneously.

Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Thompson , No. 14428, 1990 WL 106217 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 25, 1990).
(2) Regular mail service is invalid when certified mail service is returned unclaimed and, after the court has entered judgme nt against the tenant and the writ of restitution has been executed, the regular mail service is returned “moved left no address.”Castle Apts., Inc. v. Allgood, 43 Ohio App.3d 163, 539 N.E.2d 1154 (9th Dist. 1988).
(3) Residence service is invalid when the s ummons and complaint are not left at the residence with a person of suitable age and discretion then residing therein. Tucker v. Grigsby, No. CA-3154, 1985 WL 4492 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 13, 1985); Real Estate Servs., Inc. v. Leigh , No.

77AP-130, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 8899 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 2, 1977); Burton v. Murry , 8 Ohio Op.3d 179 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1973); Cotterman v. Fahrig, 55 Ohio App.2d 15, 378 N.E.2d 742 (2nd
Dist. 1972); Szabo v. Humphrey, 66 Ohio Misc.2d 55, 642 N.E.2d 1173 (Ohio Mun., Wadsworth, 1993); Tompkins v. Goins , No. 93 -CVG- 01097 (Ohio Mun., Zanesville, Dec. 13, 1993).
(4) Personal service is invalid when the plaintiff (landlord) is the person who served the summons and complaint. Lyon v. Wymer, No. 81-CVG- 10 (Ohio Mun., Findlay, Feb. 9, 1981).

##### c. Conflict Between R.C. 1923.06 Rules and Civil Rules

4.1 and 4.6 – Non-Eviction Claim The conflicts between R.C. 1923.06 and Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 (see Section I.G.3.b, above) create an additional issue for complaints that containan FED claim and a monetary claim. The issue is whether Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply to the monetary claim, irrespective of whether they apply to the FED claim. If Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply to these monetary claims, the R.C. 1923.06 service of proc ess rules are invalid insofar as they are in conflict with these Civil Rules and apply to these monetary
claims. See Hiatt v. S. Health Facilities, Inc., 68 Ohio St.3d 236, 626 N.E.2d 71 (1994).

Civil Rule 1(C) is the sole authority for exempting a claim from the service of process requirements of Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6. See Ohio R. Civ. P. 1(A) and 1(C).

The Civil Rule 1(C) exemption applies to “forcible entry and detainer” claims.

For a four-fold reason, the Civil Rule 1(C) term “forcible entry anddetainer” does not encompass the monetary claim, when a landlord elects to join in a complaint an 49 FED claim and a monetary claim for unpaid rent and/or damage to the premises. First, the term “forcible entry and detainer” is an unambiguous and well -established term under Ohio law. See Section I.A.2, above. Second, its plain meaning under Ohio law is an FED claim under R.C. Chapter 1923, which is a claim for, and only for, obtaining restitution of possession of real property, based on a party’s present right to possession of the property. See Section I.A.2, above. Third, this plain meaning does not include the landlord’s monetary claim, which is a breach of contract claim for money damages (see Dennis v. Morgan , 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 419, 732 N.E.2d 391 (20 00)) and/or a statutory claim under R.C. 5321.05(C) for money damages ( see Sherwin v. Cabana Club Apts., 70 Ohio App.2d 11, 18, 433 N.E.2d 932 (8th Dist. 1980)). Fourth, when a term in a civil rule (or a statute) is unambiguous, courts are required to apply its plain meaning, without any further assistance of the rules of construction. State ex rel.

Dunbar v. Ham, 45 Ohio St.2d 112, 114, 341 N.E.2d 594 (1976) (civil rule); Cheap Escape Co., Inc. v. Haddox, L.L.C. , 120 Ohio St.3d 493, 900 N.E.2d 601, ¶ 9 ( 2008) (statute).

When a landlord elects to join in a complaint an FED claim and a monetary claim for unpaid rent and/or damage to the premises, the claims are joined under Civil Rule 18(A). See Section I.E.2.f, above.

When a plaintiff elects under Civil Rule 18(A) to join in a complaint two separate claims, each claim must be treated independently and ruled upon in accordance with the laws governing that claim. See Carter v. Bernard , No. 06 -MA-54, 2006 WL 3849855, ¶¶ 55,56 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Dec. 28, 2006) (fraud and breach of contract actions, each seeking money damages and rescission); Raskow v. Fortner, No. 18399, 1998 WL 178570, at *2-3 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 15, 1998) (action for damages for flooding and action for injunction to remove dam to prevent future flooding); Peed v.

Moore, No. 857, 1981 WL 5165, at *3 -4 (Ohio App., 12 th Dist., July 22, 1981) (multiple causes of action, seeking various forms of relief).See also 4 Moore’s Federal Practice, § 18.23 (Matthew Bender 3d ed. 2012) (thejudgment on each claim “must be given according to the respective rights and liabilities of the parties”).36 Accordingly, the court’s jurisdiction with respect to each claim joined under Civil Rule 18(A) must be determined independently. Sommer v. Mount Carmel Health, No.

94-APE07-1087, 1995 WL 141354, at *4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 28, 1995) (court 36 Ohio “Rule 18 is the same as Federal Rule 18.” Ohio R. Civ. P. 18, Staff Notes (1970).

had subject-matter jurisdiction in the action for an injunction, but not in the action for money damages). If a court lacks jurisdiction to hear one of t wo claims joined under Civil Rule 18(A), the court must dismiss the claim for which it lacks jurisdiction, notwithstanding the court’s jurisdiction to hear the other claim. Id.

Therefore, when a landlord elects under Civil Rule 18(A) to join in a complaint the FED claim and the monetary claim, the FED claim and the monetary claim remain independent claims. Ohio R. Civ. P. 18(A) (“may join, as independent or alternative claims”). See, e.g., Tolle v. Morris, No. 90AP-040034, 1990 WL 163807, at *1 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 18, 1990) (“A claim for back rent or damages may be joined with a complaint in forcible entry and detainer....Appellee’s second cause of action for back rent and damages is separate from the forcible entry and detainer action ....”). The FED claim is still a claim for “the right to possession and nothing else.” Seventh Urban,
Inc. v. Univ. Circle Prop. Dev., Inc. , 67 Ohio St.2d 19, 25 n.11, 423 N.E.2d 1070 (1981). The monetary claim is still a breach of contract claim for money damages and/or a statutory claim under R.C. 5321.05(C) for money damages.

In short, the monetary claim does not become an FED claim simply because it has been alleged in a complaint that also alleges an FED claim. The monetary claim, alone, is not a claim or proceeding within the purview of Civil Rule 1(C), and its joinder with the FED claim does not alter this conclusion. In these circumstances, the “forcible entry and detainer is a summary proceeding and [the] suit for damages is a regular civil proceeding...” Smith v. Wright, 65 Ohio App.2d 101, 103, 416 N.E.2d 655 (8th Dist.

1979).

Because the monetary claim is “a regular civil proceeding” to which Civil Rule 1(C) does not apply, perforce, Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 (and all other provisions of the Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure) apply to the monetary claim.

Nonetheless, the R.C. 1923.06 case law is split on whether Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply to the monetary claim.

Court decisions (and court rules) holding that Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 apply to these monetary claims include: Pisani v. Wright , No. 2005 -CVG-1233 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Apr. 7, 2008); Amherst Vill. Mgmt. v. Vestal , No. 99 -CVG-00936 (Ohio Mun., Bowling Green, Sept. 29, 1999) (magistrate’s decision) (“Service in the action by posting and regular mail ... would be imperfect for the second cause of action [monetary claim] as clearly violative of the Civil Rules”), objection overruled, (Ohio Mun., Bowling Green, Oct. 19, 1999), aff’d on other grounds, No. WD-99-075, 2000 WL 1595719 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 27, 2000); Mount Vernon Municipal Court, 51 Local Rules of Practice, Rule 304 (eff. May 6, 2013); Licking County Municipal Court, Notice (2012) (if eviction a nd monetary claims, “[p]osting of the summons does not meet the legal requirement for seeking monetary damages”); Memorandum from David C. Stockdale, Administrative/Presiding Judge Elect, Hamilton County Municipal Court, to Magistrates and Chief Deputy Clerk Deters (Sept. 27, 1999) (Joint Session consensus: R.C. 1923.06 provisions that allow “service of process of the second and third causes of action for back rent and other damages in forcible entry and detainer actions in a manner other than as prescribe d in Civil Rules 4 through 4.6 ...

are unconstitutional”).

Court decisions holding that Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6 do not apply to these monetary claims include: Vargyas v. Brasher , No. L -14-1193, 2015 WL 502854 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 6, 2015); 37 Harris v. Hampton , No. 2005 -CVG-43533 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Mar. 24, 2006).

In addition, two appellate court decisions assume arguendo, without deciding, that R.C. 1923.06 applies to the monetary claim, and then h old that due process prohibits service of process by ordinary mail and posting for the monetary claim if the landlord knew or reasonably should have known that the tenant did not reside in the property when the service was executed . Romp v. Jean -Pierre, No. L -15-1123, 2016 WL 4035605 (Ohio App., 6t h Dist., July 22, 2016); C & W Inv. Co. v. Midwest Vending Inc., No. 03AP-40, 2003 WL 22054062 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 4, 2003). See also Hous. Auth. of City of Atlanta v. Hudson , 250 Ga. 109, 296 S.E.2d 558 (1982) (complaint alleging eviction claim and monetary claim for unpaid rent; due process prohibits a default judgment on the monetary claim, where service of process is by ordinary mail and posting and the tenant does not answer the complaint or appear at a hearing on either claim).

37 The Vargyas court relies heavily on the decision in G.K.G. Builders, Inc. v. Burgess , 13 N.E.2d 745, 2014 -Ohio-2431 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 2014). This reliance on Burgess is misplaced because the complaint in Burgess alleged only an FED claim and the decision in Burgess addressed only the application of the Civil Rules to an FED claim. The reasoning in the Vargyas decision is also flawed by the court’s failure to acknowledge (let alone address) the distinction between the FED claim and the monetary claim, which results in the court holding only implicitly that the monetary claim is an FED claim for Civil Rule 1(C) purposes.

##### d. R.C. 1923.06 Additional Rules – Deceased Resident of

Manufactured Home Park When the adverse party in an eviction action is a deceased resident of a manufactured home park, the plaintiff must provide the clerk of court with certain information from the probate court (where applicable) or certain information on the deceased resident’s spouse and any other members of the deceased resident’s immediate family, if known to the plaintiff. R.C. 1923.06(F),(I).

If the plaintiff provides the information from the probate cour t, the clerk of court is required to serve the executor or administrator with a copy of the summons and complaint by certified mail, return receipt requested. Id.

If the plaintiff provides the information on the spouse or other immediate family members, the clerk of court is required to serve them with a copy of the summons and complaint by certified mail, return receipt requested, by ordinary mail, and by publication in a newspaper of general circulation. Id.

#### 4. Time and Method of Service, Drug-Related R.C.

1923.02(A)(6) Eviction In an eviction action filed under R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a) (see Section I.C.5, above), the complaint and summons must be served in accordance with Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6. R.C. 1923.051(A)(1).38 Furthermore, the service and returnof the summons shall be made, if possible, within three working days after the filing of the complaint. R.C.

1923.051(A)(1).

In these eviction actions, the court must schedule a trial date for the eviction claim that is not later than the thirtieth calen dar day after the date on which the tenant is properly served with the summons. R.C. 1923.051(A)(2).

### H. Answer

In an eviction action, any defense may be asserted at trial, irrespective of whether a written answer has been filed with the court. R.C. 1923.061 (A); Marysville Estates
v. Bruce, No. 14-13-12, 2013 WL 5347438, ¶ 16 n.3 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 23, 2013); Schmidt v. Hummell, 81 Ohio App. 167, 73 N.E.2d 806 (10th Dist. 1947). See 38 When the legislature amended R.C. 1923.06 in 1999 (see note 5, above), it did not make any corresponding amendment to R.C. 1923.051(A)(1).

53 also Shaffer v. Mease, 66 Ohio App.3d 400, 408, 584 N.E.2d 77 (4th Dist. 1991). A tenant is not required to file a written answer in order to assert a defense at trial.Id. If a tenant files a written answer, the tenant may assert additional defenses at trial.

Marysville Estates v. Bruce, 2013 WL 5347438, ¶ 16 n.3;Schmidt v. Hummell, 81 Ohio
App. 167, 73 N.E.2d 806 (10th Dist. 1947).

When a complaint includes an eviction claim and one or more non-eviction claims, the tenant has at least twenty -eight days to file an answer to the non -eviction claims.

Wodzisz v. Bayes, No. 96-APE07-891, 1997 WL 142715, at *4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Mar. 25, 1997); Smith v. Wright, 65 Ohio App.2d 101, 103, 416 N.E.2d 655 (8th Dist.

1979). For a discussion on consolidation of the trials on these claims, see Section I.M.1, below.

### I. Counterclaims


#### 1. Permissive Counterclaims

In an eviction action, the tenant may file a permissive counterclaim under Civil Rule 13(B). Jemo Assocs. v. Garman , 70 Ohio St.2d 267, 436 N.E.2d 1353 (1982).

See also Swaney v. Syndicate Mgmt., Inc. , No. 71422, 1997 WL 2 09223 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 24, 1997).

#### 2. Compulsory Counterclaims

If the eviction complaint alleges only a claim for possession, Civil Rule 13(A) (compulsory counterclaim rule) does not apply to the eviction action. Soliel Tans, L.L.C. v. Timber Bentley Coe, L.L.C., No. 108125, 2019 WL 6358476 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 27, 2019); Forney v. Climbing Higher Enters., Inc., 158 Ohio App.3d 338, 815 N.E.2d 722 (9th Dist. 2004); Carter v. Russo Realtors, No. 99AP-585, 2000 WL 249137 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 7, 2000); Haney v. Roberts , 130 Ohio App.3d 293, 720 N.E.2d 101 (4th Dist. 1998);Chef Italiano Corp. v. Crucible Dev. Corp., No.

CV-2003-12-7277, 2004 WL 5491217 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Oct. 28, 2004).

If the eviction complaint alleges both a claim for possession and one or more non- eviction claims, Civil Rule 13(A) applies to the non -eviction claims. Soliel Tans, L.L.C. v. Timber Bentley Coe, L.L.C., No. 108125, 2019 WL 6358476 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 27, 2019);Adams v. Romine, 130 N.E.3d 1050, 2019-Ohio-482 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., 2019); Forney v. Climbing Higher Enters., Inc., 158 Ohio App.3d 338, 815 N.E.2d 722 (9th Dist. 2004); Sherman v. Pearson, 110 Ohio App.3d 70, 673 N.E.2d 643 (1st Dist. 1996); Lable & Co. v. Flowers, 104 Ohio App.3d 227, 661 N.E.2d 782 (9th Dist. 1995); McCord v. Costy, No. CA87-05-036, 1987 WL 27190 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 7, 1987); Salyer v. Chapman, No. 1771, 1986 WL 11594 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Oct. 21, 198 6); Maduka v. Parries , 14 Ohio App.3d 191, 470 N.E.2d 464 (8th Dist. 1984); Chef Italiano Corp. v. Crucible Dev. Corp. , No. CV-2003-12-7277, 2004 WL 5491217 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Oct. 28, 2004).

In these cases, Civil Rule 13(A) may apply to the non -eviction claim even if the non-eviction claim is dismissed under Civil Rule 41(A)(1)(a), provided the dismissal occurs after the time period for filing an answer and counterclaims.Soliel Tans, L.L.C.
v. Timber Bentley Coe, L.L.C., No. 108125, 2019 WL 6358476 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Nov. 27, 2019).

Absent evidence that the landlord’s monetary claim reached a judgment, Civil Rule 13(A) does not bar a tenant’s subsequent action against the landlord for money
damages. Douglas v. Lewis, No. C-180201, 2019 WL 1474214, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Apr. 3, 2019).

In these eviction actions, the determination on whether a counterclaim is a compulsory counterclaim is based on the “logical relations test” set forth in Rettig
Enters. v. Koehler, 68 Ohio St.3d 274, 626 N.E.2d 99 (1994). Soliel Tans, L.L.C. v.

Timber Bentley Coe, L.L.C. , No. 108125, 2019 WL 6358476 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Nov. 27, 2019); McAlpine v. Patrick , No. 86453, 2006 WL 562191 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 9, 2006); Forney v. Climbing Higher Enters., Inc., 158 Ohio App.3d 338, 815 N.E.2d 722 (9th Dist. 2004); Sherman v. Pearson , 110 Ohio App.3d 70, 673 N.E.2d 643 (1st Dist. 1996).

When the claim (potential counterclaim) arises from the landlord -tenant relationship, it meets the Rettig logical relations test. Id.

Whether the counterclaim is a contract claim or a tort claim is irrelevant to this
determination. Sherman v. Pearson, 110 Ohio App.3d 70, 673 N.E.2d 643 (1st Dist.

1996). Likewise, whether the counterclaim is a claim for personal injury is irrelevant to this determination. Id.

The case law illustrates the range of claims that may be compulsory counterclaims in these eviction actions.

A compulsory counterclaim may be an R.C. 5321.02, 5321.04, 5321.15, invasion of privacy, trespass, or emotional distress claim. McCord v. Costy, No. CA87-05-036, 1987 WL 27190 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 7, 1987); Maduka v. Parries, 14 Ohio App.3d 191, 470 N.E.2d 464 (8th Dist. 1984). Cf. Salyer v. Chapman, No. 1771, 1986 55 WL 11594 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Oct. 21, 1986) (commercial tenant’s monetary claim for landlord’s failure to repair roof and heating/cooling system was compulsory counterclaim to landlord’s eviction and monetary claims for nonpayment of rent).

A compulsory counterclaim may be an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim. 39 Adams v. Romine , 130 N.E.3d 1050, 2019 -Ohio-482 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., 2019);

Sherman v. Pearson, 110 Ohio App.3d 70, 673 N.E.2d 643 (1st Dist. 1996); Derrera
v. Hamlin, No. 02-CV-4851, 2003 WL 25500203 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., July 11, 2003).

A compulsory counterclaim may be a race discrimination claim. Lable & Co. v.

Flowers, 104 Ohio App.3d 227, 661 N.E.2d 782 (9th Dist. 1995). Cf. Turner v.

Crawford Square Apts. III, L.P., 449 F.3d 542 (3d Cir. 2006) (Pennsylvania res judicata law barred the FHA claim in federal court).

But a tenant’s personal injury claim sustained in a storeroom that is not a common area for tenants is not a compulsory counterclaim. Kerr v. Lakewood Shore Towers, Inc., No. 93462, 2010 WL 323424 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 28, 2010). It is a common law negligence claim based on a breach of a common law duty that does not arise under the landlord-tenant relationship. Id.

#### 3. Compulsory Joinder – Counterclaims of Tenant’s

Non-Party Child If a tenant’s child is a member of the tenant’s household but not named as a party in the eviction action, the child’s claims against the landlord relative to the tenancy are not compulsory counterclaims. McCord v. Costy, No. CA87-05-036, 1987 WL 27190, at *3-4 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 7, 1987).

However, in these circumstances, if the tenant asserts a counterclaim against the landlord based on the personal injury of the tenant’s minor child (e.g., the elevated levels of lead in the blood of the tenant’s minor child), the claim of the tenant’s minor child agains t the landlord for these personal injuries falls under the Civil Rule 19.1(A)(3) compulsory joinder rule. Mason v. Douglas, No. 12-CA-000011, 2012 WL 5984906 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 26, 2012).

#### 4. Practice Issues

Any counterclaim in an eviction action m ust filed in writing. Northgate Apts. v.

39 For a discussion on R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claims, see Section IX.B, below.

Gonzalez, No. CA80-34, 1981 WL 6340 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 26, 1981); Clark
Props. v. Hawk, No. 76AP-490, 1976 Ohio App. LEXIS 8193 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Dec. 21, 1976). R.C. 1923.061(A), which permits a te nant to assert any eviction defense at trial without filing a written answer (see Section I.H, above), does not apply to counterclaims in an eviction action. Id.

If an eviction complaint alleges only a claim for possession, any counterclaim filed after the court has entered a judgment for the plaintiff, but before the court has ruled on timely objections, may be dismissed under Civil Rule 12(B)(6) on the ground that the counterclaims cannot “be filed at that point in the litigation.”Phillips v. Thompson,
No. 29803, 2021 WL 6068489, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 22, 2021).

A counterclaim in an eviction action may be determinative of whether the landlord or tenant is entitled to possession of the premises. E.g., R.C. 1923.061(B);40 Behrle v.

Beam, 6 Ohio St.3d 41, 451 N.E.2d 237 (1983) (counterclaim for specific performance of a purchase option in a lease). However, it also may be unrelated to the possession
issue. E.g., Smith v. Wright, 65 Ohio App.2d 101, 416 N.E.2d 655 (8th Dist. 1979).

See also Ohio R. Civ. P. 13(B).

### J. Jury Demand


#### 1. Party’s Right to a Trial by Jury

Any party in an eviction action has a right to a trial by jury, provided the jury demand is filed timely, the requisite advance deposits are paid timely or waived, and the tenant has not waived the right to a trial by jury.

The right to a trial by jury in an eviction action is established in R.C. 1923.10.

Fodor v. First Nat’l Supermarkets, Inc. , 63 Ohio St.3d 489, 589 N.E.2d 17 (1992);

Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Mountjoy, 154 N.E.3d 141, 2020-Ohio-2772 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., 2020); Martin v. Rogers, 42 Ohio App.3d 110, 536 N.E.2d 665 (8th Dist. 1987);

40 For a discussion on R.C. 1923.061(B), see Section II.O, below.

57 Pernell v. Southall Realty, 416 U.S. 363, 384-85 & n.34 (1974).41 The summons in an eviction action provides notice of this right with the statement that “[y]ou may request a trial by jury.” R.C. 1923.06(B).

The right to a trial by jury in an eviction action is also established in the Ohio
Constitution. Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Martin, No. 97-CA-9, 1998 WL 100416 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998). Article I, Section 5 of the Ohio Constitution “preserves the right of trial by jury in cases where under the principles of the common law it existed previously to the adoption of the Ohio Constitution.”Belding v. State ex
rel. Heifner, 121 Ohio St. 393, 396, 169 N.E. 301 (1929).Accord Hoops v. United Tel.
Co. of Ohio, 50 Ohio St.3d 97, 98-99, 553 N.E.2d 252 (1990); Hocking Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Martin, No. 97-CA-9, 1998 WL 100416, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998). The righ t to a trial by jury in actions to recover land, including summary eviction actions, existed under the principles of the common law in 1791 and thus prior to the adoption of the Ohio Constitution. See Pernell v. Southall Realty, 416 U.S. 363 (1974); Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Martin, No. 97-CA-9, 1998 WL 100416 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998);Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Lewis, No. 06-CVG-01137 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, June 27, 2006).

#### 2. Time Period for Filing a Jury Demand

The time period in which a jury demand may be filed, absent a local court rule, is unclear.

R.C. 1923.09 provides that, in an action under R.C. Chapter 1923, “if neither party demands a jury on the return day of the summons, a judge of the court shall try the case.”42 However, the return day of the summons, under R.C. 1923.09, is not a timeframe 41 See also Bennington v. Robinson , No. 1999 -CA-00212, 2000 WL 222156 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Feb. 7, 2000); Oakwood Mgmt. Co. v. Richards, No. 99AP-627, 1999 WL 1124692 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999); Long v. Abele, No. CT98-0018, 1998 WL 752040 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 9, 1998);Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Martin, No. 97-CA-9, 1998 WL 100416 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998);Nenadal v. Landerwood Co., No. 65428, 1994 WL 189375 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 12, 1994); Stacy v. Carr, No. CA-519, 1992 WL 37783 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Feb. 20, 1992); Lable & Co. v. Francis , No. 89 -J-39, 1991 WL 24982 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 22, 1991).

42 A revision of R.C. 1923.09 in 1987 made this provision applicable to any court that is hearing an eviction action. Before then, R.C. 1923.09 applied only to county courts. Ohio Rev. Code
Ann. § 1923.09 (Anderson 1983).

for the filing of a jury demand. Bonham v. Mills, 39 Ohio St. 534 (1883) (construing statutory provision that is now R.C. 1923.09);Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Lewis, No.

06-CVG-01137 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, June 27, 2006); Miller v. Schmidt, 5 Ohio Dec. 4, 1894 WL 687 (Ohio C.P., Hamilton Cty., 1894);Hill v. Hollister, 8 Ohio Dec. Reprint 116, 1880 WL 5818 (Ohio C.P., Huron Cty., Nov. 1880). See also Savage v. Wright,
No. L-75-123, 1975 Ohio App. LEXIS 8370 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 21, 1975) (R.C. 1923.09 timeframe too restrictive for jury demand filing; in interest of justice, filing is permissible up to three days before date set for trial). But see Showe Mgmt.
Corp. v. Mountjoy , 154 N.E.3d 141, 2020-Ohio-2772 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., 2020) (applied the R.C. 1923.09 time period for the filing of a jury demand).

Until 1987, R.C. 1901.24 included a time period for filing a jury demand in municipal court that could be used in an eviction action. See, e.g., Adm’r of Veterans Affairs v. Jackson , 41 Ohio App.3d 274, 276 -77, 535 N.E.2d 369 (9th Dist. 1987);

Savage v. Wright, No. L-75-123, 1975 Ohio App. LEXIS 8370 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Nov. 21, 1975). It provided that the time period for filing a jury demand in municipal court is the time period established in the local court rule or, absent a local court rule, not less than three days before the date set for trial. Ohio Rev. Code Ann. § 1901.24 (Anderson 1983). See Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Lewis, No. 06-CVG-01137 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, June 27, 2006).

But, when R.C. 1901.24 was revised in 1987, the three-day rule was supplanted by a general cross -reference to the Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure. See Lorain Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Lewis , No. 06-CVG-01137 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, June 27, 2006). R.C.

1901.24 now reads, in relevant part: “A jury trial in a municipal court shall be demanded in the manner prescribed in the Rules of Civil Procedure ....” Civil Rule 38(B) provides that the time period for filing a jury demand is “at any time after the commencement of the action and not later than fourteen days after the service of the last pleading directed to such issue.” 43 However, by filing a written answer in an eviction action, a tenant could extend this time period up to (and beyond) the trial date. Consequently, at least two court decisions have held that Civil Rule 38(B) does not apply to eviction actions. Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Mountjoy, 154 N.E.3d 141, 2020-Ohio-2772 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., 2020);Litoff v. D. H. Overmyer Co., Inc., 43 Civil Rule 38(B) does not preclude a more restrictive time period for filing a jury demand under a local court rule, provided the time period is reasonable. Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Martin, 97-CA-9, 1998 WL 100416, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998).

59
No. 32989 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 21, 1974).

Whatever statutory, Civil Rule, or local court rule requirement is applicable, it must establish a time period for filing a jury demand that is reasonable so as to comply with the Ohio Constitution. “[T]here may not constitutionally be any impairment or denial of the right of trial by jury [guaranteed by Article I, Section 5 of the Ohio Constitution].” Walters v. Griffit h, 38 Ohio St.2d 132, 133, 311 N.E.2d 14 (1974).

This means that “that right may be subject to moderate and reasonable regulation” only.
Id. Accord Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Martin, No. 97-CA-9, 1998 WL 100416, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1 998); Cuyahoga River Assocs. Ltd. P’ship v. MJK Corp., No. 68673, 1996 WL 17292, at *6 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 18, 1996); Kelly
v. Abu-Aun, No. 64869, 1994 WL 86215, at *1-2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 17, 1994).

While no court decision directly addresses this issue, the case law provides some guidance on what constitutes a reasonable time period for filing a jury demand, relative to the requirements of Article I, Section 5 of the Ohio Constitution. The decision most directly on point is Savage v. Wrig ht, No. L -75-123, 1975 Ohio App. LEXIS 8370 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 21, 1975). It struck down a local court rule that incorporated the R.C. 1923.09 time period for filing a jury demand (i.e., by the return day of the summons). It used an “interest of justice” standard, which is analogous to the “reasonable time” standard of Article I, Section 5 of the Ohio Constitution. Under this standard, it held that the time period for filing a jury demand in an eviction action is up to three days before the trial date. But cf. Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Martin,
No. 97-CA-9, 1998 WL 100416, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998) (upheld constitutionality of a local court rule that provided that, if a tenant in an eviction action fails to post a $175 deposit within three days of the service of the summons, the tenant waives the right to a trial by jury).

#### 3. Advance Deposit Requirements for Filing a Jury

Demand

##### a. Introduction

Two types of advance deposits may be required of a tenant who requests a trial by jury in an eviction action. The first is the advance deposit that a municipal court may require under R.C. 1901.26(A)(3). This deposit is discussed in Section I.J.3.b, below.

The second is an advance deposit of current rent that a court may require, togetherwith an order to deposit subsequent rent as it becomes due, under the court’s inherent power or R.C. 1923.08. This deposit is discussed in Section I.J.3.c, below.

##### b. R.C. 1901.26(A)(3) Deposit Requirement

R.C. 1901.26(A)(3) provides that the rules of a municipal court may require a party who demands a trial by jury in a civil action “to make an advance deposit as fixed by rule of court, unless, upon affidavit or other evidence, the court concludes that the party is unable to make the required deposit.” It also provides that the fees of a jury shall be taxed as costs.

Because Article I, Section 5 of the Ohio Constitution establishes the right to a trial by jury in an eviction action (see Section I.J.l, above), and because this constitutional right may be subje ct only to moderate and reasonable regulation ( see Walters v.

Griffith, 38 Ohio St.2d 132, 311 N.E.2d 14 (1974)), the amount of any R.C.

1901.26(A)(3) advance deposit requirement may not exceed the amount that constitutes moderate and reasonable regulationof this constitutional right. See Walters
v. Griffith, 38 Ohio St.2d 132, 311 N.E.2d 14 (1974); Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Martin, No. 97 -CA-9, 1998 WL 100416 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998);

Cuyahoga River Assocs. Ltd. P’ship v. MJK Corp., No. 68673, 1996 WL 17292 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 18, 1996); Kelly v. Abu-Aun, No. 64869, 1994 WL 86215 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 17, 1994).

The case law provides some guidance on the advance deposit amounts that meet this moderate and reasonable regulation standard. The Ohio Supreme Court has held that “requiring the cost of summoning jurors and the fees of jurors to be secured in advance by the party demanding the jury ... is ... a moderate and reasonable restriction upon the enjoyment of the right of a trial by jury and is not an impairment of that right.” Miller v. Eagle, 96 Ohio St. 106, 110, 117 N.E. 23 (1917). Accord Walters v. Griffith, 38 Ohio St.2d 132, 133, 311 N.E.2d 14 (1974).

Also, courts have held that advance deposit requirements in the amounts of ten dollars, one hundred fifty dollars, and one hundred seventy -five dollars, respectively, do not constitute a violation of Article I, Section 5 of the Ohio Constitution. Walters
v. Griffith, 38 Ohio St.2d 132, 311 N.E.2d 14 (1974) ($10 advance deposit);Dondorfer
v. Wolf, No. 13 -010, 1988 WL 105568 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 7, 1988) ($150 advance deposit); Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Martin , No. 97 -CA-9, 1998 WL 100416 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998) ($175 advance deposit).

In contrast, an appellate court judge, in a concurring opinion, found that a requirement “of an advance deposit of $300.00 to $400.00 is not a moderate and 61 reasonable restriction of the right of a jury trial and constitutes an unconstitutional abridgement and impairment of the right of trial by jury.” Lewis Furniture Co. v.

Dunkin, No. 1175, 1979 Ohio App. LEXIS 12447, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Di st., Mar.

26, 1979) (Stephenson, J., concurring).

When a party is unable to make the R.C. 1901.26(A)(3) advance deposit because of the party’s indigency, the court is required to waive this deposit requirement. R.C.

1901.26(A)(3) exempts from its advance deposit requirement those parties that the court concludes are unable to make the required deposit. If the party’s affidavit or other evidence demonstrates the party’s inability to make this advance deposit, under R.C. 1901.26(A)(3), the court is required to determine whether in fact the party is indigent and, if the determination is affirmative, the court is required to waive the R.C.

1901.26(A)(3) advance deposit. Cf. Lable & Co. v. Francis , No. 89-J-39, 1991 WL 24982 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 22, 199 1) (construing analogous provision of R.C.

1907.24(A) on waiver of advance deposits for jury demands in county courts).44 Furthermore, because the Ohio Constitution establishes the right to a trial by jury in an eviction action (see Section I.J.1, above), the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution requires a waiver of the R.C.

1901.26(A)(3) advance deposit requirement for indigent parties.See Lecates v. Justice of Peace Court No. 4, Etc., 637 F.2d 898 (3d Cir. 1980).

##### c. Rent Deposit Requirement

When the tenant’s filing of a jury demand requires the court to continue the trial date of the eviction action, as a condition precedent to the jury trial, the court may, in the exercise of its discretion, order the tenant to deposit with the court the rent for the current month and, thereafter, to deposit the rent as it becomes due. 45 E.g., State ex
rel. Dooley v. Riley, No. 14447, 1994 WL 53838 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 24, 1994);

44 A county court is required to waive for indigent parties any advance deposit require ment for filing a jury demand in an eviction action notwithstanding R.C. 1923.101, which provides that in such actions “a party demanding a jury shall first deposit money with the court sufficient to pay the jury fee.” Lable & Co. v. Francis, No. 89-J-39, 1991 WL 24982 (Ohio App., 7th Dist.,
Feb. 22, 1991).

45 These rent deposit orders are not final appealable orders. See McCarty v. Evans, No. 02-CA- 17, 2003 WL 1563771 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 19, 2003) (bond order not final appealable order, where tenant’s jury request on landlord’s money claim is conditioned on posting a bond in the amount of landlord’s rent claim).

Gem Mgmt. Co. v. Walker, No. 60139, 1991 WL 26635 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 28, 1991); Martin v. Rogers, 42 Ohio App.3d 110, 536 N.E.2d 665 (8th Dist. 1987). See also Cuyahoga River Assocs. Ltd. P’ship v. MJK Corp., No. 68673, 1996 WL 17292 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 18, 1996); State ex rel. GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Callahan, 65 Ohio App.3d 335, 583 N.E.2d 1339 (11th Dist. 1989).

The authority on which some courts rely for these rent deposit orders is R.C.

1923.08. Id. This reliance on R.C. 1923.08 seems misplaced. R.C. 1923.08 applies only when the tenant requests a continuance for a period of longer than eight days, and the court grants it. See Section I.K, below. Since a request for a trial by jury is not a request for a continuance, it seems to fall outside the purview of R.C. 1923.08.

These rent deposit orders are more appropriately issued under the court’s inherent
authority. “[A] court has inherent power to order the escrow of funds likely to be the subject of the final judgment.” Meyer v. Chieffo , Nos. 08AP -867,877, 2009 WL 1653561, ¶ 22 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 11, 2009). Therefore, a trial court has inherent authority to order a tenant to deposit with the court the tenant’s monthly rent while an eviction action is pending. Id., ¶ 23 (monthly land installment payments);PK
Mgmt. LLC v. Hayes, No. 2011-CVG-008188 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 7, 2011) (monthly rental payments). See also Cunningham v. Phoenix Mgmt., Inc. , 540 A.2d 1099 (D.C. App. 1988);Bell v. Tsintolas Realty Co., 430 F.2d 474 (D.C. Cir.

1970).

A requirement to pay rent as the rent becomes due during the pendency of the eviction action is a reasonable limitation on the tenant’s right to a trial by jury. See Kargman v. Dustin , 5 Mass. App. Ct. 101, 110, 359 N.E.2d 971 (1977); Bell v.

Tsintolas Realty Co., 430 F.2d 474, 482 (D.C. Cir. 1970). Therefore, it does not violate the tenant’s constitutional right to a trial by jury. See Section I.J.3.b, above.

#### 4. Waiver of the Right to a Trial by Jury

Ohio law prohibits any residential rental agreement provision that purpo rts to waive the right of the parties to a trial by jury in an eviction action.Herman and Kettle Props., Inc. v. Klenk, No. 18-CVG-02895 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton, Mar. 15, 2019). This prohibition arises from R.C. 5321.06, which prohibits any provision in a rental agreement that is inconsistent with or prohibited by R.C. Chapter 5321 or any other rule of law. Id. A jury waiver provision is inconsistent with R.C. 1923.10, which is a statutory rule of law that established the right to a trial by jury in eviction actions. Id.

A jury waiver provision is also inconsistent with the Ohio Constitution, which includes 63 a constitutional rule of law that, for the reasons discussed in Section I.J.1, above, establishes the right to a trial by jury in eviction actions.

The Eleventh Appellate District, in two decisions, has held that these jury waiver provisions are not unconscionable under R.C. 5321.14 and thereupon has enforced
them. Mentor Lagoons, Inc. v. Mayor, No. 10-180, 1985 WL 7807 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 1, 1985); Mentor Lagoons, Inc. v. Geldart , No. 6 -265, 1979 WL 208017 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Jan. 29, 1979). But, in these decisions, the courts did not rule on (or even consider) whether these jury waiver provisions are prohibited under R.C.

5321.06. Since these jury waiver provisions are prohibited under R.C. 5321.06, whether they are unconscionable under R.C. 5321.14 is immaterial.

However, a party may waive the right to a trial by jury on other g rounds. The waiver may result from, among other things: (1) a failure to file timely a jury demand,
(2) a failure to pay a required advance deposit or rent deposit, (3) a failure to file jury instructions in accordance with a local court rule,or (4) a failure to object at trial to the lack of a jury, when a jury has been demanded timely.

In an eviction action, like any other action, the failure of a party to demand timely a trial by jury constitutes a waiver of the party’s right to a trial by jury. Preston v.

Thornton, No. 29771, 2021 WL 1293724 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 7, 2021 );

Oakwood Mgmt. Co. v. Richards, No. 99AP-627, 1999 WL 1124692 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999); Long v. Abele, No. CT98 -0018, 1998 WL 752040 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 9, 1998); Cuyahoga River Assocs. Ltd. P’ship v. MJK Corp., No. 68673, 1996 WL 17292 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 18, 1996); Knoll Group Mgmt. Co. v.

Wolfe, Nos. 93-CA-553,554, 1994 WL 326347 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1994);

Adm’r of Veterans Affairsv. Jackson, 41 Ohio App.3d 274, 535 N.E.2d 369 (9th Dist.

1987).

The local court rules determine whether a non -indigent party’s failure to pay an R.C. 1901.26(A)(3) advance deposit constitutes a waiver of the right to a trial by jury.

If the local court rule states that nonpayment of the advance deposit constitutes a waiver of the right to a trial by jury, nonpayment of the deposit will constitute a waiver of that right.46 Walters v. Griffith, 38 Ohio St.2d 132, 311 N.E.2d 14 (1974);Arlington Natural Gas Co. v. Martens , 173 Ohio App.3d 450, 456, 878 N.E.2d 1088 (3rd D ist.

2007); Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Martin, No. 97-CA-9, 1998 WL 100416, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998). If the local court rule fails to state that nonpayment of the advance deposit constitutes a waiver of the right to a trial by jury, nonpayment of the deposit cannot constitute a waiver of that right. Warrick v.

Gaudynski, No. L-86-358, 1987 WL 18442, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 16, 1987).

When a court orders a tenant to make an advance deposit with the court in connection with t he tenant’s jury demand, the tenant’s failure to make the deposit constitutes a waiver of the right to a trial by jury.Burton Carol Mgmt., LLC v. Tessmer,
No. 2015-L-035, 2015 WL 6126186, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 19, 2015);Gem
Mgmt. Co. v. Walk er, No. 60139, 1991 WL 26635, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb.

28, 1991); Martin v. Rogers , 42 Ohio App.3d 110, 111, 536 N.E.2d 665 (8th Dist.

1987).

When a court rule requires any party who demands a trial by jury in an eviction action to file timely j ury instructions with the court, a party’s failure to comply with that requirement constitutes a waiver of the party’s right to a trial by jury. Cotofan v.

Cully, No. 58285, 1991 WL 76028 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 9, 1991).

When a party has demanded time ly a trial by jury but the court nonetheless conducts a bench trial to which no objection is made, the case law is split on whether the failure to object to the lack of a trial by jury constitutes a waiver of the right to a trial by jury. Compare Benningt on v. Robinson , No. 1999 -CA-00212, 2000 WL 222156 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Feb. 7, 2000) (waiver), Nenadal v. Landerwood Co. ,
No. 65428, 1994 WL 189375 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 12, 1994), and Henning v.

Steiner, No. 2725, 1992 WL 281326 (Ohio App., 9th Dis t., Oct. 7, 1992) with Carl Sectional Home, Inc. v. Key Corp. , 1 Ohio App.3d 101, 439 N.E.2d 915 (10th Dist.
1981) (no waiver).

46 Nonpayment of an R.C. 1901.26(A)(3) advance deposit can constitute a waiver of the right to a trial by jury even in the absence of notic e to the party of the local court rule. Burfitt v.

Stewart, Nos. 55530,55531, 1989 WL 113079, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 28, 1989);

Dondorfer v. Wolf, No. 13-010, 1988 WL 105568, at *1-2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 7, 1988).

The clerk of court d oes not have a duty to notify a party of the local court rule requiring an advance deposit for filing a jury demand. Dondorfer, 1988 WL 105568, at *1 -2. A party is required to know the local court rules. Burfitt, 1989 WL 113079, at *1.

65

#### 5. Withdrawal of a Jury Demand

When a party has filed a jury demand, the party may withdraw the jury demand only if the opposing party consents to the withdrawal. Ohio R. Civ. P. 38(D).

### K. Continuance and Bond

Courts have authority to grant continuances under their general supervisory power over their docket. “Independent of statute, as an incident to their authority to hear and determine cases, courts have power to grant continuances.” State ex rel. Buck v.

McCabe, 140 Ohio St. 535, 537, 45 N.E.2d 763 (1942). Accord Kidd v. Cincinnati Transit Co., 24 Ohio App.2d 101, 102, 265 N.E.2d 297 (1st Dist. 1970).

This authority to grant conti nuances of the trial date is generally applicable to an eviction action. See See v. Sabau , No. 62047, 1992 WL 30951, at *2 -4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 20, 1992); Swartz v. Schutte, No. 14717, 1991 WL 2022, at *2 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 9, 1991); Bar Hotel Co. v. Lloyd MacKeown Buick Co., 104 Ohio
App. 69, 74, 146 N.E.2d 879 (3rd Dist. 1957). The only eviction actions in which the court may not continue the trial date are drug -related eviction actions that have been filed under R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a). See R.C. 1923.051(C).

In an eviction action, the decision whether to grant a continuance of the trial date is committed to the discretion of the trial court. Krantz v. Pahnke, No. 2021-CA-0043, 2022 WL 39486, ¶ 37 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 5, 2022); Oakwood Mgmt. Co. v.

Richards, No. 99AP -627, 1999 WL 1124692, at *2 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999); Youngstown Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Barry , No. 94-CA-147, 1996 WL 734017, at *2 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Dec. 16 , 1996); Cuyahoga River Assocs. Ltd. P’ship v.

MJK Corp., No. 68673, 1996 WL 17292, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 18, 1996);

Swartz v. Schutte, No. 14717, 1991 WL 2022, at *2 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 9, 1991).

Likewise, the decision on the length of any such continuance is generally committed to the discretion of the trial court. Bar Hotel Co. v. Lloyd MacKeown Buick Co., 104 Ohio App. 69, 146 N.E.2d 879 (3rd Dist. 1957).

However, R.C. 1923.08 establishes certain constraints on the court’s authority to grant a continuance in excess of eight days on an eviction claim, when the landlord or the tenant has requested the continuance.

When a landlord requests a continuance in excess of eight days on an eviction claim, R.C. 1923.08 prohibits the court fromgranting the continuance unless the tenant consents to it.

When a tenant requests and is granted a continuance in excess of eight days on an eviction claim, R.C. 1923.08 requires the tenant to post a bond.

The amount of the R.C. 1923.08 bond is the amountof rent that may accrue during the period of the continuance. State ex rel. Dooley v. Riley , No. 14447, 1994 WL 53838 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 24, 1994); Gem Mgmt. Co. v. Walker , No. 60139, 1991 WL 26635 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 28, 1991); Brown v. Fay Apts., 62 Ohio Misc.2d 188, 594 N.E.2d 190 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1991). E.g., Fair Acres MHC LLC v. Arrington, No. 2020 -CVG-0003R (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Jan. 28,
2020) (for two-week continuance, bond set at the rent amount prorated for two weeks).

See also Seventh Urban, Inc. v. Univ. Circle Prop. Dev., Inc. , 67 Ohio St.2d 19, 30, 423 N.E.2d 1070 (1981).

R.C. 1923.08 does not require the tenant to deposit with the court alleged past due
