rent. Riley, 1994 WL 53838, at *1; Walker, 1991 WL 26635, at *1; Brown, 62 Ohio Misc.2d at 189.

This R.C. 1923.08 bond requirement is mandatory, not discretionary. Cuyahoga River Assocs. Ltd. P’ship v. MJK Corp., No. 68673, 1996 WL 17292, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 18, 1996); State ex rel. Dooley v. Riley, No. 14447, 1994 WL 53838, at *1 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 24, 1994); State ex rel. GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v.

Callahan, 65 Ohio App.3d 335, 338-39, 583 N.E.2d 1339 (11th Dist. 1989).

Because this bond requirement is mandatory, the court may not grant a tenant a continuance of indefinite duration on an eviction claim. Callahan, 65 Ohio App.3d at
339. “The exact duration is needed not only to determine whether the bond must be posted before the continuance is granted, but it is also needed to determinewhether the posted bond is sufficient to cover the possible loss the landlord could sustain.” Id.

The purpose of the bond is to insure that, “if the defendant [is] ... granted a continuance, the delay will not ... [be] monetarily prejudicial to the plaintiff.” Hilltop
Vill. v. West , No. L -88-120, 1989 WL 10277, at *5 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 10, 1989). Accord State ex rel. Dooley v. Riley, No. 14447, 1994 WL 53838, at *1 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 24, 1994); Brown v. Fay Apts., 62 Ohio Misc.2d 188, 1 89, 594 N.E.2d 190 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1991).

Accordingly, if the landlord prevails on the eviction claim, the rent deposited with the court under R.C. 1923.08 may be released to the landlord. West, 1989 WL 10277, at *5; Talbot v. Resler, 64 Ohio L aw Abs. 461, 462, 113 N.E.2d 21, 22 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1952).

When a tenant is required to post a bond under R.C. 1923.08 and fails to do so, the 67 tenant is not entitled to a continuance, irrespective of the tenant’s reason for requesting the continuance. Alex-Bell Oxford Ltd. P’ship v. Woods, No. 16038, 1998 WL 289028, at *4 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 5, 1998). However, such tenants are still entitled to a hearing on the eviction claim. Bester v. Owens, No. 17388, 1999 WL 179261, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 31, 1999).

When a continuance in excess of eight days is granted on an eviction claim, but it is not granted pursuant to a request by the tenant, R.C. 1923.08 does not require the tenant to post a bond.47 Ambrosia Coal & Constr. Co. v. C.B.G., Inc., No. 94-CA-199, 1996 WL 242915, at *3 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., May 9, 1996);Talbot v. Resler, 64 Ohio Law Abs. 461, 462, 113 N.E.2d 21 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1952).

For example, R.C. 1923.08 does not require the tenant to post a bond when the landlord has requested and the tenant has consented to the continuance. Talbot, 64 Ohio Law Abs. at 462. See also Bester v. Owens, No. 17388, 1999 WL 179261, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 31, 1999).

Likewise, R.C. 1923.08 does not require the tenant to post a bond when the court, on its own, continues the eviction hearing. Galilee Missionary Baptist Church v.

Bibby, No. 25746, 2011 WL 4059951, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 14, 2011);

Ambrosia Coal & Constr. Co., 1996 WL 242915, at *3.

Without any apparent support in the language of R.C. 1923.081, 48 a few court decisions in the 1970s seemed to construe R.C. 1923.081 as additional authority to grant a continuance of the trial date on an eviction claim, at least where the ground for the continuance was one of the R.C. 1923.081 examples of “good cause.”49 E.g., Weiss
v. Roberts, No. 38363, 1978 Ohio App. LEXIS 9956 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 16, 47 When the R.C. 1923.08 bond requirement is inapplicable, the court still has inherent authority to order a tenant to deposit with the court rent that accrues during the pendency of the
continuance. See Meyer v. Chieffo , Nos. 08AP -867,877, 2009 WL 1653561, ¶¶ 22,23 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 11, 2009);PK Mgmt. LLC v. Hayes, No. 2011-CVG-008188 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 7, 2011). See also Cunningham v. Phoenix Mgmt., Inc. , 540 A.2d 1099 (D.C. App. 1988); Bell v. Tsintolas Realty Co., 430 F.2d 474 (D.C. Cir. 1970).

48 R.C. 1923.081 provides that, when a complaint alleges a claim for possession and a claim for past due rent and other damages under the rental agreement, the court may consolidate the trials on these claims or continue the trial on the monetary claim for good cause shown. See Section I.M.1, below.

49 The R.C. 1923.081 definition of good cause includes three examples: a tenant’s request to file an answer, a tenant’s request to file a counterclaim, and a tenant’s request to conduct discovery.

1978); Clark Props. v. Hawk , No. 76AP -490, 1976 Ohio App. LEXIS 8193 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 21, 1976); Lobo v. Hoelscher , 44 Ohio Misc. 46, 336 N.E.2d 475 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, 1975).

These courts further held that the decision on whether to grant this R.C. 1923.081 continuance is committed to the discretion of the trial court. Weiss v. Roberts , No.

38363, 1978 Ohio App. LEXIS 9956 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 16, 1978).

## L. Jurisdictional Issues


#### 1. Certification to a Court of Common Pleas

Municipal courts, county courts, and courts of common pleas have concurrent original jurisdiction in eviction actions. Seventh Urban, Inc. v. Univ. Circle Prop.

Dev., Inc., 67 Ohio St.2d 19, 423 N.E.2d 1070 (1981). Nonetheless, eviction actions usually are filed in a municipal or county court.

In such cases, because municipal and county courts are courts of limited monetary jurisdiction,50 the filing of a counterclaim may requ ire the proceeding to be certified to the court of common pleas under Civil Rule 13(J). This certification is mandatory when a properly filed counterclaim requests monetary relief in excess of the court’s monetary jurisdiction. Widder & Widder v. Kutnick, 113 Ohio App.3d 616, 626, 681 N.E.2d 977 (8th Dist. 1996) (municipal court); O’Hara Realty v. Lloyd , 116 Ohio App.3d 439, 440, 688 N.E.2d 544 (7th Dist. 1996) (county court).

If a municipal or county court is without jurisdiction to hear a properly filed counterclaim in an eviction action because the counterclaim’s monetary claim exceeds the court’s monetary jurisdiction, the court is required to certify the entire case, including the claim for possession, to the court of common pleas. 51 Scott v. Scott, __ N.E.3d __, 2022-Ohio-1052 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., 2022);O’Hara Realty v. Lloyd, 116 Ohio App.3d 439, 688 N.E.2d 544 (7th Dist. 1996); Marino v. Grewell , No. 93AP - 020012, 1993 WL 364937 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 5, 1993); Pete’s Grill, Inc. v.

50 For a discussion on the monetary jurisdiction of municipal and county courts, see Section XX.B, below.

51 In an eviction action filed in a housing division of a municipal court, the amount of the monetary relief sought in a counterclaim does not affect the jurisdiction of the housing division to hear the eviction action and the counterclaim. See Section XX.E, below. A housing division currently exists in two municipal courts in Ohio, the Cleveland Municipal Court and the Toledo Municipal Court. R.C. 1901.011.

69 Entercorp, No. 61547, 1992 WL 369301 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 10, 1992);

Morrow v. Gates, No. CA91-11-021, 1992 WL 156116 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 29, 1992); Carter v. Bayes, No. 1889, 1990 WL 71862 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., May 1, 1990); Bohinc v. Stafford, No. 52335, 1987 WL 8138 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 19, 1987);52 State ex rel. Penn v. Swain, 21 Ohio App.3d 119, 486 N.E.2d 1187 (11th Dist.

1984); Richmond Homes, Inc. v. Brown , 3 Ohio App.3d 204, 444 N.E.2d 463 (10th
Dist. 1981). See also Crossings Dev. Ltd. P’ship v. H.O.T., Inc., 96 Ohio App.3d 475, 478, 645 N.E.2d 159 (9th Dist. 1994).

A few appellate court decisions, based on an erroneous conclusion of law, have imposed a qualification to this certification requirement. E.g., Lyons v. Link, No. 03- CA-000006, 2003 WL 21213656, (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 27, 2003), Isaiah’s Wings, LLC v. McCourt, No. 2005-CA-39, 2006 WL 1901015 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 7, 2006) (follows Lyons); New Asian Super Market v. Weng , 110 N.E.3d 131, 2018-Ohio-1248 (Ohio App ., 10th Dist., 2018) (follows Isaiah’s Wings).53 Their erroneous conclusion of law is their conclusion that Civil Rule 13(J) does not apply to FED actions. Since a transfer of an eviction claim under Civil Rule 13(J) simply substitutes judges, it does not interfere with the summary nature of eviction actionsand therefore applies to eviction claims (as held by the court decisions cited in the immediately pr eceding paragraph) . E.g., Batavia Woods LLC v. Wainwright , No.

2013-CVG-0218 (Ohio Mun., Clermont Cty., Aug. 7, 2013) (in certifying the FED claim along with the fair housing and defective condition counterclaims, court rejects landlord’s argument that certification of the FED claim would delay the FED hearing and thereby prejudice the landlord). See generally Section I.B., above.

The qualification that Lyons, Isaiah’sWings, and Weng impose, in lieu of Civil 52 The Eighth Appellate District decisions in Pete’s Grill and Bohinc effectively overrule the contrary rulings in J & E Mgmt., Inc. v. Wolf , No. 35563, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 7324 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 10, 1977), and Swenson’s Ice Cream v. Swenson’s, Inc., 1 Ohio B. 324 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., 1982).

53 In Lyons, the court held that the eviction for nonpayment of rent was not directly related to the R.C. 5321.04 violation counterclaim on the erroneous theory that R.C. 5321.07 rent depositing is the exclusive remedy for such violations. In Isaiah’s Wings, the court held that the requisite direct relationship existed where the eviction was for nonpayment of rent and the counterclaim alleged an agreement for reduced rent. In Weng, the court held that the requisite direct relationship existed where the comm ercial eviction was for nonpayment of rent and the counterclaim alleged agreements for waiver of certain rent in exchange for a 25% partnership interest and lack of certain repairs.

Rule 13(J), is their limitation of the certif ication requirement to cases where a direct relationship exists between the eviction claim and the counterclaim. These court decisions thus permit the municipal court (or county court) to retain jurisdiction of the eviction claim and to certify only the co unterclaim to the common pleas court , if the eviction claim is not directly related to the counterclaim.

If the municipal court (or county court) is without jurisdiction to hear the counterclaim in an eviction action for reasons other than the court’s limited monetary jurisdiction, the court is not permitted to certify the case to the court of common pleas.

Bowshier v. Bowshier , No. 2013 -CA-33, 2013 WL 5314565 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Sept. 20, 2013); City of Cleveland v. A.J. Rose Mfg. Co. , 89 Ohio App.3d 267, 624 N.E.2d 245 (8th Dist. 1993); Wells v. Cunningham , 56 Ohio Misc.2d 9, 564 N.E.2d 758 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, 1990). Instead, it must dismiss the counterclaim without prejudice and retain jurisdiction of the claim for possession.54 Id.

“Despite the mandatory wording of Civ.R. 13(J), Ohio courts have found that certification is not automatic.” Adams Robinson Enters. v. Envirologix Corp. , 111 Ohio App.3d 426, 432, 676 N.E.2d 560 (2nd Dist. 1996). Accord Colombo Enters.,
Inc. v. Fegan, 142 Ohio App.3d 551, 555, 756 N.E.2d 211 (8th Dist. 2001); Widder & Widder v. Kutnick, 113 Ohio App.3d 616, 626, 681 N.E.2d 977 (8th Dist. 1996).

“The court must first determine if the counterclaim satisfies the formalities of the civil rules and states a claim demonstrating that the party is entitled to relief.” Adams Robinson Enters. , 111 Ohio App.3d at 432. Accord Rickard v. Solley , 188 Ohio App.3d 205, 211, 935 N.E.2d 64 (7th Dist. 2010);Crisalli v. Mearini, No. 84245, 2004 WL 2578868, ¶ 2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., N ov. 10, 2004); Hersch v. Debreczeni , 33 Ohio App.2d 235, 238-39, 294 N.E.2d 918 (8th Dist. 1973).

“The counterclaim must, on its face, set forth a short and plain statement that the counterclaimant is entitled to relief.” Widder & Widder , 113 Ohio App.3d at 626.

Accord Hersch, 33 Ohio App.2d at 238 -39. “Merely making a demand for judgment in excess of the municipal court’s jurisdiction is insufficient.” Id.

If a motion to strike the counterclaim has been filed pursuant to Civil Rule 12(F), 54 Similarly, when a plaintiff files a supplemental complaint requesting monetary relief in excess of the municipal court’s monetary jurisdiction, the municipal court is not permitted to certify the case to the court of common pleas under Civil Rule 13(J). State ex rel. Nat’l Employee Benefit Servs., Inc. v. Court of Common P leas of Cuyahoga County , 49 Ohio St.3d 49, 550 N.E.2d 941 (1990). Instead, the municipal court must dismiss the action without
prejudice. Id.

71 the municipal court (or county court) may rule on the motion before it rules on the certification issue. Swenson’s Ice Cream v. Swenson’s, Inc., 1 Ohio B. 324 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., 1982).

If a motion to dismiss the counterclaim for failure to state a claim for la ck of standing is filed, the municipal court (or county court) may rule on the motion before it rules on the certification issue. Crisalli v. Mearini, No. 84245, 2004 WL 2578868 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 10, 2004).

However, if the counterclaim includes the requisite short and plain statement of the claim and otherwise satisfies the formalities of the Civil Rules, the municipal court (or county court) is required to certify the case to the court of common pleas without holding a hearing on the merits of t he counterclaim.55 Gravill v. Parkhurst, 27 Ohio App.3d 100, 103-04, 499 N.E.2d 913 (8th Dist. 1985); Hersch v. Debreczeni, 33 Ohio App.2d 235, 239, 294 N.E.2d 918 (8th Dist. 1973).

#### 2. Jurisdictional-Priority Rule – Two Pending Cases

When two cases involving the same parties and the same causes of action are pending in different courts and those courts have concurrent jurisdiction, the court first acquiring jurisdiction of the claim retains it to the exclusion of the other court. State ex rel. Dunlap v. Sarko , 135 Ohio St.3d 171, 985 N.E.2d 450 (2013); State ex rel.

Brady v. Pianka, 106 Ohio St.3d 147, 832 N.E.2d 1202 (2005); State ex rel. Sellers v.

Gerken, 72 Ohio St.3d 115, 647 N.E.2d 807 (1995); State ex rel. Judson v. Spahr, 33 Ohio St.3d 111, 515 N.E.2d 911 (1987); State ex rel. Phillips v. Polcar, 50 Ohio St.2d 279, 364 N.E.2d 33 (1977);Lewis v. Harding, 182 Ohio App.3d 588, 913 N.E.2d 1048 (8th Dist. 2009).

“[T]he jurisdictional-priority rule can apply even when the causes of a ction and relief requested are not exactly the same, as long as the actions present part of the same ‘whole issue.’” State ex rel. Dunlap v. Sarko , 135 Ohio St.3d 171, 985 N.E.2d 450, ¶ 10 (2013).

However, the jurisdictional -priority rule “does not apply when the court first securing in personam jurisdiction is unable to afford the plaintiff all the relief that he 55 However, if “the counterclaim [does] not coherently state sufficient facts to ascertain the basis for the claim or the monetary relief requested,” a municipal court (or county court) might not abuse its discretion by receiving “evidence relating to defendant’s counterclaim prior to making any determination concerning certification in the case.” Lewallen v. Mentor Lagoons, Inc., 85 Ohio App.3d 91, 96, 619 N.E.2d 98 (8th Dist. 1993).

or she is legally or equitably entitled because its powers are limited or defective.” Adams Robinson Enters. v. Envirologix Corp., 111 Ohio App.3d 426, 430, 676 N.E.2d 560 (2nd Dist. 1996). Accord Scott v. Dohse, 194 Ohio App.3d 364, 956 N.E.2d 363 (2nd Dist. 2011).

For purposes of the jurisdictional -priority rule, the determination of which court first acquired jurisdiction is based on the date of completion of proper service, not the date of filing of the complaint. State ex rel. Balson v. Harnishfeger, 55 Ohio St.2d 38, 377 N.E.2d 750 (1978); Adams Robinson Enters. v. Envirologix Corp. , 111 Ohio App.3d 426, 676 N.E.2d 560 (2nd Dist. 1996); State ex rel. Cook v. Court of Common Pleas of Marion County , 28 Ohio App.3d 82, 502 N.E.2d 245 (3rd Dist. 1986). But see Mark I. Wallach & Anthony J. LaCerva, Instant Replay of the Race to the Courthouse: Ohio’s Rule of Priority of Jurisdiction Confronts the Civil Rules, 2 Ohio Civil Practice Journal 61 (1991) (contending that Balson is incorrectly decided and that, under the Civil Rules, this determination should be based on the date of filing of the complaint).

The jurisdictional-priority rule applies to eviction actions. For example, when a defendant in a declaratory judgment action pending in a court of common pleas files an eviction counterclaim and thereafter files an eviction action in a municipal court, involving the same parties and subject matter, the court of common pleas acquires the right to adjudicate the eviction action to the exclusion of the municipal court. C & C
Invs. v. Mackie , No. 87AP -183, 1987 WL 31262 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 21, 1987).

Likewise, if the vendor of a land installment contract brings an action in a court of common pleas seeking forfeiture of the contract, during the pendency of that act ion, the vendor cannot maintain a subsequent eviction action against the vendee in a municipal court. Stratton v. Robey , 70 Ohio App.2d 4, 433 N.E.2d 938 (10th Dist.

1980).

Also, when a tenant commences an action in a court of common pleas seeking declaratory and injunctive relief with respect to the tenant’s entitlement to continued occupancy of the premises, during the pendency of that action, the landlord arguably cannot commence an eviction action in a municipal court. Compare Lacheta v. Hall,
No. 8-90-CVG-494 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Oct. 22, 1990) (municipal court without jurisdiction in this circumstance)with State ex rel. Charron-Kofta v. Corrigan,
No. 69434, 1995 WL 603157 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 12, 1995) (writ of prohibition denied bec ause whether municipal court has jurisdiction in this circumstance is 73 unclear).

However, an action to quiet title in a court of common pleas does not deprive a municipal court of jurisdiction to hear an eviction action involving the same parties and property because the two causes of action involve different subject matters. Haas
v. Gerski, 175 Ohio St. 327, 194 N.E.2d 765 (1963); Hicks v. Holland , No. 53363, 1987 WL 17217 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 17, 1987).See also State ex rel. Carpenter
v. Warren Mun. Court, 61 Ohio St.2d 208, 400 N.E.2d 391 (1980). Aneviction action only determines which of the parties has a present right to possession (see Section I.A.2, above), whereas an action to quiet title determines which of the parties has a present right to title. Haas, 175 Ohio St. at 330. Cf. Disher v. Bannick, No. 28903, 2021 WL 1499975 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 16, 2021) (a judgment in an eviction action cannot, under res judicata, bar an action to quiet title).

Similarly, a collateral action b ringing title to real property into question – a declaratory judgment action as to an alleged settlement agreement – is not the same cause of action as an eviction claim and, therefore, the jurisdictional-priority rule does not apply. State ex rel. Bradyv. Pianka, 106 Ohio St.3d 147, 832 N.E.2d 1202 (2005).

#### 3. Title Issues

In an eviction action, the right to present possession 56 may depend on present ownership of record title to the premises. See Haas v. Gerski, 175 Ohio St. 327, 330, 197 N.E.2d 765 (1963); Hallmark Mgmt. v. Tartt , No. 49014, 1985 WL 9036, at *4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 23, 1985) (“an occupant can properly deny any interest derived from the purported landowner and assert the occupant’s own ownership to defeat an eviction action”).

“In Ohio, a deed does not have to be recorded to pass title. Whether or not recorded, a deed in Ohio passes title upon its proper execution and delivery, so far as the grantor is able to convey it.” Wayne Bldg. & Loan Co. of Wooster v. Yarborough, 11 Ohio St.2d 195, 212, 228 N.E.2d 841 (1967). Accord In re Estate Ault , 80 Ohio App.3d 399, 402, 609 N.E.2d 568 (12th Dist. 1992).

Therefore, when a right to possession depends on when title has transferred to a new owner, the new owner has standing to bring the eviction action as of the date that the deed is executed and delivered, irrespective of whether the deed has been recorded.

56 The right to present possession is the ultimate issue in any eviction action. See Section I.A.2, above.

Delli-Gatti v. Kokolari, No. 23469, 2007 WL 3356900 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 14, 2007).

When the right to present possession depends on present ownership of record title and there is no dispute as to which party holds present record title, municipal and county courts have jurisdiction to hear the eviction action.Estate of Allen v. Allen, No.

91-T-4580, 1992 WL 188346, at *2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 12, 1992). See also Eckart v. Newman, No. WM-18-006, 2019 WL 3765412, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Aug. 9, 2019). In such cases, a municipal or county court “as an incident to determining the right to possession may determine in whom the present title rests ....”57 Haas, 175 Ohio St. at 130.

In such cases, present record title conclusively establishe s the right to present
possession. Haas v. Gerski, 175 Ohio St. 327, 330 -31, 197 N.E.2d 765 (1963); First Bank Nat’l Ass’n v. Willis, Nos. 56263,56763, 1989 WL 69409, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 22, 1989); Fredricks v. Stromberg , No. 42601, 1981 Ohi o App. LEXIS 10469, at *4-5 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 2, 1981). A party cannot raise invalidity of the present record title as a defense to the eviction action. Willis, 1989 WL 69409, at *2; Fredricks, 1981 Ohio App. LEXIS 10469, at *4-5.

In such cases, if the validity of the present record title is the subject of a pending action in a court of common pleas, the pendency of that action is not a permissible ground for granting a stay of the eviction action. State ex rel. Carpenter v. Warren
Mun. Court, 61 Ohio St.2d 208, 210, 400 N.E.2d 391 (1980). See also State ex rel.

Weiss v. Hoover, 84 Ohio St.3d 530, 705 N.E.2d 1227 (1999); Dvorak v. Habeeb, No.

60971, 1991 WL 106059 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 13, 1991). Cf. CS/RW Westlake Indoor Storage, L.L.C. v. Kesi, L.L.C. , No. 102535, 2015 WL 6819281, ¶¶ 6,7,17 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 5, 2015) (pending action on disputed purchase price of business); Clark v. Reynolds , No. 77749, 2000 WL 1714927 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Nov. 16, 2000) (pending action on life interest in the property).

However, when the right to present possession depends on present ownership of record title and there is a dispute as to which party holds present record title, municipal 57 In making this determination, a municipal court has jurisdiction to determine whether the deed on which a party relies to claim present ownership of record title and right to present possession is defective on its face, and thus did not convey valid legal title to the property. See Hallmark Mgmt. v. Tartt , No. 49014, 1985 WL 9036 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 23, 1985) (deed defective because it was acknowledged before the grantee as a notary).

75 and county courts are without jurisdiction to hear the e viction action. Fenner v.

Parkinson, 69 Ohio App.3d 210, 590 N.E.2d 339 (10th Dist. 1990) (both parties claimed title under separate deeds). See also Hardy v. Hardy , No. 89905, 2008 WL 1822394, at *1 n.1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 24, 2008) (in FED defe nse based on defendant’s color of title, “the defendant must show that there is a bona fide controversy as to title”). Municipal courts are without jurisdiction to hear such actions because R.C. 1901.18 does not grant municipal courts jurisdiction over qu iet title
actions. Id. Likewise, county courts are without jurisdiction to hear such actions because R.C. Chapter 1907 does not grant county courts jurisdiction over quiet title
actions. See R.C. 1907.05 (county court jurisdiction “in civil actions in which the title to real estate may be drawn in question” established for two types of actions, neither of which is an eviction action).

When the eviction claim is based on present ownership of record title and the defense does not dispute that ownership but rather asserts an equitable defense based on equitable title, municipal and county courts have jurisdiction to hear the action.

Behrle v. Beam, 6 Ohio St.3d 41, 451 N.E.2d 237 (1983) (municipal court); Bowshier
v. Bowshier, No. 2013 -CA-33, 2013 WL 531456 5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 20,
2013) (county court); Atwood v. Bare, No. 1854, 1988 WL 117999 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 28, 1988) (municipal court); Sears v. Fechuck, No. 1999AP-080048, 2000 WL 874727 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 6, 2000) (county court). See also Brainard v.

Cardwell, Nos. 75870,75896, 2000 WL 897793 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 6, 2000).
Cf. Williams v. Bjork , 112 Ohio Misc.2d 57, 753 N.E.2d 995 (Ohio County Ct., Ashtabula Cty., 2001) (county court has jurisdiction in an eviction ac tion to enter a judgment that declares a cancellation and forfeiture of a land installment contract).But see Ryan v. Kenley , No. 19534, 2003 WL 1949795 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 25,
2003) (held that the equitable title defense brought the matter of the plaintiff’s legal title into question and thus deprived the county court of jurisdiction to hear the FED action).

#### 4. Domestic Relations Court


##### a. Marital Residence

When an eviction action concerns a marital residence and is filed before the issuance of a final d ecree of divorce and division of property, municipal courts and county courts are without subject -matter jurisdiction to hear the eviction action.

Yergan v. Yergan , No. 2901 -M, 1999 WL 493942 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 14, 1999); Slansky v. Slansky , 33 O hio App.2d 127, 293 N.E.2d 302 (8th Dist. 1973);

Blevins v. Gosa, No. 2003-CVG-4465 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Sept. 22,
2003) (common law marriage); Humphrey v. Heine, No. M9201-CVG-004176 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Mar. 3, 1992) (common law marriage).

However, municipal courts and county courts have subject -matter jurisdiction to hear an eviction action concerning a marital residence when the eviction action is filed after the final decree of divorce and division of property.58 Baker v. Revels, No. 03AP- 410, 2004 WL 453273 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 11, 2004); Eisler v. Rudy , No.

1466, 1989 WL 70066 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 23, 1989);Talbott v. Talbott, Nos.

86AP-248,342,370, 1986 WL 13924 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 13, 1986).

When an issue in a pending divorce proceeding is whether the property is a marital residence, and a third party brings an eviction action against the wife (or husband) to secure possession of the property, the municipal court may stay the eviction action until there is a final order in the divorce proceeding. Amick v. Sickels, No. 2006-CVG- 01277 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., Feb. 12, 2007), aff’d on other grounds, 177 Ohio App.3d 337, 894 N.E.2d 733 (4th Dist., 2008). In Amick, the husband and wife lived for six years in a property owned by the husband’s mother, with the expectation that she would give the property to them. When they began a divorce proceeding, the husband’s mother sold the property to a third-party.

##### b. Civil Protection Order

In domestic violence c ases, a court of common pleas may file a Consent Agreement and Domestic Violence Civil Protection Order, which grants possession of real property to one party for a specified time period.

During that time period, if the other party files an eviction action against the party granted possession under the Civil Protection Order, the municipal court (or county
court) is without subject -matter jurisdiction to hear the eviction action. Mims v.

DuBose, No. 04-CVG-0779 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Sept. 8, 2004).

After that time period, if the other party files an eviction action against the party 58 A rental agreement does not exist, and R.C. Chapter 5321 does not apply, where an occupancy under a divorce decree provides that one party s hall retain, for a specified period of time, an exclusive right to possession of a house that both parties owned prior to the marriage
dissolution. Steiger v. Mack, No. 1-81-25, 1981 WL 6743 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Dec. 17, 1981) (ex-husband not liable for self-help eviction under R.C. 5321.15).

77 granted possession under the Civil Protection Order, the municipal court (or county
court) has subject -matter jurisdiction to hear the eviction action. E.g., Zipfel v.

Reimonenque, No. L -19-1083, 2019 WL 6817411 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 13, 2019).

#### 5. Probate Court

With respect to real property in a probate proceeding, R.C. 2101.24 does not provide the court of common pleas, probate division, with exclusive jurisdiction over an executor’s claim for possession against an occupant of the property. Estate of Wos
v. Wos, No. L-05-1408, 2006 WL 2383966 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 18, 2006).

The executor may bring an eviction action against the occupant under R.C. Chapter
1923. Id. If the occupant is not a tenant, the executor may bring the eviction action under R.C. 192 3.02(A)(5), which permits eviction actions against occupiers of land who are without color of title. Id.

### M. FED Trial Issues


#### 1. Consolidated or Separate Trials

In an eviction action, the parties may assert non -eviction claims. The complaint often alleges an eviction claim and a monetary claim. See Section I.E.2.f, above. The tenant may file counterclaims. See Section I.I, above.

The court may consolidate the trials on these claims and counterclaims. R.C.

1923.081; Ohio R. Civ. P. 42. R.C. 1923.081 “permits joinder of causes of action for possession, past due rent, damages, and counterclaims, in one trial.” Forney v.

Climbing Higher Enters., Inc., 158 Ohio App.3d 338, 345, 815 N.E.2d 722 (9th Dist.

2004). Accord Brown v. Brown , No. 04 -CA-000018, 2005 WL 91 4490, ¶ 23 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 14, 2005).

The trial on the eviction claim and the trial on the landlord’s monetary claim may be consolidated. Smith v. Wright, 65 Ohio App. 2d 101, 103, 104 n.2, 416 N.E.2d 655 (8th Dist. 1979). The trial on the ev iction claim and the trial on the tenant’s counterclaims may be consolidated. See, e.g., Behrle v. Beam, 6 Ohio St.3d 41, 451 N.E.2d 237 (1983). The trial on the landlord’s monetary claim and the trial on the tenant’s counterclaims may be consolidated. See Ohio R. Civ. P. 42(A).

Alternatively, the court may hold separate trials on any of these claims and
counterclaims. Hamdan v. Traish , No. 14 -MA-14, 2015 WL 6697330, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Oct. 26, 2015);Oakwood Mgmt. Co. v. Richards, No. 99AP-627, 1999 WL 1124692, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999);Sholiton Indus., Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd., No. 17480, 1999 WL 355898, at *9 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 4, 1999);

Smith v. Wright, 65 Ohio App. 2d 101, 103, 104 n.2, 416 N.E.2d 655 (8th Dist.1979);

Siegler v. Batdorff , 63 Ohio App.2d 76, 84 -85, 408 N.E.2d 1383 (8th Dist. 1979);

Gibraltar Constr. Corp. v. Fortune, No. 396, 1979 Ohio App. LEXIS 9223 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Nov. 28, 1979). See generally Ohio R. Civ. P. 42(B).

The decision on whether to hold consolidated or separate trials on these claims and counterclaims generally is committed to the discretion of the trial court. Sholiton Indus., Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd., No. 17480, 1999 WL 355898 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 4, 1999); Siegler v. Batdorff, 63 Ohio App.2d 76, 408 N.E.2d 1383 (8th Dist.

1979). See also Palombo v. Hayes, Nos. 65781,66714, 1995 WL 229120 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 13, 1995) (R.C. 1923.081 consolidation is discretionary).

“Where an action for forcible entry and detainer is joined with an action for unpaid rent or other damages, the matter is typically divided into two separate hearings. In the first hearing on the claim for forcible entry and detainer (the ‘first cause’), the court determines whether the plaintiff, typically a landlord or property owner, is entitled to possession of the premises. If the plaintiff prevails on the first cause and the occupant is evicted, a second hearing will be held on the ‘second cause’ for unpaid rent or
damages. Often, until an occupant has been evicted and has vacated the premises, a plaintiff cannot know the full extent of his or her damages in the second cause.” Mathews v. Cooper, No. 109974, 2021 WL 3560200, ¶ 77 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug.

12, 2021).

However, the decision on whether to consolidate the trial on the eviction claim and the trial on the landlord’s monetary claim is subject to the requirements of R.C.

1923.081 and Civil Rule 12(A)(1).

R.C. 1923.081 provides that the trials on these landlord claims may be consolidated unless there is good cause not to do so. R.C. 1923.081 specifies three examples of good cause not to consolidate the trials: a tenant’s request to file an answer, a tenant’s request to file a counterclaim, and a tenant’s request to conduct discovery. R.C.

1923.081 also provides that the trials on these landlord claims cannot be consolidated if the adverse party in the eviction action is a deceased resident of a manufactured 79 home park.59 Civil Rule 12(A)(1) bars consolidation of the trials on these landlord claims if the date of the consolidated trial precedes the expiration of the Civil Rule 12(A)(1) time period for filing an answer to the landlord’s monetary claim and, as of the trial date, the tenant has not filed an answer. Wodzisz v. Bayes, No. 96-APE07-891, 1997 WL 142715, at *4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 25, 1997);Smith v. Wright, 65 Ohio App.2d 101, 103, 416 N.E.2d 655 (8th Dist. 1979). See also Ruminski v. Matulis , No. 1219, 1985 WL 4235 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 6, 1985).

When a tenant files a counterclaim under R.C. 1923.061(B),60 the court is required to consolidate the trial on the eviction claim and the trial on the tenant’s counterclaims.

Studer v. Roark , No. 94 -CA-38, 1995 WL 347963 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Mar. 21, 1995); Sandefur Mgmt. Co. v. Smith, 21 Ohio App.3d 145, 486 N.E.2d 1234 (10th Dist.

1985). In these cases, the court cannot determine the eviction claim without an adjudication of the tenant’s counterclaims. Id.

#### 2. Magistrate, Bench, or Jury Trial

In an eviction action in a municipal court, the trial may be heard by a magistrate, a judge, or a jury. The right to a trial by jury is discussed in Section I.J.1., above. If a municipal court has more than two judges, one or more magistrates must be appointed to he ar the eviction actions in which the right to a trial by jury is waived or not
demanded. Rules of Superintendence for the Courts of Ohio,Rule 19.1. If a municipal court has two or fewer judges, one or more magistrates may be appointed to hear the eviction actions in which the right to a trial by jury is waived or not demanded. Id.

Typically, eviction actions are tried to a judge (“bench trial”) in one of two
circumstances. The first is where the municipal court has two or fewer judges, the court decides not to appoint a magistrate, and the right to a trial by jury is waived or not demanded. The second is where the tenant files a jury demand, the judge is 59 For a discussion on whether R.C. 1923.081 also prohibits a joinder of these claims, where the adverse party in the eviction action is a deceased resident of a manufactured home park, see Section I.E.2.f, above.

60 For a discussion on R.C. 1923.061(B), see Section II.O, below.

scheduled to conduct the jury trial, 61 and the tenant withdraws the jury demand.62

#### 3. Court Reporter

For the trial in an eviction action, the court is required to provide an official court reporter to record the evidence, if a party to the action properly requests those services.

State ex rel. Brady v. Youngstown Mun. Court, No. 88-CA-162, 1990 WL 18139(Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 26, 1990).

The local court rules may permit a party to retain the services of a private reporter who the court may then designate as the official court reporter for the trial.

#### 4. Witness Testimony Requirement

Civil Rule 43 applies to FED trials. T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly , 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020 -Ohio-4279 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020). Therefore, at the FED trial, generally the testimony of witnesses must be taken in open court, and not via affidavits.
Id. 63

#### 5. Hearsay Evidence

“The Rules of Evidence apply in trials of forcible entry and detainer actions.”T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly , 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020 -Ohio-4279, ¶ 38 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020). Therefore, in an eviction action, as in other cases, generally hearsay evidence is not admissible. Id.

A landlord’s affidavit of facts in support of an eviction claim is hearsay. Id., ¶¶ 38,39. Upon a timely objection, a court is required to deny the admission of the affidavit into evidence. Carl Edward Miller Trust v. Jones , No. 2018 -CVG-15385 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., June 13, 2018). Absent a timely objection, the court may admit the affidavit, but only if it meets the requisite particularized guarantees of 61 Civil Rule 53(C)(1)(c) permits a magistrate to conduct a jury trial, but only if the parties consent to it.

62 The tenant may withdraw the jury demand only if the landlord consents to the withd rawal.

See Ohio R. Civ. P. 38(D).

63 In T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly , 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020 -Ohio-4279, ¶ 46 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020), the court overrules Oakbrook Realty Corp. v. Blout , 48 Ohio App.3d 69, 548 N.E.2d 305 (10th Dist. 1988), “to the ext ent that it has been interpreted as permitting the municipal court to grant judgment on a forcible entry and detainer claim relying solely on statements contained in an affidavit without any testimony being offered in open court.” 81
trustworthiness. T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly , 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020 -Ohio-4279, ¶¶ 40,41 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020).

The case law has addressed some of the common examples of hearsay evidence in an eviction action.

The hearsay rule may preclude a manage r from testifying on the substance of complaints that the manager has received from other tenants.Associated Estates Corp.
v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985).

The hearsay rule may preclude a manager from testifying on the subst ance of complaints that the manager has received from the manager’s employees. Trail W.
Apts. v. Zigan , No. 05 -CVG-01267 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., June 23, 2005). Cf.

First Merit Venture v. Stringer, No. C-050238, slip op. at 3 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec.

14, 2005) (failure to prove drug activity where “manager conceded that she had no knowledge of drug activity at Stringer’s apartment other than what the police had told her”).

The hearsay rule may preclude a manager from introducing into evidence the written complaints that the manager has received from other tenants.Norwalk N. Apts.
v. Mackey , No. H -93-38, 1994 WL 262508 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 1994);

Norwalk N. Apts. v. Holiday , No. H-93-37, 1994 WL 262509 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 1994).

The hearsay rule may preclude a landlord from introducing a police report or a lab report on contraband, when the landlord fails to present a witness to authenticate the report, which is not self-authenticating. Detroit Terrace Ltd. v. Bender, No. 11-CVG- 19350 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 5, 2011) (police report); CMHA v.

Johnson, No. 2004-CVG-10962 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug. 11, 2004) (lab report).

The hearsay rule may preclude a police officer from testifying as to statements made to the officer by a non -tenant concerning the residency of the non -tenant and another non-tenant in the tenant’s unit. Geauga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Biggs, No. 98- G-2207, 2000 WL 665567 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., May 19, 2000).

The hearsay rule may preclud e a police officer from testifying as to statements made to the officer by a confidential informant concerning unlawful property (guns) in a tenant’s apartment, where the testimony is sought as circumstantial evidence of the tenant’s knowledge of the exist ence of the property in the apartment. See Chicago
Hous. Auth. v. Rose, 203 Ill. App.3d 208, 217-18, 560 N.E.2d 1131 (1990). Cf. Camco,
Inc. v. Lowery , 362 Ill. App.3d 421, 434, 839 N.E.2d 655 (2005) (arrest and police reports inadmissible since they generally contain hearsay evidence).

#### 6. Trial – Tenant Not Present

In an eviction action, the tenant is not required to attend the trial, and the tenant may be represented at the trial even if the tenant is not present.Forman v. Macias, No.

79AP-122, 1979 Ohi o App. LEXIS 11693 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 26, 1979);

CMHA v. Miller, No. 2003-CVG-09190 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 3, 2003). See generally Schaedler v. Shinkle, No. CA99-09-025, 2000 WL 1283776, at *1 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Sept. 11, 2000) (“nothing requires a defendant to appear at trial where he is represented by counsel”).

If a landlord wants the tenant to be present at the trial, the landlord may subpoena the tenant. Forman v. Macias, No. 79AP-122, 1979 Ohio App. LEXIS 11693 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 26, 1979).

If a tenant does not make an appearance in the eviction action, does not attend the trial, and is not represented by counsel at the trial, R.C. 1923.07 requires the court to try the case as though the tenant were present. “Thus, under R.C. 1923.07, even when a tenant fails to appear after being properly served, the municipal court must conduct a formal judicial examination of the evidence and reach a determination on a complaint for forcible entry and detainer.” T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly, 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020- Ohio-4279, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020).

Therefore, the landlord is still obligated to introduce competent evidence that proves the elements of the landlord’s eviction claim. T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly , 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020 -Ohio-4279 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020); Aultman Home for Aged Women v. Ott , No. CA -7688, 1989 WL 64055 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 5, 1989); Fields v. Travis, No. 87AP-578, 1987 WL 873892 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Oct.

22, 1987); Hampshire Heights, Inc. v. Van Wormer , 17 Ohio Op.3d 287 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 1980).

Civil Rule 55 is not applicable to FED actions. Hampshire Heights, Inc. v. Van Wormer, 17 Ohio Op.3d 287 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 1980);Siegler v. Batdorff, 63 Ohio App.2d 76, 408 N.E.2d 1383 (8th Dist. 1979) (dicta).

If the landlord (or landlord’s attorney) has not been sworn in, the landlord’s (or landlord’s attorney’s) statements at trial are not co mpetent evidence to prove the elements of the eviction claim. Aultman Home for Aged Women v. Ott, No. CA-7688, 1989 WL 64055 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 5, 1989) (attorney’s statements);Fields v.

Travis, No. 87AP -578, 1987 WL 873892 (Ohio App., 10th Dist ., Oct. 22, 1987) 83 (landlord’s statements);Hampshire Heights, Inc. v. Van Wormer, 17 Ohio Op.3d 287 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 1980) (attorney’s statements).

#### 7. Trial – Unrepresented Party

“‘It is well established thatpro se litigants are presumed to have knowledge of the law and legal procedures and that they are held to the same standard as litigants who are represented by counsel.’” State ex rel. Fuller v. Mengel, 100 Ohio St.3d 352, 354, 800 N.E.2d 25 (2003) (quotingSabouri v. Ohio Dep’t of Job & Family Serv., 145 Ohio App.3d 651, 654, 763 N.E.2d 1238 (2001)). Accord OH Seven LLC v. Lee, No. 2020- CA-00057, 2021 WL 286534, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 28, 2021); Landings at Beckett Ridge v. Holmes, No. CA2020-04-050, 2020 WL 7690283, ¶ 32 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 28, 2020); Di Fiore v. Booker, No. 108946, 2020 WL 2992105, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 4, 2020); Eckart v. Newman, No. WM-18-006, 2019 WL 3765412, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 9, 2019); Holmes v. Cobblestone Grove ,
No. CA2016-04-075, 2017 WL 74986, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 9, 2017);

Rasberry v. Taylor, No. 26510, 2013 WL 2327020, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 29, 2013); Cook v. Criminger , No. 22313, 2005 WL 957023, ¶ 7 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,
Apr. 27, 2005); Holman v. Keegan, 139 Ohio App.3d 911, 918, 746 N.E.2d 209 (6th
Dist. 2000); Meyers v. First Nat’l Bank of Cincinnati , 3 Ohio App.3d 209, 210, 444 N.E.2d 412 (1st Dist. 1981); Liggins v. Westminster Arms, Inc. , Nos. 98AP-377,378, 1998 WL 894613, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 22, 1998).

“A pro se litigant cannot expect or demand special treatment from the judge, who must be impartial.” Frew v. Frew, No. 2007-CA-17, 2008 WL 3856300 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 19, 2008). Accord Rasberry v. Taylor, No. 26510, 2013 WL 2327020, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 29, 2013); Kilroy v. B.H. Lakeshore Co. , 111 Ohio App.3d 357, 363, 676 N.E.2d 171 (8th Dist. 1996).

“A pro se litigant ... must accept the results of his or her own errors or mistakes.” Holman v. Keegan , 139 Ohio App.3d 911, 918, 746 N.E.2d 209 (6th Dist. 2000).

Accord OH Seven LLC v. Lee , No. 2020 -CA-00057, 2021 WL 286534, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 28, 2021); Landings at Beckett Ridge v. Holmes, No. CA2020-04- 050, 2020 WL 7690283, ¶ 32 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 28, 2020); Eckart v.

Newman, No. WM -18-006, 2019 WL 3765412, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 9, 2019); Holmes v. Cobblestone Grove , No. CA2016 -04-075, 2017 WL 74986, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 9, 2017);Cook v. Criminger, No. 22313, 2005 WL 957023, ¶ 7 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 27, 2005); Liggins v. Westminster Arms, Inc. , Nos.

98AP-377,378, 1998 WL 894613, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,Dec. 22, 1998);Meyers
v. First Nat’l Bank of Cincinnati, 3 Ohio App.3d 209, 210, 444 N.E.2d 412 (1st Dist.

1981).

However, to “insure self-represented litigants the opportunity to have their matters fairly heard, a judge may make reasonable accommodation to a self-represented litigant consistent with the law.” Ohio Code of Judicial Conduct, Rule 2.2, Comment [4]. For example, the judge may provide “brief information about the proceeding and evidentiary and foundational requirements.”Id., Rule 2.6, Comment [1A]. E.g., Henry
v. Richardson , 193 Ohio App.3d 375, 382, 951 N.E.2d 1123 (12th Dist. 2011);

Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Morgan, 155 Ohio App.3d 189, 194, 800 N.E.2d 64 (1st Dist. 2003), rev’d on other grounds, 104 Ohio St.3d 445, 820 N.E.2d 315 (2004).

In an eviction action, the court is obligated under this law to treat unrepresented landlords the same as if they had retained counsel.Milano v. Jones, No. M8906-CVG- 20458 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., July 10, 1989). But see Wright v. Miller, No. 752, 1991 WL 37926 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 11, 1991).

The court may assist the unrepresented landlord in basic procedural matters, but not in the presentation of the case to the detriment of the tenant. Milano v. Jones, No.

M8906-CVG-20458 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., July 10, 1989). If an unrepresented landlord fails to prove the landlord’s case because of ignorance of the law, the case should be judged on the evidence that has been produced. Id. Absent a request by the landlord, the court shouldnot offer a continuance to the landlord to enable the landlord to prove the eviction claim at a subsequent hearing. Id.

#### 8. Collateral Estoppel and Res Judicata


##### a. Collateral Estoppel

The doctrine of collateral estoppel applies to an eviction action. 64 Hamdan v.

Traish, No. 14-MA-14, 2015 WL 6697330, ¶ 40 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Oct. 26, 2015);

Great Lakes Mall, Inc. v. Deli Table, Inc., No. 93-L-154, 1994 WL 587559 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 16, 1994). E.g., Palombo v. Hayes , Nos. 65781,66714, 1995 W L 229120 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 13, 1995); Chan v. Birch , No. 15249, 1992 WL 64190 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 25, 1992); Harrison v. Webb , 62 Ohio Law Abs.

64 For an article advocating the position that, in Ohio, the doctrine of collateral estoppel should not apply to an eviction action, see Kimberly E. O’Leary, The Inadvisability of Applying Preclusive Doctrines to Summary Evictions, 30 U. Tol. L. Rev. 49 (1999).

85 273, 100 N.E.2d 728 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., 1951).

“[C]ollateral estoppel ... precludes the relitigation, in a second action of an issue that has been actually and necessarily litigated and determined in a prior action which was based on a different cause of action.”Whitehead v. Gen. Tel. Co. of Ohio, 20 Ohio St.2d 108, 112, 254 N.E.2d 10 (1969). Accord Namenyi v. Tomasello, No. 2013-CA- 75, 2014 WL 5089113 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 10, 2014);McAlpine v. Patrick, No.

86453, 2006 WL 562191 (Ohio App., 8th Dist. Cty., Mar. 9, 2006).

Therefore, the court’s determination of an issue, when it rules on the eviction claim, is binding on the parties when the court later rules on other claims or counterclaims in that action or in a second action. The case law below is illustrative.

If the court finds nonpayment of rent when ruling on the evictionclaim, this finding is binding on the parties when the court later rules on the landlord’s monetary claim for nonpayment of rent. Great Lakes Mall, Inc. v. Deli Table, Inc., No. 93-L-154, 1994 WL 587559, at *1-3 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 16, 1994);Chan v. Birch, No. 15249, 1992 WL 64190, at *2 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 25, 1992).

If the court finds in a first eviction action that a tenant overpaid her rent and, as a result, does not owe any rent for certain future months, this finding is binding on the parties when the court rules in a second eviction action for nonpayment of rent for one (or more) of those specified months. Boyd v. Williams , Nos. 07 -CVG-11772,14908 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Jan. 18, 2008).

If the court finds no racial discrimination in th e nonrenewal of the lease when it rules on the eviction claim, this finding is binding on the parties when the court later rules on the tenant’s counterclaim for racial discrimination. Palombo v. Hayes, Nos.

65781,66714, 1995 WL 229120, at *7-8 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 13, 1995).

If an eviction judgment is based on the existence of an oral lease, this finding is binding on the parties when the court later rules on the tenant’s counterclaim for breach of an alleged land installment contract. Tillimon v. Rideout, No. L-00-1388, 2001 WL 1518111, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 30, 2001).

##### b. Res Judicata

The doctrine of res judicata (i.e., claim preclusion) does not apply to an eviction action, relative to any non -eviction claims between the parties. 65 R.C. 1923.03 65 For a discussion on permissive joinder of eviction and non -eviction claims, see Section I.E.2.f, above.

provides that “[j]udgments under this chapter are not a bar to a later action brought by either party.” Consequently, “a judgment in a forcible entry and detainer action does not bar a later action between the same parties arising out of the same subject matter.” Marous/Church, LLC v. Stanich , No. 2000 -L-188, 2001 WL 1561107, at *2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 7, 2001). Accord N.T.I. Enters., Ltd. v. Dental Prods. & Servs.,
Inc. No. 2018 -T-0018, 2019 WL 117829 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Jan. 7, 201 9);

McAlpine v. Patrick , No. 86453, 2006 WL 562191 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 9, 2006); Great Lakes Mall, Inc. v. Deli Table, Inc. , No. 93 -L-154, 1994 WL 587559 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 16, 1994). See also Hamilton Brownfields Redevelopment, LLC v. Duro Tire & Wheel, 156 Ohio App.3d 525, 806 N.E.2d 1039 (12th Dist. 2004); Haney v. Roberts, 130 Ohio App.3d 293, 720 N.E.2d 101 (4th Dist.

1998); Reck v. Whalen, 114 Ohio App.3d 16, 682 N.E.2d 721 (2nd Dist. 1996); Chef Italiano Corp. v. Crucible Dev. Cor p., No. CV -2003-12-7277, 2004 WL 5491217 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Oct. 28, 2004).

Courts are split on whether a final eviction judgment on the merits 66 precludes relitigation of the propriety of the eviction judgment. Compare Tillimon v. Rideout ,
No. L -00-1388, 2001 WL 1518111, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 30, 2001)
(precluded) with Machshonba v. Cleveland Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 96811, 2011 WL 6920728, ¶¶ 11-13 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 29, 2011) (not precluded).

But res judicata does not precludea second eviction action for new grounds, which arise after the first eviction action. Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Hazelbaker, No. CA2006- 01-004, 2006 WL 3479856 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 4, 2006). The second eviction action may be filed even during a pending appeal in the first eviction action, where the appeal is from a judgment entered in favor of the landlord but stayed pending the
appeal. Id.

A related issue, which the courts have not yet addressed directly, is whether res judicata bars a second eviction action for grounds that existed before the filing of the first eviction action but were not raised in the first eviction action. While general res judicata law would bar a second eviction action for these grounds, R.C. 1923.03 may require a different result.

66 An eviction judgment declaring the eviction claim to be moot because of the tenant’s vacation of the premises is not a judgment rendered on the merits. Haney v. Roberts, 130 Ohio App.3d 293, 301, 720 N.E.2d 101 (4th Dist. 1998).

87

#### 9. Burden of Proof Issues, Assisted Housing

In an eviction action for nonpayment of rent against an assisted housing tenant, 67 when the tenant’s defense is that the nonpayment of rent is due to circumstances beyond the tenant’s control (see Section III.G.1.a, below), the tenant has the burden of proving that the tenant did not pay the rent for reasons beyond the tenant’s control.

Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Harris , No. CA-8687, 1992 WL 90131 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 20, 1992).

In any eviction acti on against an assisted housing tenant, the landlord has the burden of proving that it served the tenant with the applicable federal notice of termination (see Sections III.A-E, below). Nat’l Church Residences of Worthington v.

Timson, 78 Ohio App.3d 798, 605 N.E.2d 1346 (10th Dist. 1992).

However, if a tenant asserts as a defense the failure of a landlord to serve the applicable federal notice of termination, the tenant has the burden of proving the type of assisted housing in which the tenant resides. See Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Konich, No. 17177, 1995 WL 553250 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 20, 1995);Conifers
Ltd. P’ship v. McWilliams, No. 13721, 1993 WL 224755 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 23, 1993). But see Normar Corp. v. Richardson , No. 93 -CVG-9922 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Mar. 3, 1994) (eviction claim dismissed because “Plaintiff has failed to present evidence showing which federal regulations in regard to Section 8 housing apply to the rental premises....Without such information the Court is prevented from making a crucial determination on whether Plaintiff or Defendant complied with the appropriate federal regulations”).

#### 10. Miscellaneous Evidence and Proof Issues

“Before trying the cause in an action for forcible entry and detainer in the absence of the defendant, the court or referee [now magistrate] must first determine whether the summons was properly served.” Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Stuckey, No. L-92-102, 1993 WL 315626, at *3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 13, 1993).

In an eviction action for disturbing the neighbors, the testimony of neighbors may not be required to show that the tenant’s conduct disturbed the neighbors. E.g., Label & Co. v. Taylor, No. 86-C-26, 1987 WL 12887 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 17, 1987) (security guard’s testimony as to thenoise at the premises deemed sufficient).

67 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, below.

In an eviction action for repeated damage to the premises, the landlord may submit evidence of past incidents of damage to the premises. Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Kirksey, No. CA -9116, 1993 WL 135735 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 19, 1993). In such cases, the landlord has not waived those prior lease violations. Id.

In an eviction action for an unauthorized occupant, the determination as to whether a person is a guest or an occupant of the premises is a question of fact. New Boston Kiwanis Hous., Etc. v. Sparks, No. 1957, 1992 WL 79561 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Apr.

14, 1992).

When a local court rule requires a trial statement that identifies the witnesses who will be testifying and the documents that will be introduced, a landlord’s failure to file a trial statement is a potential ground for excluding the testimony of witnessesand the introduction of documents in the eviction action. See, e.g., Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Moon , No. 2006 -CVG-10893 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 21, 2006).

### N. Magistrate’s Decision and a Party’s

Objections

#### 1. Content of a Magistrate’s Decision

When an eviction action is tried to a magistrate, the magistrate is required to prepare and file a magistrate’s decision. Ohio R. Civ. P. 53(D)(3)(a).

In eviction actions, a magistrate’s decision may be devoid of both findings of fact and conclusions of law. Miele v. Ribovich, 90 Ohio St.3d 439, 443-44, 739 N.E.2d 333 (2000);68 State ex rel. GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Callahan , 45 Ohio St.3d 51, 55, 543 N.E.2d 483 (1989).

However, a magistrate may sua sponte, or in response to a party’s motion under Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(a)(ii), include findings of fact and/or conclusions of law in the magistrate’s decision. State ex rel. GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Callahan , 45 Ohio St.3d 51, 55, 543 N.E.2d 483 (1989).

Whenever a magistrate includes findings and fact and/or conclusions of law in the magistrate’s decision (or amended decision), the decision (or amended decision) must 68 Miele implicitly overruled Cresthaven Dev., Inc. v. Franklin , No. 76079, 2000 WL 336525 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 30, 2000), and Sholiton Indus., Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd., No. 17480, 1999 WL 355898 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 4, 1999).

89 be filed within seven working days after the trial on the eviction claim. The Callahan decision held that, in an eviction action, Civil Rule 52 does not apply but a court may prepare findings of fact and conclusions of law, provided they are filed within seven working days after the trial. 69 The Miele decision does not affect this Callahan
holding. Miele, 90 Ohio St.3d at 444 n.5.

#### 2. Court Review of a Magistrate’s Decision, Absent

Objections In an eviction action, absent objections to the magistrate’s decision (see Section I.N.3, below), the court may adopt the magistrate’s decision without making an independent analysis of the issues. Miele v. Ribovich, 90 Ohio St.3d 439, 443, 739 N.E.2d 333 (2000). Under Civil Rule 53(D)(4)(c), the court is only required to determine whether there is “an error of law or other defect on the face of the magistrate’s decision.” Miele, 90 Ohio St.3d at 443.70

#### 3. Objections to a Magistrate’s Decision

In an eviction action, a party may file objections to the magistrate’s decision under Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(b). 71 See Sears v. Fechuck , No. 1999AP -080048, 2000 WL 874727, at *1-2 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 6, 2000);Woodruff Vill. Apts. v. Jones, No.

L-90-145, 1991 WL 35165, at *4 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 15, 1991).72 Cf. Lewis v.

Powers, No. 15461, 1997 WL 335563, at *7 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 13, 1997).

But see Summit Mgmt. Servs. v. Gough, No. 19714, 2000 WL 1226605, at *2,4 (Ohio 69 Post-Callahan, appellate court decisions holding that Civil Rule 52 is not applicable to FED actions include: Oneida Props., Inc. v. Pickett, No. 24626, 2009 WL 3119647 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 30, 2009); Davis v. Reed , Nos. 68699,68700, 1996 WL 347814 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 20, 1996); Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v. Osborne, No. 16208 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug.

20, 1993); City of Cleveland v. Sundorph Aeronautical Corp. , No. 59739, 1991 WL 34719 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 14, 1991).

70 The Miele decision quotes the corresponding pre-2006 Civil Rule 53(E)(4)(a) provision (see note 7, above).

71 For further support for (and a general discussion of) this proposition, see note 74, below.

72 These pre-2006 court decisions cite to the corresponding pre -2006 Civil Rule 53(E)(3) (see note 7, above).

App., 9th Dist., Aug. 30, 2000).73 Moreover, to preserve the right to appeal from the trial court’s decision (on legal conclusions or factual findings) in an eviction action tried to a magistrate, a party generally must file objections under Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(b)(iv).74 DMHA v. Sacksteder,
No. 19253, 2005 WL 78547 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 14, 2005);Akron Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Jacobs, No. 20671, 2002 WL 432035 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 20, 2002);

Bennington v. Robinson, No. 1999-CA-00212, 2000 WL 222156 (Ohio App., 5th Dist.,
Feb. 7, 2000); Fed. Prop. Mgmt. v. Brown, No. 17424, 1999 WL 961275 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 25, 1999); Timbercreek Vill. Apts. v. Myles , No. 17422, 1999 WL 335307 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 28, 1999); U.S. Sec’y of Hous. & Urban Dev. v.

Chancellor, No. 73970, 1999 WL 126170 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 25, 1999);

73 This appellate district arguably overruled this decision on the right to file objections when it later held, in Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jacobs , No. 20671, 2002 WL 432035 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 20, 2002), that, under Civil Rule 53(E)(3)(b), the failure to file objections to a magistrate’s finding or conclusion in an FED action waives the right to challenge either the finding or the conclusion on appeal.

74 The pre-2006 court decisions cited in this paragraph cite to the corresponding pre-2006 Civil Rule 53(E)(3)(b) (see note 7, above).

The court decisions cited in this paragraph also implicitly hold that a party has a right to file objections to a magistrate’s decision in an eviction action. Their holding that a party must file such objections in order to preserve the right to app eal from the trial court’s FED judgment is necessarily premised on (and thus recognizes) the tenant’s right to file the objections.

But query whether this Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(b)(iv) provision should apply to eviction actions.

The purpose of the provision is to disallow appellate review where the alleged error was not called to the attention of the trial court at a time when the trial court could have corrected the
error. See Proctor v. Proctor, 48 Ohio App.3d 55, 58, 548 N.E.2d 287 (3rd Dist. 1988). In a n eviction action, timely objections do not automatically stay execution of the judgment (see Section I.N.4, below) and, therefore, do not necessarily call the error to the attention of the trial court at a time when it can take meaningful corrective action, e.g., prevent the tenant’s eviction.

Compare Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(b)(i) (objections to be filed within fourteen days of the filing of the magistrate’s decision)with R.C. 1923.14 (writ of restitution to be executed within ten days after its issuance, which often occurs within a day of the filing of the magistrate’s decision). In Kettering Square Apts. v. Crawford, Nos. 27504,27545,27548, 2017 WL 6398837, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 15, 2017), the court summarizes well why “[t]he objection provision s in Civ.R. 53 create a procedural quagmire when applied in a forcible entry and detainer proceeding….” But the court opts not to decide whether they apply to FED actions because, in the context of that case, the error would be harmless. See also Gold Key Realty v. Collins, No.

2013-CA-57, 2014 WL 5409159, ¶¶ 18-20 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 24, 2014) (discusses this issue without ruling on it; cites to this footnote, as set forth in the third edition).

91 Summit Mobile Home Park v. Brode, No. 97-P-0080, 1998 WL 34002598 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 16, 1998). See also Rescue Temple Church of Godv. Jones, No. 1509, 1993 WL 175538 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 26, 1993); Kelly Midway Supply, Inc. v.

Cotter, No. 91-L-001, 1991 WL 233823 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Nov. 8, 1991);Atwood
v. Bare, No. 1854, 1988 WL 117999 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 28, 1988).

“However, a party is not prohibited from assigning error on appeal related to the court’s adoption of a magistrate’s factual findings if the required language of Civ.R.53(D)(3)(a)(iii) is not included in the magistrate’s decision.” Kramanak v.

Myers, No. 2013-CA-00010, 2013 WL 3477269, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 8, 2013). Accord Marble Builder Direct Int’l, Inc. v. Hauxhurst, No. 2011-L-040, 2012 WL 1288722, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Apr. 16, 2012).

Given that the requirement to file objections applies to FED actions, the Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(b)(i) time period for filing objections – “[w]ithin fourteen days of the filing of the magistrate’s decision”– must likewise apply to eviction actions.

Furthermore, this fourteen-day period does not interfere with the summary nature of eviction proceedings. See generally Miele v. Ribovich, 90 Ohio St.3d 439, 443-44, 739 N.E.2d 333 (2000). Under Civil Rule 53(D)(4)(e)(i), the court may enter a judgment during this fourteen -day period.75 And, in evict ion actions, the automatic stay provision of Civil Rule 53(D)(4)(e)(i) does not apply. See Section I.N.4, below.

“An objection to a factual finding, whether or not specifically designated as a finding of fact under Civ.R. 53(D)(3)(a)(ii), shall be supported by a transcript of all the evidence submitted to the magistrate relevant to that finding or an affidavit of that evidence if a transcript is not available.” Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(b)(iii). “If an affidavit is employed, it must purport to comprise all the relevant evidence submitted to the referee [now magistrate] on the issue, instead of just the evidence the objecting party believes was disregarded.” Pappenhagen v. Payne , 48 Ohio App.3d 176, 178, 549 N.E.2d 208 (8th Dist. 1988).

Under Civil Rule 53(D)(3 )(b)(iii), the objecting party is required to “file the transcript or affidavit within thirty days after filing objections, unless the court extends 75 The pre-1995 Civil Rule 53 (see note 7, above) required courts to wait for timely objections or the expiration of the fourteen-day period before entering a judgment. Because of this delay, under pre-1995 Civil Rule 53(E), the fourteen-day period for filing objections did not apply to eviction actions. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jackson, 67 Ohio St.2d 129, 423 N.E.2d 177 (1981). The Jackson ruling on this issue should no longer be controlling since the delay that it relied on no longer exists.

the time in writing for preparation of the transcript or other good cause.” This thirty - day provision applies to eviction actions. Grant-Ross v. Dunsmore, No. L -20-1203, 2021 WL 4480900 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 30, 2021); Bowshier v. Bowshier, No.

2012-CA-40, 2013 WL 425813 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 1, 2013) . If a trial court denies an objection before the filing of the transcript (or affidavit) and before the expiration of this thirty -day period, it constitutes: noncompliance with Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(b)(iii); plain error; and grounds to reverse the trial court’s ruling on appeal.

Id.

Absent the filing of the required transcript or affidavit, the court will accept as true the magistrate’s factual findings.Stewart v. Hickory Hills Apts., 52 N.E.3d 259, 2015- Ohio-5046, ¶¶ 11,12 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., 2015); Pappenhagen v. Payne , 48 Ohio App.3d 176, 178, 549 N.E.2d 208 (8th Dist. 1988).76 When ruling on objections under Civil Rule 53(D), “the [trial] court shall undertake an independent review as to the objected matters ....” Ohio R. Civ. P.

53(D)(4)(d). “[T]he trial court must conduct an independent review of the facts and conclusions contained in the magistrate’s report and enter its own judgment.” Liggett
v. Shitaker Props., No. 23425, 2010 WL 1410758, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 9, 2010). Accord Aban v. Schaeffer, 185 Ohio App.3d 250, 923 N.E.2d 1168, ¶ 24 (5th
Dist. 2009). “Civ.R. 53 obligates the trial court to make its own determination, without deference to the the magistrate ....” Aban, 185 Ohio App.3d at 255. “Thus, the trial court’s standard of review of a magistrate’s decision is de novo.” Liggett v. Shitaker Props., No. 23425, 2010 WL 1410758, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 9, 2010).

Accord Aban, 185 Ohio App.3d at 255.

If the court sustains the objections before it has entered a judgment, the court may reject the magistrate’s decision, in whole or in part. Civil Ru le 53(D)(4)(b). If the court sustains the objections after it has entered a judgment, the court may vacate the
judgment. Civil Rule 53(D)(4)(e)(i).

#### 4. Stay Pending a Ruling on Objections

The tenant’s filing of objections to a magistrate’s decision on an e viction claim does not operate as an automatic stay under Civil Rule 53(D)(4)(e)(i). Miele v.

Ribovich, 90 Ohio St.3d 439, 739 N.E.2d 333 (2000);Colonial Am. Dev. Co. v. Griffith, 76 Because Pappenhagen is a pre -2006 court decision, it cites to the corresponding pre -2006 Civil Rule 53(E)(3)(b) (see note 7, above).

93 48 Ohio St.3d 72, 549 N.E.2d 513 (1990);77 Oneida Props., Inc. v. Pickett, No. 24626, 2009 WL 3119647 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 30, 2009) (applies Miele and Griffith to Civil Rule 53(D)(4)(e)(i)); Lewis v. Powers , No. 15461, 1997 WL 335563 (Ohio
App. 2nd Dist., June 13, 1997).

Nevertheless, “the court retains its discretion t o grant a stay while it reviews objections.” Calhoun v. Ivey , No. 2006 -CVG-27001, slip op. at 1 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 28, 2007). “The court can protect a plaintiff’s interest in prompt execution of judgment by ruling promptly on objections when it has granted a discretionary stay.” Id., slip op. at 2.

Therefore, it is often necessary and appropriate for a tenant to move for a stay of execution pending the court’s ruling on the tenant’s objections. The customary time period between the filing of a magistrate’s decision and the execution of the writ of restitution (see Section I.O.2, below) varies significantly among jurisdictions, but it may be as short as a few days.

If a tenant does not secure a stay pending a ruling on the tenant’sobjections and is consequently evicted under a writ of restitution, the eviction action may be subject to a dismissal as moot, without any ruling on the merits of the objections. See, e.g., Gold Key Realty v. Collins, No. 2013-CA-57, 2014 WL 5409159 (OhioApp., 2nd Dist., Oct.

24, 2014).

### O. Judgment and Writ of Restitution


#### 1. Introduction

In an eviction action, the court must determine whether to enter a judgment for the landlord or for the tenant and, if the judgment is for the landlord, when and against whom to issue the writ of restitution (also known as the writ of execution). The writ of restitution is discussed in Section I.O.2, below. Issues concerning the persons against whom an eviction judgment may be enforced are discussed in Section I.O.3, below.

When a court enters an eviction judgment against a manufactured home park resident, the judgment will provide the park operator with additional rights for 77 The Griffith decision cites to the corresponding pre -1995 Civil Rule 53(E)(7) (see note 7, above). The Miele decision retains the Griffith holding. Miele v. Ribovich, 90 Ohio St.3d 439, 444 n.5, 739 N.E.2d 333 (2000).

removing the manufactured or mobile home from the manufactured home park. In these eviction actions, the park operator also has additional duties with respect to any personal property that remains on the residential premises. These additional rights and duties are discussed in Section I.O.4, below.

If the court makes an R.C. 1923.15 finding that the premises are not in compliance with R.C. 5321.04 or R.C. 4781.38 (former R.C. 3733.10), the eviction judgment may also include a remedial order under R.C. 1923.15. These remedial orders are discussed in Section I.O.5, below.

#### 2. Writ of Restitution

A writ of restitution instructs the bailiff’s (or sheriff’s) office to enforce an eviction judgment that has been entered in favor of the landlord. R.C. 1923.13. It instructs the bailiff’s office to remove the tenant’s person and property from the premises.78 Id.

A writ of restitution is not final and may not be issued until the judge signs it. 79 Del Manor v. Peterson, No. 93-P-0039, 1994 WL 45269 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Feb.

11, 1994). Therefore, a writ of restitution signed only by the clerk of courts may not be issued. Id.

The courts usually issue the writ of restitution on or shortly after the date on which they have filed the judgment for the landlord. However, a court may delay its issuance of the writ of restitution when advised that time is necessary to fi nd another dwelling unit, particularly if there are hardship factors related to elderly persons, persons with disabilities, or children in the household. 80 E.g., Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Foster, No. 88-CV-05432 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Aug. 31, 1988). See generally Zeid v. Brown , No. 12 -259, 1988 WL 59831, at *1 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 3, 1988).

The court may make enforcement of the writ contingent on the landlord’s payment 78 If necessary, a landlord may be granted a writ of mandamus to secure enforcement of the writ of restitution. State ex rel. Marsol Apt. Co. v. Vannuci , 68 Ohio App.2d 181, 428 N.E.2d 468 (8th Dist. 1980).

79 If a writ of restitution is issued without the j udge’s signature, the court may vacate the writ under the court’s inherent powers, independent of Civil Rule 60(B). Del Manor v. Peterson ,
No. 93-P-0039, 1994 WL 45269 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Feb. 11, 1994).

80 A moratorium on the issuance of writs of restitution due to the Christmas season, if for a significant period of time ( e.g., 18 days), may violate the landlord’s due process and First Amendment rights. See Morrison v. Lipscomb, 877 F.2d 463 (6th Cir. 1989).

95 of a deposit that covers the cost of execution. State ex rel. Marsol Apt. Co. v. Vannuci, 68 Ohio App.2d 181, 428 N.E.2d 468 (8th Dist. 1980).

R.C. 1923.14 requires the bailiff’s office to execute the writ of restitution within ten days of receiving it. State ex rel. GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Wellington, No. 03-MA- 232, 2004 WL 47164, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Jan. 7, 2004). “There is no provision in the statute to allow the sheriff [or bailiff] to establish a grace period within which the tenant may have an opportunity to vacate the premises.” Id.

The tenant may not obtain a writ of prohibition to prevent enforcement of the writ of restitution. State ex rel. Cully v. Flanagan , 50 Ohio St.3d 180, 553 N.E.2d 655 (1990). There are two reasons for this conclusion: (1) the bailiff’s enforcement of a writ of restitution is n ot an exercise of judicial or quasi -judicial power; and (2) an appeal of the eviction judgment is an adequate remedy at law. Id.

When the bailiff removes the tenant’s property from the premises, generally, the bailiff places the property either outside by an adjacent curb or in storage. E.g., Riolo
v. Navin, No. 79809, 2002 WL 502408 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 4, 2002) (curb);

Castle Apts., Inc. v. Allgood, 43 Ohio App.3d 163, 539 N.E.2d 1154 (9th Dist. 1988) (curb); Ringler v. Sias, 68 Ohio App.2d 230, 428 N.E.2d 869 (10th Dist. 1980) (curb);

State ex rel. Marsol Apt. Co. v. Vannuci , 68 Ohio App.2d 181, 428 N.E.2d 468 (8th
Dist. 1980) (storage). The practice varies among jurisdictions.

After the bailiff has removed the property from the premises, the landlord does not have an affirmative duty to protect it, even if the property has been placed on the landlord’s land. Ringler v. Sias , 68 Ohio App.2d 230, 428 N.E.2d 869 (10th Dist.

1980).

If the property is placed in storage, the tenant will be required to pay the storage fee in order to recover the property. E.g., State ex rel. Marsol Apt. Co. v. Vannuci, 68 Ohio App.2d 181, 428 N.E.2d 468 (8th Dist. 1980).

#### 3. Persons Against Whom an Eviction Judgment

May Be Enforced When a court enters a judgment for the landlord in an eviction action, the persons against whom the judgment may be enforced are, in some circumstances, limited to the persons who have been named as defendants in the eviction complaint.

For example, if a subtenant resides in th e premises and the landlord brings an eviction action against the tenant and fails to name the subtenant as a party defendant, generally the judgment cannot be enforced against the subtenant.81 Hooper v. Seventh Urban, Inc., 70 Ohio App.2d 101, 434 N.E.2d 1367 (8th Dist. 1980).

However, one municipal court has held that, in these circumstances, the judgment may be enforced against the subtenant if: (1) the eviction complaint names as defendants the tenant “and all other occupants,” (2) the eviction complaint is served by residential service in accordance with Civil Rule 4.1, (3) the landlord has no knowledge of the subtenant’s occupancy or that anyone other than the tenant is occupying the premises, and (4) the landlord took all reasonable steps available to prohibit and prevent the subtenancy, e.g., inclusion of a provision in the lease that prohibited subleasing without the landlord’s prior written consent. Mar-Lis Props. v. Ratliff, No.

M8502-CVG-04841 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 2, 1985).

If a husb and and wife reside in the premises and one of them is not named as a defendant in the eviction complaint, whether the eviction judgment may be enforced against the non-party spouse depends on whether the non-party spouse is a tenant under the rental agreement. If the non-party spouse is not a tenant under the rental agreement, the eviction judgment may be enforced against the non-party spouse. Studer v. Roark,
No. 94-CA-38, 1995 WL 347963 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Mar. 21, 1995); Dondorfer v.

Wolf, No. 13 -010, 1988 WL 105568 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 7, 1988). But cf.

Arrieta v. Mahon , 31 Cal.3d 381, 182 Cal. Rptr. 770 (1982) (due process bars the eviction of adults who are not named in the writ of restitution, unless they are afforded certain post-judgment notice and hearing opportunities). If the non -party spouse is a tenant under the rental agreement, the eviction judgment may not be enforced against the non-party spouse. Milano v. Jones, No. M8906-CVG-20458 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., July 10, 1989).

Based on Studer and Dondorfer, it appears that the eviction judgment may also be enforced against children of the tenant who are not named as defendants in the eviction
complaint. See also New York County Dist. Attorney’s Office v. Oquendo, 147 Misc.2d 125, 553 N.Y.S.2d 973 (Civ. Ct. 1990). The children, like the non -party spouse in Studer and Dondorfer, are not tenants under the rental agreement. The children of the tenant reside in the premises only “as an incident to the family relationship.” Id. at 81 When an owner maintains an eviction action against a subtenant in possession of the premises, the tenant who is not in possession is not an indispensable party to the action.

Baughman v. Semaan, 29 Ohio App.3d 365, 505 N.E.2d 643 (9th Dist. 1986). T he reason for this conclusion is that the eviction action relates solely to the right of present possession. Id.

97 127.

If a bailiff attempts to enforce a writ of restitution against a non -party occupant who is not subject to the eviction judgment, the non -party occupant may need to intervene and file a counterclaim in the eviction action, or file a new complaint, seeking injunctive relief against the enforcement. The non -party occupant may not obtain a writ of prohibition to prevent the improper enforcement of the writ of restitution. See Section I.O.2, above. If the writ of restitution is enforced improperly again st a non- party occupant, the non-party occupant may have a damage claim against the landlord, but not against the bailiffs. See Castle Apts., Inc. v. Allgood, 43 Ohio App.3d 163, 539 N.E.2d 1154 (9th Dist. 1988).

#### 4. Manufactured Home Park, Summary Process for

Judicial Sale of the Home

##### a. Introduction
