When a court enters an eviction judgment against a manufactured home park resident, R.C. 1923.09(B) and R.C. 1923.11(B) require that the judgment entry include the authority of the park operator to remove the manufactured (or mobile) home and to exercise the right of sale, destruction, or transfer of ownership of the home under R.C.

1923.12, 1923.13(B), and 1923.14(B) (“summary sales process”). Oak Park Mgmt.
Corp. v. Via , No. 07 -CA-0022, 2008 WL 2168505 (Ohio App. , 9th Dist., May 27, 2008).

However, the failure of a trial court to include this right of sale, destruction, or transfer of ownership in the judgment entry does not deprive the park operator of this right and, therefore, constitutes harmless error. Id.

A manufactured home park operator may not invoke this right of summary sale after: (1) it has failed to inform the court that it may elect to proceed under R.C.

1923.13(B); (2) it has executed a writ of restitution and thereby has assumed dominion and control over both the land and any personal property; and (3) thereupon, the court has closed the eviction action. Colonial Oaks Prop., LLC v. Kilby , No. 2018-CVG- 03281 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, Dec. 9, 2020).

A manufactured home park operator also may not invoke this right of summary sale if it has not complied with the procedural requirements for the sale (see Section I.O.4.b, below). Id.

##### b. Procedural Requirements for the Sale

The summary sales process has sixteen basic provisions.82 First, the home must be unoccupied for a period of three days following the entry of the eviction judgment. R.C. 1923.12(A).

Second, the park operator may then provide the titled owner of the home with a written notice to remove the home from the park within fourteen days from thedate of the delivery of the notice. Id. The required content of the notice is specified in R.C.

1923.12(B). The notice may be served by personal delivery or by ordinary mail sent to the last known address of the owner. R.C. 1923.12(A).

Third, if the owner does not remove the home during the fourteen-day period, the park operator may request the court to use the summary sales process. R.C.

1923.12(A), 1923.13(B).

Fourth, the park operator must conduct a search of the appropriate public records and make reasonably diligent inquiries to identify any and all persons who have an outstanding right, title, or interest in the home. R.C. 1923.12(C).

Fifth, if the park operator requests the court to use the summary sales process, the request must include: (1) th e park operator’s certification of service of the fourteen - day notice, and (2) based on the park operator’s searches and inquiries, the name and last known address of each person who has an outstanding right, title, or interest in the home (“identified interested person”). R.C. 1923.12(C).

Sixth, if personal property has been abandoned on the residential premises and the park operator has knowledge of any person who has an outstanding right, title, or interest in any of the personal property, the park oper ator must list those items of personal property, and the name and last known address of those persons, on its request for the writ of execution. R.C. 1923.12(C), 1923.13(B), 1923.14(B)(2),(7).

Seventh, the clerk of court may require the park operator to pay an advance deposit sufficient to secure payment of the appraisal of the manufactured home and the advertisement of the sale of the home. R.C. 1923.12(C).

Eighth, upon the park operator’s request, the court shall issue a writ of execution that authorizes the use of the summary sales process, provided the requirements of R.C.

1923.12 have been satisfied. R.C. 1923.13(B).

82 Additional requirements are specified in R.C. 1923.12(D),(E) for cases in which the titled owner of the home is deceased.

99 Ninth, the clerk of court shall send the writ of execution by regular mail to the last known address of the titled owner of the home an d each identified interested person.

R.C. 1923.14(B)(1). When R.C. 1923.14(B)(1) requires notice to the countytreasurer and auditor, it does not make them necessary parties to the FED action under Civil Rule 19. Marysville Estates v. Bruce , No. 14 -13-12, 2013 WL 5347438, ¶¶ 10 -13 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 23, 2013).

Tenth, within sixty days after receiving the writ of execution, if the manufactured home is abandoned, the bailiff (or sheriff) shall commence proceedings for the sale of the home in accordance with the procedures for the sale of goods on execution under R.C. Chapter 2329. R.C. 1923.14(B)(3). In addition to all notices required under R.C.

2923.13, the bailiff shall serve the identified interested persons with a written notice of the date, time, and place of the sale. Id. Notwithstanding any statutory provision to the contrary, the titled owner of the home may not secure a stay of execution relative to the sale. Id.

Eleventh, if the manufactured home is sold under the summary sales process, the park operator may deny the purchaser of the home the right to maintain the home in the manufactured home park. R.C. 1923.14(B)(3).

Twelfth, if the manufactured home cannot be sold on two occasions “due to a want of bidders,” the clerk of court shall issue a certificate of title of the home to the park operator, free and clear of all security interests, liens, and encumbrances.Id. The clerk shall issue the certificate of title in the manner prescribed in R.C. 4505.10. Id.

Thirteenth, if the manufactured home is abandoned and has a value of less than three thousand dollars and if there is no identified interested person, after servin g the notices required under R.C. 2923.13, the bailiff may cause the destruction of the home or the clerk of court may issue a certificate of title of the home to the park operator, free and clear of all security interests, liens, and encumbrances. R.C. 1 923.14(B)(4).

The clerk shall issue the certificate of title in the manner prescribed in R.C. 4505.10.

Id.

Fourteenth, at any time prior to the issuance of the writ of execution, the titled owner of the manufactured home may remove the home from the park (or other place of storage) upon payment of all outstanding tax liens on the home and, unless the owner is indigent, all unpaid court costs in the eviction action. R.C. 1923.14(B)(5).

Fifteenth, after the issuance of the writ of execution, the titled own er of the manufactured home may remove the home from the park (or other place of storage) at any time up to the day before the scheduled sale, destruction, or transfer of ownership, upon payment of all moving and storage costs, all outstanding tax liens on the home, and, unless the owner is indigent, all unpaid court costs in the eviction action. Id.

Sixteenth, at any time after the issuance of the writ of execution, the holder of any outstanding lien, right, title, or interest in the manufactured home may stop the summary sales process by: (1) commencing a proceeding to repossess the home under R.C. Chapters 1309 and 1317, and (2) then paying all outstanding monthly rent due the park operator, for the period between the issuance of the writ and the sale o f the property under R.C. Chapters 1309 and 1317. R.C. 1923.14(B)(2),(6).

#### 5. R.C. 1923.15 Remedial Order

R.C. 1923.15 authorizes the court, during an eviction action, to order an appropriate governmental agency ( e.g., local housing department, county gener al health district) to inspect the premises and determine whether they are in compliance with the requirements of R.C. 5321.04 or R.C. 4781.38 (former R.C. 3733.10). E.g., Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. v. Russell , No. 7 -89-CVG-336 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Aug. 18, 1989); Marino v. Smart, No. 7-88-CVG-510 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Dec. 13, 1988).

The court may order this inspection at any time during the eviction action. For example, in Marino, the court held that it had jurisdiction to order, and t hen ordered, an R.C. 1923.15 inspection at the same time that it granted a motion to dismiss the
complaint. The Marino court dismissed the eviction claim as moot because the tenant had vacated the premises; it dismissed the monetary claim because of the l andlord’s failure to amend the complaint to cure its noncompliance with Civil Rule 10(D).

If the governmental agency determines and the court finds that the premises are not in compliance with those requirements, R.C. 1923.15 authorizes the court to issue certain remedial orders in the eviction action. The court may issue these remedial orders even if the tenant has not filed a counterclaim. Saddoris v. Sterling, No. L-89- 062, 1989 WL 135301, at *3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 9, 1989).

An R.C. 1923.15 rem edial order may forbid the landlord from re -renting the premises after the tenant has vacated, unless and until the R.C. 5321.04 or R.C. 4781.38 (former R.C. 3733.10) violations are corrected. E.g., Kerrigan v. Campbell, No. 21- CVH-01039 (Ohio Mun., Akron , May 18, 2021); Fisher v. Bly , No. 03 -CVG-19705 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 14, 2003);Wilburn v. Watson, No. 81-CVG- 64993 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Sept. 15, 1981); Irvin v. Carson, No. 79- CV-471 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Feb. 21, 1979).

101 This remedial order may be entered in cases in which the tenant already has vacated the premises and in cases in which, on the landlord’s eviction claim, the court enters a judgment for the landlord. R.C. 1923.15. See also Papadelis v. French, No. 89-CVG- 3932 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Sept. 11, 1989).

This remedial order also may be entered on a conditional basis in cases in which, on the landlord’s eviction claim, the court enters a judgment for the tenant. Saddoris
v. Sterling, No. L-89-062, 1989 WL135301, at *3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 9, 1989).

In such cases, the remedial order becomes effective if the tenant decides to vacate the premises, notwithstanding the tenant’s right to remain in possession. Id.

When a court enters this remedial order , if the landlord re -rents the premises in violation of the order, the former tenant may file a contempt motion against the
landlord. E.g., Papadelis v. French, No. 89-CVG-3932 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Sept. 11, 1989).

An R.C. 1923.15 remedial order also may require the landlord to correct the R.C.

5321.04 or R.C.4781.38 (former R.C. 3733.10) violations.E.g., Kerrigan v. Campbell,
No. 21-CVH-01039 (Ohio Mun., Akron, May 18, 2021). This remedial order may be issued in cases in which, on the landlord’s eviction claim, the court enters a judgment for the tenant. R.C. 1923.15. E.g., Saddoris v. Sterling , No. L -89-062, 1989 WL 135301, at *3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 9, 1989). When a court issues this remedial order, it also may appoint a housing specialist to monitor the landlord’s compliance with the order. Saddoris, 1989 WL 135301, at *3.

In conjunction with any R.C. 1923.15 remedial order, if the court finds that the tenant caused the R.C. 5321.04 or R.C. 4781.38 (former R.C. 3733.10) violations, “the court may award damages to the landlord equal to the reasonable cost of correcting such conditions.” R.C. 1923.15.

### P. Post-Judgment Motions


#### 1. Civil Rule 59 Motion

Civil Rule 59 applies to eviction actions. Witkowski v. Arditi, 123 Ohio App.3d 26, 702 N.E.2d 1231(7th Dist. 1997) (ruling on time for filing the motion in an eviction action); Agler Green Coop. v. Rivers , No. 87AP -915, 1988 WL 37063 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 29, 1988);Mt. Olive-Metro Hous., Inc. v. Craft, No. 93-G-2858 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Feb. 28, 1994); Greenfield Meadows v. Malone, No. M9111- CVG-040019 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 17, 1992).But see Summit Mgmt. Servs.
v. Gough , No. 19714, 2000 WL 1226605 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 30, 2000); 83 Zellner v. Turner , No. 92 -CA-41, 1993 W L 15640 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 26, 1993).84 In eviction actions, a Civil Rule 59 motion is often used to contest the court’s conclusions of law, similar to a Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(b) objection to a magistrate’s
decision. However, to preserve the right to appeal the trial court’s decision in an eviction action tried to a magistrate, a party must file a Civil Rule 53(D)(3)(b) objection regarding the relevant conclusion of law (or finding of fact). See Section I.N.3, above.

#### 2. Civil Rule 60(B) Motion

Civil Rule 60(B) applies to eviction actions when its application is consistent with the summary nature of eviction proceedings. Consol. Mgmt., Inc. v. R.C.T., Inc., Nos.

2001-L-153,156, 2002 WL 31862650 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 20, 2002);Lewis v.

Powers, No. 15461, 1997 WL 335563 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 13, 1997); Owners
Mgmt. Co. v. Osborne , No. 15398, 1992 WL 174640 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 22, 1992); Norris v. Red Barn Rests., Inc. , No. 13652, 1989 WL 1655 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 11, 1989); Agler Green Coop. v. Rivers , No. 87AP -915, 1988 WL 37063 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 29, 1988); Larson v. Umoh , 33 Ohio App.3d 14, 514 N.E.2d 145 (8th Dist. 1986). See also Mitchell v. Walker, No. C-050546 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 6, 2006); Urban Hollow Apts. v. Dunning , No. 93AP -830, 1994 WL 55522 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 22, 1994);Hampshire Heights, Inc. v. Van Wormer, 17 Ohio Op.3d 287 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 1980).

When a Civil Rule 60(B) motion is filed before the execution of the writ of restitution, generally the application of Civil Rule 60(B) is consistent with the summary nature of eviction proceedings. E.g., Norris, 1989 WL 1655, at *2; Rivers, 1988 WL 37063, at *1; CMHA v. Holliman , No. 2003 -CVG-019864 (Ohio Mun., 83 The Gough decision relied solely onZellner v. Turner, No. 92-CA-41, 1993 WL 15640 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 26, 1993), which is a decision that may have been overruled for the reason stated in note 84, below. Also, the Gough decision is inconsistent with the court’s e arlier decisions that applied Civil Rule 60(B) to eviction actions. See Owners Mgmt. Co. v. Osborne,
No. 15398, 1992 WL 174640 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 22, 1992); Norris v. Red Barn Rests., Inc., No. 13652, 1989 WL 1655 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 11, 1989).

84 This appellate district arguably overruled this Civil Rule 59 decision when it later held, in Lewis v. Powers, No. 15461, 1997 WL 335563 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 13, 1997), that Civil Rule 60(B) applies to FED actions.

103 Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 12, 2003); Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Mitchell, No. 91- CVG-840 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, June 4, 1991). See also Osborne, 1992 WL 174640, at *1-2; Dunning, 1994 WL 55522, at *1-3.

When a Civil Rule 60(B) motion is filed after the execution of the writ of restitution, generally the application of Civil Rule 60(B) is not consistent with the summary nature of eviction proceedings. Larson v. Umoh , 33 Ohio App.3d 14, 17, 514 N.E.2d 145 (8th Dist. 1986). E.g., Consol. Mgmt., Inc. v. R.C.T., Inc., Nos. 2001- L-153,156, 2002 WL 31862650 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 20, 2002) (one and one- half years after judgment and tenant’s vacation, and one year after building had been razed).

The execution of the writ of restitution is not an absolute bar to a Civil Rule 60(B)
motion. Larson, 33 Ohio App.3d at 17. E.g., Mitchell v. Walker, No. C-050546 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 6, 2006) (reversed denial and remanded for hearing, where motion filed five months after tenant was “physically evicted”); Mt. Olive-Metro Hous., Inc.
v. Craft, No. 93-G-2858 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Feb. 28, 1994).

But “the trial court should weigh such delay heavily against granting relief from an eviction judgment.” Larson, 33 Ohio App.3d at 17. Two of the factors that courts consider in determining whether to grant a Civil Rule 60(B) motion after the execution of the writ of restitution are the length of the delay and any justification for the delay.

Id.

As a condition for granting the tenant’s Civil Rule 60(B) motion, the court may require the tenant to deposit with the court all rent then due and owing and to deposit with the court, as it becomes due, the rent that becomes due pending the court’s decision on the motion. Deerwood Mgmt. Co. v. Bernsdorf, No. 78AP-470, 1978 Ohio
App. LEXIS 10021 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 28, 1978). The authority for imposing this rent deposit condition is the Civil Rule 60(B) provision that permits a court to relieve a party from a final judgment “upon such terms as are just.” Id.

When a rent deposit condition has been imposed and the tenant fails to deposit rent with the court as ordered, the tenant’s noncompliance with the rent deposit condition is a permissible ground for denying the Civil Rule 60(B) motion and thereupon issuing a writ of restitution. Id.

In an eviction action, one common reason for a tenant’s Civil Rule 60(B) motion is the tenant’s failure to appear at trial because of transportation problems.E.g., Urban Hollow Apts. v. Dunning, No. 93AP-830, 1994 WL 55522 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb.

22, 1994); Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Mitchell , No. 91 -CVG-840 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, June 4, 1991).

The Dunning court found excusable neglect where the tenant failed to appear at trial because: (1) she did not own a car, (2) she had arranged to obtain a ride to the trial but that person failed to appear, (3) she made other transportation arrangements, (4) she called the court and left a message explaining why she was late and that she was on her way, and (5) she arrived one hour late.

The Mitchell court found that relief was appropriate under Civil Rule 60(B)(5) where the tenant failed to appear at trial because: (1) she did not own a car, (2) she had difficulty arranging transportation, (3) she was ten minutes late for the trial, (4) she sat in the courtroom to which she was directed and forty minutes later, when asked the reason for her presence, she first learned that the trial had proceeded in her absence.85 Another common reason for a tenant’s Civil Rule 60(B) motion is the tenant’s tender and the landlord’s acceptance of one or more future monthly rent payments after the entry of the eviction judgment and, thereafter, the landlord’s attempt to enforce the eviction judgment.

In these circumstances, the landlord’s acceptance of the future monthly rent payment(s) creates a new month -to-month tenancy between the landlo rd and tenant.

Showe Mgmt. v. Malone, No. 07-CVG-02191, slip op. at 4 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty.,
Oct. 23, 2007) (“the fact that Plaintiff continued to allow her to live there and accepted future payments created a new tenancy”). See also Bill Swad Chevrolet, Inc. v. Ricart Jeep Eagle, Inc., 129 Ohio App.3d 501, 718 N.E.2d 470 (10th Dist. 1998) (landlord’s acceptance of rent from holdover tenant creates a new tenancy); Steiner v. Minkowski, 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist. 1991) (same); Craig Wrecking Co. v.

Loewendick & Sons, Inc. , 38 Ohio App.3d 79, 526 N.E.2d 321 (10th Dist. 1987) (same). See generally FMJ Props. v. Hinton, No. 50314, 1986 WL 4396, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 10, 1986) (landlord’s acceptance of rent from month -to-month tenant renews the month-to-month tenancy).

The new tenancy confers upon the tenant the right to remain in possession of the premises notwithstanding the outstanding eviction judgment. If the landlord attempts to enforce the eviction judgment and thereby deprive the tenant of this newly created 85 The Mitchell court found that these circumstances do not constitute inadvertence or excusable neglect because, “knowing of a transportation problem, it was her responsibility to take steps to insure her prompt arrival.” Mitchell, slip op. at 3.

105 possessory right, the court should grant the tenant’s Civil Rule 60(B)(4) or 60(B)(5)
motion. E.g., Showe Mgmt. v. Malone, No. 07-CVG-02191 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty.,
Oct. 23, 2007).

#### 3. Motion to Vacate Judgment

A judgment rendered by a court lacking subject -matter jurisdiction or personal jurisdiction is void ab initio. State ex rel. Ballard v. O’Donnell, 50 Ohio St.3d 182, 183, 553 N.E.2d 650 (1990) (personal jurisdiction); Patton v. Diemer, 35 Ohio St.3d 68, 70, 518 N.E.2d 941 (1988) (subject-matter jurisdiction). “[T]he authority to vacate a void judgment is not derived from Civ.R. 60(B), but rather constitutes an inherent power possessed by Ohio courts.” Patton v. Diemer, 35 Ohio St.3d 68, 70, 518 N.E.2d 941 (1988).

Therefore, a motion to vacate the eviction judgment is the appropriate remedy when the court lacked personal jurisdiction over the tenant because of the court’s failure to serve the summons and complaint in conformity with the applicable law. 86 G.K.G. Builders, Inc. v. Burgess, 13 N.E.2d 745, 2014-Ohio-2431, ¶ 7 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 6, 2014). E.g., Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Stuckey , No. L -92-102, 1993 WL 315626 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 13, 1993). Cf. Corley v. Sullivan -Busman, No.

99420, 2013 WL 4976381, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 23, 2013) (“Where a defendant seeking a motion to vacate (a void judgment) makes an uncontradicted sworn statement that he never received service of the complaint, he is entitled to have the judgement against [him] vacated”).

Likewise, a motion to vacate the eviction judgment is the appropriate remedy when the court lacked subject -matter jurisdiction because of the landlord’s failure to serve properly an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate (see Section I.C.1.b, above) . Eureka Multifamily Group v. Terrell, No. L-14-1152, 2015 WL 2329168, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 15, 2015). See also MLR Props., Inc. v. Hemmelgarn, No. L-94-349, 1995 WL 504629 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 25, 1995);Oda v. Davis, No. 13985, 1994 WL 444758 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Aug. 17, 1994).

When the court lacked subject-matter jurisdiction, a motion to vacate the eviction judgment should be granted even if the eviction judgment is a consent entry. “[T]he issue of subject matter jurisdiction c annot be waived.” State ex rel. Wilson-Simmons 86 For a discussion on the appli cable law on service of process in an FED action, see Section I.G.3, above.
v. Lake County Sheriff’s Dep’t, 82 Ohio St.3d 37, 40, 693 N.E.2d 789 (1998). “The parties may not, by stipulation or agreement, confer subject -matter jurisdiction on a court, where subject-matter jurisdiction is otherwise lacking.” Fox v. Eaton, 48 Ohio St.2d 236, 238, 358 N.E.2d 536 (1976), overruled on other grounds, Manning v. Ohio State Library Bd., 62 Ohio St.3d 24, 577 N.E.2d 650 (1991). Accord Bernard Group
v. New Hope Alternative Therapy Research , 153 Ohio App.3d 393, 396, 794 N.E.2d 141 (8th Dist. 2003); Myers v. Clinebell, No. S-98-048, 1999 WL 300620, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 14, 1999); State ex rel. Richard v. Cuyahoga County Bd. of Comm’rs, 100 Ohio App.3d 592, 595, 654 N.E.2d 443 (8th Dist. 1995).

#### 4. Motion to Amend Agreed Judgment

Judgments by consent are permissible. Wallick Props. Midwest, LLC v. Jama, No.

20AP-299, 2021 WL 3629962,¶ 10 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Aug. 17, 2021). An agreed judgment entry is a contract. Id. Therefore, an agreed judgment entry is subject to the same rules of construction as a contract. Id.

“The court’s inherent power to modify a consent decree or other injunction is now encompassed in Rule 60(b)(5) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure,” Thompson v.

U.S. Dep’t of Hous. & Urban Dev., 404 F.3d 821, 826 (4th Cir. 2005), and thus in Rule 60(B)(4) of the Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure. Ohio Civil Rule 60(B)(4) is virtually the same as Federal Civil Rule 60(b)(5).

The standard for determining whether to modify a consent decree because of a change in circumstances is set forth inRufo v. Inmates of Suffolk County Jail, 502 U.S.

367 (1992). Cmty. Dev. Props. Cleveland, Inc. v. Griffin , No. 87954, 2007 WL 613956, ¶¶ 19 -24 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 1, 2007). The party seeking the modification bears the initial burden of establishing that a significant change in circumstances, in the facts or the law, warrants a modification of the decree. Id. A significant change in facts warrants a modification of the decree if the change was unforeseen and, as a result of the change, the decree proves to be unworkable. Id. If the moving party meets this initial burden, the court must then determine whether the proposed modification is suitably tailored to the changed circumstances. Id.

An example of a court applying theRufo standard in granting a motion to amend a consent decree in an eviction action isCMHA v. Matthew, No. 2007-CVG-16146 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 30, 2008). The court modified a consent decree so as to extend the time period for the tenant to tender unpaid rent. The court relied on a significant change in facts, namely: as a result of a recent strain on social service 107 agencies due to the foreclosure crisis, the tenant was unable to foresee that she would be unable to get an appointment and receive financial assistance from a social service agency within the agreed upon time period for tendering the unpaid rent.

### Q. Appeal


#### 1. Introduction

An appeal from an eviction judgment generally is subject to the same requirements and restrictions as an appeal in other civil actions. Matters of particular significance in a tenant’s appeal from an eviction judgment include the final appealable order requirement (see Section I.Q.2, below), the stay and bond requirements (see Section I.Q.3, below), the mootness restriction (see Section I.Q.4, below), and the appeal time period, if a post-judgment motion or objection is filed (see Section I.Q.5, below).

The tenant’s decision on whether to appeal from an eviction judgment must be made on the merits of the appeal and not solely to provide the tenant with more time to reside in the premises. If a court finds that a notice of appeal is filed solely to provide the tenant with more time to reside in the premises, the filing of the notice of appeal may constitute bad faith conduct in violation of R.C. 2323.51, even if there is a legal foundation to the appeal. E.g., Hildreth v. Mims , 70 Ohio App.3d 282, 590 N.E.2d 1353 (8th Dist. 1990). Cf. 217 Williams, LLC v. Worthen , No. C-180101, 2019 WL 2613331 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 26, 2019) (filed objections in FED action in order to delay the eviction of the tenant).

#### 2. Final Appealable Order

An eviction action often includes an eviction claim and one or more non-eviction
claims. The complaint may allege an eviction claim and a monetary claim. See Section I.E.2.f, above. The tenant may file counterclaims. See Section I.I, above.

Furthermore, the court may decide the eviction claim separate from the non - eviction claims, and the court may enter an eviction judgment before it enters a judgment on the non-eviction claims. See Section I.M.1, above.

In such cases: (1) the eviction judgment is a final appealable order, Witkowski v.

Arditi, 123 Ohio App.3d 26, 702 N.E.2d 1231 (7th Dist. 1997); Skillman v. Browne, 68 Ohio App.3d 615, 589 N.E.2d 407 (6th Dist. 1990),87 and (2) Civil Rule 54(B) does not apply, Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jackson , 67 Ohio St.2d 129, 423 N.E.2 d 177 (1981).88 Consequently, in any eviction action, the parties are permitted to appeal immediately from the eviction judgment, even if one or more non -eviction claims are still pending in the case.

At least one appellate court has held that, in these ca ses, the appeal from the eviction judgment may be filed either within thirty days from the eviction judgment or within thirty days from the order that disposes of the remaining claims.Knop v. Davet,
No. 2016-G-0074, 2017 WL 1404477, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Apr. 17, 2017).

#### 3. Stay and Supersedeas Bond

When a tenant appeals from an eviction judgment, if the tenant seeks to stay enforcement of the judgment pending the appeal, the tenant is required to post a supersedeas bond. Ohio R.Civ. P. 62(B); R.C. 2505.09, 2505.14. See also Miami Inv.
Corp. v. Baker, 109 Ohio App. 334, 165 N.E.2d 690 (2nd Dist. 1959) (land installment contract eviction). Cf. Winton Sav. & Loan Co. v. Eastfork Trace, Inc. , 119 Ohio Misc.2d 83, 774 N.E.2d 355 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty., 2001) (foreclosure).

If a supersedeas bond is not posted, the landlord and the trial court are permitted 87 See also Knop v. Davet, No. 2016-G-0074, 2017 WL 1404477 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Apr.

17, 2017); Colbert v. McLemore, No. 81961, 2003 WL 21434169, ¶ 1 n.1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 19, 2003); Cleveland Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Crowder, No. 79355, 2002 WL 192083, at *3 n.2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 7, 2002);Blosser v. Bowman, No. 00AP-1140, 2001 WL 436088, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 1, 2001); Oakwood Mgmt. Co. v. Richards , No. 99AP-627, 1999 WL 1124692, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999); Allman v. Simmers, No. 1999- AP-030014, 1999 WL 1072200, at *1 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 16, 1999); Bryant v. Dale,
No. 98-CA-36, 1999 WL 731824, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 10, 1999); Sholiton Indus.,
Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd. , No. 17480, 1999 WL 355898, at *8 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 4, 1999); Swaney v. Syndicate Mgmt., Inc. , No. 71422, 1997 WL 209223, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 24, 1997); Dayton Inv. Group -7 v. Lathern, No. 15866, 1996 WL 741822, at *1 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 13, 1996).

88 See also Oneida Props., Inc. v. Pickett, No. 24626, 2009 WL 3119647 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,
Sept. 30, 2009); Bryant v. Dale, No. 98-CA-36, 1999 WL 731824 (Ohio App., 4th Dist ., Sept.

10, 1999); Crossings Dev. Ltd. P’ship v. H.O.T., Inc. , 96 Ohio App.3d 475, 645 N.E.2d 159 (9th Dist. 1994); Chautauqua Park Apts. v. McMullen, No. 791, 1992 WL 303139 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Oct. 14, 1992).

109 to execute the writ of restitution, notwithstanding the pending appeal. 89 First Bank Nat’l Ass’n v. Willis, Nos. 56263,56763, 1989 WL 69409, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 22, 1989); Miami Inv. Corp. v. Baker, 109 Ohio App. 334, 336, 165 N.E.2d 690 (2nd Dist. 1959). See also Coshocton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Dockery, No. 99-CA-24, 2000 WL 968691, at *1 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Ju ly 5, 2000); Reese Invs. v. Tucker ,
No. 89AP-1189, 1990 WL 26119, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 13, 1990).

Under Civil Rule 62(B), the posting of an adequate supersedeas bond is the only requirement for obtaining a stay of execution pending appeal. State ex rel. Ocasek v.

Riley, 54 Ohio St.2d 488, 490, 377 N.E.2d 792 (1978).

When a stay of execution is granted and the supersedeas bond is posted, the judge “shall issue his [stay] order to the sheriff, police officer, constable, or bailiff commanding him to delay all further proceedings upon the execution.” R.C. 1923.14.

Furthermore, where the premises have been restored to the landlord before the stay is granted and the supersedeas bond is posted, “the sheriff, police officer, constable, or bailiff shall forthwith place the defendant [tenant] in possession of them ....” Id. See Bowling Green Manor Ltd. P’ship v. Kirk, No. 94-WD-125 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan.

12, 1995); Crossings Dev. Ltd. P’ship v. H.O.T., Inc., 96 Ohio App.3d 475, 645 N.E.2d 159 (9th Dist. 1994).

The supersedeas bond must initially be sought from the trial court. Ohio R. App.
P. 7(A). See Mathews v. Cooper, No. 109974, 2021 WL 3560200,¶ 9 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 12, 2021); Bibb v. Home Sav. & Loan Co., 63 Ohio App.3d 751, 752, 580 N.E.2d 52 (6th Dist. 1989). The trial court is required to rule on a tenant’s timely request for a supersedeas bond before the writ of restitution has been executed.Martin
v. Mohler, No. 89-CA-1859, 1991 WL 28306, at *1 n.2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist.,Feb. 12, 1991).

If the trial court denies the tenant’s request for a supersedeas bond altogether, or fails to order the type of bond (i.e., amount or method of payment) that the tenant has requested, the tenant may then seek a stay and a supersedeas bond from the court of
appeals. Ohio R. App. P. 7(A). See Eagle View Apts. v. Pride, No. CT06-0050 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 10, 2006);Clayton Jaycee Hous. v. Salmon, No. 2006AP-060032 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 26, 2006). However, “[d]etermining the need for the bond and its amount are discretionary matters which will not be overturned by the appellate 89 The execution of the writ of restitutio n, in turn, may cause the appeal to become moot. See Section I.Q.4, below.

court absent a showing of an abuse of discretion.” Bibb v. Home Sav. & Loan Co., 63 Ohio App.3d 751, 752, 580 N.E.2d 52 (6th Dist. 1989).Accord Bowling Green Manor
Ltd. P’ship v. Kirk, No. 94 -WD-125 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 12, 1995); Bowling Green Manor v. LaChance, No. 94-WD-117 (Ohio App., 6th Dist. Dec. 7, 1994). Cf.

Gold Key Realty v. Collins , No. 2013 -CA-12, 2013 WL 4041173, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Aug. 9, 2013) (bond for stay pending ruling on objections). Because of the deference accorded a trial court’s exercise of discretionary authority, the court of appeals may be more likely to grant a tenant’s motion for a stay and supersedeas bond when the trial court has simply denied the tenant’s motion, than when the trial court has ordered a supersedeas bond but it is not the type of bond that the tenant sought.

Notwithstanding this abuse of discretion standard, in several cases in which the trial court ordered a supersedeas bond in the amount of $2,000 or $3,000, the court of appeals reduced the amount of the supersedeas bond to a use and occupancy bond. 90 E.g., Bowling Green Manor Ltd. P’ship v. Kirk, No. 94-WD-125 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Jan. 1 2, 1995) ($3,000 supersedeas bond reduced to a use and occupancy bond);

Gorsuch Homes v. Wooten, No. 2856 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 14, 1991) ($2,000 supersedeas bond reduced to a use and occupancy bond); Forest City Mgmt., Inc. v.

Lauderback, No. 91 -CA-1972 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 15, 1991) ($2,000 supersedeas bond reduced to a use and occupancy bond). See also Portage Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Brumley , No. 2008 -P-0019 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 11, 2008) ($4,902 supersedeas bond reduced to $100 per month). But see Bowling Green Manor
Ltd. P’ship v. LaChance, No. 94-WD-117 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 7, 1994) (upheld $4,250 supersedeas bond and denied request for use and occupancy bond); Alpha Phi Alpha Home, Inc. v. Marshall , No. 96-CA-200, 1999 WL 1 48365, at *1 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar. 4, 1999) (upheld supersedeas bond of $1,524, which was rental arrearage, plus all future rent as it becomes due).

The supersedeas bond must be sufficient to pay “all money costs and damages” that the appellee may be awarded in the appeal. R.C. 2505.14.

When a tenant appeals from an eviction judgment, the “landlord may recover on such an appeal bond only those damages occasioned by the delay in enforcing the eviction order.” Langford v. Danolfo, No. 43917, 1982 WL 5 265, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 1, 1982). Ordinarily, these potential damages to the landlord (and thus 90 A use and occupancy bond requires a tenant to pay to the court or the landlord each month, during the pendency of the appeal, the amount of rent that is due for the month.

111 the proper amount of the supersedeas bond) are solely the loss of rent during the appeal.

The amount of these supersedeas bonds is not limited to the loss of rent during the pendency of the appeal in all cases. Tuteur v. P. & F. Enters., Inc. , 21 Ohio App.2d 122, 127, 255 N.E.2d 284 (8th Dist. 1970). “Consideration of additional expenses or burdens caused by the delay in returning possession is a legitimate item in the computation of [these] damages.” Id. at 130. But the amount of any alleged rental arrearage generally is not covered by the supersedeas bond because it is not related to the la ndlord’s potential damages resulting solely from the delay due to the appeal.

T&R Props. Inc. v. Wimberly, No. 2019-CVG-029217 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 4, 2021). And lost profits from a prospective purchase, and interest that would have been received on the prospective purchase price, are not covered by the supersedeas
bond. Tuteur v. P. & F. Enters., Inc., 21 Ohio App.2d at 130.

For an assisted housing landlord, 91 this potential loss of rent is generally the tenant’s portion of the contract rent, not the full contract rent. See Forest City Mgmt.,
Inc. v. Lauderback, No. 91-CA-1972 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 15, 1991). See also Lovejoy v. Intervest Corp., 794 So.2d 1205 (Ala. App. 2001); Tullahoma Vill. Apts. v.

Cyree, No. 85 -206-II, 1986 WL 16 64 (Tenn. App. Feb. 7, 1986). Cf. Brown v. Fay Apts., 62 Ohio Misc.2d 188, 594 N.E.2d 190 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1991) (R.C.

1923.08 bond).

After the court has determined the amount of the landlord’s potential damages from the granting of a stay of execution, it must determine the form of the bond to be
imposed. Courts have discretion to tailor the requisites and conditions of the bond to the needs of the litigation. T&R Props. Inc. v. Wimberly , No. 2019 -CVG-029217 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 4, 2021) (cites this book’s fifth editiondiscussion on this issue); 4 Ohio Jur. 3d Appellate Review § 229 (2011).

In an eviction action, generally the court will demand one of two basic types of
bonds. T&R Props. Inc. v. Wimberly , No. 2019-CVG-029217 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 4, 2021) (cites this book’s fifth edition discussion on this issue). One is a use and occupancy bond, whereby the tenant is required to pay to the court or the landlord each month, during the pendency of the appeal, the amount of rent that is due for the month. Id. The other is a lump sum bond in the amount of the rent that is likely to become due during the pendency of the appeal. Id.

91 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, below.

In most eviction cases, especially those involving low-income tenants, the use and occupancy bond has been deemed the appropriate type of supersedeas bond.E.g., J&V
Props. Mgmt. v. Link, No. 19-CA-6, 2019 WL 5173790, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 5th Dist.,
Oct. 11, 2019); Kettering Square Apts. v. Crawford , Nos. 27504,27545,27548, 2017 WL 6398837, ¶ 3 (Ohio App., 2 nd Dist., Dec. 15, 2017); Showe Mgmt. Corp. v.

Hazelbaker, No. CA2006-01-004, 2006 WL 3479856, ¶3 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec.

4, 2006); Classic A Props. v. Brown, No. 02-CA-2868 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 18, 2002); Cipolla v. McCloskey, No. 97-CA-006866, 1998 WL 852810, at *1 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 9, 1998); Julian Invs., Inc. v. Dudley, No. 98-CA-85, 1999 WL 64227, at *1 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 12, 1999); Reck v. Whalen , No. 95 -CA-66 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 17, 1995); Bowling Green Manor Ltd. P’ship v. Kirk, No. 94- WD-125 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 12, 1995); Gorsuch Homes v. Wooten, No. 2856 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 14, 1991);Forest City Mgmt., Inc. v. Lauderback, No. 91- CA-1972 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 15, 1991); BancPlus Mortgage Corp. v. Smith ,
No. 57641 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 9, 1989); Aultman Home for Aged Women v.

Ott, No. CA-7688 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 21, 1988);Novak v. Jackson, No. 38654 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 1, 1 977); Terrell v. Randall, No. 38341 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 11, 1977);Cent. Park Place v. McDowell, No. 33242 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Nov. 29, 1973); Burton v. Murry, No. 33011 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 5, 1973).But see Coshocton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Dockery, No. 99-CA-24, 2000 WL 968691 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 5, 2000) ($2,000 bond, assisted housing); Reese Invs. v. Tucker,
No. 89AP-1181, 1990 WL 26119 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 13, 1990) ($1,112 bond, assisted housing). But cf. Gold Key R ealty v. Collins , No. 2013 -CA-12, 2013 WL 4041173, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Aug. 9, 2013) (bond for stay pending ruling on objections; $637 per month (market rent), assisted housing).

The obvious merit and inherent fairness of the use and occupancy bo nd is that it allows any tenant, regardless of the tenant’s financial means, to pursue an appeal without jeopardizing the protection due the landlord. A use and occupancy bond adequately protects the landlord because, if the tenant misses a monthly rent payment, the court may vacate the stay of execution.

In contrast, if a court refuses to permit a use and occupancy bond and, instead, requires a low-income tenant to post a lump sum bond in the amount of several months’ rent, the tenant is effectively denie d the right to appeal. Most low -income tenants cannot acquire that amount of money at one time.

A court’s refusal to permit a use and occupancy bond may violate a low -income 113 tenant’s due process and/or equal protection rights.See Usher v. Waters Ins. & Realty Co., Inc., 438 F. Supp. 1215 (W.D.N.C. 1977);Jack Spring, Inc. v. Little, 50 Ill.2d 351, 280 N.E.2d 208 (1972); Harrington v. Harrington , 269 A.2d 310 (Me. 1970). See generally Lindsey v. Normet, 405 U.S. 56 (1972). Cf. M.L.B. v. S.L.J., 519 U.S. 102 (1996). But see Letendre v. Fugate, 701 F.2d 1093 (4th Cir. 1983). But cf. Bankers Life & Cas. Co. v. Crenshaw, 486 U.S. 71 (1988).

Because “the purpose of the supersedeas bond is [to] ensure an appellee can be made whole if the appellant is not successful with the appeal,” when the appellant is successful on the appeal, the appellee does “not have a legal basis to recover against the deposited bond money.” T&R Props. Inc. v. Wimberly , No. 2019-CVG-029217, slip op. at 3 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 4, 2021). In these cases, “[l]ogically, the appeal did not delay plaintiff’s enforcement of the eviction judgment, since the judgment was reversed.” Id.

#### 4. Mootness

In an eviction action, when the tenant appeals from the eviction judgment, a common issue is whether the tenant’s vacation of the premises during the pendency of the appeal makes the appeal moot.

The tenant’s vacation of the premises may be voluntary. E.g., Blosser v. Bowman,
No. 00AP-1140, 2001 WL 436088, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 1, 2001). Cf.

Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Moore , No. 2007-CA-00102, 2009 WL 1372988 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 14, 2009) (tenant vacated pending landlord’s appeal).

Alternatively, the tenant’s vacation of the premises may be involuntary. An involuntary vacation usually is the result of one of four circumstances:
(1) the tenant’s failure to request a stay of execution,e.g., Art Paradise, Inc.
v. Washington, No. 29230, 2021 WL 6136347, ¶¶ 2,6 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 29, 2021);Tiefenbacher v. Shorter, No. L-20-1186, 2021 WL 3239856, ¶ 22 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., July 30, 2021); Kell v. Little, No.

2018-CA-00064, 2019 WL 192788, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 5th Dist ., Jan. 14, 2019); Hussain v. Sheppard, No. 14AP-686, 2015 WL 915467, ¶¶ 3,6- 10 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 24, 2015); Millennia Hous. Mgmt., Ltd.
v. Withrow, No. 12-CA-2, 2013 WL 398944, ¶¶ 2,7,8 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Jan. 28, 2013); Miami Valley Hous. v. Jackson, No. 25020, 2012 WL 5382834, ¶¶ 4-7 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 2, 2012);AVB Props., LLC v. Chesler, No. 05-CA-008702, 2006 WL 2390243, ¶¶ 14-16 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 21, 2006).
(2) the court’s denial of the tenant’s request for a stay of exe cution, e.g., Miller v. Johnson, No. 109453, 2021 WL 631242, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 18, 2021); Lake Metro. Hous. Auth. v. McFadden, No. 2016- L-105, 2017 WL 1683178, ¶¶ 6-8 (Ohio App., 11th Dist.,May 1, 2017);

Gara v. Gara , No. 26671, 2015 WL 6410285, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 23, 2015); Riolo v. Navin, No. 79809, 2002 WL 502408, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 4, 2002); Witkowski v. Arditi, 123 Ohio App.3d 26, 28-30, 702 N.E.2d 1231 (7th Dist. 1997).
(3) the tenant’s failure to post the supersedeas bond on which the stay of execution is contingent, e.g., OIG Homes, LLC v. Stricklen , No. L-21- 1029, 2021 WL 4935720, ¶¶ 4-6 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 22, 2021);

AKP Props., LLC v. Rutledge, No. 2018-CA-00058, 2018 WL 6822573, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 21, 2018); Rithy Props., Inc. v.

Cheeseman, 63 N.E.3d 752, 2016-Ohio-1602, ¶¶ 9,10 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2016); Gromek v. Lepisto , No. 2015 -L-099, 2015 WL 5781653, ¶ 5 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 5, 2015); Valente v. Johnson, Nos. 06- CA-31,38, 2007 WL 1574769, ¶¶ 20,22 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., May 29, 2007); Long v. MacDonald, No. 3-02-10, 2002 WL 31007937, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 9, 2002); Coshocton Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Dockery, No. 99-CA-24, 2000 WL 968691, at *1 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 5, 2000); U.S. Sec’y of Hous. & Urban Dev. v. Chancellor , No.

73970, 1999 WL 126170, at *1-2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 25, 1999);

Reese Invs. v. Tucker , No. 89AP -1181, 1990 WL 26119, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 13, 1990).
(4) during a pending appeal, the landlord files a second eviction action against the same tenant, secures an eviction judgment, and enforces it, e.g., Park Lane Apts. v. Parks, No. L-20-1208, 2021 WL 4480879,¶¶ 5- 7 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Se pt. 30, 2021); Weikart v. Abbe, No. 02-CO- 32, 2003 WL 21377489 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 5, 2003); Reck v.

Whalen, 114 Ohio App.3d 16, 682 N.E.2d 721 (2nd Dist. 1996);see also Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Hazelbaker , No. CA2006 -01-004, 2006 WL 3479856 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 4, 2006).

The general rule is that, if the tenant vacates the premises (voluntarily or involuntarily), the tenant’s appeal from the eviction judgment is moot. Farla LLC v.

Pretlow, No. 21-CA-011731, 2021 WL 6050713, ¶ 5 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 20, 2021); OIG Homes, LLC v. Stricklen, No. L-21-1029, 2021 WL 4935720,¶¶ 2,3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 22, 2021);Rithy Props., Inc. v. Cheeseman, 63 N.E.3d 752, 2016- Ohio-1602, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2016). Gromek v. Lepisto, No. 2015-L-099, 2015 WL 5781653, ¶ 3 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 5, 2015); Hussain v. Sheppard, 115
No. 14AP-686, 2015 WL 915467, ¶¶ 6 -10 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 24, 2015);

Millennia Hous. Mgmt., Ltd. v. Withrow , No. 12 -CA-2, 2013 WL 398944, ¶¶ 2,7,8 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Jan. 28, 2013);Miami Valley Hous. v. Jackson, No. 25020, 2012 WL 5382834, ¶ 5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 2, 2012);Cherry v. Morgan, Nos. 2012- CA-11,21, 2012 WL 3253220, ¶ 4 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Aug. 10, 2012);Showe Mgmt.
Corp. v. Moore, No. 08-CA-10, 2009 WL 1372986, ¶ 36 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 14, 2009); Valente v. Johnson, Nos. 06-CA-31,38, 2007 WL 1574769, ¶¶ 20,22 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., May 29, 2007); Schwab v. Lattimore , 166 Ohio App.3d 12, 15, 848 N.E.2d 912 (1st Dist. 2006); AVB Props., LLC v. Chesler , No. 05-CA-008702, 2006 WL 2390243, ¶¶ 14-16 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 21, 2006);Goldstein v. Patel, Nos.

02-CA-008183,008199, 2003 WL 21976103, ¶¶ 4 -6 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 20, 2003); Tripp v. French, No. 02CA-0044-M, 2002 WL 31828886, ¶¶ 7 -9 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 18, 2002); Long v. MacDonald , No. 3 -02-10, 2002 WL 31007937, ¶¶ 8,9 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 9, 2002); Riolo v. Navin , No. 79809, 2002 WL 502408, at *1-2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 4, 2 002); Blosser v. Bowman, No. 00AP- 1140, 2001 WL 436088, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 1, 2001); U.S. Sec’y of
Hous. & Urban Dev. v. Chancellor, No. 73970, 1999 WL 126170, at *1-2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 25, 1999);Alex-Bell Oxford Ltd. P’ship v. Woods, No. 16038, 1998 WL 289028, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 5, 1998); Stacy v. Carr, No. CA-519, 1992 WL 37783, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Feb. 20, 1992). But see Huber Inv. Corp. v.

Chapman, No. 14593, 1995 WL 127896, at *1 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 22, 1995) (appeal not moot because tenant “left the premises only in response to an official court ordered eviction notice”).

When a tenant’s appeal from an eviction judgment is moot under the general rule, the court of appeals generally will overr ule the assignment(s) of error, dismiss the appeal, and/or affirm the trial court’s decision. Id.

There are six circumstances in which, notwithstanding the tenant’s vacation of the premises, appellate courts have permitted the tenant to prosecute the appe al from the eviction judgment. They are discussed below. Four of them – the first, second, third, and fourth, below– are circumstances in which the appeal is not moot, notwithstanding the tenant’s vacation of the premises. Two of them – the fifth and sixth, below – are circumstances in which the appeal is moot because of the tenant’s vacation of the premises, but the courts make an exception to the general mootness rule for certain reasons.

The first circumstance is when the tenant’s vacation of the p remises was involuntary, the tenant has not permanently relocated to another residence, and the premises remain vacant. Agler Green Coop. v. Rivers, No. 87AP-915, 1987 WL 31282 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 23, 1987).

The second circumstance is when the premises from which the tenant has vacated is project-based assisted housing.92 Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Moore, No. 08-CA-10, 2009 WL 1372986, ¶ 39 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 14, 2009); Chillicothe Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Anderson , No. 1406 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 18, 1987); Sandefur Mgmt.
Co. v. Minor, No. 84AP-220, 1985 WL 10250, at *2 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 18, 1985). But see Millennia Hous. Mgmt., Ltd. v. Withrow , No. 12 -CA-2, 2013 WL 398944, ¶¶ 12,13 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Jan. 28, 2013).

If the tenant prevails in the appeal, it can affect the tenant’s continuing eligibility for project-based assisted housing in three ways. First, it should provide the tenant with the right to lease the rental unit from which the tenant vacated, if it is available.

See Rivers, 1987 WL 31282, at *1. Second, if that rental unit has been re -rented, it should provide the tenant with the right to lease a comparable rental unit in the same assisted housing project as soon as a comparable unit becomes available. See New York City Hous. Auth., etc. v. Sullivan , 4 Misc.3d 131(A), 791 N.Y.S.2d 871 (Sup.
App. Term 2004). Third, “an unfavorable court proceeding couldaffect [the tenant’s] eligibility for future federal housing assistance.” Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Moore , No.

08-CA-10, 2009 WL 1372986, ¶ 39 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 14, 2009). See also
Hous. Auth. of City of Stamford v. Lamothe, 225 Conn. 757, 765, 627 A.2d 367 (1993).

The third circumstance is when the tenant occupied the premises from which the tenant has vacated under a Housing Choice Voucher (aka section 8 voucher) program93
tenancy. An eviction judgment, if based on an alleged lease violation, is grounds for terminating the family’s participation in the Housing Choice Voucher program. 24 C.F.R. §§ 982.551 (e), 982.552(c)(i). Because the eviction judgment carries these additional adverse legal consequences, the tenant’s vacation of the premises does not make the eviction appeal moot. Richmond’s Enter., Inc. v. Anderson, No. 26674, 2016 WL 685398 (Ohio App. , 2nd Dist., Feb. 19, 2016) ; Olympic Realty v. Zaleski , No.

11AP-668 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 29, 2012) (memorandum decision on application for reconsideration). See also Goff v. Brown, 715 N.W.2d 769, 2006 WL 92 For the definition of “project-based assisted housing,” see note 165, below.

93 For a summary of the Housing Choice Voucher program, see 24 C.F.R. § 982.1.

117 779904, at *2 (Iowa App. 2006).

The fourth circumstance is when the issue on appeal is capable of repetition yet evades review. T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly , 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020 -Ohio-4279, ¶¶ 10-13 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020); Millennia Hous. Mgmt., Ltd. v. Withrow, No.

12-CA-2, 2013 WL 398944, ¶¶ 9-11 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Jan. 28, 2013); Schwab v.

Lattimore, 166 Ohio App.3d 12, 848 N.E.2d 912, ¶¶ 12 -14 (1st Dist. 2006); Showe
Mgmt. Corp. v. Hazelbaker, No. CA2006-01-004, 2006 WL 3479856, ¶ 7 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 4, 2006); Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Martin , No. 1998 WL 100416, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998).

Two critical questions arise in connection with this mootness exception. The first is whether , in accordance with this exception’s requirements, there i s a reasonable expectation that the tenant will be subject to the same action again . See Schwab v.

Lattimore, 166 Ohio App.3d 12, 848 N.E.2d 912, ¶ 13 (1st Dist. 2006). The second is whether the eviction proceeding fails to meet the “evades review” requirement because of the opportunity to secure a supersedeas bond and stay (see Section I.Q.3, above).

Some court decisions have held that th e “evades review” requirement is not met because of the bond and stay provisions. E.g., Landings at Beckett Ridge v. Holmes,
No. CA2020-04-050, 2020 WL 7690283, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 28, 2020);

AKP Props., LLC v. Rutledge , No. 2018 -CA-00058, 2018 WL 6822573, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 21, 2018);Rithy Props., Inc. v. Cheeseman, 63 N.E.3d 752, 2016- Ohio-1602, ¶¶ 22,23 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2016).

Other court decisions have rejected this position on two alternative grounds. One is that “[t]enants faced with eviction due to failure to pay their rent will be unlikely to have the means to post a bond to obtain a stay of execution.” T&R Props., Inc. v.

Wimberly, 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020-Ohio-4279, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020). See also Olympic Realty v. Zaleski, No. 11AP-668 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 29, 2012) (memorandum decision on applic ation for reconsideration) . The second is that “the natural term of a tenant’s lease may expire during the pendency of an appeal, requiring the tenant to vacate the premises and allowing the landlord to assert the appeal is moot.” T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly, 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020-Ohio-4279, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020).

The Wimberly court found the exception applicable. It found the requisite capable of repetition based on the reasonable expectation that low -income tenants may be subject to an FED action again (and thus subject to the challenged affidavit practice again).

The Hazelbaker court, in holding the exception applicable, did not rely on (or
reference) any expectation that the complaining tenant might be subject in the future to the issue on appeal. Rather, it seemed to rely solely on its finding that the issue is “capable of repetition, yet will continue to evade review,” in other cases with other
litigants. Hazelbaker, 2006 WL 3479856, ¶ 7. The issue was whether, during the period that a first eviction judgment for a landlord is stayed pending an appeal, the landlord may file a second eviction action against the same tenant on new grounds, which arose after the filing of the first eviction action.

The Martin court, in holding the exception applicable, likewise relied solely on its finding that the issue on appeal is capa ble of repetition, yet will continue to evade review, in other cases with other litigants. Martin, 1998 WL 100416, at *2. The issue concerned rules governing the tenant’s right to a jury trial in an eviction action.

The Withrow court found the exception inapplicable because the tenant “ha[d] not shown that a reasonable likelihood exists that she will be subject to the same action again, i.e., having her lease terminated due to the presence of unauthorized persons.” Withrow, 2013 WL 398944, ¶ 11 The Schwab majority found the exception inapplicable because the tenant had not shown a reasonable expectation that the tenant would be subject to the same action again – a trial court’s failure to consider her counterclaim under R.C. 1923.061(B).

The Schwab dissent concluded that this factor is not an element of the exception and that the exception applies “when a situation is likely to occur repeatedly, albeit with different litigants.” Schwab, 166 Ohio App.3d at 18.

The fifth circumstance is when “there are other persons similarly situated who would benefit from the resolution of the issue on appeal.” Sandefur Mgmt. Co. v.

Minor, No. 84AP-220, 1985 WL 10250, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 18, 1985).

Accord Bowling Green Manor Ltd. P’ship v. LaChance , No. WD -94-117, 1995 WL 386496, at *3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 1995); Stacy v. Carr, No. CA-519, 1992 WL 37783, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Feb. 20, 1992).

This exception was applicable in Minor where the issue on appeal was whether, under the section 8 substantial rehabilitation program regulations, the tenant’s obligation to pay rent was contingent upon the landlord’s prompt recalculation of the tenant’s share of the rent. It was applicable in LaChance where the issue on appeal was whether good cause is required to terminate a tenancy under the Low Income Housing Tax Credit program. It was applicable in Carr where the issue on appeal concerned the right to a trial by jury in an eviction action.

119 The sixth circumstance is when the issue on appeal is one of great public
importance. T&R Props., Inc. v. Wimberly , 158 N.E.3d 137, 2020 -Ohio-4279, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020); Hocking Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Martin , No. 97-CA-9, 1998 WL 100416, at *1-2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 5, 1998); LaChance, 1995 WL 386496, at *3; Stacy, 1992 WL 37783, at *2;Portage Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Brown, 66 Ohio App.3d 737, 740-41, 586 N.E.2d 168 (11th Dist. 1990);Minor, 1985 WL 10250, at *1. See generally Franchise Developers, Inc. v. City of Cincinnati , 30 Ohio St.3d 28, 505 N.E.2d 966 (1987).

This exception was applicable in Wimberly where the issue on appeal concerned the practice of the Franklin County Municipal Court to enter FED judgments in which the only evidence in the case was an affidavit. It was applicable in Martin where the issue on appeal concerned the right to a trial by jury in an eviction action. It was applicable in Brown where the issue on appeal was whether the R.C. 5321.11 thirty - day notice requirement applied to a termination of a rental agreement that was based on the tenant’s illegal possession and sale of crack cocaine.

#### 5. Appeal Time Period, If Post-Judgment Motion or

Objection Filed Under Rule 4(B)(2) of the Ohio Rules of Appellate Proced ure, if a party timely files a request for findings of fact and conclusions of law under Civil Rule 52, a motion for a new trial under Civil Rule 59, or objections to a magistrate’s decision under Civil Rule 53(D), “the time for filing a notice of appeal begins to run as to all parties when the order disposing of the motion is entered.” In eviction actions, this tolling rule should apply when: (1) a party files a motion for a new trial under Civil Rule 59 because Civil Rule 59 applies to eviction actions (see Section I.P.1, above); or (2) a party files an objection to the magistrate’s decision under Civil Rule 53(D) because Civil Rule 53(D) applies to eviction actions (see Section I.N.3, above).

However, in eviction actions, this tolling rule does not apply when a party files a request for findings of fact and conclusions of law because, even though such findings and conclusions may be issued within seven working days of the trial, Civil Rule 52 does not apply to eviction actions. See Section I.N.1, above.

## II. Eviction Action, Common Defenses


### A. Deficient Content/Service of Notice to

Vacate or Notice of Termination

#### 1. R.C. 1923.04 Notice to Vacate

Proper service of an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate is an essential element of the landlord’s case in chief. Sternberg v. Washington , 113 Ohio App. 216, 221, 177 N.E.2d 525 (9th Dist. 1960).

If a landlord fails to serve a notice to vacate or serves a notice to vacate but its content or the service fails to comply with R.C. 1923.04,94 the trial court lacks subject- matter jurisdiction over the eviction action. See Section I.C.1.b, above. In such cases, if the court enters an eviction judgment for the landlord, the judgment is void ab initio.95 See Patton v. Diemer, 35 Ohio St.3d 68, 518 N.E.2d 941 (1988).

#### 2. Notice of Termination, R.C. 5321.11

When service of an R.C. 5321.11 notice of termination is required to terminate a tenancy, proper service of a proper notice is an essential element of the landlord’s case in chief. For a discussion on when this notice is required to terminate a tenancy, and on the content and service requirements for this notice, see Section I.D, above.

In such cases, if a landlord does not properly serve a proper R.C. 5321.11 notice, it is a complete defense to the eviction action. E.g., Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Anderson, No. 1406, 1988 WL 69118 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988);Sandefur
Mgmt. Co. v. Wilson, 21 Ohio App.3d 160, 486 N.E.2d 1267 (10th Dist. 1985).

Also, the court is without subject -matter jurisdiction over the evictio n action for two separate reasons: (i) it constitutes noncompliance with R.C. 1923.02(A)(8) (see Section I.D.2.g, above); and (ii) absent compliance with the R.C. 5321.11 notice 94 For a discussion on the content and service requirements for an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate, see Section I.C, above.

95 “[E]ven when not raised by either party, the issue of subject matter jurisdiction may be raised sua sponte by the court at any stage of the proceedings, including for t he first time on appeal.” Youngstown Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Higgins , No. 99 -CA-81, 2000 WL 1159418, at *2 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Aug. 4, 2000). Accord Cheap Escape Co. v. Haddox, L.L.C. , 120 Ohio St.3d 493, 494 n.1, 900 N.E.2d 601 (2008).

121 requirements, the tenancy has not been terminated (see Section I.D, above) an d, consequently, the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate has been served before the termination of the tenancy and thus prematurely (see Section I.C.3.e, above).

#### 3. Notice of Termination, R.C. 5321.17(B)

When service of an R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of termination isrequired to terminate a tenancy, proper service of a proper notice is an element of the landlord’s case in chief.

For a discussion on when this notice is required to terminate a tenancy, and on the content and service requirements for this notice, see Section I.D, above.

In such cases, if a landlord does not properly serve a proper R.C. 5321.17(B) notice, it is a complete defense to the eviction action.E.g., Mockensturm v. Razeq, No.

L-85-149, 1986 WL 1692 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 7, 1986).

Also, the trial court lacks subject -matter jurisdiction over the eviction action because, absent compliance with the R.C. 5321.17(B) notice requirements, the tenancy has not been terminated (see Section I.D, above) and, consequently, the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vaca te has been served before the termination of the tenancy and thus prematurely (see Section I.C.3.e, above).

### B. Deficient Service of Process

The service of process requirements in an eviction action are discussed in Section I.G, above. Under Civil Rule 3(A), the landlord has one year from the filing of the complaint to obtain proper service. Under Civil Rule 4(A), if service is not obtained within six months from the filing of the complaint, the court may dismiss the complaint without prejudice, unless good cause exists for the lack of service.

Therefore, in an eviction action, when the content or service of the summons is deficient, the appropriate response is a motion to quash service, if the response is filed before the expiration of the one -year period (or six -month period, if applicable) and before an eviction judgment has been entered. Cf. Thomas v. Corrigan , 135 Ohio App.3d 340, 733 N.E.2d 1213 (11th Dist. 1999) (improper to dismiss security deposit claim because the one -year period had not expired). But see Szabo v. Humphrey , 66 Ohio Misc.2d 55, 642 N.E.2d 1173 (Ohio Mun., Wadsworth, 1993) (granting motion to dismiss); Jackson View Ltd. v. Sturgill, No. 91-CVG-110 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty.,
Mar. 21, 1991) (same).

The requirement to obtain proper service of process is a jurisdictional requirement.

State ex rel. Ballard v. O’Donnell, 50 Ohio St.3d 182, 553 N.E.2d 650 (1990);Lincoln Tavern, Inc. v. Snader, 165 Ohio St. 61, 133 N.E.2d 606 (1956);O.B. Corp. v. Cordell, 47 Ohio App.3d 170, 547 N.E.2d 1201 (10th Dist. 1988).

Therefore, in an eviction action, absent proper service of process, the eviction judgment and the writ of restitution(if any) are void ab initio. Fourth & Plum Apts. v.

Tuzzolino, No. C -990568, 2000 WL 799379 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 23, 2000);

Castle Apts., Inc. v. Allgood, 43 Ohio App.3d 163, 539 N.E.2d 1154 (9th Dist. 1988);

Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Stuckey, No. L-92-102, 1993 WL 315626 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Aug. 13, 1993); Owners Mgmt. Co. v. Eslinger , No. 01 -CVG-1136 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, June 19, 2001). See generally State ex rel. Ballard v. O’Donnell, 50 Ohio St.3d 182, 553 N.E.2d 650 (1990); Thomas v. Corrigan, 135 Ohio App.3d 340, 733 N.E.2d 1213 (11th Dist. 1999).96 Furthermore, “when the affirmative defense of insufficiency of process is properly raised and properly preserved, a party’s active participation in litigation of a case does not constitute a waiver of that defense.” Gliozzo v. Univ. Urologists of Cleveland, 114 Ohio St.3d 141, 144, 870 N.E.2d 714 (2007). Accord LaNeve v. Atlas Recycling, Inc., 119 Ohio St.3d 324, 329, 894 N.E.2d 25 (2008).See also Szabo v. Humphrey, 66 Ohio Misc.2d 55, 642 N.E.2d 1173 (Ohio Mun., Wadsworth, 1993) (no waiver where tenant did not file pre-trial motion or answer, tenant appeared in court for the eviction hearing, and the court continued the hearing).

### C. Waiver of the R.C. 1923.04 Notice to

Vacate

#### 1. General Rule

A landlord’s acceptance of future rent,97 after the landlord has served the tenant with an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate, waives the notice to vacate. Associated Estates
Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985); Nat’l Corp. for 96 However, one appellate court has held that the R.C. 1923.06(B) requirement to include certain language in the summons is not a jurisdictional requirement. Northland Vill. Apts. v. Hamp ,
No. 12407, 1991 WL 108717 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 20, 1991). It held that the “ [f]ailure to include the language renders any judgment voidable, but only upon timely objection by the party prejudiced thereby.” Id.

97 For a discussion on whether and when a landlord has accepted the rent, under various methods of payment, see Section VII.F.6, below.

123 Housing P’ships v. Chapman, 18 Ohio App.3d 104, 481 N.E.2d 654 (9th Dist. 1984);

Graham v. Pavarini, 9 Ohio App.3d 89, 458 N.E.2d 421 (8th Dist. 1983);Presidential Park Apts. v. Colston, 17 Ohio Op.3d 220 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 1980); Cornerstone
Cos. v. Zipkin , 60 Ohio Misc.2d 14, 573 N.E.2d 228 (Ohio Mun., Akron, 1989);

Marchioni v. Wilson , 20 Ohio Misc.2d 10, 485 N.E.2d 1073 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1984).98 Cf. Shimko v. Marks , 91 Ohio App.3d 458, 632 N.E.2d 990 (5th Dist.
1993) (land installment contract).

This waiver of the notice is a waiver as a matter of law. Urban P’ship Bank v.

Mosezit Acad., Inc., No. 100712, 2014 WL 4244346 (Ohio App., 8th Dist. , Aug. 28, 2014); Mitchell v. Walker, No. C-050546 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 6, 2006);Colbert
v. McLemore, No. 81961, 2003 WL 21434169 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 19, 2003);

Presidential Park Apts. v. Colston, 17 Ohio Op.3d 220 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 1980).

This waiver rule remains in effect until the court issues a final judgment in response to any objections because, until that time, the eviction action is still pending. N. Face Props., Inc. v. Lin , No. CA2012 -09-083, 2013 WL 2423802, ¶ 14 (Ohio Ap p., 12th Dist., June 3, 2013).

98 See also Eureka Multifamily Group v. Terrell , No. L -14-1152, 2015 WL 2329168 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 15, 2015); Urban P’ship Bank v. Mosezit Acad., Inc., No. 100712, 2014 WL 4244346 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 28, 2014); N . Face Props., Inc. v. Lin, No. CA2010- 09-083, 2013 WL 2423802 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 3, 2013); IDC Brunswick Crossroads, LLC v. Gack, Inc., No. 10CA-0088-M, 2012 WL 177997 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 23, 2012);

Colbert v. McLemore, No. 81961, 2003 WL 21434169 (Ohi o App., 8th Dist., June 19, 2003);

King v. Dolton , No. 02-CA-0041, 2003 WL 21078088 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 14, 2003);

Bryant v. Dale , No. 98 -CA-36, 1999 WL 731824 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 10, 1999);

Sholiton Indus., Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd. , No. 17480, 1999 WL 355898 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist. , June 4, 1999); MLR Props., Inc. v. Hemmelgarn, No. L-94-349, 1995 WL 504629 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 25, 1995); Bristol Court v. Jones , No. 93 -CA-520, 1994 WL 534920 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 29, 1994); Walter v. Savanich , No. 93 -AP-120086, 1994 WL 369483 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 24, 1994); Dundon v. Balluck , No. 93 -CA-123, 1994 WL 55755 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 23, 1994); Sterling Health Care Group, Inc. v. Laughlin , No. 92 - WD-051, 1993 WL 179509 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 28, 1993); Wurzelbacher v. Johnson ,
No. CA92-03-048, 1993 WL 19538 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 1, 1993); Sheridan Manor
Apts. v. Carter, No. 92-CA-4, 1992 WL 388539 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 22, 1992); Stacy v.

Carr, No. CA -519, 19 92 WL 37783 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Feb. 20, 1992); Shelter Mgmt.

Group, Inc. v. Fawcett, No. 91-CA-000009, 1991 WL 160829 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 14, 1991); Andrews Sch. v. Brown , No. 11 -040, 1986 WL 3204 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 14, 1986); Knights Ctr. Corp. v. Thomas , No. 49636, 1985 WL 9039 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 23, 1985).

This waiver rule applies irrespective of whether the eviction action is for nonpayment of rent or for any other breach of the lease. N. Face Props., Inc. v. Lin ,
No. CA2012-09-083, 2013 WL 2423802, ¶¶ 16,17 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 3, 2013); Rogers v. Jones, No. 2014-CVG-028113 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 30,
2014) (follows N. Face Props.). E.g., Eureka Multifamily Group v. Terrell, No. L-14- 1152, 2015 WL 2329168 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 15, 2015) (other breach); IDC Brunswick Crossroads, LLC v. Gack, Inc., No. 10CA-0088-M, 2012 WL 177997 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 23, 2012) (nonpayment of rent);Colbert v. McLemore, No. 81961, 2003 WL 21434169 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 19, 2003) (other breach). But see Day
v. Baker, No. CA2003 -06-140, 2004 WL 2340656 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 18,
2004) (waiver only in eviction actions for nonpayment of rent).

“The only difference created by eviction based upon [another] breach instead of nonpayment of rent essentially is that [the landlord] has waived its three-day notice but may re -file its FED action after providing [the tenant] with another three -day notice….” N. Face Props., Inc. v. Lin , 2013 WL 2423802, ¶ 17.99 See also Cornerstone Cos. v. Zipkin , 60 Ohio Misc.2d 14, 16, 573 N.E.2d 228 (Ohio Mun., Akron, 1989). When the eviction action is based on nonpayment of rent, the landlord’s acceptance of future rent usually eliminates the tenant’s rent arrearage and thus the basis for re-filing the eviction action.

A lease provision cannot waive this waiver rule. IDC Brunswick Crossroads, LLC
v. Gack, Inc. , No. 10CA -0088-M, 2012 WL 177997 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 23, 2012).

When the landlord’s acceptance of future rent waives the notice to vacate, the trial court lacks subject -matter jurisdiction over the eviction action. See Section I.C.1.b, above.

A landlord’s acceptance of past due rent, after the landlord has served the tenant with an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate, does not waive the notice to vacate. Sholiton Indus., Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd., No. 17480, 1999 WL 355898 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 4, 1999); Marks v. Young, No. 2456-M, 1996 WL 63033 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,
Feb. 14, 1996); Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v. Johnson, No. 61691, 1992 WL 309362 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 22, 1992); Damko v. Ryncarz, No. 1906, 1988 WL 81291 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 29, 1988); Graham v. Pavarini, 9 Ohio App.3d 89, 458 N.E.2d 99 One exception to this right to re-file the FED action arises when the landlord’s acceptance of future rent constitutes a waiver of the underlying breach of the lease. S ee Section II.E, below.

125 421 (8th Dist. 1983); Presidential Park Apts. v. Colston , 17 Ohio Op.3d 220 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 1980).

But the landlord’s acceptance of future rent may occur as a result of the landlord’s acceptance of a rent payment that is partially for a past liability and partially for a future
obligation. IDC Brunswick Crossroads, LLC v. Gack, Inc., No. 10CA-0088-M, 2012 WL 177997 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 23, 2012).

“Appellate courts in this state are split on the meaning of the term ‘future rent,’ as used in this context.” Ebbing v. Mathis, No. CA2013-01-014, 2013 WL 4806940, ¶19 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Sept. 9, 2013). Accord Urban P’ship Bank v. Mosezit Acad., Inc., No. 100712, 2014 WL 4244346, ¶ 15 n.5 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 28, 2014).

Some courts have held that fu ture rent means a rent payment for any period of occupancy that is “after the date of the landlord’s acceptance of the payment” (“Carter standard”). Sheridan Manor Apts. v. Carter , No. 92 -CA-4, 1992 WL 388539, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 22, 1992). Accord Urban P’ship Bank v. Mosezit Acad., Inc., No. 100712, 2014 WL 4244346, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 28, 2014);IDC Brunswick Crossroads, LLC v. Gack, Inc., No. 10CA-0088-M, 2012 WL 177997, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 23, 2012); Sholiton Indus., Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd. , No.

17480, 1999 WL 355898, at *7 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 4, 1999).

Other courts have held that, “[i]n this context, ‘future’ [rent] payments includes payment for any period subsequent to the three-day [R.C. 1923.04(A)]eviction notice” (“Thomas standard”). Knights Ctr. Corp. v. Thomas, No. 49636, 1985 WL 9039, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 23, 1985). Accord Ondrick v. Courim, No. 98-CVG-00063 (Ohio County Ct., Belmont Cty., July 8, 1998). See also Colbert v. McLem ore, No.

81961, 2003 WL 21434169, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 19, 2003).

The following three examples illustrate these standards. First, if a landlord serves a tenant with a notice to vacate on February 6 and the landlord accepts the tenant’s tender of January and February rent on February 10, the landlord has accepted future rent under either standard. Under theCarter standard, the landlord has accepted future rent for the period February 11 to 28. Under the Thomas standard, the landlord has accepted future rent for the period February 7 to 28.

Second, if a landlord serves a tenant with a notice to vacate on February 6 and the landlord accepts the tenant’s tender of January rent on February 10, the landlord has not accepted future rent under either standard. The January rent is neither rent for a period after February 10 ( Carter standard) nor rent for a period after February 6 (Thomas standard).

Third, if a landlord serves a tenant with a notice to vacate on February 6 and the landlord accepts the t enant’s tender of January and February rent on March 1, the landlord has accepted future rent under one of the two standards. Under the Thomas standard, the landlord has accepted future rent for the period February 7 to 28. Under the Carter standard, the landlord has not accepted future rent because the landlord has not accepted rent for any period after March 1.

Under either standard, the determination on whether the landlord has accepted future rent is generally a question of fact. Mitchell v. Walker , No. C -050546 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 6, 2006); Colbert v. McLemore, No. 81961, 2003 WL 21434169 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 19, 2003); Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell , 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985);Presidential Park Apts. v. Colston, 17 Ohio Op.3d 220 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 1980).

When a tenant has been depositing rent with the court du ring the pendency of an eviction action, and the court dismisses the eviction action without prejudice and orders the release of the rent to the landlord without prejudice to the right of the landlord to pursue another eviction action, the landlord’s accep tance of those rent deposits does not constitute acceptance of future rent, relative to the notice to vacate. Wurzelbacher
v. Johnson, No. CA92-03-048, 1993 WL 19538 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 1, 1993).

When a tenancy is terminated during a period for which rent has been prepaid, the landlord’s failure to return the prepaid rent for the post-termination period may waive the notice to vacate. 1994-N1 Ohio Assocs., L.P. v. Planet Earth Entm’t, Inc. , No.

15338, 1995 WL 738416, at *5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 15, 1995). In such cases, the relevant issue is whether the landlord’s action of failing to return the prepaid rent “was so inconsistent with the notice to vacate as to amount to a waiver of the notice.”
Id. The finding on this issue is a question of fact. Id.

In 1994-N1 Ohio Assocs., L.P. , the tenant prepaid rent in the amount of $50,000 for one year, the landlord terminated the tenancy approximately four months into that year, and the landlord did not tender repayment of the prepaid rent for the p ost- termination period. Id., 1995 WL 738416, at *1,5. The court also considered the time required for completion of the eviction process and the landlord’s related damage claims against the tenant. Id., 1995 WL 738416, at *5. The court held that, “[a]lthough it is a close call, in our view, the trial court could have reasonably concluded that Ohio Associates [landlord] did not take any action, including the retention of the Planet Earth’s [tenant’s] prepaid rent, which was so inconsistent with the noticeto vacate as to amount to a waiver of that notice.” Id.

127 The decision in Summit Mgmt. Servs. v. Gough, No. 19714, 2000 WL 1226605, at *2 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 30, 2000), erroneously holds that a notice to vacate always “must specify a [vacation] date after the expiration of the term for which rent is already paid.” The Gough decision is erroneous because it fails to distinguish between a failure to terminate the tenancy, which always precludes service of the notice to vacate, and a prepayment of ren t, which precludes service of the notice to vacate only when it precludes a termination of the tenancy.

The Gough decision’s reliance onHile v. Besecker, 82 Ohio App. 301, 79 N.E.2d 582 (2nd Dist. 1947), andSchmidt v. Hummell, 81 Ohio App. 167, 73 N.E.2d 806 (10th
Dist. 1947), is misplaced for this reason. BothHile and Schmidt concerned a payment of rent in a month -to-month tenancy that renewed the tenancy for another month. In those cases, the prepayment of rent invalidated the notice to vacate only because the prepayment of rent precluded the termination of the month-to-month tenancy. In other cases, especially those concerning a lease with a fixed term, a prepayment of rent does not preclude a termination of the tenancy. E.g., 1994-N1 Ohio Assocs., L.P. v. Planet Earth Entm’t, Inc., No. 15338, 1995 WL 738416 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 15, 1995).

The Gough decision’s reliance on Bristol Court v. Jones , No. 93 -CA-520, 1994 WL 534920 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 29, 1994), is misplaced because Bristol Court concerns acceptance of future rent after service of the notice to vacate, which waives the notice to vacate for the reasons discussed in this Section II.C.1.

#### 2. Assisted Housing Tenancies

For assisted housing tenants, 100 an issue exists as to whether the landlord’s acceptance of the rent subsidy payment that is made on behalf of the tenant constitutes the acceptance of rent, for purposes of the waiver rule that is discussed in Section II.C.1., above. There are two basic types of rental assistance programs.

One type of program is tenant-based rental assistance, such as the Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”). 24 C.F.R. part 982. Under the HCVP, the family selects a dwelling unit in the private market at which it will use the rent subsid y. 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.1(a)(2). “The PHA contracts with the owner to make rent subsidy payments on behalf of the family.” Id. The contract “only covers a single unit and a specified assisted family.” 24 C.F.R. § 982.1(b)(2). The family generally may move with the rent subsidy from one dwelling unit to another dwelling unit, at the end of any lease 100 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, below.
