term. 24 C.F.R. §§ 982.1(b)(2), 982.354. The rent subsidy is tied to the family, not to the dwelling unit.

Under the HCVP, the contract rent is known as the “rent to owner,” and is defined as “the total monthly rent payable to the owner under the lease for the unit.” 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.4. The portion of the rent to owner that is paid by the family is known as the “family rent to owner.” Id. The portion of the rent to owner that is paid by the public housing authority (“PHA”) is known as a “housing assistance payment.” Id. The housing assistance payment is a “payment to the owner for rent to the owner under the family’s lease.” Id.

The second type of program is project-based assisted housing rental assistance,101 in which rental assistance is paid for families who live in specified housing developments or units. The subsidy is tied to particular dwelling units at a particular
development. Families who live in one of those dwelling units receive the benefit of the rental assistance. If the family vacates its dwelling unit, either voluntarily or involuntarily, the family no longer receives the benefit of the rental assistance.

Numerous Ohio courts, throughout the State, consistently and properly hold that a tenant-based rent subsidy (e.g., HCVP subsidy) is rent, for purposes of the waiver rule.

Those courts (and decisions) include: Rogers v. Jones, No. 2014-CVG-028113 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 30, 2014); Deegan Mgmt. Inc. v. Moss, No. 14-CVG-00417 (Ohio Mun., Euclid, Mar. 4, 2014); Walton v. Gooden , No. 2008 -CVG-7298 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug. 26, 2008); Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Sendykar , No.

07-CVG-02733 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Nov. 1, 2007); Crawley v. Brown , No.

2005-CVG-16627 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 6, 2005); Nat’l Church Residences v. Neal, No. 04-CVG-1264 (Ohio Mun., Euclid, Sept. 1, 2004); Burke v.

Wilson, No. CVG-0300781 (Ohio Mun., Washington Court House, Jan. 20, 2004);Am.
Prop. Serv. v. Henshaw , No. 03 -CVG-02766 (Ohio Mun., Fairfield Cty., Dec. 22, 2003); Wayland v. Plymire, No. 03-CVG-987 (Ohio Mun., Barberton, July 9, 1997);

Baker v. Wade , No. 2002 -CVG-15892 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 2, 2002); Tolle v. H ill, No. CVG -9600580 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Mar. 13, 1997); Orlosky v. Adams, No. 95-CVG-981 (Ohio Mun., Painesville, Sept. 21, 1995);

Wright v. Rash , No. 93 -CVG-10748 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 29, 1993); Lipford v. Ward , No. 92 -CVG-0727 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Mar. 24, 1992);

101 For the definition of “project-based assisted housing,” see note 165, below.

129 Green v. Sephus , No. 90 -CVG-1768-38 (Ohio Mun., East Cleveland, Jan. 8, 1991);

Schaefer v. Robertson, No. K86-CVG-1104 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Nov. 10, 1986).
Cf. Cheney v. Mitchell , No. 09 -CVG-01707 (Ohio Mun., Sha ker Heights, Nov. 25,
2009) (30-day notice of termination waiver).

Given the definition of the HCVP housing assistance payment and the structure of the HCVP, these holdings are the only reasonable interpretation of the waiver rule, for the HCVP. The housi ng assistance payment is solely a rent payment under a lease made by a third-party (the PHA) directly on behalf of the tenant, for all or part of the contract rent.

These holdings are also consistent with the definition of rent: “compensation rendered for use of lands by a person permitted to hold or enjoy them.”Dayton Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. McKee , 37 Ohio App.3d 102, 104, 524 N.E.2d 180 (2nd Dist. 1987) (citing Sutliff v. Atwood , 15 Ohio St. 186, 193 (1864)). The housing assistance payment is simply the full or partial compensation to the owner for permitting the tenant to reside in the dwelling unit and is thus rent.

The only exceptions to these holdings are two court decisions concerning landlords who received a single HCVP rent subsidy check for multiple units. The decisions are Premier Mgmt., LLC v. Nutt , No. 6-09-15, 2010 WL 1175201 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist.,
Mar. 29, 2010), and Vienna Forest Apts. v. Passmore, No. CA2004-05-104, 2005 WL 1131742 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 16, 2005). In these circumstances, these courts held that the landlord’s cashing of the HCVP check did not constitute a waiver of the notice to vacate.

The problem and a reasonable solution for a landlord receiving a multi -unit rent subsidy check is well described in Macleay Woods Hous. Co. v. Franks , 16 Misc.3d 1136(A), 2007 WL 2597918 (N.Y. City Ct. 2007). “To require a landlord so situated to reject such an omnibus check would potentially jeopardize its financial health and thereby its ability to provide shelter for its other low income tenants.” Id., 2007 WL 25977918, at *4. The court thereupon held that the landlord had not accepted the HCVP rent subsidy, for waiver purposes, when it cashed the multi -unit HCVP rent subsidy check and then returned (within fourteen days) the HCVP s ubsidy for the tenant’s dwelling unit only. See also Woodbridge Homes Ltd. P’ship v. Gregory, 697 S.E.2d 370 (N.C. App. 2010) (project-based rental assistance).

This procedure – cashing the HCVP check and immediately returning the amount of the subsidy payment for the relevant tenancy – is consistent with the service of the notice to vacate. The landlord cashes the check to receive the subsidy payments for the other HCVP tenancies but does not retain the subsidy payment for the relevant
tenancy. Therefore, courts should find that a landlord’s use of this procedure does not waive the notice to vacate.

When a HCVP landlord receives a rent -subsidy check solely for the relevant tenancy, the landlord may request the PHA to hold the subsidy payments pending th e eviction action. Most (if not all) PHAs will honor this request and, at the conclusion of the eviction action, tender the withheld subsidy payments to the landlord.

While the Nutt decision is distinguishable because of the multi -unit rent subsidy check, it is also significantly flawed in two aspects.

First, the Nutt decision relies on the erroneous underlying premise that the HCVP rent subsidy is not rent because the payment is made by a third party. The definition of rent, as set forth in McKee and elsewhere (see Section VII.F.1, below), does not exclude payments made by a third party on behalf of a tenant. Indeed, “there are many examples of third parties making rental payments on behalf of actual occupants of rented premises (such as parents m aking rental payments for premises occupied by their children).” Woodbridge Homes Ltd. P’ship v. Gregory , 697 S.E.2d 370, 379 (N.C. App. 2010).

Second, the above analysis of rent under the HCVP contracts and regulations undermines the Nutt finding that the HCVP rent subsidy is not rent. TheNutt decision ignores the HCVP contracts and regulations and, instead, relies on two project -based rent subsidy cases – Chapman and Helgason.

With respect to the project -based rent subsidy programs, in Ohio and amon g the states, the case law is split on whether the rent subsidy constitutes rent, for purposes of the waiver rule.

Court decisions holding that the project-based rent subsidy is rent, for purposes of the waiver rule, include: Classic A Props. v. Brown , No. 02-CA-2868, 2003 WL 22461816 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 30, 2003); White Rose Props. v. Babb, No. 00- CVF-1355 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Jan. 23, 2001);Bella Vista Apts. v. Ragland, No. 91- CVG-54-471 (Ohio County Ct., Clermont Cty., May 31, 1991). See also Woodbridge Homes Ltd. P’ship v. Gregory, 697 S.E.2d 370 (N.C. App. 2010); Royal Am. Mgmt.,
Inc. v. Godfrey, 14 Fla. Supp.2d 56 (County Ct. 1985); Greenwich Gardens Assocs. v.

Pitt, 126 Misc.2d 947, 484 N.Y.S.2d 439 (Civ. Ct. 1984); Cent. Brooklyn Urban Dev.
Corp. v. Copeland, 122 Misc.2d 726, 471 N.Y.S.2d 989 (Dist. Ct. 1984).

Court decisions holding that the project-based rent subsidy is not rent, for purposes of the waiver rule, include:Nat’l Corp. for Hous. P’ships v. Chapman, 18 Ohio App.3d 131 104, 481 N.E.2d 654 (9th Dist. 1984). See also Midland Mgmt. Co. v. Helgason, 158 Ill.2d 98, 630 N.E.2d 836 (1994);E. Lake Mgmt. & Dev. Corp. v. Irvin, 195 Ill. App.3d 196, 551 N.E.2d 272 (1990).

The better reasoned view is that the project-based rent subsidy is rent, for purposes of the waiver rule, since it is a payment under the lease of a portion of the tenant’s contract rent. When the dwelling unit is vacant, the owner of a project -based development no longer receives a rent subsidy payment for the unit. Rather, the owner may receive vacancy payments for certain periods of time. E.g., 24 C.F.R.

§§ 880.611(c),(d), 886.109(c).

The only difference between the HCVP rent subsidy and the project -based rent subsidy, for purposes of the waiver rule, is that the HCVP rent subsidy is tied to the family and the project-based rent subsidy is tied to the dwelling unit. The relevance of this difference, if any, would be that the HCVP rent subsidy more clearly is only a rent payment made on behalf of a particular tenant, whereas the project-based rent subsidy might also be considered a subsidy for maintaining the entire subsidized development.

For this reason, the project-based rent subsidy decisions are an improper basis on which to rely in HCVP rent subsidy cases.

### D. Waiver of the R.C. 5321.11 or 5321.17

Notice of Termination If a landlord accepts future rent after the expiration of the thirty -day period of an R.C. 5321.11 or 5321.17(B) notice of termination (and thus after the termination of the tenancy), it may constitute a waiver of the notice.102 Springboro Commons Ret. Villa,
Inc. v. Feltner, 168 N.E.3d 573, 2021-Ohio-544 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., 2021) (waiver of R.C. 5321.11 notice); CMHA v. Hawthorne, No. 2012-CVG-007158 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 30, 2012, and Nov. 30, 2012) (dicta); Cheney v. Mitchell,
No. 09-CVG-01707 (Ohio Mun., Shaker Heights, Nov. 25, 2009); Citizens Sav. Corp.
v. Bukoffsky, No. 90 -CVG-1851 (Ohio Mun., Massillon, Aug. 30, 1990). See also Crawley v. Brown, No. 2005-CVG-16627 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 6,
2005) (30-day notice under lease); Rababy v. City of Cleveland , No. 93-CVG-11745 102 The landlord’s acceptance of the rent for the thirty -day period (and thus before the termination of the tenancy) does not waive the R.C. 5321.11 or 5321.17(B) notice. See Section I.D.4, above.

(Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 16, 1993) (commercial month -to-month tenancy).

The rationale for this waiver is that the landlo rd’s acceptance of future rent is inconsistent with the notice’s purported termination of the tenancy. CMHA v.

Hawthorne, No. 2012 -CVG-007158 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 30, 2012, and Nov. 30, 2012); Rababy v. City of Cleveland , No. 93 -CVG-11745 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 16, 1993). Cf. Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985) (acceptance of future rent waives R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate);103 Quinn v. Cardinal Foods, Inc., 20 Ohio App.3d 194, 485 N.E.2d 741 (3rd Dist. 1984) (acceptance of future rent waives known breach of rental agreement).104 Also, if a landlord’s actions (or inactions) cause a significant delay between the expiration of the thirty -day period of an R.C. 5321.11 or 532 1.17(B) notice of termination and the service of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate, it may constitute a waiver of the notice. CMHA v. Hawthorne , No. 2012 -CVG-007158 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 30, 2012, and Nov. 30, 2012). See also Vistula Mgmt. Co.
v. Newson, 120 Ohio App.3d 500, 698 N.E.2d 467 (6th Dist. 1997) (federal notice of termination); Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Anderson, No. 1406, 1988 WL 69118 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988) (same). However, during such delays, usually the landlord will accept future rent and thereby waive the notice of termination, irrespective of whether the delay alone waives it.

With respect to the landlord’s acceptance of future rent after the expiration of the thirty-day period, an alternative arg ument (in lieu of waiver) is a new tenancy
argument. Its basic premise is that the landlord’s acceptance of future rent creates a new periodic tenancy, which gives the tenant the right to remain in possession of the premises notwithstanding the terminatio n of the prior tenancy. See Bill Swad Chevrolet, Inc. v. Ricart Jeep Eagle, Inc., 129 Ohio App.3d 501, 718 N.E.2d 470 (10th
Dist. 1998) (landlord’s acceptance of rent from holdover tenant creates a new tenancy);

Steiner v. Minkowski, 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 5 96 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist. 1991) (same);

Craig Wrecking Co. v. Loewendick & Sons, Inc., 38 Ohio App.3d 79, 526 N.E.2d 321 103 For additional case law on waiver of an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate, and for a discussion of the term future rent in that context, see Section II.C, above.

104 For additional case law on waiver of a breach of the rental agreement, see Section II.E, below.

133 (10th Dist. 1987) (same). Cf. FMJ Props. v. Hinton, No. 50314, 1986 WL 4396, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 10, 1986) (landlord’s ac ceptance of rent from month -to- month tenant renews the month-to-month tenancy).

### E. Waiver of the Breach


#### 1. Overview

When a landlord has knowledge of a tenant’s breach of the rental agreement, the landlord must decide whether to terminate the tenancy or to waive the breach and continue the tenancy. Petropoulos v. Clinical Pathology Facility, Inc., No. 87AP-685, 1988 WL 24397, at *4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 18, 1988); Quinn v. Cardinal Foods, Inc., 20 Ohio App.3d 194, 196, 485 N.E.2d 741 (3rd Dist. 1984); Brokamp v.

Linneman, 20 Ohio App. 199, 202, 153 N.E. 130 (1st Dist. 1923).

A landlord waives the breach if, with knowledge of the breach, the landlord takes action that is inconsistent with a termination of the tenancy. Id.

A landlord may waive the right to terminate a tenancy irrespective of whether that right is premised on the tenant’s failure to pay rent or the tenant’s breach of another obligation under the rental agreement.

‘The forfeiture of a lease by breach of any other condition may be waived, in the same manner as a forfeiture for non-payment of rent, or a notice to quit; for if the landlord subsequently does any act, with knowledge of the breach, which can be considered as an acknowledgment of a tenancy, still subsisting, he will be held to have waived the forfeiture.’ Quinn v. Cardinal Foods, Inc. , 20 Ohio App.3d 194, 196, 485 N.E.2d 741 (3rd Dist.
1984) (quoting l Taylor on Landlord and Tenant § 287). Accord Millennia Hous.

Mgmt., Ltd. v. Williams , No. 101627, 2015 WL 1276464, ¶ 9 (Ohio App. , 8th Dist.,
Mar. 19, 2015); Petropoulos v. Clinical Pathology Facility, Inc., No. 87AP-685, 1988 WL 24397, at *4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 18, 1988).

A waiver of the right to evict is an equitable defense. Northfield Park Assocs. v.

Northeast Ohio Har ness, 36 Ohio App.3d 14, 22, 521 N.E.2d 466 (8th Dist. 1987).

Therefore, in ruling on this defense, the trial court is “required to weigh and consider all equitable considerations before it.” Id.

#### 2. Waiver of the Breach by Acceptance of Rent


##### a. Nonpayment of Rent Breach

A waiver of the right to terminate a tenancy for nonpayment of rent may occur as a result of the landlord’s acceptance of rent, where:
(1) the landlord accepts future rent payments, with knowledge of the rent arrearage, e.g., Navicky v. Copas , No. 9 7-CVG-00295 (Ohio Mun., Cambridge, July 3, 1997); Mt. Olive-Metro Hous., Inc. v. Craft, No. 93- G-2858 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Feb. 28, 1994); Owner’s Mgmt.
Co. v. Osborne , No. 91 -CVG-774 (Ohio Mun., Barberton, Mar. 2, 1993);
(2) the landlord has a customar y practice of accepting late rent payments, with which the tenant has complied (see Section II.H, below); or
(3) the landlord accepts a partial payment of rent,105 e.g., Fairborn Apts. v.

Herman, No. 90 -CA-28, 1991 WL 10962 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan.

31, 1991); S. Heights Apts. v. Davis , No. 3737, 1985 WL 10685 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 17, 1985).

##### b. Other Breach

A waiver of the right to terminate a tenancy for a breach other than nonpayment of rent may occur as a result of the landlord’s acceptance of rent with knowledge of the
breach. Eureka Multifamily Group v. Terrell , No. L -14-1152, 2015 WL 2329168 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 15, 2015); Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Noel, No. 06-CA- 009006, 2007 WL 1661854 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 11, 2007) (dicta); Quinn v.

Cardinal Foods, Inc., 20 Ohio App.3d 194, 485 N.E.2d 741 (3rd Dist. 1984);Heller v.

Murphy Hotel Corp., 5 Ohio Law Abs. 54 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1926); Brokamp v.

Linneman, 20 Ohio App. 199, 153 N.E. 130 (1st Dist. 1923);Pabst v. Fischer, 13 Ohio
App. 302 (12th Dist. 1920); CMHA v. Hawthorne , No. 2012 -CVG-007158 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 30 , 2012, and Nov. 30, 2012); Cuyahoga Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Hairston, 124 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 790 N.E.2d 828 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., 2003); Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Hawkins, No. 2002-CVG-0039 (Ohio 105 While acceptance of partial payment of rent may waive the landlord’s right to terminate the tenancy for nonpayment of rent, the tenant is stil l obligated to pay and remains liable for the unpaid rent. See Pros Corporate Mgmt. Servs., Inc. v. Ashley S. Rose, Ltd., 228 Ill. App.3d 573, 582, 592 N.E.2d 609 (1992).

135 Mun., Alliance, Mar. 18, 2002). See also Petropoulos v. Clinical Pathology Facility, Inc., No. 87AP-685, 1988 WL 24397, at *4-5 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 18, 1988).

But see Lucas Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Carmony , No. L-00-1296, 2001 WL 672150, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 15, 2001).106 A waiver of the right to terminate a tenancy for a breach other than nonpayment of rent may occur even if the alleged breach of lease is an assisted housing 107 tenant’s alleged criminal activity. Eureka Multifamily Group v. Terrell, No. L-14-1152, 2015 WL 2329168 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 15, 2015) (project -based subsidized housing);108 CMHA v. Hawthorne , No. 2012 -CVG-007158 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., July 30, 2012, and Nov. 30, 2012) (Public Housing); Cuyahoga Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Hairston, 124 Ohio Misc.2d 1,790 N.E.2d 828 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., 2003) (Public Housing). In such cases, the waiver is consistent with federal law because federal law gives PHAs discretion in deciding whether to terminate a lease for criminal conduct. Id.

#### 3. Waiver of the Breach by Execution of New Lease

A waiver of the right to terminate a tenancy for nonpayment of rent may occur as a result of the landlord’s execution of a new lease with the tenant. E.g., Dayton Inv.

Group-7 v. Lathern , No. 15866, 1996 WL 741822 (Ohi o App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 13, 1996); Green v. Sephus , No. 90 -CVG-1768-38 (Ohio Mun., East Cleveland, Jan. 8, 1991). See also Nyack Plaza v. Parker, 18 Misc.3d 126(A), 2007 WL 4355300 (N.Y.
Sup. App. Term 2007); Joseph v. Beaumont Hous. Auth. , 99 S.W.3d 765 (Tex. App.

2003); Montgomery Gateway East I v. Herrera , 261 N.J. Super. 235, 618 A.2d 865 (1992).

Likewise, a waiver of the right to terminate a tenancy for a breach other than nonpayment of rent may occur as a result of the landlord’s execution of a new lease 106 In Carmony, the court held that the significant delay between the breach of the lease and the service of the notice of termination did not constitute a waiver of the breach because the lease stated that a delay in exercising a right under the lease did not waive the right. Lucas Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Carmony , No. L-00-1296, 2001 WL 672150, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 15, 2001). It then held that the landlord did not waive the breach even though, during this delay, the landlord accepted future rent with knowledge of the breach. Id.

107 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, below.

108 For the definition of “project-based subsidized housing,” see note 165, below.

with the tenant.109 Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Anderson , No. 1406, 1988 WL 69118, at *10 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988);Sugarcreek L.T.D. v. Tedrick, No.

9900554 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Feb. 4, 2000); Smith v. Johnson , No. 85- CVG-6928 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Sept. 23, 1985).

When the waiver of the breach is premised on the landlord’s execution of a new lease, the right to possession is based on the new lease. See Stepping Stones Assoc. v.

Seymour, 48 A.D.3d 581, 583-84, 853 N.Y.S.2d 562 (2008).

In these circumstances, the landlord may prevent a waiver of the breach by inserting an express provision in the new lease that reserves a right to terminate for any breach of the superseded lease. Millennia Hous. Mgmt., Ltd. v. Williams, No. 101627, 2015 WL 1276464 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 19, 2015); Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth.
v. Manago, No. 96-CA-006628, 1998 WL 46743 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 21, 1998).

This waiver issue often arises in the context of a new or amended assistedhousing lease.110 When a new assisted housing lease is executed to comply with new federal requirements, and the new lease does not expressly reserve the right to terminate for a breach of the superseded lease, the new lease may waive any breach of the superseded
lease. Millennia Hous. Mgmt., Ltd. v. Williams, No. 101627, 2015 WL 1276464, ¶ 14 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 19, 2015). See also Gallatin Hous. Auth. v. Montesillo ,
No. M2001 -02260-COA-R3-CV, 2002 WL 1800968 (Tenn. App. Aug. 7, 2002);

Superior Hous. Auth. v. Foote , No. 90 -1121, 1990 WL 198173 (Wis. App. Oct. 9, 1990). Cf. Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Anderson, No. 1406, 1988 WL 69118, at *2,10 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988) (waiver where landlord failed to prove that it executed the new lease solely to comply with a federal requirement).

When a new lease is executed to transfer an assisted housing tenant to another unit to permit renovation of the tenant’s prior unit, the landlord may expressly reserve in the new lease (and thus pres erve) the right to terminate the new lease for a breach of the superseded lease. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Manago, No. 96-CA-006628, 1998 109 One court has held that a waiver of the right to terminate a tenancy for a breach also may occur as a result of amendments of house rules executed by the landlord and tenant, if the amendments are incorporated into the lease and the tenant’s execution of the amendments is a condition of continued occupancy. Retiree Hous. Mgmt. Inc. v. Lynch , No. 12 -CVG-02402 (Ohio Mun., Miamisburg, July 30, 2013).

110 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, below.

137 WL 46743 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 21, 1998). See also Thornton v. Dist. of Columbia, 647 A.2d 385 (D.C. App. 1994).

Assisted housing leases are often revised, by amendment or by execution of a new lease, solely to change the rent amount and/or the household composition. The revision, when effected by a lease amendment, does not waive the right to terminate the am ended lease for a pre -amendment breach of the lease. See Millennia Hous.

Mgmt., Ltd. v. Williams, No. 101627, 2015 WL 1276464, (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar.

19, 2015). The appellate districts are split on whether the revision, when effected by executing a new lease, waives the right to terminate the new lease for a breach of the superseded lease, absent an express reservation of this right in the new lease.Compare Millennia Hous. Mgmt., Ltd. v. Williams, No. 101627, 2015 WL 1276464, (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 19, 2015) (waiver)with Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v. Madden, No. L-98-1371, 1999 WL 375526 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 11, 1999) (2 -1 decision) (no waiver).

See also Dist. of Columbia v. Suydam , 591 A.2d 856 (D.C. App. 1991) (no waiver).
Cf. Hous. Resource Group v. Price , No. 38962 -9-I, 1998 WL 251768 (Wash. App.

May 18, 1998) (revision only in rent did not constitute a new lease for a federal 60-day notice requirement).

### F. Remedy of the Condition Specified in the

R.C. 5321.11 Notice of Termination When a lan dlord seeks to terminate a tenancy on the ground that the tenant has violated an R.C. 5321.05 duty (other than R.C. 5321.05(A)(9)) and the violation materially affects health and safety, the landlord is required to serve the tenant with an R.C. 5321.11 notice of termination. See Section I.D, above. The R.C. 5321.11 notice of termination must describe with specificity the acts and omissions of the tenant that constitute the R.C. 5321.05 violation. See Section I.D.2.c, above.

If the tenant remedies the R.C . 5321.05 violation prior to the termination date specified in the R.C. 5321.11 notice of termination, the rental agreement is not terminated under the notice and the tenant’s conduct that violated R.C. 5321.05 cannot be the basis for an eviction action. See Section I.D.2.e, above. For a discussion on the tenant’s right to remedy an R.C. 5321.05 violation by paying to the landlord the cost of repairing the damages, see Section I.D.2.e, above.

When a landlord’s eviction claim is based on an R.C. 5321.11 n otice of termination, an element of the claim is that the tenant failed to remedy timely the R.C.

5321.05 violation. See Meredith v. Nash, No. 93-CVG-2334 (Ohio Mun., Massillon,
Oct. 20, 1993); Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Gwin , No. 91 -CVG-5767 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Nov. 15, 1991); Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Moore, No. 89-CVG-9248 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Feb. 9, 1990). Cf. Vesta Cleveland LLC v. Swann, No. 2003-CVG-5468 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 20, 2003) (notice of termination under lease provision).

Consequently, the landlord is required to prove that, as of the termination date specified in the R.C. 5321.11 notice of termination, the tenant did not remedy the R.C.

5321.05 violation. Id.

### G. Timely Surrender of Possession

The R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate is a condition precedent to the commencement of any eviction action. See Section I.C.1.a, above. It informs the residential tenant that, “[i]f you do not leave, an eviction action may be initiated against you.” R.C.

1923.04(A). For residential landlords, the purpose of an eviction action is to recover possession of the rental premises, if the tenant does not timely vacate the rental premises in response to the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate. See Section I.A, above.

When a tenant timely surrenders possession of the rental premises in response (or
prior) to the service of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate, it is a defense to the landlord’s claim for an eviction judgment. Rejection of the eviction claim is justified because the tenant has compl ied with the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate ( i.e., surrendered
possession) and no longer resides in the rental premises. See Castlebrook Apts. v.

Ballard, No. 22421, 2008 WL 4183980, ¶ 22 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 12, 2008).

In these circumstances, thelandlord has filed an eviction complaint without any factual basis, since the landlord already has possession.

If a tenant vacates the premises and returns the keys for the premises before the eviction hearing, the only issue in the eviction action (i.e., right to immediate
possession) is moot. Richmond’s Enter., Inc. v. Anderson , No. 26674, 2016 WL 685398 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 19, 2016). Therefore, in these cases, the trial court is required to grant a motion to dismiss on mootness grounds. Id.

### H. Course of Conduct of Accepting Late

Rent Payment A landlord’s repeated acceptance of late rent payments may establish a course of 139 conduct between the parties that waives the tenant’s obligation to pay rent by the due date specified in the rental agreement.111 Fairborn Apts. v. Herman , No. 90-CA-28, 1991 WL 10962 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 31, 1991); Crossroads Somerset Ltd. v.

Newland, 40 Ohio App.3d 20, 531 N.E.2d 327 (10th Dist. 1987); Finkbeiner v. Lutz, 44 Ohio App.2d 223, 337 N.E.2d 655 (1st Dist. 1975); Lauch v. Monning , 15 Ohio App.2d 112, 239 N.E.2d 675 (1st Dist. 1968); Bates & Springer, Inc. v. Nay, 91 Ohio Law Abs. 425, 187 N.E.2d 415 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1963); Milbourn v. Aska , 81 Ohio App. 79, 77 N.E.2d 619 (3rd Dist. 1946). But see Woda Mgmt. & Real Estate v.

Grant, No. 15 -16-43, 2017 WL 3382166 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 7, 2017) (held that the lease granted the landlord the right to select between accepting late rent payments with a late payment fee or terminating the lease, an d thus any pattern of accepting late rent payments and the fee is consistent with the lease, not a waiver of the lease).

When a waiver occurs, the due date for the tenant’s rent payments is established by the course of conduct between the parties. Id. If the tenant tenders rent in compliance with that course of conduct, the landlord is estopped from refusing the rent and from evicting the tenant for nonpayment or late payment of the rent.112 Id.

For example, if a rental agreement requires the tenant to pay rent by the fifth day of the month and, under an established course of conduct, the tenant has been permitted to pay rent by the tenth day of the month, the landlord may not refuse the tenant’s tender of the January rent on January 10 and may not evict the tenant for nonpayment or late payment of the January rent. However, if the same tenant tenders the January rent on January 12, the landlord may refuse the tenant’s tender of the January rent and may evict the tenant for nonpayment of the January ren t. See Byrneport Apts. II v.

Williams, No. L -19-1277, 2020 WL 3485726 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 26, 2020);

Southgate Woods Apts. v. Lanier, No. L-83-189, 1983 WL 7006 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Nov. 25, 1983).

111 In these eviction actions, since the frequ ency of the tenant’s late rent payments that the landlord has accepted determines whether a course of conduct has been established, a trial court’s refusal to admit evidence of those payments is reversible error. J & M Trailer Court v.

Dissette, No. 1556, 1987 WL 16917 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 9, 1987).

112 One court has held that this estoppel doctrine does not apply to a landlord’s course of conduct of accepting late rent payments after service of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate. Greenbriar,
Inc. v. Chadwick, No. WD-00-057, 2001 WL 127301 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 16, 2001).

For this waiver, the scope of the course of conduct of accepting late rent payments may be tied to a notice period, instead of a particular date of the month. For example, in Schroeder Co. v. Coates, 172 Ohio App.3d 254, 874 N.E.2d 832 (6th Dist. 2007), the court found a course of conduct of rent paym ents up to the expiration of the ten - day notices of termination. The court held that the landlord properly rejected the tenant’s tender of rent because the tenant had tendered it after the expiration of the ten- day notice period.

There is no fixed standar d for determining whether a course of conduct of accepting late rent payments has been established. However, the case law provides some guidance for this determination.

Court decisions finding that a course of conduct had been established include:

Fairborn Apts. v. Herman, No. 90-CA-28, 1991 WL 10962 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan.

31, 1991) (five late payments in two years); Finkbeiner v. Lutz, 44 Ohio App.2d 223, 337 N.E.2d 655 (1st Dist. 1975) (thirty late payments in fifteen years); McKoy v. Bell,
No. 2021-CVG-008782 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 9, 2021) (month-to- month tenancy, following a two-year lease; initial two months of the month-to-month tenancy, rent collected at the premises during the first week of the next month, creating course of conduct for purposes of the third month);GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Skowroski,
No. CVG -2001087 (Ohio Mun., Barberton, Aug. 26, 2020) (after a twenty -year tenancy, the current twelve-month written lease required rent payment by the first day of the month, but, in April, May, and June 2020, the tenant paid half the rent by the 15th and half the rent by the 30th of the month, creating a course of conduct for rent payment for July 2020); Husein v. Payne , No. 2020 -CVG-005042 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 2 8, 2020) (during five -year tenancy, tenant consistently paid the rent out of her second paycheck of the month and would generally pay the rent by the last Friday of the month); Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jeffrey, No. 09-CVG- 7378 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Jan. 4, 2010) (eight late payments in eighteen months, including “multiple occasions the payment was more than one month late”); Tolliver
v. Warren, No. 99 -CVG-13123 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 28, 1999) (five late payments in fourteen months); Cleveland Hous. Network Ltd. P’ship X v.

Salgado, No. 96 -CVG-10871 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 16, 1996) (eleven late payments in eleven months, including two bulk payments);Insignia Mgmt.

Group v. Tate , No. 94 -CVG-6277 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Jan. 20, 1995) (five late payments in one year); Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Scott , No. 91 -CVG-205 (Ohio Mun., Fremont, May 23, 1991) (four late payments in eleven months); Selnick v. Baker, No.

141 86-CV-21282 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Nov. 30, 1987) (five or six late payments in eighteen months).

Court decisions finding that a course of conduct had not been established include:

Simco Mgmt. Corp. v. Snyder, No. 98-CA-210, 2000 WL 309396 (Ohio App., 7th Dist.,
Mar. 20, 2000) (two late payments in ten months);Simoudis v. Diehl, 116 Ohio App.3d 296, 688 N.E.2d 16 (11th Dist. 1996) (initial three months of tenancy is too soon to establish a course of conduct, even with a late payment in each month);BWS Props. v.

LaSalle, Inc., No. 51119, 1986 WL 9110 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 21, 1986) (three or four late payments in thirteen years).

An established course of conduct of accepting late rent payments remains in effect unless and until the landlord provides the tenant with advance notice that the course of conduct will no longer be permitted. Fairborn Apts. v. Herman, No. 90-CA-28, 1991 WL 10962, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 31, 1991);Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth.
v. Fambro, No. C -790556, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 10243, at *7 -8 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Oct. 22, 1980); Finkbeiner v. Lutz, 44 Ohio App.2d 223, 226, 337 N.E.2d 655 (1st Dist. 1975);Bates & Springer, Inc. v. Nay, 91 Ohio Law Abs. 425, 427, 187 N.E.2d 415 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1963); GMS Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Skowroski , No. CVG - 2001087 (Ohio Mun., Barberton, Aug. 26, 2020); Husein v. Payne, No. 2020-CVG- 005042 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 28, 2020);Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth.
v. Jeffrey , No. 09 -CVG-7378 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Jan. 4, 2010); Eastcare III v.

Shockley, No. 2009-CV-5424 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., Oct. 28, 2009, and Dec.

7, 2009); Myszka v. Bledsoe, No. 00-CVG-08398 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Nov. 9, 2000);

Cleveland Hous. Network Ltd. P’ship X v. Salgado, No. 96-CVG-10871 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 16, 1996).

The advance notice must be given to the tenant a reasonable time in advance of the rent due date. Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Fambro , No. C -790556, 1980 Ohio
App. LEXIS 10243 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Oct. 22, 1980); Myszka v. Bledsoe, No. 00- CVG-08398 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Nov. 9, 2000); Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Scott , No. 91- CVG-205 (Ohio Mun., Fremont, May 23, 1991); Evergreen Terrace Props. v.

Stephenson, No. 011775 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 22, 1988).

Since the enactment of R.C. Chapter 5321, court decisions on the number of days that meet this reasonable advance notice requirement include: 113 Cincinnati Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Fambro, No. C-790556, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 10243 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Oct. 22, 1980) (20 days is reasonable); Cornerstone of Kettering v. Hood , No.

05-CVG-2546 (Ohio Mun., Kettering, June 24, 2005) (8 days is not reasonable);

Classic Real Estate v. Bowen, No. M79-CVG-01404 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Mar.

12, 1979) (30 days is reasonable; 20 days is not reasonable);Evergreen Terrace Props.
v. Stephenson , No. 011775 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 22, 1988) (7 days is reasonable, in week-to-week tenancy).

The advance notice must be in writing. Fairborn Apts. v. Herman, No. 90-CA-28, 1991 WL 10962, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 31, 1991); Bates & Springer, Inc. v.

Nay, 91 Ohio Law Abs. 425, 427, 187 N.E.2d 415 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1963); GMS
Mgmt. Co., Inc. v. Skowroski , No. CVG -2001087 (Ohio Mun., Barberton, Aug. 26, 2020); Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jeffrey, No. 09-CVG-7378 (Ohio Mun., Dayton,
Jan. 4, 2010); Cornerstone of Kettering v. Hood , No. 05 -CVG-2546 (Ohio Mun., Kettering, June 24, 2005); Maxwell v. Parker , No. CVG -951631 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, Jan. 23, 1996); Evergreen Terrace Props. v. Stephenson, No.

011775 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 22, 1988);Selnick v. Baker, No. 86-CV-21282 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Nov. 30, 1987).But see Associated Estates Corp. v. Smith,
No. 46054, 1984 WL 5227 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 4, 1984) (oral notice permitted where tenant admitted that she had received it).

The advance notice must reasonably advise the tenant that the course of conduct will no longer be permitted. Myszka v. Bledsoe , No. 00 -CVG-08398 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Nov. 9, 2000). A notice merely stating that the landlord does not want to carry any balance forward fails to meet this standard. Id.

The advance notice will not rescind an established course of conduct if, after initially serving the notice, the landlord continues to accept late rent payments and, on each such occasion, re -serves a similar advance notice. Crossroads Somerset Ltd. v.

Newland, 40 Ohio App.3d 20, 531 N.E.2d 327 (10th Dist. 1987).

113 Prior to the enactment of R.C. Chapter 5321, one court held that three days advance notice was reasonable, relative to a tenant who pays rent monthly. Bates & Springer, Inc. v. Nay, 91 Ohio Law Abs. 425, 427, 187 N.E.2d 415 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1963).

143

### I. Renewal of Periodic Tenancy Due To

Partial Payment of Rent In a month-to-month tenancy, the landlord’s acceptance of a partial payment of the monthly rent renews the tenancy. Fairborn Apts. v. Herman, No. 90-CA-28, 1991 WL 10962, at *4 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 31, 1991); FMJ Props. v. Hinton, No. 50314, 1986 WL 4396, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 10, 1986).114 This renewal occurs even if the partial payment of rent is tendered and accepted after the rent due date. See, e.g., Phipps v. Cable , No. CVG -91-0099 (Ohio Mun., Portsmouth, Mar. 14, 1991).

This renewal also occurs even if the month-to-month tenancy is established in a written lease that requires good cause for termination or nonrenewal. See, e.g. , Fairborn Apts. v. Herman, No. 90-CA-28, 1991 WL 10962 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan.

31, 1991).

When a landlord’s acceptance of a part ial payment of rent renews a month -to- month tenancy, the landlord may not evict the tenant for nonpayment of rent for that month.115 Fairborn Apts. v. Herman , No. 90 -CA-28, 1991 WL 10962, at *4 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 31, 1991); FMJ Props. v. Hinton, No. 50314, 1986 WL 4396, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 10, 1986).

### J. Timely Tender of Rent Refused

A landlord may not refuse a timely and full payment of the rent and then evict for nonpayment of that rent. Pinewood Gardens Apts. v. Whiteside, No. 25989, 2014 WL 2169720 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 23, 2014); Prof’l Invs. of Am., Inc. v. Ross, No.

44540, 1982 WL 2579 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 1982);Caringi v. 2819 Harvard, Inc., No. 42834, 1981 Ohio App. LEXIS 13998 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 12, 1981);

114 See also Nutter v. Rhoades , No. 07-CVG-02622 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Oct. 15, 2007);

Tolle v. Hostetler, No. CVG-930632 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Jan. 18, 1994); Jones v.

Ranzy, No. 93 -CVG-00904 (Ohio Mun., Shaker Heights, June 16, 1993) (follows Hinton);

Moore v. Shumaker, No. 91-CVG-1659 (Ohio Mun., Lancaster, Jan. 2, 1992) (follows Hinton);

Phipps v. Cable , No. CVG -91-0099 (Ohio Mun., Portsmouth, Mar. 14, 1991); Williams v.

Terry, No. 78-CVG-24893 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Oct. 20, 1978).

115 While acceptance of partial payment of rent renews the month-to-month tenancy, the tenant is still obligated to pay and remains liable for the unpaid rent.See Pros Corporate Mgmt. Servs.,
Inc. v. Ashley S. Rose, Ltd., 228 Ill. App.3d 573, 582, 592 N.E.2d 609 (1992).

Real Prop. Servs. Corp. v. Bonen, No. 93-CVG-7804 (Ohio Mun., Marion Cty., May 26, 1993); Ragan v. Sitzler, No. 92 -CVG-7027 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Aug. 13, 1992).

The basis for this rule is a general contract principle – the failure on the part of one party to perform in accordance with a lease will be excused when performance is prevented by the other party to the lease – which is discussed in Section II.Z, below.

“Timely tender of rent is a defense to an eviction for non -payment of rent.” Cuyahoga River Assocs. Ltd. P’ship v. MJK Corp., No. 68673, 1996 WL 17292, at *4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 18, 1996).

For this defense, a timely tender of rent includes a tender of rent during any grace
period. E.g., Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Moore, No. 08-CA-10, 2009 WL 1372986 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 14, 2009) (ten -day grace period); Columbus Metro. Hous. Auth.
v. Jackson, No. 81AP-802, 1982 WL 4010 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 4, 1982) (ten- day grace period).

A landlord may refuse a timely but partial payment of the rent and then evict for nonpayment of rent. Cuyahoga River Assocs. Ltd. P’ship v. MJK Corp., No. 68673, 1996 WL 17292, at *4-5 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 18, 1996). Cf. Julian Invs., Inc. v.

Dudley, No. 98-CA-85, 1999 WL 64227, at *7 (Ohio App., 2nd Di st., Feb. 12, 1999) (tender of partial payment of past due rent).

As a general rule , a landlord also may refuse a full but untimely payment of the rent and then evict for nonpayment of rent. See Pinewood Gardens Apts. v. Whiteside,
No. 25989, 2014 WL 2169720, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 23, 2014); Simco
Mgmt. Corp. v. Snyder, No. 98-CA-210, 2000 WL 309396, at *5 (Ohio App., 7th Dist.,
Mar. 20, 2000). But, in some circumstance, a landlord’s refusal to accept a full but untimely payment of the rent may give rise to an eviction defense. Three of those circumstances are discussed in Section II.H, above (course of conduct), Section II.BB, below (equity), and Section III.E, below (right to cure, assisted housing).

### K. Tender of Rent Excused


#### 1. Vain Act

Generally, the law does not require a party to engage in a vain act. Showe Mgmt.
Corp. v. Moore, No. 08-CA-10, 2009 WL 1372986, ¶ 42 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 14, 2009).

This general principle of law applies to a tenant’s obligation to pay rent. Id. “[A] tenant’s obligation under a rental agreement to pay or tender rent can be excused where 145 the landlord, through its conduct, renders the requirement to tender meaningless, vain, or useless.” Id. Accord Pinewood Gardens Apts. v. Whiteside, No. 25989, 2014 WL 2169720, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 23, 2014); Greenbriar Estates v. Eberle , 130 Ohio Misc.2d 11, 14-15, 824 N.E.2d 172 (Ohio Mun., Clermont Cty., 2005).E.g., Century Pacific Hous. P’ship v. Jeffries , No. 21 -CVG-4124 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Oct.19, 2021).

In Moore, applying this principle, the court held that the tenant’s failure to tender rent in Dec. 2007 was excused, based on the landlord’s refusal to accept the tenant’s tender of rent during the grace period (but after the due date) each month from June to November 2007.

In Whiteside, applying this principle, the court held that t he tenant’s failure to tender rent for August 2013 was excused because of the owner’s policy of not accepting rent payments during the pendency of their security deposit litigation, “which would have made Whiteside’s tender of payment a vain act.” 2014 WL 2169720, ¶ 17.

In Eberle, applying this principle, the court held that the tenant’s failure to tender rent for August 2004 was excused, based on: (1) the tenant’s visit to the management office with a check book and an intent to pay rent for August; (2) the manager’s statement at that time that the tenant’s rent payment for August would not be accepted until the tenant coated the roof of his mobile home; (3) the tenant’s decision not to tender the August rent as a result of that statement; and (4) the absence of authority for the landlord to condition the tenant’s rent payment on whether the tenant coated the roof.

#### 2. Landlord’s Instructions

When a tenant retains (instead of tendering) a rent payment pursuant to the landlord’s instructions, the tenant is excu sed from the obligation to tender the rent subject to the terms of those instructions. E.g., Waterford Glen LLC v. Dowell , No.

05-CVG-1055 (Ohio Mun., Crawford Cty., Apr. 7, 2006).

In Dowell, the landlord instructed the tenant to hold onto the rent untilthe landlord resolved a water leak problem. Thereupon, the landlord commenced an eviction action against the tenant for nonpayment of the rent. The court held that the landlord’s instructions excused the tenant’s obligation to tender the rent and thus de nied the landlord’s claim for possession.

### L. Absence of Timely Demand – Rent

Collection at the Premises “In the absence of an agreement to the contrary, the general rule in Ohio is that payment of rent is to be made at the leased premises and that it is the landlord’s duty to go to the premises to collect rent from the tenant.” Davis v. Scullark, No. 57456, 1990 WL 145642, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 4, 1990). See also Slothower v.

Lally, 74 N.E.2d 654 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, 1947).

Also, a rental agreem ent (expressly or via custom) may require the landlord to collect the rent at the premises. E.g., Romine v. Winters, No. 98 -CVG-00907 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Sept. 3, 1998);Foster v. Jones, No. 93-CV-G&F-185 (Ohio Mun., Alliance, Mar. 9, 1993); Douglas v. Talley , No. 91 -CVG-19274 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Sept. 19, 1991);Kaylor v. Cunningham, No. M85-CVG-26535 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 25, 1985).

When a landlord is required to collect the rent at the premises, the landlord’s failure to appear at the premises to collect the rent bars the landlord from evicting the tenant for nonpayment of that rent. Id.

Furthermore, if a landlord is required to collect the rent at the premises on a certain day (or days) of the month, the landlord must app ear at the premises and demand payment of rent from the tenant on the required day(s), in order to evict the tenant for nonpayment of that rent. Douglas v. Talley , No. 91 -CVG-19274 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Sept. 19, 1991);Kaylor v. Cunningham, No. M85-CVG-26535 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 25, 1985).

For example, if the landlord is required to collect the rent at the premises on the second or third day of the month, “he cannot evict for non-payment of rent by merely making an unplanned, unann ounced visit to the property after the third of the month and finding the defendants [tenants] absent.” Kaylor v. Cunningham, No. M85-CVG- 26535, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 25, 1985).

### M. Payment of Rent that Accrued During a

Prior Eviction Action When an eviction action is dismissed or an eviction judgment is entered in favor of the tenant, the landlord must provide the tenant with a reasonable opportunity to pay the rent that accrued during the eviction action. Mazzarella v. McGinnes , No. 91- 147 CVG-30291 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 30, 1991). Accord Belch v.

Witts, No. 96-CVG-5839 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Dec. 31, 1996);Spreading Oaks Vill. v.

Scruggs, No. 93 -CVG-4-22 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., May 21, 1993). See also Conway v. Nissley, No. 68536, 1995 WL 723298, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 7, 1995); Waterside Estate Apts., Etc. v. Keen, No. 94-CVG-2296 (Ohio Mun., Massillon,
Dec. 13, 1994). But see Julian Invs., Inc. v. Dudley, No. 98-CA-85, 1999 WL 64227, at *2,5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 12, 1999) (dicta).

Absent this reasonable opportunity, “any dismissal of a complaint in forcible entry and detainer and any judgment entered for a tenant in such a case is rendered meaningless, as immediately following the dismissal or entry of judgment in the favor of the tenant, virtually every landlord could serve the tenant a new Revised Code 1923.04 notice to vacate based upon nonpayment of rent for the month in which the action was commenced.”116 Mazzarella v. McGinnes, No. 91-CVG-30291, slip op. at 4 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 30, 1991).

A reasonable opportunity to pay the accrued rent may be five or six days from the conclusion of the eviction action. Julian Invs., Inc. v. Dudley , No. 98 -CA-85, 1999 WL 64227, at *2,5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 12, 1999) (five days was reasonable);

Belch v. Witts, No. 96-CVG-5839 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Dec. 31, 1996) (six days was reasonable).

This reasonable opportunity to pay rent, relative to rent that accrued during the eviction action, does not apply to any rent that accrued prior to the eviction action.

Conway v. Nissley, No. 68536, 1995 WL 723298, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 7, 1995).

Likewise, it does not apply to any rent that became due after the eviction action.

Bishop Park Towers v. Sefcik, No. 2001-L-137, 2002 WL 1173498, at *2-3 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., May 31, 2002).

### N. Accord and Satisfaction

When a landlord claims nonpayment of rent or a charge as the basis for an eviction 116 Generally, a landlord will accept payment of the rent that accrues during an eviction action only after the conclusion of the action. Conway v. Nissley, No. 68536, 1995 WL 723298, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 7, 1995);Mazzarella v. McGinnes, No. 91-CVG-30291 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 30, 1991). A landlord’s earlier acceptance of such rent may constitute an acceptance of future rent that waives the R.C . 1923.04 notice to vacate. See Section II.C, above.

action, an accord and satisfaction may be a defense if two conditions are met: (1) the alleged nonpayment is an unliquidated debt or the tenant in good faith disputes the amount due; and (2) the tenant has tendered a payment to the landlord in an amount less than the amount that the landlord demanded, on the express condition that it shall be payment in full settlement of the landlord’s claim. Seeds, Grain & Hay Co. v.

Conger, 83 Ohio St. 169, 93 N.E. 892 (1910); Inger Interiors v. Peralta , 30 Ohio App.3d 94, 506 N.E.2d 1199 (8th Dist. 1986); First Nat ’l Bank & Trust Co. v.

Fireproof Warehouse & Storage , 8 Ohio App.3d 253, 456 N.E.2d 1336 (10th Dist.

1983).

The condition of full settlement may be stated either on the check,Inger Interiors, 30 Ohio App.3d at 95, or in a letter accompanying the payment, First Nat’l Bank & Trust Co., 8 Ohio App.3d at 255.117 In these circumstances, the landlord has two alternative courses of action. The landlord may reject or return the entire payment to thetenant or the landlord may accept and retain the payment upon the terms of the tenant’s condition. Conger, 83 Ohio St.

at 174-75; First Nat’l Bank & Trust Co., 8 Ohio App. 3d at 255. “He cannot accept the tender in such case and recover the balance which he claims ....” Conger, 83 Ohio
St. at 174-75.118 If the landlord accepts and retains the payment, the landlord takes the payment subject to the terms of the tenant’s condition, regardless of any protest that the landlord may make to the contrary. Inger Interiors, 30 Ohio App.3d at 95-96; First Nat’l Bank & Trust Co. , 8 Ohio App.3d at 255. For example, the landlord may not prevent an accord and satisfaction by crossing out the paid in full notation and writing partial payment on the check before cashing it. Inger Interiors, 30 Ohio App.3d at 95-96.

117 “Two essential safeguards built into the doctrine of accord and satisfaction protect creditors from overreaching debtors: ‘[1] there must be a good faith dispute about the debt and [2] the creditor must have reasonable notice that the check is intended to be in full satisfaction of the debt.” Allen v. R.G. Indus. Supply, 66 Ohio St.3d 229, 232, 611 N.E.2d 794 (1993).

118 For the period 1962 to 1994, R.C. 1301.13 established a procedure by which the cred itor could accept such payments as partial payment on the debt. See Lightbody v. Rust, No. 80927, 2003 WL 21710601 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 24, 2003).

149

### O. Rent Abatement, R.C. 1923.061(B)


#### 1. Overview

In an eviction action for nonp ayment of rent, a landlord’s violation of the rental agreement, R.C. Chapter 5321, or R.C. Chapter 4781 (former R.C. Chapter 3733) may be the basis for a defense under R.C. 1923.061(B).

The R.C. 1923.061(B) defense is essentially a set -off procedure. The tenant’s counterclaim under the rental agreement, R.C. Chapter 5321, or R.C. Chapter 4781 (former R.C. Chapter 3733),119 and the tenant’s rent deposit under R.C. 1923.061(B),120 are set-off against the landlord’s rent claim.

When a tenant asserts the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense, the court issues a judgment on the landlord’s rent claim and on the tenant’s counterclaim and, based on those judgments, determines to whom a net judgment is owed. R.C. 1923.061(B). See also Sandefur Mgmt. Co. v. Smith, 21 Ohio App.3d 145, 486 N.E.2d 1234 (10th Dist. 1985).

The rent deposited with the court, if any, is used to satisfy any net judgment owed the landlord and the remainder, if any, is returned to the tenant. 121 Id. Finally, if no rent remains due after the netting of the judgments and the disbursement of the rent deposited with the court, the eviction judgment is entered in favor of the tenant. Id.

Therefore, when a tenant asserts an R.C. 1923.061(B) defense, the court must hear and issue a judgment on the landlord’s rent claim and the tenant’s counterclaim before it issues a judgment on the eviction claim. Studor v. Roark, No. 94-CA-38, 1995 WL 347963, at *2 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Mar. 21, 1995); Sandefur Mgmt. Co. v. Smith, 21 Ohio App. 3d 145, 149, 486 N.E.2d 1234 (10th Dist. 1985). Accord Schwab v.

Lattimore, 166 Ohio App.3d 12, 848 N.E.2d 912 (1st Dist. 2006) (dissent). See, e.g., Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Bragg, No. 1379, 1987 WL 16079 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug.

27, 1987).

The R.C. 1923.061(B) defense is independent of any common law set-off defense 119 For a discussion on the tenant’s right to assert a counterclaim in an eviction action, see Section I.I, above. For a discussion on some of the tenant’s potential claims under R.C. Chapter 5321, see Chapters IX-XIII, below.

120 For a discussion on rent deposits under R.C. 1923.061(B), see Section II.O.2, below.

121 This distribution of the escrowed rent is mandatory, upon the entry of a final judgment on the landlord’s rent claim and the tenant’s counterclaim.Meyer v. Chieffo, 193 Ohio App.3d 51, 950 N.E.2d 1027, ¶ 19 (10th Dist. 2011).

(see Section II.Q., below).

#### 2. Deposit of Rent with the Court

A court may order a tenant, who asserts the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense, to deposit with the court all or part of the past due rent and, as it becomes due, the rent that becomes due during the pendency of the action. R.C. 1923.061(B).

The court’s authority to order these R.C. 1923.061(B) rent deposits is
discretionary. Bester v. Owens, No. 17388, 1999 WL 179261, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 31, 1999); Sandefur Mgmt. Co. v. Smith , 21 Ohio App.3d 145, 148, 486 N.E.2d 1234 (10th Dist. 1985);Smith v. Wright, 65 Ohio App.2d 101, 107, 416 N.E.2d 655 (8th Dist. 1979). The court may, but is not required, to order them. Wright, 65 Ohio App.2d at 107.

When a court orders a tenant to deposit rent with the court under R.C. 1923.061(B), if the tenant fails to deposit the rent, the tenant forfeits the right to assert the R.C.

1923.061(B) defense. Bester v. Owens , No. 17388, 1999 WL 179261, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 31, 1999); Conway v. Nissley, No. 68536, 1995 WL 723298, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 7, 1995) (implied term of R.C. 1923.061(B)). See also Napolski v. Champney , 295 Or. 408, 419 -20, 667 P.2d 1013 (1983) (construing a provision of the Oregon Landlord and Tenant Act that is nearly identical to R.C.

1923.061(B)). Cf. Deerwood Mgmt. Co. v. Bernsdorf, No. 78AP-470, 1978 Ohio App.

LEXIS 10021, at *4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 28, 1978) (order to deposit rent issued as condition for granting Civil Rule 60(B) motion for relief from eviction judgment).

These tenants still may assert any other relevant defense in the eviction action.

Bester v. Owens, No. 17388, 1999 WL 179261, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 31, 1999). A nd these tenants still may maintain their counterclaims separate and apart from the landlord’s claim for possession. See Section I.I, above.

Several appellate districts have adopted the position that a tenant may maintain the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense onl y if the trial court, in the exercise of its discretionary authority, decides to order the tenant to deposit rent with the court.

Under one line of cases, the tenant’s right to maintain the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense exists only if the court orders the tenant to deposit with the court all of the past due rent. Martins Ferry Jaycee Hous., Inc. v. Pawlaczyk , 4 Ohio App.3d 302, 304, 448 N.E.2d 512 (7th Dist. 1982); Smith v. Wright , 65 Ohio App.2d 101, 109, 416 N.E.2d 655 (8th Dist. 1979). See also Shelton v. H uff, No. 2012 -T-0101, 2014 WL 1352663, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 31, 2014) (2 -1 decision); Dayton Tool 151 Rental v. Ealy , No. 21849, 2007 WL 2897807 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 5, 2007) (absence of R.C. 5321.07 rent deposit).

Under a second line of cases, it appears that the tenant’s right to maintain the R.C.

1923.061(B) defense exists only if the court orders the tenant to deposit with the court all or part of the past due rent. Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Bragg , No. 1379, 1987 WL 16079, at *1-2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 27, 1987);Sandefur Mgmt. Co. v. Smith, 21 Ohio App.3d 145, 149, 486 N.E.2d 1234 (10th Dist. 1985).

The position of these appellate courts has no basis in R.C. 1923.061(B). No provision in R.C. 1923.061(B) can reasonably be construed to state that a tenant may assert the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense only if the court exercises its discretionary authority to order a rent deposit.

Furthermore, the position of these appellate districts is untenable. Its premise is that, in R.C. 1923.061(B), the legislature granted courts the discretionary authority to determine, on a case-by-case basis, whether a tenant may assert the R.C. 1923.061(B)
defense. Yet, none of these courts identifies any standard, let alone a rational one, pursuant to which the courts are to make this determination.

The position of the first line of cases is also untenable because it renders meaningless the R.C. 1923.061(B) provision that states that a court may order a tenant to deposit with the court “part of the past due rent. ”122 Under the first line of cases, whenever a court issues a rent deposit order pursuant to this provision, the tenant is barred from asserting the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense, regardless of whether the tenant complies with the order. If the tenant complies with the rent deposit order, the tenant is still barred from asserting the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense because the tenant has not deposited all of the past due rent.

The Oregon Supreme Court has addressed this issue and correctly adopted a position contrary to the position of these Ohio appellate districts. Napolski v.

Champney, 295 Or. 408, 667 P.2d 1013 (1983). The Oregon Supreme Court had an opportunity to address this issue because Or. Rev. Stat. § 91.810(1) is nearly identical to R.C. 1923.061(B); both were patterned after the Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act § 4.105. The Oregon Supreme Court recognized that tenants are permitted to assert the Or. Rev. Stat. § 91.810(1) defense even if the court decides not to order a 122 In construing R.C. 1923.061(B), a court is obligated to consider the consequences of its construction and the presumption that the legislature intended each provision in R.C.

1923.061(B) to be effective. R.C. 1.47(B) and 1.49(E).

rent deposit. Napolski, 295 Or. at 420 n.15. See also Kohner Props., Inc. v. Johnson, 553 S.W.3d 280 (Mo. 2018) ( en banc ) (under the implied warranty of habitability defense, courts exercise their discretion, on a case -by-case basis, in determining whether to require the tenant, as a condition for asserting the defense, to deposit future rent (in whole or in part) during the pendency of the action).123 If a tenant has sufficient funds to deposit with the court all past due rent, an order requiring the tenant to deposit all past due rent is immensely helpful to the tenant’s effort to establish an R.C. 1923.061(B) defense. If a tenant deposits with the court all past due rent, no rent will remain due after the court disburses the rent deposit, irrespective of the judgments on the rent claim and the counterclaim. Therefore, the rent deposit order, together with the rent deposits, insure that the R.C. 1923. 061(B) defense will be successful, so long as there is a counterclaim judgment in favor of the tenant for any amount. E.g., Dees v. Abrams , No. 91 -CVG-24751 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 4, 1991) (eviction judgment in favor of the tenant as a result of a $1,210 rent deposit under R.C. 1923.061(B), a $100 judgment for the tenant on a counterclaim, and a $l,240 judgment for the landlord on the rent claim). The requirement for a counterclaim judgment in favor of the tenant is discussed in Section II.O.3, below.

If a tenant has insufficient funds to deposit with the court all past due rent, the tenant may request a rent deposit order requiring the tenant to deposit “part of the past due rent,” such as an amount that the court reasonably anticipates wi ll be due after application of R.C. 1923.061(B).124 The tenant can reasonably argue that a rent deposit of part of the past due rent is consistent with the purpose, as well as the express language, of the R.C. 1923.061(B) rent deposit provision.

The purpose of the R.C. 1923.061(B) rent deposit is to protect the interests of the
parties. See Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act § 4.105, Comment.125 Cf.

Teller v. McCoy, 162 W. Va. 367, 393 -94, 253 S.E.2d 114 (1978) (implied warranty 123 For further discussion on this issue, see Greg Tasker, Comment, The Crippled Reform:

Retention of Possession by Rent-Withholding Tenants in Ohio Under Section 1923.061(B), 54

### U. Cin. L. Rev. 1035 (1986).

124 This discussion assumes that a tenant may maintain the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense when the court orders the tenant to deposit with the court part of the past due rent.

125 R.C. 1923.061(B) is based on and nearly identical to the Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act § 4.105.

153 of habitability). The interest of the landlord is to receive any rent due after application of R.C. 1923.061(B). The interest of the tenant is not to pay rent for services and facilities mandated by law but not received.

Both interests are reasonably protected if the tena nt deposits with the court an amount of past due rent that the court reasonably anticipates will be due after application of R.C. 1923.061(B). See Napolski v. Champney, 295 Or. 408, 420 n.15, 667 P.2d 1013 (1983). Cf. Teller v. McCoy , 162 W. Va. 367, 394 , 253 S.E.2d 114
(1978) (implied warranty of habitability);Pugh v. Holmes, 486 Pa. 272, 292, 405 A.2d 897 (1979) (same).

An order to deposit part of the alleged past due rent is particularly appropriate in two circumstances. One is where the tenant cla ims a financial inability to deposit all alleged past due rent due to the tenant’s use of all or part of those funds to remedy the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04 violations.126 The other is where the tenant claims a financial inability to deposit all alleged pastdue rent due to the tenant’s prior payment of part of that rent to the landlord, notwithstanding the landlord’s allegation to the contrary.See, e.g., Bester v. Owens, No. 17388, 1999 WL 179261 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 31, 1999).

With respect to a Hou sing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”) tenancy (see Section III.H.2, below), the court should determine the amount of any R.C.

1923.061(B) rent deposit in light of the respective obligations of the landlord, the tenant, and the public housing authority (“PHA”). Under a HCVP lease, the tenant is liable for a portion of the contract rent and the PHA is liable for the remainder of the contract rent. See 24 C.F.R. part 982, subpart K; 24 C.F.R. §§ 5.628-5.634.

When the PHA has not terminated the housing assista nce payments (“HAP”) contract,127 any R.C. 1923.061(B) rent deposit order should only require the tenant to deposit with the court all or part of the tenant’s portion of the contract rent. In these cases, the tenant is not liable for the PHA’s portion of the contract rent (see 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.310(b)) and, during the eviction action, the PHA is obligated to continue to pay 126 If the tenant remedied the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04 violations, the tenant may have a set-off defense, independent of R.C. 1923.061(B). See Section II.Q., below.

127 In the HCVP, the HAP contract is the contract under which the PHA makes the HCVP rent subsidy payments to the landlord on behalf of the tenant. See 24 C.F.R. § 982.451.

the PHA’s portion of the contract rent128 (see 24 C.F.R. § 982.311(b)).129 Likewise, when the PHA has terminated the HAP contract (and thus the HCVP lease)130 because of the landlord’s failure to maintain the premises, any R.C.

1923.061(B) rent deposit order should only require the tenant to deposit with the court all or part of the tenant’s portion of the contract rent under the terminated HCVP lease.

There are at least two alternative reasons for this conclusion.

First, in these circumstances, “even though the [PHA] terminated the HAP contract with the landlord and ceased making HAP payments, the tenants never assumed the responsibility of paying the entire contract rent and cannot be forced to do so now.” Curtis v. Surrette, 49 Mass. App. Ct. 99, 103, 726 N.E.2d 967 (2000). “The tenants may be held liable only for that portion of the contract rent that they assumed and agreed to pay.” Id. at 104. For Ohio case law holding that, in these circumstances, the tenant is not liable for the housing assistance payment portion of the contract rent, see Section III.H.2, below. See also Livecchi v. Pyatt , No. 2002 -CV-9073, 2003 WL 21246096 (N.Y. City Ct. May 15, 2003). Therefore, under the R.C. 1923.061(B) rent deposit provision, the rent due or becoming due is the tenant’s portion of the contract rent under the terminated HCVP lease.

Second, in these circumstances, even if a court finds that therent due or becoming due is the full contract rent under the terminated HCVP lease, an order to deposit only part of that rent (i.e., the tenant’s portion of the contract rent under the terminated lease) is the fairest and most appropriate rent deposit ord er. The landlord’s breach of the lease ( i.e., failure to maintain the premises) caused the nonpayment of the PHA’s portion of the contract rent. The tenant’s counterclaim for the loss of this HCVP rent subsidy is likely to succeed. And R.C. 1923.061(B) states that a court may order a deposit of part (and not all) of the relevant rent.

#### 3. Net Judgment

R.C. 1923.061(B) uses the term “net judgment” in describing the procedure for 128 For a discussion on whether these pay ments may waive the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate and, if so, how to avoid the waiver, see Section II.C, above.

129 For case law on the analogous matter of a supersedeas bond for an assisted housing tenant, see Section I.Q.3, above.

130 A PHA’s termination of a HAP contract automatically terminates the HCVP lease.See HUD Tenancy Addendum, form HUD-52641-A (July 2019), ¶ 13.

155 disbursing rent that has been deposited with the court. “The party to whom a net judgment is owed shall be paid first from the money paid into court ....” R.C.

1923.061(B).

Based on this “net judgment” provision, courts have held that a requirement of the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense is a judgment in favor of the tenant on the countercla im.

Sandefur Mgmt. Co. v. Smith, 21 Ohio App.3d 145, 149, 486 N.E.2d 1234 (10th Dist.

1985). See also Conway v. Nissley , No. 68536, 1995 WL 723298, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 7, 1995); Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Bragg , No. 1379, 1987 WL 16079 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 27, 1987).

Their reasoning is that “there can be no net judgment unless both parties are entitled to judgment, the landlord, for past due rent or rent becoming due during the pendency of the action, and the tenant, upon his counterclaim ....” Sandefur Mgmt. Co.
v. Smith, 21 Ohio App.3d 145, 149, 486 N.E.2d 1234 (10th Dist. 1985).

#### 4. Written Counterclaim

The counterclaim required by R.C. 1923.061(B) must be filed in accordance with the Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure and, therefore, must be filed in writing. Northgate
Apts. v. Gonzalez, No. CA80-34, 1981 WL 6340 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 26, 1981);

Clark Props. v. Hawk, No. 76AP-490, 1976 Ohio App. LEXIS 8193 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 21, 1976).

#### 5. Notice

Prior notice to the landlord is no t a requirement for asserting a claim as a counterclaim under R.C. 1923.061(B). Lobo v. Hoelscher , 44 Ohio Misc. 46, 336 N.E.2d 475 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, 1975). However, prior notice to the landlord may be an element of the underlying claim. See Sections IX.A.2 and IX.B.2, below.

#### 6. Payment of Deficiency

R.C. 1923.061(B) provides that, “[i]f no rent remains due after application of this division, judgment shall be entered for the tenant in the action for possession.” Clearly, this provision requires an eviction judgment in favor of the tenant if the sum of the counterclaim judgment (in favor of the tenant) and the R.C. 1923.061(B) rent deposit equals or exceeds the amount of the rent claim judgment (in favor of the landlord). E.g., Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Bragg, No. 1379, 1987 WL 16079 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 27, 1987); Sandefur Mgmt. Co. v. Smith , 21 Ohio App.3d 145, 486 N.E.2d 1234 (10th Dist. 1985). See also Napolski v. Champney, 295 Or. 408, 417, 667 P.2d 1013 (1983) (construing a provision of the Oregon Landlord and Tenant Act that is nearly identical to R.C. 1923.061(B)). But see Lee v. Douglas, No. 80-CA29, 1981 WL 6083 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Nov. 24, 1981); Rains v. Temple , No. 88-CVG-74 (Ohio Mun., Hillsboro, May 22, 1988).

Less clear is whether there are any circumstances in which R.C. 1923.061(B) requires an eviction judgment in favor of the tenant even though rent remains due after application of R.C. 1923.061(B). One such circumstance may be where: (1) the court orders the tenant to deposit with the court part of the past due rent, (2) the tenant deposits that amount with the court, (3) the court enters a judgment in favor of the tenant on the counterclaim, (4) the court enters a judgment in favor of the landlord on the rent claim, (5) the sum of the counterclaim judgment and the rent deposit is slightly less than the amount of the rent claim judgment, and (6) the tenant then tenders the net rent due the landlord.

Both the implied warranty of habitability case law 131 and equity support the position that, in this circumstance, the tenant has a right to tender the net rent due the landlord and thereby obtain an eviction judgment in favor of the tenant.

Under the implied warranty of habitability case law, when a breach of the warranty results in a partial reduction in rent, the tenant has the right to maintain possession of the premises by tendering the amount of rent that the court determines is due and
owing. E.g., Pugh v. Holmes, 486 Pa. 272, 292, 405 A.2d 897 (1979);Teller v. McCoy, 162 W. Va. 367, 392, 253 S.E.2d 114 (1978);Green v. Superior Court of City & County of San Francisco , 10 Cal.3d 616, 639, 517 P.2d 1168 (1974) (en banc); Curtis v.

Surrette, 49 Mass. App. Ct . 99, 100, 726 N.E.2d 967 (2000); Javins v. First Nat’l Realty Corp., 428 F.2d 1071, 1083 (D.C. Cir. 1970); Acad. Spires, Inc. v. Brown, 111 N.J. Super. 477, 488, 268 A.2d 556 (1970).

Likewise, equity requires that tenants in this circumstance be given anopportunity to tender the net rent due the landlord and thereby obtain an eviction judgment in favor of the tenant. It would surely be an inequitable result if a tenant were evicted, for example, because the sum of the tenant’s counterclaim judgment and rent deposit was one dollar or ten dollars less than the amount of the landlord’s rent claim judgment.

131 The implied warranty of habitability case law is relevant because R.C. 1923.061(B) is essentially a codification of that case law. See Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act § 2.104, Comment.

157

### P. Implied Warranty of Habitability,

Common Law The early common law in Ohio, as in other jurisdictions, did not recognize an implied covenant to make repa irs in residential leases. Holder v. Farmakis, 66 Ohio Law Abs. 279, 117 N.E.2d 491 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 1951).

Ohio courts uniformly adhered to that position until a small number of courts, between 1972 and 1975, recognized a common law implied warranty of habitability in residential leases. E.g., Wymer v. Kimbaugh, No. 74AP-547, 1975 Ohio App. LEXIS 7359 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 13, 1975); Smith v. Lewis, Nos. B93544-47 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Sept. 30, 1974); Lable & Co. v. Brooks, Nos. B-89207,89208 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Aug. 16, 1974); Glyco v. Schultz, 35 Ohio Misc. 25, 289 N.E.2d 919 (Ohio Mun., Sylvania, 1972).

The Ohio Landlord-Tenant Act of 1974 (see Section V.A, below) did not explicitly address the status of this common law warranty. One court held that a warranty of habitability exists only insofar as the Act so provides. Laster v. Bowman , 52 Ohio App.2d 379, 370 N.E.2d 767 (8th Dist. 1977). It relied solely on its conclusion that the Act provides the exclusive remedies as to the right s and obligations contained in the Act. Id.

Thereafter, the Ohio Supreme Court overruled this conclusion of the Laster
decision. Shroades v. Rental Homes, Inc. , 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 25, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981). It held that “the new remedies given tenants inR.C. Chapter 5321 are intended to be preventive and supplemental to other remedial measures.” Id. Under Shroades, the common law may supplement the R.C. 1923.061(B) remedy.

However, a tenant still must establish that, under Ohio common law, a warrantyof habitability exists. This may require the tenant to prove that the legislature, in enacting R.C. 1923.061(B), failed to redress adequately the deficiencies of the early common law and, for that reason, the courts should establish a common law warranty of habitability defense that is more expansive than R.C. 1923.061(B). It will, in all likelihood, be a difficult position on which to prevail. But a reasonable argument for this warranty defense can be made, based on a comprehensive analysis of the impl ied warranty of habitability theory and its reliance on contract law.

When the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense is available to a tenant, a common law warranty of habitability will afford the tenant no additional rights. Accordingly, the prudent approach for a te nant would be not to rely upon a common law warranty of habitability defense in lieu of R.C. 1923.061(B), but rather to consider using it only if R.C. 1923.061(B) is unavailable.

### Q. Set-Off Against Rent, Common Law

In an eviction action for nonpayment of rent , if the tenant has a monetary claim against the landlord, a common law set-off of the tenant’s claim against the landlord’s rent claim may be a defense to the eviction action. This defense is independent of the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense (see Section II.O., above).

The set -off defense may be asserted where a tenant has repaired a defective condition at the premises that the landlord was obligated but failed to repair. “In Ohio if, upon breach of a landlord’s agreement to make repairs, the tenant himself makes the repairs, he may set off the cost of the repairs in an action by the landlord for rent.” Kuhn v. Griffin , 3 Ohio App.2d 195, 205, 209 N.E.2d 824 (6th Dist. 1964). Accord Story v. Jamison , No. 92 -CVG-22935 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 11 , 1993). See also Smith v. Padgett , 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 347 n.1, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987).132 Consequently, in an eviction action for nonpayment of rent, a tenant may treat the cost of the repairs as a set -off against the alleged unpaid rent. See Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d at 347 n.1;Kuhn, 3 Ohio App.2d at 204-05; Story v. Jamison, No. 92-CVG-22935 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 11, 1993). If the cost of the repairs equals or exceeds the amount of the alleged unpaid rent, the requirements of the set -off defense are met.

In these circumstances, the set -off defense may be more effective than the R.C.

1923.061(B) defense (see Section II.O, above) because it does not include a rent 132 In Padgett, 32 Ohio St. 3d at 347 n.1, the Ohio Supreme Court stated:

We do not decide whether a tenant, upon the landlord’s breach of a promise to make repairs, may make the repairs himself or herself, and recover the cost of such repairs in an action for damages or treat the costs of repairs as a setoff against future rent. Nevertheless, the continuing validity of any indication in Holder, supra, that a tenant cannot do so would appear dubious.

See also Lanier v. Ramsey, No. 93-CVG-13802 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div. , Mar. 21,
1994) (under doctri ne of unjust enrichment, tenant awarded $2,400 to reimburse her for the funds that she spent repairing the premises to make them habitable).

159 deposit requirement. Under the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense, a court may order the tenant to deposit with the court all past due rent. See Section II.O.2, above. The tenant may have repaired the premises with funds that the tenant otherwise would have used to pay the rent. In such cases, often the tenant is unable to comply with the rent deposit order and, therefore, is denied the right to assert the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense.

The set-off defense also may be asserted where the landlord owes the tenant money for other reasons. E.g., Milbourn v. Aska, 81 Ohio App. 79, 77 N.E.2d 619 (3rd Dist.
1946) (set-off of reasonable value of storage of landlord’s restaurant counter at the premises).

### R. Tenant Repairs in Lieu of Rent,

Agreement In an eviction action for nonpayment of rent, if the rental agreement provides that the tenant is to make certain repairs to the premises in lieu of the obligation to pay certain rent (“repair agreement”), 133 the tenant’s repairs to the premises may be a defense to the eviction action. To prevail on this defense, a tenant must prove that the repair agreement e xists, that the tenant made the relevant repairs, and that the only unpaid rent is rent that has been forgiven by the repair agreement.

If the court finds that the amount of rent owed the landlord exceeds the amount of rent forgiven by the repair agreeme nt, this defense will be unsuccessful. E.g., Oakes
v. Buffalohead, No. 87 -CVG-01011 (Ohio Mun., Medina, Feb. 9, 1988); Hawkins v.

Collier, No. 87-CVG-884 (Ohio Mun., Portsmouth, Sept. 10, 1987).

When the repair agreement requires the tenant to repair R.C. 5321.04 violations, the repair agreement does not relieve the landlord of any R.C. 5321.04 duty or liability.134 A landlord’s R.C. 5321.04 duties may not be waived or modified. R.C.

5321.13. They are non-delegable. Strayer v. Lindeman, 68 Ohio St.2d 32, 427 N.E.2d 781 (1981). And any landlord liability under R.C. 5321.04 may not be waived or
modified. R.C. 5321.13.

133 As to whether this repair agreement may be an oral agreement, see Section XXII.A, below.

134 For a discussion on th e landlord’s R.C. 5321.04 duties, see Section VII.C, below. For a discussion on the landlord’s liability for a breach of the R.C. 5321.04 duties, see Chapter IX, below.

Nonetheless, the repair agreement is valid.
