While R.C. 5321.13 and Strayer bar such waiver, modification, or delegation, they do not bar a landlord from retaining a tenant (or another third party) to repair R.C. 5321.04 violations. “Nothing in Ohio’s Landlord Tenant Act prevents parties from agreeing to allow a tenant to perform services as a rent obligation in lieu of making financial payments.”Reid v. Plainsboro Partners, III, Nos. 09AP -442,456, 2010 WL 3610931, ¶ 36 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Sept. 16, 2010). See generally Wade v. Smith , No. 3 -87-24, 1989 WL 36578 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Apr. 18, 1989).

### S. Rent Adjustment – Tenant on Active

Duty, R.C. 1923.062 When an eviction action is for nonpayment of rent against a tenant or a manufactured home park resident (or against any member of the tenant’s or resident’s immediate family), and the tenant or resident is deployed on active duty, the tenant or resident (or immediate family member) may have a defense or remedy under R.C.

1923.062.

In such cases, the defense or remedy is available if: (1) “the tenant’s or resident’s ability to pay the agreed rent is materially affected by the deployment on active duty” (R.C. 1923.062(A)) and (2) the landlord or park operator operates five or more residential premises (R.C. 1923.062(C)).

When these conditions are met, upon the tenant’s or resident’s motion, the court is required to take one of two actions. First, it may “[a]djust the obligation under the rental agreement to preserve the interest of all parties to it.” R.C. 1923.062(A)(2).

Second, it may “[s]tay the proceedings for a period of ninety days, unless, in the opinion of the court, justice and equity require a shorter period of time.” R.C.

1923.062(A)(1). When a court selects the stay option, “the court may grant the landlord or park operator such relief as equity may require.” R.C. 1923.062(B).

Absent the tenant’s or resident’s motion, a court still may, on its own motion, grant one of these alternative defenses or remedies. R.C. 1923.062.

### T. Retaliation, R.C. 5321.02,.03


#### 1. Scope of the Retaliation Defense

Subject to the R.C. 5321.03 exceptions (see Section II.T.3, below), R.C.

161 5321.02(A) prohibits a landlord from bringing an eviction action against a tenant: (1) because the tenant complained to an appropriate governmental agency of a code violation at the premises th at materially affects health and safety, 135 (2) because the tenant complained to the landlord of an R.C. 5321.04 violation, or (3) because the tenant joined a tenant organization.136 A landlord’s violation of this prohibition is a defense to the eviction action.137 R.C.

5321.02(B)(1). The defense is known as the retaliation defense.138

#### 2. Proof Issues

“Retaliation is not often proved by direct evidence but the law, of course, recognizes no difference between circumstantial evidence and direct evidence.” Blue
v. Castlerock Props., Inc. , 77 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 3, 665 N.E.2d 295 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1996).

To establish the retaliation defense, a tenant must prove, by a preponderance of the 135 R.C. 5321.02(A) does not apply if the tenant complained to an appropriate governmental agency of a code violation at the premises that does not materially affect health or safety. Cf.

Voyager Vill., Ltd. v. Sudduth, No. 81-CA-63, 1982 Ohio App. LEXIS 15638 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 15, 1982) (construing R.C. 3733.09(A) (now R.C. 4781.36(A)), the manufactured home park anti-retaliation provision, which is essentially the same as R.C. 5321.02).

136 R.C. 5321.02(A) does not prohibit a landlord from bringing an eviction action against a tenant because of a personal conflict between the landlord and tenant. Cf. Troy v. Casale, No.

2976, 1982 WL 5894 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 31, 1982) (construing R.C. 3733.09(A)).

137 If a landlord violates this prohibition, the tenant also may have monetary claims against the landlord under R.C. 5321.02(B) and common law. See Chapter XI, below. But a tenant is permitted to assert the retaliation defense in the eviction claim regardless of whether the tenant asserts a retali ation monetary counterclaim. Kassem v. Barnes , No. C -190539, 2020 WL 4673121, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Aug. 12, 2020).

138 The decision in Reck v. Whalen, 114 Ohio App.3d 16, 20, 682 N.E.2d 721 (2nd Dist. 1996), erroneously holds that the retaliation de fense is “in the nature of an equitable defense,” which permits a court to “weigh all the other equitable circumstances in determining whether to issue a writ of restitution.” The holding is erroneous because R.C. 5321.02(B)(1) categorically states that, if a landlord brings an eviction action in violation of R.C. 5321.02(A), “the tenant may use the retaliatory action of the landlord as a defense to the action.” This categorical statement in R.C. 5321.02(B)(1) establishes a legal defense that is not subje ct to any weighing of the
equities. The Reck decision’s rationale for its holding is that it will preclude tenants who are delinquent in rent from asserting the retaliation defense. Its rationale is baseless since R.C.

5321.02(A) is subject to R.C. 5321. 03, and R.C. 5321.03(A)(1) precludes tenants who are delinquent in rent from asserting the retaliation defense.

evidence, that the landlord’s decision to evict was in response to an acti vity of the tenant that R.C. 5321.02(A) protects. Karas v. Floyd, 2 Ohio App.3d 4, 6, 440 N.E.2d 563 (2nd Dist. 1981). See also Timbercreek Vill. Apts. v. Myles, No. 17422, 1999 WL 335307, at *7 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 28, 1999);Davis v. Reed, Nos. 68699,68700, 1996 WL 347814, at *6 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 20, 1996); Howard v. Simon, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 18, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist. 1984);Tolle v. Cross, No. CVG-1400181 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Dec. 12, 2014). Cf. Voyager Vill. Ltd. v. Willia ms, 3 Ohio App.3d 288, 299, 444 N.E.2d 1337 (2nd Dist. 1982) (construing R.C. 3733.09(A) (now R.C. 4781.36(A)).

This burden of proof requires a causal link between the tenant’s complaint (or tenant organization activities) and the landlord’s decision to te rminate the tenancy (“required causal link”). It generally exists if, absent the tenant’s complaint or tenant organization activities, “the eviction action would probably not have been commenced.” Karas, 2 Ohio App.3d at 6. See generally Higbee v. Starr , 698 F.2d 945 (8th Cir. 1983).

An essential element of the required causal link is that, when the landlord decided to evict the tenant, the landlord knew of the tenant’s engagement in the protected
activity. Absent that knowledge, the required causal lin k cannot be shown. E.g., Felman v. Coleman , No. 15686, 1996 WL 325501 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 14,
1996) (failure to prove retaliatory motive because landlord served tenant with notice of termination before landlord knew of tenant’s complaint to the ci ty inspection department); Braff Mobile Home Co. v. McFarland, No. 8-265, 1982 WL 5815 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 23, 1982) (failure to prove retaliatory motive because no evidence that landlord knew of tenant’s complaint about the water supply or the tenant’s contacts with various governmental agencies regarding the water supply).See generally Barber v. Am. Sec. Bank, 655 F. Supp. 775, 779 (D.D.C. 1987) (“As a matter of law, there can obviously be no retaliation if the retaliator did not know about the protected activity”), appeal dismissed, 841 F.2d 1159 (D.C. Cir. 1988).

Other evidence that is needed to establish the required causal link varies from case to case, but often includes evidence that falls into one of two categories.

The first category is evidence that the landlord decided to evict the tenant soon after the tenant’s engagement in a protected activity. Such evidence may be a substantial factor in determining whether the required causal link exists. Cont’l Enters., Ltd. v. Franklin, No. 102899, 2016 WL 1941209 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 19, 2016); Reid v. Plainsboro Partners, III , Nos. 09AP-442,446, 2010 WL 3610931 163 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 16, 2010); Karas v. Floyd, 2 Ohio App.3d 4, 440 N.E.2d 563 (2nd Dist. 1981).

However, it does not create a presumption of unlawful retaliation. Howard v.

Simon, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist. 1984); 139 Karas, 2 Ohio App.3d at 6; Polson v. Peterson, No. 91-CVG-55 (Ohio Mun., Newton Falls, Mar. 19, 1991).

“[T]he finder of fact must indepen dently determine the reasons behind a landlord’s action.” Weishaar v. Strimbu, 76 Ohio App.3d 276, 286, 601 N.E.2d 587 (8th Dist.

1991).

The second category is evidence of the landlord treating the tenant in a different manner than the landlord has treated other tenants. For example, inBelvoir Cliffs Apts.
v. Bembry, 56 Ohio Misc. 37, 383 N.E.2d 1170 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, 1978), the tenant proved retaliation by, among other things, proving that the landlord refused to accept the tenant’s late rent payme nt even though the landlord customarily had accepted such payments from other tenants.

Other circumstantial evidence in support of the retaliation defense may include:
(1) that the tenant paid rent timely and otherwise acted in accordance with the requirements of the rental agreement, (2) that the landlord never complained to the tenant about the conduct alleged in the complaint as the reason for the eviction, and/or
(3) that the landlord is evasive in responding to inquiries about the reason for the eviction.

If a tenant proves that the landlord brought the eviction action in violation of R.C.

5321.02(A), the burden of proof shifts to the landlord. Karas v. Floyd, 2 Ohio App.3d 4, 6, 440 N.E.2d 563 (2nd Dist. 1981). See also Timbercreek Vill. Apts. v. Myles, No.

17422, 1999 WL 335307, at *7 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 28, 1999). See generally Meacham v. Knolls Atomic Power Lab., 554 U.S. 84, 91 (2008) (“‘[w]hen a proviso ...

carves an exception out of the body of a statute or contract, those who set up suc h exception must prove it’” (quoting Javierre v. Cent. Altagracia , 217 U.S. 502, 508 (1910))). At this point, the landlord may still defeat the retaliation defense by proving that one of the R.C. 5321.03 exceptions applies. Id.

139 In Howard, the court found no unlawful retaliation where: “the tenant’s complaint was lodged in an obnoxious manner[;] [t]he landlord immediat ely told the tenant that he found this conduct unacceptable and dangerous; and the landlord had previously received 40 -50 complaints from the tenant without retaliating.” 18 Ohio App.3d at 18.

#### 3. R.C. 5321.03 Exceptions

R.C. 5321.03 bars the R.C. 5321.02 retaliation defense if any of the following four circumstances exists.
(1) The tenant is in default in the payment of rent. R.C. 5321.03(A)(1).See Di Fiore v. Booker , No. 108946, 2020 WL 2992105 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 4, 2020); Mushi v. Dietz Prop. Group, No. 18-AP-774, 2019 WL 4450155 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 17, 2019);Timbercreek Vill.
Apts. v. Myles, No. 17422, 1999 WL 335307 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 28, 1999); Witkowski v. Arditi, 123 Ohio App.3d 26, 702 N.E.2d 1231 (7th Dist. 1997);Weldon Square Apts. v. Lyons, No. 84AP-06, 1985 WL 9901 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 19, 1985); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Watkins, 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984);

Gerber v. Bistor, No. 1795, 1982 WL 2806 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Oct.

27, 1982).
(2) The condition about which the tenant complained was caused by the tenant or the tenant’s guest. R.C. 5321.03(A)(2).
(3) The performance of the repairs necessary t o achieve code compliance would effectively deprive the tenant of the use of the premises. R.C.

5321.03(A)(3). See Wagar v. Hatnavy, No. 1433, 1985 WL 4734 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 26, 1985).
(4) The tenant is holding over the tenant’s term. R.C. 5321.03(A)(4).

The R.C. 5321.03(A)(4) exception (“holdover exception”) applies when the alleged retaliatory act is the landlord’s refusal to renew a fixed-term lease. E.g., Krantz
v. Pahnke, No. 2021-CA-0043, 2022 WL 39486 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 5, 2022) (nonrenewal of a lease upon the expiration of its one-year term); Indian Hills Senior Cmty., Inc. v. Sanders, No. 78780, 2001 WL 958055 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 23,
2001) (nonrenewal of a lease upon the expiration of its two -year term); Kaleda v.

Whitehurst Co., No. L -00-1048, 2000 WL 1205420 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 25,
2000) (nonrenewal of a lease upon the expiration of its one -year term). See also McCarthy v. Caplan , No. 79721, 2001 WL 1671422 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 6,
2001) (dicta).

The holdover exception also applies to month-to-month tenancies when the alleged retaliatory act occurred after the tenant became a holdover tenant. E.g., Siegler v.

Batdorff, 63 Ohio App.2d 76, 83, 408 N.E.2d 1383 (8th Dist. 1979). InSiegler: (1) the thirty-day notice of termination was served on March 31, (2) the tenants engaged in a 165 protected activity ( i.e., complained of conditions) on April 13, (3) the tenancy was terminated as of May 1, and (4) the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate was served on May

#### 2. The holdover exception applied inSiegler because the landlord’s alleged retaliatory

act – service of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate – occurred on May 2, when the tenants were holdover tenants.

The holdover exception should not apply to month -to-month tenancies when the alleged retaliatory act is the service of the R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of termin ation (see Section I.D, above). But the appellate districts are split on this issue.

Three appellate districts have held that the holdover exception does not apply i n these circumstances: Ruble v. M & L Props., Ltd. , No. 10 -COA-006, 2010 WL 5452110 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 20, 2010); Reid v. Plainsboro Partners, III, Nos.

09AP-332,446, 2010 WL 3610931 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 16, 2010); Voyager
Vill. Ltd. v. W illiams, 3 Ohio App.3d 288, 444 N.E.2d 1337 (2nd Dist. 1982) (construing R.C. 3733.091(A)(3) (now R.C. 4781.37(A)(3)), the manufactured home park holdover provision that is essentially the same as R.C. 5321.03(A)(4)). See also Tolle v. Cross , No. CVG -1400181 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Dec. 12, 2014) (follows Ruble).

One appellate district has held that the holdover exception does apply in these circumstances: Associated Estates Realty Corp. v. Samsa , No. 84297, 2004 WL 2847838 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2004).

In determining whether Ruble, Reid, and Voyager Vill. are correctly decided, or Samsa is correctly decided, a court is obligated to consider which decision(s) better advance the object sought to be obtained by the R.C. 5321.02,.03 prohibition a gainst
retaliation. See R.C. 1.49(A). In making this determination, a court is also obligated to consider the consequences of the statutory construction in each of these decisions (see R.C. 1.49(E)) and the presumption that the legislature intended each provision in R.C. 5321.02 to be effective (see R.C. 1.47(B)).140 Under these criteria, Ruble, Reid, and Voyager Vill. are correctly decided because they, and not Samsa, advance the object sought to be obtained by the R.C. 5321.02,.03 prohibition against retaliation. The Samsa decision is also flawed because it renders meaningless the R.C. 5321.02(B) retaliation defense.

140 Also, R.C. Chapter 5321 is a remedial statute. See Section V.A, above. Therefore, it must be liberally construed to promote its object and assist the parties in attaining justice. See R.C.

1.11.

The purpose of the R.C. 5321.02,.03 prohibition a gainst a landlord’s retaliatory termination of a tenancy and the related eviction action (“purpose of the retaliation prohibition”) is as follows:

In order to protect the tenant in the exercise of his new statutory rights and to promote reporting of housin g code violations, the new Act restricts the landlord’s absolute right to terminate a periodic tenancy.

Traditionally, some landlords would respond to a complaining tenant by terminating the lease. The effect of termination was to silence that particular tenant while setting an example for other tenants. Since retaliatory eviction on the part of the landlord has the potential to undermine the entire Landlord -Tenant Act, the legislature has prohibited such conduct.

Robert J. Croyle, Comment, Ohio Residential Landlord-Tenant Act, 8 Akron L. Rev.

519, 538-39 (1975). See also John E. Campion, Note, The Ohio Landlord and Tenant Reform Act of 1974, 25 Case W. Res. L. Rev. 876, 929-30 (1974-75).

The Ruble, Reid, and Voyager Vill. decisions correctly recognize that the purpose of the retaliation prohibition can be attained only if the R.C. 5321.02(A) prohibition encompasses a landlord’s retaliatory service of an R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of
termination. In retaliatory eviction actions against month -to-month tenant s, the landlord invariably claims a right to possession under the landlord’s service of an R.C.

5321.17(B) notice of termination. If R.C. 5321.02(A) does not encompass the landlord’s retaliatory motive for serving the R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of termination, the landlord’s eviction claim will never constitute a violation of R.C. 5321.02(A), notwithstanding the landlord’s retaliatory motive.

As noted in Voyager Vill. , if the holdover exception were applicable to the landlord’s service of the R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of termination, it would “vitiate the protection afforded by the defense of retaliation as regards any periodic tenant, since it could be reasoned that a periodic tenant becomes a holdover after the landlord declares the tenancy terminated or decline s to renew.” Voyager Vill., 3 Ohio App.3d at 295. Accord John E. Campion, Note, The Ohio Landlord and Tenant Reform Act of 1974 , 25 Case W. Res. L. Rev. 876, 941 (1974 -75). “Such an interpretation is circular, it makes a nullity of the retaliation defen se because the label of ‘holdover’ summarily disposes of the entire issue, i.e., whether the landlord had a right to evict in the first instance.” Id.

Furthermore, if the holdover exception were applicable to the landlord’s service of 167 the R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of termination, it would also directly conflict with the R.C.

1.47(B) presumption that “the entire statute is intended to be effective.” Since the R.C.

5321.02 retaliation defense would be unavailable to both month-to-month tenants and tenants with a fixed-term lease ( i.e., virtually all tenants), this construction of R.C.

5321.03(A)(4) would render meaningless the R.C. 5321.02 retaliation defense.

Ruble, Reid, and Voyager Vill. recognize that, relative to month -to-month tenancies, applying the hol dover exception only to cases in which the landlord’s retaliatory act occurred after the tenant became a holdover tenant, as in Siegler, is consistent with the purpose of the retaliation prohibition. In those cases, the landlord’s retaliatory act did not cause the tenant to be without a possessory right. “Where the tenant no longer has possessory rights to protect he should not be protected from eviction.” Voyager Vill. Ltd. v. Williams, 3 Ohio App.3d 288, 295, 444 N.E.2d 1337 (2nd Dist. 1982). Accord John E. Campion, Note, The Ohio Landlord and Tenant Reform Act of 1974, 25 Case W. Res. L. Rev. 876, 941 (1974-75).

#### 4. Dual Motive Issue

A common issue in retaliatory eviction cases is the “dual motive” issue. The issue arises when a landlord is evicting a tenant for two or more reasons and one of them is
retaliation. The issue is, in such circumstances, to what extent, if any, may the landlord’s motivation be retaliation without violating the law.

An early Ohio decision on the dual motive issue is Gateway Apts. v. Darrah, Nos.

76-CVG-725,928 (Ohio Mun., Lyndhurst, Aug. 22, 1977). It held that R.C. 5321.02 is violated whenever the retaliation is a resultant factor in the decision to terminate the
tenancy. Id.

Other jurisdictions provide a broad spectrum of responses to this issue. See Comment, Landlord-Tenant: Proving Motive in Retaliatory Eviction – Minnesota’s Solution, 61 Minn. L. Rev. 523 (1977); Comment, Prohibition of Retaliatory Eviction in Landlord-Tenant Relations: A Study of Practice and Proposals, 54 N.C.L. Rev. 861, 871-76 (1976). At one end, courts have held that retaliation must be the sole motive.

E.g., Dickhut v. Norton , 45 Wis.2d 389, 173 N.W.2d 297 (1970). At the other end, courts have held that any retaliatory motive is sufficient to e stablish the retaliation
defense. E.g., Silberg v. Lipscomb, 117 N.J. 491, 285 A.2d 86 (1971).141 An Oregon Supreme Court decision on this dual motive issue – Elk Creek Mgmt.
Co. v. Gilbert, 353 Or. 565, 303 P.3d 929 (2013)– may be the most instructive decision for determining this issue for Ohio. Ohio and Oregon have similar retaliation provisions, and both are modeled on the Uniform Residential Landlord -Tenant Act (“URLTA”).

In Gilbert, the Court analyzed in detail the URLTA retaliation provision, rel ative to the dual motive issue, including the URLTA comments and the case law relied on in drafting the provision. It thereupon held that the proper test is a “but for” test. Under this test, a tenant “may prevail by proving that the tenant’s protected a ctivity was a ‘material and substantial’ factor in the landlord’s decision. The tenant need not prove that the tenant’s protected activity was the ‘sole’ or ‘dominant’ reason for the landlord’s decision.” Gilbert, 353 Or. at 584-85.

### U. Discrimination


#### 1. Federal and Ohio Statutes

When a tenant’s race, color, religion, sex, handicap, familial status, or national origin is a significant factor in the landlord’s decision to terminate a tenancy, generally it is a defense to the eviction action under one (or more) of the following five statutes.
(1) The federal Fair Housing Act. 42 U.S.C. § 3604.
(2) Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973 (“Section 504”), if disability discrimination is alleged and the landlord receives federal financial assistance.142 29 U.S.C. § 794.
(3) Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act (“ADA”), if disability discrimination is alleged and the landlord receives state or local government financial assistance. 42 U.S.C. §§ 12131, 12132.
(4) The Civil Rights Act of 1866, if race discrimination is alleged. 42 141 The U.S. Supreme Court is similarly split on the meaning of the term “because of” in the context of both the ADEA ( see Gross v. FBL Fin. Servs., Inc. , 557 U.S. 167 (2009) (5 -4 decision)) and Title VII retaliation ( see Univ. of Texas Southwestern Med. Ctr. v. Nassar , 570 U.S. 338 (2013) (5-4 decision)).

142 Landlords who receive federal financial assistance include the lan dlords who participate in the assisted housing programs that are discussed in Chapters III and XXIV, below.

169 U.S.C. § 1982.
(5) The Ohio Civil Rights Act. R.C. 4112.02(H).

The federal Fair Housing Act does not apply to any tenancy in a building that contains four or fewer dwelling units, if the landlord resides in one of the units. 42 U.S.C. § 3603(b)(2). The other statutes do not contain this exception.

“[W]hen reviewing R.C. Chapter 4112 claims, Ohio courts may consider [analogous] federal statutes and case law.” Reid v. Plainsboro Partners, III , Nos.

09AP-442,446, 2010 WL 3610931, ¶ 54 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 16, 2010).

Accord K&D Mgmt., L.L.C. v. Masten, No. 98894, 2013 WL 3422890, ¶ 16 n.1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 3, 2013); Carter v. Russo Realtors , No. 00AP -797, 2001 WL 537019, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 22, 2001); Strader v. Johnson, No. 98AP- 202, 1998 WL 894602, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 22, 1998).

For a comprehensive review of the discrimination defense (including relevant case
law) under these federal statutes, see the following resources.
(1) Books: Robert G. Schwemm, Housing Discrimination: Law and Litigation (Thomson Reuters 2008-2021); John P. Relman, Housing Discrimination Practice Manual (Thomson Reuters 2009-2020).
(2) Pamphlet: Bazelon Center for Mental Health Law,What “Fair Housing” Means for People with Disabilities (201 8) (available at www.bazelon.org/our-work/mental-health-systems/housing).
(3) Fair Housing Fact Sheets : Bazelon Center for Mental Health Law (available at www.bazelon.org/our-work/mental-health-systems/ housing).
(4) Joint Statement of the Department of Housing and Urban Development and the Department of Justice: Reasonable Accommodations Under the Fair Housing Act (May 17, 2004) (available at www.justice.gov/ sites/default/files/crt/legacy/2010/12/14/joint_statement_ra.pdf).
(5) Joint Statement of the Department of Housing and Urban Development and the Departmentof Justice: Reasonable Modifications Under the Fair Housing Act (Mar. 5, 2008) (available at www.hud.gov/sites/ documents/reasonable_modifications_mar08.pdf).
(6) HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1, ch.2.143
(7) Websites: National Fair Housing A lliance (www.nationalfairhousing.

org/legal-resources); Bazelon Center for Mental Health Law (www.bazelon.org).

#### 2. Reasonable Accommodation Requirements

The Fair Housing Act requires a landlord “to make reasonable accommodations in the rules, policies, practices, and services,” when i t is necessary to accommodate the needs of a tenant with a disability. 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(B). This requirement also exists under Section 504 and the ADA. Roe v. Hous. Auth. of City of Boulder, 909 F.
Supp. 814, 821 (D. Colo. 1995). See also Anast v. Commonwealth Apts., 956 F. Supp.

792, 800 (N.D. Ill. 1997).

In the eviction context, “a reasonable accommodation is required where there is a causal link between the disability for which the accom modation is requested and the misconduct that is the subject of the eviction or other challenged action.”Boston Hous.
Auth. v. Bridgewater, 452 Mass. 833, 848, 898 N.E.2d 848 (2009).

Absent the requisite causal link, the tenant is not entitled to the r easonable
accommodation. See, e.g., Norwich Apts. II v. Sanders, 159 N.E.3d 340, 2020-Ohio- 4540 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 2020) (insufficient evidence to find a causal link between the disability and the failure to pay rent);Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Brown, No.

C-120580, 2013 WL 5432087, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept. 25, 2013) (insufficient evidence of the requisite link between the disability and the misconduct – threats and yelling at other tenants).

“Once a tenant shows that a disability is the reason for noncompliance with a lease, the tenant must show that an accommodation will allow the tenant to remain compliant with the lease.” Bazelon Center, Fair Housing Information Sheet #4, Using Reasonable Accommodation to Prevent Eviction (rev. Dec. 11, 2003), at 2.

The tenant’s initial request for the reasonable accommodation may be made during the eviction action. Radecki v. Joura, 114 F.3d 115 (8th Cir. 1997);Roe v. Hous. Auth.

of City of Boulder, 909 F. Supp. 814 (D. Colo. 1995);Roe v. Sugar River Mills Assocs., 820 F. Supp. 636 (D.N.H. 1993); Boston Hous. Auth. v. Bridgewater, 452 Mass. 833, 143 The term “HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV -1” means U.S. Dep’t of Hous. & Urban Dev., Occupancy Requirements of Subsidized Multifamily Housing Programs Handbook, 4350.3 REV-1 (November 2013), as amended.

171 898 N.E.2d 848 (2009); Lebanon County Hous. Auth. v. Landeck, 967 A.2d 1009 (Pa.
Super. 2009); Douglas v. Kriegsfeld Corp., 849 A.2d 951 (D.C. App. 2004); Cornwell & Taylor LLP v. Moore , No. C8-00-1000, 2000 WL 1887528 (Minn. App. Dec. 22, 2000).

The reasonable accommodation requirement does not apply to any tenant whose tenancy would constitute a direct threat to the health or safety of other individuals. 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(9) (Fair Housing Act); 24 C.F.R. § 8.3 (qualified individual with
handicaps) (Section 504); 56 Fed. Reg. 35693, 35701 (July 26, 1991) (refers to 28 C.F.R. § 36.208) (ADA). See, e.g., Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Brown , No. C- 120580, 2013 WL 5432087 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept. 25, 2013).

However, when determining whether such threat to health and safety exists, the court is required to consider the threat (if any) that would exi st if the reasonable accommodation were granted. Boston Hous. Auth. v. Bridgewate r, 452 Mass. 833, 841, 898 N.E.2d 848 (2009); Douglas v. Kriegsfeld Corp. , 849 A.2d 951, 960 (D.C.
App. 2004); Roe v. Sugar River Mills Assocs., 820 F. Supp. 636, 639-40 (D.N.H. 1993).

The health and safety exception “does not come into play until after an effort for accommodation has been made and the court has determined, through a probing factual inquiry, that no reasonable accommodation could ameliorate the situation sufficiently to protect the health, safety, and property of others.”Douglas v. Kriegsfeld Corp., 849 A.2d 951, 960 (D.C. App. 2004).

Also, the health and safety exception is an affirmative defense to the duty to provide a reasonable accommodation. Twp. of W. Orange v. Whitman , 8 F. Supp.2d 408 (D.N.J. 1998). The burden of proof is on the landlord to prove this defense.

Dadian v. Vill. of Wilmette, 291 F.3d 831 (7th Cir. 2001). The landlord is required to prove that “no ‘reasonable accommodation’ will eliminate or acceptably minimize the risk that he [the tenant] poses to other residents” of the property. Roe v. Sugar River Mills Assocs., 820 F. Supp. 636, 640 (D.N.H. 1993). Accord Roe v. Hous. Auth. of City of Boulder, 909 F. Supp. 814, 822-23 (D. Colo. 1995).

When a landlord has made a reasonable accommodation in the rules and the tenant violates the modified rules, the landlord may evict the tenant for noncompliance with the modified rules. E.g., Parma Pub. Hous. v. Hoag, No. 07-CVG-00695 (Ohio Mun., Parma, May 2, 2007) (accommodation permitted one cat for therapeutic purposes and tenant kept two or more cats, even after landlord’s notice of violation). See also Woodside Vill. v. Hertzmark , No. SPH9204 -65092, 1993 WL 268293 (Conn. Super.

June 22, 1993) (failing to walk dog in designated area and failing to clean up after it), aff’d on other grounds, 36 Conn. App. 73, 647 A.2d 759 (1994).

For a summary of the reasonable accommodation requirement in the eviction context, see the Bazelon Center, Fair Housing Information Sheet #4, Using Reasonable Accommodation to Prevent Eviction (rev. Dec. 11, 2003). For articles on the reasonable accommodation requirement in the eviction context, see Liam Garland, Fairer Housing for People with Disabilities: Securing an In-Place Tenant’s Right to Reasonable Accommodation, 40 Clearinghouse Rev. 503 (2007); Fred Fuchs, Using Reasonable-Accommodation Provision of the Fair Housing Act to Prevent the Eviction of a Tenant with Disabilities, 41 Clearinghouse Rev. 272 (2007).

Under the Supremacy Clause, state courts are required to consider Fair Housing Act defenses in eviction actions. Rodriguez v. Westhab, Inc. , 833 F. Supp. 425 (S.D.N.Y. 1993).

Ohio courts recognize the right of tenants to assert a Fair Housing Act defen se in eviction actions. E.g., Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Brown , No. C-120580, 2013 WL 5432087, ¶¶ 2-5,11-19 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept. 25, 2013);K&D Mgmt., L.L.C.
v. Masten, No. 98894, 2013 WL 3422890, ¶¶ 15 -17,19 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 3, 2013); Olympic Realty v. Zaleski, No. 11AP-971, 2013 WL 1303777, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 29, 2013); Palombo v. Hayes, Nos. 65781,66714, 1995 WL 229120, at *7-8 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 13, 1995). See also Lable & Co. v. Flowers, 104 Ohio App.3d 227, 661 N.E.2d 782 (9th Dist. 1995) (fair housing counterclaim in FED action); Gladden v. Walker, No. 12-CV-29809 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Aug. 15,
2014) (fair housing third-party complaint in FED action).

Ohio eviction actions in which the court ordered a reasonable accommodation and, for that reason, denied the landlord’s eviction claim include:Shiloh Grove Ltd. P’ship
v. Ransom , No. M9408 -CVG-025818 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 12, 1994) (reasonable accommodat ion: accept rent three weeks late); Showe Mgmt. Corp. v.

Stover, No. M9306 -CVG-17876 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 23, 1993) (reasonable accommodation: permit temporary absence from the rental unit);

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Humphries , No. 93 -CVG-6480 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 29, 1993) (reasonable accommodation: provide periodic assistance from social worker, transportation to psychiatric assistance, and training to staff, before evicting for inappropriate tenant behavior). Cf. LeGare v. Cypress Point, LLC, No. 3:08 -cv-01591-JGC (N.D. Ohio Oct. 22, 2008) (settlement agreement and order: where tenant’s sole source of income is disability benefits, tenant permitted to pay rent by first business day after third day of the month, withoutincurring a late fee);

173 Manor Park Apts., LLC v. Garrison , No. 2004-L-029, 2005 WL 940871 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Apr. 22, 2005) (reversed judgment for unpaid rent on ground that tenant was entitled to accommodation to vacate in middle of one year lease, where unit caused tenant to suffer claustrophobia and landlord denied transfer request). See generally Reid v. Plainsboro Partners, III, Nos. 09AP-442,446, 2010 WL 3610931 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 16, 2010) (R.C. 4112(I) retaliation claim for rent incr ease and eviction).

### V. Lease, Procedures for Termination

“[A] landlord must strictly comply with the [termination procedure] terms of a lease before instituting forfeiture proceedings.” Holmes v. Convenient Food Mart, Inc., No. 98-L-272, 2000 WL 263736, at *2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 3, 2000).

A lease may include procedures for a termination of the lease based on the tenant’s breach of the lease. E.g., Holmes v. Convenient Food Mart, Inc., No. 98-L-272, 2000 WL 263736 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 3, 200 0) (notice of default and opportunity to cure); Gallagher v. Borden, Inc., 84 Ohio App.3d 185, 616 N.E.2d 577 (10th Dist.
1992) (notice of default and opportunity to cure);Sandefur Co. v. Jones, 9 Ohio App.3d 85, 458 N.E.2d 390 (10th Dist. 1982) (notice of termination).

The landlord’s failure to comply strictly with those procedures is a defense to the eviction action. Id.

A lease may include a provision for its automatic renewal unless and until the landlord or tenant provides the other party with cert ain notice. E.g., Frank v. Flynn, 120 Ohio App. 361, 197 N.E.2d 657 (8th Dist. 1964).

Such provisions generally are enforceable. 144 Id. See also Note, Residential Renewal Leases and the Ohio Statute of Conveyances: Invalidation and Subsequent Treatment, 27 Clev. St. L. Rev. 231 (1978) (review of Ohio case law on automatic renewal provisions and on the relationship between such provisions and the statute of conveyances).145 A lease may include a tenant option to renew for a specified period of time. In re Kindinger, 219 B.R. 214, 216-17 (Bankr. N.D. Ohio 1998) (applying Ohio law). When 144 For a discussion on renewal provisions in a lease, see Section VII.J, below.

145 The relationship between automatic renewal provisions and the statute of conveyances is also discussed in Section XXII.A.1, below.

two or more tenants are parties to a lease that gives the tenants an option to renew, the option may not be exercised by only one of the tenants.L & H Realty Co. v. Oberholtz, 65 Ohio App.2d 279, 418 N.E.2d 401 (9th Dist. 1979).

However, if a lease with a fixed term includes neither a notice of default provision nor a renewal provision, the procedural requirements for the landlord to terminate the tenancy and commence an eviction action are limited. During the term of the lease, with one exception,146 prior notice to the tenant is not required to effect a forfeiture of the lease. R.C. 1923.02(A)(9).147 At the expiration of the term of the lease, the lease automatically terminates, without any notice. Gladwell v. Holcomb, 60 Ohio St. 427, 54 N.E. 473 (1899). Upon the forfeiture or expiration of the lease, the only notice requirement for commencing the eviction action is the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate (see Section I.C.1.a, above).

### W. Lease, Non-Material Breach

A lease is a contract and, therefore, is subject to the traditional rules governing contract interpretation. See Section VII.A, below.

Under “common contract rules a party will not be found to have breached a contract so long as that party is in substantial compliance with the agreement.” Moonlight Mobile Home Parks, Inc. v. Eichner , No. L-93-028, 1993 WL 434584, at *5 n.2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 29, 1993). See also Ohio Farmers Ins. Co. v.

Cochran, 104 Ohio St. 427, 427, 135 N.E. 537 (1922) (“merely nominal, trifling, or 146 The exception arises when the forfeiture is based on conduct of the tenant that violates both a lease provision and an R.C. 5321.05 provision that materially affects health and safety. In such cases, the thirty-day notice and cure period of R.C. 5321.11 applies. When the forfeiture is based on conduct of the tenant that violates the lease but not an R.C. 5321.05 provision that materially affects health and safety, the thirty-day notice and cure period of R.C. 5321.11 does not apply. For a discussion on these R.C. 5321.05, 5321.11 issues, see Section I.D.2, above.

147 “Pursuant to R.C. 1923.02(A)(9), a forcible entry and detainer proceeding can be had against ‘tenants who have breached an obligation imposed upon them by a written rental agreement.’ The statute empowers landlords to regain possession of their property upon a tenant’s breach of the rental agreement.” Dennis v. Morgan, 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 418, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000).

R.C. 1923.02(A)(9) seems to supplant the common law rule that permits forfeiture only if and to the extent that the lease so provides. But see Berkacs v. Perkins, 165 Ohio App.3d 576, 579, 847 N.E.2d 481 (6th Dist. 2006) (“Appellant’s life lease with appellee did not contain a provision for forfeiture as a remedy for breach of the terms of the lease. Therefore, we find that forfeiture was not an available remedy to appellant”).

175 technical departures are not sufficient to breach the contract”); Kersh v. Montgomery Developmental Ctr., Etc., 35 Ohio App.3d 61, 62, 519 N.E.2d 665 (10th Dist. 1987) (“default by a party who has substantially performed does not relieve the other party from performance”).

This contract principle has long been applied to leases in Ohio. E.g., Denison W.

Twenty-Fifth St. Improvement Co. v. Great Atl. & Pac. Tea Co. , 46 Ohio Law Abs.

246, 69 N.E.2d 79 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1946).

A landlord may evict a tenant for a breach of a lease only if the breach is material.

Moonlight Mobile Home Parks, Inc. v. Eichner , No. L-93-028, 1993 WL 434584, at *5 n.2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 29, 1993); ACME Precision Bldg., Ltd. v. Dayton Forging & Heat Treating, Inc., 23 B.R. 79, 83-84 (Bankr. S.D. Ohio 1982) (applying Ohio law); Fitzpatrick Realty v. Wilkey, No. 78-CVG-8102 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Nov.

17, 1978).

A landlord may not evict a tenant for an insign ificant or a hypertechnical breach of the lease. ACME Precision Bldg., Ltd. v. Dayton Forging & Heat Treating, Inc., 23 B.R. 79, 83-84 (Bankr. S.D. Ohio 1982) (applying Ohio law).

### X. Lease, Ambiguous Forfeiture Provision

A lease is a contract and, therefore, is subject to the traditional rules governing contract interpretation. See Section VII.A, below.

When the language of a forfeiture provision in a lease is ambiguous, a court is required to construe it against the party who was responsible for d rafting it. Bevy’s Dry Cleaners & Shirt Laundry, Inc. v. Streble, 2 Ohio St.2d 250, 256, 208 N.E.2d 528 (1965); Sholiton Indus., Inc. v. Royal Arms, Ltd., No. 17480, 1999 WL 355898, at *6 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 4, 1999); McClorey v. Hamilton County Bd. of Elections, 130 Ohio App.3d 621, 625, 720 N.E.2d 954 (1st Dist. 1998); GMS Mgmt. Co. v.

Seminaro, No. 63007, 1993 WL 277073, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 22, 1993);

Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. McCollum , 45 Ohio App.2d 197, 200, 341 N.E.2d 857 (1st Dist. 1975).

Likewise, when the punctuation of a forfeiture provision in a lease is ambiguous, a court is required to construe it against the party who was responsible for drafting it.

Farrell v. Deuble , 175 Ohio App.3d 646, 651, 888 N.E.2d 514 (9t h Dist. 2008);

Mandat v. Reinecker’s Bakery, No. 16241, 1993 WL 526653, at *3 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 15, 1993);Canady v. Cen. Benefits Mut. Ins. Co., 71 Ohio App.3d 363, 367, 594 N.E.2d 37 (10th Dist. 1991).

Therefore, with respect to most residentia l leases, any ambiguous forfeiture provision must be construed against the landlord, since the landlord typically is the party responsible for drafting it.

“Language [in a lease] is ambiguous if it is reasonably susceptible of two or more constructions.” McClorey v. Hamilton County Bd. of Elections, 130 Ohio App.3d 621, 625, 720 N.E.2d 954 (1st Dist. 1998). Accord Krantz v. Pahnke, No. 2021-CA-0043, 2022 WL 39486, ¶ 50 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 5, 2022).

### Y. Lease, Unconscionable Provision, R.C.

5321.14 Under R.C. 5321.14, any unconscionable provision in a residential lease is
unenforceable. See Section VII.K.1, below.

Therefore, a defense to an eviction action is that the lease provision on which the landlord relies to bring the action is unconscion able. E.g., Hi-Land Terrace, Inc. v.

Dinwiddie, No. 546, 1985 WL 6560 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 1, 1985) (lease provision requiring registration of and authorization for overnight guests); Fitzpatrick Realty v. Wilkey , No. 78 -CVG-8102 (Ohio Mun., Dayton , Nov. 17, 1978) (lease provision granting landlord right to declare forfeiture of lease for any tenant breach of the lease, however slight);Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Smith, No. 78-CVG-268 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, May 8, 1978) (lease provision requiring registration of overnight guests).

### Z. Lease, Landlord Prevents Performance

“Non-performance of a promise or condition in a lease is excused where performance is prevented by the other party to the lease.” 148 Prof’l Invs. of Am., Inc.
v. Ross, No. 44540, 1982 WL2579, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 1982).Accord Gary Crim, Inc. v. Rios, 114 Ohio App.3d 433, 436, 683 N.E.2d 378 (7th Dist. 1996);

Ambrosia Coal & Constr. Co. v. C.B.G., Inc., No. 94-CA-199, 1996 WL 242915, at *3 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., May 9, 1996); Greenbriar Estates v. Eberle, 130 Ohio Misc.2d 11, 15, 824 N.E.2d 172 (Ohio Mun., Clermont Cty., 2005). See also Barnes v.

148 This implements a general contract principle– Restatement (First) of Contracts, § 295 (1932)
– to which Ohio adheres.E.g. Mortgage Network, Inc. v. AmeriBanc Mortgage Lending, L.L.C., 177 Ohio App.3d 733, 741, 895 N.E.2d 917 (10th Dist. 2008); Ruddock v. C unningham, 43 Ohio App.3d 146, 149, 539 N.E.2d 1141 (6th Dist. 1987).

177 Welborn, No. 04-6855, 2005 WL 5533987 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., July 5, 2005) (counterclaim for unpaid rent, after wrongful eviction).

This rule has been a defense in eviction actions for nonpayment of rent. One example is where the landlord refuses a timely and full payment of rent. See Section II.J, above. Another example is where the landlord sets up a system for rent payments from a financial institution (using the tenant’s direct deposit of benefit payments) but fails to inform the financial institution of the tenant’s rent increase.Gary Crim, Inc. v.

Rios, 114 Ohio App.3d 433, 683 N.E.2d 378 (7th Dist. 1996).

This rule should likewise be a defense in eviction actions for other grounds, if the landlord’s action prevented the tenant from complying with the lease requirement.
AA. Repayment Agreement, Contract of Adhesion A contract of adhesion is “a standardized form contract prepared by one party, and offered to the weaker party, usually a consumer, who has no realistic choice as to the contract terms. See Black’s Law Dictionary (8th Ed. 2004) 342.” Taylor Bldg. Corp.

of Am. v. Benfield, 117 Ohio St.3d 352, 363, 884 N.E.2d 12 (2008). Accord Worldwide Asset Purchasing, L.L.C. v. Easterling , 186 Ohio App.3d 478, 481 n.1, 928 N.E.2d 1148 (10th Dist. 2009);W.K. v. Farrell, 167 Ohio App.3d 14, 25, 853 N.E.2d 728 (2nd
Dist. 2006).

“[A] contract of adhesion is not in all ins tances unconscionable.” Taylor Bldg.
Corp. of Am. v. Benfield, 117 Ohio St.3d 352, 363, 884 N.E.2d 12 (2008).Accord Bohl
v. Hauke, 180 Ohio App.3d 526, 532-33, 906 N.E.2d 450 (4th Dist. 2009).

A contract of adhesion is unenforceable if the party challenging it can prove “that the agreement is both procedurally and substantively unconscionable.” Taylor Bldg.
Corp. of Am. v. Benfield , 117 Ohio St.3d 352, 359, 884 N.E.2d 12 (2008). Accord W.K. v. Farrell, 167 Ohio App.3d 14, 25, 853 N.E.2d 728 (2nd Dist. 2006).

“Substantive unconscionability exists when the contract terms are determined to be commercially unfair and unreasonable.” W.K. v. Farrell, 167 Ohio App.3d 14, 25, 853 N.E.2d 728 (2nd Dist. 2006).

“Procedural unconscionability, on the other hand,exists when it is determined that there was no voluntary meeting of the minds by the parties to the contract under circumstances particular to that contract. Id. at 25.

Therefore, a contract of adhesion may be unenforceable if the less advantaged party proves: “(1) that the contract terms are one -sided or unreasonably favorable to the other party, (2) that disparity between the parties’ bargaining power denies the less advantaged party meaningful choice in accepting the terms of the contract, and (3) that the less advantaged party cannot obtain the desired product or service except by acquiescing in the contract.” Id. at 25.

When an eviction action is based on the tenant’s breach of a repayment agreement, and the repayment agreement is a contract of adhesion and unconscionable, a defense to the eviction action may be that the repayment agreement is unenforceable. E.g., Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jennings, No. 05-CVG-01669 (Ohio Mun., Lorain, Oct.

18, 2006).

In Jennings, the repayment agreement required the tenant to pay $145.15 per month, for 24 months, for fire damage to the dwelling unit. The tenant’s total income was $25 per month – a monthly gift from his brother to enable him to pay his $8.00 per month rent. There was no evidence that the tenant had started the fire or was otherwise responsible for it. The court held that the repayment agreement was unenforceable as a contract of adhesion.
BB. Equity

#### 1. Overview

It has long been the rule that “[e]quity abhors a forfeiture [of a leasehold] and will only decree it when such relief is clearly required.”149 Whitmore v. Meenach, 33 Ohio Law Abs. 95, 99, 33 N.E.2d 408 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1940). Accord Erie Metroparks Bd. o f Comm’rs v. Key Trust Co. of Ohio, N.A. , 145 Ohio App.3d 782, 788, 764 N.E.2d 509 (6th Dist. 2001); Galier v. Feder Pontiac, Inc., No. 56233, 1989 WL 142397, at *4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 22, 1989); Zanetos v. Sparks, 13 Ohio App.3d 242, 244, 468 N.E.2d 938 (10th Dist. 1984).

This rule applies to a forfeiture of a residential lease as well as a commercial lease.

Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Harris , Nos. C-820540,820541, 1983 WL 8893, at *2 n.3 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 15, 1983).

“The trial court, within its discretion, is entitled to weigh all equitable considerations in determining whether a forfeiture is to be declared.” S. Hotel Co. v.

149 R.C. 1901.18(C) confers upon municipal courts jurisdiction to hear this equity defense. See Section I.A.4, above, and Section XX.C.2, below.

179 Miscott, 44 Ohio App.2d 217, 222, 337 N.E.2d 660 (10th Dist. 1975). Accord Galier
v. Feder Pontiac, Inc. , No. 56233, 1989 WL 142397, at *3 -4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Nov. 22, 1989); Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Nusser, No. 1183, 1986 WL 7882, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 3, 1986).

“When a party raises an equitable defense, it is the responsibility of the court to weigh the equitable considerations before imposing a forfeiture.”150 Gorsuch Homes,
Inc. v. Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 436, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist . 1992). Accord Takis, L.L.C. v. C.D. Moreland Props., Inc., 180 Ohio App.3d 243, 250-51, 905 N.E.2d 204 (10th Dist. 2008); David v. Edwood Dev. Co., No. 19252, 2000 WL 46107, at *2 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 12, 2000); Greene Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Manning, No. 98- CA-55, 1999 WL 76456, at *5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 19, 1999); Urban Hollow
Apts. v. Johnson , No. 93 -APG10-1495, 1994 WL 232274, at *2 -3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 26, 1994).

“This is a fact specific analysis and ‘no one equitable maxim provides a complete answer’ to every case.”Esho v. Shamoon, Inc., No. L-06-1189, 2007 WL 949756, ¶13 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 30, 2007) (quoting S. Hotel Co. v. Miscott, Inc. , 44 Ohio App.2d 217, 222, 337 N.E.2d 660 (10th Dist. 1975)). Accord Portage Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Brumley, No. 2008-P-0019, 2008 WL 4693200, ¶ 93 (Ohio App., 11th Dist.,
Oct. 24, 2008).

If a trial court fails to consider a tenant’s equity defense, when entering a judgment for the landlord, the appellate court, as a matter of law, should reverse and remand the trial court decision. E.g., Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v. Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 435- 36, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992).

“Once the trial court has weighed the equitable considerations and reached a decision on whether to decla re a forfeiture, appellate review is limited to a determination of whether the lower court abused its discretion in its weighing of the equities.” BWS Props. v. LaSalle, Inc., No. 51119, 1986 WL 9110, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 21, 1986). Accord Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Hazelbaker , No. CA2005- 150 The equitable considerations may include, among other things: (1) the lack of evidence that the tenant acted maliciously or willfully, (2) where nonpayment of rent (or a charge) is at issue, the tenant’s tender of the unpaid rent (or charge) as of the time of trial, (3) the hardship on the tenant if an eviction is ordered, and, (4) where the tenant resides in assisted housing (see Section III.A, below), the tenant’s substantial interest in maintaining the rent subsidy. Urban Hollow
Apts. v. Johnson , No. 93 -APG10-1495, 1994 WL 232274 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 26, 1994); Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v. Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992).

11-031, 2006 WL 1976760, ¶ 25 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 17, 2006); Chillicothe
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Anderson , No. 1406, 1988 WL 69118, at *8 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 28, 1988); Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Nusser, No. 1183, 1986 WL 7882, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 3, 1986).

“[A] reviewing court cannot substitute its view of the equities for that of the trial court when the determination of the trial court is supported by sufficient evidence.” Galier v. Feder Pontiac, Inc. , No. 56233, 1989 WL 142397, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 22, 1989). Accord Southgate Woods Apts. v. Lanier, No. L-83-189, 1983 WL 7006 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 25, 1983) (dissenting opinion).

Notwithstanding this standard of review, appellate courts have reversed trial court decisions on a tenant’s equity defense.

They have reversed trial court decisions denying the equity defense. E.g., Greene
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Manning, No. 98-CA-55, 1999 WL 76456 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Feb. 19, 1999) (blind tenant’s failure to recertify timely due to housing authority’s failure to provide notice in an accessible format and its refusal to provide tenant with any auxiliary aid); Ward v. Washington Distribs., Inc., 67 Ohio App.2d 49, 425N.E.2d 420 (6th Dist. 1980) (tenant’s failure to exercise timely an option to renew the lease, as a result of an honest, good faith mistake). See also Urban Hollow Apts. v. Johnson,
No. 93 -APG10-1495, 1994 WL 232274 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 26, 1994) (remand to reconsider equity defense, relative to nonpayment of $239.66 repair charge, where trial court “made a conclusory assertion that defendant had not established grounds for equitable relief, [but] the findings of fact do not support that conclusion”).

They also have reversed trial court decisions granting the equity defense. E.g., Mecca Mgmt., Inc. v. Gouse, No. L-97-1185, 1997 WL 728635 (Ohio, App., 6th Dist.,
Nov. 21, 1997); Hilltop Vill. v. West, No. L-88-120, 1989 WL 10277 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 10, 1989).

The equity defense has been applied in virtually all types of eviction actions.151 Its application in eviction actions for nonpayment (or late payment) of rent is discussed
below. Its application in eviction actions against project-based assisted housing tenants 151 Only one court decision has placed any limit on the category of eviction actions to which the equity defense may be applied. The decision is Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Kilgore, 194 Ohio App.3d 767, 958 N.E.2d 187 (2nd Dist. 2011) (2 -1 decision). The Kilgore majority held that the equity defense does not apply in eviction actions when a “positive statute” is the basis for the eviction claim. The Kilgore decision, and related “innocent tenant” court decisions that rely on the equity defense, are discussed in Section III.G.8.d, below.

181 is discussed in Section II.BB.2, below. Its application in eviction actions against manufactured home park residents is discussed in Section II.BB.3, below.

It also has been applied in: (1) eviction actions for failing to maintain the premises, e.g., Galier v. Feder Pontiac, Inc., No. 56233, 1989 WL 142397 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Nov. 22, 1989); Newman Club of Columbus v. Higgy , 13 Ohio App. 227 (10th Dist.

1920); and (2) eviction actions for failing to renew timely a lease, e.g., Ward v.

Washington Distribs., Inc., 67 Ohio App.2d 49, 425 N.E.2d 420 (6th Dist. 1980).

The equity defense is often asserted in eviction actions for nonpayment (or late
payment) of rent. “A lease will not be forfeited for mere non -payment of rent where the equities of the parties can be otherwise adjusted.” Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Nusser,
No. 1183, 1986 WL 7882, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 3, 1986). Accord Zanetos
v. Sparks, 13 Ohio App.3d 242, 244, 468 N.E.2d 938 (10th Dist. 1984). “It is well established law in Ohio that a clause in a lease providing for a forfeiture for non - payment of rent is only for the purpose of securing payment of the rent ....” Nusser, 1986 WL 7882, at *2. Accord Zanetos, 13 Ohio App.3d at 244.

Therefore, a tenant’s tender of the unpaid rent, as of the time of trial, may enable the tenant to succeed on the equity defense.152 E.g., BWS Props. v. LaSalle, Inc., No.

51119, 1986 WL 9110 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 21, 1986); Zanetos v. Sparks, 13 Ohio App.3d 242, 468 N.E.2d 938 (10th Dist. 1984); S. Hotel Co. v. Miscott, 44 Ohio App.2d 217, 337 N.E.2d 660 (10th Dist. 1975); Peppe v. Knoepp, 103 Ohio App. 223, 140 N.E.2d 26 (10th Dist. 1956); Newman Club of Columbus v. Higgy, 13 Ohio App.

227 (10th Dist. 1920). 153 But see Winchester Station Coop., Inc. v. Walters , No. 96- APG02-204, 1996 WL 465240 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Aug. 13, 1996) (tendered rent, but not court costs and attorney’s fees, for which tenant was liable under the cooperative agreement); Meyer v. Carmichael, 29 Ohio App.2d 183, 279 N.E.2d 622 (1st Dist. 1971).

Likewise, if a landlord refuses to accept a third -party’s tender of the tenant’s full rental arrearage, before the time of trial, the tenant may prevail in the equity defense.

152 The list of court decisions in this paragraph are eviction actions against private housing or commercial tenant s. For court decisions in eviction actions against project -based assisted housing tenants, see Section II.BB.2, below. For court decisions in eviction actions against manufactured home park residents, see Section II.BB.3, below.

153 See also Kamvouris v. B anks, No. 00 -CVG-1586 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, July 24, 2000); DeVore v. Higgins, No. 99-CVG-06094 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Aug. 24, 1999).

E.g., Liberty Rental Props. LLC v. Rohr, No. 21-CVG-00705 (Ohio Mun., Akron, May 3, 2021) (equity defense applied where landlord refused rental assistance funds from Summit County Cares program). See also 16 Apts. Assocs., Inc. v. Lewis, 24 Misc.3d 127A, 889 N.Y.S.2d 884 (N.Y. App. D iv. 2009) (landlord did not have the right to refuse checks offered on tenant’s behalf by the Department of Social Services and by a charitable organization – Westchester Residential Opportunities).

However, if the tenant’s nonpayment (or late payment) o f rent is willful or malicious, equity will not relieve a forfeiture. Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Nusser, No. 1183, 1986 WL 7882, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 3, 1986); Zanetos v. Sparks, 13 Ohio App.3d 242, 244, 468 N.E.2d 938 (10th Dist. 1984). One co urt decision found “willful” late payment based on: (1) the tenant’s knowledge of the relevant eviction policy (three late payments within any twelve -month period) and, (2) with respect to the third late payment, the tenant’s negligent entrustment of a third party to deliver the payment, instead of placing it in the landlord’s mailbox, when she attended a family
funeral. Winchester Station Coop., Inc. v. Walters , No. 96 -APG02-204, 1996 WL 465240 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Aug. 13, 1996).

#### 2. Project-Based Assisted Housing

The equity defense is often asserted by tenants who reside in project-based assisted housing.154 A strong equitable consideration against a forfeiture of their tenancy is that, if a forfeiture is granted, the tenant loses a tenancy with a subsidized rent and with a lease term that the tenant may renew continually and the landlord must renew absent good cause to terminate the tenancy. Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Nusser , No. 1183, 1986 WL 7882, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 3, 1986); Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Harris, Nos. C-820540,820541, 1983 WL 8893, at *3 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 15, 1983). See also Urban Hollow Apts. v. Johnson , No. 93 -APG10-1495, 1994 WL 232274, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 26, 1994);Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v. Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 436, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992).

Under this defense, tenants who reside in project -based assisted housing have prevented a forfeiture of their tenancy in a variety of circumstances, including those set forth below.

154 For the definition of “project-based assisted housing,” see note 165, below.

183
(1) Nonpayment or late tender of rent .155 E.g., Cincinnati Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Brown, No. C-010459 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Feb. 27, 2002) (late tender of rent); Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Nusser, No. 1183, 1986 WL 7882 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 3, 1986) (late tender of rent due to reduced income and effective date of rent recertification); Stark Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Allen, No. 2016-CVG-2468 (Ohio Mun., Canton, June 21, 2016) (PHA confusion as to the amount of rent due); Ottawa Cove Apts. II v.

Butts, No. CVG -09-22798 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., Feb. 11,
2010) (late tender of rent due to caring for ailing mother out of town);

Morning Star Towers v. Robinson , No. 06 -CVG-26743 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 11, 2007) (tenant with disability and funds to pay rent arrearage at trial); Insignia Mgmt. Group v. Tate , No. 94 - CVG-6277 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Jan. 20, 1995) (late tender of rent);

Columbus Metro. Hous. v. Wagner , No. M9410 -CVG-31661 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 5, 1994) (late tender of rent due to uncle’s death); Shiloh Grove Ltd. P’ship v.
