Tyler , No. 89 -CVG-85 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Mar. 17, 1989) (nonpayment of rent due to loss of employment); Ecco Manor, Ltd. v. Pass, No. M88-CVG-42788 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Feb. 8, 1989) (late tender of rent); Indian Valley
Apts. v. Kamerer, No. R88-CVG-0978 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Sept.

26, 1988) (late tender of rent); Clinton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Arrington,
No. 88-CVG-139 (Ohio Mun., Wilmington, May 19, 1988) (late tender of rent due to late receipt of ADC check, which was not tenant’s fault);

Columbia-Athens, Ltd. v. Bagley, No. CIG-87-2-23 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., July 1, 1987) (nonpayment of rent due to theft of cash from the rental unit). But see Hilltop Vill. v. West, No. L-88-120, 1989 WL 10277 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 10, 1989); Marshall Plaza v. Watkins , No.

4161, 1987 WL 12113 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 3, 1987); Southgate Woods Apts. v. Lanier , No. L-83-189, 1983 WL 7006 (Ohio App., 6th 155 When an assisted housing tenant’s nonpayment or late tender of rent is due to circumstances beyond the tenant’s control, the tenant also may have a legal defense, based on the absence of good cause for the landlord’s termination of the tenancy. See Section III.G.1.a, below.

Dist., No v. 25, 1983); Capital Park Apts. v. Funderburke , No. M90 - CVG-18599 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., July 20, 1990).
(2) Nonpayment or late payment of charges. E.g., Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v.

Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992) (nonpayment of $9 07 charge for removing graffiti painted by tenant’s son, where tenant disputed the amount of the charge);156 Lovett v. Brown,
No. 97-CVG-5586 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Aug. 20, 1997) (late payment of utility charges); ABC Mgmt. Co. v. Young , No. M9410 -CVG-030140 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 9, 1994) (nonpayment of $36.34 repair charge); Sumner v. McKinley , No. M9306 -CVG-17388 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., July 30, 1993) (nonpayment of $20 charge for changing lock). See also Urban Hollow Apts. v. Johnson , No. 93-APG10-1495, 1994 WL 232274 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 26, 1994) (remand to reconsider equity defense, relative to nonpayment of $239.66 repair charge).
(3) Criminal activity of the tenant’s child or guest. See the court decisions and discussion on “innocent tenants” in Sections III.G.8.d and III.G.8.e, below.
(4) Recertification of income. E.g., Greene Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Manning,
No. 98-CA-55, 1999 WL 76456, at *5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 19,
1999) (blind tenant’s failure to recertify timely due to housing authority’s failure to provide notice in an accessible format and its refusal to provide tenant with any auxillary aid).
(5) Damage to the premises . E.g., Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Mayson ,
No. 97 -CVG-1293 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, May 5, 1998) (fire caused by tenant’s daughter’s use of candles during power outage;

tenant recovering from surgery, offered to pay cost ofrepair, and, along with daughter, took fire safety classes).
(6) Unauthorized occupant. E.g., Bella Vista Apts. v. Herzner , 125 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 796 N.E.2d 593 (Ohio Mun., Clermont Cty., 2003) (unauthorized occupancy by new wife and her three children);

Columbus Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Johnson , No. M9201 -CVG-00579 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Feb. 13, 1992) (unauthorized occupancy for 156 “Generally, cour ts, in balancing the equities, will relieve a tenant from the harsh consequences of a forfeiture where the payment of money damages will adequately compensate the landlord.” Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v. Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 436, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd
Dist. 1992). “That is true even where the forfeiture is requested because of damages done by a tenant’s children.” Id.

185 fourteen days by friend escaping from domestic dispute).

Four appellate court decisions that upheld the denial (or denied) the equity defense of project-based subsidized housing tenants also provide some helpful guidance.

First, Portage Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Brumley , No. 2008 -P-0012, 2008 WL 4693200 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 24, 2008), upheld the trial court’s denial of the equity defense where, at meetings before the trial, the PHA had offered to permit the tenant to stay, if she removed the offending household member (her son) from her
lease. The tenant refused this offer consistently, and refused to cooperate with the PHA, until after the trial had commenced.

Second, Schroeder Co. v. Coates, 172 Ohio App.3d 254, 874 N.E.2d 832 (6th Dist.

2007), upheld the trial court’s denial of the equity defense where the tenant did not tender the rent payment until eight days after the notice o f termination period had expired and never offered an excuse for the nonpayment of rent.

Third, Mecca Mgmt., Inc. v. Gouse, No. L-97-1185, 1997 WL 728635 (Ohio, App., 6th Dist., Nov. 21, 1997), reversed a trial court decision granting the equity defense where the emergency assistance needs of the tenant’s mother were only relevant to one of the three months of unpaid rent.

Fourth, Hilltop Vill. v. West, No. L-88-120, 1989 WL 10277 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Feb. 10, 1989), reversed a trial court decision granti ng the equity defense where the tenant lacked the requisite “clean hands” because of her delay of 43 days in reporting an increase in income and because of her failure to pay any rent while she disputed the amount due.

In some eviction actions, especially those against project -based assisted housing tenants, the facts may give rise to an argument that there was no material breach of the lease or, in the alternative, that the court should exercise its equity power to relieve the tenant from forfeiture of the lease, even if a material breach occurred. If a jury is hearing the eviction action, the issue of whether a material breach occurred is a fact question for the jury to decide, whereas the issue of whether equity should relieve a forfeiture is a matter fo r the judge to decide. See Ashmore v. Eversole , No. 15672, 1996 WL 685568, at *4 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 29, 1996). See generally Takis, L.L.C. v. C.D. Moreland Props., Inc. , 180 Ohio App.3d 243, 905 N.E.2d 204 (10th
Dist. 2008) (equity claim seeking rescission of lease).

A judge may be less inclined to allow the tenant to maintain possession on equity grounds following a jury finding of good cause for eviction. See, e.g. , Cincinnati
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Foster, No. 88-CV-05432 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty. Aug. 31, 1988). Therefore, when a tenant intends to assert a legal defense and an equity defense, depending on the facts and judicial assignment of the case, it may be more advantageous for the tenant not to demand a jury or to waive the tenant’s jury demand.

#### 3. Manufactured Home Park

The equity defense is often asserted by residents of manufactured home parks.

Two strong equity considerations against a forfeiture of their tenancies exist in virtually every case. One is that, if a forfeiture is grante d, the resident loses the resident’s manufactured home (if it cannot reasonably be moved) or incurs significant hardship and cost in moving the manufactured home. The other is that, if a forfeiture is granted, the resident loses a tenancy that the residen t may renew continual ly and the park operator must renew absent good cause. See Section IV.C.1, below.

Eviction actions for nonpayment of rent in which a manufactured home park resident prevailed on the equity defense include: Kason Manor Mobile Home Park v.

Payne, No. 09-CVG-01602 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Aug. 18, 2009) (park resident for 27 years, and tendered all unpaid rent and late fees prior to the eviction hearing);

Pikewood Manor v. Bellamy, 02-CVG-03259 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, Sept. 11, 2003) (left job to care for disabled child, Oct. rent unpaid due to utility payment, park operator refused Oct. 12 tender of full rent (voucher), widow’s benefits began, rent deposited with court); Westgate Vill. Mobile Home Park v. Fetro , No. CVG-950312, 1995 WL 907587 (Ohio Mun., Fostoria, Sept. 27, 1995) (park resident for 11 years, history of timely rent payments, one month rent unpaid, court allows tenant to pay arrearage and late fee to stay); Zane Vill. Mobile Home Park v. Hedrick , No. 95 -CVG-536 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Sept. 21, 1995) (unpaid May rent, park operator refused May 15 tender of full rent, SSI and disability income, domestic violence, protective order, psychiatric care).
CC. Real Party in Interest When the plaintiff in an eviction action is not the ow ner of the premises, the plaintiff may fail to meet the applicable real party in interest test.

Generally, the appellate courts have determined that, for eviction actions, the real party in interest test is the R.C. 1923.01(C)(2) definition of landlord.Hardin v. Hardin,
No. E -18-001, 2019 W L 259092 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 18, 2019); Adlaka v.

Quaranta, No. 09-MA-134, 2010 WL 5550656 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Dec. 16, 2010);

187 Alex-Bell Oxford Ltd. P’ship v. Woods, No. 16038, 1998 WL 289028 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 5, 1998); KDI Mgmt. Servs., Inc. v. Enerchem, Inc. , No. C-960587, 1997 WL 119898 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar. 19, 1997); Stout v. Duvall, No. 95-CA-0077, 1996 WL 409217 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 28, 1996); Shurtz Mgmt., Inc. v. Perry,
No. 93-CA-04, 1993 WL 290001 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 26, 1993); Oakbrook Realty Corp. v. Harris, No. 89AP-819, 1991 WL 70146 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr.

30, 1991); Prebcor, Inc. v. Fogel , No. CA86 -04-009, 1986 WL 12099 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 27, 1986); J & E Mgmt., Inc. v. Wolf , No. 35563, 1977 Ohio App.

LEXIS 7324 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 10, 1977).157 Applying this real party in interest test, “courts have held that it isnot required that the record title owner commence [the] action for forcible entry and detainer.” Tucker
v. Pfirsch, No. 2014-CA-0036, 2014 WL 3540883, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 16, 2014). “The forcible -entry-and-detainer provisions allow a landlord, not just an owner, to file an action.” Id., ¶ 25.

Four appellate court decisions expressly hold that Civil Rule 17(A) does not apply to eviction actions and that th e R.C. 1923.01(C)(2) definition of landlord applies in lieu of Civil Rule 17(A). Hardin v. Hardin, No. E-18-001, 2019 WL 259092 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 18, 20 19); Adlaka v. Quaranta , No. 09 -MA-134, 2010 WL 5550656 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Dec. 16, 2010);AlexBell Oxford Ltd. P’ship v. Woods,
No. 16038, 1998 WL 289028 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 5, 1998); Oakbrook Realty
Corp. v. Harris , No. 89AP -819, 1991 WL 70146 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 30, 1991). See also KDI Mgmt. Servs., Inc. v. Enerchem, Inc. , No. C-960587, 1997 WL 119898, at *2 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar. 19, 1997) (“applicability of Civ.R. 17 ... to actions in forcible entry and detainer is questionable, [but] we need not reach that question”). Hardin, Adlaka, Woods, and Harris hold that Civil Rule 17(A) does not apply to eviction actions solely because Civil Rule 17(A) is inconsistent with the R.C.

1923.01(C)(2) definition of landlord.

Four appellate court decisions apply the R.C. 1923.01(C)(2) definition of landlord without any discussion of Civil Rule 17(A). Stout v. Duvall, No. 95-CA-0077, 1996 WL 409217 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 28, 1996); Shurtz Mgmt., Inc. v. Perry , No.

93-CA-04, 1993 WL 290001 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 26, 1993); Prebcor, Inc. v.

157 See also Croxton v. Freisner, 62 Ohio Misc.2d 513, 603 N.E.2d 1162 (Ohio Mun., Canton, 1992); Katchmer v. Bishop , No. 90 -CVG-34263 (Ohio M un., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 3, 1991); Botnick Realty Co. v. Brown, No. 91-CVG-5445 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Aug. 13, 1991).

Fogel, No. CA86-04-009, 1986 WL 12099 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 27, 1986); J & E Mgmt., Inc. v. Wolf, No. 35563, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 7324 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Feb. 10, 1977).

One appellate district refused to apply the R.C. 1923.01(C)(2) definition of landlord where the tenant had entered into a written lease and the pla intiff (who met the definition of landlord) had not executed the lease. Ebbing v. Lawhorn , No.

CA2011-07-125, 2012 WL 2877434 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 16, 2012).

These explicit and implicit holdings that Civil Rule 17(A) does not apply to eviction actions seem to be erroneous for a two-fold reason.

First, the four court decisions that reference Civil Rule 17(A) ( Hardin, Adlaka, Woods, and Harris) use an erroneous standard for determining whether Civil Rule 17(A) applies to eviction actions. Their erro neous standard is, if any conflict exists between an R.C. Chapter 1923 provision and a Civil Rule provision, the Civil Rule provision per se does not apply. This standard is erroneous for the reasons discussed in Section I.B, above, including especially note 4, above. Under the correct standard, when such conflicts exist, the Civil Rule provision does not apply to eviction actions only if its application would frustrate the summary nature of eviction proceedings. See Section I.B, above.

Second, under the correct standard, Civil Rule 17(A) applies to eviction actions.

Civil Rule 17(A) simply defines the parties who may bring eviction actions. It does not affect in any way the time required to prosecute an eviction action and thus does not interfere with the summary nature of eviction proceedings. See generally Steiner
v. Nadal, No. 91 -CVG-8356 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Apr. 2, 1992) (applies Civil Rule 17(A) to an eviction action without any analysis as to why it applies).

If Civil Rule 17(A) applies to eviction actions, some non-owner property managers will meet its real party in interest test. A real party in interest under Civil Rule 17(A) includes “a party with whom or in whose name a contract has been made for the benefit of another.” Therefore, when a n on-owner property manager signs a lease, as managing agent for the owner, the property manager meets the Civil Rule 17(A) real party in interest test. Tishman Midwest Mgmt. Corp. of Ohio v. Hunt, No. CA-10519, 1987 WL 28181, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 10, 1987).

If the landlord is a partnership, generally the managing partner may bring an eviction action against the tenant in the managing partner’s name, provided the managing partner is acting in a representative capacity on behalf of the partnership and this representative capacity is expressly indicated on the complaint. Oda v. Davis, 81 189 Ohio App.3d 555, 611 N.E.2d 933 (2nd Dist. 1992). See also Holloway v. Wilson, No.

99-CA-76, 2000 WL 1062327 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Aug. 4, 2000); Reed v. BDS Holdings, No. 14-92-41, 1993 WL 195072 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., June 4, 1993).
DD. Fictitious Name Registration R.C. 1329.10 bars any person doing business under a fictitious (or trade) name from commencing or maintaining an action in that name, unless the fictitious name has been registered with the Secretary of State in compliance with R.C. 1329.01. Buckeye Foods v. Cuyahoga County Bd. of Revision, 78 Ohio St.3d 459, 678 N.E.2d 917 (1997).

Therefore, if a landlord is doing business under a fictitious name that has not been properly registered, the landlord is barred from commencing or maintaining an eviction
action. Fisco v. Chisholm, Nos. 52111,52112, 1987 WL 11193 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 7, 1987); Oxford Square Apts. v. Nicholson, No. 07-CVG-657 (Ohio County Ct., Tuscarawas Cty., Dec. 19, 2007); Wee v. Lawrence , No. 01 -CV-15817 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Oct. 1, 2001); Alwood Enters. v. Vance, No. 87-CVG-32 (Ohio Mun., Hillsboro, Oct. 21, 1987). Cf. Ebner v. Caudill, 93 Ohio App.3d 785, 639 N.E.2d 1231 (10th Dist. 1994) (action for collection of rent).

When a landlord owns and operates a rental property under a properly registered fictitious name, the landlord may bring , in either the landlord’s name or the fictitious name, an eviction action (and monetary claims) against the tenant. Woods v. Marcano, 122 N.E.3d 633, 2018-Ohio-4324 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2018).

Similarly, R.C. 1777.04 bars any partnership transacting business under a fictitious name from commencing or maintaining an action in the partnership name, unless the name has been registered with the county recorder in compliance with R.C. 1777.02.

Therefore, if a landlord is a partnership transacting business under a fictitious name that has not been properly registered, the landlord is barred from commenci ng or maintaining an eviction action. 158 See generally Dayton Sec. Assocs. v. Avuta , 105 Ohio App.3d 559, 664 N.E.2d 954 (2nd Dist. 1995).

An issue under this defense is whether the R.C. 1329.10(B) bar against commencement of an action is violated where the fictitious-name plaintiff registers with the secretary of state during the pendency of the action – after the filing of the 158 When a landlord is a trust, and not a partnership, the R.C. 1777.02 registration requirement does not apply. DPS Trust v. Sherman, No. 2001-L-133, 2002 WL 1750868 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 26, 2002).

case but before the date of final judgment. The case law is split on this issue. For the reasons discussed below, the better reasoned view is that the plaintiff cannot cure the violation during the pendency of the action.

R.C. 1329.10(B) “provides that an action may not be commenced or maintained until the name is registered.” Frate v. Al -Sol, Inc., 131 Ohio App.3d 283, 287, 722 N.E.2d 185 (8th Dist. 1999). “R.C. 1329.10(B) ... provides expressly that such action may not be commenced ... until first complying with the licensing [registration] requirements.” Nat’l Crime Reporting, Inc. v. McCord & Akamine, L.L.P., 177 Ohio App.3d 551, 555-56, 895 N.E.2d 255 (10th Dist. 2008).

A failure to register a fictitious name in compliance with R.C. 1329.10 (or R.C.

1777.02) “denies the user of [the] fictitious name the capacity to sue.”159 Frate, 131 Ohio App.3d at 287. Accord Nat’l Crime Reporting, Inc., 177 Ohio App.3d at 555-56.

Since R.C. 1329.10(B) denies the user of a fictitious name the capacity to sue, the controlling facts are those at the time of the commencement of the action. “Capacity to sue ‘refers to the eligibility of a person to commence an action.” Wanamaker v.

Davis, No. 2005 -CA-151, 2007 WL 2405708, ¶ 43 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Aug. 24, 2007). See, e.g. , Nat’l Crime Reporting, Inc. , 177 Ohio App.3d at 554 (“the trial court’s rationale for granting the dismissal was that at the time of the filing of the complaint, the plaintiff lacked the capacity to maintain an action in Ohio due to its failure to obtain a license pursuant to R.C. 1703.29”).

Therefore, any registration with the secretary of state after the commenc ement of the action is immaterial to the plaintiff’s capacity to sue.

However, in conflict with the foregoing analysis, the Eighth Appellate District has held that registration up to the date of final judgment cures the noncompliance that existed at the commencement of the action. Dodd v. Keybank, No. 85949, 2006 WL 62432 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 12, 2006); MBA Realty v. Little G, Inc. , 116 Ohio App.3d 334, 688 N.E.2d 39 (8th Dist. 1996). See also LAMMB Mgmt. v. Perkins, No.

159 Lack of capacity to sue is an affirmative defense. “Civ.R. 9(A) governs capacity [to sue]....Thus, Civ.R. 9(A) places the pleading burden upon a defendant to deny, by specific negative averment or with particularity, a plaintiff’s capacity to sue.”Wanamaker v. Davis, No.

2005-CA-151, 2007 WL 2405708, ¶ 43 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Aug. 24, 2007).

“Failure to raise the plaintiff’s lack of capacity to sue in the answer waives the issue.” Frate v.

Al-Sol, Inc., 131 Ohio App.3d 283, 287, 722 N.E.2d 185 (8th Dist. 1999). “The defense of lack of capacity to sue is typically waived when an answer only contains a general denial and when the defense is not raised by specific averment.” Wanamaker, 2007 WL 2405708, ¶ 43.

191 2006-CVG-27727 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 13, 2006) (follows Dodd in eviction action).

The Dodd and MBA Realty interpretation of R.C. 1329.10(B) violates a fundamental rule of statutory construction, namely: “The entire statute is intended to be effective.” R.C. 1.47(B). Accord D.A.B.E., Inc. v. Toledo -Lucas County Bd. of Health, 96 Ohio St.3d 250, 256, 773 N.E.2d 536 (2002). By effectively limiting the scope of the prohibition to maintenance of an action, it renders inoperative the statute’s prohibition against commencement of an action.

The Dodd and MBA Realty decisions misconstrue the R.C. 1329.10(B) phrase “but upon compliance, such an action may be commenced or maintained on any contracts or transactions entered into prior to compliance.” This phrase simply means that, when an action is dismissed under R.C. 1329.10(B), it shall be dismissed without prejudice.

Nat’l Crime Reporting, Inc. v. McCord & Akamine, L.L.P., 177 Ohio App.3d 551, 555- 56, 895 N.E.2d 255 (10th Dist. 2008);New Method Textiles, Inc. v. TGI Friday’s, Inc.,
No. 93-APG10-1360, 1994 WL 312915, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 28, 1994).

Thereafter, “upon compliance with R.C. 1329.10(B), like R.C. 1703, such an action may be commenced.” Nat’l Crime Reporting, Inc., 177 Ohio App.3d at 556. Accord New Method Textiles, Inc., 1994 WL 312915, at *3.
EE. Unauthorized Practice of Law

#### 1. Corporation Without Counsel

A corporation may not maintain litigation or appear in court except by and through an attorney. Union Sav. Ass’n v. Home Owners Aid, Inc., 23 OhioSt.2d 60, 262 N.E.2d 558 (1970). See also Bd. of Educ. of Worthington City Sch. Dist. v. Bd. of Revision of Franklin County, 85 Ohio St.3d 156, 707 N.E.2d 499 (1999).

Therefore, a corporation may not file or maintain an eviction action without an
attorney. Sheridan Mobile Vill., Inc. v. Larsen, 78 Ohio App.3d 203, 604 N.E.2d 217 (4th Dist. 1992); Fone-X, Inc. v. Strohm, No. CA-3653, 1991 WL 261824 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 12, 1991); YWCA of Canton, Inc. v. Hager , No. CA-7351, 1988 WL 29658 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Mar. 7, 1988); Mentor Green Mobile Estates v. Shopak,
No. 15-CVG-761 (Ohio Mun., Mentor, Oct. 6, 2015);Crabtree Invs., Inc. v. Clevinger,
No. 99-CVG-289 (Ohio Mun., Hocking, Aug. 26, 1999);PGS Rentals, Inc. v. Hileman,
No. CVG91-0528 (Ohio Mu n., Portsmouth, June 24, 1991); Crystal Tower Apts. v.

Adkins, No. 83-CVG-223 (Ohio Mun., East Cleveland, Feb. 25, 1983); Philnat Corp.
v. McCune, No. R79-CVG-0725 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., June 28, 1979).

If a corporation files or maintains an evictio n action without an attorney, it is grounds to dismiss the action.160 Id. See Nat’l Church Publ’ns v. Hiser, No. CA-2388, 1986 WL 6988 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 12, 1986).

When a non -attorney files an eviction complaint on behalf of a corporation, the defect is not cured by either: (1) an attorney representing the corporation at the eviction hearing, Larsen, 78 Ohio App.3d at 205; Tr. of Kokosing Valley Trust v. Myers , No.

13-CVG-00428 (Ohio Mun., Mt. Vernon, Aug. 19, 2013) (trust); or (2) an amendment of the complaint to substitute an attorney’s signature for the non-attorney’s signature, Fone-X, Inc. v. Strohm, No. CA-3653, 1991 WL 261824 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov.

12, 1991).

The case law is split on whether the defect deprives the court of subject -matter jurisdiction or is an affirmative defense that may be waived.Compare Alliance Group,
Inc. v. Rosenfield , 115 Ohio App.3d 380, 685 N.E.2d 570 (1st Dist. 1996) (subject - matter jurisdiction), and Iberia Cabinet Mfg. Co. v. Walston , 121 Ohio Misc.2d 121, 782 N.E.2d 1259 (Ohio Mun., Marion, 2002) with Rental Mgmt. v. Au , No. 92-C-66, 1993 WL 307614 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Aug. 10, 1993) (affirmative defense), and Two-32 Enters., Inc. v. Taylor , No. 91 -CA-2917, 1992 WL 274731 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Sept. 9, 1992).

If a counterclaim is served prior to the dismissal of a complaint under this defense, the counterclaim may survive the dismissal of the complaint. See Alliance Group, Inc.
v. Rosenfield, 115 Ohio App.3d 380, 685 N.E.2d 570 (1st Dist. 1996).

#### 2. Limited Liability Company Without Counsel

An R.C. Chapter 1705 limited liability company (“limited liability company”), like a corporation, may not commence or maintain a court action without an attorney. In re ICLNDS Notes Acquisition, LLC, 259 B.R. 289 (Bankr. N.D. Ohio 2001) (applying Ohio law).

Therefore, a limited liability company may not file or maintain an eviction action without an attorney. Ohio State Bar Ass’n v. Ross, 154 Ohio St.3d 328, 114 N.E.3d 179 (2018); Kasapis v. Burnett, No. 2011-CVG-330 (Ohio Mun., Stark Cty., Feb. 10, 160 Unauthorized practice of law also gives rise to a civil action for actual damages. R.C.

4705.07(C)(2). See also Greenspan v. Third Fed. Sav. & Loan Ass’n. , 122 Ohio St.3d 455, 458, 912 N.E.2d 567 (2009).

193 2011, and Feb. 16, 2011); GF Props., LLC v. Adams , No. 2005 -CVG-12616 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 18, 2005); GF Props., LLC v. Shean , No. 2004 - CVG-29619 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 30, 2004); Markin Props. v.

Gray, No. 01-CVG-04904 (Ohio Mun., Clark Cty., Mar. 7, 2002); Hoffman Arabians, LLC v. Estep , No. 01 -CVG-869 (Ohio Mun., Circleville, Nov. 9, 2001); Wee v.

Lawrence, No. 01-CV-15817 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Oct. 1, 2001); Ritter Co. v.

Clark, No. 1999-CVG-30891 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Feb. 2, 2000); DDM LLC v.

Fiorentini, No. 97-CVG-06455 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 15, 1997).

If a limited liability company files or maintains an eviction action without an attorney, it is grounds to dismiss the action. Id. And, as with a corporation, the defective filing of an eviction complaint without an attorney is not cured by an attorney representing the corporation at the eviction hearing. GF Props., LLC v. Shean , No.

2004-CVG-29619 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 30,2004) (applies Larsen, discussed in Section II.EE.1, above).

If a counterclaim is filed and served prior to the dismissal of a complaint under this defense, the counterclaim may survive the dismissal of the complaint. See Wee v.

Lawrence, No. 01-CV-15817 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Oct. 1, 2001); Ritter Co. v.

Clark, No. 1999-CVG-30891 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Feb. 2, 2000).

#### 3. Partnership Without Counsel

A partnership, like a corporation, may not commence or maintain a court action without an attorney. Rasberry v. Taylor, No. 26510, 2013 WL 2327020, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 29, 2013) (dicta).

A non-attorney limited partner of a partnership may not commence or maintain an eviction action on behalf of the partnership without an attorney. See Lakeside Ave.
Ltd. P’ship v. Cuyahoga County Bd. of Revision, 85 Ohio St.3d 125, 707 N.E.2d 472 (1999); Hicks Roofing Co. v. Shah, Shah & Patel Inv. Co., Nos. 88AP-100085,020025, 1989 WL 63323 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 16, 1989).

A non-attorney general or managing partner of a partnership may not commence or maintain an eviction action on behalf of the partnership without an attorney. See Hicks Roofing Co. v. Shah, Shal & Patel Inv. Co. , Nos. 88AP-100085,020025, 1989 WL 63323 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 16, 1989 ). See also In re ICLNDS Notes Acquisition, LLC , 259 B.R. 289 (Bankr. N.D. Ohio 2001). But see Reed v. BDS Holdings, No. 14-92-41, 1993 WL 195072 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., June 4, 1993) (held partner permitted to appear on behalf of partnership; partner appeared pro se but court did not address unauthorized practice of law issue).161 A non -attorney employee of a partnership may not commence or maintain an eviction action on behalf of the partnership without an attorney. Johnson Props. v.

Leasure, No. R96-CVG-2566 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Jan. 3, 1997); Sawburg Apts.
v. Magee, No. 87-CV-GF-707 (Ohio Mun., Alliance, June 26, 1987).

A non-attorney spouse of a partner of a partnership may not commence or maintain an eviction action on behalf of the partnership w ithout an attorney. Bishop Kandel Realty v. Decker, No. 89-CVG-1406 (Ohio Mun., Findlay, Apr. 2, 1990).

If a partnership files or maintains an eviction action without an attorney, it is grounds to dismiss the action. See Lakeside Ave. Ltd. P’ship v. Cuyahoga County Bd.

of Revision, 85 Ohio St.3d 125, 707 N.E.2d 472 (1999); Hicks Roofing Co. v. Shah, Shah & Patel Inv. Co., Nos. 88AP-100085,020025, 1989 WL 63323 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 16, 1989); In re ICLNDS Notes Acquisition, LLC , 259 B.R. 289 (Bankr.

N.D. Ohio 2001).

#### 4. Non-Attorney Practicing Law, R.C. 4705.01


##### a. General Rule

R.C. 4705.01 prohibits any person who is not an attorney from commencing or maintaining a court action on behalf of another. See Otto v. Patterson , 173 Ohio St.

174, 180 N.E.2d 575 (1962).

A non-attorney agent of a landlord may not sign an eviction complaint on behalf of the landlord. Furr v. Patterson , No. 2021 -CVG-004661 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., June 22, 2021); Wheeler v. Write , No. 2020 -CVG-082W (Ohio County Ct., Ashtabula, W. Div., Apr. 28, 2020); Croxton v. Freisner, 62 Ohio Misc.2d 513, 603 N.E.2d 1162 (Ohio Mun., Canton, 1992); Steiner v. Nadal , No. 91 -CVG-8356 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Apr. 2, 1992); Ogden v. Avery , No. 91 -CVG-18 (Ohio Mun., Mansfield, May 16, 1991); Wright v. Phelps, No. 89-CVGT-1086 (Ohio Mun., Miami Cty., Sept. 18, 1989); Porter v. Gibbs , No. 88 -CVG-205 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., May 31, 1988).

Likewise, if the plaintiffs are two individuals who own the property, one of the owners may not sign the complaint for the other owner, unless the signing party is an 161 Whether a partner may bring an action on behalf of the partnership is a real party in interest
issue. Whether a partner may bring such action without an attorney is an unauthorized practice of law issue. For a discussion on the real party in interest issue, see Section II .CC, above.

195
attorney. Rasberry v. Taylor , No. 26510, 2013 WL 2327020 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 29, 2013).

A non-attorney trustee of a trust may not sign an eviction complaint on behalf of the trust. Ohio State Bar Ass’n v. Ross, 154 Ohio St.3d 328, 114 N.E.3d 179 (2018);
Tr. of Kokosing Valley Trust v. Myers , No. 13-CVG-00428 (Ohio Mun., Mt. Vernon,
Aug. 19, 2013); Porter v. Mosher , No. 01 -CVG-3616 (Ohio Mun., Akron, June 13, 2001); Porter v. Myles , No. 01-CVG-3344 (Ohio Mun., Akron, June 7, 2001). See generally Williams v. Global Constr. Co., 26 Ohio App.3d 119, 498 N.E.2d 500 (10th
Dist. 1985).

If a non-attorney commences an eviction action in violation of R.C. 4705.01, it is grounds to dismiss the ac tion. See Washington County Dep’t of Human Servs. v.

Rutter, 100 Ohio App.3d 32, 651 N.E.2d 1360 (4th Dist. 1995); Williams v. Global
Constr. Co., 26 Ohio App.3d 119, 498 N.E.2d 500 (10th Dist. 1985).

##### b. Picklo Rule

The Ohio Supreme Court has determined thatR.C. 4705.01 applies to persons who meet the R.C. 1923.01(C)(2) definition of landlord bu t are not the owner of the
premises. Cleveland Bar Ass’n v. Picklo, 96 Ohio St.3d 195, 772 N.E.2d 1187 (2002).

It held that, when such persons– landlords who are agents of the owner – are plaintiffs in an eviction complaint, if they proceed pro se, they are engaging in the unauthorized practice of law. Id.

The First Appellate District has narrowed (or more clearly defined) the scope of the Picklo rule. It held tha t the Picklo prohibition does not apply to non -owner plaintiffs who are a party to the lease as the landlord. Ebbing v. Bobbitt, No. CA2012- 03-061, 2012 WL 4862548 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 15, 2012).

A violation of the Picklo rule is a defense to the eviction action. Ebbing v.

Lawhorn, No. CA2011 -07-125, 2012 WL 2877434 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 16, 2012); Batt v. Nairebout, No. L-03-1001, 2003 WL 21489414 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 2003); Begue v. Calloway, No. 03-CVG-00286 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Feb. 4, 2003).

A violation of the Picklo rule is also grounds to vacate an FED judgment and to dismiss the case under the court’s inherent authority to vacate void judgments.

Patterson v. Tegge, No. 2019-CVG-025075 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Mar. 3, 2020).

In such cases, the complaint is null and void ab initio. Id.

##### c. Power of Attorney Rule

“When a person not admitted to the bar attempts to represent another in court on the basis of a power of attorney assigningpro se rights, he is in violation of thisstatute [R.C. 4705.01].” Office of Disciplinary Counsel v. Coleman, 88 Ohio St.3d 155, 158, 724 N.E.2d 402 (2000). The representations that this rule prohibits include commencing, maintaining, or defending any action or proceeding. Id. at 157-58.

This prohibition applies to eviction actions. Dabydeen v. Ortiz, No. 2013-CVG- 011770 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 29, 2013). In Ortiz, only the person with the power of attorney appeared on behalf of the landlord at the eviction hearing.

The court thereupon dismissed the eviction action for want of prosecution.
FF. Co-Tenant Versus Co-Tenant “Under Ohio law a co -tenant may not terminate the lease rights of another co - tenant.” Burpo v. Burpo , No. 2009 -CVG-7976 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 2, 2009). Cf. Collins v. Jackson, 34 Ohio App.3d 101, 517 N.E.2d 269 (8th Dist.
1986) (co-tenant owners of real property). Therefore, if one co -tenant brings an eviction action against the other co-tenant, a defense is the plaintiff co-tenant’s lack of authority to terminate the defendant co-tenant’s right of possession.

In Burpo, a mother and her adult daughter were co-tenants of a dwelling unit. The mother was the designated head of household, both the mother and the daughter had signed the lease, and the lease did not provide the mother with any occupancy right superior to the daughter. Therefore, on the mother’s eviction claim against the daughter, the court entered a judgment for the daughter.
GG. Statute of Limitations, R.C. 1923.01(B) R.C. 1923.01(B) establishes a two-year statute of limitations for eviction actions.

This two -year st atute of limitations begins to run when the right to commence the eviction action (i.e., the right to serve the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate) comes into
existence. Reese v. Webb, No. L-04-1257, 2005 WL 1009889 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Apr. 29, 2005); Palmer v. O’Leary, No. 7745, 1975 Ohio App. LEXIS 7945 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 3, 1975).

For example, in an eviction action for nonpayment of rent, the two-year statute of limitations begins to run on the date that the tenant becomes delinquent in the tenant’s rental obligation, not on the date that the landlord serves the tenant with an R.C.

197 1923.04 notice to vacate. Id.

However, each month that a month-to-month tenant fails to pay rent, a new cause of action accrues. Causey v. Causey, No. 599, 1990 WL138564 (Ohio App., 4th Dist.,
Sept. 25, 1990). Therefore, if a month-to-month tenant fails to pay rent for a period of more than two years, the two-year statute of limitation is not a bar to an eviction action for nonpayment of rent that became due during the two -year period immediately preceding the filing of the action. Id.

In an eviction action for the failure of an assisted housing tenant to report an increase in income (see Section III.G.4, below), the two -year statute of limitation begins to run on the date that the landlord first became aware of this alleged fraud, not the date of any criminal conviction for it. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Sanders , No.

02-CVG-2692 (Ohio Mun., Akron, July 12, 2002),appeal dismissed, No. 21259 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 10, 2003).

If a tenant does not plead this statute of limitation defense or assert it at trial, the tenant waives the limitation. Haas v. Gerski, 175 Ohio St. 327, 331, 194 N.E.2d 765 (1963). A tenant may not assert this defense for the first time in a motion for a new
trial. Id.
HH. Defense for Drug-Related R.C.

1923.02(A)(6) Eviction Under R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a), a landlord may evict a tenant for certain drug-related activity at the premises, if the landlord has “actual knowledge or reasonable c ause to believe” that the tenant or the tenant’s guest has engaged in such activity.

A landlord has this “actual knowledge or reasonable cause to believe” only if all of the elements of the R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a)(i) definition of that term are met. The definition is set forth in Section I.C.5, above.

A potential defense in these eviction actions is that one (or more) of the elements of the R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a)(i) definition of “actual knowledge or reasonable cause to believe” has not been met. The case law provides four examples of this defense.

First, if a search warrant has not been issued and executed for the drug -related activity, the landlord may not evict the tenant under R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a).162 Ezzard Charles Apts. v. Washington, No. 98-CV-28786 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., June 22, 1999). Cf. Liggins v. Westminster Arms, Inc., Nos. 98AP-377,378, 1998 WL 894613 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 22, 1998) (in granting landlord’s application for release of rent deposit, court held that landlord did not violate R.C. 5321.04(A)(9) because no search warrant had been executed at the neighbor’s unit).

Second, if a search warrant is executed for and a controlled substance is found at another rental unit in the building, but not the tenant’s rental unit, the landlord may not evict the tenant under R.C. 1923.02(A)(6)(a). Gorsuch Mgmt. v. Cole, No. 94-CVG- 511 (Ohio Mun., Lancaster, Sept. 22, 1994).

Third, if the execution of the search warrant produces no evidence of a controlled substance at the premises, the landl ord may not evict the tenant under R.C.

1923.02(A)(6)(a). Maliga Props. v. Williams , No. 92 -CVG-2998 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 3, 1992).

Fourth, if the landlord fails to introduce into evidence the affidavit for the search warrant and the se arch warrant, the landlord may not evict the tenant under R.C.

1923.02(A)(6)(a). Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Wells, No. L-00-1224, 2001 WL 279773 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 23, 2001). The requirement to introduce the affidavit and the warrant exists “[e]ven if the executing officer testified that the warrant complied with Crim.R. 41.” Id., 2001 WL 279773, at *4. Absent the introduction of the affidavit and the warrant, the court cannot determine whether the warrant complies with Criminal Rule 41, “given the fact that a warrant can be found to be so defective on its face that it is rendered invalid and therefore not in compliance with Crim.R. 41.” Id.

162 However, in these circumstances, R.C. 1923.02(A)(6) does not preclude a landlord from relying on R.C. 1923.02(A)(9) to evict a tenant for breach of a lease provision that prohibits drug-related criminal activity on the premises. See Section I.C.5, above.

199

## III. Eviction Action, Additional

Defenses, Assisted Housing

### A. Introduction, Federal Notice of

Termination and Good Cause To terminate the tenancy of a tenant who resides in Public Housing or subsidized housing (collectively referred to as assisted housing), 163 the landlord must serve the tenant with a notice of termination in accordance with federal requirements. Th e federal notice of termination is a notice that proposes to terminate the tenancy and informs the tenant of the reason for the proposed termination of tenancy.164 If the tenant resides in project -based assisted housing,165 the notice also informs the tenant of the tenant’s right to attend an administrative hearing or meeting before an eviction action is commenced.

A landlord’s termination of an assisted housing tenancy is also subject to a good cause requirement. A project -based assisted housing landlord may not terminate a lease during its term or refuse to renew a lease at the end of its term, except for good
cause. A Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”) landlord may not terminate a lease during its term except for good cause. But a HCVP landlord may refuse to renew 163 “Assisted housing” means rental housing that receives assistance under one or more of the HUD programs that are listed in Section XXIV.B, below. “Subsidized housing” means all assisted housing except rental housing that is assisted under the Public Housi ng program. The term “assisted housing” does not include rental housing that is assisted under the Low Income Housing Tax Credit (“LIHTC”) program. The LIHTC program is rental housing that receives assistance for its below -market rents, but HUD does not provide that assistance. The LIHTC program is discussed separately in Section III.L, below.

164 This is a different type of notice than the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate, which is a prerequisite to the filing of an eviction action. For a discussion on the time for serving an R.C.

1923.04 notice to vacate, when a tenancy has been terminated by service of a federal notice of termination, see Section I.C.3.e, above.

165 “Project-based assisted housing” means all assisted housing except rental housing assisted under the Housing Choice Voucher (aka section 8 voucher) program or the Shelter Plus Care (tenant-based) program. “Project-based subsidized housing” means all assisted housing except rental housing assisted under the Public Housing, Housing Choice Voucher , or Shelter Plus Care (tenant-based) program.

a lease at the end of its term without good cause. 24 C.F.R. § 982.310. See Nason v.

Mike, No. HDSP-123262, 2003 WL 21213707 (Conn. Super. May 19, 2003); Carol Rickert & Assocs. v. Law, 54 P.3d 91, 95 (N.M. App. 2002). The decision on whether good cause exists is generally made in a state court eviction action.

The notice and good cause requirem ents for assisted housing were originally established by due process litigation. 166 E.g., Jeffries v. Ga. Res. Fin. Auth. , 503

### F. Supp. 610 (N.D. Ga. 1980), aff’d, 678 F.2d 919 (11th Cir. 1982) (Section 8

Certificate); Joy v. Daniels, 479 F.2d 1236 (4th Cir. 1973) (Section 221(d)(3) and Rent Supplement); Escalera v. New York City Hous. Auth. , 425 F.2d 853 (2d Cir. 1970) (Public Housing).167 They are now established by federal regulations. 168 The regulations state the required content, method of service, and time of service of the notice of termination.

The regulations define good cause to be any material noncompliance with the rental agreement,169 material failure to carry out obligations un der any state landlord and tenant act,170 certain criminal activity, certain alcohol abuse, or other good cause.

The regulations do not include an exclusive definition of other good cause. Some of the program regulations include examples of other good caus e. E.g., 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.310(d). Some of the program regulations require the landlord to provide the tenant with advance notice of any conduct that the landlord deems to be other good
cause. E.g., 24 C.F.R. § 247.3(b); HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV -1,171 ¶ 8-16. And 166 For a discussion of this due process issue, see National Housing Law Project, HUD Housing Programs: Tenants’ Rights¶¶ 11.3.2.2 and 11.3.3.1 (5th ed. 2018).

167 Ohio state court decisions recognizing a project-based assisted housing tenant’s due process property interest in continued occupancy include : Heritage Court, L.L.C. v. Merritt , 187 Ohio App.3d 117, 931 N.E.2d 194 (3rd Dist. 2010);Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Hazelbaker, No. CA2005- 11-031, 2006 WL 1976760 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 17, 2006); Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v.

Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992).

168 The assisted housing program regulations on termination of tenancy and eviction are l isted in Section XXIV.B.6, below.

169 The Public Housing, Section 8 Moderate Rehabilitation, and HCVP regulations use the phrase serious or repeated violation of the terms of the lease, instead of material noncompliance with the rental agreement.

170 The Section 8 Moderate Rehabilitation and HCVP regulations refer to violations of federal and local laws, as well as state laws, concerning the tenant’s use of the premises.

171 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

201 HUD has clarified that “other good cause” is subject to (and does not preempt) any state or local laws that restrict or prohibit a termination of the tenancy. HUD Notice PIH 2009-18 (HA) (issued June 22, 2009, expired June 30, 2010).

Ohio state co urt decisions on the obligation of assisted housing landlords to comply with these federal regulations are discussed in Section III.B, below. Ohio state court decisions on the content requirements for the federal notice of termination are discussed in Sec tion III.C, below. Ohio state court decisions on the service requirements for the federal notice of termination are discussed in Section III.D, below.

Ohio state court decisions on a tenant’s opportunity to cure a breach during the period of the federal notice of termination are discussed in Section III.E, below. Ohio state court decisions on the administrative hearing or meeting requirement are discussed in Section III.F, below. Ohio state court decisions on the good cause requirement are discussed in Section III.G, below.172 A landlord’s failure to comply with the applicable federal notice of termination requirements is a complete defense to the eviction action. See Sections III.B-E, below.

Two courts have held that a failure to comply with these requ irements does not constitute a jurisdictional defect. Geauga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Biggs, No. 98-G-2207, 2000 WL 665567 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., May 19, 2000); Fairborn Apts. v. Herman,
No. 90-CA-28, 1991 WL 10962 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 31, 1991). However, they did not address whether such noncompliance caused the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate to be served before the termination of the tenancy and thus prematurely (see Section I.C.3.e, above). Id.

### B. Strict Compliance with Federal Notice

Rules, Ohio Case Law When an assisted housing landlord seeks to evict a tenant, the landlord must comply with the notice of termination and good cause requirements of the applicable federal regulations. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Younger , 93 Ohio App.3d 819, 639 N.E.2d 1253 (8th Dist. 1994) (Public Housing); Swartz v. Schutte , No. 14717, 1991 WL 2022 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 9, 1991) (Section 8 Moderate 172 For other court decisions on the federal notice of termination and good cause requirements, see National Housing Law Project, HUD Housing Programs, Tenants’ Rights ch. 11 (5th ed.

2018).

Rehabilitation); Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell , 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985) (Section 8 New Construction); Park Lane Apts. v. Rogers, No. C- 830006, 1983 WL 5322 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Nov. 23, 1983) (project -based subsidized housing); Sandefur Co. v. Jones, 9 Ohio App.3d 85, 458 N.E.2d 390 (10th
Dist. 1982) (Section 8 Substantial Rehab ilitation); Zanesville Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Callipare, No. CA-81-13, 1981 WL 6582 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 10, 1981) (Public Housing); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Brown, No. 43566, 1981 WL 4963 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 21, 1981) (Public Housing); Ivywood Apts. v. Bennett , 51 Ohio App.2d 209, 367 N.E.2d 1205 (10th Dist. 1976) (project -based subsidized housing);

Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. McCollum, 45 Ohio App.2d 197, 341 N.E.2d 857 (1st
Dist. 1975) (Public Housing).

These landlords also must c omply with the notice of termination and good cause requirements of the applicable HUD handbooks. Hidden Meadows Townhomes v.

Ross, No. C-120045, 2012 WL 6674412 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 21, 2012) (project- based subsidized housing). “As the operator of federally subsidized housing, Hidden Meadows had to comply with federal regulations governing the program, HUD’s multi-family occupancy handbook, and HUD’s model lease.”Id., ¶ 37.

In addition, when an assisted housing lease includes (explicitly or by r eference) the notice requirements of an inapplicable federal regulation, the landlord must comply with the notice requirements of the applicable federal regulations, as well as the inapplicable regulations that the lease incorporated. Sandefur Co. v. Jone s, 9 Ohio App.3d 85, 87, 458 N.E.2d 390 (10th Dist. 1982).

The landlord must comply strictly, not merely substantially, with the federal notice of termination requirements. Park Lane Apts. v. Rogers , No. C -830006, 1983 WL 5322 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Nov. 23, 1983) (project -based subsidized housing);

Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Lynch, No. CVG-13-00642 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe,
Sept. 4, 2013) (Public Housing); Detroit Terrace Ltd. v. Bender, No. 11-CVG-19350 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 5, 2011) (project-based subsidized housing);

Forest City Mgmt., Inc. v. McDonald, No. 01-CVG-5026 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Sept. 5,
2001) (project-based subsidized housing); Alpha Phi Alpha Homes, Inc. v. Smith, No.

2001-CVG-2773 (Ohio Mun., Akron, June 12, 2001) (pr oject-based subsidized housing); Winchester Gardens v. Swickheimer, No. M9410-CVG-031135 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 14, 1994) (project-based subsidized housing); Wiseman v. Turner,
No. M9112 -CVG-042342 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 3, 1992) (Section 8 Certificate); Canal Park Tower v. Brown, No. 91-CVG-3327 (Ohio Mun., Akron, May 203 31, 1991) (Section 8 Substantial Rehabilitation); Plains Plaza, Ltd. v. McKinley , No.

CIG-90-5-34 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., Feb. 8, 1991) (Section 8 New Construction);

Countrybrook Apts. v. Forsythe , No. M86 -CVG-20896 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty.,
Aug. 28, 1986) (Section 236); Green v. Brown , No. 86 -CV-00466 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Mar. 25, 1986) (Section 8 Property Disposition); Wildwood Vill. Apts.
v. Eckard, No. M84-CVG-28561 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 13, 1984) (project- based subsidized housing); Sec’y of HUD v. Winchester , No. 82 -CV-10191 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., June 3, 1982) (HUD-owned). See also Hedco, Ltd. v. Blanchette, 763 A.2d 639, 643 (R.I. 2000). Cf. Holmes v. Convenient Food Mart, Inc., No. 98-L- 272, 2000 WL 263736, at *2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 3, 2000) (“In Ohio, the law abhors a forfeiture and a landlord must strictly comply with the terms of a lease before instituting forfeiture proceedings”). But see Real Props. Servs. Mgmt. v. Harigle, No.

3-96-21, 1997 WL 430773 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., July 30, 1997) (2-1 decision).

### C. Federal Notice of Termination (Content),

Ohio Case Law

#### 1. Omission or Misstatement of a Required Element

If the notice of terminati on omits or misstates any of the elements that the applicable federal regulations require, the defect in the notice is a defense to the eviction action.173 The content of the notice must strictly comply with the requirements of the applicable federal regulations. See Section III.B, above.

Ohio state court decisions finding a notice invalid due to its omission or misstatement of a required element are set forth below, along with the relevant regulation (or lease or handbook provision) and the omission or misstatement.
(1) 24 C.F.R. § 247.4(a)(3) (project -based subsidized housing): the notice failed to advise the tenant that the landlord may seek to enforce the termination of tenancy only by bringing a judicial action, at which time the tenant may present a defense. Park Lane Apts. v. Rogers , No. C - 830006, 1983 WL 5322 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Nov. 23, 1983); Alpha 173 All of the required elements must be contained in the same document. Cherry Lee Apts. v.

Walcott, No. 03-CVG-00510 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Apr. 25, 2003);Uptown Vill. Apts. Ltd.
v. White, No. M9203-CVG-008281 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 22, 1992). The regulations do not permit an assisted housing landlord to serve two notices of terminati on that in combination but not individually contain all of the required elements. Id.

Phi Alpha Homes, Inc. v. Smith , No. 2001 -CVG-2773 (Ohio Mun., Akron, June 12, 2001);Countrybrook Apts. v. Forsythe, No. M86-CVG- 20896 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 28, 1986); Eatondale Apts. v.

Brown, No. 85-CV-26859 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Dec. 11, 1985);

Westover Vill. v. Dawson, No. 85-CV-8331 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty.,
Oct. 27, 1985); Wildwood Vill. Apts. v. Eckard , No. M84-CVG-28561 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 13, 1984);Neighborhood Dev. Corp. v.

Cook, No. M79-CVG-30481 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 2, 1980).
(2) 24 C.F.R. § 247.4(e) (project -based subsidized housing) : the notice failed to state the amount of the alleged past due rent or the effective date of the computation. Retiree Hous. Mgmt., Inc. v. Myers , No. 09- CVG-00091 (Ohio Mun., Miamisburg, Feb. 17, 2009);Kimberly W. Co.
v. Smith, No. M8710 -CVG-33507 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 1, 1987); Inner City Hous. v. Sebastian , No. 81 -CV-15029 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., June 29, 1981); Neighborhood Dev. Corp. v. Cook, No.

M79-CVG-30481 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 2, 1980); Fed. Prop.
Mgmt. Corp. v. Jackson, No. 79-CVG-8820 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Nov.

1, 1979).
(3) 24 C.F.R. § 247.4(e) (project -based subsidized housing) : the notice misstated the amount of the unpaid rent. Rockefeller Park Towers, Ltd.
v. White, No. 05-CVG-24239 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan.

6, 2006).
(4) 24 C.F.R. § 247.4(a)(1) (project -based subsidized housing): the notice failed to state the date on which the lease will be terminated. Millennia
Hous. Mgmt. Ltd. v. Williams , No. 2018 -CVG-015007 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 6, 2019); Independent Mgmt. Servs. of Ohio, Inc. v. Davis, No. 2014-CVG-01244 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 18, 2014); ABC Mgmt. Co. v. Vinson , No. M8901 -CVG-000935 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Mar. 8, 1989).
(5) Subsidized housing lease, ¶ 23(e);174 HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1,175 ¶ 8-13(B) (project-based subsidized housing): the notice failed to advise the tenant that the tenant has ten days within which to discuss the proposed termination of tenancy with the landlord. It is set forth in HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1, App. 4-A (see note 143, above).

Apts. v. White , No. 2015 -CVG-5930 (Ohio Mun., Stark Cty., Jan. 13, 2016); Cherry Lee Apts. v. Walcott, No. 03 -CVG-00510 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Apr. 25, 2003); Botnick Realty Co. v. Brown , No. 91 - CVG-5445 (Ohio Mun., Akron, July 10, 1991); Canal Park Tower v.

Auth. v. Hammonds , No. 92 -CVG-803 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Oct. 20, 1992); Lakeview T errace v. Smith , No. 92 -CVG-8034 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 21, 1992); Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Ellis, No. 92-CVG-1392 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Mar. 3, 1992).
(9) 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(3)(ii) (Public Housing): the notice failed to inform the tenant of the tenant’s right to reply to the notice of termination or the tenant’s right (if any) to request a grievance procedure hearing. 176 176 Under 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(3)(ii ), the notice of termination must inform the tenant of the tenant’s right to reply even if the tenant is not entitled to use the grievance procedure.Columbus
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Horsley , No. M9510 -CVG-028670 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Feb. 2, 1996).

Columbus Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Horsley , No. M9510 -CVG-028670 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Feb. 2, 1996); Lakeview Terrace v. Smith,
No. 92-CVG-8034 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 21, 1992).
(10) 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(3)(ii) (Public Housing) : the notice incorrectly stated the time period for requesting a hearing in accordance with the housing authority’s grievance procedure.Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth.
v. Mallet, No. 93-CVG-09659 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan.

7, 1993).
(11) 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(e)(i) (HCVP): the notice failed to state the reason for the termination of tenancy. Mosley & Mosley Props. Ltd. v. Graham,
No. 99-G-3616 (Ohio Mun., Akron, May 24, 1999).

#### 2. Statement of Reason with Specificity

The regulations for the assisted housing programs require that the notice of termination state, with specificity, the reason for the proposed termination of tenancy.177 The reason for this specificity requirement is “‘to insure that the tenant is adequately informed of the nature of the evidence against him so that he can effectively rebut that evidence.’” Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 10, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985) (quotingEscalera v. New York City Hous. Auth., 425 F.2d 853, 862 (2d Cir. 1970)). Accord Lucas Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Kincade, No. L-94-211, 1995 WL 112963, at *5 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 17, 1995);Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Younge r, 93 Ohio App.3d 819, 825, 639 N.E.2d 1253 (8th Dist. 1994). Cf.

Billington v. Underwood, 613 F.2d 91, 94 (5th Cir. 1980) (denial of Public Housing application); Edgecomb v. Hous. Auth. of Town of Vernon, 824 F. Supp. 312, 314 (D.
Conn. 1993) (termination of participation in Section 8 Certificate program).

To meet this specificity requirement, the notice of termination, in its statement of the reason for the proposed termination, must (1) “refer to specific instances of conduct,” Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 10, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985), and (2) specify the dates on which the alleged incidents occurred and the names of the individuals involved in the alleged incidents, Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v.

Stern, No. 67445, 1995 WL 23152, a t *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 19, 1995);

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Younger, 93 Ohio App.3d 819, 826, 639 N.E.2d 1253 177 The assisted housing regulations on termination of tenancy and eviction, which include this notice requirement, are listed in Section XXIV.B.6, below.

207 (8th Dist. 1994). But see Lable and Co. v. Francis, No. 89-J-39, 1991 WL 24982 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 22, 1991) (reference to lease provision met specificity requirement).

In short, “the notice should describe the specific instances of tenant misconduct on which the landlord is relying, and should, generally speaking, answer the questions, who, what, where, when, and how as to each of the specific instances.” United Prop.
Mgmt. Co. v. Mundell , No. R86 -CVG-0850, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty.,
Oct. 22, 1986). Accord Emerald Dev. & Economic Network, Inc. v. Foster, No. 2014- CVG-003506 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 30, 2014); Chillicothe Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Lynch, No. CVG-13-00642 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Sept. 4, 2013).See also Rainbow Group Apts. v. Eatmon, No. 2006-CVG-19273 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Nov. 7, 2006); Parma Pub. Hous. Agency v. Smiley , No. 05-CVG-00759 (Ohio Mun., Parma, May 5, 2005); Mt. Olive-Metro Hous., Inc. v. Craft , No. 93 -G- 2858 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Feb. 28, 1994).

A notice of termination fails to state the reason for the proposed termination of tenancy with the requisite specificity if “the notice is blanketed in broad language and does not refer to specific instances of conduct” or is “written in ‘vague and conclusory’ language, and fail[s] to set forth a factual statement of the reason for termination.” Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d at 10. Accord Kincade, 1995 WL 112963, at *5;Younger, 93 Ohio App.3d at 825.

A notice of termination also fails to state the reason for the proposed termination of tenancy with the requisite specificity if it fails to notify the tenant of the dates of the alleged incidents or the individuals involved in the alleged incidents. Owner’s Mgmt.
Co. v. Stern, No. 67445, 1995 WL 23152, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 19, 1995);

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Younger, 93 Ohio App.3d 819, 826, 639 N.E.2d 1253 (8th Dist. 1994). Cf. Edgecomb v. Hous. Auth. of Town of Vernon, 824 F. Supp. 312, 315 (D. Conn. 1993).

In nonpayment of rent cases, a notice of termination fails to state the reason for the proposed termination of tenancy with the requisite specificity if it fails to specify “the amount due as of a certain date, and the months for which the rent was not paid.” Mt.

Olive-Metro Hous., Inc. v. Craft, No. 93-G-2858, slip op. at 8 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Feb. 28, 1994). Accord Kimberly W. Co. v. Smith, No. M8710 -CVG-33507 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 1, 1987); Inner City Hous. v. Sebastian, No. 81-CV- 15029 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., June 29, 1981).

In nonpayment of market rent cases against project -based subsidized housing tenants, “the two-step procedure of raising a tenant’s rent to market rent, then evicting for nonpayment of that rent, must allow a tenant to challenge in an eviction proceeding the basis for the landlord’s decision to raise the rent to market rent. It follows that the notice of termination for nonpayment of that rent must specifically state the reason the landlord raised the tenant’s rent to market rent. Otherwise, a tenant can never be prepared to defend against the landlord’s evidence of good cause for its decision to raise the rent.” Boardwalk Glenville Apts. v. Cage, No. 2007-CVG-3065, slip op. at 8 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 13, 2007). Accord Independent Mgmt. Servs.

of Ohio, Inc. v. Davis, No. 2014-CVG-01244, slip op. at 3 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July18,
2014) (“The ten-day notice here did not specify why Defendant’s rent increased [to market rent] and, therefore, this case should be dismissed”).

Even if the tenant is aware of the reason for the proposed termination of tenancy, the written notice of te rmination must state the reason with the requisite specificity.

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Younger, 93 Ohio App.3d 819, 826, 639 N.E.2d 1253 (8th Dist. 1994) (“CMHA’s response that ... Younger knew full well what activities caused it to issue the notice of termination and, therefore, Younger was not precluded from adequately responding to the termination process does little to persuade this court that it complied with the federal regulations”);Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Lynch,
No. CVG-13-00642, slip op. at 3 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Sept. 4, 2013) (“Even if the tenant is aware of the reason for the proposed termination, the written notice of termination must state the reason with the requisite specificity. The question is whether or not the Public Housing Authority complied with the federal regulations, not whether the tenant was aware of the specific violations”);178 Boardwalk Glenville Apts. v. Cage,
No. 2007-CVG-3065, slip op. at 8 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 13, 2007) (“A landlord may not cure the lack of specifics in a notice by explaining the specific reasons to the tenant in a later meeting”);Parma Pub. Hous. Agency v. Smiley, No. 05- CVG-00759, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun., Parma, May 5, 2005) (“Plaintiff’s contention that the Defe ndant ‘lived under these conditions and was fully aware of them’ is
irrelevent. As Judge Harper noted in Younger, supra, the issue revolves around whether or not the public housing authority complied with federal regulations, not whether the tenant was aware of the specific violations”);Mt. Olive-Metro Hous., Inc.
v. Craft, No. 93 -G-2858, slip op. at 8 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Feb. 28, 1994) 178 In Lynch, the court cites to this book’s fourth edition, page 223.

209 (“The notice, standing alone, must set forth [the requisite] factual statement;” explanations of the reasons at a meeting “is ineffective to correct the deficiency of the [written] notice”). But see Forest City Mgmt., Inc. v. Tackett , 148 Ohio App.3d 667, 775 N.E.2d 555 (11th Dist. 2002) (dicta) (after finding requisite specificity in notice standing alone, finds th at tenant also “was made aware of the evidence against him” by other unspecified means); Northland Vill. Apts. v. Hamp , No. 12407, 1991 WL 108717 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 20, 1991) (holds that a court may consider relevant facts and circumstances that a re known to the family but not stated in the notice) ;

Kettering Square Apts. v. Crawford , Nos. 27504,27545,27548, 2017 WL 6398837 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 15, 2017) (relying on Hamp, holds that a court may consider relevant facts and circumstances that are known to the family but not stated in the notice).

The decision in Driver v. Hous. Auth. of Racine County , Nos. 2005-AP-410,411, 2006 WL 288152 (Wis. App. Feb. 8, 2006), summarizes well the three primary reasons that the tenant’s actual knowledge is and should be immaterial to whether a notice of termination has met the specificity requirement. First, the HUD regulations require the landlord to provide the tenant with written notice of the reasons for the proposed termination of tenancy, with the requisite specificity. Second, due process places the burden of providing adequate notice on the landlord. With an “actual knowledge” exception, this burden often would be transferred to the tenant, relative to whether or not the tenant received an alleged or al notice or otherwise already knew. Third, an “actual knowledge” exception would invite landlord noncompliance with the regulation’s written notice requirement.

Ohio state court decisions finding inadequate specificity in the notice’s statement of the reason are set forth below.
(1) Reason: “failure to comply with Item 13e of your lease by making noise and committing acts that disturb others which includes loud music, yelling, profanity, trash in hall, loud television and frightened others.” Owner’s Mgmt. Co. v. Stern, No. 67445, 1995 WL 23152, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 19, 1995) (project-based subsidized housing).179
(2) Reason: “unauthorized occupants in your unit,” “possible illegal activities in your residence,” and “disturbance of your neighbors peaceful enjoyment.” Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Younger , 93 Ohio App.3d 819, 821, 639 N.E.2d 1253, 1254 (8th Dist. 1994) (Public Housing).
(3) Reason: “serious, repeated damage to the unit” and “[r]epeated disturbance.” Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 10, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985) (Section 8 New Construction).
(4) Reason: “destruction of property.”Emerald Dev. & Economic Network,
Inc. v. Foster , No. 2014 -CVG-003506 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., June 30, 2014) (project-based subsidized housing).
(5) Reason: “Plaintiff’s Notice of Termination simply restated the lease provision allegedly violated. It contained no facts, no names, no dates.” Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Lynch , No. CVG -13-00642 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Sept. 4, 2013) (Public Housing).
(6) Reason: statement that police were called because of “an incident on August 13, 2012 taking place by the elevator,” but does not specify what occurred that constituted a material breach of the lease. Hamilton Place
Assocs. LP v. Love , No. 2012 -CVG-016117 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Feb. 22, 2013) (Section 8 New Construction).
(7) Reason: “DMHA has received several complain[t]s from other residents in the building regarding loud music and noise coming from your unit at all hours of the day and night.” Greater Dayton Premier Mgmt. v.

Phillips, No. 12-CVG-1549 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, May 16, 2012) (Public Housing).
(8) Reason: “Drug activity and excessive traffic” with two attachments – a municipal court docket stating Defendant was charged with “trafficking in drugs” and a common pleas court case summary indicating charges of trafficking offenses, drug possession, and possessing criminal tools.

Detroit Terrace Ltd. v. Bender , No. 11 -CVG-19350 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., De c. 5, 2011) (project -based subsidized 179 But see Forest City Mgmt., Inc. v.Tackett, 148 Ohio App.3d 667, 775 N.E.2d 555 (11th Dist.
2002) (finds requisite specificity where the notice identifies the provisions of the lease that were violated, lists the dates of the incidents, and describes the tenant’s conduct on those dates to be “acts that disturbed the rights and comforts of other residents and staff and which were intimidating, alarming, abusive and obscene to residents and staff”).

211 housing).
(9) Reason: “criminal activity.” Cmty. Props. of Ohio Mgmt. Servs. v.

Taylor, No. 2009 -CVG-001131 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 11,
2009) (project-based subsidized housing).
(10) Reason: tenant violated “the conditi ons and terms of the lease due to serious or repeated damage to the dwelling unit, creation of physical hazards in the unit, common ground or parking area of the unit.”Parma
Pub. Hous. Agency v. Smiley, No. 05-CVG-00759 (Ohio Mun., Parma, May 5, 2005) (Public Housing).
(11) Reason: “unit’s housekeeping was not up to HUD Standards.” New Longwood Assocs. v. Robertson , No. 02 -CVG-8420 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 24, 2002) (project -based subsidized housing); New Longwood Assocs. v. Williams, No. 02-CVG-1431 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 12, 2002) (same).
(12) Reason: “lease termination.”Lawami Corp. v. Briggs, No. 2001-CVG- 9454 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Dec. 5, 2001) (HCVP, eviction for other good cause, sale of property).
(13) Reason: “[Y]ou continue to maintain your suite in a deplorable condition even after our written notice dated September 11, 1998 .... That you continue to maintain the suite in a deplorable condition is evidenced by our inspection of October 12, 1998. Your lack of housekeeping materially affects the health and safety of the residents in the apartment building in which you live.” 180 S&F Mgmt. v. Horvath, No. 98-CVG- 25253 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 6, 1999) (project-based subsidized housing).
(14) Reason: “failure to use your dwell ing as your only residence” and “violating the peaceful enjoyment of neighbors/tenants.” Vistula Mgmt.
Co. v. Singleton, No. CVG-94-12643 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div.,
Nov. 29, 1994) (project-based subsidized housing).
(15) Reason: “bad housekeeping” and failing to keep the premises “safe, clean and decent.” Winchester Gardens v. Swickheimer , No. M9410 - 180 In response to the tenant’s motion for summary judgment, the landlord filed an affidavit that, according to the court, illustrated the required specificity. S&F Mgmt. v. Horvath , No. 98 - CVG-25253, slip op. at 4 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 6, 1999). The affidavit stated: “the apartment was completely cluttered with property over theentire floor area of every room, including books, newspapers, clothing, boxes, small appliances, bags and the like” and “the stove was cluttered with debris creating an obvious fire hazard.” Id.

CVG-031135 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 14, 1994) (project-based subsidized housing).
(16) Reason: “failure to comply with agre ement to pay rental arrearages (nonpayment of rent).” Mt. Olive-Metro Hous., Inc. v. Craft, No. 93-G- 2858 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Feb. 28, 1994) (project -based subsidized housing).
(17) Reason: statement that tenant’s actions have continued to disturb the neighbors’ peaceful enjoyment of the premises.Marchetta v. Wolfe, No.

92-CVG-12931 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Mar. 4, 1993) (Section 8 Moderate Rehabilitation).
(18) Reason: statements that “are blanketed in broad language and do not refer to specific instances or times or dates sufficient for the Defendant to prepare a meaningful rebuttal.” McConnelsville Assocs. v. Gladden,
No. 92 -CV-G-56 (Ohio County Ct., Morgan Cty., Sept. 28, 1992) (project-based subsidized housing).
(19) Reason: statement that tenant was in violation of the lease obligation to refrain from destroying, defacing, damaging, or removing any part of the premises. Lakeview Terrace v. Smith , No. 92 -CVG-8034 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 21, 1992) (Public Housing).
(20) Reason: restatement of the lease language that prohibits unlawful activity in the unit or in the common areas, and a statement that the illegal activity related to drug activity. Uptown Vill. Apts. Ltd. v. White,
No. M9112 -CVG-043446 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 31, 1992) (project-based subsidized housing).
(21) Reason: statement that “restate[d] the language in the lease agreement which describes in a very general sense the type of violations which the plaintiff alleges the defendant committed.” Canterbury Way Apts.

Rogers, No. M9004 -CVG-012282 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., June 5,
1990) (project-based subsidized housing).
(22) Reason: statement that is “merely a restatement of the lease language that defines material noncompliance as including ‘serious or repeated interference with the rights and quiet enjoyment of other tenants.’” Uptown Vill. Apts. v. Stephens , No. M8907 -CVG-25518 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 4, 1989) (project-based subsidized housing).
(23) Reason: “numerous violations of conditions of lease, specifically #30.” Avondale Hous. A ction Program v. Butler , No. 88 -CV-42805 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Mar. 23, 1989) (Section 8 Property Disposition).

213
(24) Reason: comprehensive list of general housekeeping violations that tenants might commit, but which has no relationship to the actual housekeeping violations noted in the landlord’s inspection report.

Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Williams, No. 88-CVG-1268 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, July 8, 1988) (Public Housing).
(25) Reason: “As a result of your violations of the rental agreement which disrupt the livability of the project, adversely affect the health and safety of other tenants, prevent the quiet enjoyment of the leased premises by other tenants, interfere with the management of the project.”Associated Estates Corp. v. Lockhart, No. 87-G-2605 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls,
Mar. 4, 1988) (project-based subsidized housing).
(26) Reason: statement that tenant performed acts that disturbed her neighbors and that the tenant created physical hazards. Burney View
Apts. v. Zeisler, No. 87-CV-17778 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Feb. 3,
1988) (project-based subsidized housing).
(27) Reason: conclusory language from the lease or regulations. United
Prop. Mgmt. Co. v. Mundell, No. R86-CVG-0850 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 22, 1986) (Section 8 New Construction).

#### 3. Eviction Solely for Reason(s) in the Notice

Assisted housing landlords generally may not rely on any ground for eviction that is not stated in the notice of termination. E.g., 24 C.F.R. §§ 247.6(b), 880.607(c)(3);

subsidized housing lease,181 ¶ 23(f). See Palmer Gardens v. Rodgers, No. L-20-1003, 2020 WL 6256858 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 23, 2020) (project -based subsidized housing); Gold Key Realty/Senior Vill. Apts. v. Phillips, No. 26450, 2015 WL 3932449 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 26, 2015) (project -based subsidized housing); Cincinnati Metro Hous. Auth. v. Patterson , No. C -130161, 2013 WL 6409302 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 6, 2013) (Public Housing); Owners Mgmt. Co. v. Moore, 111 Ohio App.3d 820, 677 N.E.2d 400 (6th Dist. 1996) (project -based subsidized housing); Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985) (Section 8 New Construction);Zanesville Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Callipare, No. CA-81-13 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 10, 1981) (Public Housing); Boardwalk Glenville Apts. v. Cage,
No. 2007 -CVG-3065 (Oh io Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 13, 2007) (project - based subsidized housing); Jackson v. Hammond, No. 2006-CVG-31171 (Ohio Mun., 181 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb. 15, 2007) (HCVP); Warren Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Sizemore, No. 2002-CVG-00593 (Ohio County Ct.,Warren Cty., Nov. 6, 2002) (Public Housing); Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Singleton , No. CVG -94-12643 (Ohio Mun., Toledo,
Hous. Div., Nov. 29, 1994) (project-based subsidized housing); Showe Mgmt. Corp. v.

Stover, No. M9306-CVG-17876 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug . 23, 1993) (project- based subsidized housing).

But, for project -based subsidized housing, there is an exception to this rule for grounds not known by the landlord when the notice was sent. 24 C.F.R. § 247.6(b).

#### 4. Consolidation of Federal Notice of Termination

and State Notice to Vacate A recurring issue is whether a federal notice of termination complies with the federal regulations if the notice is consolidated with an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate.182 The federal rules permit these notices to run concurren tly. 24 C.F.R.

§ 966.4(l)(3)(iii) (Public Housing program ); 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(e)(1)(ii) (Housing Choice Voucher program); the subsidized housing lease (see note 174, above) (most project-based subsidized housing programs).

Ohio case law generally permit s consolidation of these notices for project-based subsidized housing tenancies . E.g., Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Armstrong , No. L -93-168, 1994 WL 262571 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 1994); Pataskala Green II v.

Strickler, No. CA -3581, 1991 WL 122358 (Ohio A pp., 5th Dist., June 25, 1991);

Arlington Square ABC Mgmt. v. Clevenger, No. 88CA-004447, 1989 WL 52620 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 17, 1989);ABC Mgmt. Co. v. Gamble, No. 83AP-788, 1983 Ohio
App. LEXIS 16070 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 15, 1983); Sandefur Co. v. Jones, 9 Ohio App.3d 85, 458 N.E.2d 390 (10th Dist. 1982).

182 A consolidation of the federal notice of termination and the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate raises a second issue – whether, in consolidating these notices, the assisted housing landlord is serving the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate before the termination of the tenancy and thus
prematurely. See Section I.C.3.e, above. A consolidated notice may comply with the federal regulations but still violate state law on the time for serving an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate.

As HUD noted in its introdu ctory comments to 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(3)(iii): “Federal law does not affect the time when the State notice may be given, provided the State law notice procedure does not interfere with the Federally required notice of lease termination, or abridge the tenant’s Federal right to a grievance hearing.” 56 Fed. Reg. 51559, 51565 (1991).

215 However, one Ohio court hel d, relative to project-based subsidized housing, that consolidation of these notices is notpermitted if the language of the consolidated notice fails to fulfill the purpose of the federal regulations. Crossroads Somerset Ltd. v.

Newland, 40 Ohio App.3d 20, 24, 531 N.E.2d 327 (10th Dist. 1987). “[T]hat purpose envisions a meeting between landlord and tenant in which both parties attempt to resolve whatever proble m gave rise to the ten -day notice.” Id. Accord Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v. Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 434, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992).

In Newland, the court held a consolidated notice to be invalid because it included the statement: “If you refuse to le ave this unit within ten (10) days, Crossroads/Somerset may enforce this notice ONLY by bringing a judicial action.” Newland, 40 Ohio App. 3d at 24. The court held that this language was contrary to the intent of the federal regulations because it indica ted that the administrative meeting would be useless and that the tenant would be required to vacate despite the tenant’s opportunity to request a meeting with the landlord. Id.

### D. Federal Notice of Termination (Service),

Ohio Case Law

#### 1. Time of Service


##### a. Public Housing

In Public Housing, th e time period for the notice depends on the grounds for the termination of tenancy.

For nonpayment of rent cases, under HUD rules, there are two alternative time periods.

One is a thirty-day period, which applies during national emergencies, when HUD determines that additional time is required for families to secure available funding.

HUD interim final rule, 86 Fed. Reg. 55693 (Oct. 7, 2021); HUD Notice PIH 2021-29, H 2021-06, issued Oct. 7, 2021.183 On October 7, 2021, HUD made this determination and thereby invoked this thirty-day notice requirement. Id. HUD determined that this thirty-day notice requirement will remain in effect until a subsequent notice is issued rescinding the determination. Id.

Ohio courts have held that an assisted housing184 landlord’s failure to comply with this thirty-day notice requirement is grounds to grant a tenant’s motion to dismiss.E.g., Neilan Park Apts. 2, LLC v. Carr , Nos. 21 -CVG-01273,01276,01278,01293 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Nov. 4, 2021); Beavers v. Patawaran, No. 2020-CVG-009066 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 23, 2020); Adedapo v. Brown , No. 2020 - CVG-22643 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 2, 2020).

The other time period fornonpayment of rent cases,which applies when the thirty- day period is not invoked, isa fourteen-day period.185 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(3)(i).
