This fourteen-day period also applies to: (1) eviction actions alleging a tenant’s breach of an agreement to make periodic payments to satisfy a rent arrearage, Dayton Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. McKee, 37 Ohio App.3d 102, 524 N.E.2d 180 (2nd Dist. 1987); and (2) eviction actions alleging payment of delinquent rent after the date specified for the payment in the notice of t ermination, Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Myers , 30 Ohio App.3d 100, 506 N.E.2d 933 (9th Dist. 1986).

The time period is a “reasonable time considering the seriousness of the situation (but not to exceed 30 days)”in cases in which (1) the health or safetyof other residents 183 In all of the assisted housing programs (see note 163, above), for nonpayment of rent cases, a thirty-day notice of termination requirement was established in the CARES Act, Pub. L. 116- 136, 134 Stat. 281, enacted Mar. 27, 2020, at§ 4024(c) (“CARES Act provision”). The CARES Act provision, on its face, does not contain a sunset provision and, therefore, seems to be a permanent law. However, based on the HUD interim final ru le, 86 Fed. Reg. 55693 (Oct. 7, 2021), HUD’s position appears to be that the CARES Act provision has sunset. The reason for this conclusion is that the HUD interim final rule: (1) does not implement the CARES Act provision; (2) instead, establishes a temporary thirty-day notice of termination requirement, for the period that HUD determines a national emergency requires it; and (3) applies its thirty -day notice of termination only to Public Housing and project -based subsidized housing (and not to all assisted housing programs).

184 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, above.

185 If the housing authority granted the tenant an extension of time to pay the rental arrearage, it may not serve the tenant with the fourteen -day notice of termination until after the extended date for payment. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Bederman, No. 96-CVG-02208 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb. 28, 1996).

217 or housing authority employees is threatened, (2) any member of the household engaged in any drug -related or violent criminal activity, or (3) any member of the household has been convicted of a felony. 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(3)(i).

For this notice rule, drug-related criminal activity is not per se a threat to the health and safety of other residents or housing authority employees. Hous. Auth. of Jersey City v. Myers, 295 N.J. Super. 544, 549, 685 A.2d 532 (1996). “Rather, some drug - related criminal activity constitutes such a threat to PHA tenants and employees ..., while other drug-related criminal activities are not such a threat.” Id.

For example, in Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Coley , No. 2001 -CVG-19335 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hou s. Div., May 2, 2003, and Jan. 29, 2004), the housing authority provided the tenant with a three -day (instead of thirty-day) notice of termination, based on the tenant’s drug-related criminal activity. The tenant had been charged with a minor misdemeanor for possession of one marijuana cigarette in her rental unit. The court held that this criminal activity did not constitute the requisite threat to health and safety for a time period of less than thirty days.

Lastly, in all other cases, the time period is thirty days. 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(3)(i).

##### b. Project-Based Subsidized Housing

In project-based subsidized housing, the time period for the notice depends on the ground for the termination of tenancy.186 For nonpayment of rent cases, under HUD rules, there are two alternative time periods.

One is a thirty-day period, which applies during national emergencies, when HUD determines that additional time is required for families to secure available funding.

HUD interim final rule, 86 Fed. Reg. 55693 (Oct. 7, 2021); HUD Notice PIH 2021-29, H 2021-06, issued Oct. 7, 2021.187 On October 7, 2021, HUD made this determination 186 In the Section 8 Moderate Rehabilitation program, under the HUD rules, the time periods are differ ent than those used in the other project -based subsidized housing programs. For nonpayment of rent cases, the time period is five working days. 24 C.F.R. § 882.511(d). In cases of violations of the lease or applicable federal, state, or local law, the t ime period is determined by state and local law. Id. In “other good cause” cases, the time period is thirty
days. Id.

187 For these nonpayment of rent cases, a thirty -day notice of termination requirement was established in the CARES Act, Pub. L. 116 -136, 134 Stat. 281, enacted Mar. 27, 2020, at § 4024(c) (“CARES Act provision”). For a discussion on the terms and status of the CARES Act provision, see note 183, above.

and thereby invoked this thirty-day notice requirement. Id. HUD determined that this thirty-day notice requirement will remain in effect until a subsequent notice is issued rescinding the determination. Id.

Ohio courts have held that an assisted housing188 landlord’s failure to comply with this thirty-day notice requirement is grounds to grant a tenant’s motion to dismiss.E.g., Neilan Park Apts. 2, LLC v. Carr , Nos. 21 -CVG-01273,01276,01278,01293 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Nov. 4, 2021); Beavers v. Patawaran, No. 2020-CVG-009066 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 23, 2020); Adedapo v. Brown , No. 2020 - CVG-22643 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 2, 2020).

The other time period for nonpayment of rent cases, which applies when the thirty- day period is not invoked, is ten days. HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1,189 ¶¶ 8-13(B), 8-16(B); subsidized housing lease,190 ¶ 23(e).

For “other good cause” cases, the time period is thirty days. Id.

In all other cases, the time period is ten days. Id.

##### c. HCVP

In the Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”),under HUD rules, a two-fold time period is used in all cases. First, the notice of termination may be served at or before the commencement of the eviction action. 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(e). Second, it must be served before the termination of the tenancy.24 C.F.R. § 982.310(e) (“tenancy does not terminate before the owner has given this notice”). See also 64 Fed. Reg.

56893, 56904 (Oct. 21, 1999) (“Section 982.310(e) has been revised to specify that the tenancy does not terminate during the term of the lease before the owner has given notice to the tenant that specifies the grounds for termination”) ; 42 U.S.C.

§ 1437f(d)(1)(B)(iv) (“termination of tenancy shall be preceded by the owner’s provision of written notice to the tenant specifying the grounds for such action”).

Under Ohio law, the seco nd part of the HCVP time period arguably requires service of the notice of termination before service of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate.

Ohio law bars service of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate until after the termination of the tenancy (see Section I.C.3.e, above), whereas the HCVP notice of termination 188 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, above.

189 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

190 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

219 must be served before the termination of the tenancy. Since the HCVP notice of termination may terminate the tenancy upon its issuance, instead of a specified number of days thereafter, the R.C. 1923 .04 notice to vacate may be served at any time after the service of the HCVP notice of termination.191 For nonpayment of rent cases, another potential HCVP notice of termination requirement is the thirty-day notice of termination requirement that was established in the CARES Act, Pub. L. 116-136, 134 Stat. 281, enacted Mar. 27, 2020, at § 4024(c) (“CARES Act provision”). For a discussion on the terms and status of the CARES Act provision, see note 183, above.

#### 2. Method of Service

If the method by which the federal notice of termination is served fails to meet any of the requirements of the applicable federal regulations, it is a defense to the eviction
action. The method of service must strictly comply with all of the requirements of the applicable federal regulations. See Section III.B, above.

Ohio state court decisions finding a method of service invalid are set forth below, along with the relevant regulation and the method of service.
(1) 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(k)(1) (Public Housing): the notice of termination was served upon a child of eight to ten years of age.Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Brown, No. 43566, 1981 WL 4963 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 21, 1981).
(2) 24 C.F.R. § 247.4(b) (project-based subsidized housing): the notice of termination was served by personal service at the dwelling unit, but not also by mail service. Fairborn Apts. v. Herman , No. 90-CA-28, 1991 WL 10962 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 31, 1991); Biltmore Apts. v.

Birdsong, No. 11-CVG-5315 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Oct . 5, 2011). But see Marshall Plaza v. Watkins, No. 4161, 1987 WL 12113 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 3, 1987) (personal service but no mail service; tenant does not deny receiving the notice; because absence of mail service did not prejudice the tenant, app ellate court refused to reverse eviction judgment despite the violation of the regulation); M.L. Gabriel Commons v. King, No. 13159, 1992 WL 288781 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Oct. 14, 1992) (follows Watkins).

191 The Ohio case law is split on whether the date of service of the federal notice of termination must precede the date of service of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate. For a discussion on that case law, see Section I.C.3.e, above.

#### 3. Service of a Second Notice of Termination

One Ohio court has held, “as a matter of la w[,] that where an action in forcible entry and detainer is dismissed, prior to filing a subsequent action in forcible entry and detainer, any and all notices required by either statute or the contract at issue must be re-served by the Plaintiff upon the Defendant.” Apt. Mgmt., Inc. v. Woods , No. 05 - CVG-650, slip op. at 4 (Ohio Mun., Lorain, June 1, 2005). In Woods, the required notice was a federal notice of termination under a project -based subsidized housing program.

The court in Woods set forth a two-fold rationale for this holding. “It is axiomatic that where a notice is required to be served in one instance, it must be served in another.

Moreover, when a complaint is dismissed, for whatever reason, it is logical to assume that the defendant will conclude that the matter is over and that the plaintiff, should he wish to ‘resurrect’ his allegations, must do so from scratch.” Id., slip op. at 3-4. But
cf. Voyager Vill. Ltd. v. Williams , 3 Ohio App.3d 288, 444 N.E.2d 1337 (2nd Dist.
1982) (R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of termination).

### E. Federal Notice of Termination,

Opportunity to Cure the Breach The assisted housing regulations do not explicitly state that, during the period of the federal notice of termination,192 the tenant has a right to cure the breach of duty that is specified in the notice and thereby prevent a termination of the tenancy.193 However, in affording the tenant a right to meet with the landlord in response to the federal notice of termination,194 the regulations may implicitly afford the tenant that right to cure. National Housing Law Project, HUD Housing Programs: Tenants’ Rights ¶ 11.3.3.2.2 (5th ed. 201 8). See Hedco, Ltd. v. Blanchette , 763 A.2d 639, 642 (R.I.

192 For a discussion on the various time periods of the federal notice of termination, see Section III.D.1, above.

193 In contrast, under the Rural Housing Service rental programs, the regulations (7 C.F.R.

§ 3560.159(a)) expressly afford the tenant an opportun ity to cure the violation that is specified in the notice. E.g., Summertree Terrace v. Thatcher , No. CVG -0600419 (Ohio Mun., Washington Court House, July 13, 2006);Aspen Green/WODA v. Howard, No. 04-CVG-00467 (Ohio Mun., Cambridge, Sept. 30, 2004).

194 This right to meet with the landlord exists in the project -based assisted housing programs, but not the Housing Choice Voucher program. See Section III.F, below.

221
2000) (“the notice ... compl[ied] with the ten-day time period, during which a tenant could cure the breach of nonpayment as required by section (sic) 4350.3 of the HUD Handbook and section 23(b) of the parties’ lease agreement”);Vesta Cleveland LLC v.

Swann, No. 2003 -CVG-5468, slip op. at 5 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 20, 2003) (“A notice [of termination] requirement like the one in the parties’ [project- based subsidized housing] lease offers a tenant an opportunity to correct a problem once the landlord has identified it”); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Bederman, No.

96-CVG-02208, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb. 28, 1996) (“The purpose of the 14 Day Notice of Termination is to provide a resident of public housing an opportunity to avoid eviction by curing an alleged nonpayment of rent”). Cf.

Madtson v. Flick, No. 94-CVG-224 (Ohio County Ct., Sandusky Cty., Jan. 17, 1995) (reason for eviction was tenant’s notice of vacation of premises; eviction denied because, among other things, during the ten -day period of the notice of termination, tenant rescinded his notice of vacation and tendered all rent due).

In the project -based subsidized housing program, the regulations require “a meeting between landlord and tenant in which both parties attempt to resolve whatever problems gave rise to the ten-day notice [of termination].” Crossroads Somerset Ltd.
v. Newland , 40 Ohio App.3d 20, 24, 531 N.E.2d 327 (10th Dist. 1987). Accord Independent Mgmt. Servs. of Ohio, Inc. v. Davis , No. 2014-CVG-01244, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 18, 2014) (held the notice of termination invalid because it “suggests that the decision to terminate Defendant’s tenancy already occurred” and thus “[i]t is not clear what benefit could be derived from the tenant’s ‘discussion’ of a matter already decided”).

The purpose of the meeting “is to attempt to resolve the controversy in a mutually satisfactory manner, that will, if possible, avoid the tenant’s loss of subsidized housing while protecting the rights of the landlord.” Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v. Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 434, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992). Accord Independent Mgmt. Servs.

of Ohio, Inc. v. Davis, No. 2014-CVG-01244, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 18,
2014) (as to the meeting, “this is not a mere formality, but confers on Defendant, beneficiary of the federal subsidy, a first chance at procedural due process to meet with Plaintiff to try to avoid the potential termination”).

If a tenant cures the breach at or before the meeting with the landlord, generally the cure of the breach should resolve the controversy. In such circumstances, if the landlord still seeks to terminate the tenancy, the landlord arguably violates the landlord’s obligation to attempt in good faith to resolve the controversy at the meeting.

However, if a tenant repeatedly has breached a lease obligation, the tenant might not be entitled to another opportunity to cure the breach. See, e.g., Vill. S. Apts. v.

Jensen, No. CA96-10-018, 1997 WL 148067, at *2 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Mar. 31, 1997); Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Armstrong, No. L-93-168, 1994 WL 262571, at *5 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 1994). Cf. Fed. Prop. Mgmt. v. Brown, No. 17424, 1999 WL 961275, at *5-6 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 25, 1999) (applying R.C. 5321.11 right to cure).

A landlord may include language in a federal notice of termination that creates an explicit right (in addition to the implicit right) to cure. When the notice informs the tenant to take or cease certain action or the lease will be terminated, and t he tenant timely complies with the directive, the landlord may not terminate the tenancy for the underlying issue. See Rockville Ctr. Hous. Auth. v. Boggen, 20 Misc.3d 1126(A), 2008 WL 2927834 (N.Y. Dist. Ct. July 31, 2008) (tenant complied with directive : “Please have your guest vacate your apartment immediately or your lease will be terminated and you will be evicted”).

Also, if the reason for the proposed termination of tenancy is tenant conduct that breaches an R.C. 5321.05 obligation that materiallyaffects health and safety, the tenant has a right to cure the breach and thereby avoid a termination of the tenancy under R.C.

5321.11. See Sections I.D.2.d and I.D.2.e, above.

In the Public Housing program, a tenant may not have a right to cure the breac h during the pendency of a grievance procedure hearing, which generally is held after the expiration of the time period of the federal notice of termination. See, e .g., Trumbull Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Reid , No. 3673, 1987 WL 18019 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., S ept. 30, 1987) (reason for termination was tenant’s refusal to sign required releases; tenant contested the termination in the grievance procedure, and sought to cure the breach after the parties had submitted their evidence to the hearing panel but before the panel had rendered its decision; court held that the tenant did not have a right to cure the breach at that time).

### F. Administrative Hearing/Meeting, Ohio

Case Law In project-based assisted housing, when a landlord proposes to terminate a tenancy, generally the tenant is entitled to an opportunity to contest the proposed termination in an administrative hearing or a meeting, before the commencement of an eviction action 223 against the tenant.

Subject to three exceptions, a Public Housing tenant is entitle d to an opportunity to contest the proposed termination in an administrative hearing under the housing authority’s grievance procedure. 24 C.F.R. §§ 966.4(l)(3), 966.4(n), 966.51(a). The three exceptions are cases in which the termination involves; (1) any criminal activity that threatens the health, safety, or right to peaceful enjoyment of the premises of other residents or housing authority employees, (2) any violent or drug -related criminal activity on or off the premises, or (3) any criminal activity that resulted in felony conviction of a household member. Id.

In addition, a Public Housing tenant may file an R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal (see Section XXIV.C, below) from a grievance procedure decision on a proposed termination of tenancy, even if an eviction action has been filed. Perry v. Akron Metro.
Hous. Auth., No. CV-05-01-0290 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Feb. 28, 2005). However, pending the R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal, unless the eviction action is stayed, the eviction court will likely enter a final order and thereby determine the R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal under res judicata or collateral estoppel principles. In Perry, the court of common pleas entered an order that stayed the pending eviction action in the Cuyahoga Falls Municipal Court, “pending a fina l decision in the administrative appeal.” Perry v.

Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. CV -05-01-0290 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Mar. 1, 2005).

A project-based subsidized housing tenant is entitled to an opportunity to contest the proposed termination in a meeti ng with the landlord. 24 C.F.R. § 880.607(c)(1);

HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1,195 ¶ 8-13(B). Neither the federal regulations nor due process provide project -based subsidized housing tenants with a right to an administrative hearing. Perry v. Royal Arms Apts., 729 F.2d 1081 (6th Cir. 1984) (no due process right to an administrative hearing). Nonetheless, at the meeting, the landlord is required to attempt to resolve the controversy and thereby avoid the tenant’s loss of subsidized housing. Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v. Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 434, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992); Crossroads Somerset Ltd. v. Newland , 40 Ohio
App. 3d 20, 24, 531 N.E.2d 327 (10th Dist. 1987).

Ohio state court decisions on these administrative hearing and meeting rights are set forth below.

195 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.
Fed. Prop. Mgmt. v. Brown, No. 17424, 1999 WL 961275 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 25, 1999) (Section 8 New Construction) The notice of termination stated that the tenant had ten days within which to discuss the proposed termination with the landlord. This satisfied the requirement to provide the tenant with an opportunity to meet with the landlord, notwithstanding the landlord’s letter to the tenant, fourteen days earlier, stating that “it is unfortunate that that there is no need to request meet ings or telephone to discuss this matter; the context is very clear.” The earlier letter did not foreclose the possibility of a meeting between the landlord and tenant. The tenant did not request a meeting in response to the notice of termination.
Vill. S. Apts. v. Jensen , No. CA96-10-018, 1997 WL 148067 (Ohio App., 12th Dist.,
Mar. 31, 1997) (project-based subsidized housing) During the required meeting, the landlord told the tenant that there was nothing that she could do to avert the termination of the tenancy because she repeatedly had breached the lease agreement despite numerous warnings. The landlord’s position did not deny the tenant the required opportunity to meet with the landlord. The requirement to meet does not require “the landlord to resolve the dispute in a manner favorable to the tenant or to refrain from filing a forcible entry and detainer action simply because the tenant requests a meeting to discuss the termination.” Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Armstrong , No. L -93-168, 1994 WL 262571 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 1994) (Section 8 New Construction) “While we recognize the purpose of the ten -day meeting required under the federal regulations and realize that a landlord should not enter into a meeting already having decided to evict the tenan t, that does not mean that a landlord must absolutely let a tenant cure her breach.” Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v. Wooten, 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992) (Section 8 New Construction) “The purpose of the requirement of the federal regulationsand the lease agreement that a tenant must be afforded an opportunity to meet with the landlord to discuss a proposed termination of tenancy is to attempt to resolve the controversy in a mutually satisfactory manner, that will, if possible, avoid the tenant’s loss of subsidized housing while protecting the rights of the landlord.” The landlord’s failure to give the tenant an opportunity to meet with the landlord to discuss the notice of proposed termination, after the tenant had requested timely such a mee ting, was grounds to reverse the eviction judgment.

Chautauqua Park Apts. v. McMullen, No. 791, 1992 WL 303139 (Ohio App., 4th Dist.,
Oct. 14, 1992) (project-based subsidized housing) 225 The landlord met the requirement for a meeting even though thelandlord’s agent at the meeting did not have personal authority to halt the proposed termination of tenancy.

The landlord’s agent at the meeting “served as a conduit through which appellants [tenants] could reach the party who did have authority.” Nothing in the record suggests that the tenant’s arguments were not listened to and given due consideration.

Crossroads Somerset Ltd. v. Newland, 40 Ohio App.3d 20, 531 N.E.2d 327 (10th Dist.
1987) (project-based subsidized housing) At the meeting between the la ndlord and tenant, the parties are required to attempt to resolve whatever problems gave rise to the notice of termination.

Trumbull Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Reid , No. 3673, 1987 WL 18019 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 30, 1987) (Public Housing) The tenant did not have a right to cure, during the pendency of her grievance procedure hearing, her prior refusal to furnish certain income verification information.

Fitzpatrick v. Wallick Props., Inc., No. L-83-036, 1983 WL 6817 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 3, 1983) (Section 8 New Construction) The landlord’s cancellation of an informal meeting, which had been scheduled pursuant to 24 C.F.R. § 880.607, and the landlord’s failure to afford the tenant any other opportunity to meet with the landlord, did not prejudice the tenant and therefore were not grounds to reverse the eviction judgment.

Independent Mgmt. Servs. of Ohio, Inc. v. Davis , No. 2014-CVG-01244 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 18, 2014) (project-based subsidized housing) The meeting “is not a mere formality, but conf ers on Defendant, beneficiary of the federal subsidy, a first chance at procedural due process to meet with Plaintiff to avoid the potential termination.” Held the notice of termination invalid because it “suggests that the decision to terminate Defendant ’s tenancy already occurred” and thus “[i]t is not clear what benefit could be derived from the tenant’s ‘discussion’ of a matter already decided.” Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Wilkinson, No. 2001-CVG-110 (Ohio Mun., Akron, April 25, 2001) (Public Housing) The housing authority asserted two grounds for termination of the tenancy. With respect to one (i.e., unauthorized occupant), the tenant was entitled to an opportunity for a grievance procedure hearing. With respect to the other (i.e., drug-related criminal activity), the tenant was not entitled to an opportunity for a grievance procedure
hearing. In such circumstances, the housing authority may not serve the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate until after the tenant has been afforded an opportunity for a grievance procedure hearing on the relevant ground.

Plains Plaza Ltd. v. Rupe , No. 90 -CVG-11-43 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., Mar. 11,
1991) (Section 8 New Construction) Under 24 C.F.R. § 880.607 and HUD Handbook 4350.3, ¶ 4-20 (now HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV -1,196 ¶ 8-13(B)), the landlord’s failure to meet with the tenant and meaningfully discuss the proposed termination of tenancy prior to filing the eviction action deprived the court of jurisdiction to evict the tenant. Settlement discussions between counsel for the parties and discovery proceedings are not a substitute for the required informal meeting between the landlord and tenant. At the required informal meeting, the landlord’s representative must be an impartial decision maker.

Plains Plaza, Ltd. v. McKinley , No. CIG -90-5-34 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., Feb. 8,
1991) (Section 8 New Construction) The landlord failed to provide the tenant with an informal meeting that satisfied the requirements of HUD Handbook 4350.3 (now HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV -1, ¶ 8 - 13(B)) because the landlord’s representative at the meeting had been involved with the gathering of the disputed information.

Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Lawson , 40 Ohio Misc.2d 25, 532 N.E.2d 1323 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, 1987) (Public Housing) The housing authority’s fa ilure to comply with several grievance procedure requirements (24 C.F.R. §§ 966.54-.58) was a valid defense to the eviction action.

Deerwood Mgmt. Co. v. Flint, No. M85-CVG-24397 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct.

15, 1985) (Section 8 Additional Assistance) 24 C.F.R. § 886.128 required the landlord to give the tenant a meaningful opportunity to be heard at a meeting. The meeting is not a mere formality. The meeting must be conducted by an agent for the landlord who is at least willing to listen and considerthe stated position of the tenant. This standard was not met because the landlord’s agent had his mind made up before the meeting was commenced. [Note: the requirement in issue here has since been deleted from 24 C.F.R. § 886.128, but it still exists in HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1, ¶ 8-13(B).] Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Westfall , No. 79-CVG-7932 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Oct.

15, 1979) (Public Housing) Where the tenant’s alleged breach of the lease concerned a fight between the tenant’s child and a neighbor’s child, it was not unreasonable for the housing authority to consider the alleged breach to be a threat to health and safety as defined at 24 C.F.R.

§§ 866.4(l)(2)(ii) and 866.51 (redesignated §§ 966.4(l)(2)(ii) and 966.51). Therefore, under 24 C.F.R. § 866.51, the housing authority was authorized to deny the tenant an 196 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

227 opportunity to contest the proposed eviction in the housing authority’s grievance procedure.

### G. Good Cause, Ohio Case Law


#### 1. Nonpayment of Rent


##### a. Circumstances Beyond Tenant’s Control

Nonpayment of rent is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy if:
(1) it is due to circumstances beyond the tenant’s control, (2) the tenant promptly informs the landlord of the circumstances, and (3) the tenant makes diligent efforts to pay the rent. E.g., Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Ruffin, No. CA-8751, 1992 WL 147443 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 15, 1992), on remand, No. 91 -CVG-5463 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Oct. 2, 1992) (late welfare payment); Fairborn Apts. v. Herman, No. 90-CA- 28, 1991 WL10962 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 31, 1991) (purse stolen and non-receipt of child support payment); Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Green , 41 Ohio App.3d 365, 536 N.E.2d 1 (1st Dist. 1987) (money stolen); Northlake Hills Coop., Inc. v.

Barrett, No. 9699, 1987 WL 14238 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 8, 1986) (money stolen); Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Bickmeyer , No. 06-CVG-00631 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Apr. 19, 2006) (Bureau of Child Support failed to forward child support payments); Stark Metro. Hous. v. Short, No. 96-CVG-1298 (Ohio Mun., Canton, July 9, 1996) (husband abandoned household); Lima Towers v. Franklin , No. 96 -CVG- 00191 (Ohio Mun., Lima, Apr. 8, 1996) (purse stolen); New Prince Hall Hous. Auth.
v. Thornton , No. 91 -CVG-1754 (Ohio Mun., Clark Cty., Ju ly 9, 1991) (Worker’s Compensation benefits temporarily stopped); Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Lawson ,
No. 85 -CVG-9998 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Nov. 20, 1985) (late welfare payment);

Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Cantrell , No. 85-CVG-7589 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Oct.

10, 1985) (late welfare payment); Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Thompson , No. 80 - CVG-9597 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Dec. 3, 1980) (initial welfare payment delay);Belvoir Cliffs Apts. v. Bembry , 56 Ohio Misc. 37, 383 N.E.2d 1170 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
1978) (in itial welfare payment delay). See also Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Cockrell, No. C-070647 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 17, 2008) (recognizes the defense, but upholds the finding that the nonpayment was not due to circumstances beyond the tenant’s control); Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. McQueen , No. CA -8422, 1991 WL 136099 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 15, 1991) (remand for factual findings on the cause for termination).

One Ohio appellate court held that, in the appeal, a ruling on the “beyond tenant’s control” issue was premature because the trial court had failed “to resolve the underlying issue of whether a single missed payment of rent is sufficient to warrant eviction from federally assisted public housing.” CMHA v. Manns, No. 107156, 2019 WL 1755414, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 18, 2019). The court remanded the case for further proceedings to resolve that issue. Id.

A deduction of child support payments is not a circumstance beyond the tenant’s
control. See Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. E dwards, 174 Ohio App.3d 174, 881 N.E.2d 325 (1st Dist. 2007) (child support payments deducted from Social Security disability payments).

##### b. Improperly Calculated Rent

Nonpayment of an improperly calculated rent is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy. “A landlord of federally subsidized housing has good cause to terminate a tenant for nonpayment of rent only if the landlord has properly determined the amount of rent that the tenant owes.”New Longwood Assocs. v. Smith,
No. 2006-CVG-7635, slip op. at 3 (Ohio Mun. Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 1, 2006).

The improper rent computations for which courts have denied a landlord’s eviction claim for nonpayment of rent frequently have been a landlord’s failure to compute (or to compute properly) an interim rent revision. E.g., Amherst Vill. Mgmt. v. Vestal, No.

WD-99-075, 2000 WL 1595719 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 27, 2000) (failure to grant interim rent reduction for change in household composition); Gold Key Realty v.

Harris, No. 03-CVG-00475 (Ohio Mun., Fairborn, Apr. 22, 2003) (improper effective date of interim rent decrease); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Binion , No. 2002 - CVG-5589 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug. 29, 2002) (failure to grant interim rent reduction for loss of employmen t); Amherst Vill. Mgmt. v. Bock , No. 99 -CVG- 00259 (Ohio Mun., Bowling Green, Mar. 30, 1999) (improper effective date of interim rent increase); Skyline Terrace Apts. v. Brunner , No. 97 -CVG-6830 (Ohio Mun., Canton, Mar. 3, 1998) (failure to grant timely int erim rent reduction for loss of employment); Pendergrass v. Kelly, No. M9110-CVG-036723 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Feb. 3, 1992) (failure to grant interim rent reduction for loss of employment);Hi- Land Terrace v. Bartley , No. 90 -CVG-222 (Ohio Mun., Hillsb oro, Nov. 14, 1990) (failure to grant interim rent reduction).

Other improper rent computations for which courts have denied a landlord’s eviction claim for nonpayment of rent include:Boardwalk Glenville Apts. v. Cage, No.

229 2007-CVG-3065 (Ohio Mun., Clevel and, Hous. Div., July 13, 2007) (improper rent increase to market rent); New Longwood Assocs. v. Smith, No. 2006-CVG-7635 (Ohio
Mun. Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 1, 2006) (improper estimate of self -employed tenant’s annual income); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth . v. Binion , No. 2002 -CVG- 5589 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug. 29, 2002) (failure to disregard certain earned income); Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Cajka, No. 88-CVG-1065 (Ohio Mun., Lorain, Feb. 6, 1989) (improper computation of tenant’s income byincluding monthly child support payments that were deducted from his pay by wage assignment);197 Agler Green Coop. v. Frost , No. M88 -CVG-13272 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 20,
1988) (improper rent increase to market rent);Hilltop Vill. v. West, No. CVG-85-16250 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., Jan. 27, 1986) (improper rent increase to market rent).198 See also Weldon Square Apts. v. Lyons, No. 84AP-06, 1985 WL 9901 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 19, 1985) (landlord estopped from evicting tenant for nonpayment of rent where tenant notified landlord of her loss of employment and landlord failed to advise her of verification procedure for obtaining federal rent subsidy that would have reduced her rental obligation); Pauley v. Columbus Metro. Hous.

Auth., No. 07-CVF-12-17104 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty., Nov. 5, 2008) (failure to grant hardship exemption from minimum rent; eviction action stayed to permit tenant to exercise this right).

##### c. Improper Rent Deposit

Nonpayment of rent is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy if the tenant timely deposited rent with the clerk of court under R.C. 5321.07 and, thereafter, the court released the deposited rent to the landlord because of the tenant’s noncompliance with the R.C. 5321.07 written notice requirement. Real Props. Servs.
Mgmt. v. Anatra, No. 3-96-22, 1997 WL 430778 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., July 30, 1997);

Real Props. Servs. Mgmt. v. Harigle, No. 3-96-21, 1997 WL 430773 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., July 30, 1997).

197 The Cajka conclusion on the underlying rent computation issue – deducted child support payments are not income for the rent computation – was thereafter rejected in HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1, ¶ 5-10(F) (see note 143, above) and Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Edwards, 174 Ohio App.3d 174, 881 N.E.2d 325 (1st Dist. 2007) (2-1 decision).

198 One court has held that a landlord’s improper rent increase to market rent is not a defense to an eviction action, if the tenant fails totender the pre-rent increase amount. Showe Mgmt. Corp.
v. Hazelbaker, No. CA2005-11-031, 2006 WL 1976760 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 17, 2006).

##### d. Rent Refused Due to Unpaid Charge

An assisted housing landlord may not refuse a tenant’s tender of rent, on the basis of the tenant’s nonpayment of a charge, and then evict the tenant for nonpayment of
rent. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Noel, No. 06-CA-009006, 2007 WL 1661854 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 11, 2007) (maintenance charge). See also Seldin Co. v. Calabro, 702 N.W.2d 504 (Iowa App. 2005) (late payment fee); Watertown Hous. Auth. v.

Kirkland, 2 Misc.3d 280, 766 N.Y.S.2d 790 (City Ct. 2003) (utility and maintenance charges). But see W ilmington Green III v. Rickman , No. CA90 -07-013, 1991 WL 81850 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 13, 1991) (late payment fee).199 In Noel, the landlord assessed the tenant a $50 maintenance charge, in addition to her $32 per month rent, and refused to accept the tenant’s tender of rent without payment of the maintenance charge as well. “By including the maintenance charge in the rent statement and demanding the payment of either all or none, then by suing to terminate appellee’s [tenant’s] tenancy solely on the basis of the non-payment of rent, appellant [landlord] has effectively expanded the definition of rent to encompass other charges.” Noel, 2007 WL 1661854, at *3. The court held that this is impermissible and, thereupon, upheld the eviction judgment in favor of the tenant.

In these circumstances, a landlord may seek to terminate the tenancy for nonpayment of the maintenance charge.Noel, 2007 WL 1661854, at *3. A termination for nonpayment of a charge differs from a termination for nonpayment of rent in a t least four respects. First, the nonpayment of the charge may be a minor violation of the lease and thus not good cause to terminate the tenancy. See Section III.G.3, below.

Second, before an assisted housing landlord may terminate a tenancy for nonpayment of a disputed charge, there may be a requirement for an opportunity for a prior hearing before a neutral adjudicator. See Section III.G.3, below. Third, for Public Housing, the notice requirement for nonpayment of a charge differs from the notice requirement for nonpayment of rent. See 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(3). Fourth, the charge may arise from a violation of R.C. 5321.05, which triggers the tenant’s right to a notice and an opportunity to cure under R.C. 5321.11. See Section I.D.2, above.

An additional factor prohibits a landlord from refusing a tenant’s tender of rent and then evicting for nonpayment of rent, if the refusal is based on the tenant’s nonpayment 199 Based on the sound reasoning in Noel and Calabro, the holding in Rickman is erroneous.

231 of a late payment fee. HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1,200 ¶ 6-23(F) expressly prohibits a subsidized housing landlord from evicting a tenant for nonpayment of a late payment
fee. Therefore, a landlord’s eviction action for nonpayment of rent in these circumstances is simply an attempt to do indirectly what it is prohibited from doing
directly. Seldin Co. v. Calabro, 702 N.W.2d 504, 507 (Iowa App. 2005).

#### 2. Late Tender of Rent

A late tender of rent, if tendered a few days late, is a minor violation of the lease and, for that reason, may fail to constitute good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy (see Section III.G.12, below). E.g., Sumac Real Estate Holding, LLC v.

Mitchell, No. 2014-CVG-00670 (Ohio Mun., Euclid, Apr. 18, 2014); Jenkins v. Rung,
No. 93-CVGT-601 (Ohio Mun., Miami Cty., July 5, 1993) (five days late); Columbus
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Baker, No. 91-CVG-010490 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 19,
1991) (one day late). See also Gersten Cos. v. Deloney , 212 Cal. App.3d 1119, 261
Cal. Rptr. 431 (1989) (five days and seven days late). Cf. Skyline Terrace Apts. v.

Pease, No. 95-CVG-2392 (Ohio Mun., Canton, July 17, 1995) (“in the interest of fair play, one should not be evicted from her residence for being a day or two late”);

Amherst Vill. Mgmt. Co. v. Martinez, No. 97-CVG-00620 (Ohio Mun., Bowling Green, July 15, 1997) (two days late under repayment agreement for rent arrearage).

A late tender of rent, with a tender of the late payment fee, if tendered within the month that the rent is due, is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy “where the tardiness was not flagra nt or for long periods of time.” Park Forest of Blackman v. Smith, 112 Mich. App. 421, 429, 316 N.W.2d 442 (1982). For subsidized housing tenancies, it is not good cause to terminate the tenancy even if the tenant repeatedly makes such payments, unless i t has an adverse impact on the building, as defined in 24 C.F.R. § 247.3(c)(2). Am. Nat’l Bank & Trust Co. v. Dominick, 154 Ill.

App.3d 275, 280 n.3, 507 N.E.2d 512 (1987).201 Also, a lease provision imposing a late payment fee arguably “implies that the only penalty for a late rental payment is the late fee charge, not termination of the lease.” Park Forest of Blackman v. Smith , 112 Mich. App. 421, 428 -29, 316 N.W.2d 442 (1982). Accord Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Armstrong, No. L-93-168, 1994 WL 262571, at 200 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

201 In these circumstances, the tenant also may have a defense based on the landlord’s course of conduct of accepting late rent payments. See Section II.H, above.

*6 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 1994) (“While paragraph five of the lease [providing for a late payment fee if rent is paid after the fifth day of a month] may evidence an obligation to accept late rent, at least in an isolated incidence where a tenant tend ers rent past the fifth business day of the month, this does not mean that a landlord must continually accept late rent since the lease itself provides for termination for material noncompliance, which is defined as ‘repeated late payment of rent’”); Sumac Real Estate Holding, LLC v. Mitchell , No. 2014 -CVG-00670, slip op. at 5 (Ohio Mun., Euclid, Apr. 18, 2014) (“A lease provision in posing a late fee implies that the only penalty for a late rental payment is the late fee charge, not termination of the lease”).

An assisted housing landlord’s promise to accept rent by a certain date, which is after the lease due date, might not be enforceable.See, e.g., Marshall Plaza v. Watkins,
No. 4161, 1987 WL 12113 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 3, 1987).

#### 3. Nonpayment of a Charge

Nonpayment of a late payment fee is not good cause to terminate a project -based subsidized housing tenancy. HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV -1,202 ¶ 6-23(F). E.g., Waterside Estate Apts. v. Keen , No. 94 -CVG-2296 (Ohio Mun., Massillon, Dec. 13, 1994). See also Seldin Co. v. Calabro, 702 N.W.2d 504 (Iowa App. 2005).

Nonpayment of a small charge may be a minor violation of the lease and, for that reason, may fail to constitute good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy (see Section III.G.12, below). E.g., Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Johnson, No. 2021-CVG- 01447 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, Aug. 13, 2021) (tenant without income unable to pay charge for locks installed for security concerns and annual fee ($57.27) for seasonal use of PHA’s air conditioner in a high-rise unit); Dayton Inv. Group-7 v. Lathern, No. 15866, 1996 WL 741822 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 13, 1996) ($10, $11, and $25 charges);

Jones v. Burns , No. R90 -CVG-2776 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Nov. 29, 1990) ($3 charge); ABC Mgmt. Co. v. Williams , No. M86 -CVG-34323 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 19, 1986) ($5 and $10 charges). Cf. Sumner v. McKinley, No. M9306-CVG- 17388 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., July 30, 1993) (equity, $20 charge).

Nonpayment of a disputed charge may be good cause to commence an eviction action against an assisted housing tenant, provided certain procedural requirements have been satisfied. One appellate court has held that, before an assisted housing landlord may c ommence an eviction action against a tenant for nonpayment of a 202 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

233 disputed charge, the tenant “must be afforded an opportunity to be heard before a neutral adjudicator,” before whom the tenant must be afforded “an opportunity to confront and to cross -examine adverse witnesses.” Gorsuch Homes, Inc. v. Wooten , 73 Ohio App.3d 426, 429, 597 N.E.2d 554 (2nd Dist. 1992). Since the reference to the right to confront and cross -examine adverse witnesses would be unnecessary if the neutral adjudication required a court action (and judgment), it suggests that the neutral adjudication may be an administrative hearing.

However, one municipal court has held that, before an assisted housing landlord may commence an eviction action against a tenant for nonpayment of a dispu ted charge, the landlord must obtain a court judgment that establishes both the existence and the amount of the tenant’s liability. Wilmington Green Apts. v. Estep , No. 88 - CVG-64 (Ohio Mun., Wilmington, Apr. 7, 1988). Cf. Hous. Auth. of City of Atlantic City v. Taylor , 171 N.J. 580, 796 A.2d 193 (2002) (nonpayment of a charge cannot serve as basis for eviction for nonpayment of rent, but may serve as basis for action for monetary judgment). The Estep court further held that, if the landlord obtains a monetary judgment and the tenant fails to satisfy it, the landlord may then commence an eviction action against the tenant for nonpayment of the monetary judgment.

#### 4. Recertification – Failure to Furnish Information


##### a. Project-Based Assisted Housing – Termination or

Market Rent If a project-based assisted housing tenant fails to furnish (or furnishes inaccurate) information at a recertification, the landlord is permitted, in some circumstances: (i) to terminate the tenancy, e.g., Trumbull Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Reid, No. 3673, 1987 WL 18019 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 30, 1987); Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Cox,
No. 01-CV-24085 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., June 4, 2002); or (ii) alternatively, to terminate the tenant’s rent subsidy, increase the tenant’s rent tomarket rent, and, upon the tenant’s nonpayment of the market rent, evict the tenant for nonpayment of the market rent, e.g., Woodruff Vill. Apts. v. Jones, No. L-90-145, 1991 WL 35165 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 15, 1991); Newark Green v. Hull , No. CA -3532, 1990 WL 125477 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 24, 1990); Vesta Corp. v. Jones, No. 2017-CVG- 01908 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, Jan. 2, 2019). See also HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1,203 203 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

¶¶ 8-3 to 8-7.

The landlord’s right to terminate the tenancy or the rent subsidy in these circumstances is subject to at least three requirements.

First, the landlord may terminate the tenancy or the rent subsidy in these circumstances only if the tenant’s omission or misrepresentation of information is
fraudulent. HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1, ¶¶ 8-13(A), 8-17, and 8-18. To establish the requisite fraud in these cases, the landlord must prove each of the common law elements of fraud, including intent. See Powell v. D.C. Hous. Auth. , 818 A.2d 188 (D.C. App. 2003).

Unintentional omissions or errors do not constitute the requisite fraud. HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1, ¶¶ 8-13(A), 8-17, and 8-18. See also Boardwalk Glenville
Apts. v. Cage , No. 2007 -CVG-3065 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 13, 2007); Chancellor Manor v. Thibodeaux, 628 N.W.2d 193 (Minn. App. 2001).

Second, the landlord may terminate the tenancy or the rent subsidy in these circumstances only if the landlord has complied with the recertification notice requirements (relative to content and service). If the landlord fai ls to comply with those requirements, the termination of the rent subsidy and the rent increase to market rent is invalid and unenforceable. Hidden Meadows Townhomes v. Ross , No. C - 120045, 2012 WL 6674412 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 21, 2012);Independent Mgmt.
Servs. of Ohio, Inc. v. Davis, No. 2014-CVG-01244 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 18, 2014).

See also Somerset Homes v. Woodard , 2014 WL 2574037 (N.J. Super. , App. Div.,
2014) (eviction action); Lower E. Side I Assocs. LLC v. Estevez , 9 Misc.3d 632, 787 N.Y.S.2d 636 (Civ. Ct. 2004) (money claim for market rent).

When the market rent is invalid and unenforceable for these or other reasons, the landlord is then barred from evicting the tenant for nonpayment of the market rent.

Hidden Meadows Townhomes v. Ross, No. C-120045, 2012 WL 6674412 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 21, 2012) (improper notices);Independent Mgmt. Servs. of Ohio, Inc. v.

Davis, No. 2014-CVG-01244 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 18, 2014) (improper notices);

Boardwalk Glenville Apts. v. Cage , No. 2007-CVG-3065 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., July 13, 2007) (absence of good cause). See also Somerset Homes v.

Woodard, 2014 WL 2574037 (N.J. Super., App. Div., 2014) (improper notices). When an assisted housing landlord brings an eviction action for nonpayment of the market rent, the tenant has a right to challenge in the eviction action the basis for the landlord’s decision to raise the rent to the market rent. Id.

Third, when a tenant does not report income because the tenant is unaware of its 235 existence, the tenant has not breached his recertification reporting requirements and is not liable for any rent increase based on the unknown income. Ezzard Charles Apts.
v. Collins , No. 12 -CV-22452 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Dec. 5, 2013). In these circumstances, since any increase in the tenant’s rent based on the unknown income is invalid, the landlord may not evict the tenant for nonpayment of the increased rent.Id.

##### b. Housing Choice Voucher Program

In the Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”), if a family fails to furnish (or furnishes inaccurate) information at a recertification, the housing authority is permitted, in some circumstances, to terminate the family’s participation in the HCVP and thereby terminate the family’s HCVP lease.

One of the HCVP family obligations is to “supply any information requested by the PHA or HUD for use in a regularly scheduled reexamination or interim reexamination of family in come or composition in accordance with HUD requirements.” 24 C.F.R. § 982.551(b)(2). A violation of the HCVP family obligations is grounds for terminating the family’s HCVP participation. 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.552(c)(1)(i). If the family’s HCVP participationis terminated, the family’s lease terminates automatically. HUD Tenancy Addendum, form HUD -52641-A (July 2019), ¶ 13.

However, if the family’s failure to report full and accurate information is an unintentional error, and not fraud, it is not grounds for terminating the family’s HCVP
participation. McClarty v. Greene Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 2011 -CA-30, 2011 WL 3890295 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 2, 2011); Smith v. Hamilton County, Ohio, No.

C-060315, 2007 WL 1095680 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Apr. 13, 2007). See also Sweeney
v. Hous. Auth. of Chester County, 215 B.R. 97 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 1997);Ellis v. Ritchie, 803 F. Supp. 1097 (E.D. Va. 1992).

#### 5. Unauthorized Occupant/Authorized Guest

Assisted housing tenants have a right to provide their guests with reaso nable
accommodations. E.g., 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(d)(1). However, they may not permit a person to become an occupant of the premises without management authorization.E.g., subsidized housing lease,204 ¶ 13.

The lease identifies the permissible occupants of the premises. Subsidized housing 204 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

lease, ¶ 13 (project -based subsidized housing); 24 C.F.R. § 982.551(h) (HCVP); 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(d)(1) (Public Housing).

If an assisted housing tenant permits a person to become an unauthorized occupant of the premises, it is good cause to terminate the tenancy. E.g., Streetsboro Green
Apts. v. Rundo , No. 90 -P-2145, 1990 WL 174140 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Nov. 9, 1990).

However, HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV -1,205 ¶ 8 -13(A)(4) categorizes an unauthorized occupant violation as a min or violation of the lease. Therefore, at least for project-based subsidized housing, an unauthorized occupant is grounds to terminate a tenancy only if it is a repeated violation (see Section III.G.12) and it adversely affects the development under one (o r more) of the factors specified in 24 C.F.R.

§ 247.3(c)(2).

For a discussion on guest issues and the related law (including law that applies only to assisted housing), see Section VII.G, below. Those issues concern registration and/or prior approval po licies for overnights guests or short -term visitors, the matter of when a short -term visitor becomes an unauthorized occupant, a tenant’s right to invite guests, and the interplay between that right and a landlord’s ban of certain persons.

#### 6. Non-Criminal Conduct of Tenant’s Child

“It is understandable that there is some level of noise coming from the normal day to day activity of a household with two small children.” New Longwood Assocs. v.

Perry, No. 09-CVG-12567, slip op. at 11 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 22, 2010). The noise associated with a tenant’s child is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy if the noise is “the normal, daily noises associated with children and the by-product of living with them and raising them.” Fairborn Apts. v.

Walker, No. 91 -CVG-356 (Ohio Mun., Fairborn, July 24, 1991). Accord New Longwood Assocs. v. Perry, No. 09-CVG-12567 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Apr. 22, 2010); Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Scott , No. 92 -CVG-76 (Ohio Mun., Fremont, May 6, 1992).

In some circumstances, the improper conduct of a tenant’s child is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy if: (1) it is the first instance of such conduct,
(2) the tenant had no knowledge of the child’s conduct, and/or (3) the tenant could not 205 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

237 reasonably have foreseen or prevented the child’s conduct.

This standard has been applied when, based on the child’s improper conduct, the landlord alleges a violation of a lease provision under which the tenant agrees not to permit certain conduct. 206 E.g., Mid-Northern Mgmt., Inc. v. Heinzeroth , 234 Ill.

App.3d 240, 599 N.E.2d 568 (1992) (“not to make or permit noises or a cts that will disturb the rights or comfort of neighbors,” subsidized housing lease,207 ¶ 13(e)). Cf.

Investors Diversified Prop. Mgmt., Inc. v. Brown , No. 87 -360-II, 1988 WL 102781
(Tenn. App. Oct. 7, 1988) (applies this standard where an eleven-year-old son violated a lease provision barring violent acts by any household member).

The improper or unlawful conduct of a tenant’s non -household member child is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy if the conduct occurs when the child is not a guest. E.g., Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Marshall , No. 95 -CVG-0676 (Ohio Mun., Canton, May 15, 1995). See also Hous. Auth. of City of Jersey City v.

Thomas, 318 N.J. Super. 191, 723 A.2d 119 (1999);Charlotte Hous. Auth. v. Fleming, 123 N.C. App. 511, 4 73 S.E.2d 373 (1996); Edwards v. Christian, 61 A.D.2d 1045, 403 N.Y.S.2d 119 (1978). For a discussion on conduct of uninvited guests generally, see Section III.G.10., below.

The improper or unlawful conduct of a tenant’s non-household member child may be good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy if the conduct occurs when the child is a guest. Chavez v. Hous. Auth. of City of El Paso , 973 F.2d 1245 (5th Cir.

1992).

#### 7. Non-Criminal Conduct of Guest

The improper conduct of a guest may be good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy. E.g., Agler Green Coop. v. Rivers, No. 87AP-915, 1988 WL 37063 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 29, 1988). See also Chavez v. Hous. Auth. of City of El Paso, 973 F.2d 1245 (5th Cir. 1992).

However, in some circumstances, the conduct of a guest is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy if: (1) the tenant neither knows nor has reason to know that the guest is inclined to engage in improper conduct, (2) the tenant had no knowledge of the guest’s improper conduct, and/or (3) the tenant could not reasonably 206 For cases applying a similar standard relative to the conduct of the tenant’s guest, see Section III.G.7, below.

207 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

have foreseen or prevented the guest’s improper conduct.

This standard has been applied when, based on the guest’s improper conduct, the landlord alleges a violation of a lease provision under which the tenant agrees not to permit certain conduct. 208 E.g., Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Harris , No. 92 -CVG- 3133 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, May 12, 1993) (“refrain from and cause other persons who are on the premises with the tenant’s consent to refrai n from illegal or other activity which impairs the physical or social environment of the development,” housing authority lease). See also Chicago Hous. Auth. v. Rose, 203 Ill. App.3d 208, 560 N.E.2d 1131 (1990) (not to permit guns in the rental unit); Henry v. Wild Pines Apts., 183 Ga. App. 491, 359 S.E.2d 237 (1987) (“not to make or permit noises or acts that will disturb the rights or comfort of neighbors” and “not to engage in or permit unlawful activities in the unit, in the common areas, or on the pro ject grounds,” subsidized housing lease,209 ¶¶ 13(c),(e)); Peabody Props., Inc. v. Donnelly, No. 18066
(Mass. Super., Bristol Cty., Apr. 26, 1985), aff’d without opinion, 22 Mass. App. Ct.

1101, 490 N.E.2d 817 (1986) (“not to make or permit noises or acts t hat will disturb the rights or comfort of neighbors” and “not to engage in or permit unlawful activities in the unit, in the common areas, or on the project grounds,” subsidized housing lease, ¶¶ 13(c),(e)).

R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) establishes a similar standard for terminating a tenancy based on the improper conduct of a guest, and it may apply to assisted housing tenancies. It requires a tenant to “personally refrain and forbid any other person who is on the premises with his permission from intentionally o r negligently destroying, defacing, damaging, or removing any fixture, appliance, or other part ot the premises.” The R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) standard does not make the tenant strictly liable for a guest’s improper conduct. Allstate Ins. Co. v. Dorsey, 46 Ohio App.3d 66, 545 N.E.2d 920 (9th Dist. 1988). “R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) does not require the tenant to prevent damage to the leased premises, but rather personally to refrain from such activity and to forbid others from engaging in it.” DAK, PLL v. Borgerding, No. 02AP-1051, 2003 WL 21469136, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 26, 2003).

Therefore, a tenant is not liable under R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) for a guest’s improper 208 For cases applying a similar standard relative to the conduct of the tenant’s child, see Section III.G.6, above.

209 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

239 conduct: (1) if the tenant was not in the rental unit when the guest engaged in the conduct, Uptown Cmty. Partners, L.P. v. Killings , No. C-110012, 2011 WL 5438652 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Nov. 10, 2011); (2) if the tenant was unaware of the conduct, Allstate Ins. Co. v. Dorsey , 46 Ohio App.3d at 67 -68; Ohio Cas. Ins. Co. v. Wills , 29 Ohio App.3d 219, 504 N.E.2d 1164 (3rd Dist. 1985); (3) if, upon observing the conduct, the tenant verbally commands the guest to discontinue it, Wills, 29 Ohio App.3d at 221; or (4) if the damage to the leased premises is the result of the guest’s assault of the tenant, DAK, PLL v. Borgerding, 2003 WL 21469136, ¶ 18. See Section VII.D.3, below.

R.C. 5321.06 bars any lease provision that is inconsistent with the R.C.

5321.05(A)(6) standard. Allstate Ins. Co. v. Dorsey, 46 Ohio App.3d 66, 545 N.E.2d 920 (9th Dist. 1988).

Therefore, an assisted housing lease is subject to the R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) standard.

This means that, with respect to the R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) requirements (and any similar assisted housing lease provisions), a guest’s conduct may not constitute a violation of the lease (or good cause to terminate the lease) if it does not constitute a violation of R.C. 5321.05(A)(6).

Any conflict between the R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) standard and an assisted housing lease provision that federal law requires may raise a federal pree mption issue. 210 However, because “there is no federal law that requires that a tenant be held liable for the negligence of others,” federal law does not preempt R.C. 5321.05(A)(6). Uptown
Cmty. Partners, L.P. v. Killings , No. C-110012, 2011 WL 5438652, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Nov. 10, 2011). Cf. Hous. Auth. of City of Everett v. Terry , 114 Wash.2d 558, 566, 789 P.2d 745 (1990) (Public Housing notice of termination rules did not preempt state landlord-tenant law on ten-day notice and opportunity to cure).

210 Federal law may preempt state law when the state law actually conflicts with the federal law.

Hillsborough County, Fla. v. Automated Med. Labs., Inc. , 471 U.S. 707, 713 (1985). “Such conflict occurs when compliance with both federal and state law is impossible, …or when the state law ‘stands as an obstacle to the accomplishment and execution of the full purposes and objectives of Congress.” Hillman v. Maretta, 569 U.S. 483, 490 (2013).

#### 8. Criminal Conduct of Tenant, Household Member,

or Guest

##### a. HUD Regulations

HUD regulations specify the criminal activities that are grounds for terminating an assisted housing tenancy. With one exception, HUD regulations provide that any termination of an assisted housing tenancy based on these criminal activities is within the discretion of the landlord. The one exception is that, if the tenant or a household member is convicted for manufacture of methamphetamine on the premises of assisted housing, termination of the assisted housing tenancy is mandatory.

A description of each of the specified criminal activities is set forth below, along with a statement as to whether it applies to the conduct of the tenant, household members, or guests, 211 the assisted housing program(s) to which it applies, and the relevant HUD regulation(s).
(1) Conviction for manufacture of methamphetamine on the premises of assisted housing (tenant or household member).

Public Housing: 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(5)(i)(A).
(2) Currently engaging in illegal use of a drug (tenant or household member).

Public Housing: 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(5)(i)(B).

Project-based subsidized housing: 24 C.F.R. § 5.858.

HCVP: 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(c)(1).
(3) A pattern of illegal use of a drug tha t interferes with the health, safety, or right to peaceful enjoyment of premises of other residents (tenant or household member).

Public Housing: 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(5)(i)(B).

Project-based subsidized housing: 24 C.F.R. § 5.858.

HCVP: 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(c)(1).
(4) Drug-related criminal activity engaged in on or off the premises (tenant, 211 It does not violate substantive due process to evict an entire household for the criminal conduct of a household member or a guest. Dep’t of Hous. & Urban Dev. v. Rucker, 535 U.S.

125 (2002). See also Chavez v. Hous. Auth. of City of El Paso, 973 F.2d 1245 (5th Cir. 1992);

Spence v. Gormley, 387 Mass. 258, 439 N.E.2d 741 (1982).

241 household member, or guest).

Public Housing: 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(5)(i)(B).
(5) Drug-related criminal activity engaged in on or near the premises (tenant, household member, or guest).

Project-based subsidized housing: 24 C.F.R. §§ 5.858, 247.3(a)(3), 880.607(b)(1)(iii).

HCVP: 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(c)(1).
(6) Violent criminal activity engaged in on or near the premises (tenant, household member, or guest).

HCVP: 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(c)(2)(i)(C).
(7) Criminal activity that threatens the health, safety, or right to peaceful enjoyment of premises of other residents (tenant, household member, or guest).

Public Housing: 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(5)(ii)(A).

Project-based subsidized housing: 24 C.F.R. §§ 5.859(a), 247.3(a)(3), 880.607(b)(1)(iii).

HCVP: 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(c)(2)(i).
(8) Fleeing to avoid prosecution, or custody or confinement after conviction, for a felony (tenant).

Public Housing: 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(5)(ii)(B).

Project-based subsidized housing: 24 C.F.R. § 5.859(b)(1).

HCVP: 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(c)(2)(ii)(A).
(9) Violating a condition of probation or parole (tenant).

Public Housing: 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(5)(ii)(B).

Project-based subsidized housing: 24 C.F.R. § 5.859(b)(2).

HCVP: 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(c)(2)(ii)(B).

##### b. On or Near the Premises

Among the categories of criminal activity that are described in Section III.G.8.a, above, two establish a standard under which the criminal activity must occur on or near the premises. One is the standard for drug related criminal activity relative to a project- based subsidized housing tenancy or a HCVP tenancy. See Section III.G.8.a.(5),
above. The second is the standard for violent criminal activity relative to a HCVP
tenancy. See Section III.G.8.a.(6), above.

HUD regulations do not define the “near the premises” element of the standard.

However, HUD provides some guidance in its preamble:

HUD tracks the statutory standar d, and does not attempt to further define when a crime location is considered ‘near’ the assisted project or building. In general, this standard would cover drug crime in a street or other right of way that adjoins the project or building where a Section 8 unit is located. A landlord -tenant court can apply the statutory standard to the circumstances of a particular case.

60 Fed. Reg. 34660, 34673 (July 3, 1995) (HCVP rule).

For example, criminal activity “in front of the housing building” is near the
premises. Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Wheaton, No. 1998-CA-00128, 1999 WL 34696 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 25, 1999). But criminal activity more than one mile from the premises is not near the premises. Lake Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Journigan , No.

CVG-940539 (Ohio Mun., Painesville, June 21, 1994). Cf. Jones v. Cincinnati Metro.
Hous. Auth. , No. A -0709492 (Ohio C.P., Hamilton Cty., Apr. 15, 2008) (HCVP termination, 2/3 of mile did not meet the “immediate vicinity” test under 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.551(l)).212 The “on the premises” element requires a landlord to prove th at the criminal activity, and not simply the arrest of the guest for the criminal activity, occurred on the
premises. First Merit Venture v. Stringer , No. C-050238 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec.

14, 2005); Warren Heights Apts. v. Bristol, No. 96-CVG-19 (Ohio Mun., Warren, Jan.

10, 1996). See also Detroit Terrace Ltd. v. Bender, No. 11-CVG-19350 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 5, 2011) (a court journal entry proved that the court accepted the tenant’s guilty plea on drug-related criminal activity, but it did not prove that this criminal activity occurred on or near the premises).

##### c. Threat to Health, Safety, or Peaceful Enjoyment of

Premises Among the categories of criminal activity that are described in Section III.G.8.a, above, two establish a standard under which the criminal activity must threaten the 212 Likewise, in Powell v. Hous. Auth. of City of Pittsburgh, 760 A.2d 473 (Pa. Commw. 2000), rev’d on othergrounds, 812 A.2d 1201 (Pa. 2002), the court held that .8 miles did not meet the § 982.551(l) “immediate vicinity” test in this HCVP termination case.

243 health, safety, or peaceful enjoyment of the premises of other residents. One is the standard for a pattern of illegal use of a drug. See Section III.G.8.a.(3), above. The second is the standard for criminal activity that is not otherwise specified in the HUD
regulations. See Section III.G.8.a.(7), above. The second does not require multiple criminal activities by a tenant or household member. Portage Metro Hous. Auth. v.

Brumley, No. 2008-P-0019, 2008 WL 4693200, ¶ 91 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 24, 2008).

Three Ohio court decisions that found that the requisite threat to other residents was not met are summarized below.
(1) Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Wheeler , No. 16973, 1995 WL 338538 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 7, 1995) (tenant’s son threatened and assaulted a housing authority officer on housing authority property and resisted arrest, but no evidence that this conduct occurred in proximity to other residents or disturbed other residents’ peaceful en joyment of their homes or impaired the physical or social environment of the development).
(2) Heritage Place Apts. v. Ely, No. 08-CVG-791 (Ohio Mun., Steubenville,
Aug. 29, 2008) (baby-sitter, with whom tenant had dating relationship, placed tenant’s child in to scalding water, causing severe burns; the crime – child endangerment – was directed solely toward the minor child; the tenant was not present and had no control over the babysitter’s criminal activity).
(3) Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jones , No. 94 -CVG-10415 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Jan. 19, 1995) (altercation and arrest in ball field across the street from the housing development, but no evidence that it was viewed by anyone at the development or in any way affected the development).

##### d. Innocent Tenant Issue, Public Housing

The term “innocent tenant” is used in cases in which the termination of an assisted housing tenancy is based on the criminal activity (usually, drug -related criminal
activity) of a household member or a guest. In this context, innocent tenant me ans a tenant who did not participate in, and neither knew nor reasonably should have known of, the criminal activity.

The U.S. Supreme Court has ruled that 42 U.S.C. §1437d(l)(6) does not bar a housing authority from terminating the Public Housing tenancy of an innocent tenant, even when the criminal activity occurred outside the rental unit. Dep’t of Hous. & Urban Dev. v. Rucker , 535 U.S. 125 (2002). However, it also held that a housing authority has discretionary authority not to terminate the tenancy of an innocent tenant.213 Id.

HUD provided housing authorities with two guidance letters relative to theRucker
decision. Letter from Mel Martinez, Secretary, U.S. Dep’t of Hous. & Urban Dev., to Public Housing Directors (Apr. 16, 2002); Letter from Mich ael M. Liu, Ass’t Secretary, U.S. Dep’t of Hous. & Urban Dev., to Public Housing Directors (June 6, 2002).

In his April 16 letter, Secretary Martinez urged housing authorities “to be guided by compassion and common sense in responding to cases involving illegal use of drugs.” He stated that “[e]viction should be the last option explored, after all others have been exhausted.” In his June 6 letter, Assistant Secretary Liu noted that “theRucker decision upholds HUD regulations that, since 1999, have made it clear that ... PHAs are not required to evict an entire household - or, for that matter, anyone, - every time a violation of the lease clause occurs.” He urged the housing authorities to consider and balance a variety 213 A related issue is whether 42 U.S.C. § 1437d(l)(6) or 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(5)(i) requires a housing authority to consider mitigating circumstances, inclu ding those listed in § 966.4(l)(5)(i), in determining whether to terminate a tenancy for drug -related criminal
activity. The case law on this issue is split.

Court decisions holding that it is required include: E. Carolina Reg’l Hous. Auth. v. Lofton, 369 N.C. 8, 789 S.E.2d 449 (2016); Carter v. Lynn Hous. Auth. , 450 Mass. 626, 880 N.E.2d 778 (2008); Jersey City Hous. Auth. v. Ford , No. A -5657-08T3, 2010 WL 5395806 (N.J. Super.
Dec. 28, 2010); Hassan v. Dakota County Cmty. Dev. Agency, No. A08-0373, 2009 WL 437775
(Minn. App. Feb. 24, 2009); Hicks v. Dakota County Cmty. Dev. Agency, No. A06-1302, 2007 WL 2416872 (Minn. App. Aug. 28, 2007); Gaston v. CHAC, Inc. , 375 Ill. App.3d 16, 872 N.E.2d 38 (2007); Oakwood Plaza Apts. v. Smith , 352 N.J. Super. 467, 800 A.2d 265 (2002);

Housing Auth. for Prince George’s County v. Williams , 784 A.2d 621 (Md. App. 2001);

Allegheny County Hous. Auth. v. Hibbler , 748 A.2d 786 (Pa. Commw. 2000); Brooks v. Lee,
No. 96-CS-02424 (Cal. Super., Sacramento Cty., Jan. 12, 1999); Lucas Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Correa, No. CVG-0501010 (Ohio Mun., Sylvania, Nov. 9, 2005); City of Jacksonville v. Byrd,
No. 95-0025-AP, 4 Fla. L. Weekly 497a (Cir. Ct. Nov. 21, 1995).

Court decisions holding that it is not required include : Stevens v. Hous. Aut h. of S. Bend , 720

### F. Supp.2d 1013 (N.D. Ind. 2010), aff’d on other grounds , 663 F.3d 300 (7th Cir. 2011);

Robinson v. Dist. of Columbia Hous. Auth. , 606 F. Supp.2d 6 (D.D.C. 2009); Peterson v.

Washington County Hous. & Redevelopment Auth. , No. A10-2053, 2011 WL 3557818 (Minn.
App. Aug. 15, 2011); Syracuse Hous. Auth. v. Boule , 265 A.D.2d 832, 701 N.Y.S.2d 541 (1999); Allegheny County Hous. Auth. v. Liddell , 722 A.2d 750 (Pa. Commw. 1998); Hous.
Auth. of City of York v. Dickerson, 715 A.2d 525 (Pa. Commw. 1998).

245 of relevant factors in d eciding whether to evict the entire household of an innocent tenant.

Notwithstanding the Rucker decision, Ohio law recognizes an equity defense in eviction actions (see Section II.BB, above), which innocent tenants of Public Housing have used successfully, before and after the Rucker decision.

Those pre-Rucker decisions include: Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Harris, Nos.

C-820540,820541, 1983 WL 8893 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 15, 1983) (minor children of tenant engaged in certain criminal conduct); Trumbull Metro. Hous. Auth.
v. Edmonds , No. 00 -CVG-0030801 (Ohio Mun., Warren, Aug. 10, 2000) (when babysitting, brother of tenant sold drugs in the rental unit; tenant did not participate in or know of the drug activity); Trumbull Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Redd, No. 00-CVG-614 (Ohio Mun., Warren, July 19, 2000) (guest of tenant sold drugs in the rental unit; tenant did not participate in or know of the drug activity); Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Foster, No. 97 -CV-06298 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Nov. 18, 1997) (tenant’s son engaged in one drug sale; tenant neither knew nor had any reason to know it wou ld occur). Cf. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Rice, No. 88-CV-04013 (Ohio Mun., Akron, June 22, 1988) (conviction and incarceration of tenant for stabbing a person was not just cause to evict tenant’s wife and children).

Those post-Rucker decisions include: CMHA v. Ramsey , No. 2018 -CVG-01334 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 12, 2018) ( tenant believed her daughter had overcome her drug problem at a drug rehab facility; she picked her up there after a six- month stay, took her to the rental unit for a short visit, after a few hours left her alone for ten minutes, during which she had a fatal drug overdose); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Mundy, No. 2007-CVG-25841 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 17,
2007) (remains of guest’s handrolled cigarette contained marijuana and tenant did not know or have any reason to know that the cigarette contained marijuana); CMHA v.

Taylor, No. 2007-CVG-3787 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 5, 2007) (two percoset pills and drug paraphernalia in guest’s pocket, and tenant did not know or have any reason to know of the content of the guest’s pocket);Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Harris, 139 Ohio Misc.2d 96, 861 N.E.2d 179 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., 2006) (guest arrested on federal warrant, crack cocaine in guest’s pocket, and tenant did not know or have any reason to know of the content of the guest’s pocket).

In addition, three post-Rucker Ohio appellate court decisions address the authority of a trial court to apply the equity defense in 42 U.S.C. § 14 37d(l)(6) eviction actions against innocent tenants.

In Portage Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Brumley, No. 2008-P-0019, 2008 WL 4693200, ¶¶ 93-97 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 24, 2008), the court held that a trial court may apply the equity defense in such cases . The trial court had considered the equity defense and had denied it based on the totality of the relevant circumstances. The Court of Appeals affirmed the trial court’s decision on the ground that the trial court had not abused its discretion.

In Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Kilgore , 194 Ohio App.3d 767, 958 N.E.2d 187 (2nd Dist. 2011) (2-1 decision), the court held that the trial court erred on two grounds in sustaining the innocent tenant equity defense. First, it held that equity does not apply to “positive statutes” and, according to the Court, 42 U.S.C. §1437d(l)(6) is a “positive statute.” Second, it found and relied on a distinction between Ms. Kilgore’s case and Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Harris, 139 Ohio Misc.2d 96, 861 N.E.2d 179 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., 2006). Unlike the Harris tenant, Ms. Kilgore was not an innocent tenant because she had “furthered her guests’ criminal purposes” by leaving them alone in her apartment and thereby “making her apartment open and available” for them to engage in the illegal drug activity. Kilgore, 194 Ohio App.3d 767, ¶ 29. The court did not address whether 42 U.S.C. § 1437d(l)(6) preempts the equity jurisdiction of a trial court in eviction actions. And the court did not reject the Harris holding.

In Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Davis, 197 Ohio App.3d 411, 967 N.E.2d 1244 (8th Dist. 2011), the court held (at least implicitly) that a trial court may apply the equity defense in such cases. The court: (1) briefly reviewed Harris and Kilgore, (2) noted that Kilgore distinguished Harris on the facts, and (3) thereupon ruled “[w]e find the facts in the present case more akin to Kilgore than Harris” (id., ¶ 33). The court, reviewing the facts under the competent, credible evidence standard, found sufficient evidence to support the trial court’s finding that the tenant was not an innocent tenant and, thereupon, upheld the trial court’s denial of the innocent tenant equity defense.

Only one Ohio court has addressed whether the federal law on innocenttenants, as construed in Rucker, preempts214 the equity jurisdiction of Ohio municipal courts in eviction actions. In Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Harris , 139 Ohio Misc.2d 96, 861 N.E.2d 179 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., 2006), the court held that the federal law does not preempt its equity jurisdiction in innocent tenant cases. It relied 214 See note 210, above.

247 on a HUD guidance that explained that the “rule does not ... preempt State law.” Harris, 139 Ohio Misc. 2d at 99, quoting 66 Fed. Reg. 28776, 28791 (2001) (rule on eviction for criminal activity). It thereupon held that “ Rucker does not alter this conclusion [of HUD] and does not provide a basis for preemption or limiting this court’s equity powers.”Id. Accord Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Mundy, No. 2007- CVG-25841 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 17, 2007). See also Chateau Foghorn LP v. Hosford, 455 Md. 462, 168 A.2d 824 (2017) (42 U.S.C. § 1437d(l)(6) does not preempt the state law requirement that, for an eviction, the tenant’s breach of the lease must be substantial and warrant eviction); Hous. Auth. of Covington v. Turner, 295 S.W.3d 1123 (Ky. App. 2009) (42 U.S.C. §1437d(l)(6) does not preempt the state law right to cure); Newport Hous. Auth. v. Reynolds, No. ND2002-0290 (R.I. Super., Newport Cty., Aug. 26, 2002) (“ Rucker ... has not preempted state law on this subject”); Maryland Park Apts. v. Robinson , No. CX -02-4044 (Minn. Dist. Ct., Ramsey Cty., June 17, 2002) (“ Rucker do[es] not preempt ... applicable Minnesota eviction laws [which bar eviction of an innocent tenant]”).Cf. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Hairston, 124 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 790 N.E.2d 828 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., 2003) (Ohio law on waiver of a breach– applied here to alleged criminal conduct
– is consistent with federal law that gives a PHA discretion in deciding whether to terminate the tenancy for the criminal conduct).215 But cf. Milwaukee City Hous. Auth.
v. Cobb, 361 Wis.2d 359, 860 N.W.2d 267 (2015) (42 U.S.C. § 1437d(l)(6) preempts the state statutory right to cure); Boston Hous. Auth. v. Garcia , 449 Mass. 727, 871 N.E.2d 1073 (2007) (42 U.S.C. § 1437d(l)(6) preempts the state statutory innocent tenant defense); Scarborough v. Winn Residential L.L.P. , 890 A.2d 249 (D.C. App.
2006) (42 U.S.C. § 1437d(l)(6) preempts the state statutory right to cure).

The Rucker decision did not address the preemption issue. For further discussion of the preemption issue, se e Lawrence R.
