McDonough & Mac McCreight, Wait a Minute: Slowing Down Criminal -Activity Eviction Cases to Find the Truth , 41 Clearinghouse Rev. 55, 76-77 (2007). See also Hous. Auth. of City of Everett v. Terry, 114 Wash.2d 558, 566, 789 P.2d 745 (1990) ( in establishing rules for terminating a tenancy but leaving any eviction action to the available state court proceedings, Congress and HUD opted to rely on the state’s procedural and substantive eviction laws).

215 For a discussion on Ohio law on waiver of a breach of a lease, see Section II.E, above.

In summary, two post-Rucker appellate court decisions (Davis and Brumley), along with Harris and other trial court decisions, establish that Ohio trial courts have equity jurisdiction to consider and, where appropriate, apply the innocent tenant equity
defense. Insofar as Kilgore relied on the positive statute theory, it remains an isolated view, inconsistent with well-established Ohio eviction law.

##### e. Innocent Tenant Issue, Project -Based Subsidized

Housing The term “innocent tenant” is defined in the first paragraph of Section III.G.8.d, above.

Some courts have held that, in certain circumstances, landlords of project -based subsidized housing may not evict an innocent tenant. These holdings have relied on one of two grounds.

One ground is based on the subsidized housing lease, 216 ¶ 13(c), which provide s that the tenant shall not engage in or permit certain unlawful activity. In eviction actions based on ¶ 13(c) violations, courts have held that the innocent tenant did not engage in or permit the unlawful activity and, therefore, did not violate ¶ 13(c).217 E.g., Owners Mgmt. Co. v. Moore, 111 Ohio App.3d 820, 677 N.E.2d 400 (6th Dist. 1996);

Schroeder Co. v. Oviedo , No. CVG-93-01124 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., Feb.

11, 1993). See also Kimball Hill Mgmt. Co. v. Roper, 314 Ill. App.3d 975, 733 N.E.2d 458 (2000); Henry v. Wild Pines Apts., 183 Ga. App. 491, 359 S.E.2d 237 (1987); Cf.
Hous. Opportunities Comm’n of Montgomery County v. Lacey, 322 Md. 56, 585 A.2d 219 (1991) (Public Housing lease provision); Hodess v. Bonefont, 401 Mass. 693, 519 N.E.2d 258 (1988) (state assisted housing lease provision).

A second ground is based on the material noncompliance and “other good cause” provisions of the subsidized housing lease,218 ¶ 23. Courts have held that, as a matter of contract law, these provisions prec lude the eviction of an innocent tenant. E.g., Kimball Hill Mgmt. Co. v. Roper , 314 Ill. App.3d 975, 733 N.E.2d 458 (2000);

Diversified Realty Group, Inc. v. Davis, 257 Ill. App.3d 417, 628 N.E.2d 1081 (1993).
Cf. Spence v. Gormley , 387 Mass. 258, 439 N.E.2d 741 (1982) (good cause 216 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

217 For analogous case law, relative to non -criminal conduct of a tenant’s child or guest, see Sections III.G.6,7, above.

218 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174 above.

249 requirement under state law, applied in a Public Housing eviction);Boston Hous. Auth.
v. Bell, 428 Mass. 108, 697 N.E.2d 130 (1998) (follows Spence).

In addition to these lease grounds, Ohio law recognizes an equity defense in eviction actions (see Section II.BB, above), which innocent tenants of assisted housing have used successfully. See Section III.G.8.d, above.

##### f. Drug-Related Criminal Activity

A category of criminal activity that is referenced in Sections III.G.8.a.(4) ,(5), above, is “drug-related criminal activity.” For these purposes, “Drug-related criminal activitymeans the illegal manufacture, sale, distribution, or use of a dru g, or the possession of a drug with intent to manufacture, sell, distribute or use the drug.” 24 C.F.R. § 5.100.

Four Ohio court decisions, in concluding that the tenant did not violate the prohibition against drug -related criminal activity, provide hel pful guidance on this prohibition.

One Ohio court held that, if an assisted housing tenant is convicted for possession of marijuana under a criminal law that does not explicitly include the element of intent to use, it is not “drug-related criminal activity,” which means that it is not grounds to terminate the tenancy. Lucas Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Correa, No. CVG-0501010 (Ohio Mun., Sylvania, Nov. 9, 2005).

A second Ohio court held that the tenant’s alleged conduct – overdosing on an unknown drug – is not criminal conduct and thus not drug -related criminal activity, and thereupon entered the eviction judgment for the tenant. Stark Metro. Hous. Auth.
v. Minocchi, No. 2021-CVG-3485 (Ohio Mun., Stark Cty., Aug. 26, 2021).

A third Ohio court, in a case in which it found that the juveniles had smoked marijuana during the course of the evening, held: “The Court believes that law requires proof by a preponderance of the evidence that drugs (marijuana) were located or used in the apartment or found on the juveniles’ persons.” PK Mgmt., LLC v. McGee, No.

2014-CVG-282, slip op. at 3 (Ohio County Ct., Jefferson Cty., Dist. No. 1, Jan. 27, 2015). Since the police did not find drugs in the apartment or on the juveniles, the court entered the eviction judgment for the tenant. Id., slip op. at 4.

A fourth Ohio court held that the PHA did not prove the requisite drug -related criminal activity where its only evidence wasthe testimony of a witness that “he could smell marijuana in the hallway and that he sniffed defendant’s doorway.”Stark Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Sanders, No. 2015-CVG-2490, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun.,Massillon, Jan.

22, 2016).

##### g. Misdemeanor

If the criminal activity is a misdemeanor, it may be a minor violation of the lease.

E.g., Denhart v. Whaley, No. 88-CV-16092 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Nov. 2, 1988) (attempted promotion of prostitution). See also Hous. Auth. of Decatur v. Brown, 180
Ga. App. 483, 349S.E.2d 501 (1986) (possession of less than one ounce of marijuana).

A minor violation of an assisted housing lease is not good cause to terminate the lease, unless there are repeated minor violations. See Section III.G.12, below.

##### h. Conduct of Minor, Juvenile Court

The activity of a minor, for which a juvenile court has jurisdiction, may constitute criminal activity for purposes of an assisted housing lease and HUD regulations.

Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Browning , No. C -010055, 2002 WL 63491 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Jan. 18, 2002). See also CMJ Mgmt. Co. v. Wilkerson, 91 Mass. App.
Ct. 276, 75 N.E.3d 605 (2017); Hous. Auth. for Prince George’s County v. Williams, 784 A.2d 621 (Md. App. 2001).

##### i. Violation of Condition of Probation or Parole

A category of criminal activity that is referenced in Section III.G.8.a.(9), above, is “violating a condition of probation or parole.” A tenant’s violation of an Intervention in Lieu of Conviction does not constitute a violation of probation or parole. Retiree Hous. Mg mt. Inc. v. Lynch , No. 12 -CVG- 02402 (Ohio Mun., Miamisburg, July 30, 2013). “Intervention in Lieu of Conviction
(ILC) is a type of diversion, as set forth in the Ohio Revised Code. It is not a criminal conviction, unless and until a defendant fails to complete the Court imposed conditions of ILC.” Id., slip op. at 4.

##### j. Absence of Criminal Conviction

The criminal conduct of a tenant, a household member, or a guest may be good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy, regardless of whether the person has been arrested or convicted for the conduct. 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(l)(5)(iii) (Public Housing); 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(c)(3) (HCVP); 24 C.F.R. § 5.861 (project -based subsidized housing), which applies to criminal conduct under 24 C.F.R. §§ 5.859 and 5.860 and is thereby incorporated into 24 C.F.R. § 247.3(a). See New Longwood
Assocs. v. Perry , No. 09 -CVG-12567 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 22, 251
2010) (subsidized housing lease,219 ¶ 23(c)(6)). Cf. Woodruff Vill. v. Bowers , No. L- 95-248, 1996 WL 339912 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 21, 1996) (criminal conviction not required by the lease);Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Blake, No. 98-CVG-05998 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug. 19, 1999) (criminal conviction not required under former 24 C.F.R. § 966.4).

The eviction action may proceed even if a criminal action is pending and awaiting the commencement of trial. New Longwood Assocs. v. Perry, No. 09-CVG-12567, slip
op. at 10 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 22, 2010). In the eviction action, the court concludes whether the landlord “has established by a preponderance of the evidence that the defendant [tenant] has engaged in criminal activity ..., for the limited purpose of being in breach of the terms of the Lease. This conclusion is not intended to indicate that the defendant has been found guilty of having committed a crime;

clearly in a criminal matter, the law requires the prosecution to prove its case beyond a reasonable doubt. These issues remain to be tried in another case, in another court.” Id., slip op. at 9.

##### k. Self-Defense

In eviction actions for alleged criminal activity, if thetenant’s conduct falls within the privilege of self-defense, the tenant’s conduct does not constitute unlawful activity for the eviction claim. Millennia Hous. Mgmt. Ltd. v. Mitchell, No. 2013-CVG-008410 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 19, 2013).

##### l. Pre-Admission Criminal Conduct

Absent a tenant’s fraud or misrepresentation in the application process, an assisted housing landlord is not permitted to terminate a tenancy based on a tenant’s pre - admission criminal conduct. See Bennington Hous. Aut h. v. Bush , 182 Vt. 133, 933 A.2d 207 (2007); Wellstone Hous. Auth. v. Murphy, 131 S.W.3d 378 (Mo. App. 2004).

See also 66 Fed. Reg. 28776, 28781 (May 24, 2001) (HUD preamble on criminal activity grounds for terminating a tenancy: “Activity occurring only prior to the time the leaseholder signed the lease, or the household member or guest joined the household or became a guest, would not be a basis for termination of tenancy”).

This is true even if the pre -admission criminal conduct constituted a mandatory ground for denying admission (e.g., tenant subject to a lifetime registration requirement 219 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

for a sex offense) and the application approval was the result of a landlord mistake.

E.g., CMHA v. Gauntt , No. 2014 -CVG-001938 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Dec. 4, 2014) (granting tenant’s motion for partial summary judgment); CMHA v.

Stewart, No. 12 -CVG-20068 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 27, 2013);

CMHA v. Stewart , No. 06 -3698 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 1, 2006).

See also Perkins-Bey v. Hous. Auth. of St. Louis County , No. 4:11CV310 JCH, 2011 WL 939292 (E.D. Mo. Mar. 14, 2011); Miller v. McCormick, 605 F. Supp.2d 296 (D.
Me. 2009). Cf. Alicia Holding LLC v. Cutri , No. 08 -CVG-00531 (Ohio Mun., Steubenville, July 24, 2008) (non-elderly person admitted to elderly only project).

##### m. Ohio Case Law Finding Good Cause

Ohio appellate court decisions finding good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy, based on the criminal conduct of the tenant, a household member, or a guest, include those set forth below.
(1) Sandusky Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jackson, No. S -19-046, 2020 WL 6377003 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 30, 2020) (Public Housing, tenant placed on community control for five years and, during that period, tested positive for marijuana on three occasions).
(2) Gorsuch Homes Inc. v. LeMaster , No. 10 -15-18, 2016 WL 3057971 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., May 31, 2016) (project-based subsidized housing, tenant assisted the trespass entry upon the premises by her husband).
(3) Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v . Patterson, No. C -130161, 2013 WL 6409302 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 6, 2013) (Public Housing, guest (tenant’s grandson), on two occasions, engaged in criminal conduct in the immediate vicinity of the premises – first, burglary of a neighbor’s unit; second, upon leaving tenant’s apartment, possession of marijuana in his pocket).
(4) Youngstown Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Scott , No. 99 -CA-238, 2001 WL 744496 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 26, 2001) (Public Housing, guest possessed drugs in the unit, without tenant’s knowledge).
(5) Lucas Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Carmony , No. L -00-1296, 2001 WL 672150 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 15, 2001) (Public Housing, guest possessed drugs in the unit, with tenant’s knowledge).
(6) Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Wheaton , No. 1998-CA-00128, 1999 WL 34696 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 25, 1999) (Public Housing, guest (tenant’s son) engaged in drug activity near the unit).

253
(7) Skyline Terrace Apts. v. Woodson , No. 1996 -CA-00107, 1996 WL 753199 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 9, 1996) (proj ect-based subsidized housing, guest discharged firearm on project property and thereafter hid in the unit, and tenant lied to security officer about guest’s presence in the unit).
(8) Woodruff Vill. v. Bowers, No. L-95-248, 1996 WL 339912 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 21, 1996) (project-based subsidized housing, household member used drugs in the unit).

#### 9. Violence Against Women Act

The federal Violence Against Women Act of 20 13, Pub. L. 113 -4, 127 Stat. 54, together with the federal Violence Against Women Reauthorization Act of 2005, Pub.
L. 109-162, 119 Stat. 2960 (collectively, “VAWA”), provide certain rights to assisted housing tenants who are victims of domestic violence, dating violence, or stalking. 24 C.F.R. §§ 5.2001-5.2011. See also 24 C.F.R. §§ 880.504(f), 880.607(c)(5), 882.407, 882.511(g), 882.514(c), 883.605, 884.216(c), 884.223(f), 886.128, 886.132, 886.328, 886.329(f) (Section 8 project -based subsidized housing); 24 C.F.R. §§ 891.575(f), 891.610(c), 891.630 (Sections 202 and 811 PRAC); 24 C. F.R. §§ 960.103(d), 960.200(b)(8), 960.203(c)(4), 966.4(a)(1)(vi), 966.4(e)(9) (Public Housing); 24 C.F.R.

§§ 982.53(e), 982.201(a), 982.202(d), 982.307(b)(4), 982.310(h)(4), 982.315(a)(2), 982.315(b)(4), 982.353(b), 982.452(b)(1), 982.551(e), 982.551(l), 982.552(c)(2)(v) (Housing Choice Voucher program).

With respect to assisted housing eviction actions, VAWA provides tenants with two general protections. The first is that an incident (or incidents) of actual or threatened domestic violence, dating violence, or stalking is not a serious or repeated lease violation by the victim and is not good cause to terminate the tenancy of the
victim. 24 C.F.R. § 5.2005(c). The second is that criminal activity directly related to domestic violence, dating violence, or stalking, engaged in by a member of a tenant’s household or any guest or other persons under the tenant’s control, shall not be cause for termination of the tenancy of the victim, if the victim is the tenant or an immediate family member of the tenant. 24 C.F.R. § 5.2005(b)(2).

In short, “[if] a tenant violates the terms of the lease and that violation directly resulted from domestic violence, the tenant cannot be evicted from their housing.” Norwich Apts. II v. Ingram , No. L-19-1239, 2020 WL 3032860, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 5, 2020).

Two Ohio court decisions that rely on VAWA in entering the eviction judgment for the tenant are: Norwich Apts. II v. Ingram , No. L -19-1239, 2020 WL 3032860 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 5, 2020); New Longwood Assocs. v. Wells, No. 09-CVG- 12569 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Sept. 16, 2009).

#### 10. Conduct of Uninvited Person

The improper conduct of an uninvited person, who is neither a household member nor a guest, is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing te nancy, regardless of the person’s relationship with the tenant. Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985); ABC Mgmt. Co. v. Gamble , No. 83AP - 788, 1983 Ohio App. LEXIS 16070 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 15, 1983 ); Shiloh Grove Ltd. P’ship v. Odom, No. M9404-CVG-010436 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., June 2, 1994); Urban Hollow Apts. v. Lewis , No. M9403 -CVG-007533 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 25, 1994); Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Singleton , No. CVG -9412643 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., Nov. 29, 1994); Lakeview Terrace, Etc. v. Overton,
No. 91-CVG-25457 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 18, 1991). See also Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Curtis , No. CA -9462, 1994 WL 45875 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Feb. 14, 1994); Countrybrook Apts. v. Forsythe , No. M86 -CVG-20896 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 28, 1986).

The improper conduct of such persons is not good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy even if the conduct is criminal activity, provided the tenant did not have advance knowledge of or participate in the criminal activity. E.g., Stark Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Dorsey , No. 2001 -CA-00057, 2001 WL 1699 (Ohio App., 5th Dist.,
Aug. 20, 2001) (tenant instructed babysitter not to allow a specified person to enter the unit and sought housing authority’s assistance, but babysitter let the person enter the unit, at which time he smoked marijuana; the barred person was not tenant’s guest or invitee and his conduct did not constitute a violation of the lease); Stark Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Marshall, No. 95-CVG-0676 (Ohio Mun., Canton, May 15, 1995) (tenant did not know of her non-household member son’s criminal plans, and the criminal activity did not occur when the son was the tenant’s guest);Insignia Mgmt. Group v. McCrae,
No. 94-CVG-4696 (Ohio Mun., Stark Cty., Oct. 20, 1994) (tenant not responsible for her non-household member daughter’s drug activity in the unit, where daughter entered the unit without tenant’s knowledge or permission, when tenant was out of town).See also Hous. Auth. of City of Jersey City v. Thomas, 318 N.J. Super. 191, 723 A.2d 119
(1999) (tenant unaware that her non -household member son “had surreptitiously entered the apartment” before his drug arrest; federal statute does not require “eviction 255 of tenant w hose apartment is invaded by a transgressor against his or her will”);

Charlotte Hous. Auth. v. Fleming, 123 N.C. App. 511, 473 S.E.2d 373 (1996) (tenant not responsible for the acts of her non -household member son, who engaged in drug- related criminal activity on project property, but not in tenant’s apartment and not when he was tenant’s guest); Edwards v. Christian , 61 A.D.2d 1045, 403 N.Y.S.2d 119
(1978) (“no showing that the petitioner [tenant] knew of, or in any way condoned, her son’s conduct” or that “the robbery occurred on an occasion when Ronald [son] was visting his mother”). Cf. Tyson v. New York City Hous. Auth. , 369 F. Supp. 513 (S.D.N.Y. 1974) (based on constitutional claims, held tenant not responsible for acts of non-household member son, who engaged in criminal activity on project property, but not in tenant’s apartment and not when he was tenant’s guest).

One Ohio appellate court has held that, when a non-household member is arrested in the rental unit, the burden is on the tenant “to present evidence that the person who was arrested in her apartment was not there with the consent of a household member.” Youngstown Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Scott , No. 99 -CA-238, 2001 WL 744496, at *3 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 26, 2001).

#### 11. Forbearance Agreement to Vacate, Breach

The good cause grounds for terminating an assisted housing tenancy are limited to those set forth in the HUD regulations. Cmty. Props. of Ohio Mgmt. Servs. v. Taylor,
No. 2009-CVG-001131 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 11, 2009).

A tenant’s breach of a forbearance agreement, under which the landlord agrees not to file an eviction action and the tenant agrees to vacate the rental unit by a specified date, is not one of the permissible grounds for terminating an assisted housing tenancy under the HUD regulations. Id. Therefore, it is not a good cause ground to terminate an assisted housing tenancy. Id. See also Dakota County HRA v. Blackwell , 602 N.W.2d 243 (Minn. 1999). Cf. Hous. Auth. of Portland v. Asana, 165 Or. App. 531, 1 P.3d 1025 (2000) (stipulated judgment from earlier eviction for nonpayment of rent;

tenant complied with rent payment requirement, but allegedly breached requirement to work with intervention specialist; the breach is subject to the federal st atute’s good cause requirement; reversal and remand to consider whether the breach constituted good cause).

Because HUD approval is required for any lease amendment, the forbearance agreement may not be construed as an amendment to the lease, absent HUD approval.
Cmty. Props. of Ohio Mgmt. Servs. v. Taylor , No. 2009 -CVG-001131 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 11, 2009). Therefore, absent HUD approval, the breach of the forbearance agreement is not grounds to terminate the lease as a lease violation. Id.

In these circumstances, the landlord’s options are: (1) to bring an eviction action for the underlying breach of the rental agreement that prompted the forbearance agreement, or (2) to bring an action to enforce the forbearance agreement. Id.

Also, when an agreed judgment entry (in contrast to a forbearance agreement) requires a tenant to vacate the rental unit if certain conditions occur, the court may enforce its entry. Id.

#### 12. Minor Violation of Lease

One minor violation of a lease is not good cause to termi nate an assisted housing
tenancy. 24 C.F.R. §§ 247.3(c)(2), 880.607(b)(3)(ii), 966.4(l)(2)(i), 982.310(a)(1).

Generally, repeated minor violations of a lease are good cause to terminate an assisted housing tenancy. Id. However, for project-based subsidized housing, repeated minor violations of the lease are good cause to terminate the tenancy only if they :
(1) disrupt the livability of the project, (2) adversely affect the health or safety of another person, (3) adversely affect another tenant’s right tothe quiet enjoyment of the premises, (4) interfere with management of the project, or (5) have an adverse financial effect on the project. 24 C.F.R. §§ 247.3(c), 880.607(b)(3).

Repeated violations, for these purposes, means: more than one violation, Label &
Co. v. Taylor , No. 86 -C-26, 1987 WL 12887 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 17, 1987) (two violations constitute d repeated violations) ; and may requires a pattern of violations, not isolated incidents, see Mid-Northern Mgmt., Inc. v. Heinzeroth, 234 Ill.

App.3d 240, 599 N.E.2d 568 (1992); N. Shore Plaza Assocs. v. Guida , 117 Misc.2d 778, 459 N.Y.S.2d 685 (Civ. Ct. 1983).

HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV -1,220 ¶ 8-13(A)(4) sets forth examples of minor violations of an assisted housing lease. They are: “Tenant keeps una uthorized occupants[;] Tenant fails to pay utilities[;] Tenant behaves or acts in a manner that continually disrupts the right of other residents to enjoy the property[;] Tenant damages, destroys, or defaces the unit or property[;] Tenant fails to pay the cost of all repairs caused by carelessness or neglect on the part of the tenant.” Ohio court decisions provide additional examples of minor violations of an assisted housing lease. The decisions (and examples) include those set forth below.

220 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

257
(1) Conviction for misdemeanor. Denhart v. Whaley , No. 88 -CV-16092 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Nov. 2, 1988) (attempted promotion of prostitution).
(2) Poor housekeeping . Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Williams , No. 88 - CVG-1268 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, July 8, 1988).
(3) Loud noise. Label & Co. v. Taylor, No. 86-C-26, 1987 WL 12887 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 17, 1987).
(4) Child involved in one fight. Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Westfall, No.

79-CVG-7932 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Oct. 15, 1979).
(5) Late payment of rent. Jenkins v. Rung, No. 93-CVGT-601 (Ohio Mun., Miami Cty., July 5, 1993) (five days late);Columbus Metro. Hous. Auth.
v. Baker , No. 91 -CVG-010490 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 19,
1991) (one day late).
(6) Nonpayment of a small charge . Dayton Inv. Group -7 v. Lathern , No.

15866, 1996 WL 741822 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 13, 1996) ($10, $11, and $25 charges);Jones v. Burns, No. R90-CVG-2776 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Nov. 29, 1990) ($3.00 charge); ABC Mgmt. Co. v.

Williams, No. M86 -CVG-34323 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 19,
1986) ($5 and $10 charges).
(7) Oral notice (instead of written notice) of change in household
composition. Gorsuch Mgmt. v. Shaver , No. 97 -CA-1, 1997 WL 33471179 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 31, 1997).
(8) Water bill not in tenant’s name . Sumac Real Estate Holding, LLC v.

Mitchell, No. 2014-CVG-00670 (Ohio Mun., Euclid, Apr. 18, 2014).

Generally, a conviction for a felony is a substantial, not a minor, violation of the
lease. E.g., Zonner v. Heritage Hills, Ltd., 44 Ohio App.3d 214, 542 N.E.2d 697 (4th
Dist. 1988) (felony, cultivation of marijuana in the rental unit).

#### 13. Other Grounds

Ohio court decisions that address other potential grounds for terminating an assisted housing tenancy are summarized below.

Poor housekeeping:

Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Cox , No. 01 -CV-24085 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., June 4, 2002) Tenant’s repeated failure to maintain the rental unit in compliance with the housing authority’s housekeeping standards was a material breach of the lease and constituted a threat to the health and safety of other tenants.

Court found sufficient cause to evict, and rejected an equity defense, because of tenant’s repeated failures to comply, tenant’s refusal to attend classes offered to improve her housekeeping, and the threat to health and safety.

Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Williams , No. 88 -CVG-1268 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, July 8, 1988) One instance of poor housekeeping was not grounds to evict the tenant because it was neither a serious nor repeated violation of the lease. Poor housekeeping that is not a threat to health or safety is a minor violation of the lease.

Madston Corp. v. Ahrens , No. 87 -CVG-815 (Ohio Mun., Port Clinton,
Feb. 5, 1988) The poor housekeeping of a mentally ill tenant, who had alocal counseling agency assisting him with housekeeping matters and where the poor housekeeping was not a hazard or danger to other tenants, was not good cause to evict the tenant.

Large Appliances Prohibited by Lease:

Taylor v. Marshall Plaza II , No. 88-CA-004372, 1989 WL 10309 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Feb. 8, 1989) The landlord’s rule that banned washers and dryers in the dwelling units was enforceable only if the rule was reasonable. The basis for the reasonableness requirement is the subsidized housing lease,221 ¶ 14(a).

Because there was a genuine issue as to whether the rule was reasonable, the granting of summary judgment was improper.
Mt. Moriah Dev. Corp. v. Smith, No. 83-CV-36104 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Sept. 17, 1984) The tenant’s refusal to remove a compact freezer from her apartment was not a material breach of the rental agreement justifying termination of the
tenancy. Because the lease provision in issue prohibited all freezers, it was overly broad on its face and thus unenforceable. A rat ional rule regulating size or load limit would be enforceable.

221 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

259 Repair of Premises:

Wagar v. Hatnavy, No. 1433, 1985 WL 4734 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec.

26, 1985) Good cause to evict the tenant existed because the repairs of the premises that were necessaryto comply with orders of the county health department required vacation of the premises.

Colonial Am. Dev. v. Morey, No. M88-CVG-42042 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Feb. 15, 1989) Because the number of repairs that the landlord made to the premises due to the tenant’s negligence was not unreasonable, and the tenant paid all of the charges associated with those repairs, the repairs and charges were not sufficient grounds to evict the tenant.

Fire:

Neal v. Manor, No. L-07-1055, 2008 WL 203648 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Jan. 25, 2008) A fire at the tenant’s rental unit rendered it uninhabitable, but the landlord completed the rehab of the rental unit by the time of the preliminary injunction hearing. In these circumstances, the tenant was a valid leaseholder of the rental unit and entitled to reoccupy the rental unit because there had not been any termination of the tenancy by a court proceeding.

Canterbury Way Apts. v. Barnes, No. 87AP-1106, 1988 WL 44529 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 5, 1988) A tenant’s refusal to reimburse the landlord for repair costs arising from a fire at the premises, which was started by an electric motor on the tenant’s bed, was good cause to evict the tenant.

Martin v. Grandview Apts., No. 91-CV-1215 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty.,
Mar. 9, 1992) Under the subsidized housing lease,222 ¶ 24, when a fire damages a rental unit to the extent that it is unlivable, the tenant’s lease is not terminated and, upon completion of the r epair of the unit, the tenant has the right to recommence the tenant’s occupancy of the unit. To terminate a tenancy in these circumstances, the landlord must comply with 24 C.F.R. part 247 222 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

and the subsidized housing lease, ¶ 23.

Mayes v. Grandview Apts., No. 91-CV-000871 (Ohio C.P.,Clermont Cty.,
Sept. 25, 1991) (preliminary injunction) Where a fire damaged a rental unit to the extent that it was not habitable, the landlord was required to place the tenant on a waiting list for the next available rental unit. If the landlord seeks to evict the tenant, it may pursue its legal remedies under R.C. Chapter 1923.

Mandatory transfers:

Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Pitsinger, No. 21126 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Apr. 28, 2006) A Public Housing tenant and her family resided in a three -bedroom wheelchair-accessible unit, which accommodated her husband who was
paraplegic. After her husband died and her two daughters moved from the unit, the PHA notified the tenant that she was required to move to a one - bedroom unit, so that the wheelchair -accessible unit would be available for a family requiring it. The tenant’s refusal to move constituted good cause to terminate the tenancy.

Altercation:

Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Cantrell , No. 85 -CVG-7589 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Oct. 10, 1985) The tenant’s involvement in an altercation not as the aggressor, but as the victim who attempted to defend herself, was not sufficient grounds to evict the tenant.

### H. Housing Choice Voucher Program Issues


#### 1. Service of Notice to Vacate on the PHA

In the Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”), a landlord who seeks to evict a tenant is required to serve the public housing authority (“PHA”) with a copy of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate that the landlord serves on the tenant. 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.310(e)(2)(ii). “The owner must give the PHA a copy of any owner eviction notice at the same time the owner notifies the tenant.” HUD Tenancy Addendum, form HUD-52641-A (July 2019), ¶ 11(b).

“It is essential that the PHA be given timely notice of the commencemen t of the proceedings to terminate the tenancy, not only so that it does not continue to make 261 housing subsidy payments on behalf of a tenant who is no longer in possession, but also to enable it to monitor the actions of the landlord and to afford it the op portunity to intervene if it deems it necessary to protect the interest of the Section 8 tenant.” Lamlon Dev. Corp. v. Owens , 141 Misc.2d 287, 294, 533 N.Y.S.2d 186 (Dist. Ct.

1988). Accord Sultanik v. Byrd , 15 Misc.3d 1141(A), 841 N.Y.S.2d 823 (Just. Ct .

2007); Homestead Equities, Inc. v. Washington , 176 Misc.2d 459, 672 N.Y.S.2d 980
(Civ. Ct. 1998).

A landlord’s failure to serve timely the PHA with a copy of the notice to vacate is a defense to the eviction action. 223 Asbury Apts. v. Hibbler , No. 13932 , 1994 WL 579845 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 19, 1994); Nittskoff v. Sanford, No. 2010 -CVG- 183 (Ohio Mun., South Euclid, May 12, 2010);Cotton v. McQueen, No. CVG-0700796 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, July 10, 2007); Kosak v. Sadler, No. 04-CVG-5145 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Sept. 18, 2004); Am. Hous. Foundation v. Bridge, No. 02-CVG- 01157 (Ohio Mun., Middletown, June 20, 2002);Green v. Sephus, No. 90-CVG-1768- 38 (Ohio Mun., East Cleveland, Jan. 8, 1991). See also Winns v. Rosado , 440 N.J.
Super. 98, 111 A.3d 155 (2014); Santouse v. Scott , No. HDSP137470, 2006 WL 1600385 (Conn. Super. May 31, 2006); Zeoli v. Vargas, No. CV-040287703S, 2004 WL 2361544 (Conn. Super. Aug. 19, 2004); Fenelon v. Jones , No. LT -000174/11, 2011 WL 2022631 (N.Y. Dist. Ct. May 24, 2011); Sultanik v. Byrd , 15 Misc.3d 1141(A), 841 N.Y.S.2d 823 (Just. Ct. 2007); Homestead Equities, Inc. v. Washington, 176 Misc.2d 459, 672 N.Y.S.2d 980 (Civ. Ct. 1998). Cf. Lee v. Handel, No. 85-CVG- 6674 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Aug. 30, 1985) (Section 8 Moderate Rehabilitation, lease requirement).

#### 2. Abatement/Termination of Payments Based on

Landlord’s Failure to Make Repairs In the Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”), the public housing authority (“PHA”) makes rent subsidy payments to a private landlord on behalf of a tenant pursuant to a housing assistance payments (“HAP”) contract. 24 C.F.R. § 982.451.

One of the landlord’s obligations under the HAP contract is to maintain the premises 223 Some courts have held that a failure to serve the PHA with a copy of this notice deprives the court of subject-matter jurisdiction. E.g., Nittskoff v. Sanford, No. 2010-CVG-183 (Ohio Mun., South Euclid, May 12, 2010); Cotton v. McQueen, No. CVG-0700796 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, July 10, 2007). See also Winns v. Rosado, 440 N.J. Super. 98, 111 A.3d 155 (2014).

in compliance with HUD’s housing quality standards (“HQS”). See 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.404(a).

If a landlord fails to maintain the premises in compliance with the HQS, the PHA may: (1) abate the rent subsidy payments, while maintaining the HAP contract in full force and effect, and/or (2) terminate the HAP contract. Typically, a PHA abates the rent subsidy payments for a specified period of time ( e.g., thirty days) and, if the landlord fails to remedy the HQS violation during the abatement period, the PHA then terminates the HAP contract.

When a PHA abates the rent subsidy payments, the tenant’s payment of the tenant’s portion of the contract rent,224 during the period of the abatement, precludes an eviction for nonpayment of rent. 24 C.F.R. §§ 982.310(b), 982.451(b)(4)(iii).E.g., Weintraub v. Moore , No. 00G -9521 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Dec. 5, 2000); Lessem v.

Wellman, No. 91-CVG-11375 (Ohio Mun., Akron,Oct. 31, 1991). See also Sunflower Park Apts. v. Johnson, 23 Kan. App.2d 862, 937 P.2d 21 (1997); Soliman v. Cepeda, 269 N.J. Super. 151, 634 A.2d 1057 (1993). Cf. Denning v. Swanstrom , No. A -97- 761, 1998 WL 867250 (Neb. App. Nov. 3, 1998) (money claim).

When a PHA terminates the HAP contract, typically, the landlord will demand rent payments in an amount equal to the full contract rent, the tenant will be unable to make those rent payments, and the landlord will then file an eviction action for nonpayment of rent.225 In these circumstances, there are at least three potential eviction defenses.

The first potential defense is the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense, which is discussed in Section II.O, above. The landlord’s failure to maintain the premises in complian ce with the HQS is a violation of the rental agreement (and probably R.C. 5321.04) for which the tenant may assert a counterclaim. E.g., Kenwood Courts Apts. v. Williams,
No. K90-CVG-1043 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 25, 1991). The damages that the tenant suffers as a direct result of the landlord’s violation of the rental agreement 224 The HUD term used to describe the tenant’s portion of the contract rent is “family rent to owner.” 24 C.F.R. § 982.515.

225 If the tenant vacates the property by the effective date of the HAP contract cancellation, the tenant still may bring a money claim against the landlord for the tenant’s damages resulting from the landlord’s failure to make the requiredrepairs, the HAP contract cancellation, and the tenant’s move. Watson v. Chapman-Bowen, No. 101295, 2014 WL 6679591 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 26, 2014) ($4,224 damages).

263 include the loss of the HCVP rent subsidy payments that the PHA would have paid to the landlord on behalf of the tenant, if the landlord had maintained the premises in compliance with the HQS. 226 Id. They also include the damages that a tenant may recover, under contract and negligence per se claims, for defective conditions at the rental premises.227 See Sections IX.A.,B, below.

When asserting the R.C. 1923.061(B) defense, the tenant may be required to address the matter of an R.C. 1923.061(B) rent deposit order. For a discussion on the appropriate rent deposit order for these HCVP tenants, see Section II.O.2, above.

The second potential defense is that the tenant should n ot be liable for, and thus should not be evicted for nonpayment of, the subsidy portion of the rent ( i.e., the amount of HCVP rent subsidy not paid due to the termination of the HAP contract).

The tenant should not be liable for the subsidy portion of the rent for two reasons.

One reason is that the landlord, through the landlord’s action or inaction, caused the termination of the HCVP rent subsidy payments.See Crawley v. Brown, No. 2005- CVG-16627, ¶¶ 42,43 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 6, 2005 ) (“where a subsidy is abated or cancelled due to a landlord’s failure to repair or maintain, courts have declined to enforce rent liability against subsidized tenants beyond their obligation under the HAP contract....In the present matter, plaintiff’s own actions, or lack thereof, caused the termination of the subsidy. As such defendant cannot be held responsible for more than her agreed -upon portion”); Kenwood Courts Apts. v.

Williams, No. K90-CVG-1043 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 25, 1991) (“It was th e Plaintiff’s own actions, or lack thereof, that caused it to lose the rental subsidy. The Defendants cannot therefore be held responsible for more than their family contribution of $30.00.”); Wallpe v. McCurdy , No. 84 -CV-36081 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Oct. 15, 1985) (“Plaintiff, by his own actions, precluded Section 8 certification on and after October 1, 1984 and can make no claim for the difference between defendant’s portion of the monthly rent and the previously agreed rental price”); ATKM Mgmt. Gro up LLC v. Talley , No. 2018 -CVG-018737, ¶ 30 ((Ohio 226 Absent the HQS violations, the HCVP rent subsidy payments would have continued at least until the expiration of the current lease term. This time period generally is the remainder of a one-year lease term (see 24 C.F.R. § 982.309(a)) or the time required to terminate a month -to- month tenancy under R.C. 5321.17(B).

227 For a discussion on the measure of general damages under a HCVP tenant’s contract claim, see Section IX.A.8.b, below.

Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 30, 2019) (“However, defendant’s liability for rent is limited to the tenant portion for these months and defendant is not responsible for the housing assistance payment portion that EDEN stopped paying in November 2018 when the contract was terminated”) . Cf. Rosier v. Brown , 158 Misc.2d 748, 601 N.Y.S.2d 554 (City Ct. 1993) (implied warranty of habitability reduced rent by amount of lost subsidy).

A second reason the tenant should not be liable for the subsidy portion of the rent is that the tenant never agreed to pay it. Curtis v. Surrette, 49 Mass. App. Ct. 99, 103, 726 N.E.2d 967, 970-71 (2000); New Hempstead Terrace LLC v. Reeves, 18 Misc.3d 1113(A), 856 N.Y.S.2d 500 (Dist. Ct. 2008); Livecchi v. Pyatt , No. 2002 -CV-9073, 2003 WL 21246096 (N.Y. City Ct. May 15, 2003). “[E]ven though the WHA [the PHA] terminated the HAP contract with the landlord and ceased making HAP payments, the tenants never assumed the responsibility of paying the entire contract rent and cannot be forced to do so now.” Curtis v. Surrette, 49 Mass. App. Ct. at 103.

“The tenants may be held liable only for that portion of the contract rent that they assumed and agreed to pay.” Id.

The third potential defense is equity. The landlord is not receiving the HCVP rent subsidy solely because of the landlord’s wrongful action– the landlord’s breach of the HQS obligations and thus the HAP contract and the lease. In these circumstances, it is not equitable to declare a forfeiture during the reasonable time that the tenant needs to transfer the HCVP subsidy to another rental unit, provided the tenant continues to pay the tenant’s HCVP portion of the rent.Cf. H-1 Mgmt. LLC v. Perryman, No. 2006- CVG-28200 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 10, 2007) (denial of landlord’s unjust enrichment claim for rent). The PHA determines this reasonable time when it sets the term of the voucher for the move. Generally, the term is between 60 and 120 days.

#### 3. Excess Tenant Rent Demanded by Landlord

In the Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”), a landlord may not demand or accept any rent payment from a tenant in excess of the tenant’s portion of the contract rent228 (“excess tenant rent”). HUD Tenancy Addendum, form HUD-52641- A (July 2019), ¶ 5(e).

Therefore, a landlord may not evict a tenant for nonpayment of excess tenant rent, 228 See note 224, above.

265 even if the tenant agreed to pay it. E.g., Jackson v. Hammond, No. 2006-CVG-31171 (Ohio Mun., Cleve land, Hous. Div., Feb. 15, 2007); Greene v. Edwards , No. CVG - 0301061 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, Sept. 10, 2003);McCuller v. Taylor, No. 00- CVG-13529 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 28, 2000).

If a tenant has paid excess tenant rent, and then fails to pay the tenant’s portion of the contract rent for one or more months, the excess tenant rent may be set-off against the unpaid rent. E.g., Evans v. Washington , No. 98 -CVI-4274 (Ohio Mun., Akron,
Aug. 20, 1998).

A tenant who has paid excess tenant rent also may recover a money judgment against the landlord in the amount of those payments. E.g., Powells v. Radey , No.

96898, 2012 WL 170891 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 19, 2012); Pinkney v. Strahorn,
No. 2006 -CV-08394 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., Apr. 23, 2008); Gonzalez v.

Gonzalez, No. 2010-CVG-9940 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug. 22, 2011);

Thompson v. Womack , No. 2007 -CVG-26006 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Mar. 27, 2008, revised Apr. 24, 2008); Camp v. Thomas , No. 06 -CVF-9711 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, June 21, 2007) (partial summary judgment); Greene v. Edwards, No.

CVG-0301061 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, Mar. 18, 2004);Dennis v. Dudley, No.

2002-CVG-12760 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 28, 2003); Fletcher v.

Shell, No. 1998-CVG-23988 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 2, 2000). See also Ray v. Thirty LLC, No. A-08-1020, 2009 WL 1819288 (Neb. App., June 23, 2009);

Crutchley v. Costa, No. SP5270/01, 2001 WL 1666007 (N.Y. Dist. Ct. Dec. 6, 2001), adhered to on reargument, 2002 WL 452200 (N.Y. Dist. Ct. Feb. 13, 2002).

In these circumstances, HCVP participantsalso have succeeded on a claim against the landlord under the federal False Claim Act (“FCA”). E.g., United States, ex rel.

Carmichael v. Gregory, 270 F. Supp.3d 67 (D.D.C. 2017); United States v. Baran, No.

CV 14-02639 RGK (AJWx), 2015 WL 5446833 (C.D. Cal. Aug. 28, 2015); Coleman
v. Hernandez, 490 F. Supp. 2d 278 (D. Conn. 2007);U.S. ex rel. Smith v. Gilbert Realty Co., 840 F. Supp. 71 (E.D. Mich. 1993). See Cummings v. Hale, No. 15 -cv-04723- JCS, 2017 WL 3669622 (N.D. Cal. May 17, 2017) (utility payments);United States ex
rel. Holmes v. Win Win Real Estate, Inc. No. 2:13-cv-02149-APG-GWF, 2015 WL 6150594 (D. Nev. Oct. 19, 2015) (homeowner’s association and management fees).

See also Kelly v. Denault , 374 F. Supp.3d 884 (N.D. Cal. 2018) (motion to dismiss denied); U.S. ex rel. Sutton v. Reynolds , 564 F. Supp.2d 1183 (D. Ore. 2007) (recognizes the tenant’s FCA claim; denies summary judgment due to factual disputes).

The FCA provides for treble damages and civil penalties between $5,500 and $11,000 per violation. 28 C.F.R. § 85.3(9).

If the tenant initiated the fraud, the court may refuse to award any amount to the tenant under the FCA. E.g., Sterns v. Lane, No. 2:08-cv-175, 2010 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 96981 (D. Vt. Sept. 15, 2010). In addition, some PHAs may find it grounds to terminate the family’s HCVP participation under 24 C.F.R. § 982.552(c)(1)(iv).

If the tenant agreed to the “side payments” under duress and/or coercion, the tenant has not committed a fraud and, therefore, should not be subject to a termination of participation under 24 C.F.R. § 982.552(c)(1)(iv). See 73 Fed. Reg. 39712 (July 10,
2008) (OIG, HUD fraud alert on landlords improperly requiring HCVP tenants to pay rent in excess of the amount authorized by the HAP contract).

#### 4. Sale of the Premises During the Tenancy

Under Ohio law, a purchaser of real estate, which is in the actual possession of a tenant, acquires title subject to the tenant’s rights under the existing tenancy. See Section XXII.B, below.

This state law applies to a Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”) lease.

Letter from Howard M. Schmeltzer, Special Assistant to the HUD Associate General Counsel for Litigation, to Edward G. Weil, Esq. (1985). See also EMC Mortgage
Corp. v. Smith, No. 95-04794, slip op. at 7 (Mass. Hous. Ct., Boston Div., Jan. 5, 1996) (“A new owner can seek to terminate the existing HAP contract and lease, but only in accordance with the procedures set forth in the federal statute and regulations”).

Therefore, the purchaser of a rental unit, which is occupied by a HCVP tenant, acquires the rental unit subject to the notice and good cause requirements for termination of the HCVP lease. Id. E.g., Twin Lawn Props., Ltd. v. Adams, No. CVG- 970506 (Ohio Mun., Fostoria, Mar. 16, 1998). The purchase of the rental unit, alone, is not good cause to terminate the HCVP lease. Id.

However, a termination of a HCVP lease in order to facilitate a subsequent sale of the rental unit is good cause to terminate the lease, provided the termination occurs after the first year of the tenancy. 24 C.F.R. § 982.310(d).

#### 5. Foreclosure of a Mortgage on the Premises

During the Tenancy – Preemption Under Ohio common law, when a foreclosure action concerns tenant -occupied property and the tenant is a party to the foreclosure action, the foreclosure sale may terminate the tenancy. See Section XXII.C.2, below. If the tenant is not a party to the 267 foreclosure action, and the tenant seeks to affirm the tenancy, Ohio case law is split on whether the foreclosure sale may terminate the tenancy. Id.

The federal law on termination of a HCVP lease arguably preempts 229 this Ohio common law. Federal law requires good cause for any termination of a HCVP lease, during a lease term. 42 U.S.C. § 1437f(o)(7)(C); 24 C.F.R. § 982.310; HUD Tenancy Addendum, form HUD-52641-A (July 2019), ¶ 8.

The objective of this federal law is “to provide participating [HCVP] familieswith security of tenancy and reasonable assurance that, unless they breach a material term of their lease or the landlord has ‘good cause’ to terminate the tenancy, they can continue to reside in their homes.” Webster Bank v. Occhipinti, No. CV-970059147S, 1998 WL 846105, at *4 (Conn. Super. Nov. 20, 1998) (quoting EMC Mortgage Corp.
v. Smith, No. 95-04794, slip op. at 17 (Mass. Hous. Ct., Boston Div., Jan. 5, 1996)).

Accord German v. Fed. Home Loan Mortgage Corp. , 899 F. Supp. 1155 (S.D.N.Y.

1995).

Court decisions in other jurisdictions,230 addressing this precise preemption issue, have held that this federal law preempts any state foreclosure law that would terminate a HCVP lease, during a lease term, without good cause.231 E.g., German v. Fed. Home Loan Mortgage Corp., 899 F. Supp. 1155, 1164 (S.D.N.Y. 1995); Webster Bank v.

Occhipinti, No. CV -970059147S, 1998 WL 846105 (Conn. Super. Nov. 20, 1998);

Bristol Sav. Bank v. Savinelli, No. CV-950377478S, 1996 WL 166396 (Conn. Super.
Mar. 21, 1996); EMC Mortgage Corp. v. Smith , No. 95 -04794 (Mass. Housing Ct., Boston Div., Jan. 5, 1996); Fed. Home Loan Mortgage Corp. v. Surzhukov , No. 95 - SP-1487 (Mass. Hous. Ct., Hampden Div., Aug. 9, 1995). But see Atlantic Mortgage & Inv. Corp. v. Pervis, No. SPNH-970651076, 1997 WL 866672 (Conn. Super. Dec.

3, 1997).

Federal law does not require good cause for nonrenewal of a HCVP lease at the expiration of a lease term. See Section III.A., above. Therefore, federal law does not conflict with or preempt any state foreclosure law that terminates a HCVP lease, without good cause, at the expiration of a lease term.

229 See note 210, above.

230 Ohio court decisions have not addressed this preemption issue.

231 Under the federal law, a sale of the premises, alone, is not good cause to terminate a HCVP lease during a lease term. See Section III.H.4, above.

For the additional rights that areafforded these HCVP tenants under the Protecting Tenants at Foreclosure Act, see Section XXII.C.3, below.

### I. Additional Retaliation Defenses


#### 1. First and Fourteenth Amendments

The constraints of the First and/or Fourteenth Amendments of the U.S.

Constitution apply to Public Housing and to certain project -based subsidized housing
developments. See National Housing Law Project, HUD Housing Programs: Tenants’ Rights ¶ 14.9.2.2.2 (5th ed. 2018). The determination as to whether the First and/or Fourteenth Amendments apply to any particular project -based subsidized housing development will depend on whether there is sufficient government action. Id.

The First Amendment and the Fourteenth Amendment, when applicable, prohibit a landlord from evicting a tenant in retaliation for tenant association or tenant organizing activity. Davis v. Vill. Park II Realty, 578 F.2d 461 (2d Cir.1978); Herring
v. Chicago Hous. Auth., No. 90-C-3797, 1993 WL 489767 (N.D. Ill. Apr. 22, 1993);

Walton v. Darby Town Houses, Inc. , 395 F. Supp. 553 (E.D. Pa. 1975); McQueen v.

Druker, 317 F. Supp. 1122 (D. Mass. 1970),aff’d on other grounds, 438 F.2d 781 (1st
Cir. 1971); Belvoir Cliffs Apts. v. Bembry, 56 Ohio Misc. 37, 383 N.E.2d 1170 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, 1978). See generally Thorpe v. Hous. Auth. of City of Durham, 393 U.S. 268 (1969). Cf. Carriage Hill Tenants’ Union v. Wallick Props., Inc. , No. CI- 827-44 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., Sept. 29, 1983) (landlord enjoined from interfering with the publication, distribution, or delivery of the tenant organization’s newsletter).

One benefit of this retaliation defense is that it applies to circumstances to whi ch the R.C. 5321.02 retaliation defense (see Section II.T, above) does not apply. For example, it applies to an eviction action commenced in retaliation for tenant association activity even if the tenant is in default in rent payments.E.g., Belvoir Cliffs
Apts. v. Bembry, 56 Ohio Misc. 37, 383 N.E.2d 1170 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, 1978).

#### 2. HUD Regulations

For project -based subsidized housing, HUD regulations establish the right of a tenant to engage in a wide range of tenant organization activities and requi re the landlord to allow the tenant to engage in these activities. 24 C.F.R. part 245, subpart 269

### B. Furthermore, the subsidized housing lease, 232 ¶ 28 requires the landlord to allow

the tenant to engage in these activities.

If a landlord of project -based subsidized housing commences an eviction action against a tenant because the tenant engaged in one (or more) of these activities, the landlord is not allowing the tenant to engage in the activity. Therefore, in such cases, the landlord has violated HUD regulations and the lease.

These violations of the HUD regulations and the lease constitute a retaliation defense to the eviction action. And, unlike the retaliation defense under the First and/or Fourteenth Amendments (see Section III.I.1., above), tenants m ay invoke this retaliation defense irrespective of whether the landlord’s conduct constitutes government action.

### J. Bankruptcy


#### 1. Overview

Under the bankruptcy laws, an assisted housing tenant 233 can prevent an eviction for nonpayment of rent and reinstate the lease, provided the tenant otherwise qualifies for bankruptcy relief.

A project-based assisted housing tenant 234 may secure this relief in a chapter 13
bankruptcy. A Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”) tenant also may secure this relief in a chapter 13 bankruptcy, but generally there is little benefit in reinstating a HCVP lease because it does not require good cause for nonrenewal (see Section III.A, above).235 See, e.g., In re Burch , 401 B.R. 153 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 2008) (due to the landlord’s nonrenewalof the HCVP lease, there did not exist an unexpired lease term for the tenant to assume in the chapter 13 bankruptcy).

In some jurisdictions, a Public Housing tenant may also secure this relief in a chapter 7 bankruptcy. When a Public Housing tenant files a chapter 7 bankruptcy, a primary issue is whether the anti -discrimination provision (11 U.S.C. § 525(a)) 232 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

233 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, above.

234 For the definition of “project-based assisted housing,” see note 165, above.

235 However, when the eviction is for nonpayment of rent, a bankruptcy may prevent the public housing authority from terminating the family’s HCVP participation.

precludes a public housing authority (“PHA”) from evicting a tenant on the basis of nonpayment of discharged, prepetition rent. The case law on this issue is split.

The better-reasoned view is that the PHA is precluded from evicting the tenant.

The reasoning for this view, in Stoltz v. Brattleboro Hous. Auth., 315 F.3d 80 (2d Cir.

2002), is discussed in Section III.J.2, below.

Court decisions holding that the PHA is precluded from evicting the tenant include:

Stoltz v. Brattleboro Hous. Auth ., 315 F.3d 80 (2d Cir. 2002); In re Aikens, 503 B.R.

603 (Bankr. S.D.N.Y. 2014); Biggs v. Hous. Auth. of City of Pittsburgh , No. 07-CV- 0007, 2007 WL 654247 (W.D. Pa. Feb. 28, 2007); 236 Couture v. Burlington Hous.

Auth., 225 B.R. 58 (D. Vt. 1998); In re Curry , 148 B.R. 966 (S.D. F la. 1992); In re Bain, No. 10-11316-RAM (Bankr. S.D. Fla. Apr. 20, 2010); In re Kelly, 356 B.R. 899
(Bankr. S.D. Fla. 2006); In re Batista, No. 05-11033-BKC-RAM, 2005 WL 2451736
(Bankr. S.D. Fla. June 2, 2005);Johnson v. Chester Hous. Auth., 250 B.R. 521 (Bankr.

E.D. Pa. 2000); In re Jackson, No. 94-18512 DWS (Bankr. E.D. Pa. May 4, 1995); In re Szymecki, 87 B.R. 14 (Bankr. W.D. Pa. 1988); Sudler v. Chester Hous. Auth. , 71 B.R. 780 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 1987); Gibbes v. Hous. Auth. of New Haven , 9 B.R. 758
(Bankr. D. Conn. 1981), supplemented, 12 B.R. 737 (D. Conn. 1981),aff’d in relevant part & remanded in part on other grounds, 76 B.R. 257 (D. Conn. 1983).

Court decisions holding that the PHA is not precluded from evicting the tenant include: Hous. Auth. of City of Pittsburgh v. Smith , No. 2:14 -cv-00976 (W.D. Pa.

2014); Hous. Auth. of New Orleans v. Eason, 12 So.3d 970 (La. 2009); In re Valentin,
No. 03-24290T, 2004 WL 1064227 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. May 10, 2004);In re Hobbs, 221 B.R. 892 (Bankr. M.D. Fla. 1997 ); In re Bacon, 212 B.R. 66 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 1997);

In re Collins, 199 B.R. 561 (Bankr. W.D. Pa. 1996);In re James, 198 B.R. 885 (Bankr.

W.D. Pa. 1996).

Generally, project-based subsidized housing tenants237 cannot secure this relief in a chapter 7 bankruptcy because 11 U.S.C. § 525(a), on which Public Housing tenants rely, “protects debtors from discrimination by governmental units with regard to certain grants.” Stoltz v. Brattleboro Hous. Auth., 315 F.3d 80, 83 (2d Cir. 2002).

Project-based subsidized housing that is not owned by a govenrmental unit is not 236 The court in Hous. Auth. of City of Pittsburgh v. Smith, No. 2:14-cv-00976 (W.D. Pa. 2014), determined that the Biggs decision on this point is informational but not a holding.

237 For the definition of “project-based subsidized housing,” see note 165, above.

271 a governmental unit for § 525(a) purposes. Stoltz, 315 F.3d at 90 n.5; In re Marcano, 288 B.R. 324 (Bankr. S.D.N.Y. 2003); In re Rosemond, 105 B.R. 8, 9 (Bankr. W.D.
Pa. 1989); In re Lutz , 82 B.R. 6 99, 702-03 (Bankr. M.D. Pa. 1988). But see In re Okentowicz, 314 B.R. 638 (Bankr. E.D. Mich. 2004) (applicant, Section 8 project - based assisted housing).

However, project-based subsidized housing that is owned by a governmental unit is a governmental unit for § 525(a) purposes. In re Bain, No. 10-11316-RAM (Bankr.

S.D. Fla. Apr. 20, 2010) (county -owned Section 8 project -based housing); In re Marcano, 288 B.R. 324 (Bankr. S.D.N.Y. 2003) (city-owned property).

#### 2. Stoltz Analysis of Chapter 7, § 525(a) Issue

11 U.S.C. § 525(a) prohibits governmental units from, among other things, refusing to renew a person’s grant because of the person’s debt, if the grant is protected under § 525(a) and the debt has been discharged in a bankruptcy proceeding.

Congress enacted § 525(a) to codify the decision in Perez v. Campbell, 402 U.S.

637 (1971). S. Rep. No. 95 -989, at 81, reprinted in 1978 U.S.C.C.A.N. 5787, 5867.

In Perez, the Supreme Court invalidated, under the Supremacy Clause, a state law that suspended the drivers’ licenses of debtors who failed to satisfy motor -vehicle-related tort judgments. The Supreme Court held that the state statute was in conflict with the Bankruptcy Act because it frustrated the “fresh start” policy of the Bankruptcy Code.

“Notably, the text of Section 525(a) does not limit its scope to the facts presented in Perez. Section 525(a) applies to any governmental unit, not just a State agency or department; it covers not only licenses, but also permits, charters, franchises, and other similar grants ....” Stoltz v. Brattleboro Hous. Auth., 315 F.3d 80, 87 (2d Cir. 2002).

As noted in Section III.J.1, above, when a Public Housing tenant files a chapter 7 bankruptcy, a primary issue, on which the case law is split, 238 is whether § 525(a) precludes a public housing authority (“PHA”) from evicting a tenant on the basis of nonpayment of discharged, prepetition rent.

The answer to this issue depends on four sub -issues: (1) whether a PHA is a governmental unit for § 525(a) purposes; (2) whether the Publ ic Housing lease – the tenant’s right to continue to participate in the Public Housing program – falls within the § 525(a) “other similar grants” provision; (3) whether the alleged unpaid rent that has been discharged is the proximate cause of the eviction – the “solely because” 238 The court decisions are listed in Section III.J.1, above.

requirement;239 and (4) whether the § 525(a) protection trumps the PHA’s general right under 11 U.S.C. § 362(b)(22)240 to pursue state law remedies, including eviction, for a prepetition breach of the lease, when the trustee rejects the lease.

The only federal appellate court to rule on this issue is the Second Circuit, inStoltz
v. Brattleboro Hous. Auth ., 315 F.3d 80 (2d Cir. 2002). It analyzed each of the four sub-issues and thereupon held that § 525(a) prohibits the PHA from evic ting a Public Housing tenant for nonpayment of discharged prepetition rent.

First, the Court found that a PHA is a governmental unit for § 525(a) purposes.

Stoltz, 315 F.3d at 88. The PHA did not dispute this finding. Id.

Second, the Court held that “other similar grants” include the Public Housing lease and the right to continue to participate in the Public Housing program.Stoltz, 315 F.3d at 89-91. The Court rejected the PHA’s argument “that a public housing tenant’s future right to participate in the public housing program is the only protected public housing grant under section 525(a).” Id. at 89.

Third, the Court held that, in these circumstances, the eviction claim is solely because of the unpaid rent that has been discharged in a chapter 7 bankruptcy. Stoltz, 315 F.3d at 91. The Court rejected the PHA’s argument “that it desires to evict Stoltz only because she breached her public housing lease, not because she failed to pay prepetition rent that was discharged in bankruptcy. Id. The Court n oted that “this unpersuasive argument [of the PHA] is premised on an ‘illusory distinction’ between the breach of the lease and the nonpayment of the discharged prepetition rent.” Id.

Fourth, the Court held that, in this context, § 525(a) and 11 U.S.C. § 365 (now 239 For § 525(a) purposes, a governmental unit’s action is “solely because” of the failure to pay a discharged debt if the nonpayment is “the proximate cause of the cancellation – the act or event that triggers the agen cy’s decision to cancel, whatever the agency’s ultimate motive in pulling the trigger may be.” FCC v. NextWave Personal Commc’ns Inc., 537 U.S. 293, 301-02 (2003). As the Supreme Court explained: “When the statute refers to failure to pay a debt as the sole cause of cancellation (‘solely because’), it cannot reasonably be understood to include, among the other causes whose presence can preclude application of the prohibition, the governmental unit’s motive in effecting the cancellation. Such a reading w ould deprive § 525 of all force. It is hard to imagine a situation in which a governmental unit would not have some further motive behind the cancellation ....” Id., at 301.

240 The corresponding provision to 11 U.S.C. § 362(b)(22), before the 2005 BAPCPA amendments, was 11 U.S.C. § 365. The same type of conflict exists between 11 U.S.C.

§ 362(b)(22) and § 525(a) as existed between 11 U.S.C. § 365 and § 525(a), and the same principles apply to this conflict. In re Aikens, 503 B.R. 603, 607 (Bankr. S.D.N.Y. 2014).

273 § 362(b)(22))241 are in conflict, and that § 525(a) controls because it is the more specific
provision. Stoltz, 315 F.3d at 92-95.

In Stoltz, the Court also analyzed and then rejected the conclusions and holdings in: In re Bacon, 212 B.R. 66 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 1997); 242 In re Collins, 199 B.R. 561
(Bankr. W.D. Pa. 1996); and In re James, 198 B.R. 885 (Bankr. W.D. Pa. 1996).

The holding in Bacon primarily relies on its conclusion that the § 525(a) protection does not encompass continued participation in a program ( e.g., the Public Housing program), but rather prohibits discrimination only in any future application to participate in a program (e.g., a future application for admission to the Public Housing program). Under this view, no conflict exists between § 525(a) and § 365.

The holdings in Collins and James primarily relied on their conclusion that § 365 is more specific than and thus trumps § 525(a).243

#### 3. Unexpired Lease Term, Chapters 13 and 7

In the bankruptcy proceeding, an issue may arise as to whether the lease is “unexpired” as of the filing of the bankruptcy petition. In a chapter 13 bankruptcy, the tenant may assume and reinstate the assisted housing lease only if it is an “unexpired lease.” See 11 U.S.C. §§ 365(a), 1322(b)(7). In a chapter 7 bankruptcy, the right (if
any) of the tenant to retain the Public Housing lease under 11 U.S.C. § 525(a) exists only if it is an unexpired lease. See Stoltz, 315 F.3d at 86;Couture v. Burlington Hous.

Auth., 225 B.R. 58, 61 -63 (D. Vt. 1998); In re Bacon, 212 B.R. 66, 70 (Bankr. E.D.

241 See note 240, above.

242 The decisions in In re Valentin, 309 B.R. 715 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 2004), and In re Hobbs, 221 B.R. 892 (Bankr. M.D. Fla. 1997), follow the Bacon decision. Therefore, the Stoltz analysis of Bacon applies with equal force to Valentin and Hobbs.

243 The James decision also relies on its erroneous holding that, because the trustee by operation of law rejected the lease, the lease was no longer in effect, which constituted a valid ground (aside from nonpayment of the discharged re nt) for the eviction. This holding is erroneous because “the automatic rejection of a lease pursuant to § 365(d)(1) affects an abandonment of the lease to the debtor.” In re Bacon, 212 B.R. 66, 68 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 1997). Accord Stoltz v.

Brattleboro Hous. Auth., 315 F.3d 80, 85 n.1 (2d Cir. 2002); In re Collins, 199 B.R. 561, 565
(Bankr. W.D. Pa. 1996). It does not terminate the lease. Id.
Pa. 1997); Sudler v. Chester Hous. Auth., 71 B.R. 780, 785 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 1987).244 The term “unexpired” is not defined in the Bankruptcy Code. Stoltz v. Brattleboro
Hous. Auth., 315 F.3d 80, 86 (2d Cir. 2002); In re Williams, 144 F.3d 544, 546 (7th
Cir. 1998). In defining this term:

Some courts have concluded that a residential lease may be in default or may have been terminated under state law and may still be assumable in bankruptcy as an unexpired lease. These courts have looked to state law, not to determine the point at which the tenant’s leasehold interest is considered to be terminated, but to determine the point at which the tenant’s leasehold interest, incl uding actual possessory interest, is finally and conclusively extinguished, so that there is nothing to assume at the time the petition is filed.

In re DiCamillo , 206 B.R. 64, 68 (Bankr. D.N.J. 1997). E.g., Stoltz, 315 F.3d at 86 (Vermont, expires not soo ner than issuance of writ of possession); In re Parker, 269 B.R. 522 (D. Vt. 2001) (Vermont, expires upon execution of writ of possession);In re Ross, 142 B.R. 1013 (S.D. Fla. 1992) (Florida, expires upon execution of writ of possession); In re DiCamillo, 206 B.R. 64 (Bankr. D.N.J. 1997) (New Jersey, expires later than entry of eviction judgment); In re Whitsett, 163 B.R. 752 (Bankr. E.D. Pa.
1994) (Pennsylvania, expires upon execution of writ of possession); In re Talley, 69 B.R. 219 (Bankr. M.D. Tenn. 19 86) (Tennessee, expires upon execution of writ of possession). But see In re Williams, 144 F.3d 544, 546 (7th Cir. 1998) (Illinois, expires when notice of termination expires, unless tenant has valid defense to eviction);245 In re Moore, 290 B.R. 851 (Ban kr. N.D. Ala. 2003) (Alabama, expires when notice of 244 In a chapter 7 bankruptcy, the tenant cannot assume the lease. Stoltz v. Brattleboro Hous.

Auth., 315 F.3d 80, 85 n.1 (2d Cir. 2002); In re Marcano, 288 B.R. 324, 329 (Bankr. S.D.N.Y.

2003); In re Bacon, 212 B.R. 66, 69 n.4 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 1997). While the trustee may assume the lease, the trustee will rarely if ever assume an assisted housing lease. Stoltz, 315 F.3d at 85 n.1; In re Marcano , 288 B.R. at 329; In re Day , 208 B.R. 358, 365 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 1997).

When the trustee rejects the lease, it “effects an abandonment of the lease to the debtor [tenant].” In re Bacon, 212 B.R. at 68. Accord Stoltz, 315 F.3d at 85 n.1; In re Marcano, 288 B.R. at 329;

In re Day, 208 B.R. at 365.

245 The Seventh Circuit held that the bankruptcy court did not abuse its discretion in “modifying the automatic stay to permit the forcible entry action to proceed so tha t the state court could determine the merits of Williams’defenses, and, more to the point, whether the lease had any value that could be assumed under her plan.” 144 F.3d at 550. Accord In re Bennett, 165 F.3d 31 (table), 1998 WL 756838 (7th Cir. 1998).

275 termination expires).

With respect to Ohio law, one bankruptcy court has held that a residential lease expires for § 365(a) purposes not later than the entry of the eviction judgment. In re Collier, 163 B.R. 118 (Bankr. S.D. Ohio 1993). Because the Collier tenant filed her bankruptcy petition post-entry of the eviction judgment and pre -execution of the writ of possession, the court did not need to determine, and opted not to determine, the precise stage in the eviction process that the lease expired. Id. at 121 n.2.

#### 4. Prompt Cure, Chapter 13

If a tenant files a chapter 13 bankruptcy, another issue that may arise is whether, under the tenant’s plan, the tenant will promptly pay the unpaid rent. The tenant may assume the unexpired lease only if the tenant provides adequate assurance that the tenant will “promptly cure” the unpaid rent. See 11 U.S.C. § 365(b)(1)(A). E.g., In re Whitsett, 163 B.R. 752 (Bankr. E.D. Pa. 1994) (slightly less than two years, $1,800 – prompt cure); In re Ross, 142 B.R. 1013 (S.D. Fla. 1992) (five months– prompt cure);

In re Liggins , 145 B.R. 227 (Bankr. E.D. Va. 1992) (48 to 60 months, $8,769 – not prompt cure); In re Yokley, 99 B.R. 394 (Bankr. M.D. Tenn. 1989) (approximatelytwo years, $1,076.50 – not prompt cure). See also In re DiCamillo, 206 B.R. 64, 66 (Bankr.

D.N.J. 1997) (“The courts have consistently held that a proposed cure over a period of two years or more was not ‘prompt’ for purposes of§ 365(b)(1)”).

#### 5. Automatic Stay

If an eviction action against the tenant is pending and an eviction judgment has not been entered against the tenant as of the date that the tenant files the bankruptcy petition, the bankruptcy filing generally operates as an automatic stay of the eviction
proceeding. 11 U.S.C. § 362. See, e.g., Hayes v. Kelly , No. L -14-1153, 2015 WL 995446 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 6, 2015).

The one exception to this general rule is that, under 11 U.S.C. §§ 365(b)(23), 365(m), the automatic stay may not apply to the pending eviction action if the landlord files with the bankruptcy court, and serves on the tenant, a certification that: (1) the tenant is endangering the real property or is using (or is allowing to be used) controlled substances on the property; and (2) the pending eviction action is based on these
grounds. Within 15 days of the filing of the certification, the tenant may file objections to the landlord’s claim under this exception, on the ground that the alleged underlying facts giving rise to the landlord’s claim did not exist or have been remedied. If the tenant demonstrates either of these grounds, the automatic stay will continue to apply to the pending eviction action.

If an eviction action against the tenant is pending and an eviction judgment against the tenant has been entered as of the date that the tenant files the bankruptcy petition, generally the bankruptcy filing does not operate as an automatic stay of the eviction
proceeding. 11 U.S.C. §§ 362(b)(22), 362(l)(a)(3).

The one exception to this general rule is that the bankruptcy filing operates as an automatic stay of the eviction proceeding for thirty days, if the tenant files with the bankruptcy petition and serves upon the landlord a certification that: (1) un der applicable non -bankruptcy law, “there are circumstances under which the debtor [tenant] would be permitted to cure the entire monetary default that gave rise to the judgment for possession, after that judgment for possession was entered;” and (2) the tenant “has deposited with the clerk of the court, any rent that would become due during the 30-day period after the filing of the bankruptcy petition.”246 Id. If, during the 30-day period, the tenant cures the entire monetary default that gave rise to the eviction judgment, the automatic stay will continue to apply to the eviction proceeding.
Id. However, “§ 525(a) eliminates the need for a Public Housing debtor to cure a prepetition default as a condition to rendering the stay exception in § 362(b)(22) inapplicable.” In re Kelly, 356 B.R. 899, 901 (Bankr. S.D. Fla.2006).

Since Ohio law does not provide a tenant with a right to cure a monetary default after the entry of an eviction judgment, this exception to the general rule appears to be inapplicable to Ohio tenants.

If an eviction judgment is subject to de novo review, it is not an eviction judgment for § 362(b)(22) purposes. In re Alberts, 381 B.R. 171 (Bankr. W.D. Pa. 2008) (under Pennsylvania law, a district magistrate court’s eviction judgment issubject to de novo review in the court of common pleas and, therefore, is not an eviction judgment for § 362(b)(22) purposes). For Ohio tenants, this means that a magistrate’s eviction decision, which is subject to de novo review by a judge (see Ohio R. Civ. P. 53(D)), is not an eviction judgment for § 362(b)(22) purposes.

246 See, e.g., In re Harris, 424 B.R. 44 (Bankr. E.D.N.Y. 2010) (landlord permitted to enforce pre-filing eviction judgment because tenant failed to file the §§ 362(b)(22),362(l)(a)(3) certification with the filing of the bankruptcy petition).

277

### K. Public Housing Turnkey III

(Homeownership) Some public housing authorities (“PHAs”) administer a homeownership program known as the Turnkey III program. See 24 C.F.R. part 904.

Under the program, a family enters into an agreement (“Homebuyers Ownership Opportunity Agreement”) that provides the family with an opportunity to reside in and eventually purchase a PHA dwelling unit. 24 C.F.R. § 904.103(c)(2). If the family violates its ob ligations under the agreement or no longer meets the homeownership potential requirements, the PHA may terminate the agreement. 24 C.F.R.

§§ 904.107(m),(o).

In an eviction action against such families, a potential issue is whether the agreement is a land installment contract (and thus subject to R.C. 5313.07) or a lease with an option to purchase. If the agreement is a land installment contract, and if the agreement has been in effect for at least five years or the family has paid at least twenty percent of the purchase price, the PHA may not enforce its right of possession (if any) through an eviction action. See Section XXIII.E.2., below. In these circumstances, the PHA may enforce its right of possession (if any), and terminate the family’s interest in the property, only through a foreclosure action. Id.

One Ohio appellate court has held that the agreement is a land installment contract (and thus subject to R.C. 5313.07). Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Watkins, 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984).

The case law from other states is split on this issue. Compare Hous. Auth. of City of Spartanburg v. Elmore , No. 94 -CP-42-832 (S.C.C.P., Spartanburg Cty., Nov. 30,
1994) (follows Watkins) with Philadelphia Hous. Auth. v. Barbour, 592 A.2d 47 (Pa.
Super. 1991) (the agreement is a lease with an option to purchase, not a long-term sales contract). See also Ayers v. Philadelphia Hous. Auth. , 908 F.2d 1184 (3d Cir. 1990) (HUD Turnkey III regulations preempt state foreclosure law notice and opportunity to cure requirements; reserved for the state court the matter of whether Turnkey III homebuyers are ten ants subject to an eviction action or sufficiently similar to mortgagors to require ejectment in the court of common pleas). Cf. Kopanuk v. AVCP Reg’l Hous. Auth., 902 P.2d 813 (Alaska 1995) (barred use of eviction action for breach of homeownership agreement under analogous Indian housing authority program).

### L. Low Income Housing Tax Credit

Program

#### 1. Introduction

The federal Low Income Housing Tax Credit (“LIHTC”) program (see 26 U.S.C.

§ 42) uses income tax credits to fund construction, rehabilitation, and acquisition/ rehabilitation of rental units for low-income families. For a general description of the LIHTC program, see National Housing Law P roject, HUD Housing Programs:

Tenants’ Rights, ¶ 1.11 (5th ed. 2018). The Ohio Housing Finance Agency (“OHFA”) administers the LIHTC program for the state of Ohio.

Sections III.L.2-4, below, provide a brief summary of the program’s set -aside of low-income units, rent structure, and eviction rules.

#### 2. Set-Aside of Low-Income Units

The owner of a LIHTC project is required to set aside a certain percentage of units for low-income tenants (“low-income units”). 26 U.S.C. § 42(g).

Before 2018, generally, two options existed for the set-aside requirement: (1) 20% (or more) of the units for tenants whose income is at or below 50% of area medium income or (2) 40% (or more) of the units for tenants whose income is at or below 60% of area medium income. Id. The LIHTC program uses the HUD definition of area median income.

For LIHTC properties developed since 2018, a third set -aside option has been a standard known as income averaging. 26 U.S.C. § 42(g). It permits applicants with incomes up to 80% of area medium in come, provided the average rent and income limit of the designated units does not exceed 60% of the area medium income. This income averaging standard permits a project to use rent/income levels for particular units at 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%, 60%, 70%, or 80% of area medium income.

These income limits apply only at the time of a tenant’s initial occupancy. Id. If the development is 100% low -income units, after initial occupancy, a tenant is not subject to any income limit. Id. If the development has both low -income units and market-rent units, after initial occupancy, if the income of a low -income unit tenant exceeds the initial income limit by more than 40%, the tenant is deemed to be a market- rent tenant and the next available unit at the development must be leased to a qualifying low income tenant. Id.

279 For the LIHTC program, “[t]enant income is calculated in a manner consistent with the determination of annual income under section 8 of the United States Housing Act of 1937 (‘Section 8’) ....” 26 C.F.R. § 1.42-5(b)(vii). Therefore, in computing tenant income, the LIHTC program relies on 24 C.F.R. § 5.609 and HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1.247 See IRS, Guide for Completing Form 8823, Low -Income Housing Credit Agencies Report of Noncompliance or Building Disposition (Rev. 01 -2011), ch. 4 (available at www.irs.gov/Businesses/Small-Businesses-&-Self-Employed/Guide-for- Completing-Form-8823-Low-Income-Housing-Credit-Agencies-Report-of- Noncompliance-or-Building-Disposition).

#### 3. Rent for Low-Income Units

For LIHTC projects with post -1989 tax credit allocations, gross rent for a low - income unit may not exceed 30% of the initial income limit for the unit, b ased on an assumed household size of 1.5 persons per bedroom (or one person for an efficiency).

26 U.S.C. § 42(g). For example, the gross rent for a two-bedroom unit may not exceed 30% of the initial income limit for a three-person household.

For LIHTC p rojects with pre -1990 tax credit allocations, gross rent for a low - income unit may not exceed 30% of the initial income limit for the unit, based on the size of the actual household that occupies the unit.

All utilities, except telephone, cable television, and internet services, are included in the gross rent restriction. 26 C.F.R. § 1.42-10. If a tenant is required to pay for one of the utilities, either directly to the utility supplier or to the landlord under a submeter system, the tenant’s rent is red uced by the amount of the applicable utility allowance(s). 26 C.F.R. § 1.42 -10; Internal Revenue Service, Notice 2009 -44 (application of § 1.42 -10 to submetered utilities) (available at www.irs.gov/pub/irs - drop/n-09-44.pdf).

The IRS regulations – 26 C.F.R. § 1.42 -10 – permit several alternative methods for determining a development’s utility allowance(s). For Ohio LIHTC properties, OHFA will accept seven of those alternative methods: HUD’s utility allowance, for the properties it regulates; Rural Housing Service’s utility allowance, for the properties it assists; the relevant PHA’s utility allowance; the HUD utility schedule model; utility company estimates; and the OHFA consumption utility allowance model. OHFA, Utility Allowance Policies and Procedures (eff. Jan. 11, 2010, rev. Apr. 18, 2011).

247 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

A HCVP family may reside at a LIHTC project and thereby pay only the tenant’s portion of the HCVP rent. A LIHTC landlord cannot refuse to lease a unit to a HCVP family because of the family’s participation in the HCVP. 26 U.S.C. §42(h)(6)(B)(iv);

26 C.F.R. § 1.42-5(c)((1)(xi); HUD Notice PIH 2002-15 (June 7, 2002) (reinstates and extends indefinitely Notice PIH 2001-2 (issued Jan. 18, 2001, expired Jan. 31, 2002)).
