Also, for a HCVP tenancy at a LIHTC property, “the [contract] rent will be considered reasonable if it does not exceed the greater of (1) the rent for other LIHTC … assisted units in the project not occupied by families with tenant -based assistance, or (2) the payment standard established by a PHA for a unit of the size involved.” 73 Fed. Reg.

71037, 71039 (Nov. 24, 2008) (HUD Notice, 42 U.S.C. § 1437f(o)(10)(F)).

#### 4. Eviction Rules for Low-Income Units


##### a. Notice of Termination

Every Ohio LIHTC project lease, in an OHFA mandated Lease Addendum, establishes a notice of termination requirement. The OHFA Lease Addendum became effective July 1, 2019.

The notice of termination requirement under the OHFA Lease Addendum is as follows: “Prior to terminating or refusing to renew the Lease for a Minor Violation, …the Owner must first deliver written notice to the Tenant supplying sufficient details of the violation to adequately inform Tenant of its scope and provide a reasonable period of time, but in no case less than thirty (30) days from the date notice is delivered, for the Tenant to cure the violation.” OHFA Lease Addendum,¶ 2.a.

For purposes of the OHFA Lease Addendum, a Minor Violation includes: “those violations not related to health, safety, criminal activity, actions by the Tenant creating a hostile environment for other tenants of the Owner, inflicting damage to the Premises or property of Owner, or Program eligibility issues caused by Tenant’s misrepresentation or failure to provide required information.” Id.

The federal LIHTC statute and regulations do not establish a notice of termination requirement for an owner’s nonrenewal (or termination) of a low-income unit tenancy.

When the development funds for a LIHTC project include HOME funds ( see 42 U.S.C. §§ 12741-56; 24 C.F.R. part 92), in order to t erminate or refuse to renew the tenancy, the owner must serve the tenant with a written notice of termination. 42 U.S.C. § 12755(b); 24 C.F.R. § 92.253(c). The owner must serve the notice at least thirty days before the termination or nonrenewal. Id. The notice must specify the 281 grounds for the termination or nonrenewal. Id.

Whether due process requires a notice of termination for the owner’s nonrenewal (or termination) of the tenancy will depend primarily on whether the requisite government action exists.248 See Bowling Green Manor Ltd. P’ship v. Kirk, No. WD- 94-125, 1995 WL 386476 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 1995);Bowling Green Manor
Ltd. P’ship v. LaChance, No. WD -94117, 1995 WL 386496 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 1995). In most cases, the requisite government action will not exist.

Kirk and LaChance concluded that the requisite government action existed at a LIHTC property, but only with respect to a HCVP tenancy, and the continuing validity of this conclusion is unclear. At the time of these decisions, HUD regulations required good cause for an owner not to renew a HCVP tenancy. See former 24 C.F.R.

§§ 882.215(c) and 887.213 (1995). Both decisions relied on this good cause requirement in concluding that the requisite government action exis ted. This HCVP good cause requirement no longer exists. See Section III.A, above.

If a due process notice of termination requirement exists, it will likely be the same as the notice required in the other assisted housing programs. See Bowling Green Manor Ltd. P’ship v. Kirk, No. WD-94-125, 1995 WL 386476 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 1995);Bowling Green Manor Ltd. P’ship v. LaChance, No. WD-94-117, 1995 WL 386496 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 1995). This means it will likely require an advance notice that proposes to terminate the tenancy and informs the tenant of the reason for the proposed termination of tenancy. Id. See Section III.A, above.

For nonpayment of rent cases, another potential LIHTC notice of termination requirement is the thirty-day notice of termination that was established in the CARES Act, Pub. L. 116 -136, 134 Stat. 281, enacted Mar. 27, 2020, at § 4024(c) (“CARES Act provision”). The CARES Act provision applies to the LIHTC program in the same manner as it applies to the assisted housing programs. For a discussion on the terms and status of the CARES Act provision, see note 183, above.

##### b. Good Cause

Every Ohio LIHTC project lease, in an OHFA mandated Lease Addendum, establishes a good cause requirement for any owner termination or nonrenewal of the 248 Given the good cause requirement for termination or nonrenewal of the tenancy (see Section III.L.4.b, below), the tenant has a cognizable property interest in continued occupancy (see Section III.A, above).
lease. The OHFA Lease Addendum became effective July 1, 2019.

The good cause requirement under the OHFA Lease Addendum i s as follows:

“Owner may not terminate the tenancy or refuse to renew theLease or rental agreement of Tenant except for good cause, which includes, but is not limited to, serious or repeated violations of the material terms and conditions of the Lease or house rules or a violation of applicable federal, state, or local law.” OHFA Lease Addendum,¶ 2.

Also, under federal law, a LIHTC landlord may not terminate a LIHTC tenancy, or refuse to renew a LIHTC lease, except for good cause. 26 U.S.C. § 42(h)(6)(B)(i), based on its cross -reference to subclauses (I) and (II) of 26 U.S.C. § 42(h)(6)(E)(ii).

For a comprehensive analysis of this statutory good cause requirement, see Marc Jolin, Good Cause Eviction and the Low Income Housing Tax Credit, 67 U. Chi. L.Rev. 521 (2000).

In 2004 and 2005, the IRS confirmed the existence of this good cause requirement in two issuances: IRS Revenue Ruling 2004 -82, § C (available at www.irs.gov/irb/2004-35_IRB/ar09.html); and IRS Revenue Procedure 2005 -37 (available at www.irs.gov/irb/2005-28_IRB/ar14.html).249 Court decisions relying on IRS Revenue Ruling 2004 -82, § C, in holding that a good cause requirement exists for any nonrenewal of a LIHTC lease, include:

Galbraith Pointe Apts. v. Humphrey , No. 03-CV-29876 (Ohio Mun., H amilton Cty.,
Aug. 20, 2004). See also Lend Lease Apt. Mgmt. v. Stribling, No. HWA-30495, 2004 WL 2595805 (Conn. Super. Sept. 8, 2004).

Even prior to the IRS issuances, some courts had concluded that 26 U.S.C.

§ 42(h)(6) establishes a good cause requirement for nonrenewal of a LIHTC tenancy.

E.g., Carter v. Maryland Mgmt. Co. , 377 Md. 596, 835 A.2d 158 (2003); Cimarron
Vill. v. Washington, 659 N.W.2d 811 (Minn. App. 2003); Fennelly v. Kimball Court
Apts. Ltd. P’ship, No. 0049041, 2001 WL 1524452 (Mass. Su per. Sept. 25, 2001) (dicta).

When the development funds for a LIHTC project include HOME funds, a good 249 An IRS noncompliance guide provides misleading (if not erroneous) guidance on this good cause requirement for nonrenewal of a LIHTC lease. See IRS, Guide for Completing Form 8823, Low-Income Housing Credit Agencies Report of Noncompliance or Building Disposition
(Rev. 01 -2011), ch. 26 (available at www.irs.gov/Businesses/Small-Businesses-&-Self- Employed/Guide-for-Completing-Form-8823-Low-Income-Housing-Credit-Agencies-Report- of-Noncompliance-or-Building-Disposition). It purports to limit this good cause requirement to an obligation to prove good cause in court, if and when someone raises the issue in court. Id.

283 cause requirement is also established by 42 U.S.C. § 12755(b) and 24 C.F.R.

§ 92.253(c).

When the requisite government action exists at a LIHTC projec t, the good cause requirement is arguably established by due process as well.See Bowling Green Manor
Ltd. P’ship v. Kirk, No. WD-94-125, 1995 WL 386476 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 1995); Bowling Green Manor Ltd. P’ship v. LaChance , No. WD -94-117, 1995 WL 386496 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 1995). But see Vienna Forest Apts. v.

Passmore, No. CA2004-05-104, 2005 WL 1131742 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 16, 2005).

In Passmore, a HCVP tenant relied solely on due process as the basis for a good cause requirement for nonrenewal of the lease. The court held that, unlike Section 8 project-based assisted housing, the HCVP does not have a good cause requirement for nonrenewal and, thereupon, rejected the tenant’s good cause argument. The tenant did not argue, and the court did not address, the good cause requirement for nonrenewal under the LIHTC statute and the IRS implementing issuances.

## IV. Eviction Action, Additional

Defenses, Manufactured Home

### A. Introduction

The Ohio landlord-tenant law for manufactured ho me parks (“mh parks”) – R.C.

Chapter 4781 (former R.C. Chapter 3733) – is discussed in Chapter XXI, below. For eviction actions concerning a manufactured home, there are four types of tenancies relative to whether R.C. Chapter 5321 and/or R.C. Chapter 4781 applies.

The first is tenants (“mh park tenants”) who reside in and lease a manufactured home that is located in a mh park and who lease the home from the park operator. The mh park tenant is a “tenant” of the park operator under R.C. 5321.01(A) and R.C .

4781.01(T) (former R.C. 3733.01(D)). Therefore, the rights and responsibilities of these tenants include the tenant rights and responsibilities under R.C. Chapter 5321 and R.C. Chapter 4781. Schwartz v. McAtee , 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 19 n.2, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986); Cooper v. Curtis, No. CA-8378, 1991 WL 147797, at *2 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 22, 1991).

The second is tenants (“non -mh park tenants”) who reside in and lease a manufactured home, but who do not lease the home from a park operator. The non - mh park tenant is a “tenant” of the owner of the home under R.C. 5321.01(A) but not R.C. 4781.01(T). Therefore, the rights and responsibilities of these tenants include the tenant rights and responsibilities under R.C. Chapter 5321 but not R.C. Chapter 4781.250 Schwartz, 22 Ohio St.3d at 19 n.2; Cooper, 1991 WL 147797, at *2.

The third is tenants (“mh park owners”) who reside in and own a manufactured home that is located in a mh park and who lease the lot on which the home is located from the park operator. With respect to the park operator, the mh park owner is an “owner” under R.C. 4781.01(U) (former R.C. 3733.01(E)), but not a “tenant” under R.C. 5321.01(A) or R.C.4781.01(T). Therefore, the rights and responsibilities of these owners include the owner rights and responsibilities under R.C. Chapter 4781, but not the tenant rights and responsibilities under R.C. Chapter 5321 or R.C. Chapter 4781.

250 Because the park operator is not the landlord of the non -mh park tenant, the park operator may not serve the non-mh park tenant with an R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of termination. Cooper
v. Curtis, No. CA-8378, 1991 WL 147797 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 22, 1991).

285 Schwartz, 22 Ohio St.3d at 19; May v. Sampsel , No. 06 -CA-000034, 2007 WL 1651851, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 4, 2007).

The fourth is tenants (“non -mh park owners”) who reside in and own a manufactured home that is not located in a mh park and who lease the lot on which the home is located. With respect to the owner of the lot, the non-mh park owner is not an “owner” under R.C. 4781.01(U) and is not a “tenant” under R.C. 5321.01(A) or R.C.

4781.01(T). Therefore, the rights and responsibilities of these owners include none of the tenant rights and responsibilities under R.C. Chapter 5321 or R.C. Chapter 4781 and none of the owner rights and responsibilities under R.C. Chapter 4781.

Eviction actions filed against tenants who fall within any of the above four categories are brought pursuant to R.C. Chapter 1923. R.C. 1923.01(C)(10),(11), as revised in 2007, clarifies the application of R.C. Chapter 1923 to manufactured homes that are not located in mh parks.

Under some agreements for the purchase of a manufactured home, the title to the home transfers at the end of a series of payments. When the buyer occupies the home pursuant to the agreement and before title transfers, the occupancy is not a landlord - tenant relationship and, upon the buyer’s breach of the agreement, the seller is not permitted to file an eviction action against the buyer. Costantino v. Lyons, No. 3774, 1987 WL 31096 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 18, 1987); Lunsford v. Hurt, No. CVG- 0500914 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., May 26, 2005). These agreements are installment contracts “governed by Title XIII of the Revised Code, Commercial Transactions.” Costantino, 1987 WL 31096, at *3. Upon the buyer’s breach of the agreement, the seller may commence a replevin action to repossess the home. Id.

### B. Conventional Defenses

In an eviction action filed against a mh park tenant or a non -mh park tenant, the potential defenses include all of the defenses that are discussed in Chapter II, above.

Insofar as those defenses are based on R.C. Chapter 5321, they are potential defenses because these tenants are “tenants” under R.C. 5321.01(A).

In an eviction action filed against a mh park owner, the potential defenses include all of the defenses that are discussed in Chapter II, above, exce pt the defenses based on R.C. 5321.17(B). Insofar as these defenses are based on R.C. Chapter 5321, they are potential defenses because R.C. Chapter 4781 includes a corresponding provision to the relevant R.C. Chapter 5321 provision.

A chart of the corresponding provisions between R.C. Chapter 4781, former R.C.

Chapter 3733, and R.C. Chapter 5321 is set forth in Section XXI.B, below. Summaries of court decisions under R.C. Chapter 4781 (and former R.C. Chapter 3733) are set forth in Section XXI.C.4, below.

The defenses based on R.C. 5321.17(B) do not apply to a mh park owner because R.C. Chapter 4781 does not contain a provision similar to R.C. 5321.17(B). Schwartz
v. McAtee, 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986); May v. Sampsel , No. 06 -CA- 000034, 2007 WL 1651851 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 4, 2007);Cooper v. Curtis, No.

CA-8378, 1991 WL 147797 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 22, 1991).

As a result, a park operator may not terminate a month -to-month tenancy without
cause. Id.; Blue Water Manor v. Reyn olds, No. CVG-2100003 (Ohio Mun., Newton Falls, Mar. 11, 2021); Oldaker v. Warsaw Park Place, LLC, No. 2020-CI-0289 (Ohio C.P., Coshocton Cty., Feb. 26, 2021); Oak Park Estates LLC v. Burnley , No. 2015 - CVG-001007 (Ohio Mun., Wayne Cty., June 29, 2015); GS Holdings Brookside Ltd.
v. Rutherford, No. 13-CVG-1535 (Ohio Mun., Clark Cty., July 10, 2013); Daniels v.

Decker, No. 08-CVG-00042, 2008 WL 5537151 (Ohio County Ct., Harrison Cty., June 10, 2008); Stillberger v. Springman, No. 90-CVG-1644, 1990 WL 692200 (Ohio Mun., Findlay, Oct. 23, 1990); Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. v. Russell, No. 7-89-CVG-336, 1989 WL 572090 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Sept. 6, 1989).

For a discussion of the cause requirement for any termination of a mh park tenancy, see Section IV.C.1, below.

### C. Additional Defenses


#### 1. Cause Requirement

The grounds on which a park operator may evict a mh park owner are limited to those set forth in R.C. 1923.02(A)(1),(2),(10),(11),(12). See Schwartz v. McAtee, 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986). 251 This establishes a cause requirement for such evictions.

R.C. 1923.02(A)(1) permits a park operator to evict a mh park owner for holding over the term of the rental agreement. R.C. Chapter 4781 (former R.C. Chapter 3733) establishes the standard for determining whether a mh park owner is a holdover tenant.

Schwartz v. McAtee, 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 19, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986). Under R.C. Chapter 251 The Schwartz decision does not refer to R.C. 1923.02(A)(11) because the Schwartz decision preceded the legislature’s enactment of that provision.

287 4781, a mh park owner is not a holdover tenant unless: (1) the owner commits a material violation of an R.C. 4781.45(former R.C. 3733.13) obligation,252 (2) the park operator gives the owner written notice of the violation in accordance with R.C.

4781.45, and (3) the owner fails to remedy the violation by the date specified in the
notice. Schwartz v. McAtee, 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 19, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986); Thompson
v. KMV II, Ltd., No. 2001-P-0125, 2003 WL 943871, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar.

7, 2003); Moonlight Mobile Home Parks, Inc. v. Eichner , No. L -93-028, 1993 WL 434584, at *4 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 29, 1993 ); M.L.R. Props., Inc. v. Baer, No.

L-85-330, 1986 WL 6703, at *6 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 13, 1986).

R.C. 1923.02(A)(2) permits a park operator to evict a mh park owner for nonpayment of rent, if the owner is leasing the lot under an oral tenancy.

R.C. 1923.02(A)(10) permits a park operator to evict a mh park owner for nonpayment of rent or for breach of the terms of the rental agreement. Schwartz v.

McAtee, 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 20, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986).

R.C. 1923.02(A)(11) permits a park operator to evi ct a mh park owner who has committed two material violations of the mh park rules (or certain health and safety
codes) and has been notified of the violations in accordance with R.C. 4781.45.Hamlet Mobile Home Park v. Sigmund , 118 Ohio App.3d 29, 691 N.E .2d 1081 (11th Dist.

1997); W. Ridge Green Co. v. Ortiz, No. 94CA-005819, 1994 WL 432162 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 10, 1994).

If the violation of a park rule is not a material violation (but rather is de minimis), it is not grounds to evict a mh park own er. Buckeye Lake Estates Mobile Home Park
v. Abel, No. 01 -CVG-01777, 2002 WL 32001271 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., 2002).

See also MHC5, LLC v. Martin , No. 2011 -CVG-03184 (Ohio Mun., Clermont Cty.,
Oct. 26, 2011). For this purpose, “[m]aterial means someth ing of real importance or great consequence.” Abel, 2002 WL 32001271, at *1.

R.C. 1923.02(A)(12) permits a park operator to evict a mh park owner for a combination of nonpayment of rent and an unoccupied home (without notice to the park operator) for thirty consecutive days. See Section I.O.4, above.

252 The legislature enacted the material violation standard in place of a materially affects health and safety standard, when it revised R.C. 3733.13, effective February 25, 1987. See Hamlet Mobile Home Park v. Sigmund , 118 Ohio App.3d 29, 691 N.E.2d 1081 (11th Dist. 1997).

Because the Schwartz decision pre-dates this 1987 revision, it refers to the materially affects health and safety standard. Id.

R.C. 1923.02(A) does not permit a park operator, who has entered into a month - to-month rental agreement with a mh park owner, to terminate the rental agreement without cause and thereupon evict the owner.253 See Section IV.B, above.

This cause requirement is summarized in Freyermuth v. Navarre Vill. Homes Austin Square, Inc., No. 1999-CA-00187, 2000 WL 94048, at *2 (Ohio App., 5th Dist.,
Jan. 24, 2000):

In particular, R.C. 3733.11(A)(2) [now R.C. 4781.40(A)(2)] explains in detail how rental agreements are to be renewed and terminated.

Explicit in the statutory section is the right of an owner to have continuous renewal of the lease. Said section provides that the one to reject the continuance of the lease inthe first part is the owner, thereby expressing a legislative philosophy that an owner can remain at his/her will if all other obligations of the owner are fulfilled. See, R.C.

3733.101 and R.C. 3733.091 [now R.C. 4781.39 and 4781.37].

#### 2. Unlawful Rent Increase

R.C. 4781.40(A) (former R.C. 3733.11(A)) requires a park operator to offer a mh park owner a written rental agreement that has an initial term of one year (or more).254 If the park operator fails to comply with this requirement, the owner’s rental agreement is deemed to have an initial term of one year. Reck v. Lucas, No. 2006-CVG-00235, 2006 WL 4399761 (Ohio Mun., Auglaize Cty., Aug. 25, 2006); Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. v. Russell , No. 7 -89-CVG-336 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Jan. 12, 1990).

R.C. 4781.40(B) (former R.C. 3733.11(B)) bars any increase in the rental fee during the term of the rental agreement. E.g., Reck v. Lucas, No. 2006-CVG-00235, 2006 WL 4399761 (Ohio Mun., Auglaize Cty., Aug. 25, 2006); Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. v. Russell , No. 7-89-CVG-336 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Jan. 12, 253 When the term of the rental agreement of an mh park owner is a month-to-month tenancy, it can be due to the absence of a mutually agreed upon l ease term, after the expiration of a one - year (or longer) lease term. See R.C. 4781.40(A)(2). E.g., Gitler v. Gerity, No. L-94-260, 1995 WL 458690 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 4, 1995); Cooper v. Curtis, No. CA-8378, 1991 WL 147797 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 22, 1991); Blue Water Manor v. Reynolds , No. CVG - 2100003 (Ohio Mun., Newton Falls, Mar. 11, 2021).

254 R.C. 4781.40(A) (former R.C. 3733.11(A)) requires a one -year lease only for the first year of the tenancy. Gitler v. Gerity, No. L-94-260, 1995 WL 458690 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug.

4, 1995).

289 1990).

If a park operator purports to increase the rent fee during the term of a rental agreement and brings an eviction action for nonpayment of the alleged rent increase, a defense to the eviction action is that the rent increase is unlawful. Id.

#### 3. Nondisclosure of Fees

A park operator is required to disclose, in writing, to each mh park owner and to each mh park tenant, all of the fees, including rental fees, that the park operator proposes to charge the owner or tenant. R.C. 4781.40(B) (former R.C. 3733.11(B)).

The park operator is required to make this disclosure before the owner (or tenant) executes a rental agreement andbefore the owner (or tenant) assumes occupancy in the mh park. Id.

The park operator is also required to provide each mh park owner and each mh park tenant with at least thirty days advance written notice of any change in these fees.

Id.

A park operator’s noncompliance with these disclosure requirements bars the park operator from assessing or collecting any fee not so disclosed ( see R.C.

4781.40(B),(H)(4) (former R.C. 3733.11(B),(H)(4)) and from evicting the owner or tenant for nonpayment of any fee not so disclosed (see R.C. 4781.40(B)). E.g., May v.

Petrick, No. 2009-CA-00031, 2010 WL 1452604 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 8, 2010);

Callahan v. Croxford, No. 2006-CVF-00621, 2007 WL 4359907 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., July 31, 2007); Reck v. Lucas, No. 2006-CVG-00235, 2006 WL 4399761 (Ohio Mun., Auglaize Cty., Aug. 25, 2006);Bennett v. Croxford, No. 96-CVH-000488, 1997 WL 810044 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., Apr. 18, 1997); Evangelinos v. Nixon, No. 95- CVG-00076, 1997 WL 810049 (Ohio County Ct., Belmont C ty., Jan. 11, 1997). For additional court decisions on this issue, see Section XXI.C.4.d (disclosure of fee/rent), below.

In an eviction action, any alleged failure to meet this disclosure requirement is an affirmative defense for which the mh park owner or mh park tenant has the burden of
proof. Harrison Parks, Inc. v. Bozarth, No. 20147, 2004 WL 917333 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 30, 2004); Dearwester’s Gem City Estates v. Nelson, No. 14320, 1994 WL 681104 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 7, 1994).

#### 4. Sale/Purchase of the Home

A park operator may not evict a mh park owner based on a sale of the manufactured
home. R.C. 4781.40(H)(2) (former R.C. 3733.11(H)(2)). E.g., Freyermuth v. Navarre
Vill. Homes Austin Square, Inc. , No. 1999 -CA-00187, 2000 WL 94048 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 24, 2000); Cmty. Gardens Park & Sales, Inc. v. Roe, No. M8109-CVG- 027176 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 6, 1981); Mentor Trailer Park, Inc. v. Clark,
No. 79-CVG-492 (Ohio Mun., Mentor, June 7, 1979).

A park operator may not evict a purchaser of a manufactured home, located in the mh park, based on the seller’s refusal to hire the park operator as the agent for the sale.

R.C. 4781.40(F),(K) (former R.C. 3733.11(F),(K)). E.g., Jameson v. Sommer’s Mobile Home Sales, Inc., No. 83769 (Ohio C.P., Lorain Cty., May 13, 1980).

A park operator may not unreasonably refuse to enter into a rental agreement with a purchaser of a manufactured home, located within the mh park. R.C. 4781.40(H)(3) (former R.C. 3733.11(H)(3)). E.g., Kantner v. Gibson, No. 2-07-17, 2007 WL 414177 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Nov. 26, 2007); Czerwonko v. Sahara Mobile Home Park & Sales, Inc., 137 Ohio App.3d 266, 738 N.E.2d 461 (11th Dist. 2000);White v. Superior Mobile Homes, Inc., No. 98-T-0011, 1999 WL 1320188 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec.

10, 1999); Santilla v. Sahara Mobile Home Park & Sales , No. 93 -L-028, 1993 WL 407233 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993). For additional court decisions on this issue, see Section XXI.C.4.d (sale of home), below.

291

## V. Ohio Landlord-Tenant Act,

General Background

### A. Remedial

“In 1974, the General Assembly enacted R.C. Chapter 5321, which embodies what is commonly known as the Ohio Landlord -Tenant Act.” Vardeman v. Llewellyn, 17 Ohio St.3d 24, 26, 476 N.E.2d 1038 (1985). The Act is Ohio’s adaptation of the Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act. Ricciardo v. Weber , No. CA -3452, 1989 WL 155719, at *5 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 22, 1989).

R.C. Chapter 5321 is a remedial statute. Anderson v. Ballard , No. L -10-1007, 2010 WL 3292685, ¶ 39 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2010); Howard v. Simon, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 15 -16, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist. 1984); Reith v. Skruck , 71 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 2, 653 N.E.2d 755 (Ohio Mun., Wadsworth, 1995).See also Klein v. Moutz, 118 Ohio St.3d 256, 258, 888 N.E.2d 404 (2008) (R.C. 5321.16 is remedial).

Therefore, it must be liberally construed in order to promote its object and assist the parties in attaining justice. See R.C. 1.11. This means that “the Act should be read liberally in favor of tenants’ rights.” LaCourse v. Fleitz, 28 Ohio St.3d 209, 212, 503 N.E.2d 159 (1986).

### B. Purpose

R.C. Chapter 5321 is the legislature’s response to “the greater power traditionally held by the landlord.” Pool v. Insignia Residential Group, 136 Ohio App.3d 266, 270, 736 N.E.2d 507 (1st Dist. 1999). Accord Ricciardo v. Weber, No. CA-3452, 1989 WL 155719, at *5 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 22, 1989).

“[T]he Act was an attempt to balance the competing interests of landlords and tenants.” Shroades v. Rental Homes, Inc. , 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 25, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981). Accord Mann v. Northgate Investors, L.L.C. , 138 Ohio St.3d 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶ 10 (2014); Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Mountjoy, 154 N.E.3d 141, 2020-Ohio-2772, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., 2020); Cont’l Enters., Ltd. v. Franklin, No. 102899, 2016 WL 2941209, ¶ 30 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 19, 2016);Ricciardo v. Weber, No. CA- 3452, 1989 WL 155719, at *5 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 22, 1989); King v. Neufeld,
No. WD-85-14, 1986 WL 1296, at *4 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 31, 1986).

“The intent behind the Landlord -Tenant Act was to ensure equitable dealings between landlords and tenants.” Pool v. Insignia Residential Group, 136 Ohio App.3d 266, 270, 736 N.E.2d 507 (1st Dist. 1999).

To achieve this balance and equity, the legislature “intended ... to afford greater protection and remedies to tenants.” King v. Neufeld, No. WD-85-14, 1986 WL 1296, at *4 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 31, 1986).Accord Conner v. Conner, 114 N.E.3d 281, 2018-Ohio-2698, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 20, 2018) ( “enacted R.C. Chapter 5321 … to clarify and broaden tenants’ common -law rights”); Anderson v. Ballard ,
No. L -10-1007, 2010 WL 3292685, ¶ 39 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2010) (“intended to provide tenants with greater rights than existed for their protection at common law”); Mullins v. Grosz, No. 10AP-23, 2010 WL 3244139, ¶ 23 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Aug. 17, 2010) (“attempt to clarify and broaden tenants’ rights”);Brady v.

Koehnke, No. C-930240, 1994 WL 481758, at *2 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept. 7, 1994) (“attempt to clarify and broaden tenants’ rights”);Ricciardo v. Weber, No. CA-3452, 1989 WL 155719, at *5 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 22, 1989) (“overall thrust of the act is to define and establish remedies for a tenant”);Howard v. Simon, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 15-16, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist. 1984) (“intended to provide tenants with greater rights”).

### C. Effect on Common Law

“The thrust of the new act [R.C. Chapter 5321] is to abrogate t he Ohio common law of caveat emptorand independent covenants and interpret residential leases in light of modern contract law.” Ricciardo v. Weber, No. CA-3452, 1989 WL 155719, at *5 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 22, 1989) (quoting Comments, Ohio Residential Landlord Tenant Act, 8 Akron L. Rev. 519, 520-21 (1975)).

“Ohio’s Landlords and Tenants Act imposes duties on landlords which were absent at common law.” Miller v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 224, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989).

Accord Shroades v. Rental Home s, Inc., 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 21 -22, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981).

It also provides tenants with remedies that were absent at common law. Shroades, 68 Ohio St.2d at 21-25.

However, “neither R.C. Chapter 5321 nor Shroades eliminated a tenant’s right to seek common law remedies.” Winston v. Wolery, No. 96-CA-2442, 1997 WL 28535, at *2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Jan. 14, 1997).

“[T]he new remedies given tenants in R.C. Chapter 5321 are intended to be preventive and supplemental to other remedial measures.” Shroades v. Rental Homes, 293 Inc., 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 25, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981). Accord Ayres v. Simmons, No. 93- CA-827, 1993 WL 405489 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993) (implied covenant of quiet enjoyment under common law); Meacham v. Miller , 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992) (tort claims under common law); Cent. Mut. Ins. Co. v.

Faller, 71 Ohio Misc.2d 58, 654 N.E.2d 213 (Ohio C.P., Licking Cty., 1995) (contract claim for damages).

To find that the Act abrogates a longstanding rule of common law , a court must find an intent to do so “expressly declared by the legislature or necessarily implied in the language of the statute.” LaCourse v. Fleitz, 28 Ohio St.3d 209, 212, 503 N.E.2d 159 (1986). Applying this standard, court decisions have found th at the Act does not abrogate certain rules of common law under which a landlord is relieved of a duty.

E.g., LaCourse v. Fleitz, 28 Ohio St.3d 209, 212, 503 N.E.2d 159 (1986) (landlord is not obligated to keep common areas clear of natural accumulations o f ice and snow);

Plush v. Nat’l Church Residences, No. L -89-293, 1990 WL 106437 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., July 27, 1990) (landlord is not obligated to remove natural accumulations of leaves from common areas). See Section VII.C.4, below.

### D. Effect on Local Ordinances

Municipalities are not permitted to enact any ordinance that is in conflict with R.C.

Chapter 5321. R.C. 5321.19.255 Three court decisions finding ordinances not to be in conflict with R.C. Chapter 5321 are summarized below.
(1) Hiznay v. Boardman Twp. , No. 201 5-MA-0122, 201 7 WL 1193839 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar. 29 , 201 7) (township resolution: landlord rental unit registration and annual fee; minimum property maintenance standards for unit interior, for landlords and tenants, with enforcement 255 Insofar as R.C. 5321.19 purports to prohibit any ordinance “ that regulates the rights and obligations of parties to a rental agreement that are regulated by this chapter,” regardless of whether the ordinance conflicts with R.C. Chapter 5321, R.C. 5321.19 seems to violate Article XVIII, Section 3 of the Ohio Constitution (home rule provision). See State ex rel. Morrison v.

Beck Energy Corp., 143 Ohio St.3d 271, 37 N.E.3d 128, ¶ 15 (2015) (“a municipal ordinance must yield to a state statute if (1) the ordinance is an exercise of the police power, rather than local self-government, (2) the statute is a general law, and (3) the ordinance is in conflict with the statute”); City of Cleveland v. State , 138 Ohio St.3d 232, 5 N.E.3d 644, ¶ 16 (2014) (in striking down a statutory provision, held that the provision “violates the third prong of the Canton test by purporting to limit legislative power of a municipal corporation to set police, sanitary, or similar regulations”).

by civil fines).
(2) Mariemont Apt. Ass’n v. Vill. of Mariemont, No. C-050986, 2007 WL 120727 (Ohio App., 1st Dist. , Jan. 19 , 20 07) (ordinance: landlord registration and fee, along with inspection for compliance with Village’s building and zoning codes; and submission of names of tenants).
(3) Cotrell v. City of Piqua, No. 2000-CA-44, 2001 WL 62811 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 26, 2001) (ordinance: city may order landlords to abate nuisances, even those caused by tenants, and if a landlord fails to abate timely the city may abate and assess the costs to the landlord).
(4) Prof’l Invs. of Am. v. McCormick, 14 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 469 N.E.2d 1357 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., 1984) (ordinance: for buildings converted to condo miniums, certain tenants may terminate their lease without penalty or charge, upon forty-five days written notice).

295

## VI. Ohio Landlord-Tenant Act,

Applicable Tenancies

### A. Overview

R.C. Chapter 5321 applies to residential tenancies. R.C. 5321.01. See also Mann
v. Northgate Investors, L.L.C. , 138 Ohio St.3d 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶ 8 (2014);

Vardeman v. Llewellyn, 17 Ohio St.3d 24, 26, 476 N.E.2d 1038 (1985); Shroades v.

Rental Homes, Inc., 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 21-22, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981).

It does not apply to commercial tenancies. Maggiore v. Kovach, 101 Ohio St.3d 184, 187-88, 803 N.E.2d 790 (2004); Hendrix v. Eighth & Walnut Corp., 1 Ohio St.3d 205, 208, 438 N.E.2d 1149 (1982).

It establishes rights and responsibilities of landlords and tenants who are parties to a rental agreement for residential premises. R.C. 5321.01.See also Mann v. Northgate Investors, L.L.C. , 138 Ohio St.3d 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶ 8 (2014); Vardeman v.

Llewellyn, 17 Ohio St.3d 24, 26, 476 N.E.2d 1038 (1985); Shroades v. Rental Homes, Inc., 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 21-22, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981).

The definition and scope of the terms rental agreement, tenant, landlord, and residential premises are discussed, in turn, in Sections VI.B-F, below.

### B. Rental Agreement

A rental agreement is any agreement, written or oral, that establishes or modifies the terms concerning the use and occupancy of residential premises by one of the
parties. R.C. 5321.01(D).

For discussions on construi ng written rental agreements and on the rent prerequisite, see Sections VII.A and VII.F.3, below, respectively.

Four common residential occupancies that are not occupancies under a rental agreement are:
(1) An occupancy agreement in a cooperative. Kohler v. Snow Vill., Inc., 16 Ohio App.3d 350, 475 N.E.2d 1298 (8th Dist. 1984).
(2) An occupancy under a divorce decree that provides that one party shall retain, for a specified time, exclusive right to possession of a house that both parties owned prior to the marriage dissolution.256 Steiger v. Mack,
No. 1-81-25, 1981 WL 6743 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Dec. 17, 1981) (ex- husband not liable under R.C. 5321.15).
(3) An occupancy of a former mortgagor who remains in possession after a foreclosure action, without establishing a landlord-tenant relationship with the new title holder. 257 Adm’r of Veterans Affairs v. Jackson, 41 Ohio App.3d 274, 535 N.E.2d 369 (9th Dist. 1987).
(4) A life estate, even if it is designated a life lease.Evans v. Willis, No. 13- CA-120, 2014 WL 1759190 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 22, 2014).

Also, an option to purchase agreement is not a rental agreement, even if the option to purchase is an addendum to a rental agreement. Delker v. Kerr, No. L-96-110, 1996 WL 532319 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 20, 1996). See also Stacy v. Zupp, No. CA- 9566, 1994 WL 369899 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 20, 1994).

Likewise, in some circumstances, an addendum to a purchase agreement that permits the purchasers to take possession of the premises prior to closing is not a rental
agreement. E.g., Kott Enters., Ltd. v. McClain , No. L -15-1086, 2016 WL 530886 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 29, 2016).

### C. Tenant

The definition of tenant under Ohio law is comprised of four elements, three established by R.C. 5321.01(A) and one established by common law.

R.C. 5321.01(A) defines a tenant as “a person entitled under a rental agreement to the use and occupancy of residential premises to the exclusion of others.” Under this statutory definition, an occupant is a tenant only if:

256 Nonetheless, if an ex -husband (or ex -wife) is entitled to possession of a house under the terms of a final divorce decree and the ex -wife (or ex-husband) wrongfully retains possession of the house, the ex-husband (or ex-wife) may file an eviction action against the ex-wife (or ex-
husband) under R.C. 1923.02(A)(5). Eisler v. Rudy , No. 1466, 1989 WL 700 66 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 23, 1989).

But, prior to the entry of a final divorce decree, a municipal court lacks jurisdiction to hear an eviction action in which a husband (or wife) seeks to evict his wife (or her husband) from marital property. See Section I.L.4, above.

257 For a discussion on the occupancy of a tenant who remains in possession after a foreclosure sale, see Section XXII.C, below.

297
(1) The occupancy is under a rental agreement (see Section VI.B, above);
(2) The occupancy is of residential premises (see Section VI.E, below);
(3) The occupant has exclusive use of the dwelling unit.

The fourth element of the definition of tenant, established by common law, is: the occupant must pay rent in consideration for the right to occupy the dwelling unit. 258 See Section VII.F.3, below.

“An individual who lives in a residence with another without a rental agreement and without the payment of rent is not a tenant ...” Stone v. Cazeau , No. 07 -CA- 009164, 2007 WL 4146777, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 26, 2007). Accord Lee v.

Wallace, 186 Ohio App.3d 18, 926 N.E.2d 328 (8th Dist. 2010);Amick v. Sickles, 177 Ohio App.3d 337, 894 N.E.2d 733 (4th Dist. 2008).

The exclusive use requirement reflects the common law on lease agreements for real property. Cuvier Press Club v. Fourth & Race St. Assocs., Ltd. , 1 Ohio App.3d 30, 34, 439 N.E.2d 443 (1st Dist. 1981). The indispensable elements of such agreements are the rights to exclusive possession of a certain quantity of land for a term certain. Id.

The exclusive use requirement also reflects the common law distinction between tenant and lodger: “‘The tenant is put into the exclusive possession of his rooms, while the boarder or lodger has merely the use of them without the actual or exclusive possession ....’” Linwood Park Co. v. Van Dusen, 63 Ohio St. 183, 200, 58 N.E. 576
(1900) (quoting 1 McAdam, Landlord and Tenant 621). Accord Branham v. Fordyce, 103 Ohio App. 379, 145 N.E.2d 471 (2nd Dist. 1957) (finding the occupant to be a tenant, not a lodger, and that th e landlord’s right to make weekly inspections was not inconsistent with a landlord -tenant relationship); Jamieson v. Harris , No. 85 -CV- 39131 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Apr. 14, 1986).

The exclusive use requirement has been a significant factor in determining whether an occupancy at any particular transitional housing facility is a landlord -tenant
relationship. Three courts that found (and relied on) the absence of the requisite exclusive use, in determining that the occupants of particular transitional hou sing facilities were not tenants, are: Parker v. Salvation Army, 971 N.E.2d 995, 2012-Ohio- 2069 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2012); Coyne v. YWCA of Greater Toledo, No. CVG-06- 15975 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., Nov. 7, 2006) (preliminary injunction denied);

258 For a discussion on what constitutes rent, see Sections VII.F.1,2, below.

Coleman v. Faith Mission , No. 01 -CVH-10988 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty., June 17,
2002) (preliminary injunction denied). Each of these courts based its finding on the right of the owner, under the occupancy agreement, to inspect the dwelling unit at any time.259 Another reasonable interpretation of such provisions is that, in granting an unlimited right of entry (without notice): the provision does not convert what otherwise would be a rental agreement into a non -landlord-tenant occupancy agreement; rather, it constitutes a provision in a rental agreement that is an unlawful waiver ( see R.C.

5321.13) of the R.C. 5321.04(A)(8),(B) requirements that govern a landlord’s entry into the dwelling unit.

A roommate’s occupancy status generally falls under one of three categories. First, a roommate may be a co -tenant, based on a rental agreement among the tenant, the roommate, and the landlord. Second, a roommate may be a subtenant of the tenant, based on a rental agreement between the tenant and the subtenant for the roommate’s exclusive use of a bedroom (and any additional specified portion) of the residential
premises. Third, a roommate may be a guest or licensee of the tenant, based on the absence of the roommate’s obligation to pay rent (or right to the exclus ive use of the bedroom).

When a roommate is a co-tenant, the exclusive use requirement refers to a tenant’s rights in relation to the public, not in relation to the roommate. See Torbeck v.

Chamberlain, 138 Or. App. 446, 452, 910 P.2d 389 (1996).

Whether a live-in caretaker meets the definition of tenant depends on the particular
facts. Lee v. Wallace, 186 Ohio App.3d 18, 926 N.E.2d 328 (8th Dist. 2010). In Lee, the live-in caretaker, who occupied a room at the residential premises, was not a tenant because she: (1) did not pay rent (in money or services); (2) did not execute a rental agreement; (3) did not have exclusive use of the room; (4) did not identify the room’s address as her residence on any account or license; and (5) maintained another residential room elsewhere.

When an initial lease is not terminated (even though the tenant has vacated) and the landlord purports to execute a second lease with a third party for the remainder of the term of the initial lease, the occupant under the purported second lease is not a tenant under R.C. 5321.01(A). Tillimon v. Timmons, 72 N.E.3d 1153, 2016-Ohio-7424 259 The Coyne and Coleman decisions also based their determinations on the conclusory statements in the occupancy agreements that declared that the agreement is not a lease or a landlord-tenant agreement.

299 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 21, 2016) (not a tenant and thus no R.C. 5321.05 liability).

Under the initial lease, the landlord has granted exclusive use of the dwelling unit to the tenant. Id. Therefore, during the term of the initial lease, the landlord has no right of possession to grant to a third party. Id.

A potential purchaser under a land contract with an option to purchase was not a tenant, where the option required the potential purchaser to make monthly payments, secure financing, and repair the uninhabitable property.Bond v. Sharrock, No. 07-CA- 65, 2008 WL 3132929 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 6, 2008).

### D. Landlord

A landlord is the owner, lessor or sublessor of residential premises, or any person that the owner, lessor or sublessor authorizes to manage the premises or to receive rent from a tenant under a rental agreement. R.C. 5321.01(B).

When an owner (or lessor or sublessor), for a fee , authorizes a person to manage the residential premises, the person is a real estate broker under R.C. 4735.01. As real estate brokers, such persons are permitted to manage the residential premises only if they are licensed real estate brokers. R.C. 4735.02.

When a vendee of a land installment contract (see Chapter XXIII, below) leases the property to a third-party, the vendor is not a landlord of the vendee’s tenant.Baraby
v. Swords, 166 Ohio App.3d 527, 851 N.E.2d 559 (3rd Dist. 2006). The vendorretains only bare legal title and reserves only those rights of entry and control as necessary to preserve the integrity of the building. Id.

### E. Residential Premises

Residential premises are “a dwelling unit for residential use and occupancy and the structur e of which it is a part, the facilities and appurtenances therein, and the grounds, areas, and facilities for the use of tenants generally or the use of which is promised the tenant.” R.C. 5321.01(C).

A dwelling unit is “a structure or the part of a struc ture that is used as a home, residence, or sleeping place by one person who maintains a household or by two or more persons who maintain a common household.” R.C. 5321.01(D).

“The yard of a single-family home is considered to be part of the tenant’s property to possess and control.” Good v. Murd, No. L-13-1235, 2014 WL 2170302,¶ 10 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 23, 2014). Accord Brown v. Terrell, 114 N.E.3d 783, 2018-Ohio- 2503, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., 2018).

The common areas of residential premises “generally include areas such as sidewalks, parking lots, foyers, hallways ... which [are] reasonably necessary to the use and enjoyment of the leased property.” Hau v. Gill , No. 98 -CA-007061, 1999 WL 492633, at *5 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 14, 1999). Accord Burrell v. Iwenofu , No.

81230, 2003 WL 1090635, ¶¶ 15,16 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 13, 2003). They do not include “public sidewalks abutting the property.”Ryder v. McGlone’s Rentals, No.

3-09-02, 2009 WL 1664777, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., June 15, 2009).

Residential premises include only those portions of the premises that are for the use of tenants. For example, residential premises do not include grounds tha t are left in a wild and natural state to serve as a privacy screen. Kueber v. Haas , 47 Ohio App.3d 62, 546 N.E.2d 1351 (1st Dist. 1988). Likewise, residential premises do not include a space that is under renovation and behind a door marked “Danger-Keep Out.” Lukehart v. W. Ninth St. Partners , 137 Ohio App.3d 361, 738 N.E.2d 839 (8th Dist.

2000).

Residential premises include dwelling units that a college owns or operates, but student tenants who occupy those dwelling units are denied two basic tenant ri ghts under R.C. Chapter 5321. R.C. 5321.01(H), 5321.031, 5321.07(D). First, these student tenants may not rent deposit or use the other R.C. 5321.07 tenant remedies (see Chapter VIII, below). R.C. 5321.07(D). Second, a college may terminate the rental agreement of a student tenant and, without filing an eviction action, require the student tenant to vacate the dwelling unit, so long as the college follows its notice and hearing
procedures. R.C. 5321.031. See also R.C. 1923.02(D).

R.C. 5321.01(C) speci fies ten types of properties or occupancies that are not residential premises for R.C. Chapter 5321 purposes. They are prisons, hospitals, transient occupancies in hotels and other similar facilities, certain elementary and secondary boarding schools, orp hanages, certain farm residences, certain agriculture labor camps, occupancies by an owner of a condominium unit, certain occupancies in certain licensed SRO facilities, and transient occupancies in non -profit emergency
shelters. R.C. 5321.01(C)(1)-(10).

Under the R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) exception, residential premises do not include the rental of a vacation house in a recreational area for a short -term, such as four days.

Tomasko v. Sohnly , No. 15CAE -10-0078, 2016 WL 1643036 (Ohio App., 5th Dist.,
Apr. 21, 2016).

The R.C. 5321.01(C)(9)(a)(ii) exception, relative to shelters for homeless persons, is not limited to facilities that only take persons literally from the streets. Coyne v.

301 YWCA of Greater Toledo, No. CVG-06-15975 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., Nov.

7, 2006) (preliminary injunction denied).

For a discussion on the term “transient occupancy,” for R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) and R.C. 5321.01(C)(10) purposes, see Section VI.F., below.

### F. Transient Occupancy, for Residential

Premises Exclusions

#### 1. Introduction

Both R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) and R.C. 5321.01(C)(10) use the term “transient occupancy” in their respective exclusions from the definition of residential premises.

R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) provides that “residential premises” do not include “tourist homes, hotels, motels, recreational vehicle parks, recreational camps, combined park- camps, and other similar facilities where circumstances indicate a transient occupancy.” R.C. 5321.01(C)(10) provides that “residential premises” do not include “emergency shelters ... for persons whose circumstances indicate a transient occupancy, including homeless people, victims of domestic violence, and juvenile runaways.” However, R.C. Chapter 5321 does not define or establish criteria for determining the meaning of the term transient occupancy, for purposes of R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) or R.C. 5321.01(C)(10). This Section VI.F analyzes the relevant law (including existing case law) for making that determination.

#### 2. Application of the Rule of Statutory Construction

for the Use of Identical Words in Same Statute A well-established general rule of statutory construction is that identical words used in different parts of the same statute are presumed to have the same meaning (“the general presumption”). Nijhawan v. Holder , 557 U. S. 29, 38 -39 (200 9); Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner & Smith v. Dabit, 547 U.S. 71, 86 (2006);Henry v. Trs. of Perry Twp., 48 Ohio St. 671, 30 N.E.2d 1122 (1891).

The general presumption yields only when there is “such variation in the connection in which the words are used as reasonably to warrant the conclusion that they were employed in different parts of the act with different intent.” Envtl. Defense
v. Duke Energy Corp., 549 U.S. 561, 574 (2007). Accord Gen. Dynamics Land Sys.,
Inc. v. Cline, 540 U.S. 581, 595 (2004). See also Henry v. Trs. of Perry Twp., 48 Ohio
St. 671, 30 N.E. 1122 (1891).

In both R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) and R.C. 5321.01(C)(10), the legislature used the exact same phrase – “circumstances indicate a transient occupancy;” and the shared phrase is used for precisely the same purpose – to establish a short -term occupancy requirement for exclusions from the definition of residential premises.

Given the common purpose of the shared “transient occupancy” phrase, the general presumption should apply to its use in R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) and R.C. 5321.01(C)(10).

Higdon v. Sign of the Cross, 126 Ohio Misc.2d 84, 803 N.E.2d 876, ¶ 14 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 2003). There is no reasonable basis for concluding that the legislature employed the shared “transient occup ancy” phrase with a different intent in these
subsections. Id.

Therefore, under the general presumption, the definition of the term “transient occupancy” should be the same for R.C. 5321.01(C)(10) purposes as it is for R.C.

5321.01(C)(3) purposes. Id.

The decision inParker v. Salvation Army, 971 N.E.2d 995, 2012-Ohio-2069 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2012), erroneously rejects the foregoing analysis and conclusion. It recognizes the general presumption, but erroneously determines that the exception to the general presumption applies.

The Parker court presents two grounds for its determination, each of which is without merit. The first ground is that, in determining the meaning of transient occupancy for R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) purposes, courts have relied oncode sections related to certain hotel taxes and those code sections are not relevant to R.C. 5321.01(C)(10).

The first ground is without merit because the guidance of these other code sections, relative to the legislature’s intent in using the phrase “transient occupancy,” does not indicate in any fashion that the legislature intended the phrase to have a different meaning in R.C. 5321.01(C)(10) than in R.C. 5321.01(C)(3). The second ground is that the Higdon decision’s ruling on this issue is not appli cable to the Parker case because of differences between the Higdon program and the Parker program. This second ground is without merit because these program differences are irrelevant to the determination of the legislature’s intent in using (and hence the meaning of) the term “transient occupancy,” which is a length of time irrespective of the program and is a standard to be applied to the program.

303

#### 3. Transient Occupancy Means up to Thirty Days

Since R.C. Chapter 5321 does not define or establish criter ia for determining the meaning of the term transient occupancy in R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) or R.C.

5321.01(C)(10), courts are required to consider other relevant factors for that determination.

Five relevant factors, which are summarized below, support the con clusion that, for both R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) and 5321.01(C)(10), the term transient occupancy means up to one month.

Whether any of these five factors relates more directly to R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) than to R.C. 5321.01(C)(10) is immaterial. The general presum ption of statutory construction, relative to the legislature’s use of identical words in different parts of the same statute (“the general presumption”), applies to the term transient occupancy in R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) and R.C. 5321.01(C)(10). See Section VI.F.2, above. The general presumption compels the conclusion that, if the term transient occupancy in R.C.

5321.01(C)(3) means an occupancy up to thirty days, the term transient occupancy in R.C. 5321.01(C)(10) also means an occupancy up to thirty days. Higdon v. Sign of the Cross, 126 Ohio Misc.2d 84, 803 N.E.2d 876, ¶ 14 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 2003).

The first factor is, with respect to the hotels and motels to which the R.C.

5321.01(C)(3) exclusion applies, the “common understanding of the length of occupancy in such facilities is that it is measured in days or weeks.”Higdon v. Sign of the Cross, 126 Ohio Misc.2d 84, 803 N.E.2d 876, ¶ 14 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 2003). Accord Alter v. Tucker Lake of the Woods of Farmersville, Inc. , No. 04-CV- 00259, 2004 WL 5255401 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., Oct. 8, 2004).

The second factor is two Ohio hotel tax provisions that are consistent with this common understanding. One is the R.C. 5739.01(N) sales tax definition of transient guests: “persons occupying a room or rooms for sleeping accommodations for less than thirty consecutive days.”260 The other is the R.C. 3731.01(A)(2) definition of transient hotel: certain hotels and motels that “are offered for pay to transient guests for a period of thirty days or less.” Since the R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) exclusion applies to transient occupancies in ahotel, 260 The R.C. 5739.01(N) definition of transient guest is used in connection w ith the sales tax that is levied on all transactions in which lodging by a hotel is furnished to transient guests.

R.C. 5739.01(B)(2).

it is reasonable to infer that the legislature intended the R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) definition of transient occupancy to be consistent with the R.C. 5739.01(N) definition of transient guests in a hotel and the R.C. 3731.01(A)(2) definition of a transient hotel. Therefore, an occupancy of a dwelling unit for more than thirty consecutive days is a significant factor for finding a non -transient occupancy under R.C. 5321.01(C)(3). But cf.

Franklinton Coalition v. Open -Shelter, Inc., 13 Ohio App.3d 399, 469 N.E.2d 861 (10th Dist. 1983).

The third factor is the length of a typical residential rental agreement. R.C.

5321.01(C)(3) and R.C. 5321.01(C)(10) rely on the transient nature of the occupancy to exclude it from the definition of residential premises and thus residential rental
agreements. Therefore, it is reasonable to construe the term transient occupancy, in these statutory provisions, as a time period significantly less than the length of a typical residential rental agreement.

The length of a typica l residential rental agreement is one year or less. David Genesove, The Nominal Rigidity of Apartment Rents, 85 Rev. Econ. & Statistics 844, 849 (Nov. 2003) (length of residential leases in the United States: 15.4% – no lease/ month- to-month; 40% – less than one year; 43% – one year; 2.3% – more than one year).

The fourth factor, which courts in other jurisdictions have relied on when construing transient occupancy provisions in their residential landlord tenant law, is the definition of transient guests in 42 U.S.C. § 2000a(b)(1). E.g., Lyons v. Kamhoot, 281 Or. 615, 620, 575 P.2d 1389 (1978) (transient occupancy where one -month stay and then returning to home, after fire repair); City of Evanston v. O’Leary , 244 Ill.

App.3d 190, 194 -96, 614 N.E.2d 114 (1993) (not transient occupancy where three - month stay and resident without alternative housing). Lyons and City of Evanston consider the resident’s intent for relatively permanent housing, based on the length of the stay and whether the resident will be returning to an existing home. They rely on the § 2000a(b)(1) transient occupancy definition, for the length of stay criterion, and court decisions have “generally accepted [it] as meaning short -term guests who stay from one day to a few weeks but whodo not reside permanently in the establishment.” Lyons v. Kamhoot, 281 Or. 615, 620, 575 P.2d 1389 (1978). Cf. Woods v. Foster, 884

### F. Supp. 1169, 1173 -74 (N.D. Ill. 1995) (a shelter for otherwise homeless families

meets the definition of dwelling unitunder the federal Fair Housing Act (“FHA”), even though their occupancies are limited to 120 days); Intermountain Fair Hous. Counsel
v. Boise Rescue Mission Ministries, 717 F. Supp.2d 1101, 1109-12 (D. Idaho 2010) (a 305 shelter for otherwise homeless persons does not meet the FHA definition of dwelling unit because, unlike Woods, the occupancies are limited to 17 consecutive nights).

The fifth factor is the common law criteria for distinguishing among a tenant, a lodger, and a guest. See, e.g., Baker v. Doubletree, Inc., No. 81-CA-38, 1982 WL 4867 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 19, 1982).

Under common law, this distinction is determined by a consideration of all the surrounding facts and circumstances. Baker, 1982 WL 4867, at *2. The factors supporting a tenancy include: (1) the occupant pays a fixed sum at weekly or monthly intervals (in contrast to daily intervals),261 (2) the occupancy is for a specified time (in contrast to a day -to-day indefinite basis), (3) the dwelling unit is the occupant’s sole place of occupancy, (4) the occupant has exclusive use of the dwelling unit, (5) the occupant is responsible for cleaning the dwelling unit, (6) there are facilities within the dwelling unit to prepare meals ( e.g., stove, refrigerator), (7) the owner is not responsible for providing any meals to the occupant, (8) there are extensive residential furnishings within the dwelling unit, and (9) there are numerous possessions of the occupant within the dwelling unit. Baker v. Doubletree, Inc., No. 81-CA-38, 1982 WL 4867 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 19, 1982); Gen’l Motors Acceptance Corp. v.

Kehnert, 30 Ohio Law Abs. 270, 72 N.E.2d 793 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1939).

In Howard v. Shangri-La Inn, No. 48219, 1984 WL 195551 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Nov. 29, 1984), the court appli ed these factors in reviewing the R.C. 5321.01(C)(3) transient occupancy issue in an R.C. 5321.15 claim. It reversed the summary judgment for the owner because reasonable minds could reach different conclusions on the issue.

The support for a transient o ccupancy was the evidence that the occupant signed the equivalent of a hotel register and paid hotel taxes. The support for a non -transient occupancy was the evidence that: (1) the occupant paid rent monthly, paid a damage deposit, and paid Ohio Bell dire ctly for the telephone service in the room, (2) the occupant partially furnished the room, and (3) the occupant (and other seventh and eighth floor occupants) did not receive a myriad of hotel type services (e.g., linens and maid service) provided all other occupants of the hotel.

The decision in Parker v. Salvation Army, 971 N.E.2d 995, 2012-Ohio-2069 (Ohio 261 The rate and terms for payment will not, per se, determine whether the relationship is a
tenancy. Baker v. Doubletree, Inc., No. 81-CA-38, 1982 WL 4867 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb.

19, 1982); New S. Hotel Co. v. Kingston , 23 Ohio Law Abs. 115, 72 N.E.2d 782 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 1939).

App., 8th Dist., 2012), erroneously rejects the foregoing analysis and conclusion. It erroneously determines that the general presumption does not app ly (see Section VI.F.2, above) and then erroneously determines that the nine-month occupancies at the “Project Share” transitional housing facility meet the R.C. 5321.01(C)(10) “transient occupancy” requirement.

The Parker court presents two basic grounds in support of its transient occupancy determination, each of which is without merit. The first ground is that the nature of the facility suggests a transient occupancy. The second ground is that the expectation of the occupants is that the stay is temporary.

Neither ground has merit for the reasons discussed in detail in the Reply Brief of Appellant, in theParker appeal. Three examples of those reasons are as follows. First, the court’s reliance on the dwelling unit being furnished and without cooking facilities is misplaced because tenants often lease furnished dwelling units and a dwelling unit may be (and, in this case, was) simply a “sleeping place” ( see R.C. 5321.01(F)).

Second, the court’s reliance on the occupant’s understanding that his nine-month stay “was temporary” is misplaced because (1) every tenant understands that the tenant’s stay is limited to the lease term, (2) virtually all residential leases are month-to-month or one-year terms, and (3) therefore, the nine -month occupancy at th e Project Share facility is no more temporary than the typical residential tenancy. Third, the court’s reliance on the social service requirements for the occupants is misplaced because rental agreement provisions that require the tenant to participate i n certain social service programs are not unusual (see, e.g., 24 C.F.R. § 582.315(b) (Shelter Plus Care rental subsidy program)).

### G. Employee Tenant

An employee may lease and reside in residential premises pursuant to a contract of employment (“employee tenant”).262 R.C. Chapter 5321 has modified the common law rules regarding employee tenants.

Under common law, an employee tenant is either a servant or a tenant. The 262 A related but different circumstance is a tenant who, during the tenancy, accepts a n employment position (e.g., janitor) with the landlord, the compensation for which is a reduction or elimination of the tenant’s rental obligation. E.g., Kearny Court Assocs. v. Spence, 262 N.J.
Super. 241, 620 A.2d 1056 (1993); Mayer v. Norton, 62 Misc.2d 887, 310 N.Y.S.2d 576 (Civ.
Ct. 1970). In this circumstance, the employment and the tenancy are a dual relationship, which means that a termination of the employment does not terminate the tenancy. Id.

307 employee tenant is a servant, and thus not a tenant, where the occupancy is connected with, or is required for the necessary or better performance of, the employment
services. Annotation, Nature of Occupancy of Person Occupying Premises of Employer as Part of Compensation, 39 A.L.R. 1145, 1145-46 (1925).

Under common law, an employee tenant’s (servant’s or tenant’s) right to possession of the premises terminates as soon as the employment contract is terminated, even if the employer wrongfully terminated the contract. McKibben v.

Rosselot, 4 Ohio App. 331 (12th Dist. 1915). See also Annotation, Right After Termination of Employment to Continue Occupancy of House Owned by Former Employer, 35 A.L.R. 576, 577 (1925).

R.C. Chapter 5321 has modified this common law distinction between servant and
tenant. Whenever a person resides in residential premises pursuant to an employment contract, the R.C. 5321.01 definitions of rental agreement, tenant, and landlord supersede the common law.

These employment contracts are rental agreements under R.C. 5321.01(D) because they establish rules and conditions c oncerning the use and occupancy of residential
premises. They meet the R.C. 5321.01(D) definition of rental agreement irrespective of whether the employee’s occupancy is related to the performance of the employment services.

Likewise, the employee is a te nant under R.C. 5321.01(A) and, if the employer owns or manages the residential premises, the employer is a landlord under R.C.

5321.01(B). See Grant v. Detroit Ass’n of Women’s Clubs , 443 Mich. 596, 505 N.W.2d 254 (1993) (when the essential characteristics of a landlord-tenant relationship are present, an employment contract may create a tenancy).

The status of tenant for employee tenants means that they are protected by the R.C.

Chapter 5321 tenant rights. For example, upon termination of the employment contract, the employer landlord may not seek to recover possession of the premises by terminating the utilities or changing the locks. See Chapter XII, below. Also, a month- to-month or week-to-week employment contract/rental agreement263 may be a periodic tenancy whose termination is subject to the R.C. 5321.17 notice requirements.

However, to preserve this right, the employee tenant may be required to tender the fair 263 The term “employment contract/rental agreement” is used in this Section VI.G to mean an employment contract that establishes rules and conditions concerning the employee’s use and occupancy of residential premises.

market rent each month after the employment has been terminated and the tenancy arguably continues. Otherwise, the employer landlord may simply evict the employee for nonpayment of rent.

R.C. Chapter 5321 also expands the substantive rights of employee tenants to remain in possession of the premises. For example, if the employer landlord is terminating the employment contract/rental agreement because the employee tenant complained to the employer landlord about R.C. 5321.04 violations, the R.C. 5321.02 prohibition against retaliation may apply. See Section II.T, above. And, if the employer landlord is terminating the employment contract/rental agreement due to employee tenant conduct that violates both R.C. 5321.05 and the employment contract/rental agreement, the R.C. 5321.11 notice and right to cure may apply. See Section I.D.2.e, above.

309

## VII. Rental Agreement Provisions


### A. Contract Law Governs Leases

“It is well established that leases are contracts and, as such, are subject to traditional rules governing contract interpretation.” Heritage Court, L.L.C. v. Merritt, 187 Ohio App.3d 117, 931 N.E. 2d 194, ¶ 14 (3rd Dist. 2010). Accord Krantz v.

Pahnke, No. 2021-CA-0043, 2022 WL 39486,¶ 42 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 5, 2022);

Langfan v. Carlton Gardens Co. , 183 Ohio App.3d 260, 916 N.E.2d 1079, ¶ 9 (3rd
Dist. 2009); Brown v. Spitzer Chevrolet Co. , 181 Ohio App.3d 642, 910 N.E.2d 490, ¶ 36 (5th Dist. 2009); Stauffer v. TGM Camelot, Inc., No. CA2005-12-508, 2006 WL 1974612, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 17, 2006); Pool v. Insignia Residential Group, 136 Ohio App.3d 266, 270, 736 N.E.2d 507 (1st Dist. 1999); Christe v. GMS
Mgmt. Co., 124 Ohio App.3d 84, 88, 705 N.E.2d 691 (9th Dist. 1997); Russell Realty
Co. v. Feghali, 121 Ohio App.3d 291, 294, 699 N.E.2d 976 (8th Dist. 1997);Delker v.

Kerr, No. L-96-110, 1996 WL 532319, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 20, 1996).

See also U.S. Corrections Corp. v. Ohio Dep’t of Indus. Relations, 73 Ohio St.3d 210, 216, 652 N.E.2d 766 (1995) (“a lease is a contract”).

The purpose of lease construction is to discover and effectuate the intent of the
parties. Krantz v. Pahnke, No. 2021-CA-0043, 2022 WL 39486, ¶ 42 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 5, 2022); Heritage Court, L.L.C. v. Merritt, 187 Ohio App.3d 117, 931 N.E.

2d 194, ¶ 14 (3rd Dist. 2010); Stauffer v. TGM Camelot, Inc. , No. CA2005 -12-508, 2006 WL 1974612, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 17, 2006); Local Marketing Corp.
v. Prudential Ins. Co. of Am. , 159 Ohio App.3d 410, 824 N.E.2d 122, ¶ 8 (1st Dist.

2004); Wauseon Plaza Ltd. P’ship v. Wauseon Hardware Co., 156 Ohio App.3d 575, 807 N.E.2d 953, ¶ 26 (6th Dist. 2004);Buckeye Union Ins. Co. v. Consol. Stores Corp., 68 Ohio App.3d 19, 24, 587 N.E.2d 391 (10th Dist. 1990).

The intent of the parties is presumed to reside in the language that the parties chose to use in their lease agreement. Id.

When the language of a lease is clear and unambiguous, the court must follow and enforce the plain language of the lease as written, and not look beyond the plain language of the lease to determine intent. Langfan v. Carlton Gardens Co., 183 Ohio App.3d 260, 916 N.E.2d 1079, ¶ 18 (3rd Dist. 2009); Brown v. Spitzer Chevrolet Co., 181 Ohio App.3d 642, 910 N.E.2d 490, ¶ 36 (5th Dist. 2009);Stauffer v. TGM Camelot, Inc., No. CA2005-12-508, 2006 WL 1974612, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 17, 2006); Pool v. Insignia Residential Group, 136 Ohio App.3d 266, 270,736 N.E.2d 507 (1st Dist. 1999); Russell Realty Co. v. Feghali, 121 Ohio App.3d 291, 294, 699 N.E.2d 976 (8th Dist. 1997); Delker v. Kerr , No. L -96-110, 1996 WL 532319, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 20, 1996).

When the language of a lease provision i s ambiguous, the court may consider parole evidence in its determination of the meaning of the provision. Altman v.

Kardous Bayard Props., No. C-200406, 2021 WL 4269933 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept.

15, 2021). For a discussion on contract construction rul es, relative to an ambiguous lease forfeiture provision, see Section II.X, above.

“In interpreting the language of a lease agreement, common words are presumed to hold their ordinary meaning, unless ‘(1) manifest absurdity results, or (2) some other meaning is clearly evidenced from the instrument.’”Heritage Court, L.L.C. v. Merritt, 187 Ohio App.3d 117, 931 N.E. 2d 194, ¶ 14 (3rd Dist. 2010). Accord Alexander v.

Buckeye Pipe Line Co., 53 Ohio St.2d 241, 245-46, 374 N.E.2d 146 (1997).

### B. Incorporation of R.C. Chapter 5321

Provisions Every residential rental agreement incorporates by operation of law the rights and duties that are established in R.C. Chapter 5321.Shoemaker v. Whitt, 129 Ohio App.3d 591, 595, 718 N.E.2d 932 (2nd Dist. 1998); Delker v. Kerr, No. L-96-110, 1996 WL 532319, at *3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 20, 1996); Rand v. Washington, No. 7822, 1983 WL 2448, at *1 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 26, 1983); Laster v. Bowman , 52 Ohio App.2d 379, 382, 370 N.E.2d 767 (8th Dist. 1977);Craver v. Doogan, No. 2003- CVC-00982, 2005 WL 4985282 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty., May 27, 2005).

Therefore, every residential rental agreement includes the:
(1) R.C. 5321.02,.03 prohibition against retaliation (see Section II.T, above, and Chapter XI, below);
(2) R.C. 53 21.04 landlord duty to maintain and provide services for the premises (see Section VII.C, below);
(3) R.C. 5321.05 tenant duty to maintain and properly use the premises (see Section VII.D, below);
(4) R.C. 5321.04 and 5321.05 rights and duties regarding landlord entry upon the premises (see Section VII.E and Chapter X, below);

311
(5) R.C. 5321.11 requirement for terminating a rental agreement for tenant conduct that violates R.C. 5321.05 (see Section I.D.2, above);
(6) R.C. 5321.15 prohibition against self-help eviction and distraint of rent (see Chapters XII and XIII, below);
(7) R.C. 5321.16 rights and duties regarding security deposits (see Chapter XVI, below);
(8) R.C. 5321.17 requirement for terminating a periodic tenancy (see Section I.D.3, above); and
(9) R.C. 5321.18 landlord duty to provide the tenant with written notice of the owner’s (and owner’s agent’s) name and address.

Residential rental agreements also may incorporate the contract remedies that R.C.

Chapter 5321 establishes. See, e.g., Kelly v. Chillicothe Metro. Hous . Auth., No. 10- 00430 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Apr. 5, 2011). In Kelly, a Public Housing tenant asserted R.C. 5321.04, 5321.15, and common law tort claims against a public housing authority (“PHA”). The court held that the PHA’s immunity under R.C. Chapter 2744 (see Section XXII.I, below) did not bar the R.C. 5321.04 contract claim or the portion of the R.C. 5321.15 claim that is a contract claim. It then held that this contract claim included the tenant’s right under R.C. 5321.15 to recover reasonable attorney’s fees.

### C. Landlord Duty to Maintain and Provide

Services for the Premises

#### 1. Scope of the R.C. 5321.04 Duty


##### a. Summary of the Statutory Requirements

“R.C. 5321.04(A) sets forth the obligations of a landlord who is a party to a rental agreement.” Mann v. Northgate Investors, L.L.C., 138 Ohio St.3d 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶ 9 (2014).

An obligation of all residential landlords, including those who lease single family homes, is to maintain and provide services for the premises in compliance with the requirements of R.C. 5321.04(A). Murray v. Bargo , No. CA86 -11-080, 1987 WL 15440, at *2 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Aug. 10, 1987) (single family homes are not exempt).

The R.C. 5321.04(A) requirements are to:
(1) comply with the requirements of applicable building, housing, health and safety codes that materially affect health and safety, R.C.

5321.04(A)(1) (see Section VII.C.2, below);
(2) make all repairs and do whatever is reasonably necessary to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition, R. C. 5321.04(A)(2) (see Section VII.C.3, below);
(3) keep all common areas of the premises in a safe and sanitary condition, R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) (see Section VII.C.4, below);
(4) maintain in good and safe working order and condition all electrical, plumbing, sanitary , heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances, and elevators, supplied or required to be supplied by the landlord, R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) (see Section VII.C.5, below);
(5) provide garbage cans and provide for garbage removal if there are fou r or more rental units in the building, R.C. 5321.04(A)(5); and
(6) supply running water, reasonable amounts of hot water, and reasonable heat at all times, subject to certain exceptions, R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) (see Section VII.C.6, below).

##### b. Duty to a Tenant’s Social Guest

“[A] landlord owes to a tenant’s guest the same duty that the landlord owes a tenant.”264 Mann v. Northgate Investors, L.L.C., 138 Ohio St.3d 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶ 33 (2014). Accord Shump v. First Cont’l-Robinwood Assocs., 71 Ohio St.3d 414, 644 N.E.2d 291 (1994).

Therefore, all of the duties that a landlord owes a tenant under R.C. 5321.04(A) extend to a tenant’s guest, where applicable.Mann v. Northgate Investors, L.L.C., 138 Ohio St.3d 175, 5 N.E.3d 594 (2014); Shump v. First Cont’l-Robinwood Assocs., 71 Ohio St.3d 414, 644 N.E.2d 291 (1994). It is irrelevant whether the duty concerns the common areas or the tenant’s dwelling unit.Mann v. Northgate Investors, L.L.C., 138 Ohio St.3d 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶ 23 (2014).

The Mann decision, in holding that the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) common area duty extends to a tenant’s guest, resolved a conflict among the appellate districts.

Compare Mann v. Northgate Investors LLC, No. 11AP-684, 2012 WL 2393945 (Ohio 264 The landlord’s R.C. 5321.04 obligations do not extend to a landlord’s social guest. Carlton
v. Williams , No. 77853, 2000 WL 1807230 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 7, 2000) (landlord resided in one of two apartments in a duplex).

313 App., 10th Dist., June 26, 2012) (common area duty extends to tenant’s guest)265 with Shumaker v. Park Lane Manor of Akron , No. 25212, 2011 WL 809492 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 9, 2011) (common area duty does not extend to tenant’s guest).266 Also, the Mann decision, in holding that a lan dlord owes to a tenant’s guest the same duty that the landlord owes a tenant, seems to overrule implicitly the decision in Owens v. French Vill. Co., No. 99-CA0058, 2000 WL 1026690 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 26, 2000). InOwens, the court refused to extend to a tenant’s guest the landlord’s duty, under an implied agreement arising from a course of conduct, to remove natural accumulations of ice and snow from the sidewalks.267

##### c. Prohibition Against Waiver

R.C. 5321.13 and R.C. 5321.06 prohibit any agreement between a landlord and tenant that purports to waive any of the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04 obligations to maintain and provide services for the premises. Miller v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 225, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989). E.g., Anderson v. Ballard, No. L-10-1007, 2010 WL 3292685, ¶ 39 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2010) (“Pursuant to R.C. 5321.13(A), this obligation [to maintain the premises in a habitable condition under R.C.

5321.04(A)(2)] may not be modified or waived by agreement”). But see Marcum v.

Young, No. CA9004 -037, 1990 WL 160371 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 22, 1990) (holding that, by the terms of the lease, the tenant assumed the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04 duty to repair the water heater).

Also, a “tenant’s occupancy of defective rental premisesover a lengthy period of time does not constitute a waiver of the landlord’s duty to maintain the premises as required by R.C. 5321.04 ....” Miller, 45 Ohio St.3d at 222, syllabus no. 2.

265 See also Wiles v. Miller, 3 N.E.3d 226, 2013-Ohio-3625 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2013);Bierl
v. BGZ Assocs. II, LLC, No. 9-12-42, 2013 WL 684707 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Feb. 25, 2013);

Smith v. Finn, No. L-04-1244, 2005 WL 736669 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 31, 2005);Mowery
v. Shoaf , 148 Ohio App.3d 403, 773 N.E.2d 1053 (7th Dist. 2002); Hodges v. Gates Mills Towers Apt. Co., No. 77278, 2000 WL 1429421 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 28, 2000).

266 See also Rios v. Shauck, No. 97-CA-006753, 1998 WL 289692 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 3, 1998); Sanders v. Bellevue Manor Apts. , No. 95 -CA-006067, 1996 WL 1768 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 3, 1996).

267 For a discussion on the landlord duty to the tenants to keep the common areas of the premises clear of natural accumulations of ice and snow, based on an implied agreement arising from a course of conduct, see Section VII.C.4.c, below.

However, a tenant may contract with a landlord to make certain repai rs to the premises in lieu of the tenant’s obligation to pay a certain portion of the rent. See Section II.R, above. Likewise, a tenant may contract with a landlord to make certain repairs to the premises in lieu of the tenant’s obligation to make certain cash payments for an option to purchase. In either case, the repair agreement would not relieve the landlord of any R.C. 5321.04 duty or liability. See Strayer v. Lindeman, 68 Ohio St.2d 32, 427 N.E.2d 781 (1981) (R.C. 5321.04 obligations are non -delegable). But, if the tenant fails to make the repairs properly and it results in a violation of R.C. 5321.04, the tenant would be liable for a breach of the repair agreement.

With respect to the option to purchase agreement, at least two limits exist for th e repair agreement. First, if the repair agreement does not reduce the cost of the option, the repair agreement would be without consideration and, therefore, invalid. Second, the total cost of the option – the sum of the reasonable value of the repair w ork plus the tenant’s remaining cash payment obligation – is subject to an unconscionability
prohibition. See Section XVI.B.6, below.

#### 2. Comply with the Building, Housing, Health, and

Safety Codes, R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) requires landlords “ to comply with the requirements of all applicable building, housing, health, and safety codes that materially affect health and safety.” E.g., McHugh v. Zataar, No. 14-CA-010591, 2015 WL 7273177 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 20, 2015) (city code, duty to in stall handrail on basement stairway);

Burdick v. Nevel, No. 98AP-697, 1999 WL 235435 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 20,
1999) (city code, duty to install and inspect smoke detectors); Primes v. Milbry, No.

18236, 1997 WL 760698 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 19, 1997) (building code, width and handrail requirements for stairways).

If the alleged defect does not violate an applicable building, housing, health, or safety code, it cannot constitute a violation of R.C. 5321.04(A)(1). McDaniels v.

Petrosky, No. 97-APE08-1027, 1998 WL 54363 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 5, 1998) (tree stump in backyard did not violate any applicable code);Taylor v. Alexander, No.

3550, 1986 WL 7800 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 11, 1986) (absence of handrail within interior of apartment did not violate any applicable code).
