Also, if the alleged code violation does not materially affect health and safety, it cannot constitute a violation of R.C. 5321.04(A)(1).Pedra Props., L.L.C. v. Justmann,
No. 102909, 2015 WL 9438446 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 2015) (code violation 315
– roof leak in laundry room located within the dwelling unit – did not constitute a violation of R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) because trial court found it did not materially affect health and safety and appellate reco rd did not include a transcript or an appropriate substitute); Wenzke v. Baird, No. L-13-1244, 2014 WL 3408590 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., July 11, 2014) (code violation– six inoperable windows– did not constitute a violation of R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) because the trial court failed to find the code violations materially affected health and safety).

#### 3. Put and Keep the Premises in Fit and Habitable

Condition, R.C. 5321.04(A)(2)

##### a. Introduction

R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) requires a landlord to “make all repairs and do whateve r is reasonably necessary to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition.” This requirement is similar to the common law implied warranty of habitability,268 which other jurisdictions recognize and some Ohio courts recognized before the enactment of R.C. Chapter 5321 (see Section II.P, above).

However, R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) requires a landlord to maintain the premises in both a fit condition and a habitable condition. A defective condition in the premises may cause the premises to be less than fit, in violation of R.C. 5321.04(A)(2), even though it does not render the premises uninhabitable. Tellema v. Liette, No. 92-CA-14, 1993 WL 79270, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 11, 1993). Cf. Walling v. Holman, 858 F.2d 79, 83 (2d Cir. 1998) (New York law: rejects landlord’s contention that “the warranty [of habitability] is limited to conditions that endanger life, health, and safety, because the warranty extends to maintenance of the premises ‘in accord with the uses reasonably intended by the parties’”). But see V.R. v. Cincinnati -Hamilton County
Cmty. Action Agency, No. C-140230, 2014 WL 6090396, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 1st Dist.,
Nov. 14, 2014).

268 See Pagoda Co. v. Smith, No. 37936, 1979 Ohio App. LEXIS 11691, at *12 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 8, 1979) (finding that the implied warranty of habitability in Glyco v. Schultz , 35 Ohio Misc. 25, 289 N.E.2d 919 (Ohio Mun., Sylvania, 1972), “sought to develop the same remedies through case law that the legislature later enacted through the statute”).

##### b. General Factors for Determining Whether a Defective

Condition Violates the Duty Factors for determining whether a defective condition violates R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) include: (1) whether the alleged defect violates housing laws, regulations, or ordinances, (2) the seriousness of the defect, (3) the extent of inconvenie nce suffered by the tenant as a result of the defect, and (4) the length of time the defect has persisted.

Pagoda Co. v. Smith, No. 76-CVG-1548, slip op. at 5 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights,
Feb. 25, 1977), aff’d, No. 37936, 1979 Ohio App. LEXIS 11691, at *11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 8, 1979); Weingarden v. Eagle Ridge Condos., 71 Ohio Misc.2d 7, 13, 653 N.E.2d 759 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., 1995).269 Whether the defect violates an applicable housing, building, health, or safety code is not a dispositive factor. R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) imposes an obligation to maintain the premises in a fit and habitable condition irrespective of whether there are any such codes, and in addition to any such codes. Lloyd v. Roosevelt Props., No. 105721, 2018 WL 3814972, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 9, 2018).

The basis for this conclusion is two-fold. First, it is evident from the legislature’s decision to establish a “fit and habitable” standard in R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) that is separate from the R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) obligation to comply with such codes.

Second, it is consistent with the implied warranty of habitability case law in other jurisdictions, which holds that a breach of the warranty may occur regardless of whether a municipal code violation exists. E.g., Glasoe v. Trinkle, 107 Ill.2d 1, 10-12, 479 N.E.2d 915 (1985); Hilder v. St. Peter , 144 Vt. 150, 160, 478 A.2d 202 (1984);

Pugh v. Holmes , 486 Pa. 272, 290, 405 A.2d 897 (1979); Park W. Mgmt. Corp. v.

Mitchell, 47 N.Y.2d 316, 328, 418 N.Y.S.2d 310 (1979); Boston Hous. Auth. v.

Hemingway, 363 Mass. 184, 200 n.16, 293 N.E.2d 831 (1973). It recognizes that “not all towns and municipalities have housing codes; where there are codes, the particular problem complained of may not be addressed.” Hilder, 144 Vt. at 160.

“The concept of fitness and habitability is concerned with defects that force a tenant to endure serious deprivations of basic needs or threat to the tenant’s health or safety ….” Wilmoth v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 27746, 2016 WL 3322551,¶ 21 269 See also Glasoe v. Trinkle , 107 Ill.2d 1, 14, 479 N.E.2d 915 (1985) (implied warranty of habitability); Detling v. Edelbrock, 671 S.W.2d 265, 270 (Mo. 1984) (same); Teller v. McCoy, 162 W. Va. 367, 391-92, 253 S.E.2d 114 (1978) (same); Mease v. Fox, 200 N.W.2d 791, 796- 97 (Iowa 1972) (same).

317 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 15, 2016). It is not a warranty against all discomfort and
inconvenience. Id.

The R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) “[f]itness and habitability entails such defects as lack of water or heat, faulty wiring, or vermin infestation.” Aldridge v. Englewood Vill., Ltd.,
No. 10251, 1987 WL 15015, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 22, 1987). Accord Goodman v. Dan Rich, LLC, No. 109581,2021 WL 927001,¶ 31 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Mar. 11, 2021); Gress v. Wechter, No. H-12-023, 2013 WL 1092731, ¶¶ 20,21 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 15, 2013); St. Germain v. Newell , No. 9 -15-14, 2015 WL 5319833, ¶ 28 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 14, 2015).

It also entails other significant interference with a tenant’s use and enjoyment of the premises. E.g., Holmes v. Cobblestone Grove, No. CA20-04-075, 2017 WL 74986 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 9, 2017) (after repair of massive plumbing leak, mold and mildew in the unit and repeated and ongoing intrusion from remediation efforts ( e.g., drywall debris everywhere, large fans and dehumidifiers running for weeks, large holes in drywall, and inaccessibility to tenant’s furnishings) that deprived tenant of reasonable use and enjoyment of the kitchen and living areas); McNelly v. Conde, 166 N.E.3d 697, 2021-Ohio-146, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 2021) (upon arriving to move in, “apartment was filthy, with water behind the walls, leakage, mold all over, and exposed wires in the kitchen and attic, which were a safety hazard”).

##### c. Court Decisions on Whether Particular Structural

Defects Violate the Duty Court decisions that illustrate the types of structural deficiency that violate (or do not violate) R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) are set forth below.
(1) Miller v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989) (violation
– faulty and dangerous electrical wiring, holes in the floors, inadequate plumbing, and no heating system).
(2) McKinney v. Hartz & Restle Realtors, Inc. , 31 Ohio St.3d 244, 510 N.E.2d 386 (1987) (no violation – absence of fence along portion of the premises that abuts a railroad right-of-way).
(3) Shroades v. Rental Homes, Inc. , 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 427 N.E.2d 774
(1981) (violation – defective exterior stairs leading to dwelling unit).
(4) Goodman v. Dan Rich, LLC, No. 109581, 2021 WL 927001 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 11, 2021) (no violation – patio contained loose pavers).
(5) Lloyd v. Roosevelt Props., No. 105721, 2018 WL 3814972 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 9, 2018) (violation – inadequate heat, lack of drinkable water, constant exposure to cigarette smoke (in building advertised as smoke-free), and rodent infestation in common area).
(6) Tillimon v. Richardson-Long, No. L-16-1055, 2017 WL 169267 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 13, 2017) (violation – failing to install heating tape on pipes and/or to close crawlspace vents needed to prevent waterline in crawlspace from bursting in winter).
(7) Arnett v. Mong, 65 N.E.3d 72, 2016-Ohio-2893 (Ohio App., 12th Dist.,
2016) (no violation – hole in the gutter and/or gutter sagging).
(8) St. Germain v. Newell, No. 9-15-14, 2015 WL 5319833 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 14, 2015) (no violation – loose handrail).
(9) Wenzke v. Baird , No. L -13-1244, 2014 WL 3408590 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., July 11, 2014) (no violation – six inoperable or ill -functioning windows).
(10) Gress v. Wechter , No. H -12-023, 2013 WL 1092731 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 15, 2013) (no violation – lack of gutters).
(11) Mullins v. Grosz , No. 10AP -23, 2010 WL 3244139 (Ohio App., 10 th Dist., Aug. 17, 2010) (no violation – lack of a handrail or gating in the porch/step/walkway area of the premises).
(12) January Inv., LLC v. Ingram, No. CA2009-09-127, 2010 WL 1757940 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 3, 2010) (no violation – tenant’s expenses on exterior light fixtures, landscaping, vertical blinds, and chimney sweep).
(13) Mounts v. Ravotti, No. 07-MA-182, 2008 WL 4415819 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Sept. 26, 2008) (violation – defective steps that provided the sole means of ingress to the rental unit).
(14) Cipollone v. Hoffmeier, No. C-060482, 2007 WL 2141578 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., July 27, 2007) (no violation – absence of window latches).
(15) Heck v. Whitehurst Co., No. L-03-1134, 2004 WL 1857131 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2004) (violation – cigarette smoke from neighbor’s apartment prevented tenant from using bedroom and caused clothes in closet to smell of smoke).
(16) McGuire v. K & G Mgmt. Co., No. 98-CA-35, 1998 WL 666756 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 27, 1998) (no violation – loose fitting screen, which served its purpose of providing ventilation and keeping out insects and birds, but did not prevent tenant’s child from exiting through 319 the window).
(17) McDaniels v. Petrosky , No. 97 -APE08-1027, 1998 WL 54363 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 5, 1998) (no viola tion – tree stump in the backyard).
(18) McKenzie v. Marlowe , No. 70714, 1996 WL 715502 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 12, 1996) (violation – faulty electrical wiring).
(19) Paul v. Pulskamp , No. CA96 -06-049, 1996 WL 707132 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 9, 1996) (no violation – hole, one foot deep and two feet in diameter, in the concrete near the edge of the driveway).
(20) Roberson v. Insignia Mgmt. Group, L.P. , No. C -940968, 1995 WL 757831 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 20, 1995) (no violation– loose fitting screen, which se rved its purpose of providing ventilation and keeping out insects and birds, but did not prevent tenant’s child from exiting through the window).
(21) Pease v. Croxton Realty, No. CA-8779, 1992 WL 362463 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 7, 1992) (no violation – “a three to four inch ‘defect’ [that] existed by the driveway and cement slab and the carport”).
(22) Glover v. Massey, Nos. 56351,56802, 1990 WL 1328 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 11, 1990) (violation – open drain in the basement floor that regularly backed up, causing the floor area near the drain to flood).
(23) Aldridge v. Englewood Vill., Ltd. , No. 10251, 1987 WL 15015 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 22, 1987) (no violation– threshold, leading from a back door to a patio, was three-quarters of an inch too high).
(24) Simpson v. Delta Prop. Mgmt., Inc., Nos. C-850562,850575, 1986 WL 7136 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 25, 1986) (violation– defective fixtures such as kitchen cabinets that may fall and cause injury).
(25) Taylor v. Alexander, No. 3550, 1986 WL 7800 (Ohio App., 11th Dis t., July 11, 1986) (no violation – lack of handrail).
(26) Hershberger v. Shelmar Realty, Inc., No. CV2014-07-3154 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Jan. 13, 2016), aff’d on other grounds, No. 28110, 2017 WL 420698 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., J an. 31, 2017) (no violation – no gutters or awning over front steps, which allowed water to accumulate and then freeze).

##### d. Bed Bugs – Duty and Liability Issues

A bed bug infestation at rental premises may constitute a violation of R.C.

5321.04(A)(2). E.g., Bynum v. Cotterman, No. 26222, 2015 WL 751720 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 20, 2015); Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Wright , No. 12-CVG-00724 (Ohio Mun., Clinton Cty., Oct. 26, 2012).See alsoKolb v. DeVille I Props., LLC, 326 S.W.3d 896 (Mo. App. 2010) (implied warranty of habitability violation – bed bugs); Ludlow Props., LLC v. Young , 4 Misc.3d 515, 780 N.Y.S.2d 853 (Civ. Ct. 2004) (implied warranty of habitability violation – bed bugs). Cf. Morel v. Marks Group, LLC, Nos.

HCH-598, H-1532, 2011 WL 2712996 (Conn. Super. June 21, 2011) (implied warranty of habitability violation – bed bugs and cockroaches).

When a landlord is aware of a bed bug infestation at the rental premises (and thus is aware of the R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) violation), the landlord is obligated to remedy the infestation (like any other R.C. 5321.04(A) violation).

If the landlord fails to remedy timely the bed bug infestation ( i.e., the R.C.

5321.04(A)(2) violation), the tenant’s remedies are the remedies that are available for any R.C. 5321.04(A) violation ( see Chapters VIII and IX, below), including a claim for money damages. E.g., Bynum v. Cotterman, No. 26222, 2015 WL 751720 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 20, 2015) (damage award of $200 per month for eleven months, for total of $2,200); Sanders v. McGee, No. 13-CV-003983 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty.,
Apr. 3, 2014) (compensatory damage award included $4,000 for personal property damage and $5,000 for incidental expenses).

A tenant generally is not liable for the landlord’s costs for remediating the infestation (i.e., the R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) violation), unless the landlord can establish that the infestation is the result of the tenant’s violation of an R.C. 5321.05 duty.E.g., Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Mountjoy, 154 N.E.3d 141, 2020-Ohio-2772 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., 2020) (tenant liable for cost of bed bug extermination where the bed bugs were the result of tenant’s carelessness/misuse/neglect);Spring Hill Town Homes v. Pounds, 972 N.E.2d 1121, 2012-Ohio-2800, ¶¶ 27-32 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 2012) (tenant not liable for cost of bed bug extermination because trial court did not find tenant failed to keep the premises safe and sanitary) ; Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Wright , No. 12 -CVG- 00724 (Ohio Mun., Clinton Cty., Oct. 26, 2012) (tenant not liable for cost of extermination because landlord did not establish at trial the source of the bed bugs).

The tenant has a duty to cooperate in the landlord’s efforts to remediate the
infestation. See Section IX.A.6, below. If a tenant violates this duty, it may be a defense to the tenant’ s claim for the R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) violation. E.g., Kenwood Gardens Ass’n, LLC v. Shorter, No. L-10-1315, 2011 WL 3654493 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 19, 2011).

The duties relative to a tenant’s personal property, when a bed bug infestation of 321 the rental premises causes an infestation of the personal property, raise two issues.

The first issue is whether the landlord has a duty to remediate (or compensate the tenant for) the infestation of the tenant’s personal property. If the landlord’s action or inaction caused the infestation of the rental premises and thereby the personal property, the landlord is liable for any damages resulting from this R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) violation (see Chapter IX, below), including the personal property remediation (or compensation). If the landlord’s action or inaction did not cause the infestation of the rental premises and thereby the personal property, the landlord has not violated R.C.

5321.04(A)(2) and thus is not liable for the personal property remediation (or compensation). See Chesterfield LLC v. Mackow , No. 08 -CVG-16093 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 5, 2009).

The second issue concerns the duty and rights of the tenant relative to the remediation or disposition of the tenant’s personal property. A landlord may elect not to remediate the infestation of the tenant’s personal property270 if the landlord’s action or inaction did not cause the infestation. In such cases, the landlord may ask the tenant either to remediate the infestation of the tenant’s personal property or to remove the personal property from the dwelling unit, within a reasonable period of time.

If the tenant fails to take either course of action, the issue is whether the landlord may then remove the tenant’s personal property from the rental premises in order to remediate fully the infestation of the premises. R.C. Ch apter 1923 provides the landlord with an expeditious process for a court to remove forcibly the tenant and the tenant’s personal property. If the landlord elects instead to remove the property unilaterally (i.e., via a “self-help” approach), it might constitute unlawful conversion.

#### 4. Keep the Common Areas in Safe and Sanitary

Condition, R.C. 5321.04(A)(3)

##### a. Introduction

R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) requires a landlord to keep all common areas of the premises in a safe and sanitary condition.

This requirement is similar to the landlord’s common law duty concerning 270 According to many experts, the best practice is for the landlord to remediate, at the same time, the infestation of the dwelling unit and th e infestation of the tenant’s personal property.

The experts state that this approach is both effective and prevents the spread of the infestation (which disposition of the personal property may cause).

common areas. “At common law, a landlord was charged with a general duty to exercise reasonable care to keep the premises retained in his control for the common use of his tenants in a reasonably safe condition.” Brady v. Koehnke, No. C-930240, 1994 WL 481758, at *2 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept. 7, 1994). Accord Wiggans v.

Glock, No. 15967, 1997 WL 111745 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 14, 1997).

Likewise, “the landlord’s duty to keep a ll common areas in a safe and sanitary condition [under R.C. 5321.04(A)(3)] is analogous to a premises owner’s duty to a business invitee.” Howson v. Amorose, No. 00AP-8, 2000 WL 1753112, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 30, 2000).Accord McDaniels v.Petrosky, No. 97-APE08-1027, 1998 WL 54363, at *4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 5, 1998).

Generally, the court decisions seem to view the R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) obligation as a codification, not an expansion, of this common law.

However, one court decision upheld an R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) violation that was based on repeated vandalism of a tenant’s car, a burglary of a tenant’s apartment, garbage spread on the lawn, and drug -dealing in the complex. Turski v. Loesch, No.

L-05-1273, 2006 WL 3378415 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 22, 2006).

##### b. Open and Obvious Doctrine Inapplicable

“A violation of R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) constitutes negligence per se and obviates the open-and-obvious doctrine.” Mann v. Northgate Investors, L.L.C. , 138 Ohio St.3d 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶ 33 (2014).

In Mann, and in the corresponding R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) decision in Robinson v.

Bates, 112 Ohio St.3d 17, 857 N.E.2d 1195 (2006), the Supreme Court is applying well-established Ohio law: “[W]hen the General Assembly has enacted statutes the violation of which constitute negligence per se, the open-and-obvious doctrine will not protect a defendant from liability.” Lang v. Holly Hill Motel, Inc., 122 Ohio St.3d 120, 909 N.E.2d 120, ¶ 15 (2009).

These Supreme Court decisions overrule the earlier appellate court decisions that relied on common law and the analysis in LaCourse v. Fleitz, 28 Ohio St.3d 209, 503 N.E.2d 159 (1986), to justify their use of the open and obvious doctrine to define the 323 scope of the R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) obligation. 271 Those earlier de cisions include:

Mowery v. Shoaf, 148 Ohio App.3d 403, 773 N.E.2d 1053 (7th Dist. 2002) (no duty – lack of exterior lighting in the common area); Howson v. Amorose, No. 00AP-8, 2000 WL 1753112, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 30, 2000) (no duty – water-filled pothole in the parking lot); McDaniels v. Petrosky , No. 97 -APE08-1027, 1998 WL 54363, at *4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 5, 1998) (no duty – tree stump in the backyard); Wiggans v. Glock , No. 15967, 1997 WL 111745 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Mar. 14, 199 7) (no duty – excessive grass clippings resulting from lawn mowing);

Hayes v. Murtha, No. 96-APE04-512, 1996 WL 589268 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Oct.

10, 1996) (no duty – wet grass).

For discussions on the open and obvious doctrine in negligence claims, seeSection IX.B.7.a, below (R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claims) and Section IX.C.2, below (common law premises liability claims).

##### c. Removal of Ice/Snow from Common Areas

R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) requires a landlord to keep the common areas of the premises clear of unnatural accumulations of ice and snow, if the accumulation results in a condition that is substantially more dangerous than would have resulted naturally.

Burress v. Associated Land Group, No. 2008-10-096, 2009 WL 1459699 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 26, 2009); Saunders v. Greenwood Colony, No. 14-2000-40, 2001 WL 196498 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Feb. 28, 2001); Rampersaud v. Madison Dev. Co., No.

97-CA-006768, 1998 WL 332956 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 24, 1998).

“[A]n unnatural accumulation is ‘man -made,’ and created by a person doing something that would cause ice and snow to accumulate in an unexpected place or way.” Au v. Waldman, No. 2010-CA-112, 2011 WL 1832719, ¶ 34 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 9, 2011). Accord Burress v. Associated Land Group , No. 2008-10-096, 2009 WL 1459699 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 26, 2009); Martin v. Edgewater Props., No. 92-CA-005307, 1992 WL 357321 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 25, 1992);

271 The LaCourse decision refers to the open and obvi ous doctrine in describing the longstanding rule of common law that a landlord has no duty to keep common areas clear of natural accumulations of ice and snow. It holds that R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) does not modify this rule of common law. The basis for its holding is a principle of statutory construction: in order to find legislative intent to abrogate a rule of common law, “[s]uch an intention must be expressly declared by the legislature or necessarily implied in the language of the statute.” 28 Ohio St.3d at 212.

Klein v. Ryan’s Family Steak House, No. 20683, 2002 WL 1022974 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 15, 2002). See also Morgan v. Mamone , No. 87612, 2006 WL 3804527 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 21, 2006).

R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) does not require a landlord to keep the common areas of the premises clear of natural accumulations of ice and snow. LaCourse v. Fleitz, 28 Ohio St.3d 209, 503 N.E.2d 159 (1986). See also Walker v. 24570 Lakeshore Prop. L.L.C., No.105017, 2017 WL 1055294 (Ohio App. , 8th Dist., May 11, 2017); Burress v.

Associated Land Group, No. 2008-10-096, 2009 WL 1459699 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 26, 2009); Barner v. Continent Apt. Homes , No. 04AP-774, 2005 WL 1022909 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 3, 2005); Worthan v. Bryan Hous. Ltd., No. WM-97-028, 1998 WL 230583 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 1, 1998);McCornell v. Bridges, 124 Ohio App.3d 610, 707 N.E.2d 8 (8th Dist. 1997);Moore v. Lupica Towers, No. 71551, 1997 WL 711295 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 13, 1997); Burns v. Barrington Group, Inc. ,
No. 2 -CA-92, 1992 WL 319152 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 2, 1992); Rivera v.

Associated Estates Corp., No. 53502, 1987 WL 13044 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 18, 1987).

However, where a landlord has superior knowledge of a particular danger caused by a natural accumulation of snow and ice, the landlord may have a duty to the tenant to remedy the danger. Barner v. Continent Apt. Homes , No. 04AP -774, 2005 WL 1022909 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 3, 2005); Garden Woods Apts. v. Gee , No.

13962, 1993 WL 385818 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 27, 1993);Martin v. Edgewater Props., No. 92-CA-005307, 1992 WL 357321 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 25, 1992).

See also Klein v. Ryan’s Family Steak House , No. 20683, 2002 WL 1022974 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 15, 2002).

A landlord may assume the obligation to keep the common areas of the premises clear of natural accumulations of ice and snow by an express agreement. LaCourse v.

Fleitz, 28 Ohio St.3d 209, 211, 503 N.E.2d 159 (1986); Barner v. Continent Apt.

Homes, No. 04AP-774, 2005 WL 1022909, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 3, 2005);

Garden Woods Apts. v. Gee, No. 13962, 1993 WL 385818, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Sept. 27, 1993).

The traditional view has been that a landlord also may assume the obligation to keep the common areas of the premises clear of natural accumulations of iceand snow 325 by an implied agreement arising from the landlord’s course of conduct.272 Oswald v.

Jeraj, 146 Ohio St. 676, 67 N.E.2d 779 (1946); Espurvoa v. Calhoun Inv. Co. , No.

91AP-406, 1992 WL 15260 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 23, 1992); Rogers v. Ayjay Dev., Inc. , No. 2 -91-1, 1991 WL 240046 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Nov. 14, 1991);

Hammond v. Moon, 8 Ohio App.3d 66, 455 N.E.2d 1301 (10th Dist. 1982).

But several appellate districts have rejected or questioned the continued validity of this traditional view. See, e.g. , Brewton v. Monnette , No. L -13-1068, 2013 WL 5210514 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 13, 2013);Thatcher v. Lauffer Ravines, LLC, No.

11AP-851, 2013 WL 816428 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 5, 2013); Hill v. Monday Villas Prop. Owners Ass’n, No. 24714, 2012 WL 690316 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar.

2, 2012); Sanfilippo v. Vill. Green Mgmt. Co., No. CA2010-04-027, 2010 WL 3481981 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Sept. 7, 2010); Strazinsky v. Tri -County Props., Inc. , No.

71015, 1997 WL 253169 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 15, 1997); Brooks v. Lee , No.

CA95-05-091, 1995 WL 708227 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 4, 1995); Yanda v.
Consol. Mgmt. Inc. , No. 57268, 1990 WL 118703 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 16, 1990).

Also, if the language of a lease provision is substantially the same as the language of R.C. 5321.04(A)(3), the lease provision does not require the landlord to keep the common areas of the premises clear of natural accumulations of ice and snow. Lewis
v. Byram, No. L-92-286, 1993 WL 57293 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,Mar. 5, 1993). But cf.

Rogers v. Ayjay Dev., Inc., No. 2-91-1, 1991 WL 240046 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Nov.

14, 1991) (landlord construed the lease provision as imposing the obligation and the court so enforced it, as an express agreement or, in the alterna tive, an implied agreement arising from the landlord’s course of conduct).

##### d. Other Court Decisions

Court decisions on the R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) obligation, excluding those on removing ice/snow (see Section VII.C.4.c, above), are summarized below.
(1) Obligation to provide temporary lighting for an internal staircase when a power outage is caused by the actions of a third party. Mayhew v.

272 Some courts have held that this obligation cannot be implied from a course of conduct if the written lease states that it is the “entire agreement.” E.g., Garden Woods Apts. v. Gee , No.

13962, 1993 WL 385818 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 27, 199 3); Sheaffer v. Terrace Assocs. ,
No. 12483, 1991 WL 227701 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 18, 1991).

Massey, 86 N.E.3d 758, 2017-Ohio-1016 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., 2017).
(2) Obligation to keep the dumpster area in a safe and sanita ry condition.

Bierl v. BGZ Assocs. II, LLC , No. 9 -12-42, 2013 WL 684707 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Feb. 25, 2013).
(3) Obligation to repair leaking exterior faucet and to repair potholes in the parking lot. Walker v. RLI Enters., Inc., No. 89325, 2007 WL 4442725 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 20, 2007).
(4) Obligation to clean grease spill met, where landlord commenced the process to remove the hazard and placed precaution signs around the area of spill. Williams v. Associated Estates Corp. , 119 Ohio App.3d 558, 695 N.E.2d 1172 (8th Dist. 1997).
(5) Obligation to remove wrecked autos from the common area parking lots.

Walker v. Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth., No. C-840007, 1984 WL 7128 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 19, 1984).
(6) No obligation to repair trivial imperfections in common area carpet, such as slight rip ortear. Pine v. Hall, No. C-040653, 2005 WL 1593929 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., July 8, 2005).
(7) No obligation to light common areas. Mowery v. Shoaf , 148 Ohio App.3d 403, 773 N.E.2d 1053 (7th Dist. 2002); Riley v. FDMA Props.,
No. 92-CA-22, 1992 WL 316356 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 7, 1992).

See also Smith v. Whitaker , No. 10448, 1987 WL 17615 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 23, 1987).
(8) No obligations relative to a sidewalk of a single-family dwelling because it is not a common area of the premises. Parks v. Menyhart Plumbing & Heating Supply Co., No. 75424, 1999 WL 1129591 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999).
(9) No obligation to remove a tree stump from the backyard. McDaniels v.

Petrosky, No. 97-APE08-1027, 1998 WL 54363 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Feb. 5, 1998). Cf. Kueber v. Haas, 47 Ohio App.3d 62, 546 N.E.2d 1351 (1st Dist. 1988) (dicta) (R.C. 5321.04(A)(2), dead trees in wooded area left in wild and natural state to serve as privacy screen).
(10) No obligation to install speed bumps or warning signs in the common area parking lots. Washington v. Arbor Crest Land Co. , No. 66307, 1994 WL 245640 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 2, 1994).
(11) No obligation to construct a playground for children in the common
area. Washington v. Arbor Crest Land Co. , No. 66307, 1994 WL 245640 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 2, 1994).

327
(12) No obligation to keep common area stairs free from moisture
accumulation. Riley v. FDMA Props., No. 92-CA-22, 1992 WL 316356 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 7, 1992).
(13) No obligation to remove natural accumulation of leaves from the common areas. Plush v. Nat’l Church Residences, No. L-89-293, 1990 WL 106437 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., July 27, 1990).
(14) No obligation to restrain or control an inebriated tenant who is observed staggering in the common area, where the inebriated tenant has not violated any provision of law or any term of the tenant’s lease to justify the tenant’s removal from the common area. Barber v. Mid -Towne Assoc., 62 Ohio App.3d 384, 575 N.E.2d 879 (1st Dist. 1990).

#### 5. Maintain Fixtures/Appliances in Good and Safe

Working Order, R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) requires a landlord to maintain in good and safe working order and condition all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances, and elevators, that the landlord has supplied or is required to supply. E.g., CMB P’ship v. Baker, No. 18159, 2000 WL 706824, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 2, 2000) (duty to repair a leaking faucet that resulted in a wet dining room carpet).

With respect to elevators, this “statutory ‘good and safe working order’ standard requires more than code compliance.” Cmty. Dev. Props. Cleveland, Inc. v. Griffin ,
No. 87954, 2007 WL 613956, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 1,2007). The standard requires a landlord, in addition to achieving code compliance, “to ensure the proper functioning of the elevators and to prevent sudden stops upon the shifting of weight in the elevator, and excessive breakdowns/malfunctions.” Id., ¶¶ 4-6,15.

“R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) requires a landlord to keep air conditioning in good working order without regard to the season.” CMB P’ship v. Baker , No. 18159, 2000 WL 706824, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 2, 2000). “R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) requires an air conditioner to perform when needed, regardless of outside temperature.” Id. See also Howard v. Simon, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 16, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist. 1984).

The R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) duty to maintain an air conditioner includes the duty to turn on the air conditioning system. “An air conditioner which is turned off results in the same uninhabitable conditions as one which is broken.”Howard v. Simon, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 16, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist. 1984).

#### 6. Supply Water, Hot Water, and Heat, R.C.

5321.04(A)(6), PUCO Rule, and Other Sources

##### a. R.C. 5321.04(A)(6)

Subject to two exceptions, R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) requires the landlord to supply the dwelling unit with running water, reasonable amounts of hot water, and reasonable heat at all times.

The first exception is that the R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) obligation does not apply if the building that includes the dwelling unit is not required by law to be equipped with those services.

The second exception is that the R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) obligation to supply hot water and heat does not apply if the utility meter, for the utility that furnishes hot water or heat to the dwelling unit, is an individual meter that serves and measures consumption at only the tenant’s dwelling unit. The R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) obligation to supply running water is not subject to this second exception.

R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) does not require a landlord to prevent an interruption (or
reduction) of the flow of water from a municipal water system to the premises, unless it is the result of a defective condition at the premises that the landlord is obligated to
remedy. Stewart v. Kalman, No. 1109, 1982 WL 3509 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 13, 1982).

Likewise, R.C. 5321.04 does not require a landlord to prevent a storm sewer overflow or a sanitary sewer back -up at th e premises, unless it is the result of a defective condition at the premises that the landlord is obligated to remedy. Conway
v. Liddell, No. 79799, 2002 WL 398687 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 14, 2002);Paul v.

Semirale, No. 70438, 1996 WL 502143 (Ohio Ap p., 8th Dist., Sept. 5, 1996). “It is settled law that municipalities have the duty to maintain their public sewer systems.” Paul, 1996 WL 502143, at *1.

An issue on which the case law is split is whether the R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) obligation to supply running water, hot water, and heat requires the landlord to pay for the utility services that furnish the running water, hot water, and heat.

The initial appellate court to address this issue concluded that the R.C.

5321.04(A)(6) obligation requires the landlord to pay for the water service. Griffin v.

Holston, No. L-83-261, 1983 WL 2328, at *1 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 16, 1983) (“the term ‘supply,’ as used in R.C. 5321.04(A)(6), means to pay for the utility charges”). Cf. Woodrow v. McGuire , No. 1740, 198 9 WL 72799, at *3 (Ohio App., 329 4th Dist., June 27, 1989) (“even if appellee [landlord] did not agree to that term [landlord to pay for the water service], R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) specifically requires the landlord to ‘supply running water ...’” and thus, in failing to secure the reinstatement of the water service, “appellee did fail in his duty to provide water”).

Thereafter, two appellate courts and and at least one municipal court have concluded that the R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) obligation does not require the landlord to pay for the water service. Jenkins v. Roger C. Perry & Co., 83 Ohio App.3d 234, 236, 614 N.E.2d 850 (10th Dist. 1992) (“the word ‘supply’ in R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) means to provide water or make it available in the physical sense and without the added burden of paying for such service”); Hodgson v. Hodgson , No. 89 -L-14-074, 1990 WL 128267, at *2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 7, 1990) (“Subsection (A)(6) does not require payment for the water by the landlord”); Bess v. McBride, No. 96-CVH-1586, slip op. at 3 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 6, 1997) (“The Court notes that there is nothing in the Landlord -Tenant Act which prohibits a landlord and tenant, as between themselves, from entering into a contractual arrangement such that the tenant agrees to pay for the water”).273 The Jenkins, Hodgson, and Bess conclusion that the R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) obligation only requires a landlord to make the water available in a physical sense causes the second exception in R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) to be nonsensical. Their conclusion means that, when the dwelling unit’s relevant utility meter is an individual meter (which triggers the second exception), the R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) obligation does not apply and thus the landlord is not required to make hot wate r and heat available in a physical
sense. None of these court decisions discusses the meaning of the second exception under its conclusion.

##### b. PUCO Rule

When the water is supplied through a mastermeter that is subject to PUCO regulation, Ohio Admin. Code § 4901:1-18-08(H) (“the PUCO rule”) may require the landlord to pay for the water service, irrespective of whether R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) requires it. The PUCO rule requires the landlord to be designated as the customer on all applications for utility service to residential mastermetered premises.

273 In 2001, the city of Cleveland enacted an ordinance that supersedes the Bess decision.

Cleveland, OH, Code pt. 3C, tit. IX, ch. 375, § 375.05. The ordinan ce requires a residential landlord to pay for the water, electric, and gas services unless certain conditions are met.

It reasonably follows that, in requiring the landlord to be the customer, the PUCO rule requires the landlord to pay for the utility service. Therefore, where the PUCO Rule applies, if the landlord requires the tenant to pay for the water service, the requirement would violate the PUCO Rule and thus R.C. 5321.06. R.C. 5321.06 prohibits any lease provision that is inconsistent with or prohibited by R.C. Chapter 5321 or any other rule of law (e.g., the PUCO Rule).

##### c. Other Sources of Landlord Duty to Provide Water

Service If a landlord is not required to pay for the water service under R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) or the PUCO rule, and if the rental agreement requires the tenant to pay for the water service and the tenant fails to do so, R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) may still require the landlord to pay for the water service. See Bess v. McBride , No. 96 -CVH-1586 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 6, 1997) (“Whether the tenant has paid for the water or not,” City of Cleveland ordinances, tog ether with R.C. 5321.04(A)(1), make the landlord “primarily liable for assuring that the water remains flowing”).

Also, if a landlord is not required to pay for the water service under R.C.

5321.04(A)(6) or the PUCO rule, and if two or more dwelling units receive the water service through a mastermeter, the landlord might require one of the tenants to contract for the water service and the other tenant(s) to reimburse that tenant for their share of the water bill. If a landlord imposes such requirements and one tenant fails to pay the tenant’s share of the water bill, the landlord may be liable for a breach of the implied covenant of quiet enjoyment. See Roger Colton & Janet Labella, Landlord Failure to Resolve Shared Meter Problem Breaches Tenant’s Right to Quiet Enjoyment , 29 Clearinghouse Rev. 536 (1995).

#### 7. No Affirmative Duty Under R.C. 5321.04(A) to

Inspect the Premises R.C. 5321.04(A) does not impose an affirmative duty on a landlord to inspect the premises to discover defective conditions. Person-Thomas v. Quilliams-Noble Apts., L.L.C., 45 N.E.3d 654, 2015 -Ohio-4277, ¶ 27 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2015);

Mandelbaum v. Smith , No. 101888, 2015 WL 1276573, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Mar. 19, 2015); Cook v. Crossings, LLC, No. 13-CA-010463, 2015 WL 1137860,¶ 12 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 16, 2015); Hollowell v. Aplis , No. 100275, 2014 WL 1327957, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 20, 2014); Renzi v. Hillyer , No. 2012 -L- 331 041, 2012 WL 5995055, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 3, 2012);Butler v. Wyndtree
Hous. Ltd. P’ship, No. CA2011-03-056, 2012 WL 37535, ¶ 42 (Ohio App., 12th Dist.,
Jan. 9, 2012); Avila v. Gerdenich Realty Co., No. L-07-1098, 2007 WL 4216972 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 30, 2007); Lilly v. Bradford Inv. Co., No. 06AP-1227, 2007 WL 1640850, ¶ 29 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 7, 2007);Boyd v. Hariani, No. 22500, 2005 WL 2087824, ¶ 31 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 31, 2005).

For a discussion on the related issue of the landlord’s constructive notice of defective conditions at the residential premises, see Section IX.B.2.b, below.

#### 8. Reasonable Security Measures Against Criminal

Activity R.C. 5321.04 does not require a landlord to protect the tenant against criminal activity of a third person. Carmichael v. Colonial Square Apts., 38 Ohio App.3d 131, 528 N.E.2d 585 (10th Dist. 1987); Cherkiss v. Thomas, No. L-83-416, 1984 WL 7829 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 13, 1984).See also Brown v. Campbell, Nos. 85698,85702, 2005 WL 1793519 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 28, 2005); Johnson v. Spectru m o f Supportive Servs., No. 82267, 2003 WL 21982915 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 21, 2003); Sayles v. SB-92 Ltd. P’ship, 138 Ohio App.3d 476, 741 N.E.2d 613 (8th Dist.

2000); Doe v. Beach House Dev. Co., 136 Ohio App.3d 573, 737 N.E.2d 141 (8th Dist.

2000); Doe v. Flair Corp. , 129 Ohio App.3d 739, 719 N.E.2d 34 (8th Dist. 1998);

Meier v. Vistula Heritage Vill. , 62 Ohio Misc.2d 632, 609 N.E.2d 1360 (Ohio C.P., Lucas Cty., 1992); Pamer v. Pritchard Bros. , 61 Ohio Misc.2d 150, 575 N.E.2d 900 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty., 1990).

However, a landlord does have a duty to provide in the common area reasonable security measures against criminal activity. Carmichael v. Colonial Square Apts., 38 Ohio App.3d 131, 528 N.E.2d 585 (10th Dist. 1987); Sciascia v. Riverpark Apts ., 3 Ohio App.3d 164, 444 N.E.2d 40 (10th Dist. 1981). See also Pieper v. Williams, No.

L-05-1065, 2006 WL 964725 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 14, 2006); Johnson v.

Spectrum of Supportive Services , No. 82267, 2003 WL 21982915 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 21, 2003); Sayles v. SB-92 Ltd. P’ship, 138 Ohio App.3d 476, 741 N.E.2d 613 (8th Dist. 2000);Doe v. Beach House Dev. Co., 136 Ohio App.3d 573, 737 N.E.2d 141 (8th Dist. 2000); Kelly v. Bear Creek Inv. Co., No. 58011, 1991 WL 19152 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 14, 1991);Flowers v. City of Cincinnati, No. C-880809, 1990 WL 27176 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar. 14, 1990); Hawk v. Fairmeadows Apts., No. CA86- 12-180, 1987 WL 13707 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 29, 1987); Pamer v. Pritchard Bros., 61 Ohio Misc.2d 150, 575 N.E.2d 900 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty., 1990).

Sciascia held that this duty arises from the implied covenant of quiet enjoyment.

3 Ohio App. 3d at 166. See also Doe v. New Bedford Hous. Auth., 417 Mass. 273, 630 N.E.2d 248 (1994). Carmichael relied on Sciascia for its holding and thus presumably for the source of the duty. Pieper, Johnson, Sayles, Doe, Kelly, Flowers, Hawk, and Pamer rely on Sciascia and/or Carmichael for their holdings and thus presumably rely on Sciascia for the source of the duty as well.

Also, a “landlord may contractually [in a lease provision] create a duty to provide security to his tenants.” Brown v. Campbell, Nos. 85698,85702, 2005 WL 1793519, ¶ 23 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 28, 2005). Accord Pamer v. Pritchard Bros., 61 Ohio Misc.2d 150, 153, 575 N.E.2d 900 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty., 1990). For example, two courts have held that, where a landlord promises to provide window locks, it may create a contractual obligation to do so and, if the breach of that obligation is the proximate cause of criminal activity, the landlord may be liable for injury resulting from the criminal activity. Blair v. Prop. Mgmt. Consultants , 40 Ohio App.3d 103, 531 N.E.2d 752 (1st Dist. 1987); Buncy v. Daniels , No. CA -6059, 1983 WL 6486 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 13, 1983). See also Brown v. Campbell , Nos. 85698, 85702, 2005 WL 1793519 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 28, 2005) (accepts theBlair rule, but finds no firm offer to install the new doors and thus no contractual duty).

But a lease provision that “states that ‘disorderly conduct of any nature will result in an eviction’ .... does not create a duty on the part of [the landlord] to protect tenants from the criminal actions of other tenants.” Flowers v. City of Cincinnati , No. C - 880809, 1990 WL 27176, at *2 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar. 14, 1990).

If a landlord provides security services to protect tenants against criminal activity, the landlord is obligated to exercise reasonable care in providing those services.Pamer
v. Pritchard Bros., 61 Ohio Misc.2d 150, 575 N.E.2d 900 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty., 1990).

#### 9. Implied Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment, Common

Law A residential landlord is required to maintain the premises in compliance with the requirements of the common law implied covenant of quiet enjoyment. Cincinnati Ins.
Co. v. Evans , No. WD -09-012, 2010 WL 2334795 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 11, 2010); Liggins v. Westminster Arms, Inc., Nos. 98AP-377,378, 1998 WL 894613 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 22, 1998); Dworkin v. Paley, 93 Ohio App.3d 383, 638 N.E.2d 333 636 (8th Dist. 1994); Howard v. Simon, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist.

1984). See also Davis v. Sean M. Holley Agency, Inc., No. 23891, 2010 WL 4311683 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 29, 2010) (commercial lease; covenant of quiet enjoyment implied in every lease for realty); Hamilton Brownfields Redevelopment, LLC v. Duro Tire & Wheel, 156 Ohio App.3d 525, 806 N.E.2d 1039 (12th Dist. 2004) (same).

The landlord’s obligations under the implied covenant of quiet enjoyment are in addition to the R.C. Chapter 5321 obligations. Ayres v. Simmons , No. 93 -CA-827, 1993 WL 405489 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993).

“The covenant [of quiet enjoyment] is breached when the landlord ‘... obstruct[s], interfere[s] with, or take[s] away from the ... [tenant] in a substantial degree the beneficial use ...’ of the leasehold.” Howard v. Simon, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 16, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist. 1984) (quoting Frankel v. Steman, 92 Ohio St. 197, 200, 110 N.E.

747 (1915)). Accord Davis v. Sean M. Holley Agency, Inc. , No. 23891, 2010 WL 4311683 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 29, 2010);Cincinnati Ins. Co. v. Evans, No. WD- 09-012, 2010 WL 2334795 (Ohio App ., 6th Dist., June 11, 2010); Hamilton Brownfields Redevelopment, LLC v. Duro Tire & Wheel , 156 Ohio App.3d 525, 806 N.E.2d 1039 (12th Dist. 2004);Liggins v. Westminster Arms, Inc., Nos. 98AP-377,378, 1998 WL 894613 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 22, 1998); Dworkin v. Paley, 93 Ohio App.3d 383, 638 N.E.2d 636 (8th Dist. 1994);Ayres v. Simmons, No. 93-CA-827, 1993 WL 405489 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993).

“The degree of impairment required [to constitute a breach] is a question for the finder of fact.” Dworkin, 93 Ohio App.3d at 386. Accord Cincinnati Ins. Co. v. Evans,
No. WD-09-012, 2010 WL 2334795 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 11, 2010); Liggins v.

Westminster Arms, Inc., Nos. 98AP-377,378, 1998 WL 894613 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Dec. 22, 1998).

In Howard, the court held that “there was sufficient evidence for the finder of fact to determine that the frequent absence of air conditioning over a two -summer period breached the covenant.” 18 Ohio App. 3d at 16.

In Evans, the court upheld the trial court ’s breach of covenant finding, based on the owner’s placement of a lock box on the front door and grant of permission to prospective purchasers to enter the house unannounced, for a three-month period. 2010 WL 2334795, ¶¶ 12,25,31,54.

In Dworkin, the cour t held that material issues of fact existed as to whether the amount of cigarette smoke being transmitted into the tenant’s upper rental unit from a lower rental unit was sufficient to constitute a violation of the covenant of quiet enjoyment, and thus reversed a summary judgment for the landlord. 93 Ohio App.3d at 388.

In Liggins, the trial court found no breach of the covenant of quiet enjoyment, based on its finding that no witness at trial other than the tenant noticed an odor of marijuana in her apartment. 1998 WL 894613, at *4. The court of appeals held that the trial court did not abuse its discretion in making this finding and therefore upheld the finding. Id.

Generally, a landlord is not liable under the covenant of quiet enjoyment for the improper conduct of a third party such as a neighboring tenant. 274 E.g., Scarnati v.

Owners of Georgetown of Columbus , 96-APE01-52, 1996 WL 339963 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 18, 1996) (conduct of neighboring tenant);Ayres v. Simmons, No. 93- CA-827, 1993 WL 405489 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993) (conduct of non - tenant third party). See also Robert S. Schoshinski, American Law of Landlord and Tenant § 3:7 (1980 & Supp. 2015); 65 Ohio Jur.3dLandlord and Tenant § 180 (2006);

49 Am. Jur.2d Landlord and Tenant §§ 486, 487 (2006).

However, if a landlord expressly or impliedly authorizes the improper conduct of a third party, the landlord may be liable under the covenant of quiet enjoyment for the third party’s conduct. Robert S. Schoshinski, American Law of Landlord and Tenant § 3:7 (1980 & Supp. 2015); 65 Ohio Jur.3dLandlord and Tenant § 180 (2006); 49 Am.

Jur.2d Landlord and Tenant §§ 486, 487 (2006).

For example, if a neighboring tenant continually makes excessive noise of which the landlord is on notice, the landlord’s failure to exercise the landlord’s legal authority to abate it (including the commencement of an eviction action if necessary) may 274 Similarly, when a third -party tenant’s conduct is racial harassment that creates a hostile housing environment, and the landlord fails to take corrective action, the landlord is not liable to the tenant under R.C. 4112.02(H)(4). Ohio Civil Rights Comm’n v. Akron Metro. Hous.

Auth., 119 Ohio St.3d 77, 892 N.E.2d 415 (2008). However, in these circumstances, the landlord may be liable unde r the federal Fair Housing Act. See 24 C.F.R. § 100.7(a)(1)(iii).

E.g., Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC , 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018). But see Francis v. Kirk Manor, Inc. , 992 F.3d 67 (2nd Cir. 2021) (en banc). And a tenant’s neighbor may b e held liable under R.C. 4112.02(H)(12) for conduct that interferes with the tenant’s exercise or enjoyment of any other R.C. 4112.02(H) fair housing right. Ohio Civil Right Comm’n v. Myers, No. 25752, 2014 WL 201674 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 17, 2014).

335 constitute a breach of the covenant of quiet enjoyment. 275 Robert S. Schoshinski, American Law of Landlord and Tenant § 3:7 (1980 & Supp. 2015) (and cases cited therein). See also Wiesman v. Hill, 620 F. Supp.2d 106, 114 (D. Mass. 2009) (Mass.

Law). Cf. Dworkin v. Paley , 93 Ohio App.3d 383, 386, 638 N.E.2d 636 (8th Dist.

1994). But see Scarnati v. Owners of Georgetown of Columbus , No. 96 -APE01-52, 1996 WL 339963 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 18, 1996).

Also, if a landlord breaches the covenant of quiet enjoyment by failing to provide in the common area reasonable security measures against criminal activity, the landlord may be liable for the criminal activity of a third party. See Section VII.C.8, above.

Ohio law on the measure of damages for a breach of the implied covenant of quiet enjoyment is somewhat unsettled. Cincinnati Ins. Co. v. Evans, No. WD-09-012, 2010 WL 2334795, ¶ 52 (Ohio App., 6th Dist. June 11, 2010). The conflict is essentially between whether a breach of the covenant relieves the tenant of the obligation to pay any rent or reduces the tenant’s rental obligation to the rental value received (in light of the breach). Id., ¶¶ 52,53. “The latter approach is substantially [actually, precisely] the same as the measure for either a breach of duty set forth in a lease or a statutory duty.” Id., ¶ 53. For a discussion on that measure of damages, see Section IX.A.8.a, below.

Since the implied covenant of quiet enjoyment is an implied term of the lease, the latter approach is the better reasoned view. Furthermore, it will properly relieve the tenant of the obligation to pay any rent when the tenant is constructively evicted from the premises.

#### 10. Additional Terms of Rental Agreement

In the express terms of a re ntal agreement, a landlord may agree to assume additional duties to maintain the premises, provided they are consistent with R.C.

Chapter 5321. See R.C. 5321.06. For example, R.C. 5321.13(F) permits a rental agreement provision under which thelandlord assumes responsibility for fulfilling one 275 In these circumstances, the landlord may also be in violation of the R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) obligation to “do whatever is reasonably necessary to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition.” See Scarnati v. Owners of Georgetown of Columbus, No. 96-APE01-52, 1996 WL 339963 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 18, 1996) (applies R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) to complaint that neighboring tenant made excessive noise, but, based on landlord’s reasonable investigation, finds no excessive noise and thus no violation). See also Cohen v. Werner , 82 Misc.2d 295, 368 N.Y.S.2d 1005 (Civ. Ct. 1975) (breach of implied warranty of habitability).

or more of the tenant’s R.C. 5321.05 obligations, which are discussed in Section VII.D, below.

### D. Tenant Duty to Maintain and Properly

Use the Premises

#### 1. Summary of the R.C. 5321.05(A) Duties

A duty of all residential tenants is to maintain and use the premises in compliance with the requirements of R.C. 5321.05(A).

The R.C. 5321.05(A) requirements are to:
(1) keep the dwelling unit safe and sanitary, R.C. 5321.05(A)(1) (see Section VII.D.2, below);
(2) dispose of g arbage in a clean, safe, and sanitary manner, R.C.5321.05(A)(2);
(3) keep all plumbing fixtures in the dwelling unit as clean as their condition permits, R.C. 5321.05(A)(3);
(4) use and operate properly all electrical and plumbing fixtures, R.C.

5321.05(A)(4);
(5) comply with all applicable housing, health and safety codes, R.C.

5321.05(A)(5);
(6) refrain personally and to forbid guests from intentionally or negligently damaging or removing any fixture, appliance, or other part of the premises, R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) (see Section VII.D.3, below);
(7) maintain in good working order and condition any range, refrigerator, washer, dryer, dishwasher, or other appliance that the landlord has supplied and that the tenant is required to maintain by the terms of a written rental agreement, R.C. 5321.05(A)(7);
(8) conduct him/herself and to require guests to conduct themselves in a manner that will not disturb the neighbors’ peaceful enjoyment of the premises, R.C. 5321.05(A)(8) (see Section VII.D.4, below); and
(9) conduct him/herself and to requireguests to conduct themselves, relative to the premises, so as not to violate the prohibitions in R.C. Chapters 2925 and 3719, or in municipal ordinances that are similar to any section of those chapters, relative to controlled substances.

337

#### 2. Keep the Dwelling Safe and Sanitary, R.C.

5321.05(A)(1) R.C. 5321.05(A)(1) generally requires a tenant to keep the dwelling unit safe and
sanitary. Sanitary means clean; it does not mean free of mess. Vesta Cleveland LLC
v. Swann, No. 2003-CVG-5468 (Ohio Mun., Clevela nd, Hous. Div., May 20, 2003).

For example, piles of clothing are messy, not unclean. Id.

Also, “R.C. 5321.05(A)(1) applies only to those situations in which the tenant is occupying and using the leased premises.” Cent. Mut. Ins. Co. v. Faller , 71 Ohio Misc.2d 58, 61, 654 N.E.2d 213 (Ohio C.P., Licking Cty., 1995). Therefore, it does not apply to a co-tenant who is not in possession or control of the premises. Id.

#### 3. Duty Relative to Damage to the Premises, R.C.

5321.05(A)(6)

##### a. General Background

R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) requires a tenant to refrain personally and to forbid any other person who is on the premises with the tenant’s permission from intentionally or negligently damaging the premises.

“R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) does not require the tenant to preve nt damage to the leased premises, but rather personally to refrain from such activity and to forbid others from engaging in it.” DAK, PLL v. Borgerding, No. 02AP-1051, 2003 WL 21469136, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 26, 2003).

To constitute a violat ion of R.C. 5321.05(A)(6), the property damage must be beyond normal wear and tear. Whitestone Co. v. Stittsworth, No. 06AP-371, 2007 WL 155299, ¶ 27 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 23, 2007).

For a discussion on normal wear and tear, see Section XVIII.B, below.

A tenant’s failure to report defective conditions that are causing damage to the rental unit may constitute negligent destruction of the property under R.C.

5321.05(A)(6). Bailey v. Fairchild , No. 10 -CA-10, 2010 WL 4867532 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 24, 2010) (damage resulting from water leaking from a shower where tiles had fallen off and water entering the basement through a sewer).

Damage to the premises by a child under seven years of age cannot, by itself, constitute an R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) violat ion. As a matter of law, these children are incapable of the requisite negligence or intent. See DeLuca v. Bowden, 42 Ohio St.2d 392, 329 N.E.2d 109 (1975). However, in such circumstances, the tenant’s negligent supervision of the child may be the basis for the R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) violation.

##### b. Damage by Third-Party

R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) does not make the tenant strictly liable for a guest’s improper
conduct. Allstate Ins. Co. v. Dorsey , 46 Ohio App.3d 66, 545 N.E.2d 920 (9th Dist.

1988).

R.C. 5321.05(A)(6)does not make the tenant liable for the negligent acts of a guest.

Uptown Cmty. Partners, L.P. v. Killings , No. C -110012, 2011 WL 5438652 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Nov. 10, 2011); Ohio Cas. Ins. Co. v. Wills, 29 Ohio App.3d 219, 504 N.E.2d 1164 (3rd Dist. 1 985); Vesta Corp. v. Harris , No. 2021 -CVG-004702 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 20, 2022) . See also Auto Owners Ins. Co. v.

Hansford, No. 1-86-38, 1987 WL 17881 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 29, 1987).

The R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) obligation, relative t o a guest’s conduct, applies only if the tenant is cognizant of the guest’s presence, intentions, and conduct. Allstate Ins.
Co. v. Dorsey, 46 Ohio App.3d 66, 545 N.E.2d 920 (9th Dist. 1988).276 Upon observing a guest’s intentional or negligent conduct tha t may damage the premises, the tenant’s R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) obligation is to give a verbal command to the guest to discontinue the conduct. Ohio Cas. Ins. Co. v. Wills, 29 Ohio App.3d 219, 504 N.E.2d 1164 (3rd Dist. 1985).

Therefore, a tenant is not liable under R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) for a guest’s improper conduct: (1) if the tenant was not in the rental unit when the guest engaged in the conduct, Uptown Cmty. Partners, L.P. v. Killings , No. C-110012, 2011 WL 5438652 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Nov. 10, 2011); (2) if the tenant was unaware of the conduct, Allstate Ins. Co. v. Dorsey , 46 Ohio App.3d at 67 -68; Ohio Cas. Ins. Co. v. Wills , 29 Ohio App.3d 219, 504 N.E.2d 1164 (3rd Dist. 1985); (3) if, upon observing the conduct, the tenant verbally commands the guest to discontinue it, Wills, 29 Ohio App.3d at 221; or (4) if the damage to the leased premises is the result of the guest’s 276 One appellate court held that a lease provision that prohibited “repeated damage to the unit” was consistent with the Dorsey rule, even though it permitted the landlord to “evict a tenant whose unit is repeatedly damaged without presenting evidence that the tenant had knowledge of the intentions of the person who caused the damage.” Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Kirksey ,
No. CA -9116, 1 993 WL 135735, at *2 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 19, 1993). The court reasoned that “the prohibition against repeated damage recognizes a logical inference that when damage occurs repeatedly over a period of time, the tenant was aware of the actions of ot hers on the property and failed to prohibit further damage to the property.” Id.

339 assault of the tenant, DAK, PLL v. Borgerding, No. 02AP-1051, 2003 WL 21469136, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 26, 2003).

In Wills, the court held that the tenants did not violate the R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) obligation, relative to a guest’s conduct, notwithstanding the guest’s role in a fire that damaged the premises. The fire started in the kitchen from grease that became overheated, while the tenants’ daughter (age twenty) was cooking. The daughter was a permissive user but not a tenant of the premises. The tenants were not present in the kitchen at the time of the fire.

In Dorsey, on facts nearly identical to those in Wills, the court likewise held that the tenants did not violate the R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) obligation. TheDorsey tenants were asleep in the premises at the time of the fire.

In Killings, the tenant was shopping when a babysitter started a fire in the kitchen, while preparing food. The court, relying on Wills and Dorsey, held that the tenant could not be liable under R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) because the tenant was not in the rental unit at the time of the fire.

Whether the Wills/Dorsey rule, relative to a guest’s conduct, appliesto the conduct of a co-tenant may depend on whether the lease contains a joint and several liability
provision. See Wayne Mut. Ins. Co. v. Parks , No. 20945, 2002 WL 1800377 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 7, 2002); Kinn v. Showe Mgmt. Corp. , No. 5 -01-46, 2002 WL 462859 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Mar. 27, 2002); Young v. McCleese, No. 97-CA-2351, 1998 WL 399861 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 13, 1998); Cent. Mut. Ins. Co. v. Faller, 71 Ohio Misc.2d 58, 654 N.E.2d 213 (Ohio C.P., Licking Cty., 1995).

Young held that the Wills/Dorsey rule does not apply to a co-tenant. Kinn rejected the Young position and held that the Wills/Dorsey rule does apply to a co -tenant.

Wayne Mutual distinguished Kinn on the ground that the Kinn lease did not contain, and the Young lease did contain, a joint and several liability provision. Wayne Mutual held that a joint and several liability provision is consistent with R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) and that, if the lease contains a joint and several liabi lity provision, the Wills/Dorsey rule does not apply to a co-tenant. Faller held that the Wills/Dorsey rule applied to the co-tenant, but then held the non-negligent co-tenant liable under the lease, without any reference to a joint and several liability provision.

A tenant is not liable under R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) for the conduct of an unknown or non-guest third party. Wilhelm v. Ison, No. 92-CA-97, 1993 WL 76961 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 15, 1993) (when delivering a refrigerator for the landlord, store personnel damaged the floor of the premises); Jenkins v. Boyce, 94 Ohio Misc.2d 98, 703 N.E.2d 392 (Ohio Mun., Akron, 1998) (the criminal conduct of trespassers damaged the premises);Aetna Cas. & Surety Co. v. Neff, 30 F. Supp.2d 990 (S.D. Ohio
1998) (appl ying Ohio law) (the intentional acts of a third party, who was on the premises without the tenant’s permission, damaged the premises).

For a discussion on the application of R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) to assisted housing leases, see Section III.G.7, above.

#### 4. Do Not Disturb Neighbors’ Peaceful Enjoyment,

R.C. 5321.05(A)(8) R.C. 5321.05(A)(8) requires a tenant and the tenant’s guests to conduct themselves in a manner that will not disturb the neighbors’ peaceful enjoyment of the premises.

If the conduct does not disturb the neighbors’ peaceful enjoyment of the premises, it does not constitute an R.C. 5321.05(A)(8) violation. Walnut Hills Apts. v. Costner, 108 Ohio Misc.2d 43, 739 N.E.2d 416 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1999) (when the tenant threatened a security guard with a gun, it did not disturb the neighbors’ peaceful enjoyment of the premises).

Some courts have excluded criminal conduct from the R.C. 5321.05(A)(8) obligation, even if the criminal conduct disturbs the neighbors’ peaceful enjoyment of the premises. E.g., Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Fonseca, 110 Ohio App.3d 292, 673 N.E.2d 1019 (9th Dist. 1996) (assaulted neighbor with steak knife); Akron Metro.
Hous. Auth. v. Sistrunk , No. 12253, 1986 WL 2214 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Feb. 12,
1986) (shot gun at neighbor); Walnut Hills Apts. v. Costner, 108 Ohio Misc.2d 43, 739 N.E.2d 416 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1999) (threatened security guard with gun);

D.C. Hughes Mgmt. Co. v. Webster , No. B101,837 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Dec. 20,
1974) (discharged gun into the air).

In these cases, the courts are excluding criminal conduct from the R.C.

5321.05(A)(8) obligation in order to avoid application of the R.C. 5321.11 notice and right to cure requirements (see Section I.D.2, above). The unintended consequence of this position is that, absent a written lease with a provision that prohibits the criminal conduct, the criminal conduct is not grounds for terminating the tenancy.

At least one court has held that criminal conduct is not excluded from the R.C.

5321.05(A)(8) obligation. College Hills Apts. v. McLaughlin, No. 2010-CVG-04025 (Ohio Mun., Clermont Cty., Mar. 10, 2011). In McLaughlin, the tenant’s daughter assaulted another resident of the apartment complex. The court held that this criminal conduct constitute d an R.C. 5321.05(A)(8) violation and, therefore, the tenant was 341 entitled to an R.C. 5321.11 notice of termination.

Two reasonable arguments support the position that criminal conduct is not excluded from the R.C. 5321.05(A)(8) obligation.

First, in 1990, the legislature revised R.C. 5321.05 and 5321.11 so as to add an R.C. 5321.05 obligation relative to certain criminal conduct (i.e., R.C. 5321.05(A)(9)) and to expressly exclude that criminal conduct from the R.C. 5321.11 requirements.

In failing to exclude any other criminal conduct from the R.C. 5321.11 requirements, the legislature evidenced an intent not to exclude any other criminal conduct from the R.C. 5321.11 requirements.

Second, R.C. 5321.05(A) does not expressly exclude criminal conduct from its list of obligations. Indeed, some of its obligations (in addition to those in R.C.

5321.05(A)(9)), on their face, constitute criminal conduct – for example, the R.C.

5321.05(A)(5) code obligations. Since the R.C. 5321.05(A) obligations , including those in R.C. 5321.05(A)(8), are not ambiguous, courts are obligated to enforce them as written, and not infer a criminal conduct exclusion. See Cheap Escape Co. v.

Haddox, L.L.C., 120 Ohio St.3d 493, 900 N.E.2d 601, ¶ 9 (2008).

#### 5. Non-R.C. 5321.05(A) Duties Under the Rental

Agreement

##### a. Introduction

A rental agreement may impose tenant obligations regarding maintenance or use of the premises, in addition to the R.C. 5321.05(A) obligations. R.C. 5321.06.

However, those obligations may not be “in consistent with or prohibited by Chapter 5321 of the Revised Code or any other rule of law. Id.

##### b. Exterior Maintenance for Single-Family Dwellings

“The yard of a single-family home is considered to be part of the tenant’s property to possess and control.” Good v. Murd, No. L-13-1235, 2014 WL 2170302,¶ 10 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 23, 2014). As a result, these premises typically do not contain a common area.

With the absence of a common area, some exterior maintenance work for these premises fall outside the R.C. 5321.04(A) obligations. A rental agreement provision that requires the tenant to perform that work would generally not violate R.C. 5321.06.

A typical example of those work items is a lease provision that requires the tenant to perform lawn care. E.g., Douglas v. Campost Props., LLC , No. 2016-CV-102094 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 21, 2020). While that lease provision may require the tenant to mow the lawn on a regular basis, it “does not remotely imply a duty by [the tenant] to care for the beds in any respect, including pulling weeds.” Id., slip op. at 4.

In large part, R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) and R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) define the limits of these exterior maintenance work items. R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) excludes any exterior maintenance work item that falls within the landlord’s obligation under an applicable code that materially affects health and safety. R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) excludes any exterior maintenance work item that fall s within the landlord’s obligation to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition. For these excluded items, the rental agreement may require the tenant to perform the work, but only if the landlord provides the tenant with reasonable compensation for it. See Section II.R., above.

Conversely, with the absence of a common area, some exterior maintenance work for these premises fall within the tenant’s obligation under R.C. 5321.05(A)(1) – “to keep that part of the premises that he occupies and uses safe and sanitary.”See Section VI.D.2, above. For those exterior maintenance work items, the landlord may assume responsibility for them under a rental agreement provision. See R.C. 5321.13(F). But absence that assumption of responsibility,those items are the tenant’s obligation, even if the rental agreement is silent on this matter.

##### c. Other Provisions

Court decisions provide some guidance on other types of rental agreement
provisions. They include the following court decisions.

A rental agreement may restrict occupancy of the premises to the persons specified in the rental agreement. E.g., Parker v. Fisher, 17 Ohio App.3d 103, 477 N.E.2d 654 (9th Dist. 1984).

A rental agreement may prohibit pets.Douglas v. Campost Props., LLC, No. 2016- CV-102094, slip op. at 3 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 21, 2020). But any such prohibition is subject to the reasonable accommodation requirements of the federal and state laws on discrimination that are listed in Section II.U.1, above.

One court found a lease provision that made the tenant liable for any damage to the premises “caused by the tenant” to be consistent with R.C. 5321.05(A)(6).Donegal
Cos. v. White, No. 98-CO-1, 1999 WL 159222, at *5 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar. 18, 1999). However, it did so only upon finding that the provision did not impose strict liability because “[t]he words ‘caused by’ imply that proof of negligence is a threshold to her [tenant’s] liability.” Id.

343 With respect to guest conduct, a rental agreement provision may not ex pand the R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) requirements. Allstate Ins. Co. v. Dorsey, 46 Ohio App.3d 66, 68, 545 N.E.2d 920 (9th Dist. 1988); Aetna Cas. & Surety Co. v. Neff, 30 F. Supp.2d 990, 997 (S.D. Ohio 1998) (applying Ohio law).

Likewise, a rental agreement may not establish any right to terminate the tenancy that is inconsistent with R.C. 5321.11. See R.C. 5321.06. Therefore, a rental agreement may not authorize a termination of the tenancy for tenant conduct that violates an R.C. 5321.05(A) obligation (or a corresponding rental agreement provision) but does not materially affect health and safety.277 See Section I.D.2.a, above.

### E. Landlord Access to the Premises

Generally, a landlord may enter the premises for any of the purposes enumerated in R.C. 5321.05(B), and a tenant may not unreasonably withhold consent for the entry, provided the landlord gives the tenant reasonable advance notice of the entry and the landlord enters the premises at a reasonable time. R.C. 5321.04(A)(8), 5321.05(B).

E.g., T.K.D. Enters. v. Zimmermann, No. 97-CA-44, 1998 WL 380496 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 2, 1998) (unlawful entry, lack of notice); Ayres v. Simmons , No. 93 -CA- 827, 1993 WL 405489 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993) (lawful entry).

The purposes for which a landlord may enter the premises under R.C. 5321.05(B) are: (1) to inspect the premises; (2) to make ordinary, necessary, or agreed repairs, decorations, alterations, or improvements to the premises; (3) to deliver parcels that are too large for the tenant’s mail facilities; (4) to supply necessary or agreed services;

and (5) to exhibit the premises to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workmen, or contractors.

Generally, twenty-four hours advance notice is presumed to be reasonable advance notice of the landlord’s entry, in the absence of evidence to the contrary. R.C.

5321.04(A)(8). See also T.K.D. Enters. v. Zimmermann , No. 97 -CA-44, 1998 WL 380496 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 2, 1998); Ayres v. Simmons, No. 93-CA-827, 1993 WL 405489 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993); Spencer v. Blackmon , 22 Ohio Misc.2d 52, 490 N.E.2d 943 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1985).

The landlord’s obligation to “give the tenant reasonable advance notice of his intent to enter” is met only if the tenant receives “some sort of notice.” Limage v.

277 For a discussion on the related rule that a non -material breach of a lease is not grounds to terminate the tenancy, see Section II.W, above.

Citiscene Props., No. 92AP-190, 1992 WL 131840, at *2 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 9, 1992). For example, this notice require ment is not met if the landlord’s only attempts to provide the tenant with notice of the entry are an unanswered telephone call on the day of entry and an unanswered knock on the door of the dwelling unit at the time of entry. Limage v. Citiscene Props., No. 92AP-190, 1992 WL 131840 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 9, 1992).

However, a landlord’s entry to perform repairs does not violate this notice requirement, notwithstanding the absence of an advance notice, if the tenant voluntarily permits the landlord to enter and the landlord’s entry is in response to the tenant’s repair request. Wayne Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jackson, Nos. 2369,2403, 1988 WL 107026 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Oct. 12, 1988).

The twenty-four-hour advance notice requirement does not apply “in the case of emergency or if it is impracticable to do so.” R.C. 5321. 04(A)(8). Generally, “[a]n emergency means an unforeseen occurrence or combination of circumstances which calls for immediate action leaving no time for deliberation.” Helvich v. George A.

Rutherford Co., 96 Ohio App. 367, 371, 114 N.E.2d 514 (8th Dist. 1953).

One court has construed the R.C. 5321.04(A)(8) emergency provision consistent with this Helvich definition. Spencer v. Blackmon, 22 Ohio Misc.2d 52, 490 N.E.2d 943 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1985). The Spencer decision rejected a landlord’s claim that the emergency provision authorized his attempted entry. The landlord attempted to enter the premises without advance notice in order to install smoke
detectors. The purported emergency was that an ordinance deadline for installing the smoke detectors had expired. But the landlord had received advance notice of the deadline.

Two courts have held that: (1) a tenant’s storage locker, by itself, does not meet the R.C. 5321.01(F) definition of dwelling unit; (2) therefore, the tenant’s storage locker is not part of the tenant’s dwelling unit under R.C. 5321.01(F) ; and (3) as a result, a landlord’s unreasonable entry into the tenant’s storage locker does not violate R.C. 5321.04(A)(7) or R.C. 5321.04(A)(8). Gibson v. Shephard, 87 N.E.3d 846, 2017- Ohio-1157, ¶ 40 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2017);Smith v. CBert Props., LLC, No. 28058, 2019 WL 102081 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 4, 2019) (follows Gibson).

A better-reasoned position for this issue is that the tenant’s storage locker is one component of (and thus part of) the tenant’s dwelling unit under R.C. 5321.01(F) and, therefore, a landlord’s entry into the tenant’s storage locker is governed by R.C.

5321.04(A)(7) and R.C. 5321.04(A)(8). The components of “residential premises” 345 under R.C. 5321.01(C) fall i nto two general categories – those solely for the use and occupancy of the tenant and those for the use of all tenants ( i.e., common areas and common facilities). The main purpose for restricting a landlord’s entry into a tenant’s dwelling unit – protection of the tenant’s privacy interests – is best advanced if the term dwelling unit includes all properties and facilities to which the tenant has sole use and occupancy (and excludes all common areas and common facilities). Under this construction, the term dwelling unit includes a tenant’s storage locker (which is akin to an attic or a closet) because the tenant has sole use and occupancy of that area.

Irrespective of whether the purpose and the advance notice of the entry meet the requirements of R.C. 5321.05(B) and R.C. 5321.04(A)(8), a landlord may not enter the premises “in an unreasonable manner” and a landlord may not make “repeated demands for entry that are otherwise lawful but have the effect of harassing the tenant.” R.C. 5321.04(B).

Also, a landlord may ‘[n]ot abuse the right of access conferred by division (B) of section 5321.05 of the Revised Code.” R.C. 5321.04(A)(7).

These R.C. 5321.04(A)(7),(8), R.C. 5321.04(B), and R.C. 5321.05(B) rights and duties, on landlord access to the premises, c annot be waived or modified by a rental
agreement. R.C. 5321.06, 5321.13(A). See also T.K.D. Enters. v. Zimmermann, No.

97-CA-44, 1998 WL 380496, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 2, 1998).

Notwithstanding these rights and duties, neither R.C. Chapter 53 21 nor common law requires a tenant, who has installed a lock on an exterior door of the tenant’s dwelling unit, to provide the landlord with a key to the lock. Spencer v. Blackmon, 22 Ohio Misc.2d 52, 490 N.E.2d 943 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1985). How ever, a lease provision may require the tenant to do so and such provisions are valid and enforceable.

### F. Rent


#### 1. Definition

Common law generally defines rent as consideration for the use and occupancy of real property for a specified term. Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. McKee , 37 Ohio App.3d 102, 104, 524 N.E.2d 180 (2nd Dist. 1987) (“The ordinary meaning of the term ‘rent’ implies an amount paid as consideration for use or occupancy of property”);

Loveless v. Erie R.R., 2 Ohio App. 404 (11th Dist. 1914) (“Rent may be defined to be the consideration agreed to be paid by one party for the use and occupancy of the real estate of another”). See also M.E. Blatt Co. v. United States, 305 U.S. 267, 277 (1938) (“Rent is a fixed sum … to be paid at stated times for the use of property”); Velez v.

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., 795 F.3d 578, 585 (6th Cir. 2015) (“The definition of rent plainly includes the tenant’s total expense for the us e of land during the term of occupancy”);Aujero v. CDA TODCO, Inc., 756 F.2d 1374, 1376 (9th Cir. 1985) (“Rent normally connotes ‘consideration paid for use or occupation of property’”).

The specified term element of the definition of rent is well-established in the case law that also defines a lease. “A lease is a conveyance of an estate in real property for a limited term, with conditions attached, in consideration for rent.” Judson v.

Lyendecker, No. 12AP-615, 2013 WL 1187618, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 10th Dis t., Mar. 21, 2013). Accord Amick v. Sickles, 177 Ohio App.3d 337, 894 N.E.2d 733, ¶ 19 (4th Dist.

2008); Fadelsak v. Hagley, No. 02-CA-41, 2003 WL 21489613, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 25, 2003); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Watkins , 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 23, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984). See also State Nat’l Bank of El Paso v. United States, 509 F.2d 832, 835 (5th Cir. 1975) (“In black letter law, a lease is a transfer of an interest in and possession of property for a prescribed period of time in exchange for an agreed consideration, called ‘rent’”).

Under common law, when the consideration due from a tenant to a landlord is not for the use and occupancy of the real property for a specified term, it is a “miscellaneous charge.” Miscellaneous charges are charges for lease violations, for security deposits, and for special services. See Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. v. McKee, 37 Ohio App.3d 102, 103, 524 N.E.2d 180 (2nd Dist. 1987) (unreasonable wear and tear penalties, late charges, and security deposits are miscellaneous charges, not rent);

Aujero v. CDA TODCO, Inc. , 756 F.2d 1374, 1376 (9th Cir. 1985) (mandatory meal plan charges (i.e., special services) are miscellaneous charges, not rent).

Generally, miscellaneous charges are not part of the rent. See Dayton Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. McKee, 37 Ohio App.3d 102, 103, 524 N.E.2d 180 (2nd Dist. 1987) (“we must determine if the retroactive payments [for past due rent] are to be considered rent or miscellaneous charges”).

A miscellaneous charge is part of the rent only if the lease so designates it. E.g., Strip Del., L.L.C. v. Landry’s Rests., Inc. , 191 Ohio App.3d 822, 947 N.E.2d 1233, ¶ 28 (5th Dist. 2010) (attorney’s fees part of the rent because the lease defined rent to include all charges under t he lease). See also In re Carol Janet, Inc. , 49 B.R. 847
