(Bankr. E.D. Wis. 1985) (late payment charges and certain costs part of the rent because the lease defined rent to include all charges under the lease).

347

#### 2. Form of Payment

The form of a rent paymen t is usually a monetary payment. Reid v. Plainsboro Partners, III, Nos. 09AP-442,456, 2010 WL 3610931 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 16, 2010).

But the form of a rent payment also may be an agreement to perform or pay for specified services. Reid v. Plain sboro Partners, III , Nos. 09AP -442,456, 2010 WL 3610931, ¶ 36 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 16, 2010). “Given its contractual basis, we see no reason to limit the term rent to financial payments. Nothing in Ohio’s Landlord Tenant Act prevents parties fro m agreeing to allow a tenant to perform services as a rent obligation in lieu of making financial payments.” Id. See also Lee
v. Wallace, 186 Ohio App.3d 18, 926 N.E.2d 328, ¶ 27 (8th Dist. 2010).

Examples of court decisions that have upheld such agre ements include: Scott v.

Schuster, No. 29253, 2019 WL 5607918, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Oct. 30, 2019) (tenant required to pay the electric bills); Ramsdell v. Ramsdell, No. L-12-1113, 2013 WL 494019, ¶ 14 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 8, 2013) (tenant re quired to pay the utilities and property tax, and to make certain repairs to the property); Reid v.

Plainsboro Partners, III , Nos. 09AP442,456, 2010 WL 3610931, ¶ 37 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 16, 2010) (tenant “performed ministerial duties and permitte d her apartment to be used as a sample unit for prospective tenants to tour”); Tucker v.

Kanzios, No. 08-CA-009429, 2009 WL 1655029 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 15, 2009) (tenant required to perform cooking, cleaning, and driving services);Quigg v. Mullins,
No. L-89-314, 1991 WL 59886 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 19, 1991) (tenant required to make certain improvements to the garage, make repairs to the landlord’s cars, and pay half of the landlord’s electric bill);Elder v. Elder, No. 06-CVG-00752 (Ohio Mun., Bowling Green, June 15, 2006) (tenant required to pay the property tax, the insurance, and the utilities). But see Amick v. Sickles, 177 Ohio App.3d 337, 894 N.E.2d 733 (4th
Dist. 2008) (tenant’s payment of the utilities, alone, did not constitute rent).

#### 3. Prerequisite to Landlord-Tenant Relationship

An obligation to pay rent, in one of the recognized forms (see Section VII.F.2, above), is a prerequisite to the existence of a landlord -tenant relationship. Mercer v.

Halmbacher, 44 N.E.3d 1011, 2015-Ohio-4167 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., 2015); Lee v.

Wallace, 186 Ohio App.3d 18, 926 N.E.2d 328 (8th Dist. 2010);Amick v. Sickles, 177 Ohio App.3d 337, 894 N.E.2d 733 (4th Dist. 2008); Stone v. Cazeau , No. 07 -CA- 009164, 2007 WL 4146777 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 26, 2007); Quigg v. Mullins,
No. L-89-314, 1991 WL 59886 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 19, 1991).

#### 4. Amount of Obligation, Initial or Increase

Ohio law does not contain rent control provisions for residential tenancies. See Mesnick v. De Ville Apts., No. 71517, 1997 WL 599376, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Sept. 25, 1997) (“Ohio law does not place property owners under an obligation to limit their rental prices to any standard of reasonableness”).

Therefore, a tenant’s rental obligat ion generally may be whatever amount the landlord and tenant agree upon, unless the tenant resides in assisted housing.278 A landlord’s right to demand a rent increase to any particular amount is subject to three limitations, which are summarized below.

One limitation to the landlord’s right to demand a rent increase is the term of the tenancy, even if it is a month-to-month tenancy.

To effect a rent increase in a month -to-month tenancy (or any other periodic tenancy), the landlord must terminate the existing tenancy, and the landlord and tenant must enter into a new tenancy with the increased rental obligation. See Steiner v.

Minkowski, 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist. 1991).

If the landlord terminates the existing month -to-month tenancy and proposes the new month -to-month tenancy with the increased rental obligation, and the tenant objects to the proposed rent increase but remains in possession of the premises, the tenant then becomes a holdover tenant. Id. See also Fredricks v. Stromber g, No.

42601, 1981 Ohio App. LEXIS 10469, at *8 -15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 2, 1981).

In these circumstances, the tenant’s rental obligation is not the increased rental obligation that the landlord proposed. Id. Rather, it is the reasonable value of the tenant’s use and occupancy of the premises. Id.

Since R.C. 5321.17(B) requires a thirty-day notice to terminate a month-to-month tenancy, a month-to-month tenant is generally entitled to thirty days advance notice of any proposed rent increase. Schwenkel v. Daley , No. 92 -CVG-2825 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, Jan. 19, 1993). But cf. McMahon v. Warner , No. 1711, 1984 WL 5695 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 17, 1984) (thirty days advance notice is not required to increase the rent of a month-to-month tenant if the tenant does not timely object to the 278 For the rent formulas for assisted housing tenancies, see the HUD regulations that are listed in Section XXIV.B. 4, below. For a discussion on these rent formulas, see National Housing Law Project, HUD Housing Programs: Tenants’ Rights ch. 4 (5th ed. 2018).

349 rent increase).

A second limitation to the landlord’s right to demand a rent increase is the R.C.

5321.02 prohibition against retaliation. R.C. 5321.02 prohibits a landlord from demanding a rent increase because the t enant: (1) complained to an appropriate governmental agency regarding code violations that materially affect health and safety;
(2) complained to the landlord regarding violations of R.C. 5321.04; or (3) joined a tenant organization. E.g., Bland v. Kovein, No. 16179, 1993 WL 473837 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 10, 1993) (rent increase in retaliation for tenant’s complaint to health department). For further discussion on the R.C. 5321.02 prohibition against retaliation, see Section II.T, above, and Chapter XI, below.

A third limitation to the landlord’s right to demand a rent increase is the R.C.

5321.14 prohibition against unconscionable lease provisions. When an alleged unconscionable provision is an increased rent, the relevant issue is whether the amount of the new rent (and not the amount of the rent increase) is unconscionable. Pojman
v. Columbia-Brookpark Mgmt., LLC, No. 88666, 2007 WL 2269682 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 9, 2007). Given the Pojman holding, an earlier trial court decision, which was vacated on jurisdictional grounds, also erred in holding that the analogous R.C.

3733.16 (now R.C. 4781.48) prohibition against unconscionable lease provisions is inapplicable to rent charges. Columbia-Brook Park Mgmt., LLC v. Schindler, No. 01- CVJ-02061 (Ohio Mun., Berea, Mar. 1, 2002), vacated and dismissed , No. 81092, 2003 WL 1090620 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 13, 2003).

#### 5. Place for Payment

The place at which a tenant is required to pay the rent is generally established in the rental agreement. For e xample, the rental agreement may require the tenant to deposit the rent payments into the landlord’s bank account (see Section VII.F.6.c,
below) or to mail the rent payments to a specified address (see Section VII.F.6.d, below).

“In the absence of an agr eement to the contrary, the general rule in Ohio is that payment of rent is to be made at the leased premises and that it is the landlord’s duty to go to the premises to collect rent from the tenant.” Davis v. Scullark, No. 57456, 1990 WL 145642, at *2 (O hio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 4, 1990). See also Slothower v.

Lally, 74 N.E.2d 654 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, 1947). For a discussion on rent collection at the premises, see Section II.L, above.

#### 6. Acceptance of Payment


##### a. Payment by Check or Money Order

When a tenant tenders a check (or money order) to a landlord for a rent payment, “the act of cashing [the] check constitutes acceptance” of the rent payment.Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 9, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985).Accord Miami Valley United Methodist Mission Soc’y v. White-Dawson, No. 17873, 2000 WL 234712, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 3, 2000). See generally First Nat’l Bank & Trust Co. v. Fireproof Warehouse & Storage , 8 Ohio App.3d 253, 456 N.E.2d 1336 (10th Dist. 1983).

A la ndlord may not negate such acceptance of the check (or money order) by tendering back to the tenant a subsequent check in the amount of the rent payment.

Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d at 9.

An exception to this rule applies when a landlord inadvertently cashes one check, out of many checks that are offered and promptly returned to the tenant, and the money for the cashed check is returned as soon as the landlord discovers the mistake.

Associated Estates Corp. v. Smith , No. 46054, 1984 WL 5227 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Oct. 4, 1984). The inadvertent element was not met in a case where the landlord accepted rent payments for April and May and then returned the payments at the end of May. Woodson v. Howard , No. 2008 -CVG-9709 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., June 30, 2008).

If the landlord does not cash the check or otherwise convert the check to the landlord’s use, but nevertheless retains the check, there may be acceptance of the rent
payment. The landlord’s retention of the check constitutes acceptance of the rent payment, unless the landlord: (1) notifies the tenant, upon receipt of the check, that it is not being accepted or that it is being held only for evidentiary purposes; and (2) returns the check to the tenant on or before the day of trial. Pace v. Buck, 86 Ohio
App. 25, 85 N.E.2d 401 (10th Dist. 1949). Accord Eureka Multifamily Group v.

Terrell, No. L -14-1152, 2015 WL 2329168 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 15, 2015);

Urban P’ship Bank v. Mosezit Acad., Inc., No. 100712, 2014 WL 4244346 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 28, 2014); N. Face Props., Inc. v. Lin, No. CA2010-09-083, 2013 WL 2423802 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 3, 2013); Wingate at Belle Meadows v.

Higgenbotham, 179 Ohio App.3d 645, 903 N.E.2d 349 (2nd Dist. 2008); King v.

Dolton, No. 02-CA-0041, 2003 WL 21078088 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 14, 2003);

Simco Mgmt. Corp. v. Snyder, No. 98-CA-210, 2000 WL 309396 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., 351
Mar. 20, 2000); Cipolla v. McCloskey, No. 97 -CA-006866, 1998 WL 852810 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 9, 1998); Dundon v. Balluck, No. 93-CA-123, 1994 WL 55755 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 23, 1994); Stacy v. Carr , No. CA -519, 1992 WL 37783 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Feb. 20, 1992); Rapid Response W. Real Estate P’ship v. Rapid Response W., Inc., No. 13212, 1988 WL 21287 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Feb. 17, 1988);

Andrews Sch. v. Brown, No. 11-040, 1986 WL 3204 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 14, 1986).279 Under the Pace standard, if a landlord fails to provide the tenant with timely notification of the landlord’s nonacceptance of the check, the landlord has accepted the tenant’s rent payment, even if the landlord tenders the check to the tenant (or to the
court) on the day of trial. E.g., Royer, Kohr & Griffith v. Hobbs, No. M88-CVG-24901 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 24, 1988);Sunbury Gardens v. Pizza, No. M85-CVG- 36063 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Feb. 6, 1986). See also King v. Dolton, No. 02-CA- 0041, 2003 WL 21078088 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 14, 2003); Dundon v. Balluck,
No. 93-CA-123, 1994 WL 55755 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 23, 1994).

Four examples of court decisions finding timely landlord notice of nonacceptance under the Pace standard are summarized below.
(1) Juergens v. Strileckyj, No. 2010-CA-36, 2010 WL 4158508 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 22, 2010) (tenant tendered rentchecks monthly March to June 2009; in June 2009, landlord notified tenant that she did not deposit or accept the checks, because of their incorrect amount, and an eviction notice would be sent if tenant did not tender the correct amount;

thereafter, on June 30, 2009, landlord served a notice to vacate; and on July 17, 2009, landlord filed an FED complaint).
(2) Wingate at Belle Meadows v. Higgenbotham, 179 Ohio App.3d 645, 903 N.E.2d 349 (2nd Dist. 2008) (landlord returned the money order one day after landlord had received it).
(3) Simco Mgmt. Corp. v. Snyder, No. 98-CA-210, 2000 WL 309396 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar. 20, 2000) (landlord provided the notice of 279 See also Rogers v. Jones, No. 2014-CVG-028113 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 30, 2014);

Woodson v. Howard, No. 2008-CVG-9709 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 30, 2008);

Geyer v. Wilcots, No. CVG-0200482 (Ohio County Ct., Butler Cty., Area II, Nov. 22, 2002);

Cleveland Hous. Network Ltd. P’ship X v. Salgado , No. 96 -CVG-10871 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 16, 1996); Royer, Kohr & Griffith v. Hobbs, No. M88-CVG-24901 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 24, 1988); Marchioni v. Wilson , 20 Ohio Misc.2d 10, 485 N.E.2d 1073 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1984).

nonacceptance of two rent checks almost three weeks before trial, when landlord returned the checks after retaining them for approximately two weeks and four weeks, respectively).
(4) Stacy v. Carr, No. CA-519, 1992 WL 37783 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Feb.

20, 1992) (tenant tendered the rent check six days before trial, and landlord provided the notice of nonacceptance at the commencement of trial).

##### b. Payment by Third Party

When a tenant tenders a Salvation Army rent voucher to a landlord for a payment of rent, the landlord’s acceptance of the voucher constitutes an acceptance of rent. W.

Alex Vill. v. Goodwin, No. 88-CVG-179 (Ohio Mun., Eaton, Oct. 19, 1988).

When a landlord accepts a partial rent payment from the Red Cross on behalf of a tenant, the landlord is bound by the non-eviction provision in the Red Cross application that the landlord signs. Brumbaugh v. Epperl ey, No. 95 -CVGT-728 (Ohio Mun., Miami Cty., May 19, 1995) (the application stated that the $100 payment from Red Cross would result in the tenant not being evicted at that time). If a landlord commences an eviction action in violation of the non -eviction provision, the tenant may enforce the provision as a defense to the eviction action. Id.

When a landlord indicates a willingness to accept a rent payment from a county department of human services (“CDHS”) on behalf of a tenant and, as a result, the tenant does not seek the rent money from other sources, the landlord is estopped from refusing the rent payment from CDHS. Kamvouris v. Banks, No. 00-CVG-1586 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, July 24, 2000).

##### c. Payment by Bank Deposit

“It has long been settled in this state that when a rental agreement provides that the tenant is to pay rent by depositing the payments into the landlord’s bank account, the tenant’s depositing of the rent into the landlord’s bank account constitutes acceptance of the rent by the landlord unless the landlord has given the bank contrary instructions.” Ebbing v. Mathis, No. CA2013-01-014, 4806940, ¶ 22 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Sept. 9, 2013). Accord Kachelmacher v. Laird, 92 Ohio St. 324, 110 N.E. 933 (1915); Urban P’ship Bank v. Mosezit Acad., Inc., No. 100712, 2014 WL 4244346 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 28, 2014); N. Face Props., Inc. v. Lin , No. CA2010 -09-083, 2013 WL 2423802 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 3, 2013);Associated Estates Corp. v. Bartell, 24 Ohio App.3d 6, 492 N.E.2d 841 (8th Dist. 1985); Rogers v. Jones , No. 2014 -CVG- 353 028113 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 30, 2014); Woodson v. Howard , No. 2008 - CVG-9709 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 30, 2008) (electronic deposit);

McConnelsville Assocs. v. Gladden, No. 92-CVG-56 (Ohio County Ct., Morgan Cty.,
Sept. 28, 1992).

##### d. Payment by Mail

When a rental agreement provides that the tenant is to pay rent by mailing the payments to the landlord, the tenant’s timely mailing of a rent payment to the landlord, in an envelope properly addressed and stamped, constitutes acceptance of the rent by the landlord. Townhomes Mgmt. v. Carter, No. 84AP-851, 1985 WL 9874 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 19, 1985);Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Riley, No. CVG-93-19379 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., Jan. 20, 1994),aff’d, No. L-94-069, 1994 WL 590503 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 28, 1994); Edwards v. Smith , No. M’8711-CVG-37183 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 8, 1987);Norskog v. Atha, 61 Ohio Law Abs. 604, 102 N.E.2d 907 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, 1951).

In such circumstances, the tenant’s responsibility does not include proper delivery of the mail by the Postal Service. Id.

When a lease permits rent payment by mail, the tenant’s rent payment is timely if the envelope containing the rent payment is postmarked by the due date, unless the lease expressly requires landlord receipt by the due date. Crossings, LLC v. Coinmach Corp., No. 2008-CVG-01528 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, June 12, 2008).

#### 7. Month to Which Payment Applies

When past and current rentare due, and the landlord accepts a rent payment that is less than the total amount due, 280 an issue may arise as to which month(s) the rent payment applies.

The rental agreement may specify a method for making this determination. For example, it may spec ify that the rent payment shall be applied chronologically, beginning with earliest month of unpaid rent.

Absent a rental agreement provision on this issue, the determination is made as follows.

The tenant may determine the month(s) to which the rent shallbe applied, provided 280 Generally, a landlord is not required to accep t a partial payment of rent. See Section II.J, above.

the tenant so informs the landlord at the time of the rent payment and the landlord accepts the rent payment. Shields v. Barner-Mead Lumber Co., 7 Ohio Law Abs. 638, 638 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1929) (“the law is universal that a debt or may direct the money to be paid upon a particular debt that he owes and, in the absence of such direction, the creditor may apply the payment to any item he desires”). See also Cent.

### W. Cas. Co. v. W.E. Wright Co. , 21 Ohio Law Abs. 355, 356 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,

1936) (“It is conceded that as between a debtor and his creditor, the debtor has a right, when payments are made, to direct their application and that if he fails to do so, the creditor may apply the payments as he sees fit”). See generally Sotir v. United States, 978 F.2d 29, 30 (1st Cir. 1992).But see Marks v. Young, No. 2456-M, 1996 WL 63033 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Feb. 14, 1996) (dicta).

The tenant’s determination, on the month(s) to which the rent payment(s) shall apply, may be evidenced by circumstances as well as words. See L & T East 22 Realty
Co. v. Earle, 192 Misc.2d 75, 745 N.Y.S.2d 369 (App. Term 2002). InEarle, the court held that it was clear to the landlord that the tenant intended to apply the payment to the current month, si nce the parties were awaiting a one -time payment by the Department of Social Services for the full arrearage.

If the tenant does not make a determination timely, the landlord may determine the months to which the rent payments shall be applied. Shields, 7 Ohio Law Abs. at 638;
Cent. W. Cas. Co. , 21 Ohio Law Abs. at 356. See also Westgate Vill. Mobile Home Park v. Fetro , No. CVG -950312, 1995 WL 907587 (Ohio Mun., Fostoria, Sept. 27, 1995).

If neither the tenant nor the landlord makes this determination tim ely, the court may determine the month(s) to which the rent payment shall be applied, based on the equities of the case. Cent. W. Cas. Co., 21 Ohio Law Abs. at 356. See generally In re Wines, 997 F.2d 852, 855 (11th Cir. 1993) (“if a creditor fails to des ignate within a reasonable time how payment is to be applied, the court may allocate the payment in an equitable manner”).

#### 8. Due Date of Payment

The date by which a tenant is required to pay the rent (“rent due date”) is generally established by a written rental agreement or by a periodic tenancy.

When a written rental agreement specifies the rent due date, at least four circumstances may modify it.

First, when a written lease contains a grace period for the payment of rent, the 355 grace period becomes the rent due date. Showe Mgmt. Corp. v. Moore, No. 08-CA-10, 2009 WL 1372986 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 14, 2009) (ten -day grace period);

Columbus Metro. Hous. Authority v. Jackson , No. 81AP-802, 1982 WL 4010 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 4, 1982) (ten-day grace period).

Second, when the rent due date is a Sunday, R.C. 1.14 extends the time for payment to the following Monday. E.g., Myszka v. Bledsoe, No. 00-CVG-08398 (Ohio Mun., Akron, Nov. 9, 2000).

Third, when an eviction action is dismissed or an eviction judgment is entered in favor of the tenant, with respect to the rent that accrued during the eviction action, the rent due date is a reasonable time after the conclusion of the eviction action. See Section II.M, above.

Fourth, a landlord’s course of conduct of accepting late rent payments from the tenant may change the rent due date to the latest date on which the landlord customarily accepts the rent payments. See Section II.H, above.

When a written rental agreement requires monthly rental p ayments, but fails to specify a rent due date, the rent due date is the last day of the month.Costanzo v. Gross,
No. 2008-CVG-4234 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Nov. 21, 2008);Crossings, LLC v.

Coinmach Corp., No. 2008-CVG-01528 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, June 12, 2008).

#### 9. Late Payment Fee


##### a. Written Authorization Prerequisite

A landlord may not assess a tenant a fee for late payment of rent without a written agreement between the parties that expressly permits the assessment of the fee.

Neubauer v. Patzkowsky, No. 91AP-1236, 1992 WL 125269 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 2, 1992); Moran v. Foster, No. 97 -CVG-300 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, Dec. 30, 1997).

##### b. Liquidated Damages or Penalty

Late payment fees are subject to the contract law on liquidated damages and
penalties. The rule for distinguishing between contract terms that are enforceable liquidated damages and contract terms that are unenforceable penalties is:

Where the parties have agreed on the amount of damages, ascertained by estimation and adjustment, and have expressed this agreement in clear and unambiguous terms, the amount so fixed should be treated as liquidated damages and not as a penalty, if the damages would be
(1) uncertain as to amount and difficult of proof, and if (2) the contract as a whole is not so manifestly unconscionable, unreasonable, and disproportionate in amount as to justify the conclusion that it does not express the true intentions of the parties, and if (3) the contract is consistent with the conclusion that it was the intention of the parties that damages in the amount stated should follow the breach thereof.

Samson Sales, Inc. v. Honeywell, Inc ., 12 Ohio St.3d 27, 465 N.E.2d 392, syllabus (1984). Accord Lake Ridge Acad. v. Carney , 66 Ohio St.3d 376, 613 N.E.2d 183 (1993); Mark-It Place Foods, Inc. v. New Plan Excel Realty Trust, Inc ., 156 Ohio App.3d 65, 804 N.E.2d 979 (4th Dist. 2004).

The principal elements of the Honeywell test, for determining whether a late payment fee is a penalty, are (1) whether the damages are uncertain as to amount and difficult of proof and (2) whether the amount of the fee is reasonably related to the landlord’s actual damages.

The Eighth Appellate District applied these factors in two late payment fee cases:

Siara Mgmt. v. Nedley , No. 61433, 1992 WL 2 92248 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 15, 1992), and 200 W. Apts. v. Foreman , No. 66107, 1994 WL 505271 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 15, 1994).

In Nedley, the court held that “the damages flowing from a breach of a lease are not uncertain as to amount and difficult of proof.” 1992 WL 292248, at *1. Based on this holding and the Honeywell test, the court held that the late payment fee was a penalty and thus unenforceable. See also Beatley v. Schwartz , No. 03AP -911, 2004 WL 1244360, ¶ 26 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 8, 2004) (“Clearly damages resulting from the breach of a residential lease are not difficult to prove or uncertain as to
amount. Thus, plaintiff’s argument that he is entitled to the security deposit because of the ‘liquidated damage fee’ provision in the lease is without merit”).

In Foreman, the court held that the late payment fee was not reasonably related to the landlord’s actual damages because the landlord failed to adduce any evidence that it had sustained actual damages. The court held that the tenant’s failure to pay rent for two months did not constitute actual damages because the landlord was awarded a money judgment for the unpaid rent. 281 Based on these holdings, the court held that the late payment fee was a penalty and thus unenfor ceable. See also ATKM Mgmt.

281 For further discussion on late payment fees, when a judgment has been entered for nonpayment of rent, see Section XVII.C.7, below.

357 Group LLC v. Talley , No. 2018 -CVG-018737, ¶ 33 ((Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., Dec. 30 2019) (“Plaintiff is not entitled to recovery [of] any late fees as plaintiff presented no evidence as to how plaintiff suffered any damages because of defendant’s late payments”).

The Nedley and Foreman rulings may support the conclusion that, with respect to any late payment fee under a residential lease, the fee is a penalty and thus
unenforceable. See Calabria v. Greene , No. 95 -T-5181, 1995 WL 787360, at *1 -2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 8, 1995). E.g., Hall v. Leonard, No. 94-CG-667 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Mar. 7, 1995).

In Calabria, 1995 WL 787360, at *2, the Eleventh Appellate District expressly refused to follow the Nedley and Foreman rulings and, instead, “concluded that an agreed upon, one-time late fee, that is reasonable in proportion to the rental rate, and that has a rational basis supporting the imposition of the charge, is proper.” In Drake Townhouses, LLC v. Woodberry, No. C-160632, 2017 WL 3168981,¶ 25 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., July 26, 2017), the First Appellate District held: “This court has not squarely addressed the issue, but we can discern nothing in Ohio’s Landlord - Tenant Act that forbids the use of a valid liquidated -damages clause with respect to late fees in residential leases.” Court decisions finding a late payment fee t o be unreasonable and thus a penalty and unenforceable include:282
(1) Wadsworth v. Starcher, No. 97-A-0054, 1998 WL 553160 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 26, 1998) (monthly rent of $450; late payment fee of $5 per day ($305 for June 1997 and $155 for July 1997)).
(2) Calabria v. Greene, No. 95-T-5181, 1995 WL 787360 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 8, 1995) (monthly rent of $265; late payment fee of $10 per day ($300 for October 1994, and $80 for November 1994).
(3) Neubauer v. Pat zkowsky, No. 91AP -1236, 1992 WL 125269 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 2, 1992) (monthly rent of $400; late payment fee 282 Like any other landlord, public housing authorities (“PHAs”) are s ubject to this state law.

See Hous. & Redevelopment Auth. of Duluth v. Lee , 852 N.W.2d 683 (Minn. 2014) (court rejected the PHA’s preemption argument, relative to the state’s 8% cap on late fees). Similarly, the Supreme Court of Vermont affirmed a trial court’s ruling that the late payment fee of $5 on the sixth day of the month and $1 per day thereafter – contained in the model lease for project- based subsidized housing – was a penalty and thus unenforceable under state law. Highgate Assocs., Ltd. v. Merryfield, 597 A.2d 1280 (Vt. 1991).

of $1 per day, starting on the fifth day of the month and continuing until the rent is paid).
(4) Obrin v. Hicks, No. 96-CVG-22876 (Ohio Mun.,Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Aug. 13, 1997) (monthly rent of $550; late payment fee of $45 per month; court reduced the fee to $25 per month because “[t]his Court has limited late fees to $25 per month finding fees in excess of that amount unrelated to landlord’s actual damages”).
(5) ABC Mgmt. Co. v. Young , No. M9410 -CVG-030140 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 9, 1994) (monthly rent of $11 (assisted housing);283 late payment fee of $30 for one month).
(6) Respiratory & Clinical Supply Corp. v. Freshour , No. 88 -CV-F-128 (Ohio Mun., Hillsboro, Dec. 23, 1988) (monthly rent of $220; late payment fee of $3 per day up to ten days and $5 per day thereafter).
(7) Schwenkel v. Daley, No. 92-CVG-2825 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, Jan. 19,
1993) (monthly rent of $300; late payment fee of $5 per day).

Court decisions finding a late payment fee to be reasonable and thus enforceable include:
(1) Milburn v. Henness, No. 03-CVH-208 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Jan. 29,
2004) (monthly rent of $615; late payment fee of $50 per month).
(2) Xenia Co. v. Cline , No. M9 005-CVG-015839 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., June 25, 1990) (late payment fee of $12 on the third day of the month and $2 per day thereafter).

##### c. Unconscionability, R.C. 5321.14

Late payment fees are subject to R.C. 5321.14, which prohibits and makes unenforceable any late payment fee that is unconscionable. This prohibition against unconscionable fees does not supplant the penalty/liquidated damages restriction, but rather supplements it. Wadsworth v. Starcher, No. 97-A-0054, 1998 WL 553160 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 26, 1998).

Court decisions finding a late payment fee to be unconscionable and thus unenforceable under R.C. 5321.14 include:
(1) Drake Townhouses, LLC v. Woodberry, No. C-160632, 2017 WL 3168981 (Ohio App., 1 st Dist., Ju ly 26, 2017) (monthly rent of $ 710;

283 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, above.

359 late payment fee of $10 per day, with a maximum of $150 per month, is unconscionable; late fee of $10 per day, with a maximum of $50, would not be unconscionable).
(2) Campus Vill. Toledo Univ. Park, LLC v. Mowrer, 68 N.E.3d 219, 2016- Ohio-4754 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 2016) (late payment fee of $5 per day is unconscionable; one -time late payment fee of $40 would not be unconscionable).
(3) Anderson v. Ballard , No. L -10-1007, 2010 WL 3292685 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2010) (monthly rent of $400; late payment fee of $55 if one day late).
(4) Miller v. Hamm , No. 15-CV-0580 (Ohio C.P., Marion Cty., June 22,
2016) (monthly lot rent of $250; late payment fee of $5 per day).
(5) N. River Place Apts. v. Hucks , No. CVG -01-00240 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Nov. 20, 2001) (monthly rent of $500; late payment fee of $35 on the sixth day of the month and $5 per day thereafter; the $5 per day provision is unconscionable and unenforceable).
(6) Gentil v. Williams, No. 96-CVG-1095 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Nov. 12,
1996) (monthly rent of $450; late payment fee of $15 per day ($405 for August 1996 and $105 for September 1996)).
(7) Respiratory & Clinical Supply Corp. v. Freshour , No. 88 -CV-F-128 (Ohio Mun., Hillsboro, Dec. 23, 1988) (monthly rent of $220; late payment fee of $3 per day up to ten days and $5 per day thereafter).
(8) Schwenkel v. Daley, No. 92-CVG-2825 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, Jan. 19,
1993) (monthly rent of $300; late payment fee of $5 per day ($280 for September 1992 and $130 for October 1992)).

One court found t hat any award of late payment fees for a specified month was unconscionable, given the circumstances in the case. The circumstances were: (1) the landlord’s prior indication that it would not accept rent for that month (due to its service of the notice to vacate); and (2) the tenant’s move -out prior to that month, on the mistaken understanding that, by so moving, she would not owe any rent for that month.

Drake Townhouses, LLC v. Woodberry, No. C-160632, 2017 WL 3168981 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., July 26, 2017).

One court found a late payment fee to be enforceable because the fee was not procedurally unconscionable as a matter of law. Good Knight Props., LLC v. Adam ,
No. L-13-1231, 2014 WL 4656870 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 19, 2014).

##### d. Actual Damages, Absent Late Payment Fee

In the absence of a valid late payment fee, a landlord is still entitled to recover “the monetary equivalent of the actual damages” that the landlord incurred as a result of the tenant’s breach of the lease. Berlinger v. Suburban Apt . Mgmt. Co., 7 Ohio App.3d 122, 125, 454 N.E.2d 1367 (8th Dist. 1982).

Therefore, in such cases, a court may find that the landlord’s loss of interest on the unpaid rent constitutes the monetary equivalent of the landlord’s actual damages, and award a judgment for that amount. E.g., Schwenkel v. Daley, No. 92-CVG-2825 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, Jan. 19, 1993) (held that the late payment fee provision in the lease was a penalty; awarded the landlord the actual damages that the tenant caused in failing to pay rent timely; held that the actual damages were lost interest, and then computed the interest at the R.C. 1343.03(A) rate of 10% per annum).

Similarly, in Allman v. Thorne, No. 2006-CVI-000629 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty.,
Aug. 10, 2006), the court found that “[l]ate fees are designed to compensate landlords for loss of use of the rent that they were due for whatever period they were deprived of it.” It then concluded that “the recovery of the full amount [of rent] due, plus interest from the date it was due, will adequately accomplish those ends. The court does not, therefore, separately award a judgment for late fees.”

##### e. Discount Rent or Late Payment Fee

A late payment fee issue arises when a lease purports to establish both a rental amount and a so-called discount amount for payment by a particular date. The issue is whether the discount amount is actually the base rent and, therefore, the difference between the discount amount and the rental amount is a late fee. For a discussion on this issue, see Friedman v. Krupp Corp., 282 Ill. App.3d 436, 668 N.E.2d 142 (1996).

##### f. Nonpayment of a Late Payment Fee

In non-assisted housing,284 a landlord may evict a tenant for nonpayment of a late payment fee. Mercer Island Partners II v. Simakis , No. 81AP -91, 1981 WL 3316 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 30, 1981).

In projec t-based subsidized housing, 285 a landlord may not evict a tenant for 284 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, above.

285 For the definition of “project-based subsidized housing,” see note 165, above.

361 nonpayment of a late payment fee. HUD Handbook 4350.3 REV-1,286 ¶ 6-23(F).

#### 10. Sale of Rental Premises, Rent Payments

After a sale (including a foreclosure sale) of occupied rental premises, the tenant is obligated to pay the future rent to the new owner. See Commercial Bank & Sav. Co.
v. Woodville Sav. Bank Co., 126 Ohio St. 587, 186 N.E. 444 (1933);Heifner v. Heifner,
No. 1-88-32, 1989 WL 145162 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Nov. 28, 1989). Cf. Ucker v.

HER Realtors , No. 89AP -520, 1989 WL 110993 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 26, 1989).

The tenant is obligated to pay to the former landlord (and not to the new owner) any rent that has accrued and remained unpaid as of the time of the sale. See Commercial Bank & Sav. Co. v. Woodville Sav. Bank Co., 126 Ohio St. 587, 186 N.E.

444 (1933); Mintz v. Tannous, 74 Ohio App.3d 636, 600 N.E.2d 269 (8th Dist. 1991);

Williams v. Martin , 83 Ohio App. 130, 82 N.E.2d 547 (1st Dist. 1948); Varnau v.

Scanlon, 17 Ohio Law Abs. 246 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., 1934). Accord Ginsberg v.

Austin, 968 F.2d 1198 (Fed. Cir. 1992).

Therefore, the new owner is obligated to timely inform the tenant: (1) of the new owner’s purchase of the property, and (2) as to whom andwhere rent payments are to be made. In cases where the new owner failed to do so and then filed an eviction action for nonpayment of rent, courts have entered the eviction judgment in favor of the tenant on equity grounds. E.g., Zanetos v. Sparks, 13 Ohio App.3d 242, 244, 468 N.E.2d 938 (10th Dist. 1984) (among the equity factors was “plaintiff’s failure to timely notify Bolin [tenant] of the sale and advise him to whom payment should be made”); Clark
v. Mills, No. 20-CVG-01530 (Ohio Mun., Akron, June 10, 2020) (cites Zanetos; after the sale of the property, tenant “was uncertain who he should pay rent to and he wanted to meet the new owner before he made payment”).

Until the new owner provides the tenant with rent payment instructions, a reasonable approach for the tenant is to hold the rent money for later payment. This would be consistent with Ohio law: “In the absence of an agreement to the contrary, the general rule in Ohio is that payment of rent is to be made at the leased premises and that it is the landlord’s duty to go to the premise to collect rent from the tenant.” Davis v. Scullark, No. 57456, 1990 WL 145642, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 4, 1990).

286 For the full citation for this handbook, see note 143, above.

If the new owner fails to provide a tenant with timely notice of the sale, it may be a direct cause of the tenant making a monthly rental payment to the former landlord, instead of the new owner. When this occurs, either: (1) the payment satisfies the tenant’s rental obligation for that month, or (2) the inaction of the new owner ( i.e., failure to notify the tenant) prevented the tenant from complying with the tenant’s rental obligation, and thus excuses the noncompliance. “Non -performance of a promise or condition in a lease is excused where performance is prevented by the other party to the lease.” Prof’l Invs. of Am., Inc. v. Ross, No. 44540, 1982 WL 2579, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 1982). For additional discussion and case law on this issue, see Section II.Z, above.

Any claim of the new owner for prepaid rent is against the former landlord, not the
tenant. See Loveless v. Erie R.R., 2 Ohio App. 404 (11th Dist. 1914).

For a discussion on the respective liability of the former landlord and the new owner, when a landlord sells rental property and, as of the time of the sale, the landlord has failed to remedy a defective condition at the rental premises in violation of R.C.

5321.04(A), see Section IX.B.6, below.

#### 11. Pending Eviction Action, Rent Payments

During an eviction action, a court may order the tenant to deposit rent with the court for a variety of reasons, including: a continuance (see Section I.K, above), a delay resulting from a jury demand (see Section I.J.3.c, above), an R.C. 1923.061(B) defense (see Section II.O.2, above), a stay pending a ruling on objections (see Section I.N.4, above), a stay pending a ruling on a Civil Rule 60(B) motion (see Section I.P.2, above), or a stay pending an appeal (see Section I.Q.3, above).

While statutory or Civil Rule authority exists for a rent deposit order in many of these circumstances, the court also has inherent power to order a rent deposit during an eviction action. See Section I.J.3.c, above. Courts often use this inherent power to order the tenant to deposit with the court current rent and future rent, as it becomes due, when the length of the eviction proceeding will exceed one month.See PK Mgmt.

LLC v. Hayes , No. 2011 -CVG-008188 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 7, 2011).

Landlords often refuse to accept a tenant’s tender of rent immediately preceding the commencement of (and pending) an eviction action in order to avoid a waiver of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate. See Section II.C, above.

When a landlord refuses a tenant’s tender of rent on the ground that an eviction 363 action is pending, the tenant is excused from tendering rent for the next month, provided the eviction action is still pending and the court has not issued an order for the payment or deposit of rent. Cont’l Inv. P’ship v. Hukill, No. 06 -CV-131 (Ohio Mun., Delaware, Mar. 8, 2006). In these circumstances, the landlord may not evict the tenant for failing to tender the rent for the next month. Id.

### G. Guests


#### 1. Tenant’s Right to Invite Guests

Tenants have a right to invite guests to their rental unit, based on two principles of Ohio common law and, in some cases, an express provision in a lease.287 One common law basis for this right is the tenant’s common law possessory rights under the lease (or tenancy). Cooper v. Roose, 151 Ohio St. 316, 323, 85 N.E.2d 545 (1949). A lease (or tenancy) gives the tenant a right to possession and control of the
premises. Id. This possessory right includes “the right to admit people to the premises and exclude them from the premises.” Id.

A second common law basis for this right is the common law implied covenant of quiet enjoyment. See Section VII.C.9, above. “In Ohio, a covenant of quiet enjoyment is implied in every lease contract for realty.” Hamilton Brownfields Redevelopment, LLC v. Duro Tire & Wheel, 156 Ohio App.3d 525, 806 N.E.2d 1039, ¶ 23 (12th Dist.

2004). “The covenant is breached when the landlord obstructs, interferes with, or takes away from the tenant in a substantial degree the beneficial use of the leasehold.” Id.

The right of a tenant to invite guests is a beneficial use of the leasehold that this covenant protects. See State v. DeCoster, 653 A.2d 891, 894 (Me. 1995); Branish v.

NHP Prop. Mgmt., 694 A.2d 1106, 1107 (Pa. Super. 1997); L.D.L. v. State of Florida, 569 So.2d 1310, 1312-13 (Fla. App. 1990).

The common law right to invite guests includes the right to use the common areas for travel to and from the rental unit. State v. Hites, No. 1-2000-22, 2000 WL 1114809 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 8, 2000); City of Kent v. Hermann, No. 95-P-0042, 1996 WL 210780 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 8, 1996). The portions and facilities of the common area not required for this travel purpose may be excluded from the right to invite guests. Id.

287 For example, public housing leases expressly grant the tenant the right to provide reasonable accommodations to their guests. 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(d)(1).

#### 2. Registration/Approval of Overnight Guests

Some landlo rds require registration and/or landlord approval for any overnight guests.

Court decisions have held such policies to be unconscionable and thus unenforceable under R.C. 5321.14. Hi-Land Terrace, Inc. v. Dinwiddie, No. 546, 1985 WL 6560 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 1, 1985); Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Smith, No. 78- CVG-268 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, May 8, 1978).

For Public Housing, court decisions have struck down such policies on two additional grounds. One is that the policy violates the tenant’s right to i nvite guests under 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(d)(1). Lancor v. Lebanon Hous. Auth., 760 F.2d 361 (1st Cir.

1985); Wells v. Hous. Auth. of Wheeling , No. 90 -0131-W(S) (N.D. W. Va. Jan. 27, 1993). The other is that the policy violates the tenant’s First Amendment ri ghts of association and privacy. McKenna v. Peekskill Hous. Auth. , 647 F.2d 332 (2d Cir.

1981).

#### 3. Guest or Unauthorized Occupant

A lease may identify the authorized occupants under the tenancy and prohibit any other persons from occupying the premises, absent prior landlord approval. For example, see Section III.G.5, above, for a discussion on the authorized occupant rules in assisted housing leases.288 As a result, an issue often arises as to when a guest becomes an unauthorized
occupant. The case law provides some guidance.

An occupancy does not exist without overnight stays. See Lucas Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Wood , No. CVG -0900329 (Ohio Mun., Sylvania, June 23, 2009) (the guest was often at the rental unit visiting with his children, but he stayed overnight only one time in six years).

Generally, a person who stays overnight up to two (and sometimes three) times a week is not an occupant of the premises. E.g., Sumac Real Estate Holding, LLC v.

Mitchell, No. 2014-CVG-00670 (Ohio Mun., Euclid, Apr. 18, 2014) (two/three nights per week); Countrybrook Apts. v. Forsythe , No. M86 -CVG-20896 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 28, 1986) (weekends);Andrews Place Assoc. v. Loebsack, No. 84- CVG-1222 (Ohio Mun., Mentor, Jan. 3, 1985) (three nights per week). See als o 288 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, above.

365 Gallatin Hous. Auth. v. Gifford, No. 89-25-II, 1989 WL 100268 (Tenn. App. Aug. 30,
1989) (two/three nights per week);Messiah Baptist Hous. Dev. Fund Co. v. Rosser, 92 Misc.2d 383, 400 N.Y.S.2d 306 (City Ct. 1977) (two/three nights per week). Cf.

Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Scott , No. 92 -CVG-76 (Ohio Mun., Fremont, May 6, 1992) (babysitting 5:30 A.M. to 7:00 or 8:00 P.M. daily, plus overnight irregularly);

Perrysburg Ecumenical Hous. v. Leu, No. 87-CVG-977 (Ohio Mun., Perrysburg, Feb.

17, 1988) (son of tenant frequently visited and, on several occasions, stayed overnight).

Generally, a person who stays up to two consecutive weeks is not an occupant of the premises. Wilmington Green Apts. v. Estep , No. 88 -CVG-64 (Ohio Mun., Wilmington, Apr. 7, 1988) (two consecutive weeks). See also Mihil Co. v. Paradiso, 107 Misc.2d 867, 436 N.Y.S.2d 115 (Civ. Ct. 1980) (seven consecutive weeks);Ritter
v. Cecil County Office of Hous. & Cmty. Dev., 33 F.3d 323 (4th Cir. 1994) (upholding public housing agency’s two-week rule in the Section 8 Certificate program).

Factors to consider in distinguishing between an occupant and a guest include: (1) the number of days per week or length of stay, (2) any use of another r esidence, (3) where clothes and other personal belongings are maintained, (4) where meals are eaten,
(5) where mail is received, (6) address used for driver’s license and other documents, and (7) other indicia of residency. Juarovisech v. Portage Metro. H ous. Auth., No.

C83-4961 (N.D. Ohio Jan. 12, 1984). See Urban Hollow Apts. v. Lewis, No. M9403- CVG-007533 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 25, 1994); Vistula Mgmt. Co. v. Scott,
No. 92-CVG-76 (Ohio Mun., Fremont, May 6, 1992). See also Gallatin Hous. Auth.
v. Gifford, No. 89-25-II, 1989 WL 100268 (Tenn. App. Aug. 30, 1989).

The lease may establish standards for this determination, which may be given deference by the courts. However, the standards may not unreasonably interfere with the tenant’s right to invite guests, which is discussed in Section VII.G.1, above. See Ashley Court Enters. v. Whittaker, 249 N.J. Super. 552, 592 A.2d 1228 (1991) (seven days per month limit was an unreasonable interference with the covenant of quiet enjoyment).

For Public Housing and project-based subsidized housing, any lease standard must be reasonable because federal law prohibits unreasonable lease terms. 42 U.S.C.

§ 1437d(l)(2) (Public Housing); 12 U.S.C. § 1715z-1b(b)(3) (project-based subsidized housing). However, i n construing these guest provisions, some courts have found relatively short time periods to be reasonable, if the time period can be extended by prior landlord approval. E.g., Heritage Court L.L.C. v. Merritt, 187 Ohio App.3d 117, 931 N.E.2d 194 (3rd Dis t. 2010) (one -week visitation period for each six months);

Norwich Hous. Auth. v. Majewski , No. CV -219883, 2000 WL 73034 (Conn. Super.
Jan. 13, 2000) (seven days per month).

A natural child of a tenant, who becomes a member of the household after the commencement of the tenancy, cannot be considered a guest or an unauthorized
occupant. See Hicks v. Hubert , No. 96 -CVG-34 (Ohio County Ct., Sandusky Cty., June 4, 1996).

#### 4. Ban Lists and Trespass Policies

A common issue is whether a landlord may ban specific p ersons (who are not
tenants) from entering the landlord’s rental property and, if so, in what circumstances.

The issue arises in two contexts.

One context is a trespass action against the banned person under R.C.

2911.21(A)(1). One element of this trespass charge is that the person is on the land or premises “without privilege.” “‘Privilege’ for purposes of criminal trespass includes permission to enter the premises given by a resident of the premises.”State v. Clelland, 83 Ohio App.3d 474, 490, 615 N.E.2d 276 (4th Dist. 1992).

A second context is an eviction action against the tenant for a lease violation.

The typical landlord practice on ban lists is comprised of four elements: (1) the landlord maintains a list of persons who are banned from the property; (2) the landlord (and police) furnish names to place on the list; (3) the landlord (or police, acting as landlord’s agent per an agreement) serve the banned person with a trespass notice; and
(4) the police enforce the ban by bringing criminal trespass charges for violations.

For entries not pursuant to a tenant’s invitation, generally the ban is permissible and, in those cases, a violation may give rise to a criminal trespass charge. See State
v. Wood, No. 03-CA-2901, 2004 WL 1631468 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 15, 2004).

For entries pursuant to a tenant’s invitation, where a lease provision authorizes the ban, the court decisions are split on whether the lease provision (and thus the ban) is permissible.

The rationale of the court decisions holding that the ban is not permissible in these circumstances is: “Under applicable property law, the owner sacrifices his possessory interests in the property, and he cannot prohibit a tenant from inviting guests into the tenant’s abode.” City of Kent v. Hermann, No. 95 -P-0042, 1996 WL 210780, at *2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 8, 1996). Accord City of Columbus v. Parks, No. 10AP- 574, 2011 WL 1781911 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 5, 2011); State v. Hites, No. 1- 2000-22, 2000 WL 1114809 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 8, 2000).

367 The rationale of the court decisions holding that the ban is permissible in these circumstances is: “[T]he tenant derived her rights as a tenant from DMHA [landlord] and, consequently, her rights to invite guests were subject to DMHA’s right to preclude certain guests by means of the criminal trespass policy,” which is part of the lease and thus qualifies the lease right to accommodate guests. State v. Scott, No. 19902, 2004 WL 103175, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 23, 2004). See, e.g., State v. Blackford,
No. 2012-CA-00146, 2013 WL 1737858 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 22, 2013). See also Williams v. Nagel, 162 Ill. App.2d 542, 643 N.E.2d 816 (1994).

For entries pursuant to a tenant’s invitation, where the tenant’s lease does not authorize the ban, the ban is impermissible and invalid. Absent any restriction in the lease, the ban would violate the tenant’s common law right to invite guests, which is an implied provision in every lease or tenancy. See Section VII.G.1, above.

In some c ases, the determination on whether an entry is pursuant to a tenant’s invitation will depend on whether a tenant may grant a person a standing invitation for any date and time, in lieu of an explicit invitation for each entry.

Some ban policies exempt tenants’ guests, and thereby avoid any interference with the right of tenants to invite guests.

One court held, where a Public Housing ban policy encompassed tenants’ guests and the manager had unfettered discretion, that the ban policy was an unreasonable interference with the right of tenants, under 24 C.F.R. § 966.4(d)(1), to invite guests.

Diggs v. Hous. Auth. of City of Frederick, 67 F. Supp.2d 522 (D. Md. 1999) (enjoined the policy, except for the persons on the ban list due to violent or drug-related criminal activity on the PHA’s property).

Conflicting case law exists on whether a Public Housing ban policy may violate the First Amendment right of banned persons, especially relative to child rearing.

Compare Ash v. City of Clarksville , No. 3:03-0380, 2004 WL 5913273 (M.D. Tenn.
Sept. 3, 2004) (violated) with Brown v. Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. C-3-93-037, 1993 WL 1367433 (S.D. Ohio Aug. 26, 1993) (not violated), Commonwealth of Virginia v. Hicks, 267 Va. 573, 596 S.E.2d 74 (2004), and State v. Hites, No. 1-2000- 22, 2000 WL 1114809 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 8, 2000). See also Johnson v. City of Cincinnati, 310 F.3d 484 (6th Cir. 2002) (held that the right of a grandmother to participate in the upbringing of her grandchildren is constitutionally prot ected and, to the extent thatThompson v. Ashe, 250 F.3d 399 (6th Cir. 2001), can be read to proclaim the absence of this right, it conflicts with Supreme Court precedent and therefore is not binding on the court).

Other issues concerning Public Housing ban policies include:
(1) Potential due process procedural issues relative to unclear standards, unclear notices as to the scope, basis, or appeal rights, lack of notice to tenants, appeal procedures, etc.; and
(2) A potential Fourth Amendment unreasonable search issue, as illustrated by the facts and decision in State v. Hayes , 188 S.W.2d 505 (Tenn.

2006).

For further discussion on Public Housing trespass policies, see: (1) No Trespass Policies – Hicks and Its Aftermath , 34 Hous. L. Bull. 129 (National Housing Law Project July 2004); (2) Gregory A. Beck, Ban Lists: Can Public Housing Authorities Have Unwanted Visitors Arrested , 2004 U. Ill. L. Rev. 1223 (2004); and (3) Elena Goldstein, Kept Out: Responding to Public Housing No -Trespass Policies, 38 Harv.

C.R.-C.L. L. Rev. 215 (2003).

### H. Spouse, Not Named on Lease

When a married couple resides in a rental unit, whether a spouse is named on the lease effects the spouse’s rights under the lease.

“When only one spouse is named on a lease, the other spouse has no rights under the lease.” Mlynek v. Mlynek, No. WD-07-056, 2008 WL 307449, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 1, 2008). Accord Smith v. McCain, 66 Ohio Law Abs. 561, 118 N.E.2d 238 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1952).

A non-tenant-spouse resides in the rental unit solely as a member of the spouse’s
household. Mlynek v. Mlynek, No. WD-07-056, 2008 WL 307449, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 1, 2008). Absent the consent of the tenant -spouse, the non -tenant- spouse does not have a right of possession. State v. O’Neal, No. C-980247, 1999 WL 162158, at *2 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar. 26, 1999).

If the right of possession of the tenant -spouse ends either voluntarily or involuntarily, the right of possession of the non -tenant-spouse also ends, since that right accrues only through the tenant -spouse. Smith v. McCain , 66 Ohio Law Abs.

561, 118 N.E.2d 238 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1952).

As to actions between spouses, R.C. 3103.04 states, in relevant part: “Neither can be excluded from the other’s dwelling, except upon decree or order of injunction made by a court of competent jurisdiction.” However, “R.C. 3103.04 is inapplicable to criminal cases.” State v. Lilly , 87 Ohio St.3d 97, 100, 717 N.E.2d 322 (1999).

369 Therefore, “a spouse may be criminally liable for trespass ... in the dwelling of the other spouse who is exercising custody or control over that dwelling.” Id.

### I. Landlord, Name and Address

R.C. 5321.18 requires the owner of residential rental properties to provide the tenant with the name and address of the owner and the owner’s agent (if any). If the parties executed a written rental agreement , the information must be contained in the
agreement. If the parties did not execute a written rental agreement, “the landlord, at the commencement of the term of occupancy, shall deliver to tenant a written notice containing the information.” R.C. 5321.18(B).

A landlord’s noncompliance with this requirement may result in a waiver of the landlord’s right, under R.C. 5321.07(A), to receive the tenant’s pre-rent deposit notice of landlord violations (and thus the opportunity to cure). R.C. 5321.18(C). This waiver is discussed in Section VIII.A, below. The noncompliance also may result in a waiver of the landlord’s right, under R.C. 5321.08(A), to receive the clerk of court notice of the rent deposit. R.C. 5321.18(C).

The owners of residential rental properties that are located in counties that have a population of more than 200,000 are also required to file, with the county auditor of the county in which the rental property is located: (1) the owner’s name, address, and telephone number; (2) the street ad dress and permanent parcel number of the residential rental property; and (3) certain other information. R.C. 5323.02. For these purposes, residential rental property includes residential premises under R.C.

5321.01(C) and R.C. 4781.01(W) (former R.C. 3733.01(J)).

A landlord’s noncompliance with this requirement may subject the landlord to a special assessment, imposed by the county auditor, in the amount of not less than $50 or more than $150. R.C. 5323.99.

### J. Renewal Provisions

“[T]he law of Ohio, as a ge neral rule, does not mandate a landlord to renew or extend a lease for a definite period of time unless provided for within the lease agreement.” Mesnick v. De Ville Apts. , No. 71517, 1997 WL 599376, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 25, 1997).

A provision for renewal of a lease, upon certain acts or omissions of the landlord or tenant, is enforceable. Frank v. Flynn , 120 Ohio App. 361, 197 N.E.2d 657 (8th
Dist. 1964).289 For example, a lease may include an option to renew that requires a party to give a specified notice in order to exercise the option.In re Kindinger, 219 B.R. 214 (Bankr.

N.D. Ohio 1998) (applying Ohio law).

Conversely, a lease may provide that its term shall automatically renew to a specified renewal term unless, by a specified number of days before the end of the term, the tenant provides the landlord with written notice of nonrenewal. The automatic renewal (and notice of nonrenewal) provision may be used with any type or combination of initial term and renewal term.

It may be a lease with an initial term of one year that renews for a one -year term.

E.g., Hackman v. Szczygiel, No. 06AP-187, 2006 WL 3199278 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Nov. 7, 2006); Angelo v. Parker, No. 13140, 1987 WL 25776 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,
Nov. 25, 1987). But see Meadowbrook Dev. Corp. v. Roberts , No. 79747, 2001 WL 1654540, at *4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 27, 2001) (the 60 -day notice requirement for nonrenewal was unreasonable under the facts of this case).

It may be a lease for a fixed-term that renews to a month-to-month tenancy. E.g., Adair v. Landis Props., Inc., No. 08AP-139, 2008 WL 4174130 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Sept. 11, 2008) (initial three-month term); Cummings v. Getz, No. CA84-09-105, 1985 WL 8164 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 11, 1985) (initial one -year term); McGowan v.

DM Group IX , 7 Ohio App.3d 349, 455 N.E.2d 1052 (10th Dist. 1982) (initial six - month term).

It may be a lease that is a written month-to-month tenancy. E.g., Knipp v. Sadler,
No. 6-09-04, 2009 WL 2710053 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug.31, 2009); Seginak v. ABC
Mgmt. Co., No. 3816, 1987 WL 17258 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 18, 1987).

However, in these cases, if the tenant provides the landlord with timely oral notice, but not written notice, the courts frequently apply principles of substantial compliance, unconscionability, and/or equity to relieve the tenant from the renewal of the tenancy.

E.g., Knipp v. Sadler, No. 6-09-04, 2009 WL 2710053 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 31, 2009); Hackman v. Szczygiel , No. 06AP -187, 2006 WL 3199278 (O hio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 7, 2006); Meadowbrook Dev. Corp. v. Roberts , No. 79747, 2001 WL 289 For a discussion on the relationship between an automatic renewal provision and the statute of conveyances, see Section XXII.A.1, below.

371 1654540 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 27, 2001); 290 Cummings v. Getz, No. CA84-09- 105, 1985 WL 8164 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 11, 1985); McGowan v. DM Group IX, 7 Ohio App.3d 349, 455 N.E.2d 1052 (10th Dist. 1982); Seginak v. ABC Mgmt.

Co., No. 3816, 1987 WL 17258 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 18, 1987).See also Adair
v. Landis Props., Inc., No. 08AP-139, 2008 WL 4174130 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept.

11, 2008) (reversal of summary judgment – genuine issue on actual notice); Ballard v.

KMG Investors, Ltd. P’ship, No. 14 -93-5, 1993 WL 293944 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist.,
Aug. 3, 1993).

Likewise, when a tenant provides the landlord with a written notice of nonrenewal that is untimely because of the tenant’s mistaken reading of the lease, a court may use its equity power to relieve the tenant of the renewal. E.g., Mentor Lagoons, Inc. v.

Ventra, No. 89 -L-14-026, 1990 WL 128270 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 7, 1990);

Mentor Lagoons, Inc. v. Velbeck, No. 10-056, 1984 WL 6285 (Ohio App., 11th Dist.,
Mar. 23, 1984). Cf. Ward v. Washington Distribs., Inc. , 67 Ohio App.2d 49, 425 N.E.2d 420 (6th Dist. 1980) (equity used to permit tenant to renew lease where tenant failed to exercise timely the option to renew).

“Where the provision for an extention or renewal [of a lease] does not expressly require notice, Ohio law holds the mere holdin g over under the lease constitutes an election to exercise the option which binds both parties for the term designated in the option.” In re Kindinger, 219 B.R. 214, 217 (Bankr. N.D. Ohio 1998) (applying Ohio law).

When two or more tenants are parties to a lease agreement that gives the tenants an option to renew, the option may not be exercised by only one of the tenants. L & H Realty Co. v. Oberholtz, 65 Ohio App.2d 279, 418 N.E.2d 401 (9th Dist. 1979).

When the renewal provision concerns a month -to-month tenancy, it must be consistent with the requirements of R.C. 5321.17(B). R.C. 5321.06 and 5321.13(A).

See Detweiter v. Galt, No. 01-CA-34, 2001 WL 1682822 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec.

26, 2001); DeBenedictus v. Gialamas , No. 96 -L-006, 1997 WL 51453 (Oh io App., 11th Dist., Jan. 24, 1997); Cary v. Galbraith, No. L-94-002, 1994 WL 476146 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 2, 1994). Cf. Seginak v. ABC Mgmt. Co. , No. 3816, 1987 WL 17258 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 18, 1987) (held enforcement of written notice 290 The Roberts court acknowledges that its holding deviates from its earlier holding in Goudreau Mgmt. Co. v. Lancaster, No. 65015, 1994 WL 97697 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 24, 1994).

requirement unconscionable where tenant’s oral notice complied with R.C.

5321.17(B)).

However, one court has held that a sixty -day notice of nonrenewal requirement does not conflict with R.C. 5321.17(B). Kenko Corp. v. Singer, No. 81-03-0023, 1982 WL 3141 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 26, 1982).

The renewal option may establish a higher rent under the renewal. Chorpenning
v. Carmack , No. 23933, 2008 WL 726553 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 19, 2008);

Bohlmann v. Cox, No. 17166, 1995 WL 641128 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 1, 1995).

In Carmack, a one-year lease had a monthly rent of $1,275 and, upon the expiration of the one-year term, a renewal provision established a month -to-month tenancy with a monthly rent of $2000.

However, where the lease does not contain t he renewal option with the increased rent and, instead, it is contained in a separate notice, the notice is only an offer to rent at the increased rent amount. Bohlmann v. Cox, No. 17166, 1995 WL 641128 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 1, 1995). In these circ umstances, the tenant’s tender of a lesser amount is a counteroffer. Id. The landlord’s acceptance of the lesser amount is an acceptance of the counteroffer. Id.

Also, an automatic renewal provision may expressly provide that the renewal will not occur if the landlord timely notifies the tenant of a change in the monthly rent (or other terms of the lease). E.g., Ganios v. Ferrick , No. 2005 -CA-00239, 2006 WL 751508 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Mar. 20, 2006). In Garios, the lease contained this nonrenewal fea ture, in a year -to-year automatic renewal provision. Prior to the expiration of the second one -year term, the landlord notified the tenant of a rent
increase. The Court held that this prevented the automatic renewal of the lease and, therefore, the tenancy became a month-to-month tenancy.

### K. Prohibited Provisions


#### 1. Unconscionable Provisions, R.C. 5321.14

If a rental agreement provision was unconscionable at the time that the parties executed the agreement, under R.C. 5321.14, a court may refuse to enforce the provision or may limit the application of the provision so as to avoid an unconscionable result.

A provision is unconscionable only if it is both substantively and procedurally
unconscionable. Oldendick v. Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016-Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 373 8th Dist., 2016); Anderson v. Ballard, No. L-10-1007, 2010 WL 3292685 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2010). See also Pojman v. Columbia-Brookpark Mgmt., LLC, No.

88666, 2007 WL 2269682 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 9, 2007) (construing R.C.

3733.16 (now R.C. 4781.48) – the manufactured home park provision that is identical to R.C. 5321.14). Cf. Taylor Bldg. Corp. of Am. v. Benfield, 117 Ohio St.3d 352, 884 N.E.2d 12 (2008) (arbitration clause) ; Harbour Portfolio VII, LP v. Pulley , Nos. C - 150080,150090, 2015 WL 6438920 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Oct. 23, 2015) (arbitration clause, land installment contract).

Substantive unconscionability focuses on the fairness of the terms. Id. Procedural unconscionability focuses on the relative bargaining position of the parties. Id.

Under R.C. 5321.14, a party may assert the unconscionability of a rental agreement provision as a defense to a claim, without asserting a counterclaim. Anderson v.

Ballard, No. L-10-1007, 2010 WL 3292685 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2010).

When the alleged unconscionable provision is an increased rent, the relevant issue is whether the amount of the new rent (and not the amount of the rent increase) is
unconscionable. Pojman v. Columbia-Brookpark Mgmt., LLC, No. 88666, 2007 WL 2269682 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 9, 2007).

The “reasonable opportunity to present evidence” clause in R.C. 5321.14(B) does not preclude a summary judgment ruling, without a hearing, when the parties submit all of the relevant evidence and no genuine issue of material fact exists. Id.

The “determination of whether a written contract is unconscionable is an issue of law.” Taylor Bldg. Corp. of Am. v. Benfield, 117 Ohio St.3d 352,884 N.E.2d 12, ¶ 35 (2008). Hence, on appeal, the unconscionability determination is reviewed de novo, but the underlying factual determinations are reviewed with deference. Id., ¶ 37,38.

The R.C. 5321.14 prohibition against unconscionable provisions does not apply to an option agreement to purchase real property. Stacy v. Zupp, No. CA-9566, 1994 WL 369899 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 20, 1994); SMJ Growth Corp. v. Norman, No. 96- CVG-02843 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug. 29, 1996).

Court decisions on the R.C. 5321.14 prohibition against unconscionable provisions are summarized below and in Section VII.F.9.c., above (late payment fees).

Automatic Renewal:

Cont’l Enters., Ltd. v. Hunt, No. 102200, 2015 WL 9460300 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 2015) Lease provision provided for automatic renewal of one -year lease term, unless tenant gave landlord 60 days (or more) advance written notice of
nonrenewal. Based on the relevant communications, it would be hypertechnical and unconscionable to find that the lease extended for another year. Those communications included: tenant’s ongoing complaints regarding a roof leak, the landlord’s refusal to transfer tena nt to another unit, tenant’s 90-advance written notice of her relocation plans and request for a month-to-month arrangement (which landlord rejected), and tenant’s 50-day advance written notice of nonrenewal.

Adair v. Landis Props., Inc. , No. 08AP -139, 2008 WL 4174130 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 11, 2008) Three-month lease with provision for automatic conversion to month -to- month tenancy, unless the tenant gave the landlord 30 days (or more) advance written notice of nonrenewal. Remand because genuine issue on whether the tenant provided the landlord with timely oral notice, but not written notice, of the tenant’s intent not to renew. If the tenant establishes that such oral notice was given, like McGowan (summarized below), in these circumstances, to require written notice would be unconscionable.

Hackman v. Szczygiel , No. 06AP -187, 2006 WL 3199278 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 7, 2006) The lease provision provided for the automatic renewal of a one-year term, unless the tenant gave the landlord 120 days (or more) advance written notice of nonrenewal by certified USPS mail. The tenant provided the landlord with timely oral notice, but not written notice, of the tenant’s intent not to renew. Like McGowan (summarized below), in the circumstances of this case, to require written notice would be unconscionable.

Meadowbrook Dev. Corp. v. Roberts , No. 79747, 2001 WL 1654540 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 27, 2001)291 The lease provision provided for the automatic renewal of aone-year term, unless the tenant gave the landlord 60 days (or more) advance written notice of nonrenewal. The tenant provided the landlord with timely oral notice, but not written notice, of the tenant’s intent not to renew. Like McGowan (summarized bel ow), in the circumstances of this case, to 291 See note 290, above.

375 require written notice would be unconscionable.

Ballard v. KMG Investors, Ltd. P’ship , No. 14 -93-5, 1993 WL 293944 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 3, 1993) The lease provision provided for the automatic commencement o f a month-to-month tenancy at the end of an initial six-month term, unless the tenant gave the landlord 30 days (or more) advance written notice of intent to vacate. The tenant provided the landlord with timely oral notice, but not written notice, of the tenant’s intent to vacate at the end of the initial six-month term. Like McGowan (summarized below), in the circumstances of this case, to require written notice would be unconscionable.

Angelo v. Parker, No. 13140, 1987 WL 25776 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov.

25, 1987) The lease provision provided for the automatic renewal of a one-year lease term, unless the tenant gave the landlord 120 days (or more) advance notice of nonrenewal. The lease provision was not unconscionable.

Seginak v. ABC Mgmt. Co., No. 3816, 1987 WL 17258 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 18, 1987) The lease provision required the tenant to provide the landlord with 30 days (or more) advance written notice to terminate their month -to-month
tenancy. The tenant provided the landlord with timely oral notice, but not written notice, of the tenant’s intent to terminate the tenancy. Like McGowan (summarized below), in the circumstances of this case, to require written notice would be unconscionable.

Cummings v. Getz , No. CA84-09-105, 1985 WL 8164 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 11, 1985) The lease provision provided for the automatic renewal of a one-year lease term, unless the tenant gave the landlord 30 days (or more) advance written notice of nonrenewal. The tenant provided the landlord with timely oral notice, but not written notice, of the tenant’s intent not to renew. Like McGowan (summarized below), in the circumstances of this case, to require written notice would be unconscionable.

McGowan v. DM Group IX , 7 Ohio App.3d 349, 455 N.E.2d 1052 (10th
Dist. 1982) The lease provision provided for the automatic commencement of a month-to-month tenancy at the end of an initial six-month term, unless the tenant gave the landlord 30 days (or more) advance written notice of intent to vacate. The tenant provided the landlord with timely oral notice, but not written notice, of the tenant’s intent to vacate at the end of the initial six-month term. The lease provision was not in itself unconscionable.

But, in the circumstances of this case, to require written notice would be unconscionable.

Kenko Corp. v. Singer, No. 81-03-0023, 1982 WL 3141 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 26, 1982) The lease provision required the tenant to provide the landlord with 60 days (or more) advance notice to terminate their month-to-month tenancy.

The lease provision was not unconscionable.

Landlord Option to Terminate Lease Without Cause:

Hessenhauer Corp. v. Drinks , No. 2002 -CVG-39464 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 7, 2002) The lease provision permitted the landlord, but not the tenant, to terminate the lease, during its one -year term, with a 30 -day notice of termination.

The lease provision was unconscionable.

Charges:

Tillimon v. Dukes, No. L-91-343, 1992 WL 206878 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Aug. 28, 1992) The lease provision required the tenant to pay a non -refundable “application and move-in fee” in the amount of $500. Accordin g to the lease provision, the fee was not a security deposit, but rather a charge for processing the credit application and coordinating the transfer of utilities and occupancy of the dwelling. The fee was unconscionable.

McIntyre v. Silver, No. 52116, 1987 WL 11397 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 21, 1987) The lease provision assessed the tenants a surcharge for heat loss if the windows were opened when the heating system was on. The lease provision was unconscionable.

Berlinger v. Suburban Apt. Mgmt. Co. , 7 Ohio App.3d 122, 454 N.E.2d 1367 (8th Dist. 1982) The lease provision prohibited the tenant from bringing motorcycles onto the premises and imposed a charge of fifty dollars per day for every day that the tenant violated this prohibition. The prohibition was not
unconscionable. The fifty dollars per day charge was unconscionable.

377 FDA Peachtree, LLC v. Crowder, No. 18-CVG-03298, (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Aug. 5, 2019) Based on two lease provisions, the court held that “the manner in which late fees were calculated is unconscionable.” The first provision allocated the tenant’s payments “first to late fees and all other outstanding debts and bills, then to any past due rent, and lastly to the current month’s rent.” The second provision imposed a 10% late fee if rent is not paid on the first day of the month.

Overnight Guest Rule:

Hi-Land Terrace, Inc. v. Dinwiddie, No. 546, 1985 WL 6560 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 1, 1985) The lease provision or house rule required tenants to register and obtain management’s permission for overnight guests. The lease provision or house rule was unconscionable.

Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Smith , No. 78-CVG-268 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, May 8, 1978) The lease provision required the tenant to report overnight guests to
management. The lease provision was unconscionable.

Jury Waiver:

Mentor Lagoons, Inc. v. Mayor, No. 10-180, 1985 WL 7807 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 1, 1985) The lease provision waived the tenant’s right to a jury trial. The lease provision was not unconscionable.

Mentor Lagoons, Inc. v. Geldart, No. 6-265, 1979 WL 208017(Ohio App., 11th Dist., Jan. 29, 1979) The lease provision waived the tenant’s right to a jury trial. The lease provision was not unconscionable.

Pets:292 Fitzpatrick Realty v. Wilkey, No. 78-CVG-8102 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Nov.

292 A lease provision that imposes restrictions on pets, but does not address “visiting or stray animals,” is not a prohibition against a tenant’s pr actice of feeding stray cats. Miami Valley
Hous. v. Williams, No. 06-CVG-9358 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Nov. 17, 2006).

17, 1978) One lease provision prohibited pets and another lease provision permitted the landlord to evict the tenant for any breach, however slight, of any covenant or condition of the lease. The prohibition o n pets was not
unconscionable. The right to evict for any breach, however slight, was unconscionable.

Lien on Tenant’s Property:

Rosace v. Hancock, No. 95-CVG-516 (Ohio Mun., Girard, Dec. 19, 1995) The lease provision granted the landlord an express contr act lien and security interest in the tenant’s personal property so as to secure compliance with the lease. The lease provision was unconscionable.

#### 2. Other Prohibited Provisions, R.C. 5321.06,

5321.13, and 5321.131 R.C. 5321.06 prohibits any rental agreem ent provision that is inconsistent with a right or duty that is established in either R.C. Chapter 5321 or any other rule of law.

For example, a provision that constitutes a penalty (see Section VII.F.9.b, above) is inconsistent with with an “other rule o f law” for R.C. 5321.06 purposes. Berkelely Park, LLC v. Pile, No. 2005-CVG-046214 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 11, 2007).

This statutory prohibition is consistent with Ohio common law that provides that a rental agreement may include any terms th at are not inconsistent with or prohibited by law or against public policy. Langfan v. Carlton Gardens Co. , 183 Ohio App.3d 260, 916 N.E.2d 1079, ¶ 30 (3rd Dist. 2009).

R.C. 5321.13 prohibits any lease provision that:
(1) modifies or waives any right or obl igation that is established in R.C.

Chapter 5321, except an agreement by the landlord to assume responsibility for any of the tenant’s R.C. 5321.05 obligations (R.C.

5321.13(A),(F));
(2) is a confession of judgment for the recovery of rent or damage to the premises (R.C. 5321.13(B));
(3) provides for the payment of the landlord’s or tenant’s attorney’s fees (R.C. 5321.13(C));
(4) limits or provides for indemnification for the landlord’s liability (R.C.

5321.13(D)); or 379
(5) permits the receipt of rent free of the obligation to comply with R.C.

5321.04 (R.C. 5321.13(E)).

The R.C. 5321.13(C) prohibition on agreements to pay attorney’s fees extends to “any other agreement between a landlord and tenant.” One issue relative to this clause is whether “any other agreement” includes a settlement agreement. If so, any provision for payment of attorney’s fees to the landlord or tenant, in a settlement agreement between a landlord and a tenant, would be unenforceable. However, similar to the ruling in LaCourse v. Fleitz, 28 Ohio St.3d 209, 503 N.E.2d 159 (1986) (discussed in Section V.C, above), courts may find that R.C. 5321.13(C) is not sufficiently explicit on this issue to abrogate the longstanding rule of common law that permits payments of attorney’s fees in settlement agreements.

R.C. 5321.131 prohibits any provision in a rental agreement that restricts a tenant’s right to display the flag of the United States or certain other specified flags, provided the flag is displayed in accordance with certain specified requirements.

Court decisions on these R.C. 5321.06 and 5321.13 prohibitions are summarized below.

Obligation to Maintain Premises in Habitable Condition:

Anderson v. Ballard, No. L-10-1007, 2010 WL 3292685 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2010) R.C. 5321.13(A) prohibits a landlord from modifying or waiving the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04 obligation to maintain the rental premises in a habitable condition. The “as is” provision in the rental agreement was an unlawful attempt to waive this obligation.

Liability for Damage to Premises or Property:

Kinn v. Showe Mgmt. Corp., No. 5-01-46, 2002 WL 462859 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Mar. 27, 2002) The lease provision made the tenant liable for any damage to the premises caused by the negligence of other parties ( e.g., guest) w ithout any determination of tenant negligence. The lease provision was inconsistent with R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) and, therefore, invalid under R.C. 5321.06.

Smith v. Ohio Edison, Inc. , No. 98 -CA-37, 1999 WL 6444 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 8, 1999) The lease provision provided that the landlord shall not be liable for any damage caused by a failure of the plumbing, heating, sewage, electrical, water, or gas system at the premises or caused by a roof leak at the
premises. The lease provision was inconsistent wi th the landlord’s obligations under R.C. Chapter 5321 and, therefore, was invalid under R.C. 5321.06 and 5321.13(A),(D).

Kearney v. Crown , No. 93 -T-4945, 1994 WL 587382 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Aug. 12, 1994) The lease provision made the tenant liable for any damage to the premises that the tenant’s pet caused. The lease provision was consistent with R.C.

5321.05 and, therefore, was enforceable.

Wilmington Green III v. Rickman , No. CA90 -07-013, 1991 WL 81850 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 13, 1991) (2-1 decision) The tenant executed a payment agreement with the landlord to reimburse the landlord for damage to the premises that an uninvited visitor (former
boyfriend) had caused. The payment agreement did not violate R.C.

5321.05 and, therefore, did not violate R.C. 5321.13.

Allstate Ins. Co. v. Dorsey, 46 Ohio App.3d 66, 545 N.E.2d 920 (9th Dist.
