1988) The lease provision provided that the tenant shall be liable for damage to property, appliances, or plumbing caused by carelessness. The lease provision, insofar as it made the tenant liable for all damages caused by anyone’s carelessness, was inconsistent with R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) and, therefore, was unenforceable under R.C. 5321.06.

Smith v. W. Terrace , No. 50938, 1986 WL 11033 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Sept. 18, 1986) The lease provision provided that the landlord shall not be liable for any damage or injury caused by water, steam, or the breaking or leaking of pipes resulting from any cause whatsoever. The lease provision violated R.C. 5321.06 and 5321.13.

Towne Realty Co. v. Henson , No. 4696, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 10248 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 14, 1977) The lease provision provided that the landlord shall not be liable for any loss or damage to the tenant’s property that is placed in the premises, regardless of the cause. The lease provision violated R.C. 5321.13(D) and, therefore, was unenforceable.

Vesta Corp. v. Harris , No. 2021-CVG-004702 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Jan. 20, 2022) 381 The lease provision made the tenant liable for any damage to thepremises caused by the negligence of other parties ( e.g., guests), without any determination of tenant negligence. The lease provision conflicts with R.C. 5321. 05(A)(6) and, therefore, was unenforceable under R.C.

5321.06.

Weingarden v. Eagle Ridge Condos., 71 Ohio Misc.2d 7, 653 N.E.2d 759 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., 1995) The lease provision assessed the tenant a fee for carpet cleaning at the end of the tenancy. To the extent that the lease provision assessed this fee independent of any evidence of damage beyond normal wear and tear, the lease provision was inconsistent with R.C. 5321.16 and, therefore, was unenforceable.

Nonrenewal or Termination of Tenancy:

Oldendick v. Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016-Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016) In a one -year lease, a provision permitted the tenant to effect an early termination of the lease if: (1) the tenant gave 60 days advance notice; (2) the landlord approved; (3) the tenant paid an early termination fee equal to one month’s rent; and (4) the tenant p aid the monthly rent until the landlord re-rented the property. The early termination fee operated as a penalty (and not liquidated damages) and thus was not enforceable. The early termination fee was also unenforceable because it conflicted with R.C. 5321.16.

Detweiter v. Galt , No. 01 -CA-34, 2001 WL 1682822 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 26, 2001) An oral agreement permitted the tenant (or landlord) to provide the other party with 30 days (or more) advance notice to terminate their month -to- month tenancy , regardless of the periodic rental date. The oral agreement’s disregard of the periodic rental date was inconsistent with R.C. 5321.17(B) and, therefore, was unenforceable under R.C. 5321.06.

Chesser v. Chesser, No. 01-CA-00009, 2001 WL 403162 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 12, 2001) The lease provision established a five-year term, but permitted either party to terminate the lease at any time by providing the other party with an advance one -month notice. The one -month notice provision did not conflict with R.C. Chapter 5321 and, therefore, was enforceable.

DeBenedictus v. Gialamas , No. 96 -L-006, 1997 WL 51453 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Jan. 24, 1997) The lease provision permitted the tenant (or landlord) to provide the other party with 20 days (or more) advance written notice to terminate their month-to-month tenancy. The 20 -day notice provision was inconsistent with R.C. 5321.17(B), and, therefore, was unenforceable.

Cary v. Galbraith, No. L-94-002, 1994 WL 476146 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Sept. 2, 1994) The lease provision required the tenant (or landlord) to provide the other party with 30 days advance written notice to terminate their month -to- month tenancy, regardless of the periodic rental date. The lease provision’s disregard of the periodic rental date was inconsistent with R.C.

5321.17(B) and, therefore, was unenforceable under R.C. 5321.06.

Kenko Corp. v. Singer, No. 81-03-0023, 1982 WL 3141 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 26, 1982) The lease provision required the tenant to provide the landlord with 60 days advance notice to terminate their month-to-month tenancy. The lease provision did not violate R.C. 5321.17(B) and, therefore, did not violate R.C. 5321.13.

Attorney’s Fees:

KGM Capital, LLC v. Jackson , No. C-130438, 2014 WL 2566286 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 6, 2014) The lease provision provided that the tenant would be liable for the landlord’s attorney’s fees that it incurred due to the tenant’s breach of the
lease. The lease provision violated R.C. 5321.13(C) and, therefore, was unenforceable.

Quinlan v. Lienesch, No. C-120716, 2013 WL 2456347 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 5, 2013) The lease provision provided for the award of attorney’s fees to the prevailing party, in the event of a legal dispute. The lease provision violated R.C. 5321.13(C) and, therefore, was unenforceable.

GMS Mgmt. Co. v. Ostrow , No. 88 -L-13-138, 1989 WL 140157 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Nov. 17, 1989) (dicta) The lease provision provided that, if the landlord relet the premises as a result of the tenant’s lease default, the tenant was liable for the reasonable attorney’s fees that the landlord incurred by reason of the default. The 383 lease provision violated R.C. 5321.13(C) and, therefore, was unenforceable.

Storage of Tenant’s Property:

Haddox v. Moreland , No. 95 -CA-20, 1996 WL 451361 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 5, 1996) The lease provision permitted the landlord to remove and store the tenant’s property, if the tenant abandoned the premises. The lease provision did not violate R.C. Chapter 5321 and, therefore, was enforceable.

### L. Prohibited Occupancy – Sex Offenders,

R.C. 2950.034, 5321.051 R.C. 2950.034 (former R.C. 2950.031) prohibits certain sex offenders from residing within 1,000 feet of any school premises or preschool or child day-care center.

The relevant terms for this prohibition are defined in R.C. 2950.034(C).

R.C. 5321.051 obligates tenants not to permit any such sex offender to occupy the tenant’s rental premises, if the premises are within 1,000 feet of any school premises or preschool or child day-care center. The relevant terms for this obligation are defined in R.C. 5321.01(L) -(N). A tenant’s violation of this obligation is grounds for the landlord to terminate the rental agreement. R.C. 5321.051(A)(2). See also R.C.

1923.01(C)(7),(8), 1923.02(A)(15), 1923.02(C).

R.C. 2950.034 and R.C. 5321.051 apply only to rental agreements that were entered into on or after July 31, 2003. Am. Sub. S.B. No. 5, 125th General Assembly, § 8. See Hyle v. Porter, 117 Ohio St.3d 165, 882 N.E.2d 899 (2008) (R.C. 2950. 034 (former R.C. 2950.031) applies prospectively only).

## VIII. Rent Depositing and Related

Remedies, R.C. 5321.07-.10

### A. Requirements and Procedures for Rent

Depositing R.C. 5321.07 establishes the requirements and procedures for a tenant to deposit rent wit h the clerk of court of the municipal or county court. It also permits one category of landlords – those who lease three or fewer dwelling units – to become exempt from this rent deposit procedure. R.C. 5321.07(C). To perfect this exemption, the landlord must deliver to the tenant, at the time of the tenant’s initial occupancy, written notice of the fact that the landlord leases three or fewer dwelling units. Id.

The rent deposit procedure is comprised of five requirements.

First, one of three conditions must be met. The landlord must be in noncompliance with an obligation under the rental agreement or R.C. 5321.04 (see Section VII.C, above), other than R.C. 5321.04(A)(9). R.C. 5321.07(A). Or, based on the condition of the premises, the tenant must reasonably believe that the landlord is in noncompliance with one (or more) of those obligations.Id. Or a governmental agency must have found that the premises are in noncompliance with an applicable building, housing, health, or safety code and the noncompliance could materially affect the health and safety of an occupant. Id.

Second, the tenant must provide the landlord with a written notice that specifies the acts, omissions, or code violations that constitute the landlord’s noncompliance with R.C. 5321.04, the rental agreement, or the applicable code. 293 R.C. 5321.07(A).

The written notice is not required to state that, if the landlord fails to remedy the condition specified in the notice, the tenant may rent deposit. See R.C. 5321.07(A).

See also Swogger v. Merkel , No. 4139, 1989 WL 572092 (Ohio County Ct., Carroll Cty., Nov. 14, 1989) (construing R.C. 3733.12(A) (now R.C. 4781.41(A)) – the manufactured home park rent deposit notice requirement that is essentially identical to 293 One court decision has held that the R.C. 5321.07(A) notice requirement can be met by a written notice of code violations that a city (and not the tenant) has served upon the landlord.

City of Lakewood v. Novak, 111 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 7 n.1, 746 N.E.2d 719 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, 2000).

385 R.C. 5321.07(A)). The tenant must send this written notice to the person or place where rent is normally paid. R.C. 5321.07(A).

Subject to one exception, the landlord waives this notic e requirement if the landlord fails to provide the tenant, at the commencement of the tenancy, with written notice of the name and address of the landlord and the landlord’s agent (if any). R.C.

5321.18(C). E.g., Shoemaker v. Whitt , 129 Ohio App.3d 591, 718 N.E.2d 932 (2nd
Dist. 1998). The landlord’s noncompliance with R.C. 5321.18 does not waive this notice requirement if the tenant has actual knowledge of the landlord’s name and
address. Highland House Co. v. Gedos, No. 47064, 1984 WL 14194 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 16, 1984).

The landlord also may waive this notice requirement if the landlord suggests or consents to the rent deposit. Liggett v. Shitaker Props., No. 23425, 2010 WL 1410758 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 9, 2010). InLiggett, during a November 5 discussion with new management, the tenant expressed reluctance to pay November rent because of defective conditions. In response, the new management suggested that the tenant deposit the rent with the clerk of court. The court held that, in these circumstances, the landlord “consented to Liggett’s escrowing the November rent money with the court and has waived his right to object.” Id., 2010 WL 1410758, ¶ 44.

Third, the tenant must give the landlord a reasonable time up to thirty days to remedy the condition specified in the R.C. 5321.07 notice (or to respond otherwise, such as furnishing information to refute the alleged noncompliance). R.C. 5321.07(B).

The determination of what constitutes a reasonable amount of time is based on the “severity of the condition and the time necessary to remedy such condition ....” Id.

E.g., Chasteen v. Dix Road Prop. Mgmt. LLC , Nos. CA2020 -04-055,056, 2021 WL 669061, ¶ 40 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 22, 2021) (three weeks met the reasonable time requirement for landlord to remedy damage from sewage flood) ; Stiffler v.

Canterbury Runn Apts. No. 19308, 2002 WL 31243541, ¶ 3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Oct. 4, 2002) (fourteen days met the reasonable time requirement for landlord to repair water leak in kitchen ceiling where “water was running down her cabinetsand onto her dishes, and mold was growing on her walls and carpet, which was becoming flooded.

In addition, the globes covering her light fixtures were filling with water”).

In any case, “R.C. 5321.07(B)(3) gives a landlord thirty day s, at the most , to remedy a condition that violates the obligations imposed by R.C. 5321.04 or that a tenant reasonably believes to violate those obligations.” CMB P’ship v. Baker, No.

18159, 2000 WL 706824, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 2, 2000) (italics original).

Accord Pedra Props., L.L.C. v. Justmann, No. 102909, 2015 WL 9438446,¶ 18 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 2015).

Fourth, based on the landlord’s response to the R.C. 5321.07 notice, the tenant must reassess whether there are still valid grounds to rent deposit. The tenant is not authorized to rent deposit if, within a reasonable time after service of the notice, the landlord remedies the condition specified in the notice or, in refuting the tenant’s noncompliance claim, the landlord negates any reasonable basis for asserting the claim.

R.C. 5321.07(A),(B).

Fifth, the tenant must be current in rent payments due under the rental agreement.

R.C. 5321.07(B).

If these five requ irements for rent depositing have been satisfied, and if the landlord is not exempt from the rent deposit procedure, the tenant may then deposit rent, as the rent becomes due, with the clerk of court.

When a tenant tenders a rent deposit, the “clerk of co urt ... is under a clear legal duty to accept ... [the tenant’s] tendered rent monies under R.C. 5321.07 ....” State ex
rel. Taynor v. Hysell, 19 Ohio App.3d 120, 121, 483 N.E.2d 156, 158 (10th Dist. 1984).

It is a “ministerial duty” of the clerk. Id.

Before accepting a rent deposit, the clerk of court may require the tenant “to sign an affidavit swearing that the statutory preconditions to rent escrow have been met.” Chernin v. Welchans, 641 F. Supp. 1349, 1355 (N.D. Ohio 1986),aff’d, 844 F.2d 322, 324 (6th Cir. 1988). See, e.g., Crease v. Woods of Centerville, No. 14819, 1995 WL 632085, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 25, 1995).

However, the clerk of court may not require the tenant to pay a fee in advance of rent depositing. State ex rel. Gudzinas v. Constantino, 43 Ohio App.3d 52, 539 N.E.2d 173 (11th Dist. 1988). “[T]he statute clearly places the onus of paying the fee on the landlord after the procedure [of R.C. 5321.09] has been completed.” Id. at 54.

Upon receiving a tenant’s rent deposit,the clerk of court is required to provide the landlord with written notice of the rent deposit, to place the deposited rent in a separate rent escrow account, and to record the deposit in a separate docket. R.C. 5321.08.

However, if the landlord failed to comply with the landlord’s R.C. 5321.18 obligation (i.e., written notice to the tenant of the landlord’s name and address, at the commencement of the tenant’s occupancy), the landlord waives the right to receive the clerk of court’s R.C. 5321.08 notice of the rent deposit. R.C. 5321.18(C).

387

### B. Release of the Rent Deposits to the

Landlord, R.C. 5321.09,.10 R.C. 5321.09 establishes the requirements and procedures for a landlord to secure the release of the R.C. 5321.07 rent deposits to the landlord.

The landlord may apply directly to the clerk of court for the release of the rent deposits, if the tenant has provided the clerk of court with a written notice stating that the condition specified in the R.C. 5321.07 notice has been remedied. R.C.

5321.09(A)(1). In these circumstances, the clerk of court is required to “forthwith release the rent deposits, less costs, to the landlord.” Id. For a discussion on the costs, see Section VIII.D, below.

Alternatively, the landlord may file an application with the court for the release of the rent deposits if one (or more) of the R.C. 5321.09(A)(2),(3) grounds for release is
applicable. Those grounds are:
(1) the tenant did not comply with the notice requirement under R.C.

5321.07(A);
(2) the tenant was not current in rent payments at the time that the tenant initiated the rent depositing;
(3) when the tenant initiated the rent depositing, the landlord was not in violation of R.C. 5321.04, the rental agreement, or the applicable codes;

or
(4) the condition specified in the R.C. 5321.07 notice has been remedied.294 The R.C. 5321.09(A)(2),(3) application is a complaint under the Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure. Anderson v. MRW II Prop., No. RE 541 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty.,
Jan. 9, 1990). See also Woodside Terrace Mobile Home Owners As s’n v. Woodside Terrace Co. Ltd. , 53 Ohio App.3d 20, 557 N.E.2d 804 (6th Dist. 1988) (construing R.C. 3733.122 (now R.C. 4781.43) – the manufactured home park provision for rent deposit release that is essentially identical to R.C. 5321.09); Stoots v. Huber Mobile Home Park, No. 1180, 1981 WL 2863 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 27, 1981) (same).

294 One court released the rent deposit to the landlord for a related but different ground, namely:

the tenant’s refusal to allow the landlord access to the premises to make the repairs, when the landlord sought access for this purpose on the date on which the parties had agreed the landlord would commence the repairs. Stoyer v. Fogelman , No. 11AP -737, 2012 WL 10596 77 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 27, 2012).

The complaint must name the tenant as a party. R.C. 5321.09(B). And it must be served on the tenant in accordance with Civil Rules 4.1 and 4.6.See Woodside Terrace Mobile Home Owners Ass’n v. Woodside Terrace Co. Ltd. , 53 Ohio App.3d 20, 557 N.E.2d 804 (6th Dist. 1988); Stoots v. Huber Mobile Home Park, No. 1180, 1981 WL 2863 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 27, 1981).

In an R.C. 5321.09 action, the tenant may file an answer and counterclaims as in other civil actions. R.C. 5321.09(B). See Ferrato v. Maurer Realty, No. WD-86-28, 1986 WL 13815 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 5, 1986). Typically, the tenant’s counterclaims will include one or more claims that are based on the defective condition at the premises for which the tenant commenced the rent deposit procedure. For a discussion on some of those potential claims, see Chapter IX, below.

If an R.C. 5321.09 countercla im exceeds the municipal court’s monetary jurisdiction, an issue exists as to whether the municipal court has exclusive jurisdiction over the R.C. 5321.09 claim only, the R.C. 5321.09 claim and the counterclaim, or neither of them. In two such cases, without expressly addressing this issue, the courts certified the R.C. 5321.09 claim and the counterclaim to the court of common pleas.

Stoyer v. Fogelman, No. 11AP-737, 2012 WL 1059677 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar.

27, 2012); Bright Roads Assocs. v. Homoelle, No. 95-APE10-1361, 1996 WL 125551 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 19, 1996).

The trial on the landlord’s R.C. 5321.09 claim for full release of the rent deposits, and on the tenant’s counterclaims (if any), must be held within sixty days of the filing of the landlord’s R.C. 5321.09 complaint. R.C. 5321.09(B), as modified by Chernin
v. Welchans , 844 F.2d 322 (6th Cir. 1988) (striking down as unconstitutional the portion of R.C. 5321.09(B) that permits the court to continue the hearing date beyond the 60 -day period). See also Ferrato v. Maurer Realty , No. WD -86-28, 1986 WL 13815, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 5, 1986).

The court may not order a release of the rent deposits under R.C. 5321.09 unless it specifically finds, after conducting a trial, that at least one of the R.C.

5321.09(A)(2),(3) grounds for release is met. See Woodside Terrace Mobile Home Owners Ass’n v. Woodside Terrace Co. Ltd., 53 Ohio App.3d 20, 557 N.E.2d 804 (6th
Dist. 1988). This requirement is not met if the court merely finds the landlord’s “application for release of rent deposits well -taken, without stating any ground therefor.” Id. at 22.

In an R.C. 5321.09 action, costs may not be assessed against the tenant unless the court specifically finds that the tenant is liable for suc h costs under R.C. 5321.09(D) 389 (see Section VIII.E, below). See Woodside Terrace Mobile Home Owners Ass’n v.

Woodside Terrace Co. Ltd., 53 Ohio App.3d 20, 557 N.E.2d 804 (6th Dist. 1988).

During the pendency of an R.C. 5321.09 action (including any appea l), the landlord may file an application under R.C. 5321.10 for a partial release of the rent deposits, in order to meet certain operating expenses. Blum v. Pennington, No. 42422, 1981 WL 10409 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 5, 1981). Those expenses are “pa yment of the periodic interest on a mortgage on the premises, the periodic principal payments on a mortgage on the premises, the insurance premiums for the premises, real estate taxes on the premises, utility services, repairs, and other customary and usual costs of operating the premises as a rental unit.” R.C. 5321.10.

“The pendency of an R.C. 5321.09 action is a jurisdictional prerequisite for the filing of an R.C. 5321.10 proceeding.” Blum v. Pennington , No. 42422, 1981 WL 10409, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 5, 1981).

Prior to ruling on a landlord’s R.C. 5321.10 application for partial release, the court must provide the tenant with an opportunity to be heard regarding the application.

Blum v. Pennington, No. 42422, 1981 WL 10409 (Ohio App., 8thDist., Feb. 5, 1981).

In determining whether to grant a landlord’s R.C. 5321.10 application for partial release, the court must consider, among other things, “the costs which may be required to remedy the condition contained in the [R.C. 5321.07] notice ....” R.C. 5321.10(B).

Generally, a trial court’s order granting a landlord’s R.C. 5321.10 application for partial release is not a final appealable order. Pratt v. Dykema , Nos. 44624,44625, 44626, 1981 WL 4724 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 31, 1981). How ever, if the trial court repeatedly issues orders granting a partial release of the tenant’s accumulated rent deposits, the trial court’s order granting another partial release may be a final appealable order. Id.

R.C. 5321.10 is the only provision for a partial release of the rent deposits to the
landlord. Stewart v. Kalman, Nos. 43-57 (Ohio Mun., Athens, Aug. 12, 1981),aff’d in part, rev’d in part on other grounds and remanded, No. 1109, 1982 WL 3509 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 13, 1982). Neither R.C. 5321.07 nor R.C. 5321.09 authorizes a partial release of the rent deposits to the landlord. Id.

### C. Release of the Rent Deposits to the

Tenant A tenant may not file an application under R.C. 5321.09 for the release of the R.C.

5321.07 rent deposits to thetenant. Robinson v. Meyer, No. CA-7735, 1989 WL 94378 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 14, 1989); Weibling v. Rine , No. 77AP -355, 1977 Ohio
App. LEXIS 7537 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Aug. 30, 1977).

However, a tenant may secure the release of the rent deposits to the tenant in at least five ways.

First, if the tenant is awarded a money judgment against the landlord on an R.C.

5321.09(B) counterclaim, the court may order the clerk of court to release to the tenant the portion of the rent deposits that is necessary to satisfy the judgment. E.g., Slovak
v. Univ. Off-Campus Hous., No. 99-CA-50, 2000 WL 680479 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., May 19, 2000); Hansgen v. Seigel , No. 00 -CVR-40822 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 29, 2001); Roundtree v. Valentine , No. 98 -CVR-23790 (Oh io Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 28, 1999); Cummings v. Cortese, No. R88-CVE-2278 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Dec. 4, 1989); Dicks v. Schmelzer , Rent Escrow No. 14 (Ohio County Ct., Muskingum Cty., Aug. 15, 1986); Gill v. Lewis Zipkin & Assocs. , No. 78 -LLT-88 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, Oct. 31, 1979).Cf. Chasteen v. Dix Road Prop. Mgmt.

LLC, Nos. CA2020 -04-055,056, 2021 WL 669061 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 22,
2021) (R.C. 5321.09 action in which the entire rent deposit is released to a tenant who did file counterclaims and who, pending the action, terminated the tenancy).

In these cases, if the court does not issue a release order, the tenant should nonetheless be able to attach the rent deposits to satisfy the judgment.

Second, if the tenant is awardeda money judgment against the landlord on a claim asserted in a complaint, the court may order the clerk of court to release to the tenant the portion of the rent deposits that is necessary to satisfy the judgment. E.g., O’Connor v. Maynard, No. 00 -CVG-01 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., Apr. 17, 2001);

Walton v. Washington, No. 93-CVG-07401 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec.

7, 1993). See also Tolliver v. Warren , No. 99 -CVG-13123 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Dec. 28, 1999) (tenant counterclaim to landlord complaint for eviction and money judgment for unpaid rent).

In these cases, if the court does not issue a release order, the tenant should nonetheless be able to attach the rent deposits to satisfy the judgment.

Third, when the rent has been depositedwith the court in conjunction with an R.C.

5321.07(B) civil action, if the tenant vacates the premises and then files a motion to dismiss the action under Civil Rule 41(A)(2), the court may dismiss the action on “such terms and conditions as the court dee ms proper,” which may include a release of the rent deposits to the tenant. E.g., Brown v. Balliett Group , No. 98-RE-6 (Ohio Mun., 391 Mansfield, July 13, 1998) (tenant vacated as a result of the city’s condemnation of the building). Cf. Miller v. Miller, No. CVH-971763R (Ohio Mun., Portsmouth, May 23,
1998) (tenant dismissed the action under Civil Rule 41(A)(1)).

Fourth, the tenant may enter into an agreement with the landlord under which: (1) the tenant agrees to give a written notice to the clerk of courtstating that the condition specified in the R.C. 5321.07 notice has been remedied; and (2) the landlord agrees to give the tenant a specified portion of the rent deposits as soon as they are released.

Upon receiving the tenant’s written notice, the clerkof court is required to release the rent deposits to the landlord. See Section VIII.B, above.

Fifth, under R.C. 5321.07(B)(2), the tenant may file an application with the court for an order releasing all or some of the rent deposits to the tenant to enable the tenant to remedy the condition specified in the R.C. 5321.07 notice. See Section VIII.H, below.

### D. Fee of Clerk of Court on Release of Rent

Deposits, R.C. 5321.08(D) When releasing rent deposits to the landlord or the tenant, the clerk of court may assess a fee for the clerk’s costs relative to the rent deposits. R.C. 5321.08(D). The amount of this fee may be up to one per cent of the amount of the rent deposited. Id.

See also State ex rel. Gudzinas v. Constantino , 43 Ohio App.3d 52, 539 N.E.2d 1 73 (11th Dist. 1988) (finding that a $25 minimum fee violated R.C. 5321.08(D)).

The clerk deducts this fee from the rent deposits when the clerk releases the deposits, regardless of the basis for the release.

This fee is different than the costs that a cou rt may assess under R.C. 5321.09(D) (see Section VIII.E, below). See Smith v. Vilk, No. 46759, 1983 WL 2902 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 15, 1983) (construing R.C. 3733.121,.122 (now R.C. 4781.42,.43)– the manufactured home park provisions on the clerk’sduties and on releasing rent deposits that are essentially identical to R.C. 5321.08,.09).

### E. Bad Faith Rent Depositing, R.C.

5321.09(D) In an R.C. 5321.09 action, if a court finds that the tenant caused the condition specified in the R.C. 5321.07 notice orthe tenant intentionally acted in bad faith in rent depositing, the tenant is liable for the landlord’s damages and costs. R.C. 5321.09(D).

See Gill v. Lewis Zipkin & Assocs. , No. 78-LLT-88 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights,
Oct. 31, 1979). If the tenant i ntentionally acted in bad faith in rent depositing, the tenant is also liable for the landlord’s reasonable attorney’s fees.Id.

For R.C. 5321.09(D) purposes, bad faith includes “‘a dishonest purpose, moral obliquity, conscious wrongdoing, breach of aknown duty through some ulterior motive or ill will partaking of the nature of fraud. It also embraces actual intent to mislead or deceive another.’” Wenzke v. Baird, No. L-13-1244, 2014 WL 3408590, ¶ 25 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., July 11, 2014) (no evidence that the tenant acted with the requisite degree of culpability).

When city inspections find code violations at the premises that constitute grounds for rent depositing, it precludes a finding of bad faith under R.C. 5321.09(D).

Houtsinger v. Renzelli, No. RE 123 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., July 31, 1987).

When a tenant is liable for costs under R.C. 5321.09(D), the costs are computed in accordance with Civil Rule 54. See Smith v. Vilk , No. 46759, 1983 WL 2902 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 15, 1983) (construing R.C. 3733.122 (now R.C. 4781.43) – the manufactured home park pr ovision on releasing rent deposits that is essentially identical to R.C. 5321.09).

### F. Interplay Between Improper Rent

Depositing and an Eviction Action When a tenant’s R.C. 5321.07 rent depositing is (or allegedly is) improper, two issues may arise in any related eviction action.

One issue arises when a tenant is rent depositing, the landlord files an eviction action for nonpayment of rent, and the landlord attempts to challenge the validity of the rent depositing in the eviction action. Cf. Maryland Apts. v. Crump, No. 96-CVG- 38691 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 14, 1997) (eviction judgment for tenant because “rent in escrow”).

The issue is whether R.C. 5321.09 is the exclusive remedy for the landlord to challenge the validity of the rent depositing. Nocourt decision has addressed this issue.

If R.C. 5321.09 is the exclusive remedy, a landlord cannot challenge the validity of the rent depositing in the eviction action unless three prerequisites are met. First, the landlord must allege an R.C. 5321.09 cl aim in the eviction complaint. Second, under R.C. 5321.09(B), the tenant must be given an opportunity to file an answer and 393 counterclaims to the R.C. 5321.09 claim as in other civil actions ( e.g., twenty-eight days to file the answer and counterclaims). Third, the trials on the eviction claim and the R.C. 5321.09 claim must be consolidated.

If these three prerequisites are not met and the landlord is thereby barred from challenging the validity of the rent depositing in the eviction action, the tenant’s r ent depositing should be a defense to the eviction action, regardless of whether the tenant complied with the R.C. 5321.07 requirements.

A second issue arises when a court releases the rent deposits to the landlord under R.C. 5321.09 and the landlord then files an eviction action against the tenant for nonpayment or late payment of the released rent.

The issue is whether the landlord may evict the tenant for nonpayment or late payment of the released rent. Court decisions are split on this issue. Compare Real
Props. Servs. Mgmt. v. Harigle, No. 3-96-21, 1997 WL 430773 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., July 30, 1997) (eviction not permitted; rent deposits released due to noncompliance with written notice requirement), and Real Props. Servs. Mgmt. v. Anatra , No. 3-96- 22, 1997 WL 430778 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., July 30, 1997)with Timbercreek Vill. Apts.
v. Myles, No. 17422, 1999 WL 335307 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 28, 1999) (eviction permitted; rent deposits released due to noncompliance with reasonable time requirement).

### G. Constitutionality of the Rent Deposit

Procedure The rent deposit procedure established in R.C. 5321.07 -.10 comports with the requirements of due process. Chernin v. Welchans, 844 F.2d 322 (6th Cir. 1988). The Chernin decision struck down only a minor portion of R.C. 5321.09(B), namely: the provision that permitted a court, for good cause shown, to hold the trial on a landlord’s R.C. 5321.09 claim more than sixty days after the filing of the R.C. 5321.09 complaint.

The Chernin decision renders inconsequential the two municipal court decisions that, prior to Chernin, found that the rent deposit procedure violated due process.

Those decisions are Morris Inv. v. Kettman, No. 80-CV-10735 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Mar. 20, 1981), and Rallie Co. v. Pastor , No. M76 -CVG-463763 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 11, 1977).

### H. Tenant Claim for Repair, Reduced Rent,

and Rent Release, R.C. 5321.07(B)(2) When a tenant has satisfied the requirements for rent depositing (see Section VIII.A, above), in addition to or instead of rent depositing, the tenant may file an action under R.C. 5321.07(B)(2). In such actions, the tenant may request: (1) an order directing the landlord to remedy the condition specified in the R.C. 5321.07 notice, (2) an order reducing the periodic rent due the landlord until such time as the landlord remedies the specified condition, and/or (3) an order releasing the rent deposits to the tenant to enable the tenant to remedy the specified condition. R.C. 5321.07(B)(2).

E.g., Stewart v. Kalman, Nos. 43-57 (Ohio Mun., Athens, Aug. 12, 1981) (repairs and rent reduction), aff’d in part, rev’d in part and remanded, No. 1109, 1982 WL 3509 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 13, 1982) (reversed and remanded only as to one of the defective conditions); Woods v. B erry, No. 76 -CVH-21607 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Sept. 1, 1976) (repairs);Player v. Martin, No. 75-CVH-16844 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Oct. 3, 1975) (repairs and release of rent deposits to the tenant).

With respect to repair orders in R.C. 5321.07(B)(2) action s, courts may order the landlord to remedy all code violations, including those which are not specified in the R.C. 5321.07 notice and those which do not materially affect health and safety.Stewart
v. Kalman, No. 1109, 1982 WL 3509, at *1-2 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 13, 1982).

With respect to rent reduction orders in R.C. 5321.07(B)(2) actions, courts typically reduce the rent to the rental value of the premises in their defective condition, similar to general damages in a tenant’s R.C. 5321.04 contra ct claim (see Section lX.A.8, below). E.g., Gammarino v. Smith , No. C -060636, 2007 WL 2284573, ¶¶ 14,22-25 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Aug. 10, 2007).

A tenant may file an R.C. 5321.07(B)(2) action at the same time that the tenant initiates rent depositin g or any time thereafter, provided the tenant still meets the requirements for rent depositing. See R.C. 5321.07(B)(2).

The R.C. 5321.07(B) provision stating that “the tenant may do one of the following” requires the tenant to select between the remedies under R.C.

5321.07(B)(1),(2) and the remedy under R.C. 5321.07(B)(3).295 It does not bar a tenant 295 One court decision has permitted a tenant to rent deposit under R.C. 5321.07(B)(1) for one month and then terminate the rental agreement under R.C. 5321.07(B)(3). Trepka v. Schultz ,
No. 75338, 2000 WL 263734 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 9, 2000).

395 from rent depositing under R.C. 5321.07(B)(1) and subsequently filing an R.C.

5321.07(B)(2) action. The basis for this conclusion is threefold.

First, it is the only reasonable interpretation of the R.C. 5321.07(B) punctuation, which includes a semi-colon at the end of R.C. 5321.07(B)(1) and a period at the end of both R.C. 5321.07(B)(2) and 5321.07(B)(3). The legislature’s distinction between semi-colons and periods must be interpreted in a manner that does not render it nugatory and without meaning. See Schwartz v. McAtee , 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 21, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986). The legislature’s distinction is meaningful only if the semi-colon between (B)(1) and (B)( 2) combines these remedies, and the period between (B)(2) and (B)(3) separates the (B)(3) remedy from the (B)(1) and (B)(2) remedies. If (B)(1), (B)(2), and (B)(3) are construed to be three separate and independent remedies from which the tenant may select only one, it would negate any distinction between the semi- colons and the periods.

Second, it is the interpretation that best advances the object sought to be obtained by R.C. 5321.07(B), and thus is the proper interpretation under R.C. 1.49(A) and 1.11.296 “The General Assembly enacted R.C. 5321.07 to provide tenants with leverage to redress breaches of [the landlord’s R.C. Chapter 5321] duties.”Miller v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 224, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989). Accord City of Lakewood v. Novak , 111 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 6, 746 N.E.2d 719 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, 2000) (“The purpose of the rent deposit is to coerce a landlord to correct the code violations”). This purpose is best advanced by an interpretation of R.C. 5321.07(B) that permits a tenant to commence rent depositing under R.C. 5321.07(B)(1) and to file subsequently an R.C.

5321.07(B)(2) action.

Third, it is a reasonable interpretation of R.C. 5321.07(B) because, in requiring the tenant to select between the R.C. 5321.07(B)(1),(2) remedies and the R.C.

5321.07(B)(3) remedy, it recognizes that, if a tenant elects to terminate the rental agreement and vacate the premises under R.C. 5321.07(B)(3), the tenant lacks standing relative to the R.C. 5321.07(B)(1),(2) remedies ( i.e., future rent depositing, futur e landlord repairs of the premises, future rent reductions, and future rent deposit releases to make repairs).

296 See note 140, above.

### I. Tenant Termination of the Rental

Agreement, R.C. 5321.07(B)(3) When a tenant has satisfied the requirements for rent depositing (see Section VIII.A, above), instead of rent depositing and/or filing an R.C. 5321.07(B)(2) action (see Section VIII.H, above), the tenant may terminate the rental agreement. R.C.

5321.07(B)(3). E.g., Lloyd v. Roosevelt Props., No. 105721, 2018 WL 3814972 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 9, 2018); Holmes v. Cobblestone Grove , No. CA2016 -04-075, 2017 WL 74986 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 9, 2017);Cont’l Enters., Ltd. v. Franklin,
No. 102899, 2016 WL 2941209 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 19, 2016); Cont’l Enters.,
Ltd. v.Hunt, No. 102200, 2015 WL 9460300 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 2015) ;

Turski v. Loesch, No. L-05-1273, 2006 WL 3378415 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 22, 2006); Stiffler v. Canterbury Runn Apts., No. 19308, 2002 WL 31243541 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 4, 2002); CMB P’ship v. Baker, No. 18159, 2000 WL 706824 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 2, 2000); Trepka v. Schultz, No. 75338, 2000 WL 263734 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 9 , 2000); Leszczynski v. Brewer, No. 12523, 1991 WL 285435 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 27, 1991);Hald v. Beatley, No. 87AP-43, 1988 WL 37456 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 29, 1988); Pearson v. Huber Inv. Corp., No. 84AP-526, 1985 WL 9913 (Ohio App., 10th Di st., Mar. 21, 1985). See also Shannon Way Apts.
v. Jackson, No. 92AP-254, 1992 WL 225567 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 15, 1992) (landlord’s reasonable and prompt remedy of the defect precluded an R.C.

5321.07(B)(3) termination of the rental agreement).

The effective date of a termination of a rental agreement under R.C. 5321.07(B)(3) may be as early as the first day of the lease term, if the R.C. 5321.07 notice is waived or precedes the commencement of the rental agreement. E.g., Shoemaker v. Whitt, 129 Ohio App.3d 591, 718 N.E.2d 932 (2nd Dist. 1998) (R.C. 5321.18 waiver of the R.C.

5321.07 notice requirement). See also Harpel v. Pierce, Nos. 17163,17168, 1999 WL 55700, at *5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 5, 1999).

With respect to the R.C. 5321.07(B)(3) remedy, appellate courts are split on whether the R.C. 5321.07 notice requirement is met where the tenant has not provided the landlord with written notice of the defective condition, but the landlord has actual notice of the condition. The Eighth Appell ate District has held that, in these circumstances, the R.C. 5321.07 notice requirement is met.Sabik v. Drake, No. 53588, 1988 WL 32111 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 17, 1988); Katzin v. Murad, No. 46553, 397 1984 WL 7150 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 5, 1984). The Sixth Appellate District has upheld a tenant’s termination of a rental agreement under R.C. 5321.07(B), in these circumstances, although the court did not explicitly address this issue. Keener v.

Ewert, 67 Ohio App.2d 17, 425 N.E.2d 914 (6th Dist. 19 79). The Seventh Appellate District has held that, in these circumstances, the R.C. 5321.07 notice requirement is not met. Rental Mgmt. v. Au, No. 92-C-66, 1993 WL 307614 (Ohio App., 7th Dist.,
Aug. 10, 1993).

When a tenant elects to terminate a rental agreement under R.C. 5321.07(B)(3), the tenant may be required to provide the landlord with oral or written notice of the
election. See Shoemaker v. Whitt , 129 Ohio App.3d 591, 594, 718 N.E.2d 932 (2nd
Dist. 1998).

A tenant is not required to rely on or comply with R.C. 5321.07 to assert a common law claim of constructive eviction. Schutte v. Nicholson , No. C -910261, 1992 WL 19396 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Feb. 5, 1992) (constructive eviction based on lack of heat for five days). For a discussion on constructive eviction, see Section XIV.C, below.

## IX. Tenant Claims for Defective

Condition at the Premises

### A. R.C. 5321.04 Contract Claim


#### 1. Introduction

When a landlord’s failure to maintain or repair the premises violates an R.C.

5321.04(A) obligation, the tenant may have a contract claim against the landlord (“R.C.

5321.04 contract claim”). Miller v. Ritchie , 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989); Laster v. Bowman, 52 Ohio App.2d 379, 370 N.E.2d 767 (8th Dist. 1977).See also R.C. 5321.12.

“A landlord who fails to maintaina rental premises as required by R.C. 5321.04 is liable ... for damages for breach of contract, as the rental contract necessarily incorporates the duties imposed by the revised code ....” Foss v. Re ddy, No. 68836, 1995 WL 643811, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 2, 1995). Accord Bozeman v.

Cleveland Metro. Hous. Auth., Nos. 92435,92436, 2009 WL 3321402, at *3 n.4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 15, 2009).

The R.C. 5321.04 contract claim has three eleme nts: (1) the landlord’s violation of an R.C. 5321.04(A) obligation (see Section VII.C, above), (2) the tenant’s damages as a direct result of the violation,297 and (3) prior notice to the landlord of the violation.

The elements of the claim and related ma tters are discussed in Sections IX.A.2 -12, below.

When a landlord’s failure to maintain or repair the premises violates an express provision of the rental agreement, the tenant’s contract claim is essentially the same as the R.C. 5321.04 contract claim. See Smith v. Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987) (oral provision); Kuhn v. Griffin, 3 Ohio App.2d 195, 209 N.E.2d 824 (6th
Dist. 1964) (written provision). For Ohio case law that establishes that leases are contracts, see Section VII.A, above.

“The breach of a contract for the lease of property may give rise to ordinary contract remedies for the injured party.”U.S.A. Parking Sys., Inc. v. Penn-Lillis Corp., 297 In an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim, a tenant may seek injunctive relief, in addition to monetary relief. See Section IX.A.7., below. When a tenant seeks injunctive relief, the prerequisites for issuing an injunction are an additional element of the claim.

399
No. 66936, 1995 WL 601186, at *4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 12, 1995). Accord Kostoglou v. Midkiff Enters., Inc. , No. 01 -CA-23, 2001 WL 1469161, at *6 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Nov. 6, 2001). Therefore, “[g]eneral contract principles govern damages recoverable in a tenant’s action for breach of a lease.” Williams v. Cotton,
No. 66312, 1994 WL 236219, at *1 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 26, 1994).

#### 2. Notice Requirement

The R.C. 5321.04 contract claim includes a notice requirement. No court decision has clearly and fully addressed this issue. However, Miller v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989), and lower court decisions seem to indicate that there is a notice requirement and it is the notice requirement that the Supreme Court initially established in Shroades v. Rental Homes, Inc. , 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981), and then clarified in Sikora v. Wenzel , 88 Ohio St.3d 493, 727 N.E.2d 1277 (2000), for the R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim. For a discussion on that notice requirement (“Shroades/Sikora notice”), see Section IX.B.2, below.

Miller refers to a requirement that the landlord be placed on notice of the defective
condition. Miller, 45 Ohio St.3d at 226. The notice requirement was met inMiller by the tenant’s oral requests to the landlord for repairs and by a written notice of code violations from a county building inspection department to the landlord.

One appellate court and one municipal court have held that, absent any notice to the landlord of the defective condition, the tenant may not bring an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim. Murray v. Bargo, No. CA86 -11-080, 1987 WL 15440 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Aug. 10, 1987); Crawley v. Brown , No. 2005 -CVG-16627 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 6, 2005).

Two appellate courts and one municipal court seem to hold that the notice requirement o f the R.C. 5321.04 contract claim does not require notice in writing.

Hofer v. Cooper, No. 1909, 1989 Ohio App. LEXIS 870 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar.

17, 1989); Doganiero v. Cox, No. 9-292, 1984 WL 6297 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Apr.

2, 1984); Lobo v. Hoelscher, 44 Ohio Misc. 46, 336 N.E.2d 475 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, 1975). But see Higgins v. Roe, No. 73-CA-3 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 2, 1979).

Application of the Shroades/Sikora notice to the R.C. 5321.04 contract claim is
reasonable. The Shroades/Sikora notice is essentially the same as the notice requirements of two analogous claims.

One is the claim for a breach of a lease covenant to make repairs. The notice requirement for this lease claim is notice from the tenant to the landlord of the need of repairs, unless the landlord has knowledge of the need. 65 Ohio Jur.3d Landlord and Tenant § 365 (2006). Accord Rittenberg v. Donohoe Constr. Co. Inc., 426 A.2d 338, 344 (D.C. App. 1981); J.C. Penney Co. v. Teddam Realty Corp. , 33 A.D.2d 1006, 1007, 307 N.Y.S.2d 750, 751 (1970); Stool v. J.C. Penney Co., 404 F.2d 562, 566 (5th
Cir. 1968).

The other is the claim for a breach of the implied warranty of habitability, which exists in other jurisdictions. The notice requirement for this implied warranty claim is notice from the tenant to the landlord of the defective condition, unless the landlord has knowledge of the defective condition. E.g., Detling v. Edelbrock, 671 S.W.2d 265
(Mo. 1984); Berman & Sons, Inc. v. Jefferson, 379 Mass. 196, 396 N.E.2d 981 (1979).

#### 3. Reasonable Time to Remedy Issue

In an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim, an issue exists as to whether the landlord is liable for an R.C. 5321.04 violation upon receiving notice of the defective condition or upon the landlord’s failure (if any) to remedy the con dition within a reasonable time after receiving such notice.

One appellate court has held that a landlord is not liable for an R.C. 5321.04 violation if the landlord remedies the defective condition within a reasonable time after receiving notice of the condition. Pedraza v. Oster Invs., Inc., No. 7-87-10, 1989 WL 29215 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Mar. 21, 1989). It concluded that, “[w]hile the landlord may have a duty to maintain the stairway in good repair, a reasonable amount of time must be allowed to effect the repairs.” Id., 1989 WL 29215, at *2. See also Stiffler v.

Canterbury Runn Apts., No. 19308, 2002 WL 31243541 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 4,
2002) (failed to remedy within reasonable time);Boice v. Emshoff, No. 13-98-23, 1998 WL 833686 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Dec. 3, 1998).

The implied warranty of habitability case law in other jurisdictions is split on this
issue. Compare Berman & Sons, Inc. v. Jefferson , 379 Mass. 196, 396 N.E.2d 981
(1979) (liability upon receiving notice), and Knight v. Hallsth ammer, 29 Cal.3d 46, 171 Cal. Rptr. 707 (1981) with Glasoe v. Trinkle, 107 Ill.2d 1, 479 N.E.2d 915 (1985) (liability upon failure to remedy within a reasonable time after notice), and Detling v.

Edelbrock, 671 S.W.2d 265 (Mo. 1984).

The reasoning of the c ourt decisions holding that the landlord is liable upon receiving notice of the defective condition is summarized in Jefferson, 379 Mass. at 202-03 (alteration in original):

The landlord argues that the existence of a serious defect in an 401 apartment is a potential breach of the warranty; the breach becomes actual only after the landlord has been notified of the defect and has had a reasonable time to repair. In Hemingway, however, we set out a very different picture of the warranty: “[The warranty] means that at the inception of the rental there are no latent [or patent] defects in facilities vital to the use of the premises for residential purposes and that these essential fac ilities will remain during the entire term in a condition which makes the property livable.” Boston Hous. Auth. v.

Hemingway, supra at 199, quoting from Kline v. Burns, 111 N.H. 87, 92 (1971). Neither at the inception nor during the term of the lease did we leave room for a reasonable time to repair. Admittedly a tenant must notify her landlord of defects as a prerequisite to a rent abatement, but the purpose of this requirement is not to assure the landlord a reasonable time to repair. The requirement is designed to minimize the time the landlord is in breach and hence mitigate the permissible period of abatement of rent. The rent abatement begins when notice is given, not at a reasonable time after notice. Time for repairs has no place in the calculus.

#### 4. Landlord Fault Issues

If a landlord is at fault for the defective condition, the landlord’s attempts to remedy the defective condition does not relieve the landlord of liability if the attempts are unsuccessful. Lawrence v. Triangle Capital Corp., 90 Ohio App.3d 105, 107, 628 N.E.2d 74 (2nd Dist. 1993) (landlord liable for roach infestation; “the fact that Triangle made an effort to eliminate the vermin from the Lawrence apartment did not make living conditions, such as they were, any more predictable”); Williams v. Wright, No.

2000-CVG-01040 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Aug. 15, 2001) (landlord liable for roach infestation; “Plaintiff [landlord] attempted to correct the problem but his efforts were unsuccessful”).

However, there are circumstances where the premises are not in compliance with the R.C. 5321.04(A) standards, and it is not the result of any action or inaction by the
landlord. For example, there may be defective conditions at the rental unit solely because of a deficiency in the municipal water or sewer system.

The issue then is whether a landlord may be liable in an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim if the landlord is not at fault for the defective condition.

Two appellate districts have held that a landlord is not liable in these
circumstances. Conway v. Liddell, No. 79799, 2002 WL 398687 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Mar. 14, 2002) (landlord not liable under R.C. 5321.04(A) for sewer back-up resulting solely from a defect in the public sewer system); Paul v. Semirale, No. 70438, 1996 WL 502143 (Ohio App., 8thDist., Sept. 5, 1996) (same);Stewart v. Kalman, No. 1109, 1982 WL 3509 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 13, 1982) (landlord not liable under R.C.

5321.04(A) for the inadequate water supply resulting solely from a defect in the municipal water system).

The implied warranty of habitability case law in other jurisdictions is split on this
issue. Compare Park W. Mgmt. Corp. v. Mitchell , 62 A.D. 291, 404 N.Y.S.2d 115
(1978) (fault irrelevant to liability), aff’d, 47 N.Y.2d 316, 418 N.Y.S.2d 310 (1979), and Berman & Sons, Inc. v. Jefferson , 379 Mass. 196, 396 N.E.2d 981 (1979) with George Washington Univ. v. Weintraub, 458 A.2d 43 (D.C. App. 1983) (fault relevant to liability).

Court decisions holding that fault is irrelevant to liability recognize that: (1) the claim is a contract claim, (2) consideration of fault is not incorporated into any analogous body of warranty law, and (3) the essential objective of the warranty is not to penalize the landlord for wrongdoing, but rather to make sure that the tenant receives the property and services for which the tenant is paying. Jefferson, 379 Mass. at 200- 02.

#### 5. Subtenant Issues

“There is no privity of contract between the original lessor and a subtenant.” City of Cleveland v. A.J. Rose Mfg. Co. , 89 Ohio App.3d 267, 271, 624 N.E.2d 245 (8th
Dist. 1993). Accord Mark-It Place Foods, Inc. v. New Plan Excel Realty Trust, Inc. , 156 O hio App.3d 65, 804 N.E.2d 979, ¶ 19 (4th Dist. 2004). Consequently, “a subtenant cannot maintain an action against the lessor on the original lease.” Id.

Likewise, “the lessor may not sue the subtenant for breach of a covenant contained in the original lease.” Id. However, the lessor may maintain an eviction action against a subtenant. Id. See Section I.O.3, above.

#### 6. Tenant Cooperation Issues

If a tenant denies the landlord entry to make the repairs and thereby violates R.C.

5321.05(B), the tenant waive s the right to recover damages for the landlord’s failure to make the repairs. E.g., A.C.S. Realty & Inv. Corp. v. Smith , No. 48664, 1985 WL 9025 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 23, 1985). Cf. Stoyer v. Fogelman, No. 11AP-737, 2012 WL 1059677 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 27, 2012) (R.C. 5321.07 rent deposit released to landlord because tenant denied landlord access to make repairs).

403 Also, there are circumstances where a landlord’s ability to remedy the defective condition requires the tenant’s cooperation beyond simply allowing the landlord access to the dwelling unit. For example, where the defective condition is a bed bug infestation, the landlord may require the tenant to prepare the dwelling unit for the extermination service. See Kenwood Gardens Ass’n, LLC v. Shorter, No. L-10-1315, 2011 WL 3654493 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 19, 2011). In such cases, the tenant’s lack of cooperation may be a defense to the tenant’s R.C. 5321.04 contract claim.

#### 7. Injunctive Relief, and Contempt Enforcement

In an R.C. 532 1.04 contract claim, the tenant may secure injunctive relief under Civil Rule 65 to enjoin the landlord from failing to remedy the R.C. 5321.04 violation.

E.g., Medi Invs. LLC v. Mitchell, No 2020-CVG-009347 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., Dec. 11, 2020) (landlord “ordered to make any and all necessary repairs to correct the mechanical failure causing a lack of heat, or to replace if necessary any and all heating elements designed to distribute the heat to the premises”);Woods v. Coleman,
No. 01-CVG-444 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., Apr. 26, 2001) (preliminary injunction enjoining landlord from refusing to make the repairs necessary to provide the premises with running water, hot water, and a working toilet).

The injunctive relief will be similar to an R. C. 5321.07(B)(2) order directing the landlord to remedy the defective condition. See Section VIII.H, above. However, unlike an R.C. 5321.07(B)(2) claim, when seeking injunctive relief, the tenant may be required to address irreparable harm and bond issues.

An injunction order (or settlement) is usually most effective if it designates with specificity the repairs and services that the landlord is required to perform, and the date by which each repair and service shall be completed. For examples of such orders (or settlements), see Hardin v. Meyer , No. CVJ -94-00160 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous.

Div., Feb. 14, 1997) (settlement); Mullins v. Vonderhaar , No. 87 -CV-07154 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., May 27, 1987) (order).

For settlement agreements, the tenant’s interests are best protected if the agreement is filed with the court as a stipulation or entry and signed by the judge. This will enable the tenant to enforce the terms of the agreement through civil contempt proceedings, if
necessary. E.g., Hardin v. Meyer, No. CVJ-94-00160 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div.,
Feb. 14, 1997), aff’d, No. L-97-1060, 1998 WL 636774 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept.

18, 1998); Lakota v. McElrath, No. 2014-CVG-003743 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., Dec. 9, 2014).

“A trial court may make a finding of contempt even if the party’s failure to comply with the order was not purposeful, willful, or intentional.” Hardin v. Meyer, No. L- 97-1060, 1998 WL 636774, at *6 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 18, 1998). “It is no defense to a finding of contempt that a party acted in good faith and not in intentional disregard of a court order.” Id.

“A court may punish disobedience of its order, pursuant to R.C. 2705.02(A) or the court’s inherent power to enforce its authority.” Id.

“[T]he power to punish for contempt is inherent in the courts.” First Bank of Marietta v. Mascrete, Inc. , 125 Ohio App.3d 257, 265, 708 N.E.2d 262 (4th Dist.

1998). “Compensation of the injured party has been a traditional function of civil contempt.” Id. Accord Oak Hill Banks v. Ison , No. 03 -CA-5, 2003 WL 22386806 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Oct. 15, 2003). “[A] trial court may award damages and attorney fees as compensation for losses which occurred as a direct result of the contempt.” Hardin, 1998 WL 636774, at *7. Accord Ison, 2003 WL 22386806, ¶ 29; Mascrete, 125 Ohio App.3d at 265.

If a court elects to award damages and attorney’s fees under its inherent authority, the limitation of R.C. 2705.05 does not apply. Hardin, 1998 WL 636774, at *7. The only limitation of R.C. 2705.05 is that, in civil contempt proceedings, it permits courts to impose fines in specified amounts.

#### 8. Monetary Relief


##### a. General Damages – General Rule

The measure of general damages in an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim is the difference between the rental value of the premises in their defective condition and the rental value of the premises without the defective condition. 298 Miller v. Ritchie , 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989); Smith v. Padgett , 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987).

The amount of rent that the tenant is obligated to pay under the rental agreement is presumptive, but not conclusive, evidence of the rental value of the premises without the defective condition. Miller, 45 Ohio St.3d at 227; Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d at 347;

298 This is the same standard that some jurisdictions use in connection with their implied warranty of habitability. E.g., Glasoe v. Trinkle, 107 Ill.2d 1, 479 N.E.2d 915 (1985); Hilder
v. St. Peter, 144 Vt. 150, 478 A.2d 202 (1984); Boston Hous. Auth. v. Hemingway , 363 Mass.

184, 293 N.E.2d 831 (1973); Mease v. Fox, 200 N.W.2d 791 (Iowa 1972).

405 Pleasant v. Greenfield Station Apts. , No. 15282, 1995 WL 614359 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 20, 1995). E.g., Heck v. Whitehurst Co., No. L-03-1134, 2004 WL 1857131 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2004) (rental value without defects is contract rent of $639 per month).

The rental value of the premises without the defective condition may be an amount either higher or lower than the contract rent. Cochran v. Widra , 35 Ohio Law Abs.

608, 41 N.E.2d 875 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., 1931). Consequently, at least in theory, the amount of the general damages may exceed the amount of the contract rent. See Whitehouse Estates, Inc. v. Thompson , 87 Misc.2d 813, 386 N.Y.S.2d 733 (Civ. Ct.

1976).

For a discussion on proof issues, relative to the rental value of the premises in their defective condition, see Sections IX.A.11,12, below.

When determining the rental value of the premises in their defective condition, the tenant should be mindful of the Supreme Court’s observation that “[r]arely, if ever, will a defective rental property have a value of zero.” Miller v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 226, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989).

But, in egregious cases, post -Miller court decisions have found a rental value of
zero. E.g., Liggett v. Shitaker Props., No. 23425, 2010 WL 1410758 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 9, 2010) (serious infestation – each day tenant killed 30 to 40 roaches and 2 or 3 mice); Pleasant v. Greenfield Station Apts., No. 15282, 1995 WL 614359 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 20, 1995) (unbearable temperatures due to air conditioner defect);

Hansgen v. Seigel, No. 00-CVR-40822 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 29, 2001) (no water and no heat except for portable heater in one room);Roundtree v. Valentine, No.

98-CVR-23790 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 28, 1999) (no hot water, raw sewage/standing water in basement, and other code violations); Proctor v. Frame, 90 Ohio Misc.2d 11, 695 N.E.2d 357 (Ohio Mun., Akron, 1998) (no electrici ty and no heat); Moody v. Brady , No. 92 -CVF-549 (Ohio Mun., Clinton Cty., Mar. 8, 1993) (defective furnace made premises unsafe).

In Miller, the Supreme Court expressly rejected the reduction -in-use measure of general damages, 299 which some jurisdictions us e in connection with their implied warranty of habitability.300 45 Ohio St.3d at 226. However, insofar as the defective condition causes a reduction -in-use, it may be relevant to the rental value of the premises in their defective condition. Id.

In an R. C. 5321.04 contract claim, a tenant may recover general damages retroactive to the date on which the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A) violation began, subject only to statute of limitations, laches, and equitable estoppel defenses. Miller, 45 Ohio St.3d at 225-26. See also Pagoda Co. v. Smith, No. 37936, 1979 Ohio App.

LEXIS 11691, at *11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 8, 1979). For a discussion on the statute of limitations for an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim, see Section XXII.G, below.

##### b. General Damages – Assisted Housing

Tenants who reside in assisted housing301 have standing to bring an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim. Foss v. Reddy , No. 68836, 1995 WL 643811 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Nov. 2, 1995). See also Bozeman v. Cleveland Metro. Hous. Auth., Nos. 92435,92436, 2009 WL 3321402, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 15, 2009); Malcom v. Tate, No.

99-CVH-21689, slip op. at 3 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 29, 2001). But
cf. Budac v. Nahmi, No. 14901, 1991 WL 115908 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 19, 1991) (ODHS payment for security deposit).

In their claims, the measure of general damages is not based on the tenant’s share of the subsidized rent, but rather is based on the full market rental value of the premises in their defective and non -defective condition, like any other tenancy. Sertich v.

299 The Miller decision also implicitly rejected two other standards that Ohio courts previously had used for measuring general damages in contract claims alleging a landlord’s failure to maintain or repair the premises. Under one, the measure of general damages was thedifference between the rental value of the premises in their defective condition and the contract rent. E.g., Howard v. Simon, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist. 1984);Woolworth Co. v. Russo, 16 Ohio Law Abs. 307 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1933); Glyco v. Schultz, 35 Ohio Misc. 25, 289 N.E.2d 919 (Ohio Mun., Sylvania, 1972). Under the other, the landlord’s failure to maintain the premises in a habitable condition relieved the tenant of the duty to pay any rent.E.g., Wymer
v. Kimbaugh, No. 74AP -547, 1975 Ohio App. LEXIS 7359 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 13, 1975); Rains v. Temple, No. 88-CVG-74 (Ohio Mun., Hillsboro, May 22, 1988).

300 E.g., Pugh v. Holmes, 486 Pa. 272, 405 A.2d 897 (1979); Green v. Superior Court of City & County of San Francisco , 10 Cal.3d 616, 111 Cal. Rptr. 704 (1974) (en banc); McKenna v.

Begin, 3 Mass. App. Ct. 168, 325 N.E.2d 587 (1975).

301 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, above.

407 Jackson, No. CVG-1801089 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, Dec. 30, 2019); Ronny
v. Bowman, No. 2007-CVG-24072 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 27, 2008, and Mar. 9, 2010); Crawley v. Brown, No. 2005-CVG-16627 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Oct. 6, 2005); Harris v. Winston , No. 2004 -CVG-28660 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 16, 2005); Malcom v. Tate , No. 99 -CVH-21689 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 29, 2001); Kenwood Courts Apts. v. Williams, No.

K90-CVG-1043 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 25, 1991). 302 See also Rosier v.

Newman, No. CVE -0500771 (Ohio Mun., Washington Court House, Mar. 5, 2007).

But see Wallpe v. McCurdy , No. 84-CV-36081 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Oct. 15, 1985). But cf. Brown v. Augu st, No. 01-CA-0024, 2002 WL 22877 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 9, 2002) (VA payment for rent).

“Because the federal rent subsidy is intended to provide Defendant [tenant] with decent housing, Defendant is entitled to damages based on the contract rent, no t her share of that rent.”Harris v. Winston, No. 2004-CVG-28660, slip op. at 3 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 16, 2005).Accord Kenwood Courts Apts. v. Williams, No.

K90-CVG-1043, slip op. at 4 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 25, 1991).

This rationale is more fully summarized inCruz Mgmt. Co. v. Wideman, 417 Mass.

771, 775-76, 633 N.E.2d 384 (1994):

“Damages for breach of the implied warranty of habitability are measured by ‘the difference between the value of the dwelling as warranted (the rent agreed on may be evidence of this value) and the value of the dwelling as it exists in its defective condition.’” .... This measure of damages is purely compensatory. It “gives a tenant ... the benefit of the bargain because the implied warranty of habita bility is part of the bargain” the tenant makes with a landlord when the tenant agrees to pay rent.... Recovery on this basis is neither a windfall to the tenant nor an award of punitive damages. It is compensation for the infringement of a contractual r ight.... Whether or not the tenant is required to pay in rent the full value of the apartment as warranted, the tenant is nonetheless entitled to damages based on the value of the premises in a liveable condition.... It is, after all, the tenant who 302 Other jurisdictions also use this approach in computing general damages forassisted housing tenants under their implied warranty of habitability. E.g., Penny Point Park Apts. v. Barnes , 2007 WL 3289133 (N.J. Super. Nov. 8, 2007); Committed Cmty. Assocs. v. Crosswell , 250 A.D.2d 845, 673 N.Y.S.2d 708 (1998); Cruz Mgmt. Co. v. Wi deman, 417 Mass. 771, 633 N.E.2d 384 (1994).

suffers when conditions in an apartment violate the implied warranty of habitability.

Accord Ronny v. Bowman, No. 2007-CVG-24072 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 27, 2008, and Mar. 9, 2010).

The rental units in the assisted housing programs have a marke t-based contract
rent. In the assisted housing programs other than Public Housing, the market -based contract rent is set forth in the lease. It is the sum of the tenant’s portion of the rent and the rent subsidy paid on behalf of the tenant. In Public Housing, it is the flat rent (see 24 C.F.R. § 960.253(b)), which is a maximum rent for the rental unit. See Walker
v. Boston Hous. Auth., No. 00-H84-CV-000149, slip op. at 19 (Mass. Hous. Ct., Boston Div., June 9, 2009) (“the flat rents best approximate the fair market value of a public housing unit”).

The market-based contract rent for an assisted housing rental unit, like the contract rent in private housing, is presumptive evidence of the rental value of the premises without the defective condition.

##### c. General Damages – Amounts Awarded

Court decisions awarding general damages in an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim are set forth below, along with the R.C. 5321.04 violation, the contract rent , and the amount of the general damages awarded.

Bynum v. Cotterman , No. 26222, 2015 WL 751720 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 20, 2015) R.C. 5321.04 violation: bed bug infestation for 11 months; lack of use of bathtub (due to poor drainage) for two weeks. Contract rent: $800 per month. Damage award: $200 per month for 11 months (total of $2,200) for bed bug infestation; $200 for lack of use of bathtub for two weeks.

Liggett v. Shitaker Props., No. 23425, 2010 WL 1410758 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr.

9, 2010) R.C. 5321.04 violation: infestation of roaches and mice– tenant “killing thirty to forty roaches a day, two to three mice a day” – causing “conditions in the unit [that] were intolerable and inexcusable.” Contract rent: $525 per month. Damage award: $525 per month for two months, total of $1,050 (released to tenant from rent deposit).

Heck v. Whitehurst Co., No. L-03-1134, 2004 WL 1857131 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug.

20, 2004) R.C. 5321.04 violation: cigarette smoke from neighbor’s apartment precluded use of 409 master bedroom and closet. Contract rent: $639 per month. Damage award: $319.50 per month for 12 months, total of $3,834.

Pleasant v. Greenfield Station Apts. , No. 152 82, 1995 WL 614359 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 20, 1995) R.C. 5321.04 violation: Failure to repair air conditioner, which caused unbearable temperatures in the apartment. Contract rent: $629 per month. Damage award: total of $629 for seven weeks.

Houshour v. Pfisterer, No. 2021-CVG-350 (Ohio Mun., Canton, June 10, 2021) R.C. 5321.04 violation: failure to provide adequate he at. Contract rent: $ 375 per
month. Damage award: $2,675 ($375 monthly rent amount for six months, plus $425 security deposit for another rental unit).

Johnson v. Rahim , No. 201 9-CVG-008127 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan.

21, 2020) R.C. 5321.04 violation: Lack of water service for 77 days. Contract rent: $ 927 per
month. Damage award: $2,310.

ATKM Mgmt. Group LLC v. Talley, No. 2018-CVG-018737 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Dec. 30, 2019) R.C. 5321.04 violation: Mold for seven months; vermin infestation and furnace not working properly for five months . Contract rent: $ 775 per month. Damage award (mold): $350 ($50 per month for seven months ); Damage award (vermin and faulty furnace): $250 ($50 per month for five months).

Baer v. Maynard, No. LLT-18-5 (Ohio Mun., Marion Cty., Apr. 29, 2019) R.C. 5321.04 violation: Frequent water and sewage backup in the basement, for five
months. Contract rent: $675 per month. Damage award: $1,750 ($350 per month, for five months).

Hamilton v. Dickerson, No. 2016-CVG-4233 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 2, 2018) R.C. 5321.04 violation: Lack of water service for 13 days. Contract rent: $450 per
month. Damage award: $200.

Brown v. Williams , No. 2012-CVG-17491 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar.

11, 2014, and May 2, 2014) R.C. 5321.04 violation: Lack of water service, the result of which tenant “lost almost all use of the property for the two weeks that the water was off.” Contract rent: $641.

Damage award: $320 (the full contract rent for the two-week period).

Ronny v. Bowman , No. 2007 -CVG-24072 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 27, 2008, and Mar. 9, 2010) R.C. 5321.04 vio lation: “primarily cosmetic” – breaker box knockout caps, tub caulking, porch painting, motion detector bulb, yard debris, step handrails, carpet attachment on stairs, smoke detector, and floor support for weak areas. Contract rent:

$772 per month. Damage award: $50 per month for six months, total of $300.

H-1 Mgmt. LLC v. Perryman , No. 2006 -CVG-28200 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., July 10, 2007) R.C. 5321.04 violation: lack of heat for two weeks; water leak to basement from ducts;

kitchen ceiling water damage, hole and major cracks, evidencing water leak; bedroom outlets not grounded; missing or broken cover plate to wires hanging by furnace; and defective paint on exterior. Contract rent: $520 per month. Damage award (lack of heat for two weeks): $ 195 (75% reduction of contract rent). Damage award (other defective conditions): $50 per month for nine months, total of $450.

Rosier v. Newman, No. CVE-0500771 (Ohio Mun., Washington Court House, Mar. 5, 2007) R.C. 5321.04 violation: laundry room (roof l eak, light fixture inoperative, paint chipping, washer drain backflow); kitchen (ceiling leak, pipes to upstairs apartment leak, sink feedline leak, cabinets defective); bathroom (tub drain clog, hot water without pressure); front porch (deck and handraildefects); backyard (debris of shingles and nails); basement (waste-water clean-up and defective pipes). Contract rent: $425.

Damage award: $225 per month for 12 months, total of $2,700.

Francis v. Peters, No. 05-CVG-1116 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Sept. 21, 2006) R.C. 5321.04 violation: basement flooding and electrical service problems, including the circuit breaker often kicking off, causing a loss of power to the house and, therefore, a loss of the furnace and hot water heater. Contr act rent: $425 per month. Damage award: $125 per month for 20 months, total of $2,500.

Clark v. Schiff, No. 05-CVG-1103 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, July 24, 2006) R.C. 5321.04 violation: defective kitchen window, roof leak, and defective carpet.

Contract rent: $375 per month. Damage award: $175 per month for 3.5 months, total of $615.

Dickson v. Lancaster, No. 06-CVG-288 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., May 18, 2006) R.C. 5321.04 violation: roof leak, causing ceiling to fall in one room, making the room
unusable. Contract rent: $400 per month. Damage award: $266.67 (pro-rated value of one of six rooms ($66.66 per month) for four months).

411 Jackson v. George, No. 05-CVF-01761 (Ohio Mun., East Cleveland, Feb. 16, 2006) R.C. 5321.04 violation: lack of wate r service, due to landlord’s failure to pay water
bill. Contract rent: $811. Damage award: $300 per month for four months, total of $1,200.

Howard v. Reeves, No. 05-CVG-1910 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Jan. 26, 2006) R.C. 5321.04 violation: electrical service problems; exposed electrical wires; defective duct work, doors, and windows; and construction debris. Contract rent: $500 per
month. Damage award: $164.26 per month for six months, total of $985.58 (released to tenant from rent deposit).

Crawley v. Brown, No. 2005-CVG-16627 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 6, 2005) R.C. 5321.04 violation: inadequate heat, exposed electrical wiring, water damage, loose/broken/missing flooring in kitchen, hole in chimney opening into a room, holes in bathroom ceilings, kitchen sink leak, missing lock hardware on rear entrance door.

Contract rent: $507. Damage award: $101 per month for seven months, total of $707.

Caraballo v. Moreno, No. 2005-CVG-00330 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Sept.

7, 2005) R.C. 5321.04 violation: kitchen sink drain problem, requiring tenant to cart water between bathroom and kitchen and to wash dishes in bucket; bedroom electrical outlet and overhead light not functional, requiring use of extension cord from kitchen to bedroom; balcony unsafe. Contract rent: $475 per month. Damage award: $200 per month for 6.5 months, total of $1,300.

Harris v. Winston , No. 2004 -CVG-28660 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar.

16, 2005) R.C. 5321.04 violation: Collapsed ceiling (preventing use ofproperty for four months);

water leak from upstairs toilet into the unit; kitchen sink back-up; windows not closing properly; insufficient heat due to furnace defects; and lead paint. Contract rent: $555 per month. Damage award (collapsed ceiling): $555 per month for four months, total of $2,220. Damage award (other defective conditions): $305 per month for 11 months plus five days, total of $3,405.

Damron v. McKnight, No. 03-CVG-524 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Dec. 12, 2003) R.C. 5321.04 violation (manufactured home): windows (most did not open and algae growth around some); kitchen (sink leak and weakened flooring); living room (mold on carpet due to window leaks, and bare electrical wires hanging from ceiling);

bedrooms (ceiling sinking, with evidence of mold growth, and floors weak and sinking); bathroom (flooring extremely weak and rotting due to bathtub and sink leaks, and hole to exterior in closet). Contract rent: $200 per month. Damage award (settlement): total of $800 for 6.5 months (based on ren t reduction from $1,300 to $500).

Pierson-Kalabins v. Shaw , No. CV2003 -12-3449, 2003 WL 25780617 (Ohio C.P., Butler Cty., Feb. 11, 2003) R.C. 5321.04 violation: lack of reasonable heat; “live wires coming from the ceiling in the kitchen, an unfinished wal l between the kitchen and the living room, several unfinished electrical outlets throughout the home, unfinished insulation in the back patio, several windows that were unframed, and broken plumbing fixtures in the bathroom;” and “roof was unfinished, and,as a result, creatures were crawling up there all of the time.” Contract rent: $1,000 per month. Damage award (lack of reasonable heat): $200 per month for four months, total of $800. Damage award (other defective conditions): $300 per month for 16 months, total of $4,800.

Parker v. Hiles, No. 02-CVH-362 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Jan. 6, 2003) R.C. 5321.04 violation: “the rental unit ... was in poor condition resulting in condemnation.” City inspector’s report found broken windows, exposed wiring, holes in walls and floors, bathroom sink unable to drain, bathtub sinking, basement full of trash and solid waste, electric or kerosene heaters due to lack of gas, and more.

Contract rent: $300 per month. Damage award: $225 per month for seven months, total of $1,575.

Williams v. Wright, No. 2000-CVG-01040 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Aug. 15, 2001) R.C. 5321.04 violation: roach infestation. Contract rent: $375 per month. Damage award: $225 per month for five months, total of $1,125.

Woods v. Coleman, No. 01-CVG-444 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., Aug. 6, 2001) R.C. 5321.04 violation: no running water or working toilet. Contract rent: $350 per
month. Damage award: $300 per month for six months, total of $1,800.

Malcom v. Tate , No. 99 -CVH-21689 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hou s. Div., June 29, 2001) R.C. 5321.04 violation: “electrical problems, backyard containing garbage and debris, window to the back door missing, lack of working stove, stains on the living room carpet, hole in the hallway floor, mildew on the bathroom wall and ceiling, part of the baseboard in the bathroom missing, several heat register covers missing, no locks on bedroom windows, doorknob on side entry door missing, doorknob on second bedroom door missing, inoperative ceiling fan in dining room, gutter on si de of house missing, and living room windows which would not open.” Contract rent: $369 per month.

Damage award: $100 per month for five months, total of $500.

413 Hansgen v. Seigel, No. 00-CVR-40822 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., May 29, 2001) R.C. 5321.04 viol ation: no water or heat except for portable heater in one room.

Contract rent: not specified. Damage award: the entire contract rent per month for five months.

O’Connor v. Maynard, No. 00-CVG-01 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., Apr. 17, 2001) R.C. 5321.04 violation: defective stove and refrigerator. Contract rent: $275 per
month. Damage award: $50 per month for six months, total of $300.

Tolliver v. Warren, No. 99-CVG-13123 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 28, 1999) R.C. 5321.04 violation: “bathroom floor was cracked, the toilet was not secured to floor, the tub was worn, the sink was not properly affixed to wall, and the ceiling had a hole in it,” defective electrical outlets, a heating vent was not secured to the wall, a screen door was missing a bottom panel, and defect in window seat drawers. Contract rent: $300 per month. Damage award: $75 per month for 12 months, total of $900.

Roundtree v. Valentine, No. 98-CVR-23790 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Jan. 28, 1999) R.C. 5321.04 violation:47 unspecified code violations and, during the last two months, no hot water and raw sewage/standing water in the basement. Contract rent: $650 per
month. Damage award: $325 per month for four months and $650 per month for the last two months.

Helm v. Peoples, No. 94-CVG-144 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., Apr. 17, 1996) R.C. 5321.04 violation: leaking water lines, improper venting of heaters, lack of heat in portion of house, and improper drainage from house. Contract rent: $250 per month.

Damage award: total of $1,400 for seven months.

Weingarden v. Eagle Ridge Condos., 71 Ohio Misc.2d 7, 653 N.E.2d 759 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., 1995) R.C. 5321.04 violation: substantial leaking into carpeted basement, whenever rain or snow melting, caused soaked car pet and mildew and, thus, rendered basement (one - third of the apartment) uninhabitable. Contract rent: $750 per month. Damage award:

$250 per month for three months, total of $750.

Crooks v. Kingery, No. 94-CVG-495 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, June 14, 1995) R.C. 5321.04 violation: “bathroom floor was noticeably wet and unsanitary, the shower failed to drain properly, the kitchen and bathroom sinks failed to drain properly, and a sewage pipe outside the house discharged sewage onto the ground resulting in an unsanitary condition and offensive smells.” Contract rent: $235 per month. Damage award: total of $770 for seven months.
