Myricks v. Reed , No. 93 -CVG-05580 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 14, 1994) R.C. 5321.04 violation: “garage in need of repair, electric box deficiency, weak porch and interior floors, missing storm windows and wall cracks,” and “insufficient heat from faulty furnace for period of at least four months for the 1992 -1993 season.” Contract rent: $350 per month. Damage award: $75 per month for nine months, total of $675.

Walton v. Washington, No. 93-CVG-07401 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec.

7, 1993) R.C. 5321.04 violation: “electrical, structural, and plumbing violations,” and “infestation of roaches and mice.” Contract ren t: $475 per month. Damage award:

$225 per month for five months, total of $1,125.

Bierman v. Maharidge, No. CVG-920275 (Ohio Mun., Vermilion, Mar. 31, 1993) R.C. 5321.04 violation: no heat to second floor bedrooms, no running water, basement constantly flooded, and back door and porch defective. Contract rent: $200 per month.

Damage award: $100 per month for 17 months, total of $1,700.

Moody v. Brady, No. 92-CVF-549 (Ohio Mun., Clinton Cty., Mar. 8, 1993) R.C. 5321.04 violation: defective furnace that made premises unsafe from January 15 to March 31, 1991, and caused the tenants, who paid rent through March 31, 1991, to vacate on January 31, 1991. Contract rent: $325 per month. Damage award: $325 per month for 2.5 months, total of $822.50.

Corrin v. Gamboa, No. 91-CVG-7366 (Ohio Mun., Canton, June 17, 1992) R.C. 5321.04 violation: “roach problem,” lack of timely repair of latch on back door, denial of access to basement area, failure to repair stove, failure to repair blower on furnace, and number of structural defects. Contract rent: not specified. Damage award:

total of $1,175 for 18 months.

Zeidman v. McCarty, No. R92-CVG-1036 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., June 16, 1992) R.C. 5321.04 violation: “numerous electrical and plumbing problems,” and “water softener failed.” Contract rent: $455 per month. Damage award: $50 per month for eight months, total of $400.

Kenwood Courts Apts. v. Williams , No. K90 -CVG-1043 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty.,
Oct. 25, 1991) R.C. 5321.04 violation: repeated flooding, repeated sewage back-ups into the kitchen sink, plumbing problems, lack of screens and air conditioning, and many other miscellaneous problems. Contract rent: not specified. Damage award: $100 per 415 month, total of $2,784.

Miller v. Gage , No. 90 -CVG-31902 (Ohio Mun., C leveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 24, 1991) R.C. 5321.04 violation: hanging electrical fixture in bedroom, broken smoke detector, infestation of roaches, leaking skylight, bullet holes in windows, flaking paint in dining room requiring family to eat in living roo m, opening in wall that led to unsecured vacant part of structure, littered front yard, unfinished paint application, and dry cold- water tap. Contract rent: $240 per month. Damage award: $75 per month for eight months, total of $600.

Peterson v. Walker, No. 89-CVG-27148 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., July 23, 1990) R.C. 5321.04 violation: drain or waste lines to lavatory deteriorated and leaking, exterior foundation in need of pointing, interior ceilings with loose or deteriorated materials, and interior wall cracks. Contract rent: $385 per month. Damage award:

$100 per month for 26 months, total of $2,600.

Cummings v. Cortese, No. R88-CVE-2278 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Dec. 4, 1989) R.C. 5321.04 violation (plaintiff Clark): not specified injudgment entry, but alleged in the complaint to be leaking roof, defective or missing downspouts, leaking shower, defective plumbing, ceilings and walls in disrepair, defective electrical wiring, missing covers for electrical outlets, back door that cannot be unlocked, and lack of handrail on porch. Contract rent (plaintiff Clark): not specified in judgment entry but alleged in the complaint to be $158 per month. Damage award (plaintiff Clark): $25 per month for 16 months, total of $400.

Guaranteed Mgmt.Co. v. DeMaio, No. 88-CVG-01120 (Ohio Mun., Willoughby, Nov.

1, 1989) R.C. 5321.04 violation: exposed electrical wiring, absence of smoke detectors, non - functioning lock on the front door, sagging bathroom floor, leaking roof, and leaking
pipes. Contract rent: $500 per month. Damage award: $125 per month for nine months, total of $1,125.

Gibson v. Suiter, No. 88-CVH-2, 1989 WL 572089 (Ohio Mun., Lawrence Cty., July 18, 1989) R.C. 5321.04 violation: low water pressure, absence of water (five times), discoloration of water (once a month), weak floors, loose vent covers, holes in wall and in bedroom door and in bedroom floor, insufficient cooling by air conditioner, cold air around windows, defective bathroom door, potholes in driveway, loose metal stripping on edge of carpet, leaking toilet, leaking front door, and leaking kitchen sink. Contract rent: $185 per month. Damage award (low water pressure): $35 per month for five months, total of $165. Damage award (all violations): $60 per month for three months, total of $180.

Mentor Lagoons, Inc. v. Ventra , No. 88 -CVF-875 (Ohio Mun., Mentor, Nov. 23, 1988), aff’d, No. 89-L-14-026, 1990 WL 128270 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 7, 1990) R.C. 5321.04 violation: defective air conditioners, which compelled the tenantand her fourteen-month-old baby to sleep at a relative’s house. Contract rent: $400 per month.

Damage award: $200 per month for two months, total of $400.

James v. Campbell, No. 87-SC-I-2 (Ohio Mun., Ironton, July 13, 1987) R.C. 5321.04 violation: bathro om facilities unusable due to malfunctioning sewage hook-ups. Contract rent: not specified. Damage award: total of $495 for three months.

Cardwell v. Carmichael , No. 84 -CVG-21049 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Nov. 18, 1985), rev’d in part on other g rounds, No. 51293, 1986 WL 13694 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 4, 1986) R.C. 5321.04 violation: attic and basement inaccessible due to landlord’s failure to remove trash from those areas. Contract rent: $300 per month. Damage award: $100 per month for 2.5 months, total of $250.

Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Bragg , No. 86 -CVG-169 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Sept. 11, 1986), aff’d in part, rev’d in part on other grounds, No. 1379, 1987 WL 16079 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Aug. 27, 1987) R.C. 5321.04 violation: inoperable ai r conditioner. Contract rent: not specified.

Damage award: total of $180 for 10 months.

Hale v. Pease, No. 84-CIV-G-1128 (Ohio Mun., Painesville, Sept. 13, 1984) R.C. 5321.04 violation: broken water lines. Contract rent: $200 per month. Damage award: $100 per month for eight months, total of $800.

Tolle v. Foote, No. 5-84-CVG-30 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Apr. 26, 1984) R.C. 5321.04 violation: failure to provide reasonable amounts of running water at all
times. Contract rent: $198 per month. Damage award: $2 per day for 425 days, total of $850.

Howard v. Simon , No. 82 -CIG-10516 (Ohio Mun., Shaker Heights, Sept. 10, 1983), aff’d as modified, 18 Ohio App.3d 14, 480 N.E.2d 99 (8th Dist. 1984) R.C. 5321.04 violation: defective air conditioning system.Contract rent: not specified.

Damage award: $90 per month for four months, total of $360.

Donley v. Davis, No. 80-LT-20 (Ohio Mun., Youngstown, June 10, 1981) R.C. 5321.04 violation: roach infestation, repeated incidents of electrical short circuits 417 and blown fuses in the kitchen, and plumbing problems. Contract rent: not specified in court’s order but stated elsewhere to be $150 per month. Damage award: $25 per month for six months, total of $150.

Faunce v. Miller, No. 80-CVI-1271 (Ohio Mun., Fremont, Apr. 2, 1981) R.C. 5321.04 violation: improperly working stove and refrigerator, which caused tenant to install tenant’s stove and refrigerator, and insufficient hot water and inadequate water supply. Contract rent: not specified. Damage award: $100 for landlord’s failure to provide a properly working stove and refrigerator, and $10 per day for six days of the water problem, total of $160.

Gill v. Lewis Zipkin & Assocs. , No. 78-LLT-88 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, Oct.

31, 1979) R.C. 5321.04 violation: broken windows and lack of weather-tight windows. Contract rent: $295 per month. Damage award: monthly amounts of $43.50, $29, $58, $45.03, total of $233.53.

Pagoda Co. v. Smith , No. 76 -CVG-1548 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, Feb. 25, 1977), aff’d, No. 37936, 1979 Ohio App. LEXIS 11691 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 8,
1979) (Defendant Smith) R.C. 5321.04 violation: front door latch jammed intermittently, windows could not be opened or closed fully, hole in kitchen wall which provided access to the apartment for mice, improperly fitted connection for garbage disposal causing water seepage, and common area problems ( i.e., failure to repair properly the building’s front entrance locks and basement door locks and basement windows, exposed and loose wiring and faulty plumbing and deteriorated ceiling in the basement of the building, defective stair and rail on building’s fire escape, inappropriate and insufficient number of refuse receptacles for the building, and failure to repair brick walkway in the building’s courtyard). Contract rent: $300 per month. Damage award: total of $350 for the period late 1974 to October 18, 1976.

Pagoda Co. v. Smith , No. 76 -CVG-1548 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland Heights, Feb. 25, 1977), aff’d, No. 37936, 1979 Ohio App. LEXIS 11691 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 8,
1979) (Defendant Beres) R.C. 5321.04 violation: various windows could not be opened, bathtub drain did not operate properly, kitchen floor peeling, hole in the kitchen wall which provided access to the apartment for mice, improperly mounted or uncovered electrical outlets in the walls, bathroom wall tiles that intermittently fell out, and common area problems (i.e., those discussed in Pagoda Co. v. Smith (Defendant Smith), above). Contract rent:

$300 per month. Damage award: total of $645 for the period late 1974 to October 18, 1976.

##### d. Special Damages

In R.C. 5321.04 contract claims, a tenant may recover any special damages that naturally and directly result from the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A) violation.

Vanderpool v. Waddell, No. 1822, 1987 WL 19853, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Nov.

12, 1987); Allen v. Lee , 43 Ohio App.3d 31, 33, 538 N.E.2d 1073 (8th Dist. 1987) (general contract principles apply). Cf. F. Enters., Inc. v. Kentucky Fried Chicken, 47 Ohio St.2d 154, 351 N.E. 2d 121 (1976) (commercial tenancy); Brown v. Spitzer Chevrolet Co., 181 Ohio App.3d 642, 910 N.E.2d 490 (5th Dist. 2009) (same).

Special damages include consequential damages if they result from the violation in the usual course of events or were otherwise foreseeable. Foard v. Severcool, 22 Ohio Law Abs. 650, 1 Ohio Op. 492 (Ohio C.P., Lucas Cty., 1933). See also Detling
v. Edelbrock, 671 S.W.2d 265 (Mo. 1984) (implied warranty of habitability);Mease v.

Fox, 200 N.W.2d 791 (Iowa 1972) (same). Cf. R & H Trucking, Inc. v. Occidental Fire & Cas. Co. , 2 Ohio App.3d 269, 441 N.E.2d 816 (10th Dist. 1981) (insurance contract); Markowitz & Co. v. Toledo Metro. Hous. Auth., 608 F.2d 699 (6th Cir. 1979) (breach of agreement to lease, applying Ohio law).

One example of these consequential damages is damage to the tenant’s personal property caused by the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A) violation. E.g., Marsh v. Lansing Gardens Apts., No. 07-BE-32, 2008 WL 2633467 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., July 2, 2008);

Stiffler v. Canterb ury Runn Apts., No. 19308, 2002 WL 31243541 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 4, 2002); Johnson v. Rahim , No. 2019 -CVG-008127 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 21, 2020); Leak v. Hale , No. 2006 -CVG-33367 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 10, 2008); Williams v. Wright , No. 2000 -CVG- 01040 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Aug. 15, 2001); Kenwood Courts Apts. v. Williams, No.

K90-CVG-1043 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 25, 1991); Glyco v. Schultz, 35 Ohio
Misc. 25, 289 N.E.2d 919 (Ohio Mun., Sylvania, 1972). See also Hall v. Myers , 49 Ohio Law Abs. 345, 77 N.E.2d 81 (Ohio Mun., Piqua, 1947) (breach of lease agreement); Miller v. Christian , 958 F.2d 1234 (3d Cir. 1992) (implied warranty of habitability); Darmetko v. Boston Hous. Auth., 378 Mass. 758, 393 N.E.2d 395 (1979) (same); Kipsborough Realty Corp. v. Goldbetter, 81 Misc.2d 1054, 367 N.Y.S.2d 916
(Civ. Ct. 1975) (same).

For a discussion on the measure of damages for damage to personal property, see Section XVIII.C.2, below.

A second of these consequentialdamages is moving expenses, when the landlord’s 419 R.C. 5321.04(A) violation has caused the tenant to move. E.g., Marsh v. Lansing Gardens Apts., No. 07-BE-32, 2008 WL 2633467 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., July 2, 2008);

Stiffler v. Canterbury Run n Apts., No. 19308 , 2002 WL 31243541 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 4, 2002); Allen v. Lee , 43 Ohio App.3d 31, 538 N.E.2d 1073 (8th Dist.

1987); Johnson v. Rahim, No. 2019-CVG-008127 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Jan. 21, 2020); Kenwood Courts Apts. v. Williams , No. K90-CVG-1043 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 25, 1991).

A tenant may not recover special damages for moving expenses if the moving costs are not the result of the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A) violation. Harris v. Winston, No.

2004-CVG-28660 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 16, 2005).

A third example of these consequential damages is the tenant’s cost in transferring and reconnecting phone service and cable television service, when the landlord’s R.C.

5321.04(A) violation has caused the tenant to move. E.g., Marsh v. Lansing Gardens Apts., No. 07-BE-32, 2008 WL 2633467 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., July 2, 2008).

A fourth example of these consequential damages is excessive utility bills resulting from the defective condition at the premises. E.g., Householder v. Edgar, No. 06 - CVG-687 (Ohio County Ct., Tuscarawas, Mar. 6, 2007); Waterford Glen LLC v.

Dowell, No. 05-CVG-1055 (Ohio Mun., Crawford Cty., Apr. 7, 2006) (R.C. Chapter 3733 (now R.C. Chapter 4781) action). See also Fair v. Negley , 257 Pa. Super. 50, 390 A.2d 240 (1978).

A fifth example of these consequential damages is the tenant’s cost in remedying the R.C. 5321.04(A) violation. E.g., Householder v. Edgar, No. 06-CVG-687 (Ohio County Ct., Tuscarawas, Mar. 6, 2007) (furnace repair); Harris v. Winston, No. 2004- CVG-28660 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 16, 2005) (furnace repair);

McClellan v. Hagerty, No. 04-CV-008 (Ohio C.P., Meigs Cty., May 27, 2004) (heating fuel).

Special damages in an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim also include incidental damages caused by the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A) violation, such as the cost of temporary housing, restaurant meals, or water purchases. E.g., Benson v. Oaks Mobile Home Park, No. 1156, 1983 WL 6313 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 15, 1983) (R.C.

Chapter 3733 (now R.C. Chapter 4781) action, cost of living expenses at the another residence, as a result of sewer problem);Waterford Glen LLC v. Dowell, No. 05-CVG- 1055 (Ohio Mun., Crawford Cty., Apr. 7, 2006) (R.C. Chapter 3733 (now R.C. Chapter
4781) action, cost of restaurant meals and purchase of water); Woods v. Coleman, No.

01-CVG-444 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., Aug. 6, 2001) (cost of purchasing water and using toilet facilities at other residences, as a result of no running water or working toilet); Beard v. Smith, No. 91-CVG-34340 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr.

24, 1991) (cost of alternative housing, as a result of no heat). See also Mease v. Fox, 200 N.W.2d 791, 797 (Iowa 1972) (implied warranty of habitability).

However, a court may deny incidental damages for the cost of temporary housing, if the court makes the tenant whole by relieving the tenant of any rental obligation to the landlord for the relevant period. Harris v. Winston, No. 2004-CVG-28660 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 16, 2005).

Special damages in an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim may not include damages for inconvenience and discomfort caused by the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A) violation.

The traditional view in Ohio is that a tenant may not recover contract damages for inconvenience and discomfort caused by the landlord’s breach of a covenant to repair.303 Cochran v. Widra, 35 Ohio Law Abs. 608, 41 N.E.2d 875 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., 1931). But cf. Deitsch v. Music Co., 6 Ohio Misc.2d 6, 453 N.E.2d 1302 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1983).

Likewise, special damages in an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim may not include damages for personal injury caused by the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A) violation. The traditional view in Ohio is that a tenant may not recover contract damages for personal injury caused by the landlord’s breach of a covenant to repair. See Cooper v. Roose, 151 Ohio St. 316, 85 N.E.2d 545 (1949). See also Auburn v. Amoco Oil Co., 106 Ill.

App.3d 60, 435 N.E.2d 780 (1982) (implied warranty of habitability). But see Boudreau v. Gen. Elec. Co., 2 Haw. App. 10, 625 P.2d 384 (1981).

However, it is insignificant whether special damages in an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim include damages for inconvenience and discomfort or damages for personal
injury. A tenant can recover these dama ges under an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se
claim. See Section IX.B.4.a, below.

##### e. Punitive Damages, Unavailable

In an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim, a tenant may not recover punitive damages.

Ohio law does not permit the recovery of punitive damages in an ac tion for breach of 303 Under the implied warranty of habitability, a few jurisdictions permit contract damages for inconvenience and discomfort caused by the landlord’s breach of the warranty. E.g., Hilder v.
St. Peter, 144 Vt. 150, 478 A.2d 202 (1984); Teller v. McCoy, 162 W. Va. 367, 253 S.E.2d 114 (1978). But see Cazares v. Ortiz , 109 Cal. App.3d Supp. 23, 168 Cal. Rptr. 108 (1980);

Quevedo v. Braga, 72 Cal. App.3d Supp. 1, 140 Cal. Rptr. 143 (1977).

421 contract, even if the alleged breach was willful, wanton, and malicious. Ketcham v.

Miller, 104 Ohio St. 372, 136 N.E. 145 (1922); Stern Enters. v. Plaza Theaters I & II, Inc., 105 Ohio App.3d 601, 664 N.E.2d 981 (11th Dist. 1995);R & H Trucking, Inc. v.

Occidental Fire & Cas. Co. , 2 Ohio App.3d 269, 441 N.E.2d 816 (10th Dist. 1981);

Schell v. Kaiser-Frazer Sales Corp., 28 Ohio App.2d 16, 274 N.E.2d 315 (2nd Dist.

1971). See also R.C. 2315.21(A)(1).

For a discussion on a tenant claim for punitive damages under an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim, see Section IX.B.4.b, below.

#### 9. Attorney’s Fees, Unavailable

In an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim, a tenant may not recover attorney’s fees.

Iwenofu v. Consol. Mgmt., Inc., 49 Ohio App.3d 33, 550 N.E.2d 505 (8th Dist. 1988).

See Section XIX.A, below.

The only R.C. 5321.04 claim in which attorney’s fees are authorized is an R.C.

5321.04(B) claim, which is based on a landlord’s unlawful entry upon the premises.

Iwenofu, 49 Ohio App. 3d at 34-35. For a discussion on those claims, see Section X.B, below.

#### 10. Waiver Issues

“The Landlords and Tenants Act prohibits ... any agreement that purports to waive the tenant’s right or ability to bring an action for damages. R.C. 5321.13; 5321.06.” Miller v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St. 3d 222, 225, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989).

A tenant’s knowledge of the defective condition before the tenant moves into the premises does not constitute a waiver of the tenant’s right to bring an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim. Brown v. August, No. 01-CA-0024, 2002 WL 22877 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 9, 2002); Pierson-Kalabins v. Shaw , No. CV2003 -12-3449, 2003 WL 25780617 (Ohio C.P., Butler Cty., Feb. 11, 2003).

A tenant’s occupancy of defective premises over a lengthy period of time does not constitute a waiver of the tenant’s right to bring an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim.Miller
v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 224 -26, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989). See also Brown v.

August, No. 01 -CA-0024, 2002 WL 22877 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 9, 2002) (applying Miller); Kiser v. Stevens, No. 92-CA-54, 1993 WL 35668 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Feb. 1, 1993) (applying Miller); Pierson-Kalabins v. Shaw , No. CV2003 -12- 3449, 2003 WL 25780617 (Ohio C.P., Butler Cty., Feb. 11, 2003) (applying Miller).

A tenant’s non-use of the R.C. 5321.07 rent deposit remedy does not constitute a waiver of the tenant’s right to bring an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim, irrespective of whether the tenant pays or fails to pay the rent to the landlord. Miller v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 224-26, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989). See also Boice v. Emshoff, No. 13- 98-23, 1998 WL 833686 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Dec. 3, 1998) (applyingMiller); Reiter
v. Fultz, No. 92 -CA-1555, 1993 WL 235522 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 30, 1993) (applying Miller). See also Arndt v. P & M Ltd., 163 Ohio App.3d 179, 837 N.E. 398, ¶¶ 23,24 (11th Dist. 2005) (construing R.C. 3733.10,.12 (now R.C. 4781.38,.41) – manufactured home park provisions that are similar to R.C. 5321.04,.07).

#### 11. Proof Issues – Tenant Testimony


##### a. Introduction

In support of an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim, the tenant may testify as to: (1) the existence of the defective condition, (2) the dates on which the defective condition has existed, (3) any tenant notifications to the landlord of the def ective condition and the landlord’s responses to those notifications, (4) exculpable conduct of the tenant in respect to the cause of the defective condition, (5) the effect of the defective condition on the tenant’s use and enjoyment of the premises, (6)the tenant’s opinion on the rental value of the premises in their defective condition (see Section IX.A.11.b, below), and
(7) relevant facts and opinion testimony concerning any special damages.

##### b. Owner Opinion Rule

A tenant, by virtue of the tenancy, is competent to give opinion testimony as to the rental value of the premises in their defective condition. Smith v. Padgett , 32 Ohio
St. 3d 344, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987). See also Miller v. Ritchie, 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989); Gammarino v. Smith, No. C-060636, 2007 WL 2284573 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Aug. 10, 2007) (applying Padgett); Boice v. Emshoff, No. 13 -98-23, 1998 WL 833686 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Dec. 3, 1998) (applyingPadgett); Weingarden
v. Eagle Ridge Condos ., 71 Ohio Misc.2d 7, 653 N.E.2d 759 (Ohio Mun., Toledo,
Hous. Div., 1995) (applying Padgett).

This tenant opinion testimony is admissible under the owner -opinion rule.

Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d at 347-48.

The weight that the judge or jury will give this tenant opinion testimony is within the sound discretion of the trier of fact. Miller, 45 Ohio St.3d at 227 n.7; Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d at 348. See also Pleasant v. Greenfield Station Apts., No. 15282, 1995 WL 614359 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 20, 1995) (applying Miller).

423 Three court de cisions illustrate this discretion, when their court findings are compared to the tenant opinion testimony on the monthly rental value of rental premises in their defective condition: Parker v. Hiles, No. 02-CVH-362 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Jan. 6, 2003) (court finding – $75; tenant opinion testimony – $25);

Grayson v. Farmer, No. 2002-CVG-14619 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb.

12, 2002) (court finding – $250; tenant opinion testimony – $250 to $300); Tolliver v.

Warren, No. 99-CVG-13123 (Ohio Mun.,Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 28, 1999) (court finding – $225; tenant opinion testimony – $175).

Under the owner opinion rule, a tenant may “testify as to the underlying factors which serve as the basis of his opinion in an effort to lend credibility to histestimony.” Weir v. Miller, No. 82-04-0044, 1983 WL 4336, at *2 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Apr. 13, 1983).

The owner opinion rule is subject to the hearsay rule. Id., 1983 WL 4336, at *2;

Hawkins v. Hawkins, 11 Ohio Misc.2d 18, 464 N.E.2d 199 (Ohio C.P.,Clermont Cty., 1984). Consequently, a tenant may not support the tenant’s opinion testimony by testifying as to rental amounts listed in advertisements for other premises.Hawkins, 11 Ohio Misc.2d at 19.

Under the owner opinion rule, a tenant is also competent to give opinion testimony as to the value of the tenant’s personal property, when the tenant’s special damages include damage to that property.McCain v. Brewer, No. 2014-CA-8, 2015 WL 302829 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 23, 2015);Smith v. Seitz, No. 97-CA-515, 1998 WL 393880 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 9, 1998); Bynum v. Huffman, No. 57730, 1990 WL 210225 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 20, 1990); Burfitt v. Stewart, Nos. 55530,55531, 1989 WL 113079 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 28, 1989). See generally Tokles & Son, Inc. v.

Midwestern Indem. Co. , 65 Ohio St.3d 621, 605 N.E.2d 936 (1992); Bishop v. East Ohio Gas Co., 143 Ohio St. 541, 56 N.E.2d 164 (1944).

Because the owner opinion rule is subject to the hearsay rule, a tenant may not simply identify a list that purports to establish the value of the personal property.Smith
v. Seitz, No. 97-CA-515, 1998 WL 393880 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 9, 1998).

Like rental v alue testimony, the owner opinion testimony is some evidence, but not conclusive evidence, of the value of the personal property.Bynum v. Huffman, No.

57730, 1990 WL 210225, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 20, 1990); Burfitt v.

Stewart, Nos. 55530,55531, 1989 WL 113079, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 28, 1989).

##### c. Rental Value Testimony Not Required

In an R.C. 5321.04 contract claim, a tenant may meet the tenant’s burden of proof for general damages without any testimony on the rental value of the premises in their defective condition. Miller v. Ritchie , 45 Ohio St.3d 222, 227, 543 N.E.2d 1265 (1989).

The tenant may meet the tenant’s burden of proof by simply proving the extent of the defective condition(s) at the premises. Id. Thereupon, “the finderof fact (judge or
jury) must determine the monetary amount by which the defects and the reduction in use have lessened the value of the leasehold.” Id. See also Gammarino v. Smith, No.

C-060636, 2007 WL 2284573 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Aug. 10, 2007) (applying Miller);

Heck v. Whitehurst Co., No. L-03-1134, 2004 WL 1857131 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug.

20, 2004) (applyingMiller); Pleasant v. Greenfield Station Apts., No. 15282, 1995 WL 614359 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 20, 1995) (applying Miller); Surrey Park Assocs.
v. Kuhner, No. 89-L-14-120, 1990 WL 222990 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 28, 1990) (applying Miller).

##### d. Use of a Diary

A tenant’s testimony may be strengthened through the use of a diary. While testifying, a tenant may be permitted to use a writt en diary to refresh the tenant’s
recollection. Ohio R. Evid. 612. E.g., Polk v. Gray , No. 36896, 1978 Ohio App.

LEXIS 9771, at *10-11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 9, 1978).

The extent to which a witness may use a written memorandum to help recollect past events is within the sound discretion of the trial court. Polk, 1978 Ohio App.

LEXIS 9771, at *10 -11; Sroufe v. Guttman , 65 Ohio App. 556, 32 N.E.2d 444 (1st
Dist. 1940).

##### e. Use of Photographs

A tenant’s testimony may be strengthened through the use of p hotographs of the
premises. Photographs of the defective condition often relate the identity and severity of the defect far more vividly than oral testimony.

Therefore, where appropriate, the tenant or another person should take photographs of the defecti ve condition for the purpose of introducing them into
evidence. The tenant can testify that the p hotographs are fair and accurate images of the scene at a particular time even if another person took the p hotographs, and such testimony is an adequate foundation for their admission into evidence.Hardy v. Miller, 425
No. 98-CA-13, 1999 WL 1256347 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999); Solomon v.

Mote, 38 Ohio Law Abs. 169, 49 N.E.2d 703 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1942).

The acceptance of photographic evidence rest s within the sound discretion of the trial court. Glover v. Massey, Nos. 56351,56802, 1990 WL 1328, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 11, 1990).

For the landlord’s analogous right to introduce into evidence relevant photographs in support of an R.C. 5321.05(C) claim, see Section XVIII.F.5, below.

##### f. Corroboration Via a Viewing of the Premises

A tenant may request a view of the premises by the jury or judge. R.C. 2315.02.

“If the court is of the opinion that it is proper for the jurors to have a view of property which is the subject of litigation, or of a place where a material fact occurred, it may order them to be conducted in a body under the charge of an officer to such property or place, which shall be shown to them by a person appointed by the cour t for that purpose.” Id.

“The allowance of a view of the property or scene at issue is within the sound discretion of the trial court.” Hardy v. Miller, No. 98-CA-13, 1999 WL 1256347, at *7 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999).

#### 12. Proof Issues – R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) Code Violation


##### a. No Requirement for Code Violation Citation

The issuance of a code violation citation by the applicable governmental entity is not a required element of an R.C, 5321.04(A)(1) contract claim (or negligence per se claim). Wilcox v. Tabler, No. 26530, 2013 WL 1324937 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr.

3, 2013). The tenant is simply required to prove that, during the relevant time, the defective condition existed and it violated the applicable code. Id.

##### b. Code Inspector Testimony

While not required (see Section IX.A.12.a, above), when a tenant alleges an R.C.

5321.04(A)(1) violation, the best proof of the violation is the testimony of a governmental employee who is responsible for enforcing the code and who has inspected the premises (“code inspector”).

The code inspector may testify as to: (1) the conditions that the code inspector observed at the premises, (2) whether the code inspector cited the landlord for any violation of the applicable codes as a result of those conditions, and (3) whether the code inspector notified the landlord of those conditions and/or of any related citation.

E.g., Surrey Park Assocs. v. Kuhner, No. 89-L-14-120, 1990 WL 222990 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 28, 1990).

The code inspector usually will reduce to writing the code inspector’s findings and citations (“inspector’s report”). A stipulation or an admission as to the authenticity of the inspector’s report and the landlord’s receipt of the inspector’s report may be sufficient to introduce it into evidence.

Otherwise, to introduce the inspector’s report into evidence, the testimony of the governmental employee who is responsible for maintaining such reports ( i.e., the custodian of the records) may be necessary. The custodian of the records can testify as to the identity of the inspector’s report and the manner in which such reports are maintained in the normal course of business. Based on that testimony, the inspector’s report should be admissible. See Ohio R. Evid. 803(6),(8).

The inspector’s report may c ontain hearsay information, such as assertions concerning the premises that the code inspector did not observe. For example, it may contain assertions by the landlord or tenant concerning conditions at the premises on a date prior to the code inspection.

However, such assertions in the inspector’s report are not always hearsay. For example, if the assertion is a declaration by the tenant and the tenant’s testimony at trial is consistent with that prior statement, and if such testimony has been impeached or attacked as a recent fabrication, the prior statement of the tenant recorded in the inspector’s report is not hearsay. Ohio R. Evid. 801(D)(1). See Polk v. Gray , No.

36896, 1978 Ohio App. LEXIS 9771, at *5 -6 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 9, 1978);

Miller v. Piqua Transfer & Storage Co., 57 Ohio Law Abs. 325, 92 N.E.2d 452 (Ohio C.P., Butler Cty., 1950). And, if the assertion recorded in the inspector’s report is a declaration by the landlord, it may be an admission by a party-opponent and therefore not hearsay. Ohio R. Evid. 801(D)(2). See Polk, 1978 Ohio App. LEXIS 9771, at *8,9.

When the inspector’s report contains some information that is inadmissible and some information that is admissible, only the inadmissible portion of the report is subject to an objection and motion to exclude. See Yuin v. Hilton, 165 Ohio St. 164, 134 N.E.2d 719 (1956).

In these circumstances, an objection to the introduction of the whole report is insufficient and should be overruled. Id.; Carson v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 156 Ohio St.

104, 100 N.E.2d 197 (1951). The objecting party is obligated to identify and object to 427 the portion of the document that constitutes hearsay or is otherwise inadmissible and, thereupon, move for the exclusion of only the incompetent evidence. Yuin v. Hilton, 165 Ohio St. 164, 134 N.E.2d 719 (1956). The court may then excise the inadmissible portion of the document and admit the excised document into evidence or, in lieu thereof, permit an appropriate witness to recite the admissible and relevant portion of the document. Sanders v. Hairston , 51 Ohio App.3d 63, 554 N.E.2d 9 51 (8th Dist.

1988).

### B. R.C. 5321.04 Negligence Per Se Claim


#### 1. Overview

A landlord’s failure to maintain or repair the premises, in violation of an R.C.

5321.04(A) obligation (see Section VII.C, above), constitutes negligence per se. 304 Sikora v. Wenzel , 88 Ohi o St.3d 493, 727 N.E.2d 1277 (2000); Shroades v. Rental Homes, Inc., 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981). E.g., Robinson v. Bates, 112 Ohio St.3d 17, 857 N.E.2d 1195, ¶ 23 (2006) (R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) and R.C.

5321.04(A)(2) violations); Mann v, Northgat e Invs. L.L.C. , 138 Ohio St.3d, 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶ 33 (2014) (R.C. 5321.04(A)(3) violations); Sabolik v. HGG Chestnut Lake Ltd. P’ship, 180 Ohio App.3d 576, 906 N.E.2d 488, ¶ 13 (8th Dist. 2009) (R.C.

5321.04(A)(4) violations); McKenzie v. FSF Beacon Hil l Assocs., LLC , No. 15AP - 1194, 2006 WL 3775857, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 26, 2006 ) (R.C.

5321.04(A)(4) violations); Trammell v. McDonald, No. 4-04-15, 2004 WL 2026414, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 13, 2004) (R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) violations).

R.C. 5321.04(A) serves as a legislative declaration of the standard of care of a reasonably prudent person applicable in negligence actions. Sikora, 88 Ohio St. 3d at 496; Sabolik v. HGG Chestnut Lake Ltd. P’ship , 180 Ohio App.3d 576, 906 N.E.2d 488 (8t h Dist. 2009); Allstate Ins. Co. v. Henry , No. CA2006 -07-168, 2007 WL 1531494 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 29, 2007);Morgan v. Mamone, No. 87612, 2006 WL 3804527 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 21, 2006).

The R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim is comprised of three elements: (1) the 304 Likewise, a park owner’s failure to maintain or repair the premises, in violation of an R.C.

4781.38 (former R.C. 3733.10) obligation (see Chapter XXI, below), constitutes negligence per
se. Lewis v. Wall, No. 2007-A-0048, 2008 WL 2633473 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 3, 2008);

Sterling v. Stevens, No. 02-JE-47, 2003 WL 22232008 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Sept. 25, 2003).

landlord’s violation of an R.C. 5321.04(A) obligation, (2) the tenant’s injury that was proximately caused by the violation, and (3) prior notice to the landlord of the
violation. Sikora v. Wenzel, 88 Ohio St.3d 493, 727 N.E.2d 1277 (2000); Shroades v.

Rental Homes, Inc., 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981).

The R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim does not extend to violations of administrative rules. Chambers v. St. Mary’s Sch., 82 Ohio St.3d 563, 697 N.E.2d 198 (1998). Nonetheless, it still applies to R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) code violations. Sikora v.

Wenzel, 88 Ohio St.3d 493, 727 N.E.2d 1277 (2000).

The R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim also may not apply where the type of injury suffered does not fall within the type of injuries that the R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) code provision is intended to avoid. Smith v. Heslop, Inc. , No. 27465, 2015 WL 5043864 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 26, 2015) (R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim did not apply because the window building code provision was intended to protect tenants from fire hazards, not from breaking glass during horseplay).

The elements of the R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim and related matters are discussed in Sections IX.B.2 -7, below. The relationship between the R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim and a common law negligence claim is discussed in Section IX.C.3, below.

#### 2. Notice


##### a. General Rule

The notice requirement for the R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim is three-fold:

the landlord received notice of the defective conditions of the rental premises, the landlord knew of the defective conditions of the rental premises, or the landlord should have known of the defective conditions of the rental premises. Shroades v. Rental Homes, Inc., 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 25-26, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981), as clarified or modified by Sikora v. Wenzel , 88 Ohio St.3d 493, 498, 727 N.E.2d 1277 (2000). See also DeFreeze v. Ly nch, No. 107090, 2019 WL 993334 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 28, 2019); Wiles v. Miller, 3 N.E.3d 226, 2013-Ohio-3625 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2013);

Holloway v. McDaniel, No. 91599, 2009 WL 2263608 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 30, 2009); Avila v. Gerdenich Realty Co., No. L-07-1098, 2007 WL 4216972 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 30, 2007); Lilly v. Bradford Inv. Co. , No. 06AP -1227, 2007 WL 1640850 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 7, 2007);Boyd v. Hariani, No. 22500, 2005 WL 2087824 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 31, 2 005); Smith v. Finn, No. L-04-1244, 2005 429 WL 736669 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 31, 2005);Walker v. Barnett Mgmt., Inc., Nos.

84188,84210, 2004 WL 2847810 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2004).

Two significant aspects of this notice requirement are clear fr om the face of the
requirement. First, if the landlord has personal knowledge of the defective condition or the landlord received notice of the defective condition from a source other than the tenant (e.g., a code inspector), the tenant is not required to provide the landlord with additional notice. Second, the notice requirement does not require notice in writing.

##### b. Constructive Notice

The notice requirement is met if the landlord had constructive notice of the defective condition. Sikora v. Wenzel , 88 Oh io St.3d 493, 495, 727 N.E.2d 1277 (2000). See also Webster v. Shaw, 63 N.E.3d 677, 2016 WL 1484, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2016); Wiles v. Miller, 3 N.E.3d 226, 2013-Ohio-3625, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2013); DeVore v. Franklin Manor Apts., No. 98AP-779, 1999 WL 236040, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 22, 1999); McKenzie v. Marlowe, No. 70714, 1996 WL 715502, at *5 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 12, 1996); Bell v. Goldsmith , No. 67893, 1995 WL 396352, at *5 -6 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 6, 1995); Straughter v. Stark
Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CA-8696, 1992 WL 127098, at *2 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 1, 1992); Rice v. Reid, No. 3-91-34, 1992 WL 81424, at *1 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Apr.

23, 1992); Klump v. Douglas, No. C-910060, 1991 WL 278262, at *1 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 31, 1991); Parrish v. Fisco, No. 58524, 1991 WL 81484, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 16, 1991); Neff v. Knisely , No. 87AP-666, 1988 WL 125687, at *3 -4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 22, 1988);Kishman v. Lakeside Six Cottages, No. E-8324, 1983 WL 2339, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 30, 1983).305 “Constructive notice can be imputed when it appears that a condition ‘existed in such a manner that it could or should have been discovered, that i t existed for a sufficient length of time to have been discovered, and that if it had been discovered it would have created a reasonable apprehension of a potential danger or an invasion of private rights.’” Webster v. Shaw, 63 N.E.3d 677, 2016 WL 1484, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 305 Cf. Wallenstein v. Marsol Towers, No. 58822, 1991 WL 378192 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug.

8, 1991) (landlord charged with constructive notic e of dangerous condition of common area floors where the condition was caused by remodeling work for which landlord contracted);

Shetina v. Ohio Univ., 9 Ohio App.3d 240, 459 N.E.2d 587 (10th Dist. 1983) (state university charged with constructive notice of defective conditions in dormitory rooms, which would have been revealed by reasonable inspection, if made).

3rd Dist., 2016) (quoting Beebe v. City of Toledo, 168 Ohio St. 203, 151 N.E.2d 738, syllabus no. 2 (1958)).

“In order to show that a landlord had constructive notice, plaintiff [tenant] has the burden of showing that the defect in questi on must have existed for such a length of time that the landlord, by exercising reasonable care, should have discovered it.” DeVore v. Franklin Manor Apts., No. 98AP-779, 1999 WL 236040, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 22, 1999). Accord Person-Thomas v. Quilliams-Noble Apts., L.L.C., 45 N.E.3d 654, 2015 -Ohio-4277, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2015); Coeurvie v.

McGonigal, No. 27095, 2014 WL 4824449, ¶ 14 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 30, 2014);

Albert v. Showe Mgmt. Corp. , Nos. 99 -CA-00016,00007, 1999 WL 547883, at *2 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 22, 1999).

If the constructive notice is based on the landlord’s (or landlord’s agent’s) visit to the dwelling unit, the tenant has the burden of showing that the defective condition existed at the time of the visit and was discernible upon a reasonable inspection at that
time. See, e.g. , Johnston v. Filson , No. CA2014 -04-007, 2014 WL 5422214, ¶¶ 12,13,25,26 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 27, 2014) (no discernible evidence of the tree’s decay before the top portion of the tree’s trunk broke off in a windstorm);Swader
v. Paramount Prop. Mgmt., No. CA2011-05-084, 2012 WL 1107722, ¶¶ 30-32 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Apr. 2, 2012) (no evidence on when floorboard became defective);

Vorhees v. Jovingo , No. 04 -CA-18, 2005 W L 2292796, ¶ 33 (Ohio App., 4th Dist.,
Sept. 19, 2005) (expert testimony that the dangerous condition of the gas pipe should have been apparent to the landlord when he tightened the pipe); Jones v. Greene Countrie Apts., No. 94-APE01-105, 1994 WL 265656, a t *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 14, 1994) (no evidence on how long step had been shaky); Abner v. Day , No.

CA92-09-173, 1993 WL 172347, at *1 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., May 24, 1993) (landlord’s “special knowledge” as a contractor did not provide him with constructive knowledge of the improper installation and thus dangerous condition of the cabinets, which he had not installed);North v. Golden Leaf Baptist Church, No. C-860310, 1987 WL 10038, at *1 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Apr. 22, 1987) (no evidence that “a defect in the ceiling was discernible upon reasonable inspection at any time before this incident”).

“Where the landlord does not cause or know of the unsafe condition, the issue is whether ‘the landlord by the exercise of reasonable care could have disc overed the condition and the unreasonable risk involved therein and could have made the condition safe.’” Malagisi v. Marble Cliff Crossing Apts. , No. 19AP-475, 2020 WL 431 1320712, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 19, 2020) (quotingMann v, Northgate Invs.

L.L.C., 138 Ohio St.3d, 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶ 21 (2014)).

R.C. 5321.04(A) does not generally set forth an affirmative duty on the landlord to inspect the premises. See Section VII.C.7, above. But “[i]nspections can serve to satisfy the landlord’s duty” relative to reasonable care. Malagisi v. Marble Cliff Crossing Apts., No. 19AP-475, 2020 WL 1320712, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar.

19, 2020). And, depending on the circumstances, these inspections “need not always be undertaken by professional inspectors.” Id.

Also, for alleged violations of the R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) duty to maintain elevators, where the landlord did not know of any mechanical issues before the incident, the tenant is required “to come forward with evidence showing why the elevator malfunctioned and that [the landlord] … should have known about the malfunction.

That showing require[s] expert evidence because ‘an elevator, like an automobile, is a complicated mechanical device.’” Mixon v. K&D Apt. Cmty. Owners and Managers,
No. 104338, 2017 WL 121770, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 12, 2017) (quoting Sabolik v. HGG Chestnut Lake Ltd. P’ship , 180 Ohio App.3d 576, 906 N.E.2d 488 , ¶ 13 (8th Dist. 2009) ). And elevators may malfunction for non -mechanical reasons such as overcrowding. Id., ¶ 9.

Also, “general knowledge of the possibility of a defect does not rise to the level of either actual or constructive notice.”Burnworth v. Harper, 109 Ohio App.3d 401, 406, 672 N.E.2d 241 (4th Dist. 1996) (fumes from space heater not constructive notice of defect producing carbon monoxide). Accord Avila v. Gerdenich Realty Co., No. L-07- 1098, 2007 WL 4216972 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 30, 2007) (manufacturer recommendation for regular maintenance check not constructive notice of garage door defect); Parks v. Menyhart Plumbing & Heating Supply Co. , No. 75424, 1999 WL 1129591 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999) (possibility that sandstone sidewalk could crack not constructive notice of latent defect in sidewalk); Craver v. Doogan,
No. 2003-CVG-00982, 2005 WL 4985282 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty., May 27, 2005) (water intrusion not constructive notice of mold).

However, a “landlord has a duty to inquire about the existence of a harmful condition on the property when the circumstances suggest that the condition may exist.” Walker v. Barnett Mgmt., Inc. , Nos. 84188,84210, 2004 WL 2847810, at *5 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2004).Accord Coeurvie v. McGonigal, No. 27095, 2014 WL 4824449, ¶ 14 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 30, 2014) In Walker, the court held that the landlord had a duty to inquire whether lead paint had been exposed because the landlord knew of flaking paint at the building, that the building existed prior to 1978, and of the common use of lead paint before 1978.306 In Coeurvie, the court held that the tenant’s notice to the landlord’s agent of mold in the closet “should have at least prompted some investigation in order to insure that the residence was in a habitable condition.” 2014 WL 4824449, ¶ 14.

Three court decisions illustrate the manner in which constructive notice is applied in R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claims: Kishman v. Lakeside Six Cottages, No. E- 83-24, 1983 WL 2339 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 30, 1983), Klump v. Douglas, No.

C-910060, 1991 WL 278262 (Ohi o App., 1st Dist., Dec. 31, 1991), and Brinson v.

Jenkins, No. C-960120, 1996 WL 626726 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Oct. 30, 1996).

Kishman concerned water leaks that caused a weak bathroom floor through which the tenant stepped. The court held that the landlo rd had constructive notice of the defective condition. The landlord’s employees, who had inspected the premises ten days before leasing it, knew (or should have known if they had inspected properly) of the defective condition.

Klump concerned a toy on which the tenant tripped in the common area. The court held that the landlord did not have constructive notice of the defective condition.

“[T]he record in this case does not contain evidence from which to infer that the landlord had knowledge of the toy on the lawn or that it was there long enough that he should have discovered the potential danger by reasonable inspection.” 1991 WL 278262, at *2.

Brinson concerned a portion of a living room ceiling falling on the tenant. The court held that the landlord did not have constructive notice of the defective condition.

Notice to the landlord of water leaking in the kitchen and bathroom ceilings did not provide constructive notice of the defective living room ceiling because “nothing in the record, testimonial or otherwise, indicates that whatever problem or defect caused the water to leak in the kitchen or bathroom would, could or did cause the living-room ceiling to fall. No water dripped from or collected on the living -room ceiling; the portion of the ceiling that fell was not wet or moist when it fell, and there is no indication that it had been wet or moist.” 1996 WL 626716, at *4.

306 Where the landlord lacked knowledge of the chipping paint (unlike Walker), a court held that the landlord did not have notice of the lead paint danger. Knox v. Clark, No. 03-CA-95, 2004 WL 1888283 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 23, 2004).

433

##### c. Specificity of the Notice – General Rule

The notice “need not specifically express the state of the defect with particularity beyond the scope of a layperson’s knowledge.” McKenzie v. Marlowe , No. 70714, 1996 WL 715502, at *5 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 12, 1996). Accord Wilhelm v.

Heritage Mgmt. Co., No. CA97-07-144, 1998 WL 24342, at *3 (Ohio App., 12th Dist.,
Jan. 26, 1998); Blakley v. Riley, No. 91AP-597, 1992 WL 1163, at *2 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 7, 1992).

However, the required specificity is not met where the notice informs the landlord in general terms that the premises are in defective condition but does not specify any particular defect. Palazzi v. Donohue , No. 92 -CA-005319, 1992 WL 368874 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 2, 1992).

Eight court decisions that illustrate the required specificity are: Robinson v.

Larchmont E. Apts., Inc. , No. L -12-1323, 2014 WL 4049874 (Ohi o App., 6th Dist.,
Aug. 15, 2014);Walker v. RLI Enters., Inc., No. 89325, 2007 WL 4442725 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 20, 2007); Scott v. Kirby , No. L -05-1287, 2006 WL 1047480 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 21, 2006); Wilhelm v. Heritage Mgmt. Co. , No. CA97 -07-144, 1998 WL 24342 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 26, 1998); Davis v. Burns, No. 9-97-39, 1997 WL 638276 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Oct. 10, 1997); Obermiller v. Tahya, No. 91- CA-005092, 1992 WL 41275 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 4, 1992); Blakley v. Riley,
No. 91AP-597, 1992 WL 1163 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 7, 1992); and White v.

Fuller, No. CA-84-15, 1984 WL 7584 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 27, 1984).

Robinson concerned damages resulting from a fire caused by defective wiring inside the wall of the utility room. The court held that the shock that the tenant received from the garbage disposal switch did not provide the landlord with constructivenotice of the faulty wiring in the building.

Walker concerned damages resulting from a fall in a pothole in a parking lot, which was filled with potholes. The court held that, because the landlord was on notice that the parking lot was filled with potho les, the landlord was on notice of the specific pothole.

Scott concerned damages resulting from a fall, when a guest started to step off a porch that chipped away and onto stairs that had no handrail. The court held that, based on a discussion with the tenant regarding the poor appearance of the steps, the landlord should have known that the steps and porch were in need of repair.

Wilhelm concerned damages resulting from a fire caused by an alleged defect in the electrical system. In reversing a summaryjudgment for the landlord, the court held that the tenants’ notices met the specificity requirement and that material issues of fact existed as to proximate cause. One tenant complained that “her electricity would blink on and off repeatedly.” 1998 WL 24342, at *3. A second tenant complained that she “had problems with an outlet near her balcony and that whenever she plugged in anything, sparks would fly out and if you turned on the light switch for the balcony, you would get a red flash of light.”Id. A third tenant complained about problems with a ceiling fan going off and “lighting would flicker off and on quite a bit.” Id.

Davis concerned damages resulting from a fall caused by a step breaking when the tenant stepped on it. The court held that the tenant’s notice met the specificity
requirement. The tenant notified the landlord that “the stairwell was defective as it swayed under a person’s movement.” 1997 WL 638276, at *2. “Although appellant [tenant] did not specifically complain about the con dition of the step to appellee [landlord], he did complain about the general condition of the stairwell.” Id.

Obermiller concerned damages resulting from a fall caused by a front step
dislodging. The court reversed a summary judgment for the landlord, since reasonable minds could reach different results as to whether the landlord had sufficient knowledge of the defect. The landlord knew that the bricks were deteriorating, there were several cracks and gaps in the caulking, and it was loose around the boards.

Blakley concerned damages resulting from a fire caused by over-amperaged fuses.

The court held that the tenant’s notice met the specificity requirement. The tenant notified the landlord of light fixtures in several rooms failing to work with regular ity and a first -floor outlet fire. These notices constituted notice to the landlord that the electrical system in the house was in a state of disrepair and thus notice of the over - amperaged fuses.

White concerned damages resulting from a carbon monoxide leak from a stove caused by a clogged chimney. The court held that the tenant’s notices did not meet the specificity requirement. The tenant’s notices and the landlord’s repairs of the stove, relative to a small fire and a burned -out motor fan, were not notice to the landlord of any other problem with the stove, the stove pipe leading to the chimney, or the chimney.

##### d. Specificity of the Notice – Lead Paint

The specificity required for the notice’s description of the defective condition has been a particularly important issue in lead-based paint cases.

435 In Winston Props. v. Sanders , 57 Ohio App.3d 28, 565 N.E.2d 1280 (1st Dist.

1989), the landlord did not know that the rental premises contained lead paint. The court held that the tenant’s notice to the landlord about peeling paint and cracked plaster was not notice of the existence of lead-based paint. On similar facts, other courts have followed the decision in Winston Props. E.g., Patterson v. Ahmed, No. L- 09-1222, 2010 WL 3449257 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept. 3, 2010); Lowery v. Ondrus,
No. L-08-1100, 2009 WL 50125 (Ohio App., 6 th Dist., Jan. 9, 2009); Murphy v. Leo Baur Realty, Inc., No. 63756, 1993 WL 425353 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 21, 1993);

Rice v. Reid, No. 3-91-34, 1992 WL 81424 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Apr. 23, 1992).

However, a landlord may have constructive notice of a lead-based paint hazard at the premises if the landlord has notice of peeling or chipping paint at the premises and the landlord is aware of the possibility of lead-based paint at the premises. See Webster
v. Shaw, 63 N.E.3d 677, 2016 WL 1484 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2016); Richardson v.

Boes, 179 Ohio App.3d 418, 902 N.E.2d 77 (6th Dist. 2008); Harris v. Tigner , 108 Ohio App.3d 152, 670 N.E.2d 304 (8th Dist. 1995); Walker v. Barnett Mgmt., Inc. ,
Nos. 84188,84210, 2004 WL 2847810 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2004); Bell v.

Goldsmith, No. 67893, 1995 WL 396352 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 6, 1995);

Straughter v. Stark Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CA-8696, 1992 WL 127098 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 1, 1992).

If a landlord is not on notice of peeling or chipping paint, the landlord may not be on notice of a lead-based paint hazard at the premises. See Knox v. Clark, No. 03-CA- 95, 2004 WL 1888283 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 23, 2004).

##### e. Rule for the R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) Duty to Maintain,

Latent Defects Prior to November 1998, the appellate courts applied the Shroades notice to R.C.

5321.04(A)(4)307 cases the same as they applied it to other R.C. 5321.04(A) cases.E.g., Patton v. Harper, No. 95-CA-20, 1996 WL 96007 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 4, 1996) (carbon monoxide emanating from heating system as a result of blockage in chimney);

Burnworth v. Harper, 109 Ohio App.3d 401, 667 N.E.2d 987 (4th Dist. 1996) (same);

Lockhart v. Mayfield , No. 14990, 1991 WL 184833 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 18, 307 R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) requires a landlord to “maintain in good and safe working order and condition all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances, and elevators, supplied or required to be supplied by him.”
1991) (defect in chimney);Murray v. Bargo, No. CA86-11-080, 1987 WL 15440 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Aug. 10, 1987) (defect in fireplace wall); White v. Fuller, No. CA- 84-15, 1984 WL 7584 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 27, 1984) (carbon monoxide emanating from heating stove as a result of a clogged chimney).

Since November 1998, the Second Appellate District has modified the Shroades (now Shroades/Sikora) notice for R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) cases. Lansdale v. Dursch, No.

16858, 1998 WL 769852 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 6, 1998) (carbon monoxide emanating from the furnace). See also Hammaker v. Maxson, No. 2003-CA-38, 2004 WL 869381 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 23, 2004) (grounding wire in stove not fastened properly); Smith v. Ohio Edison, Inc. , No. 98-CA-37, 1999 WL 6444 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 8, 1999) (defect in electrical wiring).

In R.C. 5321.04(A)(4) cases, the Second Appellate District generally does not require actual or constructive notice to the lan dlord of the defective condition. Id.

Instead, it requires actual or constructive notice to the landlord of the improper maintenance of the appliance or fixture (“Lansdale notice rule”). Id.308 The Lansdale notice rule is premised on the difference between a duty to maintain and a duty to repair. Id. A duty to maintain requires a landlord to take preventative action before a defective condition occurs, 309 whereas a duty to repair requires a landlord to remedy an existing defective condition. Id.

The notice required by the Lansdale notice rule “may be demonstrated by, inter alia, evidence regarding the procedures necessary to maintain the particular appliance;

any inspection, testing, or lack of the same, of the appliance for defects; or the landlord’s schedule for regular maintenance or lack thereof.” Lansdale, 1998 WL 769852, at *4.

The Second Appellate District does not apply theLansdale notice rule if the latent defect “would not be ... ordinarily discoverable by an inspection ordered by the landlord.” Hammaker v. Maxson , No. 2003 -CA-38, 2004 WL 869381, ¶ 40 (Ohio 308 Cf. Chambers v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 78354, 2001 WL 1034661 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 6, 2001) (applying the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur, held that water pipe in the wall would not have burst if landlord had exercised ordinary care to keep the pipe in good repair).

309 This duty to maintain and thereby prevent a defective condition is similar to the common law duty to inspect periodically and repair the common area of the premises. See Section IX.C.1, below.

437 App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 23, 2004).

Consistent with this limitation and in a decision that predates it, the Sixth Appellate District has held that the Lansdale notice rule, if valid at all, sh ould be limited to defective conditions that are “readily available for inspection by a landlord.” Abbott
v. Haight Props., Inc., No. L-98-1413, 2000 WL 491731, at *5 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Apr. 28, 2000). If an inspection of the fixture or appliance would require the landlord “to tear open the wall and the sound board,” it does not meet this readily available for inspection standard. Id. (electrical wiring in the wall was not readily available for inspection). See also Robinson v. Larchmont E. Apts., Inc., No. L-12-1323, 2014 WL 4049874, ¶¶ 19,20 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 15, 2014) (follows Abbott).

The Sixth Appellate District also has held that the Lansdale notice rule, if valid at all, should not apply to a defective condition that has existed from the time of construction, since it is “not a defect that developed due to a lack of preventative action.” Abbott, 2000 WL 491731, at *5 The Third Appellate District Court has followed the Lansdale notice rule.

Trammell v. McDonald, No. 4-04-15, 2004 WL 2026414 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept.

13, 2004).

The Ninth Appellate District has rejected the Lansdale notice rule altogether and continues to require notice of the defective condition to the landlord in R.C.

5321.04(A)(4) cases. Beere v. Timber Top Apts., No. 20843, 2002 WL 971896 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 1, 2002) (2 -1 decision); Robinson v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. ,
No. 20405, 2001 WL 866275 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 1, 2001).

##### f. Rule for the R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) Duty to Comply With

Applicable Codes The Shroades/Sikora notice applies to R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) 310 cases. Sikora v.

Wenzel, 88 Ohio St.3d 493, 495, 727 N.E.2d 1277 (2000). E.g., Stancil v. K.S.B. Inv.

& Mgmt. Co., 62 Ohio App.3d 765, 577 N.E.2d 452 (8th Dist. 1991).

In R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) cases, the notice is not required to state that the defective condition is a code violation. Martin v. Edgewater Props., No. 92-CA-005307, 1992 WL 357321 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 25, 1992).

Also, if the relevant code provision requires the landlord to instal l a particular 310 R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) requ ires a landlord to “comply with the requirements of all applicable building, housing, health, and safety codes that materially affect health and safety.” fixture at the premises ( e.g., a smoke detector) and the landlord fails to do so, the landlord has constructive notice of the code requirement and thus constructive notice of the defective condition (i.e., the code violation). Burdick v. Nevel, No. 98AP-697, 1999 WL 235435 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 20, 1999).

##### g. Social Guest of Tenant

The Shroades/Sikora notice applies to the R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim of a tenant’s social guest. E.g., Patton v. Harper, No. 95-CA-20, 1996 WL 96007 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 4, 1996). See also Primes v. Milbry , No. 18236, 1997 WL 760698, at *2 n.2 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 19, 1997) (acknowledges the Patton holding, but does not address the issue because it resolves the case on other grounds).

For a discussion on the landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A) obligations to a tenant’s social guests, see Section VII.C.1.b, above.

#### 3. Proximate Cause

“[I]n addition to negligence per se, proximate cause for the injuries sustained must be established.” Shroades v. Rental Homes, Inc. , 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 25, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981).

“‘Proximate causation’ is described as ‘some reasonable connection between the act or omission of the defendant and the damage the plaintiff has suffered.’” Stevens
v. Aaron Rental Props., Inc., No. 2009-CA-131, 2010 WL 3639339, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Sept. 15, 2010). The act or omission need not be the initial or sole cause of the injury. Id.

“‘The proximate cause of an event is that which in a natural and continuous sequence, unbroken by any new, independent cause, produces that event and without which that event would not have occurred.’” Sabolik v. HGG Lake Ltd. P’ship, 180 Ohio App.3d 576, 583, 906 N.E.2d 488 (8th Dist. 2009) (quoting Atkins v. Indus.

Comm’n, 143 Ohio St. 113, 117, 53 N.E.2d 1018 (1944)).

“There may be more than one proximate c ause of an injury. When the negligent act or failure to act continues and combines to produce the injury, both causes may be proximate to the injury.” Stevens, 2010 WL 3639339, ¶ 24 (citations omitted).

“The issue of proximate cause is one of fact.”Mounts v. Ravotti, No. 07-MA-182, 2008 WL 4415819, ¶ 38 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Sept. 26, 2008). Accord Scott v. Kirby,
No. L-05-1287, 2006 WL 1047480, ¶ 33 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 21, 2006).

Appellate court decisions finding a genuine issue of fact on pro ximate cause and, 439 therefore, reversing a summary judgment for the landlord include:
(1) Stevens v. Aaron Rental Props., Inc. , No. 2009 -CA-131, 2010 WL 3639339 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Sept. 15, 2010) (tenant fell on stairway without handrail);
(2) Lyle v. PK Mgmt., LLC, No. 5 -09-38, 2010 WL 1948360 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., May 17, 2010) (tenant fell in hallway);
(3) Morgan v. Mamone , No. 87612, 2006 WL 3804527 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 21, 2006) (tenant fell on ice patch in common area);
(4) Scott v. Kirby, No. L-05-1287, 2006 WL 1047480 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Apr. 21, 2006) (guest fell on porch steps, without handrail).

Appellate court decisions finding that the tenant failed to establish the required proximate cause include:
(1) Bailey v. Ward, No. 8-16-03, 2016 WL 5873893 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist.,
Oct. 3, 2016) (insufficient evidence – mostly speculation and conjecture
– to support gutter run-off theory as cause for snow and ice on stepsand thereupon tenant’s fall);
(2) Arnett v. Mong, 65 N.E.3d 72, 2016-Ohio-2893 (Ohio App., 12th Dist.,
2016) (evidence failed to establishwhether the guest slipped on a natural or unnatural accumulation of snow or ice);
(3) Dardy v. Thompson, No. 2013-G-3157, 2014 WL 2883278 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 23, 2014) (failed to establish that any violation of R.C.

5321.04(A) was the proximate cause of the fire because the fire inspectors were unable to determine the cause of the fire);
(4) Sabolik v. HGG Lake Ltd. P’ship , 180 Ohio App.3d 576, 906 N.E.2d 488 (8th Dist. 2009) (tenant scalded by hot water in bathtu b due to tenant’s actions– tenant accidentally turned the hot water to its highest setting, when he tried to turn the hot water off with his foot, while having a seizure);
(5) Bogner v. Titleist Club, LLC, No. WD-06-039, 2006 WL 3825228 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 29, 2006) (evidence did not establish a causal connection between the medical symptoms and the alleged exposure to mold);
(6) Mahmoud v. Dennis , No. L -04-1183, 2005 WL 1661966 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 2005) (insufficient evidence – only speculation and conjecture – as to the cause of the guest’s fall on the steps);
(7) Smalley v. Pauly , No. L -04-1106, 2004 WL 3200933 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 17, 2004) (no evidence that the lack of a handrail caused tenant’s fall on the steps);
(8) Turner v. Tiemeyer, No. CA95 -08-053, 1996 WL 56040 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 12, 1996) (no evidence that the tenant’s fall was due to the defective condition of the steps);
(9) Lilly v. Yee , No. 94 -APE06-830, 1995 WL 127886 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 23, 1995) (no evidence that the bursting of the pipe was due to any failure to maintain the steam heating system);
(10) Brady v. Koehnke , No. C -930240, 1994 WL 481758 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept. 7, 1994) (no evidence that the tenant’s fall from the front porch was due to code violations regarding the steps and handrail);
(11) Juresa v. Radan , No. 64951, 1994 WL 173654 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 5, 1994) (the tenant’s fall down the steps was due to the tenant’s negligence, not the lack of a handrail);
(12) Riley v. FDMA Props. , No. 92 -CA-22, 1992 WL 316356 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 7, 1992) (no evidence that the tenant’s fall down the steps was due to code violations regarding the bottom step);
(13) Harmon v. Schroeder , No. 4 -85-17, 1986 WL 14411 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Dec. 17, 1986) (no e vidence that the tenant’s physical injuries were due to a gas leak that the landlord repaired on the date that it was discovered).

#### 4. Types of Damages


##### a. Compensatory Damages

In an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim, the types of compensatory damages that a tenant may recover include:
(1) personal injury damages, Anderson v. Ceccardi, 6 Ohio St.3d 110, 114- 15, 451 N.E.2d 780 (1983); Allen v. Lee , 43 Ohio App.3d 31, 34, 538 N.E.2d 1073 (8th Dist. 1987); e.g., Sanders v. McGee , No. 13 -CV- 003983 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty., Apr. 3, 2014).
(2) personal property damages , e.g., Ohio Specialized Invs., Ltd. v.

Campbell, No. 2016 -CA-00176, 2017 WL 1231727 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Mar. 31, 2017) ; Sanders v. McGee , No. 13 -CV-003983 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty., Apr. 3, 2014); see also Cooper v. Feeney, 34 Ohio App.3d 282, 282-83, 518 N.E.2d 46 (12th Dist. 1986); and 441
(3) inconvenience and discomfort damages , e.g., Johnson v. Rahim , No.

2019-CVG-008127 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 21, 2020) (tenant awarded $3,850 ($50 per day) for the inconvenience and discomfort caused by the termination of water service to the dwelling unit); Nobles v. Johns , No. 91 -CVG-28244 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Mar. 25, 1992) (tenant awarded $2,200 ($100 per day) for the inconvenience of residing in a shelter for twenty -two days after her premises had been flooded and rendered unlivable by a rain-storm, as a result of a defect in the roof).

In an R.C. 5321.04 ne gligence per se claim, a tenant is not permitted to recover emotional distress damages. Allen v. Lee, 43 Ohio App.3d 31, 34, 538 N.E.2d 1073 (8th Dist. 1987). However, if the emotional distress is serious and disabling, the tenant may recover emotional d istress damages in a claim for intentional, reckless, or negligent infliction of serious emotional distress. See Section IX.E, below.

##### b. Punitive Damages

An R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim is a tort action within the purview of R.C.

2315.21. Sivit v. Vill. Green of Beachwood, L.P., 143 Ohio St.3d 168, 35 N.E.3d 508, ¶ 5 (2015).

Therefore, in an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim, a tenant may recover punitive damages. Sivit, 2015-Ohio-1193, ¶¶ 5-7.

Under R.C. 2315.21, this right to recover puniti ve damages is subject to three
requirements. First, “[a]n award of punitive damages requires that actual malice be proven ….” Sivit, 2015-Ohio-1193, ¶ 7. Second, “a plaintiff must be awarded some measure of compensatory damages to receive punitive damag es.” Niskanen v. Giant Eagle, Inc., 122 Ohio St.3d 486, 912 N.E.2d 595, ¶ 12 (2009). Third, the punitive damage award may not exceed two times the amount of the compensatory damage
award. Sivit, 2015-Ohio-1193, ¶ 6.

One court awarded punitive damages for a landlord’s willful and malicious failure to restore water service to the premises for 77 days. Johnson v. Rahim , No. 2019 - CVG-008127 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 21, 2020) ($7,103 compensatory damages; $5,562 punitive damages).

Another court awarded punitive damages for a landlord’s wil lful and reckless failure to provide heat and hot water to the premises. Monaco v. Schultz, No. 1998- CV-100425, 2001 WL 1677087 (Ohio C.P., Tuscarawas Cty., Jan. 30, 2001) ($5,000 compensatory damages; $5,000 punitive damages).

If a tenant is awarded punitive damages in an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se case, the tenant may also be entitled to an award of attorney’s fees under Ohio common law.

See Section XIX.B, below.

#### 5. Independent Contractor

When a landlord employs an independent contractor to make repairs in compliance with R.C. 5321.04(A), the landlord is liable to the tenant for any negligent acts of the independent contractor in the performance of those repairs. Strayer v. Lindeman, 68 Ohio St.2d 32, 427 N.E.2d 781 (1981). See also Wallenstein v. Marsol Towers , No.

58822, 1991 WL 378192 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 8, 1991);Simpson v. Delta Prop.

Mgmt., Inc., Nos. C -850562,850575, 1986 WL 7136 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 25, 1986).

“[N]otice [to the landlord] of the defect is not required to impute that liability to the landlord.” Kroeger v. East End Constr. Co. , No. 14080, 1994 WL 100698, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 2, 1994). Accord Wallace v. Golden Comb, Inc., 4 N.E.3d 445, 2013-Ohio-5320 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2013). But see Hodge v. Lackey, No. 93- T-4947, 1995 WL 378700 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., May 12, 1995).

The Strayer doctrine does not extend to an independent contractor’s repair of cable equipment if the cable company hired the independent contractor. Lee v. Babeli, No.

2000-CA-00371, 2001 WL 705558 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 29, 2001).

#### 6. Sale of Property, Seller’s Liability for Existing

Defective Conditions Generally, “outside an agreement or law establishing a relationshipto the contrary, the prior owners of a [rental] property are divested of all rights and obligations to the said property on the date the title to the property is transferred to the new owners.” Steele v. McNatt, 102 Ohio App.3d 558, 562, 657 N.E.2d 575 (8th Dist. 1995). Accord Wiles v. Miller , 3 N.E.3d 226, 2013 -Ohio-3625, ¶¶ 17 -23 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2010).

However, when a landlord sells rental property and, as of the time of the sale, the landlord has failed to remedy defective conditions at the property in violation of R.C.

5321.04(A), an issue that may arise is: for what period of time (if any), after the transfer of title, does the seller remain liable for injuries directly and proximately 443 caused by those conditions.311 One appellate court appli ed sections 16.1 and 16.3 of the Restatement of Property 2d, Landlord and Tenant to decide this issue. Robinson v. C & L Assocs., L.L.C., 188 Ohio App.3d 649, 936 N.E.2d 546 (2nd Dist. 2010).

It held that, under section 16.1(b), the seller remains liablefor the tenant’s personal injuries that were a direct and proximate cause of the seller’s failure, before the transfer of title, to meet its obligations under R.C. 5321.04(A). Id., ¶ 33. In support of this holding, it found: “The purpose of the Landlords and Tenants Act would be frustrated if a landlord could escape all potential liability for personal injuries directly and proximately caused by the landlord’s failure to fulfill its obligations under R.C.

5321.04 simply because the injuries occurred a fewdays after the landlord’s sale of the rental property.” Id., ¶ 34.

But “[a]s time passes and the transferee has had a reasonable opportunity to discover a defective condition, or it is reported by the tenant, the transferee landlord’s failure to make rep airs becomes an intervening cause that could or should have eliminated the hazard, creating a break in the chain of causation between the tenant’s injuries and the transferor landlord’s breach, relieving the transferor landlord of liability.” Id., ¶ 35.

In Robinson, five days after the sale of the rental property, the tenant suffered burns to her arms due to a defective stove door. Applying its statement of the relevant law, the court held the seller liable for these injuries.

#### 7. Defense Issues


##### a. Open and Obvious Doctrine

The open-and-obvious doctrine does not apply to R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se
claims. Mann v. Northgate Investors, L.L.C. , 138 Ohio St.3d 175, 5 N.E.3d 594, ¶¶ 24,33 (2014) (R.C. 5321.04(A)(3)); Robinson v. Bates , 112 Ohio St.3d 17, 857 N.E.2d 1195, ¶ 25 (2006) (R.C. 5321.04(A)(1)); Lang v. Holly Hill Motel, Inc. , 122 Ohio St.3d 120, 909 N.E.2d 120, ¶¶ 12 -15 (2009) (R.C. 5321.04(A), dicta). See also DeFreeze v. Lynch, No. 107090, 2019 WL 993334, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb.

28, 2019); Rawlings v. Springwoods Apts. of Columbus, Ltd. 125 N.E.3d 312, 2008 - 311 For a discussion on to whom the tenant has rental liabilities and for what periods, when the rental property is sold, see Section VII.F.10, above.

Ohio-4845, ¶ 41 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2018); Arnett v. Mong, 65 N.E.3d 72, 2016- Ohio-2893, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., 2016);McHugh v. Zaatar, No. 14-CA-010591, 2015 WL 7273177, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 20, 2015); Mounts v. Ravotti, No.

07-MA-182, 2008 WL 4415819, ¶ 22 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Sept. 26, 2008); Walker
v. RLI Enters., Inc. , No. 89325, 2007 WL 4442725, ¶ 53 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec.

20, 2007); Scott v. Kirby , No. L-05-1287, 2006 WL 1047480, ¶ 23 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 21, 2006); Crawford v. Wolfe, No. 01-CA-2811, 2002 WL 31521593, ¶ 56 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 26, 2002); Schoefield v. Beulah Road, Inc. , No. 98AP - 1475, 1999 WL 645273, at *8 (Ohio App.,10th Dist., Aug. 26, 1999). Cf. Base-Smith
v. Lautrec, Ltd. , No. CA2013-07-115, 2014 WL 408041, ¶¶ 20 -22 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 3, 2014) (R.C. 4781.38(A)(1)-(3) – manufactured home park provisions that are similar to R.C. 5321.04(A)(1)-(3)).

For a discussion on the open and obvious doctrine, see Section IX.C.2, below.

##### b. Comparative Negligence

The defense of comparative negligence may be asserted in an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim. Parker v. Vaughn, No. 16939, 1998 WL 639305, at *4 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Aug. 7, 1998); Hubbard v. Crutchfield, No. CA96-06-050, 1997 WL 162397, at *2 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Apr. 7, 1997); Guthrie v. Jamison , No. 94 - APE10-1560, 1995 WL 238425, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 20, 1995); Brazen
v. Brazzon, No. 50293, 1986 WL 3233, at *4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 13, 1986).

See also Burdick v. Nevel , No. 98AP-697, 1999 WL 235435, at *1 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 20, 1999); Martin v. Edgewater Props. , No. 92 -CA-005307, 1992 WL 357321, at *3-4 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 25, 1992).

##### c. Implied Assumption of Risk

The defense of implied assumption of risk is not available as an absolute bar to an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim. Anderson v. Ceccardi, 6 Ohio St.3d 110, 451 N.E.2d 780 (1983). See also Crawford v. Wolfe, No. 01-CA-2811, 2002 WL 31521593 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 26, 2002). Under the comparative negligence statute (R.C.

2315.19), the defense of implied assumption of risk is merged with the defense of contributory negligence. Id.

##### d. Primary Assumption of Risk

The defense of primary assumption of the risk is not available as a defense in an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim because the claim is based on the landlord’s 445 violation of a statutory duty (R.C. 5321.04(A)). Glover v. Massey, Nos. 56351,56802, 1990 WL 1328 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 11, 1990).

### C. Common Law Negligence, Premises

Liability

#### 1. Overview

“At early common law, a landlord generally was immune from tort liability for any injuries sustained by any person due to dangerous conditions on a leased-premises in the exclusive possession of a tenant, even if the dangerous condition existed at the commencement of the tenancy.” Shump v. First Cont’l-Robinwood Assocs., 71 Ohio St.3d 414, 417, 644 N.E.2d 291 (1994). See also Sabolik v. HGG Chestnut Lake Ltd.

P’ship, 180 Ohio App.3d 576, 906 N.E.2d 488, ¶ 11 (8th Dist. 2009).

But now, “[i]n point of fact, the exceptions have nearly swallowed up the general rule of landlord immunity.” Shump, 71 Ohio St.3d at 418.

“In order for a tenant to establis h a landlord’s negligence under common law premises liability, the plaintiff must show: (1) the existence of a duty, (2) a breach of that duty, and (3)an injury proximately resulting from the breach.” DeFreeze v. Lynch,
No. 107090, 2019 WL 993334 , ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 28, 2019). Accord Thatcher v. Lauffer Ravines, LLC, No. 11AP-851, 2013 WL 816428, ¶ 7 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 5, 2013) Lyle v. PK Mgmt., LLC , No. 5 -09-38, 2010 WL 1948360, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., May 17, 2010).

The legal duties that give rise to this common law negligence claim include the four that are summarized below.

First, “at common law, a ‘lessor [can] be held liable for the condition of premises if there were an agreement to repair ....’”Davis-Blunt v. Myatt, 134 Ohio App.3d 213, 215, 730 N.E.2d 1003 (1st Dist. 1999). Accord Shump v. First Cont’l -Robinwood Assocs., 71 Ohio St.3d 414, 418, 644 N.E.2d 291 (1994).

Second, “at common law, a ‘lessor [can] be held liable for the condition of premises if there were ... a violation of a duty imposed by statute.’” Myatt, 134 Ohio App.3d at 215. Accord Shump, 71 Ohio St.3d at 418.

Third, “at common law it has always been the rule that a landlord who undertakes and negligently makes repairs to the premises incurs liability for injuries to tenants or their invitees that are proximately caused by the negligent repair.” Myatt, 134 Ohio App.3d at 215. Accord Shump, 71 Ohio St.3d at 418. Cf. Winston v. Wolery, No. 96- CA-2442, 1997 WL 28535, at *2,3 (Ohio App., 4th D ist., Jan. 14, 1997) (landlord’s negligent design and construction of a deck).

Fourth, a “landlord owes a duty to maintain the premises under its control [ e.g., common areas] in a reasonably safe condition and to warn of unreasonably dangerous latent conditions of which the landlord has or should have knowledge.” Sauter v. One Lytle Place , No. C -040266, 2005 WL 627794, ¶ 7 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar. 18, 2005). Accord Shump, 71 Ohio St.3d at 418.
