This includes “a duty to periodically inspect and repair the common ways of the leased premises.” Davis v. Burns, No. 9- 97-39, 1997 WL 638276, at *2 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Oct. 10, 1997).

This common law negligence claim also includes a notice requirement. “Where a dangerous condition exists which has not beencreated by the premises owner or his or her agents, one injured on the premises must show that the owner had, or in the exercise of ordinary care should have had, notice of the hazard within sufficient time to remove the condition or to warn invitees about it.” Smith v. Finn, No. L-04-1244, 2005 WL 736669, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 31, 2005). Accord Waugh v. Lynch , No.

100432, 2014 WL 1340263, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 20, 2014); Maynard v.

Winters, No. 2012AP-05-0035, 2012 WL 6765613, ¶ 36 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec.

26, 2012); Odom v. Davis, No. 02-CA-43, 2003 WL 21458704, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 20, 2003); Davis v. Burns , No. 9 -97-39, 1997 WL 638276, at *2 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Oct. 10, 1997).

This notice requirement – the landlord knew or should have known – is the same as the notice requirement for an R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim (see Section IX.B.2, above). Maynard v. Winters, No. 2012AP-05-0035, 2012 WL 6765613, ¶ 25 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 26, 2012);Smith v. Finn, No. L-04-1244, 2005 WL 736669, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 31, 2005).

#### 2. Open and Obvious Doctrine

The open and obvious doctrine states that, “where a danger is open and obvious, a landowner owes no duty of care to individuals lawfully on the premises .” Lang v.

Holly Hill Motel, Inc. , 122 Ohio St.3d 120, 909 N.E.2d 120, ¶ 11 (2009). Accord Robinson v. Bates, 112 Ohio St.3d 17, 857 N.E.2d 1195, ¶ 21 (2006). This means “the owner has no duty to protect against dangers ‘which are known to the invitee or are so obvious and apparent to such invitee that he may reasonably be expected to discover them and protect himself against them.’”Howson v. Amorose, No. 00AP-8, 2000 WL 447 1753112, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 30, 2000) (quotingSidle v. Humphrey, 13 Ohio St.2d 45, 233 N.E.2d 589, syllabus no. 1 (1968)).

“The question of whether a danger is open and obvious is an objective one.” Canidate v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 101753, 2015 WL 1121444, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 12, 2015). “When reasonable minds could differ with respect to whether a danger is open and obvious, the obviousness of the risk is an issue for the jury to determine.” Id., ¶ 16.

“‘[T]he open and obvious doctrine remains viable in Ohio.’” Lang v. Holly Hill Motel, Inc., 122 Ohio St.3d 120, 909 N.E.2d 120, ¶ 11 (2009) (quoting Armstrong v.

Best Buy Co., 99 Ohio St.3d 79, 788 N.E.2d 1088, syllabus (2003)). Accord Robinson
v. Bates, 112 Ohio St.3d 17, 857 N.E.2d 1195, ¶ 21 (2006).

“[T]he open-and-obvious doctrine can excuse a defendant’s breach of a common law duty of care.”Lang v. Holly Hill Motel, Inc., 122 Ohio St.3d 120, 909 N.E.2d 120, ¶ 14 (2009).

As a result, “[t]he open and obvious doctrine ... applies to common law premises liability even when it involves claims against a landlord.” Packman v. Barton , No.

CA2009-03-009, 2009 WL 3165605, ¶ 33 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 5, 2009).

Accord Hatto v. McLaughlin, No. 109307, 2020 WL 3283624, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 18, 2020); Hanson v. Knoppe, No. 2019CAE-08-0046, 2019 WL 7290524, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 26, 2019); Rawlings v. Springwoods Apts. of Columbus, Ltd., 125 N.E.3d 312, 2018-Ohio-4845, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2018);

Levy v. Huener, No. L-17-1081, 2018 WL 388406, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 12, 2018); Callentine v. Mill Invs., LLC, No. 2017-AP-06, 2017 WL 5565502, ¶ 39 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 17, 2017); Mayhew v. Massey, 86 N.E.3d 758, 2017-Ohio-1016, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 7th D ist., 2017); Base-Smith v. Lautrec, Ltd., No. CA2013-07-115, 2014 WL 408041, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 3, 2014); Bierl v. BGZ Assocs. II, LLC, No. 9-12-42, 2013 WL 684707, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Feb. 25, 2013).

For a discussion on the inappl icability of the open and obvious doctrine to R.C.

5321.04 negligence per se claims, see Section IX.B.7.a, above.

#### 3. Supplement to R.C. 5321.04 Negligence Per Se

Claim Neither the R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim (see Section IX.B, above) nor R.C. Chapter 5321 supplants the tenant’s common law negligence claims against a
landlord. Sauter v. One Lytle Place, No. C-040266, 2005 WL 627794, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar. 18, 2005);Davis-Blunt v. Myatt, 134 Ohio App.3d 213, 215, 730 N.E.2d 1003 (1st Dist. 1999); Winston v. Wolery , No. 96 -CA-2442, 1997 WL 28535, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Jan. 14, 1997);Hoover v. Elliott, No. CA-9160, 1985 WL 4927, at *2 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 30, 1985).See also Shump v. First Cont’l-Robinwood Assocs., 71 Ohio St.3d 414, 644 N.E.2d 291 (1994).

Therefore, when a landlord fails to maintain the premises, the tenant may have both a common law negligence claim and an R.C. 5321.04 n egligence per se claim against the landlord.

### D. Common Law Negligence, Criminal

Activity of Third Party A third party’s intentional criminal acts at the premises may give rise to a negligence claim by the tenant against the landlord. The elements of this claim are:
(1) the landlord’s violation of an obligation to maintain the premises or to provide reasonable security ( e.g., statutory or code obligation, implied covenant of quiet enjoyment, express lease provision), (2) a criminal intrusion, a proximate c ause of which is the violation, (3) an injury to the tenant’s person or property as a result of the criminal intrusion, and (4) the reasonable foreseeability of the criminal intrusion and the criminal act directed at the tenant (i.e., the tenant’s injury),which may be based in part on the prior criminal activity in the neighborhood or building.

Under Ohio case law, there are at least three circumstances where a landlord’s violation of an obligation and a resulting criminal intrusion may give rise to a negligence claim by the tenant against the landlord.

The first is where the landlord violates the landlord’s obligation to provide in the common area reasonable security measures against criminal activity. See Section VII.C.8, above.

The second is where the l andlord violates an express provision of the rental agreement that requires the landlord to provide a specified security measure, such as a window lock. Blair v. Prop. Mgmt. Consultants, 40 Ohio App.3d 103, 531 N.E.2d 752 (1st Dist. 1987);Buncy v. Daniels, No. CA-6059, 1983 WL 6486 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 13, 1983). See also Cipollene v. Hoffmeier, No. C-060482, 2007 WL 2141578, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., July 27, 2007); Brown v. Campbell , Nos. 85698,85702, 2005 WL 1793519, ¶ 23 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 28, 2005).

The third is where the landlord violates the landlord’s obligation to exercise 449 reasonable care in providing the security services that the landlord does provide.Pamer
v. Pritchard Bros., 61 Ohio Misc.2d 150, 575 N.E.2d 900 (Ohio C.P., C lermont Cty., 1990).

Ohio courts generally have held that, except for the foregoing obligations, the common law does not impose an obligation on landlords to protect tenants against the criminal activity of a third party. Thomas v. Hart Realty, Inc., 17 Ohio App.3d 83, 477 N.E.2d 668 (1st Dist. 1984). See also Cowan v. Hidden Lake , No. 17053, 1995 WL 523349 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 6, 1995); Johnson v. Monroe Realty Co. , No.

67964, 1995 WL 322278 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 25, 1995); Meier v. Vistula Heritage Vill., 62 Ohio Misc.2d 632, 609 N.E.2d 1360 (Ohio C.P., Lucas Cty., 1992).

Likewise, Ohio courts generally have held that R.C. 5321.04(A) does not, per se, impose an obligation on landlords to protect tenants against the criminal activity of a third party. See Section VII.C.8, above.

Even when a landlord’s R.C. 5321.04(A) violation facilitates the criminal intrusion, courts seem reluctant to find that the R.C. 5321.04(A) violation is a proximate cause of the criminal act directed at the tenant. E.g., Runge v. Rosewater,
Nos. 47650,47651, 1984 WL 5072 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 14, 1984) (insufficient evidence on whether entry occurred through door with defective lock); Cherkiss v.

Thomas, No. L -83-416, 1984 WL 7829 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 13, 1984) (entry through window with defective latch); Emm v. Sheskey , No. 79AP -632, 1980 WL 353284 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 14, 1980) (entry through back door with defective lock). See also Brown v. Campbell, Nos. 85698,85702, 2005 WL 1793519 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 28, 2005) (entry through side door, which landlord promised to replace with more secure door).

“The foreseeability of criminal acts, that is, the defendant’s knowledge, is determined from the totality of the circumstances.” Brown v. Campbell, Nos. 85698, 85702, 2005 WL 1793519, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 28, 2005). However, the courts are mindful that “criminal behavior of third persons is not predictable to any degree of certainty.” Johnson v. Spectrum of Supportive Servs., No. 82267, 2003 WL 21982915, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 21, 2003). As a result, they require “(1) specific knowledge of a potential future problem based on past occurrences along with
(2) a substantial likelihood that such an incident would occur. ” Id., 2003 WL 21982915, ¶ 21. “[E]vidence that the [housing] complex is in a particularly crime ridden location” may be a relevant factor. Pieper v. Williams, No. L-05-1065, 2006 WL 964725, ¶ 55 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 14, 2006).

Courts seem relu ctant to find this requisite foreseeability. E.g., Brown v.

Campbell, Nos. 85698,85702, 2005 WL 1793519 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 28, 2005) (murder by non -tenant husband, who was subject to anti -stalking order, not foreseeable); Doe v. Beach House Dev. Co., 136 Ohio App.3d 573, 737 N.E.2d 141 (8th Dist. 2000) (notwithstanding a series of prior violent and aggressive acts of a third party (another tenant’s child) toward plaintiff and other residents of the complex, which included holding a knife on two young girls, the third party’s first degree felony sexual assault on plaintiff was not foreseeable); Sayles v. SB-92 Ltd. P’ship, 138 Ohio App.3d 476, 741 N.E.2d 613 (8th Dist. 2000) (it was not foreseeable that the tenant’s stalker would enter the building as a result of the exterior door’s defective lock and then start a fire at the tenant’s dwelling unit door).

### E. R.C. 5321.04 Emotional Distress Claims

When a landlord’s failure to remedy an R.C. 5321.04(A) violation causes the tenant to suffer emotional distr ess that is both serious and disabling, the tenant may have a claim against the landlord for intentional, reckless, or negligent infliction of serious emotional distress. E.g., Allen v. Lee, 43 Ohio App.3d 31, 538 N.E.2d 1073 (8th Dist. 1987); Sanders v. McGee, No. 13 -CV-003983 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty.,
Apr. 3, 2014); Woods v. Coleman, No. 01-CVG-444 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., Aug.

6, 2001); Kenwood Courts Apts. v. Williams, No. K90-CVG-1043 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 25, 1991). See also Birkenhead v.Coombs, 465 A.2d 244 (Vt. 1983);Simon
v. Solomon, 385 Mass. 91, 431 N.E.2d 556 (1982); Dorgan v. Loukas, 19 Mass. App.
Ct. 959, 473 N.E.2d 1151 (1985). See generally Yeager v. Local Union 20, Etc. , 6 Ohio St.3d 369, 453 N.E.2d 666 (1983) (intentional); Schultz v. Barberton Glass Co., 4 Ohio St.3d 131, 447 N.E.2d 109 (1983) (negligent).

In Allen, the tenant, a fifty -eight-year-old woman who suffered from multiple physical disabilities, recovered emotional distress damages in the amount of $5,000.

The R.C. 5321.04(A) violations included an infestation of rats and roaches, broken windows, large holes in interior walls, holes to the basement in the bathroom floor, a corroded bathtub that would not drain, and stained and rusty sinks.

In Sanders, the tenant recovered $30,000 for the physical and emotional pain and suffering of the tenant and her children. The R.C. 5321.04 violations included, for a period of seven months: (1) a severe bed bug infestation that caused physical injury and made the bedrooms unusa ble; and (2) a virtually unusable bathroom because of the strong urine scent and the rusted our condition of the tub.

451 In Woods, the tenant recovered emotional distress damages in the amount of $5,000. The R.C. 5321.04(A) violations were the absence of run ning water and the absence of a working toilet for a period of six months.

In Kenwood Courts Apts., the tenant recovered emotional distress damages in the amount of $2,000. The R.C. 5321.04(A) violations included repeated flooding of the premises, plumbing problems, lack of screens and air conditioning, as well as many miscellaneous problems.

The elements of a tenant’s claim against a landlord for serious emotional distress are: (1) the landlord either intended to cause the tenant to suffer emotional distress or knew or should have known that the landlord’s actions or omissions would cause the tenant to suffer emotional distress, (2) the landlord’s conduct was so extreme and outrageous as to go beyond the bounds of decency and could be considered as utterl y intolerable in a civilized community, (3) the landlord’s actions or omissions were the proximate cause of the tenant’s emotional distress, and (4) the emotional distress that the tenant suffered was serious. See Pyle v. Pyle, 11 Ohio App.3d 31, 34, 463 N.E.2d 98 (8th Dist. 1983).

Contemporaneous physical injury is not necessary to succeed on a serious emotional distress claim. Id.

Also, a tenant does “not need expert medical testimony to establish that the landlord’s conduct caused her emotional injury, if other evidence sufficiently demonstrate[s] its gravity and cause.”Allen v. Lee, 43 Ohio App.3d 31, 34, 538 N.E.2d 1073 (8th Dist. 1987). Accord Paugh v. Hanks , 6 Ohio St.3d 72, 451 N.E.2d 759 (1983); Uebelacker v. Cincom Sys., Inc. , 48 Ohio App.3d 2 68, 549 N.E.2d 1210 (1st
Dist. 1988); Foster v. McDevitt, 31 Ohio App.3d 237, 511 N.E.2d 403 (2nd Dist. 1986).

However, while expert medical testimony is not necessarily required, the Paugh “guarantee of genuineness” test requires the plaintiff to “present some evidence beyond the plaintiff’s own testimony that he or she experienced emotional distress due to the defendant’s actions.”Buckman-Pierson v. Brannon, 159 Ohio App.3d 12, 822 N.E.2d 830, ¶ 56 (2nd Dist. 2004).

The importance of medical testimony is illustrated by the decisions in Menges v.

Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. , No. 94AP-100068, 1995 WL 498820 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 12, 1995), andChilders v. Russell, Nos. 01-4142,4145, 2002 WL 31950741 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., Feb. 7, 2002). Theemotional distress claim in Menges concerned sewage back-ups into a manufactured home. The emotional distress claim in Childers concerned a wrongful termination of water service to a manufactured
home. In both cases, the claims were denied because of the absence of medical testimony relative to the alleged emotional distress.

For these claims, the tenant should also be mindful that “Ohio ... does not permit recovery for negligent infliction of emotional distress caused by witnessing the destruction of one ’s property.” Stechler v. Homyk , 127 Ohio App.3d 396, 399, 713 N.E.2d 44 (8th Dist. 1998) (neighbor tenant negligently left water running, which overflowed into tenant’s apartment).

### F. R.C. 5321.12 Claim

“Under R.C. 5321.12, a party has a statutory remedy for damages for the breach of any duty imposed under the Act [R.C. Chapter 5321].” Sherman v. Pearson, 110 Ohio App.3d 70, 74, 673 N.E.2d 643 (1st Dist. 1996).

However, with respect to R.C. 5321.04(A) violations, courts have not used R.C.

5321.12 as authority to provide tenants with any claim or remedy outside the R.C.

5321.04 contract claim (see Section IX.A, above) and the R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim (see Section IX.B, above).

### G. Receivership Claims


#### 1. Receiver to Abate Public Nuisance, R.C. 3767.41 –

General Rule When the condition of the premises is a public nuisance, R.C. 3767.41 permits the tenant to bring a civil action against the owner for an injunction that orders the owner to abate the nuisance. R.C. 3767.41(B),(C).

“A building may be the subject of a nuisance-abatement action under R.C. 3767.41 if it is a building or structure that is used or intended to be used for residential purposes.

R.C. 3767.41(A)(1).” Northeast Shores Dev. Corp. v. Euclid Beach L.P. , No. 05 - CVH-03844, 2005 WL 1131729, ¶ 7 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous . Div., Mar. 25, 2005). The R.C. 3767.41(A)(1) phrase “used or intended to be used” means that the property is occupied by residents or is suitable for residential purposes based upon the property’s design, construction, and location. Id., 2005 WL 113172 9, ¶ 17. R.C.

3767.41 does not apply to any building that is occupied by its owner and contains three or fewer dwelling units. R.C. 3767.41(A)(1).

A public nuisance means:

453 a building that is a menace to the public health, welfare or safety; that is structurally unsafe, unsanitary, or not provided with adequate safe egress; that constitutes a fire hazard, is otherwise dangerous to human life, or is otherwise no longer fit and habitable; or that, in relation to its existing use, constitutes a hazard to t he public health, welfare, or safety by reason of inadequate maintenance, dilapidation, obsolescence, or abandonment.

R.C. 3767.41(A)(2)(a).

Under this definition, most residential rental premises that have significant code violations are a public nuisance.

When the condition of the premises is a public nuisance and the owner previously has been afforded a reasonable opportunity to abate the nuisance and has failed to do so, R.C. 3767.41 also permits the tenant to bring a civil action against the owner for the appointment of a receiver, who will be responsible for abating the nuisance. R.C.

3767.41(C).

Prior to appointing a receiver in these circumstances, the court must first determine whether any mortgagee, lienholder, or other interested party associated with the building is willing and able to undertake the work and to furnish the materials necessary to abate the nuisance. Id.

If a receiver is appointed, the receiver will be given control over the property to make repairs, collect rents, make loans agai nst the property for rehabilitation, and otherwise manage the property. E.g., Winters v. Valley Homes Mut. Hous. Corp., No.

A0601318 (Ohio C.P., Hamilton Cty., Feb. 10, 2006) (nuisance – threatened termination of water, gas, electric, and trash services due to management nonpayment;

receiver, for 90 days, to collect rent and charges, pay utility bills, and maintain the property).

In deciding whether to file an R.C. 3767.41 action for receivership, the tenant’s considerations include whether the amount of e quity in the property is adequate to support a rehabilitation loan (if a loan is needed) and whether the rent generated by the property is adequate to support the costs of operating the property, including a reasonable management fee.

When a tenant decidesto file an R.C. 3767.41 action for receivership, before filing, a helpful next step for the tenant is to identify a qualified housing management company (or individual) which (who) is willing to be appointed as the receiver.

Civil actions under R.C. 3767.41 may be commenced in a court of common pleas, a municipal court, a housing or an environmental division of a municipal court, or a county court. R.C. 1901.18(A)(12), 1901.181(A), 1907.031.

When an R.C. 3767.41 action can be filed in a housing division o r an environmental division of a municipal court, the housing division or environmental division has exclusive jurisdiction over the action. Cent. Residents Council v. Lucas Metro Hous. Auth., No. CI-200902483 (Ohio C.P., Lucas Cty., Apr. 27, 2009), aff’d on other grounds, No. L -09-1137, 2010 WL 497340 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 12, 2010). Under R.C. 1901.181(A), “the housing or environmental division of a municipal court ... has exclusive jurisdiction within the territory of the court in any civil action as described in division (B)(1) of section 3767.41 of the Revised Code that relates to a public nuisance.”

#### 2. Receiver to Abate Public Nuisance, R.C. 3767.41 –

Subsidized Housing For subsidized rental housing as defined in R.C. 3767.41(A)(7), the standards and procedures for using the R.C. 3767.41 remedy vary from those used for other residential rental properties.

The R.C. 3767.41(A)(7) definition includes: Section 8 project -based assisted housing,312 RHS project -based assisted housing, Section 202 PRAC, S ection 811 PRAC, and Rent Supplement programs. It excludes: Public Housing, Section 236 (only), Section 221(d)(3) BMIR (only), Low Income Housing Tax Credit, and Housing Choice Voucher programs.

Assisted housing tenants who reside in these excluded rental units still may use the R.C. 3767.41 provisions that govern non -subsidized rental housing (see Section IX.G.1, above). Cent. Residents Council v. Lucas Metro Hous. Auth., No. L-09-1137, 2010 WL 497340 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 12, 2010) (Public Housing).

For subsidized rental housing, R.C. 3767.41(A)(2)(b) defines public nuisance. The definition is based on HUD’s physical condition standards for the project -based subsidized housing programs, 24 C.F.R. § 5.703. R.C. 3767.41(B)(2)(c) expressly requires that the definition be construed consistent with HUD and judicial interpretations of 24 C.F.R. § 5.703. R.C. 3767.41(B)(2)(c) also creates a 12 -month safe harbor for properties that receive a HUD REAC score of 75 or higher and do not 312 For the definition of “project-based assisted housing,” see note 165, above.

455 thereafter deteriorate so as to create a health or safety threat to the tenants.

For subsidized rental housing, R.C. 3767.41(B)(1)(b) establishes the notice
requirements. The notice must specify the nuisance condition and state that, if the condition is not remedied within 60 days, an R.C. 3767.41 claim may be brought. The tenant must make reasonable efforts to serve the landlord or landlord’s agent with the notice, in accordance with the Civil Rules, at the management office or at the place that tenants normally pay or send rent. If the landlord is not the owner of record, the tenant must also make reasonable efforts to serve the owner with the notice. If the owner does not receive service of the notice, the tenant must file a certification of the tenant’s attempts to serve the owner.

For subsidized rental housing, R.C. 3767.41(I)(2)(b) creates restrictions on any R.C. 3767.41 sale of the property. The sale must comply with applicable federal law and federal contracts. And the sale must be conditioned on the purchaser e xecuting a contract with HUD (or RHS) to continue the rental subsidy program, unless HUD (or
RHS) has terminated the subsidy contract.

An example of a court order appointing a receiver under R.C. 3767.41 to manage and repair subsidized housing is City of C incinnati v. PE Alms Realty LLC , No.

1500883 (Ohio C.P., Hamilton Cty., Feb. 1, 2018). The receivership order covers seven section 8 project-based properties. These properties contain a total of 660 units.

#### 3. Receiver to Manage Premises Due to Landlord’s

Noncompliance with Court Order, R.C. 2735.01 When a landlord is unwilling or unable to make repairs or provide services to the premises that a court has ordered, R.C. 2735.01 provides authority for the appointment of a receiver to manage, repair, and provide services to the premises. E.g., Avondale
Cmty. Council v. Lentine, No. C-110221, 2012 WL 171892 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Jan.

20, 2012).

R.C. 2735.01 specifies circumstances where courts of common pleas may appoint a receiver as part of their equitable powers. R.C. 2735.01(A)(4) provides for the appointment of a receiver to carry a judgment into effect. R.C. 2735.01(A)(7) provides for the appointment of a receiver in “all other cases in which receivers have been appointed by the use of equity.” The equity authority of courts has included the authority to appoint a receiver if necessary to secure the full effect of a judgment. 65
Am. Jur. 2d Receivers § 28 (1972).

This receivership authority is in addition to the R.C. 3767.41 receivership authority, which is discussed in Sections IX.G.1,2, above.

The relevant court order to make repairs or provide services may be issued, for example, under an R.C. 5321.15(C) claim (see Section XII.B.3, below) or an R.C.

5321.04 contract claim (see Section IX.A.7, above). When a tenant obtains a court order for such repairs or services and the landlord fails to comply with the order, the tenant may request the court to appoint a receiver to carry out the judgment under R.C.

2735.01(A)(4) and/or 2735.01(A)(7).

When the appointment of a receiver is ancillary to final relief in equity, a receiver may be appointed only if there is no less stringent means for protecting the rights of the parties. Hoiles v. Watkins, 117 Ohio St. 165, 157 N.E. 557 (1927).

Therefore, to justify the request for a receiver under R.C. 2735.01( A)(4) and/or 2735.01(A)(7), the tenant may be required to show that: (1) the tenant obtained an order that requires the landlord to make repairs or provide services to the premises, (2) the tenant attempted to enforce the order through contempt proceedings , (3) despite contempt orders requiring the landlord to make the repairs or provide the services, the landlord continues to fail to comply with the order, and (4) the appointment of a receiver will make a difference,i.e., the repairs or services will be made if a responsible person collects the rent and makes arrangements for the repairs or services.

When a tenant decides to file an R.C. 2735.01 request for a receiver, before filing, a helpful next step for the tenant is to identify a qualified housing man agement company (or individual) which (who) is willing to be appointed as the receiver.

### H. Restitution, Housing Code Cases

In sentencing an offender for a misdemeanor, the court may require “restitution by the offender to the victim of the offender’s crime... in an amount based on the victim’s economic loss.” R.C. 2929.28.

Therefore, in criminal housing code enforcement cases, the court may award restitution to the tenant for any relevant economic loss. E.g., State v. Fisher, No. 203- 2033 (Ohio C.P., Noble Cty., May 24, 2004).

457

## X. Tenant Claims for Improper Entry,

R.C. 5321.04,.05

### A. Rights and Duties Relative to any

Landlord Entry Upon the Premises The rights and duties of landlords and tenants, relative to any landlord entry upon the premises, are primar ily established in R.C. 5321.04(A)(7),(8), 5321.04(B), and 5321.05(B). Those rights and duties are discussed in Section VII.E, above.

### B. R.C. 5321.04(B) Remedies

If a landlord enters upon the premises in violation of R.C. 5321.04(A)(8) or 5321.04(B), under R.C. 5321.04(B), the tenant may bring an action against the landlord for actual damages, injunctive relief, and reasonable attorney’s fees, or the tenant may terminate the rental agreement.

R.C. 5321.04(B) expressly authorizes injunctive relief. “[W]hen astatute grants a specific injunctive remedy to an individual or to the state, the party requesting the injunction ‘need not aver and show, as under ordinary rules in equity, that great or irreparable injury is about to be done for which he has no adequate remedy at law.’” Ackerman v. Tri-City Geriatric & Health Care, Inc., 55 Ohio St.2d 51, 56, 378 N.E.2d 145 (1978).

An implied requirement of the R.C. 5321.04(B) right to terminate the rental agreement is that, when exercised, the right must be exercised within a reasonable time.

Limage v. Citiscene Props., No. 92AP-190, 1992 WL 131840, at *2 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 9, 1992). See also T.K.D. Enters. v. Zimmermann , No. 97 -CA-44, 1998 WL 380496, at *5 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 2, 1998).

One court has created two additional implied requirements for exercising this right to terminate the rental agreement.T.K.D. Enters. v. Zimmermann, No. 97-CA-44, 1998 WL 380496, at *4 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 2, 1998). They are: (1) tenant service upon the landl ord of a written notice of the first unlawful entry, and (2) thereafter a second unlawful entry. Id.

Examples of court decisions awarding actual damages under R.C. 5321.04(B) for unlawful entries include: Helfrich v. Mellon, No. 06-CA-69, 2007 WL 1881537(Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 27, 2007) ($2,500 compensatory damages);Fisher v. Barker, 159 Ohio App.3d 745, 825 N.E.2d 244 (2nd Dist. 2005) ($500 compensatory damages – not reviewed or modified on appeal); Blausey v. Frase , No. 06 -CVG-00748 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Feb. 28, 2007) ($500 compensatory damages and $5,000 punitive damages).

Where a landlord has violated R.C. 5321.04(A)(8) or 5321.04(B), and the tenant has not suffered actual damages as a result of the violation, the tenant is still entitled to recover nominal damages. Woodrow v. McGuire, No. 1740, 1989 WL 72799, at *5 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 27, 1989). See also Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 40, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992) (“A court will award nominal damages if a tenant cannot prove actual damages flowing from a landlord’s unlawful behavior”).
Cf. Gordon v. Morris, No. 2000-CA-69, 2001 WL 85797, at *6 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Feb. 2, 2001) (“‘Actual damages’ is a term ‘often used to denote either ‘compensatory damages’ or ‘nominal damages’”).

In an R.C. 5321.04(B) claim, the right of the tenant to recover reasonable attorney’s fees is contingent only on the entry of a judgment on the merits in favor of the tenant. See R.C. 5321.04(B). See also Iwenofu v. Consol. Mgmt., Inc. , 49 Ohio App.3d 33, 35, 550 N.E.2d 505 (8th Dist. 1988) (“R.C. 5321.04 will not justify an award of attorney fees unless the tenant ‘obtains a judgment’ for such conduct”). This prerequisite is met regardless of whether the judgment is for injunctive relief, actual damages, or nominal damages. E.g., Woodrow v. McGuire , No. 1740, 1989 WL 72799, at *5 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 27, 1989) (nominal damages only).

In Woodrow, the tenant proved an R.C. 5321.04(A)(8) violation, but not actual
damages. The tenant’s vacation of the premises made injunctive relief unavailable.

The court held that the tenant was “entitled to nominal damages and that portion of attorney’s fees which correspond to litigating the unauthorized entry.”Woodrow, 1989 WL 72799, at *5.

Three court decisions erroneously hold that a tenant may recover R.C. 5321.04(B) attorney’s fees only if the tenant has suffered actual damages as a result of the landlord’s unlawful entry. DuJardin v. Owens, No. 87-B-19, 1988 WL 109086 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Sept. 22, 1988); Geyer v. Frank, No. CA84-06-074, 1985 WL 8144 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 14, 1985); Dyche Real Estate Fund v. Graves , 55 Ohio App.2d 153, 380 N.E.2d 767 (10th Dist. 1978).

These holdings are erroneous because they ignore the fact that, on an R.C.

5321.04(B) claim, a court may enter a judgment on the merits in favor of the tenant 459 without finding or awarding actual damages. The judgment may grant only “injunctive relief to prevent the recurrence of the conduct.” R.C. 5321.04(B). Or the judgment may award only nominal damages. E.g., Woodrow v. McGuire, No. 1740, 1989 WL 72799, at *5 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 27, 1989). In either case, the landlo rd made an entry in violation of R.C. 5321.04(A)(8) or 5321.04(B) and, therefore, “the tenant may ... obtain a judgment for reasonable attorney’s fees.” R.C. 5321.04(B).

When an action includes an R.C. 5321.04(B) claim and one or more other claims, the computation of the R.C. 5321.04(B) attorney’s fees is based only on the time spent on the R.C. 5321.04(B) claim “or on claims interrelated thereto.” Helfrich v. Mellon,
No. 06-CA-69, 2007 WL 1881537, ¶ 73 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 27, 2007). See also Iwenofu v. Consol. Mgmt., Inc. , 49 Ohio App.3d 33, 550 N.E.2d 505 (8th Dist.

1988). The interrelated claims include common law privacy, trespass, and quiet enjoyment claims arising from the statutory notice violation. Helfrich v. Mellon, 2007 WL 1881537, ¶ 73.

### C. Common Law Remedies

When a landlord violates R.C. 5321.04(A)(8) or 5321.04(B), the tenant may assert common law claims against the landlord, in addition to the R.C. 5321.04(B) claim. See Section V.C, above. For example, the tenant may have a claim for:
(1) trespass, Helfrich v. Mellon, No. 06 -CA-69, 2007 WL 1881537 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 27, 2007);Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992); Baker v. Loudermilk, No. 76AP-770, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 7427 (Ohio App., 10 th Dist., Mar. 29, 1977);

Barnes v. Welborn , No. 04 -6855, 2005 WL 5533987 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., July 5, 2005);
(2) invasion of privacy , Helfrich v. Mellon , No. 06 -CA-69, 2007 WL 1881537 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 27, 2007); Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992); and/or
(3) implied covenant of quiet enjoyment violation, Helfrich v. Mellon, No.

06-CA-69, 2007 WL 1881537 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 27, 2007).

Also, in connection with these common law claims, the tenant has a cla im for punitive damages, if the landlord’s conduct is malicious. See Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992); Baker v. Loudermilk, No. 76AP- 770, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 7427 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 29, 1977).

If a tenant is awarded punitive damages in any of these common law claims, the tenant may also be entitled to an award of attorney’s fees under Ohio common law.

See Section XIX.B, below.

461

## XI. Tenant Claims for Retaliation,

R.C. 5321.02,.03

### A. Types of Retaliation Prohibited

R.C. 5321.02(A) prohibits certain types of landlord retaliation against a tenant.

Subject to the exceptions established in R.C. 5321.03, it prohibits a landlord from increasing a tenant’s rent, decreasing the services that a tenant is entitled to recei ve under a rental agreement, or bringing or threatening to bring an eviction action against a tenant because the tenant engaged in one (or more) of three protected activities. The protected activities are:
(1) Making complaints to an appropriate governmental agency of a violation of a building, housing, health, or safety code that is applicable to the premises, where the violation materially affects health and safety;
(2) Making complaints to the landlord of any violation of R.C. 5321.04; or
(3) Joining with other te nants for the purpose of negotiating or dealing collectively with the landlord on any of the terms and conditions of a rental agreement.

R.C. 5321.02(A)(1)-(3).

The scope of this retaliation prohibition, and issues relative to proving such retaliation, are discussed in Section II.T, above.

### B. R.C. 5321.02(B) Remedies

When a landlord violates the R.C. 5321.02(A) retaliation prohibition, under R.C.

5321.02(B), the tenant may bring an action against the landlord for actual damages and reasonable attorney’s fees.313 See also Laster v. Bowman , 52 Ohio App.2d 379, 370 N.E.2d 767 (8th Dist. 1977). But see Stuckey v. Vistula Mgmt. Co. , No. L-00-1257, 313 For city of Cleveland tenants, the tenant may recover minimum statutory damages, between $50 and $500, in lieu of the R.C. 5321.02(B) actual damages. Cleveland, OH Code, pt. 3C, tit.

IX, ch. 375, § 375.08. E.g., Leak v. Hale, No. 2006-CVG-33367 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., Jan. 10, 2008) ($500 statutory minimum damage award).

2001 WL 477133 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., May 4, 2001).314 Where the retaliation is the commencement of an eviction action, the tenant may also assert the retaliation as a defense to the eviction claim. See Section II.T, above.

Where the retaliation is a rent increase, the tenant is not liable for the rent increase.

E.g., Bland v. Kovein, No. 16179, 1993 WL 473837 (Ohio App. , 9th Dist., Nov. 10, 1993).

When a landlord commences an eviction action in violation of R.C. 5321.02(A), the tenant may bring an R.C. 5321.02(B) damage claim against the landlord even if the court lacks jurisdiction to hear the eviction action. See Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth.
v. Watkins, 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984) (occupancy under a land installment contract for which R.C. 5313.07 required a foreclosure action).

Actual damages under R.C. 5321.02(B) include, for example, the time and expense of looking for another dwelling unit, damage to credit, and the time and expense of defending an eviction action. E.g., Korb v. Doering, No. 91-CVG-4948 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 10, 1991) (damages awarded for lost wages).

One court decision holds that actual damages under R.C. 5321.02(B) include emotional distress damages suffered as a result of the retaliation. Gateway Apts. v.

Darrah, Nos. 76 -CVG-725,928 (Ohio Mun., Lyndhurst, Aug. 22, 1977). See also Simon v. Solomon, 385 Mass. 91, 431 N.E.2d 556 (1982) (construing Massachusetts Landlord and Tenant Act). But see Ficker v. Diefenbach, 34 Or. App. 241, 578 P.2d 467 (1978) (construing Oregon Landlord and Tenant Act). Irrespective of that holding, the tenant may have a co mmon law claim for intentional or negligent infliction of serious emotional distress. See Section XI.D, below.

Actual damages under R.C. 5321.02(B) do not include punitive damages. Davis
v. Reed, Nos. 68699,68700, 1996 WL 347814 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., J une 20, 1996).

However, the tenant has a common law claim for punitive damages, if the landlord’s conduct is malicious, intentional, and wanton. See Section XI.D, below.

Attorney’s fees may be awarded under R.C. 5321.02(B) only if the tenant has suffered actual damages. Jemo Assocs. v. Garman , 70 Ohio St.2d 267, 436 N.E.2d 314 The Stuckey decision is wrong. It erroneously holds that R.C. 5321.02 “permits a retaliation claim to be asserted only as a defense in an eviction action” and that “no independent cause of action exists” under R.C. 5321.02(B). 2001 WL 477133, at *3. The holding and the decision completely ignore the R.C. 5321.02(B) provision that expressly permits a tenant to “recover from the landlord actual damages together with reasonable attorneys’ fees.” 463 1353 (1982). See also Zamos v. Dunn , No. 2000 -P-0120, 2001 WL 1149844 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 28, 2001);Neff v. Manashian, 104 Ohio App.3d 528, 662 N.E.2d 856 (8th Dist. 1995) (dicta); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Watkins, 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984).

Where appropriate, the tenant also may seek injunctive relief to enjoin the threat or continuation of the unlawful retaliation. R.C. 5321.02 does not expressly authorize injunctive relief. This contrasts with other sections of R.C. Chapter 5321 that do expressly authorize injunctive relief (i.e., R.C. 5321.04(B) and 5321.05(C)).

However, the remedies provided tenants in R.C. Chapter 5321 are intende d to supplement other remedial measures. Shroades v. Rental Homes, Inc., 68 Ohio St.2d 20, 427 N.E.2d 774 (1981). Other remedial measures include the authority to grant injunctive relief that is conferred upon courts by R.C. 2727.03 (courts of common
pleas) and 1901.18 (municipal courts). Courts have the right and the responsibility to exercise that authority in any appropriate case. Ex parte Cattell, 146 Ohio St. 112, 64 N.E.2d 416 (1945).

### C. R.C. 5321.03 Exceptions

R.C. 5321.03 provides that, in certain specified circumstances, “[n]otwithstanding section 5321.02 of the Revised Code, a landlord may bring an action under Chapter 1923 of the Revised Code for possession of the premises.” Those circumstances are discussed in Section II.T.3, above.

By the plain meaning of the above-quoted provision, R.C. 5321.03 only concerns a landlord’s right to bring an eviction action even though it constitutes retaliation under R.C. 5321.02(A). Therefore, the R.C. 5321.03 exceptions are inapplicable altogether to any rent increase or reduction in service that is retaliation under R.C. 5321.02(A).

As to a retaliatory eviction, while R.C. 5321.03 clearly bars a tenant’s R.C.

5321.02(B) retaliation defense, the case law is split on whether it also bars a tenant’s R.C. 5321.02(B) retaliation claim for actual damages and reasonable attorney’s fees.

Compare Maurer v. Gabriel, No. 17310, 1996 WL 1740 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 3,
1996) (not a bar to the claim), Harris v. Winston, No. 2004-CVG-28660 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 16, 2005), and Belvoir Cliffs Apts. v. Bembry , 56 Ohio
Misc. 37, 383 N.E.2d 1170 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, 1978) with Fay Gardens Mobile Home Park v. Newman, 14 Ohio App.3d 144, 470 N.E.2d 164 (12th Dist. 1983) (bars the claim; construing R.C. 373 3.09,.091 (now R.C. 4781.36,.37) – the manufactured home park provisions that prohibit retaliation and are essentially the same as R.C.

5321.02,.03).

### D. Common Law Remedies

When a landlord violates R.C. 5321.02(A), the tenant may assert common law claims against the landlord, in addition to the R.C. 5321.02(B) claim. See Section V.C, above.

For example, in these cases, the tenant has a common law claim against the landlord for punitive damages, if the landlord’s conduct is malicious, intentional, and
wanton. Davis v. Reed, Nos. 68699,68700, 1996 WL 347814, at *5 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 20, 1996);Burroughs v. Douglas, No. 46030, 1983 WL 2865, at *3-4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 8, 1983).

If the tenant has suffered serious emotional distress as a resul t of the retaliation, the tenant may have a common law claim against the landlord for intentional or negligent infliction of serious emotional distress. Burroughs, 1983 WL 2865, at *6.

See generally Yeager v. Local Union 20, Etc., 6 Ohio St.3d 369, 453 N.E.2d 666 (1983) (intentional); Schultz v. Barberton Glass Co., 4 Ohio St.3d 131, 447 N.E.2d 109 (1983) (negligent).

465

## XII. Tenant Claims for Lockout or

Utility Termination, R.C. 5321.15

### A. Type of Conduct Prohibited

R.C. 5321.15(A) prohibits a landlord from r ecovering, or attempting to recover, possession of the premises by terminating the utilities or changing the locks. It also prohibits a landlord from engaging in “any [other] act ... against a tenant ... for the purpose of recovering possession of residen tial premises, other than as provided in Chapters 1923., 5303., and 5321. of the Revised Code.” These prohibitions apply even if the tenant’s right to possession has terminated.

R.C. 5321.15(A). They also apply even if the tenant has vacated the premises but continues to store personal property at the premises. See Stark v. Weimer, No. H-98- 031, 1999 WL 253451 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 30, 1999) (R.C. 5321.15(B) violation); Mounts v. Foerg , No. R90 -CVG-0375 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Dec. 5,
1990) (same).

If the utility service termination is due to the landlord’s nonpayment, and if the landlord’s failure to make the payments is not for the purpose of recovering possession of the property, the landlord’s action is not a violation of R.C. 5321.15(A). Brown v.

Williams, No. 2012-CVG-17491, slip op. at 2 -3 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Mar. 11, 2014, and May 2, 2014). However, the termination of the utility service still constitutes a violation of R.C. 5321.04(A) and the rental agreement (id.), for which the landlord is liable (see Sections IX.A,B, above).

R.C. 5321.15(A) does not prohibit a landlord from recovering possession of the premises upon a tenant’s surrender of the premises. Burkholder v. Straughn, No. 97- T-0146, 1998 WL 553623 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 26, 1998). “In order to achieve a valid surrender, both the landlord and tenant must consent to the termination [of the rental agreement].” Id., 1998 WL 553623, at *3. “Such mutual consent distinguishes surrender from abandonment, in which the tenant acts unilaterally without the consent of the landlord.” Id.

“In the absence of an express written agreement, ... a surrender may ... be implied from the conduct of the parties.” Id. Accord Tecumseh Landing, LLC v. Bonetzky, 38 N.E.3d 1 149, 2015 -Ohio-2741, ¶¶ 27,28 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., July 6, 2015);

Pietrykowski v. Hamblin, No. WD-84-86, 1985 WL 7092, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Apr. 12, 1985). “An example of this would be an oral agreement to surrender coupled with the actual physi cal delivery of the premises by the tenant to the landlord.” Burkholder, 1998 WL 553623, at *3.

R.C. 5321.15(A) also does not prohibit a landlord from recovering possession of the premises upon a tenant’s abandonment of the premises. “Abandoned property ...

is property over which the owner has relinquished all right, title, claim, and possession with the intention of not reclaiming it or resuming its ownership, possession or enjoyment.” Doughman v. Long, 42 Ohio App.3d 17, 21, 536 N.E.2d 394 (12th Dist.

1987). Accord McCain v. Brewer, No. 2014-CA-8, 2015 WL 302829, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 23, 2015); Gordon v. Morris, No. 2000-CA-69, 2001 WL 85797, at *4 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 2001). “In order to prove that property has been abandoned, it must be shown that the owner intended to abandon the property, and engaged in acts or omissions implementing that intent.” Gordon, 2001 WL 85797, at *4. Accord McCain, 2015 WL 302829, ¶ 17; Barnes v. Welborn, No. 04-6855, 2005 WL 5533987 (Ohio C.P., M ontgomery Cty., July 5, 2005). Cf. Hayes v. Hayes , No.

4892, 1985 WL 7948 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 11, 1985) (personal property).

Therefore, “[i]n order to establish an abandonment of a leasehold, actual acts of relinquishment accompanied by intention to abandon must be shown. The primary elements are the intention to abandon and the external act by which that intention is carried into effect.” Gavin L. Phillips, Annotation,What Constitutes Abandonment of Residential or Commercial Lease– Modern Cases, 84 A.L.R. 4th 183, 192 (1991). Cf.

Hamilton v. Harville , 63 Ohio App.3d 27, 30, 577 N.E.2d 1125 (12th Dist. 1989) (underground storage tank). “[O]ccasional, infrequent or even complete non-use is not sufficient in itself to establish the existence ofan ‘abandonment,’ absent other evidence tending to prove the intent to abandon.” Hamilton v. Harville, 63 Ohio App.3d 27, 30, 577 N.E.2d 1125 (12th Dist. 1989). Accord McCain v. Brewer, No. 2014-CA-8, 2015 WL 302829, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 23, 2015).

### B. R.C. 5321.15(C) Remedies


#### 1. Monetary Relief

When a landlord violates R.C. 5321.15(A), under R.C. 5321.15(C), the tenant may bring an action against the landlord for “all damages.” All damages under R.C. 5321.15(C) include general damages, which are the tenant’s damages that necessarily result fromthe R.C. 5321.15(A) violation. Gordon 467
v. Morris, No. 2000-CA-69, 2001 WL 85797, at *4 -5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 2001). In the tenant’s pleading, the tenant is not required to allege specifically the general damages. Id.

All damages under R.C. 53 21.15(C) also include special damages, which are the tenant’s damages that result from the R.C. 5321.15(A) violation but were not a necessary consequence of it. Id. In the tenant’s pleading, the tenant is required to allege specifically any special damages. Id. See also Ohio R. Civ. P. 9(G).

General damages or special damages under R.C. 5321.15(C) may include, for example:
(1) loss of the rental value of the premises , Gordon v. Morris , No. 2000 - CA-69, 2001 WL 85797, at *4-5 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 2001);
(2) lost wages, Germadnik v. Auld , No. 2017 -T-0113, 2018 WL 3533259 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 23, 2018); Craft v. Oney, No. 8766, 1984 WL 4060 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 17, 1984);Meacham v. Miller, No.

90-CIV-56 (Ohio C.P., Jackson Cty., Dec. 24, 1990), aff’d on other grounds, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1991);
(3) cost of a hotel room, Cupp v. Olp, No. 2020-CVG-001317 (Ohio Mun., Wayne Cty., Mar. 29, 2021); Meacham v. Miller, No. 90-CIV-56 (Ohio C.P., Jackson Cty., Dec. 24, 1990), aff’d on other grounds , 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1991);
(4) lost or damaged personal property ,315 Foster v. Benson , No. 107366, 2019 WL 1858391 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 25, 2019); Germadnik v.

Auld, No. 2017-T-0113, 2018 WL 3533259 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 23, 2018); Craft v. Oney , No. 8766, 1984 WL 4060 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 17, 1984); Sanchez v. Tompkins , No. 2021 -CVI-006506 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Sept. 13, 2021); Gaitawe v. Mays,
No. 11 -CVG-891 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, A ug. 15, 2011); 316 Smith v.

Akarui Enters., LLC , No. 2010 -CVH-18860 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., June 30, 2011); Walker v. Tomi Enters. , No. 2006 -CVH- 4305 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 12, 2006);Stolzenburg v.

Frush, No. 92-CVC10-8217 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty., Dec. 8, 1994);

315 For a discussion on the measure of damages for damage to personal property, see Section XVIII.C.2, below.

316 The tenant prevailed in her appeal from this decision on the trial court’s denial of her attorney fee request. See Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 4859073 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct.

12, 2012).
(5) cost of storage of personal property , Barnes v. Welborn, No. 04 -6855, 2005 WL 5533987 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., July 5, 2005);
(6) cost of moving truck rental, Barnes v. Welborn, No. 04-6855, 2005 WL 5533987 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., July 5, 2005);
(7) cost and inconvenience of using neighbor’s water, Turner v. Rose, No.

2010-CVH-374 (Ohio County Ct., Meigs Cty., Feb. 21, 2012); and
(8) discomfort and disruption of normal living routine , Foster v. Benson ,
No. 107366, 2019 WL 1858391 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 25, 2019);

Germadnik v. Auld, No. 2017-T-0113, 2018 WL 3533259 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 23, 2018); Hogan v. Mozik, No. 91-CVG-27438 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 19, 1992).

All damages under R.C. 5321.15(C) include damages for emotional distress, if the landlord’s conduct is malicious.Dirnberg v. Hack, No. S-86-39, 1987 WL 8012 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 20, 1987); Matthes v. Crow, No. 43270, 1981 WL 5033 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 6, 1981). E.g., Smith v. Akarui Enters., LLC , No. 2010 -CVH- 18860 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 30, 2011).

Likewise, all damages under R.C. 5321.15(C) include punitive damages, if the landlord’s conduct is malicious.317 Dirnberg v. Hack , No. S -86-39, 1987 WL 8012 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 20, 1987);Melvin v. Bryant, No. CA-7259, 1981 Ohio App.

LEXIS 13073 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 13, 1981); Matthes v. Crow , No. 43270, 1981 WL 5033 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 6, 1981); O’Neil v. Walburg, 70 Ohio App.2d 30, 433 N.E.2d 1286 (10th Dist. 1980). See also Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992); Burroughs v. Douglas, No. 46030, 1983 WL 2865 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 8, 1983). But see Lockhart v. Leach, No. 7880, 1983 WL 4815 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 15, 1983) (without ultimately reaching the question, stating an inclination to find that the absence of any express reference to punitive damages in R.C. 5321.15 precludes the award of such damages).

317 A comparison between R.C. 5321.15(C) and R.C. 5 321.02(B) provides a reasonable basis for the conclusion that R.C. 5321.15 authorizes an award of punitive damages even without a finding of malice. R.C. 5321.02(B) provides that, when a landlord violates R.C. 5321.02(A), the tenant may recover “actual da mages.” R.C. 5321.15(C) provides that, when a landlord violates R.C. 5321.15(A), the tenant may recover “all damages.” By using the term “all damages” instead of “actual damages” in R.C. 5321.15(C), the legislature seems to authorize an award of actual damages and all other types of damages, which includes punitive damages, when a landlord violates R.C. 5321.15(A).

469 If the requisite malice exists, the tenant is entitled to punitive damages even if the actual damage award is nominal damages. Gordon v. Morris, No. 2000-CA-69, 2001 WL 85797, at *6 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 2001). E.g., Blausey v. Frase, No. 06- CVG-00748 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Feb. 28 , 2007) ($25 nominal damages and $2,500 punitive damages); Milburn v. Henness , No. 03 -CVH-208 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Jan. 29, 2004) ($100 nominal damages and $750 punitive damages).

“The determination of the amount of punitive damages is within the discretion of the trier of fact as long as the award is not based upon passion and prejudice.” Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 40, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992).

Factors that courts consider in determining the amount of the punitive damage award includ e: “the relationship between the parties, the probability of recurrence unless the conduct is deterred, the harm that is likely to occur from similar conduct, the harm that actually occurred, the reprehensibility of the conduct, the nature of the wrong, the removal of any financial profit so that future conduct results in a loss, the financial status of the parties, the deterrence value, a reasonable relationship between compensatory damages and punitive damages, and whether the wrong is a single occurrence or a pattern of wrong conduct.” Brown v. Ray, No. 08-CVG-25314, ¶ 81 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb. 18, 2009). Accord Smith v. Akarui Enters., LLC, No. 2010-CVH-18860 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 30, 2011).

The amount of the punitive damage award “‘must be an amount that fairly punishes the [landlord] for his malicious or malevolent acts and that will deter others from similar conduct.’” Babcock v. Milligan , No. 01 -CA-013, 2002 WL 924297, at *1 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 3, 2002). When a landlord, acting with malice, executes a “self-help eviction” of the tenant in violation in R.C. 5321.15(A), a punitive damage award of $50 is inadequate. Id.

“The punitive damages should bear a reasonable relationship to Plaintiff’s [tenant’s] damages ... but need not be limited to any particular multiple of this amount.” Smith v. Akarui Enters., LLC , No. 2010 -CVH-18860 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., June 30, 2011) (punitive damage award in amount 30 times other damages).See, e.g., Fisher v. Barker , 159 Ohio App.3d 745, 825 N.E.2d 244 (2nd Dist. 2005) (unlawful entry and conversion claims – compensatory damages reduced from $2,000 to $600 and, thereupon, punitive damages reduced from $20,000 to $5,000);Gaston v.

Seals, No. CV-03-505578 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., May 4, 2004) (punitive damage award, $5,347.60, equal to the compensatory damages, $668.45, multiplied by eight, the number of tenants harmed by the landlord).

As to due process limitations, “[t]he U.S. Supreme Court did not lay down a 4-to- 1 or single-digit-ratio rule – it merely said that ‘there is a presumption against an award that has a 145-to-1 ratio’ ...– and it would be unreasonable to do so.”Mathias v. Accor
Econ. Lodging, Inc., 347 F.3d 672, 676 (7th Cir. 2003) (upheld 37.2 to 1 ratio, where injury from bed bugs in a hotel room that was not to be assigned until treated).

But query whether these claims are tort claims subject to the R.C. 2315.21 punitive damages limitation (2 to 1 ratio). See Sivit v. Vill. Green of Beachwood, L.P., 143 Ohio St.3d 168, 35 N.E.3d 508, ¶ 5 (2015) (R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim is a tort action within the purview of R.C. 2315.21).

Numerous trial court decisions have awarded punitive damages in an R.C. 5321.15
action. E.g., Smith v. Akarui Enters., LLC , No. 2010 -CVH-18860 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 30, 2011) ($468,660 – 30 times other damages – because landlord continued these practices despite prior escalating punitive damage awards up to $185,804); Brown v. Ray, No. 08-CVG-25314 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Feb. 18, 2009) ($10,000); Collins v. Loretz, No. 637836 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty.,
Aug. 6, 2008) ($11,040); Blausey v. Frase, No. 06-CVG-00748 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., Feb. 28, 2007) ($2,500); Gaston v. Seals , No. CV -03-505578 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., May 4, 2004) ($5,347.60); Babcock v. Milligan, No. 99-CI-420 (Ohio C.P., Coshocton Cty., July 19, 2002) ($250);318 Proctor v. Frame, 90 Ohio Misc.2d 11, 695 N.E.2d 357 (Ohio Mun., Akron, 1998) ($5,000); Runyan v. Irwin, No. 94-CVF- 200 (Ohio Mun., Washington Court House, July 13, 1995) ($1,000); Stolzenburg v.

Frush, No. 92 -CVC10-8217 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty., Dec. 8, 1994) ($2,500);

Bowman v. Helm, No. 89-CVH-384 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., Jan. 4, 1991) ($1,010);

Pittman v. Vaughn, No. 87-CVG-06464 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug. 4,
1987) ($2,500); Cover v. Hensley, No. 84-CVH-608 (Ohio Mun., Port Clinton, Mar.

13, 1985) ($1,000), aff’d on other grounds, No. OT-85-10, 1985 WL 4804 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 31, 1985); Moore v. Ress , No. R80 -CVG-0120 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Sept. 18, 1980) ($3,000);Lewis v. Romans, No. 78-CIF-4818 (Ohio Mun., Shaker Heights, Sept. 13, 1979) ( $200); Tarkowski v. Gaye , No. CVF -291 (Ohio Mun., 318 The trial court initially awarded the tenant punitive damages in the amount of $50. Babcock
v. Milligan, No. 99-CI-420 (Ohio C.P., Coshocto n Cty., Apr. 27, 2001). The court of appeals held that the amount of the punitive damage award was inadequate and remanded the case for “a specific just determination on the amount of punitive damages.” Babcock v. Milligan, No.

01-CA-013, 2002 WL 924297, at *1 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 3, 2002). On remand, the trial court awarded the tenant punitive damages in the amount of $250.

471 Cleveland, Mar. 1, 1977) ($1,000). See also Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992) ($2,500 punitive damage award under related tort claims); Shaw v. Mays Mobile Home Court, No. 87-CVE-233 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe,
Dec. 10, 1987) ($500 punitive damage award under R.C. 3733.17(C) (now R.C.

4781.49(C)) – the manufactured home park provision that prohibits certain acts to recover residential premises and is essentially the same as R.C. 5321.15(C)).

#### 2. Attorney’s Fees

When a tenant prevails on an R.C. 5321.15(A) claim, under R.C. 5321.15(C), the tenant is entitled to recover reasonable attorney’s fees.

The attorney fee award is mandatory, if the tenant prevails on the R.C. 5321.15
claim. Risch v. Samuel, No. C-190159, 2020 WL 1488724 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Mar.

25, 2020); Conner v. Conner, 114 N.E.3d 281, 2018-Ohio-2698 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., 2018); Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 485907 3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct.

12, 2012); Crenshaw v. Rowland , 196 Ohio App.3d 717, 965 N.E.2d 341 (6th Dist.

2011); Filyo v. Cannon, No. 95-CA-1, 1995 WL 776946 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec.

21, 1995); Lewis v. Romans, 70 Ohio App.2d 7, 433 N.E.2d 622 (8th Dist. 1980).

The amount of the attorney fee award is a matter within the discretion of the trial
court. Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 4859073 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct.

12, 2012); Crenshaw v. Rowland , 196 Ohio App.3d 717, 965 N.E.2d 341 (6th Dist.

2011); Thomas v. Papadelis, 16 Ohio App.3d 359, 476 N.E.2d 726 (9th Dist. 1984);

Lewis v. Romans, 70 Ohio App.2d 7, 433 N.E.2d 622 (8th Dist. 1980).

When an action includes an R.C. 5321.15 claim and one or more other claims, the computation of the R.C. 5321.15(C) attorney’s fees is based only on the time spent on the R.C. 5321.15 claim or on claims interrelated to the R.C. 5321.15 claim. See, e.g., Crawford v. Tivener, No. 15-CA-22, 2016 WL 5638379, ¶ 59 (Ohio App., 5th Dist.,
Sept. 22, 2016). The interrelated claims include a common law trespass claim arising from the unlawful exclusion of the tenant from the premises. Cf. Helfrich v. Mellon,
No. 06-CA-69, 2007 WL 1881537, ¶ 73 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 27, 2007) (R.C.

5321.04(B) claim). The interrel ated claims do not include the landlord’s claim for unpaid rent. Crawford v. Tivener, No. 15-CA-22, 2016 WL 5638379,¶ 59 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Sept. 22, 2016).

#### 3. Injunctive Relief

In an R.C. 5321.15(A),( C) action, the tenant may request injunctive relief, in addition to monetary relief and reasonable attorney’s fees. For a discussion on the jurisdiction of municipal courts to grant injunctive relief in these cases, see Section XX.C.2, below.

Due to the emergency nature of a utility termination or a lockout, the request for injunctive relief often includes a request for a temporary restraining order. In a utility termination case, the utility company may be an appropriate additional defendant to ensure that the tenant receives full and timely relief (i.e., immediate restoration of the utility service).

When requesting this injunctive relief, the tenant may face arguments concerning:
(1) the equity requirement of clean hands, (2) the bond requirement forthe issuance of a temporary restraining order, and (3) the absence of any express authorization in R.C.

5321.15 for granting injunctive relief.

Since the request for injunctive relief (including a temporary restraining order) is a request for equitable rel ief, it is appropriate for the court to consider whether the tenant comes into court with clean hands. However, courts typically do not deny the request for a temporary restraining order on this basis, especially when the landlord’s conduct is egregious and constitutes a clear violation of R.C. 5321.15(A). Nonetheless, when the tenant is far behind in rent payments, some courts are reluctant to issue a temporary restraining order.

Civil Rule 65(C) establishes the bond requirement for the issuance of a temporary restraining order. Under Civil Rule 65(C), the discretion committed to the court for determining the size of the bond includes the discretionary authority to require no bond at all. Vanguard Transp. Sys., Inc. v. Edwards Transfer & Storage Co. , 109 Ohio App.3d 786, 793, 673 N.E.2d 182 (10th Dist. 1996); Colquett v. Byrd, 59 Ohio Misc.

45, 49-50, 392 N.E.2d 1328 (Ohio Mun., Mansfield, 1979).

Among the reasons for which a court may exercise its discretion to require no bond (or a minimal bond) is the moving party’s lack of financial means to post a bond.

Colquett v. Byrd, 59 Ohio Misc. 45, 49-50, 392 N.E.2d 1328 (Ohio Mun., Mansfield, 1979). See also Wayne Chemical, Inc. v. Columbus Agency Serv. Corp., 562 F.2d 692, 701 (7th Cir. 1977); Doe by Doe v. Perales, 782 F. Supp. 201, 206 (W.D.N.Y. 1991);

473 Walker v. Pierce, 665 F. Supp. 831, 843-44 (N.D. Cal. 1987).319 In utility termination cases, another reason on which courts have relied for not requiring a bond is that the landlord “will sustain no damages if it is finally decided that the injunction should not have been granted.” Brown v. Ray, No. 08-CVG-25314, ¶ 41 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 26, 2008).

As a result, Ohio courts have issued injunctions in R.C. 5321.15 actions without a bond requirement or with a minimal bond requirement. E.g., Brown v. Ray, No. 08- CVG-25314 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 26, 2008);Colquett v. Byrd, 59 Ohio Misc. 45, 392 N.E.2d 1328 (Ohio Mun., Mansfield, 1979).

R.C. 5321.15 does not expressly auth orize injunctive relief. This omission contrasts with other sections of R.C. Chapter 5321 that do expressly authorize injunctive relief (i.e., R.C. 5321.04(B) and 5321.05(C)).

But the remedies provided tenants in R.C. Chapter 5321 are intended to supplement other remedial measures. See Section V.C., above. Other remedial measures include the authority to grant injunctive relief that is conferred upon courts by R.C. 2727.03 (courts of common pleas), 1901.18 (municipal courts), R.C.

1901.13(B) (municipal c ourts, Cuyahoga County), and R.C. 1901.131 (municipal courts, housing divisions). Courts have the right and the responsibility to exercise that authority in any appropriate case. Ex Parte Cattell, 146 Ohio St. 112, 64 N.E.2d 416 (1945).

When a tenant files an R.C. 5321.15 action for injunctive relief, an eviction action by the landlord is a compulsory counterclaim to the R.C. 5321.15 action. Trimble v.

Jones, No. 89-CVGT-589 (Ohio Mun., Miami Cty., June 13, 1989).

When a court grants injunctive relief in an R.C. 5321.15 action, a landlord’s noncompliance is typically grounds for a contempt order, which may mandate compliance and award the tenant compensatory damages and attorney’s fees. E.g., Brown v. Ray, No. 08-CVG-25314 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.Div., Dec. 17, 2008).

For a discussion on compensatory damages and attorney’s fees in civil contempt proceedings, see Section IX.A.7, above.

319 Civil Rule 65 is modeled after Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. Staff Notes, Ohio R. Civ. P. 65. Consequen tly, in construing Civil Rule 65(C), Ohio courts have relied on federal court decisions construing Rule 65(c) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. E.g., Vanguard Transp. Sys., Inc. v. Edwards Transfer & Storage Co. , 109 Ohio App.3d 786, 793, 673 N.E.2d 182, 186 (10th Dist. 1996); Colquett v. Byrd , 59 Ohio Misc. 45, 49, 392 N.E.2d 1328, 1331 (Ohio Mun., Mansfield, 1979).

### C. Additional Remedies

When a landlord violates R.C. 5321.15(A), the tenant may assert common law claims against the landlord, in addition to the R.C. 5321.15(C) claim. See Section V.C., above. For example, the tenant may have a claim for:
(1) trespass, Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th
Dist. 1992); Babcock v. Milligan, No. 99-CI-420 (Ohio C.P., Coshocton Cty., Apr. 27, 2001);
(2) invasion of privacy, Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992); Hall v. Lacheta , No. 92AP -020013, 1992 WL 362546 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 18, 1992);Babcock v. Milligan, No.

99-CI-420 (Ohio C.P., Coshocton Cty., Apr. 27, 2001);
(3) conversion, Foster v. Benson , No. 107366, 2019 WL 1858391 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 25, 2019); Gordon v. Morris , No. 2000 -CA-69, 2001 WL 85797 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 2001); Meacham v.

Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35,606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992);Sanchez v.

Tompkins, No. 2021 -CVI-006506 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Sept. 13, 2021); Malcom v. Tate , No. 99 -CVH-21689 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 29, 2001); and/or
(4) intentional or negligent infliction of serious emotional distress , Stolzenburg v. Frush , No. 92 -CVC10-8217 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty.,
Dec. 8, 1994).

When the R.C. 5321.15(A) violation concerns a utility termination, the utility company may be a participant in the landlord’s unlawful activity. A typical situation is where: (1) the landlord is the customer for the utility service to the rental unit, (2) the landlord is obligated under the rental agreement to pay for the utility service, (3) the landlord instructs the utility company to terminate the utility service to the rental unit, and (4) the utility company then terminates the utility service. For a discussion on potential tenant claims against the utility company in these circumstances, see National Consumer Law Center, Access to Utility Service (5th ed. 2011).

475

## XIII. Tenant Claims for Seizure of

Personal Property, R.C. 5321.15

### A. Type of Conduct Prohibited

R.C. 5321.15(B) prohibits a landlord from seizing a tenant’s personal property for the purpose of recovering unpaid rent, unless the seizure is pursuant to a court order.

This prohibition applies even if the tenant’s right to possession has terminated. R .C.

5321.15(B).

If a tenant vacates the premises but leaves some (or all) of the tenant’s personal property at the premises, common law governs: (1) as to whether the tenant has abandoned the property,320 and (2) the landlord’s obligations regarding removalof the
property. See Edwards v. C.N. Inv. Co. , 27 Ohio Misc. 57, 272 N.E.2d 652 (Ohio Mun., Shaker Heights, 1971).

In these circumstances, if the tenant has not abandoned the personal property, the R.C. 5321.15(B) prohibition applies. E.g., Stark v. Weimer, No. H-98-031, 1999 WL 253451 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 30, 1999); Mounts v. Foerg, No. R90-CVG-0375 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Dec. 5, 1990).

A lease provision that purports to establish a conclusive presumption of abandonment, relative to personal property on the premises upon the expiration of the lease, violates R.C. 5321.15(B) and is thus unenforceable. Rand v. Washington, No.

7822, 1983 WL 2448 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 26, 1983).

### B. R.C. 5321.15(C) Remedies

When a landlord seizes a tenant’s perso nal property in violation of R.C.

5321.15(B), under R.C. 5321.15(C), the tenant may bring an action against the landlord for “all damages” and reasonable attorney’s fees.

R.C. 5321.15(C) does not authorize an action by a third party, whose personal property has been unlawfully seized by the landlord, because the third party is not a
tenant. O’Neil v. Walburg, 70 Ohio App.2d 30, 433 N.E.2d 1286 (10th Dist. 1980).

For a discussion on “all damages” and reasonable attorney ’s fees under R.C.

5321.15(C), see Sections XII.B.1,2, above.

320 For a discussion on abandonment of property under common law, see Section XII.A., above.

Examples of court decisions awarding R.C. 5321.15(C) damages for R.C.

5321.15(B) violations include: Gordon v. Morris, No. 2000-CA-69, 2001 WL 85797 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 2001) ($1,000 punitive damages); Stark v. Weimer, No.

H-98-031, 1999 WL 253451 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 30, 1999) ($18,000 compensatory damages reversed and remanded because evidence supported, at most, $13,877 compensatory damages); Milburn v. Henness, No. 03-CVH-208 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Jan. 29, 2 004) ($100 nominal damages and $750 punitive damages);

Hornikel v. Watkins, No. 00-CVF-00401 (Ohio Mun., Bellefontaine, Nov. 29, 2000) ($10,000 compensatory damages).

In an R.C. 5321.15(B),(C) action, the tenant may request injunctive relief, in addition to monetary relief and reasonable attorney’s fees. For a discussion on injunctive relief in an R.C. 5321.15(C) action, see Section XII.B.3, above.

### C. Additional Remedies

When a landlord violates R.C. 5321.15(B), the tenant may assert common law claims against the landlord, in addition to the R.C. 5321.15(C) claim. See Section V.C,
above. For example, the tenant may have a claim for:
(1) bailment, Edwards v. C.N. Inv. Co., 27 Ohio Misc. 57, 272 N.E.2d 652 (Ohio Mun., Shaker Heights, 1971); cf. Kayanda v. Kamenir, 16 Ohio Misc.2d 1, 475 N.E.2d 519 (Ohio Mun., Akron, 1984); and/or
(2) conversion, Fisher v. Barker , 159 Ohio App.3d 745, 825 N.E.2d 244 (2nd Dist. 2005); Gordon v. Morris, No. 2000-CA-69, 2001 WL 85797 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 2001);Milburn v. Henness, No. 03-CVH- 208 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Jan. 29, 2004).

The tenant also may have an R.C. 5321.04(B) claim (and related common law
claims) against the landlord if the landlord entered the premises in violation of R.C.

5321.04(A)(8) or 5321.04(B), whenthe landlord seized the tenant’s personal property.

For a discussion on the R.C. 5321.04(B) claim (and the related common law claims), see Chapter X, above.

Also, instead of requesting injunctive relief in an R.C. 5321.15(C) action, the tenant may file a replevin action for the return of the property.

Two court decisions address tenant conversion claims against landlords arising from the execution of a writ of restitution in an eviction action: Castle Apts., Inc. v.

Allgood, 43 Ohio App.3d 163, 539 N.E.2d 1154 (9th Dist. 1988), and Keesey v.

Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. , No. 2000 -AP-080057, 2001 WL 293253 (Ohio App., 477 5th Dist., Mar. 20, 2001).

In Allgood, the court held the landlord liable. The tenant’s personal property had been seized pursuant to the writ of restitution and, thereafter, the return of the regular mail service disclosed that the eviction complaint had not been served in accordance with the applicable law.

In Keesey, the court held the landlord not liable. The tenant’s mobile home had been removed from the mobile home park pursuant to the writ of restitution and, thereafter, the court granted the tenant’s motion to vacate the eviction judgment based upon the landlord’s acceptance of certain rent. “Because appellee [landlord] proceeded under a lawfully issued writ of restitution, the municipal court’s subsequent decision to vacate the judgment does not operate to retroactively transform a lawful act into a unlawful one.” 2001 WL 293253, at *2. Cf. Sacksteder v. Gisslen, 197 Ohio App.3d 484, 968 N.E.2d 11 (2nd Dist. 2011) (denial of R.C. 5321.15(B) claim arising from executed writ of restitution followed by sustained objection to eviction judgment);

Jansen v. Barclay Square , 34 Ohio Misc. 14, 295 N.E.2d 443 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., 1973) (denial of tenant’s trespass claim arising from properly executed writ of restitution).

## XIV. Tenant Claims for Wrongful

Eviction, Common Law

### A. Introduction

A tenant’s right tocontinued occupancy of the premises can be defined by: (1) the terms of a tenancy ( e.g., month-to-month tenancy), (2) the terms of a lease, or (3) a statutory requirement ( e.g., good cause requirement for nonrenewal of an assisted housing lease under federal statute and regulations, see Section III.A, above).

If a landlord actually or constructively evicts a tenant in violation of the tenant’s right to continued occupancy, the tenant may have a wrongful eviction claim under common law. Four of those commo n law claims are discussed in Sections XIV.B -E, below.

### B. Contract Claim, Landlord Removal of

Tenant Forcibly or by Demand A claim on the contract for wrongful eviction has three elements. First, the tenant was occupying the premises under a lease whose t erm had not expired. Second, the tenant was not in default of any lease term. Third, the landlord pursued a course of conduct that was intended to and did cause the tenant to vacate the premises.

Examples of wrongful evictions under this claim are summarized below.
(1) The landlord forcibly took possession of the premises. Ketcham v.

Miller, 104 Ohio St. 372, 136 N.E. 145 (1922).
(2) The landlord verbally ordered the tenant to vacate the premises and the tenant vacated. Butterfield v. Snively, 60 Ohio App. 14, 19 N.E.2d 284 (5th Dist. 1934); Wharton v. Pollock, 49 Ohio App. 443, 197 N.E. 379 (5th Dist. 1934).
(3) The landlord served the tenant with a written notice to leave the premises and the tenant vacated. Wehrley v. Sunchase Am., Ltd., No. CA99-11- 191, 2001 WL 88202 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 29, 2001); Roberts v.

GMS Mgmt. Co., No. 75419, 1999 WL 1068370 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 479
Nov. 24, 1999);321 Weintraub v. Hurley, No. 93-APG10-1473, 1994 WL 171138 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 3, 1994);Armstrong Stationary Co.
v. FGM Props., Inc. , No. C -830914, 1984 WL 7016 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Oct. 24, 1984); Teicher Theaters, Inc. v. Thompson, No. 83-CA- 29, 1983 WL 2530 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 28, 1983).

As these examples demonstrate, an action on the contract for w rongful eviction may be based on a constructive eviction ( i.e., a tenant’s vacation of the premises in response to the landlord’s order to vacate). Armstrong Stationary Co. v. FGM Props., Inc., No. C-830914, 1984 WL 7016, at *2 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Oct. 24, 1984). The tenant does not forfeit this claim for money damages in electing in such circumstances to vacate the premises, rather than remain on the premises in anticipation of asserting defenses in an eviction action. Id.

In an action on the contract for wrongful eviction, the tenant may recover general damages in an amount equal to the market value of the unexpired lease term and special damages to the extent they are permitted under contract law. Butterfield v. Snively, 60 Ohio App. 14, 19 N.E.2d 284 (5th Dist. 1934).

If the tenant leases another rental unit at a higher monthly rent as a result of the wrongful eviction, the tenant may recover the amount of the rent increase for the period of the unexpired lease term. E.g., Wehrley v. Sunchase Am., Ltd., No. CA99-11-191, 2001 WL 88202 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 29, 2001). However, the tenant is required to make reasonable efforts to mitigate these damages. Id.

The tenant may not recover punitive damages. Ketcham v. Miller, 104 Ohio St.

372, 136 N.E. 145 (1922). See also R.C. 2315.21(A)(1).

### C. Contract Claim, Constructive Eviction

Under common law, constructive eviction is “surrender of possession [of the premises] by the tenant on justifiable grounds.” Liberal Sav. & Loan Co. v. Frankel Realty Co., 137 Ohio St. 489, 498, 30 N.E.2d 1012 (1940). Justifiable grounds exist when there “is such a failure or interference on the part of the landlord with the intended enjoyment of the leased premises as to be of a substantial nature and so injurious to the tenant as to deprive him of the beneficial enjoyment of the leased 321 The Roberts decision seems to establish a fourth element for this claim, which does not exist in any of the other court decisions. It seems to require the tenant, before leaving the premises, to register a protest with landlord against the notice to leave the premises.

premises.” Nye v. Schuler, 110 Ohio App. 443, 445-46, 165 N.E.2d 16 (4th Dist. 1959).

There cannot be a constructive eviction if the tenant has not relinquished possession of the premises. Lexford Props., Inc. v. Schiltz, No. 2003-CA-00025, 2003 WL 22128788 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Sept. 8, 2003); Burnside v. Ickes, No. 02-COA- 015, 2003 WL 42455 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 3, 2003); Manifold v. Schuster , 67 Ohio App.3d 251, 586 N.E.2d 1142 (4th Dist. 1990).

There also cannot be a constructive eviction before the tenant’s lease term and occupancy begins. Harpel v. Pierce, Nos. 17163,17168, 1999 WL 55700 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 5, 1999).

A constructive eviction may arise from a lan dlord’s failure to remedy a defective condition at the premises. E.g., Stiffler v. Canterbury Run n Apts., No. 19308, 2002 WL 31243541, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 4, 2002) (“apartment was infiltrated with leaking water, sewage, growing mold, soaked carpets, and a sagging ceiling”);

Harpel v. Pierce, Nos. 17163,17168, 1999 WL 55700 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 5,
1999) (severe infestation of roaches, leaking pipes, exposed electrical wiring, and gas leaks in stove and furnace); Schutte v. Nicholson , N o. C-910261, 1992 WL 19396 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Feb. 5, 1992) (lack of heat for five days); Katzin v. Murad, No.

46553, 1984 WL 7150 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 5, 1984) (broken locks, broken glass in windows, sink pulling out from the walls, failure to c lean up garbage, and inoperative refrigerator); Keener v. Ewert, 67 Ohio App.2d 17, 425 N.E.2d 914 (6th
Dist. 1979) (lack of hot water, failure of bathtub to drain, basement flooded with water and debris, and a lockout); Katz v. Comisar , 28 Ohio N.P. (n.s. ) 10 (Ohio C.P., Hamilton Cty., 1930) (infestation of vermin).

A constructive eviction also may arise from a landlord’s refusal to provide the tenant with a copy of the lease, if it prevents the tenant from securing utility services for the premises. E.g., Proctor v. Frame, 90 Ohio Misc.2d 11, 695 N.E.2d 357 (Ohio Mun., Akron, 1998) (deprived of electrical service and, consequently, heat).

A constructive eviction cannot arise from the criminal activity of a third party, if the landlord provides reasonable security measures in the common areas (see Section VII.C.8, above) and the lease does not require the landlord to provide any additional security measures. Hawk v. Fairmeadows Apts., No. CA86-12-180, 1987 WL 13707 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 29, 1987).

A tenant’s constructive eviction claim against a landlord is a contract claim. Ma Chere Hair Style Acad. v. Rolo Co., No. L-85-287, 1986 WL 7111, at *3 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 20, 1986). “A constructive eviction is a breach of the landlord’s duty 481 imposed by the covenants of possession and quiet enjoyment.” Id.
