Therefore, in a constructive eviction claim, a tenant may recover compensatory damages but not punitive damages. Id., 1986 WL 7111, at *3,7.

### D. Tort Claim, Fraud and Deceit

A tort claim, predicated upon fraud and deceit, may be a claim for wrongful
eviction. The claim arises in the context of an eviction action where the landlord’s right to possession concerns the landlord’s intended use or renovation of the premises after the eviction action.

For example, under the Housing Choice Voucher program, “other good cause” for a termination of the tenancy includes a landlord’s decision to use the dwelling unit for personal or family use or for a purpose other than as a residential rental unit. 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.310(d)(1)(iii).

Similarly, under the Housing and Rent Act of 1949, a landlord was permitted to bring an eviction action only if the landlord was seeking in good faith: (1) to use the premises for personal or family use, or (2) to alter, remodel, or repair the premises, and the alteration, remodeling, or repair could not be done with the tenant in possession.

Calvin v. Martin, 64 Ohio Law Abs. 265, 268, 111 N.E.2d 786 (8th Dist. 1952).

As a general rule, common law requires that the intent of the landlord, in seeking to evict the tenant, be determined in the original eviction action. Id. at 273 -74.

However, when the eviction action concerns a landlord’s future use or renovation of the premises, the lan dlord’s intent cannot ultimately be ascertained until after the eviction action. Id. at 274. Contra Harrison v. Webb , 62 Ohio Law Abs. 273, 100 N.E.2d 728 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., 1951).

In response to this dilemma, the courts created a claim for wro ngful eviction that is an exception to the general rule. Calvin, 64 Ohio Law Abs. at 274 -75. Contra Harrison v. Webb, 62 Ohio Law Abs. 273, 100 N.E.2d 728 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., 1951). The elements of this wrongful eviction claim are an eviction ofa tenant, during the term of the tenant’s right of occupancy, procured by fraud and deceit. Calvin, 64 Ohio Law Abs. at 268-75.

The fraud and deceit in this wrongful eviction claim concern whether the landlord has misrepresented the reason for seeking p ossession of the premises. All of the elements necessary to maintain a fraud and deceit action at common law must be
present. Id. at 272-75.

A tenant’s vacation of the premises without protest, upon receipt of a notice to leave the premises, constitutes a waiver of this wrongful eviction claim. Id. at 273.

The general rules on tort damages govern this wrongful eviction claim. The tenant is permitted to recover all damages sustained as a proximate result of the wrongful
eviction. If the landlord acted with malice, the tenant is permitted to recover punitive damages under R.C. 2315.21.

And, if the tenant is awarded punitive damages, the tenant may also be entitled to an award of attorney’s fees under Ohio common law. See Section XIX.B, below.

### E. Tort Claim, Malicious Prosecution

Generally, the tort claim of malicious civil prosecution is no longer a viable claim for wrongful eviction due to the Supreme Court’s decision inRobb v. Chagrin Lagoons Yacht Club, Inc., 75 Ohio St.3d 264, 662 N.E.2d 9 (1996). In Robb, the Supreme Court clarified that an element of a malicious civil prosecution claim is seizure of the plaintiff’s person or property during the course of the prior proceeding. Id. at 269-70.

Since a judgment entered in favor of a tenant in an eviction action will prevent any seizure of the tenant’s property during the course of that proceeding, the tenant (potential plaintiff) generally cannot meet the seizure requirement for a malicious civil prosecution claim. E.g., Deli Table, Inc. v. Great Lakes Mall, No. 95-L-012, 1996 WL 761984, at *7 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 31, 1996) (applies Robb).

The Robb decision implicitly overrules the decision in Reenan v. Klein , 3 Ohio App.3d 142, 444 N.E.2d 63 (1st Dist. 1981), which upheld a malicious civil prosecution judgment in favor of a tenant without considering the seizure element. The basis for the judgment inReenan was four-fold: (1) the landlord, a recent purchaser of the property, knew of the valid lease executed by the prior owner and the tenant, (2) before filing the eviction action, the landlord had received the timely tendered rent check for the month for which the landlord alleged nonpayment of rent, (3) the landlord nonetheless filed the eviction action because he wanted possession of the prem ises, and, (4) in the eviction action, the court entered judgment for the tenant.

483

## XV. Tenant Claims for Wrongful

Eviction, 42 U.S.C. § 1983

### A. Police Assisting Landlord

A tenant may be wrongfully evicted by a person who is acting under color of state
law. This typically occurs when a private landlord unlawfully removes a tenant from the premises by force, with the assistance of a police officer. E.g., Soldal v. Cook County, Ill. , 506 U.S. 56 (1992); Thomas v. Cohen , 304 F.3d 563 (6th Cir. 2002);

Howerton v. Gabica, 708 F.2d 380 (9th Cir. 1983); Pellowski v. Burke, 686 F.2d 631 (8th Cir. 1982). See also Jones v. Gutschenritter , 909 F.2d 1208 (8th Cir. 1990) (termination of the electrical service to the premises). Cf. Revis v. Meldrum, 489 F.3d 273 (6th Cir. 2007) (seizure of judgment debtor’s residential property under a writ).

In these circumstances, the tenant may have one or two constitutional claims. One is a claim for a violation of the tenant’s procedural due process rights under the Fourteenth Amendment. The tenant’s right to possession of the premises under the tenancy is a “property interest” for Fourteenth Amendment purposes. Thomas v.

Cohen, 304 F.3d 563 (6th Cir. 2002). See also Revis v. Meldrum , 489 F.3d 273, 284 (6th Cir. 2007). Likewise, a tenant’s right under state law not to be evicted by force, except through a court proceeding, is a “property interest” for Fourteenth Amendment
purposes. Pellowski v. Burke, 686 F.2d 631 (8th Cir. 1982).

The second is a claim for a violation of the t enant’s rights under the Fourth Amendment’s protection against unreasonable seizure. Soldal v. Cook County, Ill. , 506 U.S. 56 (1992); Tinney v. Shores, 77 F.3d 378 (11th Cir. 1996). See also Thomas
v. Cohen, 304 F.3d 563 (6th Cir. 2002) (defendants grant ed qualified immunity, 2 -1 decision); Kernats v. O’Sullivan, 35 F.3d 1171 (7th Cir. 1994) (defendants granted qualified immunity). Cf. Harvey v. Plains Twp. Police Dep’t, 421 F.3d 185 (3d Cir.
2005) (police officer assisted former joint tenant recover pro perty from tenant’s apartment).

The tenant may bring these claims under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. The “under color of state law” element of the § 1983 claim is met if the police officer actively assisted in the forcible removal of the tenant, or if the police o fficer so intimidated the tenant as to cause the tenant to refrain from exercising the tenant’s legal right to resist the
removal. Jones v. Gutschenritter, 909 F.2d 1208, 1211-13 (8th Cir. 1990); Howerton
v. Gabica, 708 F.2d 380, 382 (9th Cir. 1983). Cf. Harvey v. Plains Twp. Police Dep’t, 421 F.3d 185 (3d Cir. 2005) (a police officer directed the landlord to open the door to enable a former joint tenant to enter the apartment).

The “under color of state law” element of the § 1983 claim is not met if thepolice officer is simply standing by in case of trouble. Id. See also Mann v. Hillsborough County Sheriff’s Office, 946 F. Supp. 962 (M.D. Fla. 1996). Similarly, it is not met if the police officer appears at the scene for a short time, and does not participate in the landlord’s attempt to forcibly remove the tenant from the premises.Corbin v. Stevens,
No. 91-Civ-1054, 1992 WL 96684 (S.D.N.Y. Apr. 30, 1992).

When the wrongful eviction of the tenant is the result of random and unauthorized acts of a subordinate police officer, under the Parratt doctrine,322 a procedural due process claim might not exist if there is an adequate state law remedy. E.g., Tinney v.

Shores, 77 F.3d 378 (11th Cir. 1996) (random acts, Parratt applicable); Scott v.

O’Grady, 760 F. Supp. 1288 (N.D. Ill. 1991) (not random acts, Parratt inapplicable), aff’d on other grounds, 975 F.2d 366 (7th Cir. 1992).

Under the joint participation doctrine, these § 1983 claims may be asserted against the police officer and the private landlord. Howerton v. Gabica, 708 F.2d 380 (9th Cir.

1983).

In these § 1983 claims, the tenant may recover compensatory damages and, for malicious, willful, or wanton misconduct, punitive damages. Pellowski v. Burke, 686 F.2d 631 (8th Cir. 1982).

If the facts available to the sheriff made the existence of a tenancy questionable, the sheriff may have a qualified immunity defense, based on the ground that the sheriff could not objectively, reasonably know that the sheriff’s action violated the Fourth or Fourteenth Amendment. See, e.g., Higgins v. Penobscot County Sheriff’s Dep’t, 446 F.3d 11 (1st Cir. 2006).

### B. City Code Enforcement

“Under Ohio law, tenants holding leasehold estates have a recognized property interest.” Jones v. City of Norwood , No. C -120237, 2013 WL 454909, ¶ 58 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Feb. 6, 2013). “This property interest invokes the protection of procedural due process.” Id., ¶ 59.

322 Parratt v. Taylor, 451 U.S. 527 (1981). See also Zinermon v. Burch, 494 U.S. 113 (1990);

Hudson v. Palmer, 468 U.S. 517 (1984); Logan v. Zimmerman, 455 U.S. 422 (1982).

485 Generally, due process requires notice and a hearing opportunity before a tenant may be evicted under a city’s code enforcement. Gardner v. Evans, 920 F.3d 1038, 1057-59 (6th Cir. 2019); Flatford v. City of Monroe, 17 F.3d 162, 167 (6th Cir. 1994);

Jones v. City of Norwood, No. C-120237, 2013 WL 454909, ¶ 59 (Ohio App., 1st Dist.,
Feb. 6, 2013).

“There are, however, ‘extraordinary circumstances where some valid governmental interest is at stake that justifies postponing the hearing until after [eviction].’” Flatford, 17 F.3d at 167 (quoting Fuentes v. Shevin , 407 U.S. 67, 82 (1972)). Accord Gardner v. Evans, 920 F.3d 1038, 1057-59 (6th Cir. 2019); Grayden
v. Rhodes, 345 F.3d 1225, 1227 (11th Cir. 2003) ; Jones v. City of Norwood , No. C- 120237, 2013 WL 4549 09, ¶ 60 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Feb. 6, 2013) . “Protecting citizens from an immediate risk of serious bodily harm falls squarely within those ‘extraordinary circumstances’ contemplated by Fuentes.” Flatford, 17 F.3d at 167.

But these exceptions to the general rule are rare. Jones, 2013 WL 454909, ¶ 60.

In Jones, the alleged overcrowding – the occupancy of two individuals in a one - bedroom apartment – did not pose an immediate threat to the safety of its occupants and thus did not fall within the exception to the general rule.

In Flatford, the Sixth Circuit granted city officials qualified immunity, on the due process prior hearing claim, because they had a reasonable basis to believe that the disrepair and dilapidation of the wooden -frame structure as a whole posed an immediate danger to its occupants and the public.

In Gardner, the Sixth Circuit applied its holding in Flatford that, absent code violations that pose an immediate danger, due process requires a pre -deprivation hearing before a tenant may be evicted under the city’s code enforcement. It thereupon held that, under the summary judgment record, the requisite immediate danger did not exist and therefore the inspectors were “neither immune from suit nor entitled to summary judgment on the pre -deprivation [hearing] claims. Gardner, 920 F.3d at 1059.

A tenant may enforce these due process rights under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. E.g., Gardner v. Evans, 920 F.3d 1038 (6th Cir. 2019); Flatford v. City of Monroe, 17 F.3d 162 (6th Cir. 1994); Jones v. City of Norwood, No. C-120237, 2013 WL 454909 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Feb. 6, 2013).

## XVI. Security Deposit


### A. Definition and Legal Status of Security

Deposits R.C. 5321.01(E) defines a security deposit as “any deposit of money or property to secure performance by the tenant under a rental agreem ent.” See also Small v.

Williams, No. 44418, 1982 WL 5247, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 25, 1982) (“a security deposit is held by a lessor to insure performance of all the lessee’s duties under the lease”).

“A security deposit, although it is in the possession and under the control of the landlord, is the property of the tenant.” Dovi Interests, Ltd. v. Somerset Point Ltd.

P’ship, No. 82788, 2004 WL 254211, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 12, 2004). The tenant possesses title to the security depos it. Id. A tenant retains title to the security deposit, subject to the rights and duties established in R.C. 5321.16. In re Ctr. Apts., Ltd., 277 B.R. 747 (Bankr. S.D. Ohio 2001) (applying Ohio law).

Security deposits “are a personal obligation between the landlord and the tenant in the form of a pledge.” Dovi Interests, Ltd. v. Somerset Point Ltd. P’ship, No. 82788, 2004 WL 254211, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 12, 2004). Accord Castlebrook,
Ltd. v. Dayton Props. Ltd. P’ship , 78 Ohio App.3d 340, 604 N.E.2d 808 (2nd Dist.

1992); Tuteur v. P. & F. Enters., Inc., 21 Ohio App.2d 122, 255 N.E.2d 284 (8th Dist.

1970).

### B. Distinction Between a Security Deposit

and Other Deposits/Fees

#### 1. Pet Deposits and Pet Fees

A refundable pet deposit, given to secure pe rformance by the tenant under the lease, is a security deposit. Zeallear v. F & W Props. , No. 99AP -1215, 2000 WL 1015345 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 25, 2000); Pool v. Insignia Residential Group , 136 Ohio App.3d 266, 736 N.E.2d 507 (1st Dist. 1999).

Generally, a non -refundable, one-time pet fee is not a security deposit. Kopp v.

Associated Estates Realty Corp., No. 09AP-719, 2010 WL 1510196 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 15, 2010); Stauffer v. TGM Camelot, Inc., No. CA2005-12-508, 2006 WL 487 1974612 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 17, 2006);Ritter v. Fairway Park Props., L.L.C.,
No. 21509, 2004 WL 1104015 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 19, 2004); Zeallear v. F & W Props., No. 99AP-1215, 2000 WL 1015345 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 25, 2000).

Because the deposit is non-refundable, its purpose is not to secure performance under the lease. Id. Rather, “the fee’s purpose [is] for the contractual right to keep pets in the apartment.” Kopp, 2010 WL 1510196, ¶ 21. Accord Stauffer, 2006 WL 1974612, ¶ 27.

But three court decisions have held that, if the landlord’s purpose for imposing the non-refundable, one-time pet fee is to recoup the cost of property damage that pets cause, it is a security deposit. Leszczynski v. Brewer , No. 12523, 1991 WL 285435 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 27, 1991); Pool v. Insignia Residential Group, 136 Ohio App.3d 266, 736 N.E.2d 507 (1st Dist. 1999); Moore v. CM Props., No. 01-CV-4475, 2002 WL 34170615 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., Mar. 6, 2002).323 A non-refundable addition to the monthly rent payment, for the right to keep a pet in the rental premises, is not a security deposit. Pool v. Insignia Residential Group , 136 Ohio App.3d 266, 736 N.E.2d 507 (1st Dist. 1999).

#### 2. Advance Payment of Last Month’s Rent

When a rental agreement requires a tenant to pay (and the tenant pays) the last month’s rent at the commencement of the tenancy, the advance payment of the last month’s rent is a security deposit. Hart v. Pervan , No. 79915, 2002 WL 31528735 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 14, 2002); Y ancey v. Haehn, No. 99 -G-2210, 2000 WL 263757 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 3, 2000).

#### 3. Application Payments

A fee paid to a landlord to hold a dwelling unit for a prospective tenant until a subsequent specified date, on which the tenant’s rental agreement (if any) wi ll commence, is not a security deposit. Kimmie v. Tillman , 66 Ohio App.3d 478, 585 N.E.2d 559 (6th Dist. 1990); Spurgeon v. Sanders, No. 1362, 1988 WL 35794 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 28, 1988); Walker v. Redd , No. 91 -CVG-15960 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 28, 1992).

323 In the tenant’s appeal from this decision, the court of appeals reversed the trial court’s denial of the ten ant’s motion for class certification. Moore v. CM Props. , No. 19388, 2003 WL 1424168 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 21, 2003). On remand, the parties settled the class claim.

Moore v. CM Props., No. 01-CV-4475 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., Dec. 14, 2004).

A deposit designated in a rental application as a “full security deposit on the above premises pending execution of a lease agreement” was not a security deposit, where the prospective tenant elected not to sign the lease. Turetsky v. Miller, No. CA96-03- 005, 1996 WL 468758 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Aug. 19, 1996). As a result, the owner was not entitled to retain the deposit. Id.

“Earnest money” paid to a landlord is not a security deposit. Hartman v. Garden Woods Apts., No. 15228, 1995 WL 628021 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 25, 1995). In this context, earnest money means a payment to the landlord that is: (1) refundable if the landlord rejects the tenant’s application, (2) forfeited as liquidated damages if the landlord approves the tenant’s application and the tenant refuses to execute a lease for the premises, and (3) converted to a security deposit if the landlord approves the tenant’s application and the tenant executes a lease for the premises. Id. See also Gough v. 1031 Props., LLC, No. CA2014-09-196, 2015 WL 4207115 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 13, 2015).

These liquidated damage provisions are enforceable only if the amoun t of the deposit bears a reasonable relationship to the landlord’s actual damages. Robertson v.

Rossing, No. CA98 -02-035, 1999 WL 59698 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 8, 1999);

Williams v. Mark IV Apts., No. 65915, 1994 WL 521136 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept .

22, 1994).

Court decisions upholding the forfeiture of the deposit include: Hartman v.

Garden Woods Apts. , No. 15228, 1995 WL 628021 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 25,
1995) ($280 deposit – one-half of one month’s rent); Williams v. Mark IV Apts. , No.

65915, 1994 WL 521136 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 22, 1994) ($595 deposit – one month’s rent).

Court decisions rejecting the forfeiture of the deposit include: Robertson v.

Rossing, No. CA98 -02-035, 1999 WL 59698, at *3 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Feb. 8,
1999) (“the $200 amount bears no reasonable relation to actual damage suffered by appellee [landlord]”); Supinski v. Gold Coast Props. , No. 95-CVI-3026 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, July 15, 1996) (prospective tenant withdrew application one day after signing it and before landlord had accepted it, and landlord did not suffer any damage as a result of prospective tenant’s actions).

489

#### 4. Non-Refundable Redecorating or General

Maintenance Fee A non-refundable, one-time fee for the initial preparation of the premises, which the tenant pays at the commencement of the tenancy, is not a security deposit. Kopp
v. Associated Estates Realty Corp. , No. 09AP -719, 2010 WL 1510196 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 15, 2010);Sokol v. Sine, No. 98-T-0155, 1999 WL 959872 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 30, 1999).

A non-refundable, one-time fee for general maintenance during the tenancy, which the tenant pays at the commencement of the tenancy, is not a security deposit. Sokol
v. Sine, No. 98-T-0155, 1999 WL 959872 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 30, 1999).

#### 5. Non-Refundable Premium for Surety Bond

Some landlords offer tenants an option to purchase a surety bond, from a designated third party (“bond company”), in lieu of a security deposit payment to the landlord (“deposit payment”). E.g., Kopp v. Ass ociated Estates Realty Corp. , No.

09AP-719, 2010 WL 1510196 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 15, 2010).

To obtain the surety bond, the tenant pays a non -refundable premium to the bond
company. The bond premium is less than the deposit payment. For example,in Kopp, the bond premium was $437 .50 and the deposit payment was $850.00 (one month’s rent).

Under the surety bond, at the end of the tenancy, the landlord is reimbursed by the bond company (instead of from a security deposit) for the landlord’s unpaid rent and/or property damage claims against the tenant. The amount of the reimbursement cannot exceed the bond’s coverage (e.g., in Kopp, $2,500.00). The tenant is then liable to the bond company for the amount of the reimbursement payment to the landlord. The tenant does not receive an opportunity to dispute the landlord’s claim before the bond company pays it.324 In Kopp, the court held that the bond premium is not a security deposit for two
reasons. First, the tenant elects to purchase the surety bond (at a lower cost) in lieu of a refundable deposit payment (at a higher cost). Second, the premium is not paid to or 324 In Kopp, the court held that this surety bond system is not unconscionable under R.C.

5321.14 because the tenant has the option to pay a security deposit.

held by the landlord.325 It also held that the R.C. 5321.16(B) itemization requirement does not apply because “the parties chose to contract for something other than a security deposit.” Kopp, 2010 WL 1510196, ¶ 9.

#### 6. Deposit for Option to Purchase

When a purchase agreement between a landlord and tenant establishes the right of the tenant to purchase the premises on a future date at an agreed upon price, and requires the tenant to pay a deposit on the purchase agreement in order to preserve this option to purchase, the deposit is not a security deposit. Knoppe v. Applegate , No.

08CAG-08-0051, 2009 WL 1145073 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 27, 20 09) ($5,000 deposit); Delker v. Kerr, No. L-96-110, 1996 WL 532319 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Sept.

20, 1996) ($2,000 deposit);Stacy v. Zupp, No. CA-9566, 1994 WL 369899 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 20, 1994) ($1,500 deposit). Cf. Dye v. Ley , No. 1 -88-60, 1990 WL 11444 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Feb. 7, 1990) ($20,000 deposit – denied tenant’s unconscionability claim); Powell v. Castner, No. 11763, 1984 WL 7651 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec. 19, 1984) ($2,000 deposit – denied tenant’s penalty and un just enrichment claims).

### C. Tenant Cancels Agreement Before

Occupancy, Security Deposit Rights When a prospective tenant cancels the rental agreement before the tenant has commenced occupancy of the premises, and the reason for the cancellation is the landlord’s failure to make necessary or agreed repairs to the premises, the R.C.

5321.16(B) requirements apply to the tenant’s security deposit. Gladden v. Mitchell,
No. C -040359, 2005 WL 323685 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Feb. 11, 2005); Mayer v.

McGreevy, No. 99AP-707, 2000 WL 329043 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 30, 2000).

See also Riegel v. Huber Inv. Corp., No. 87AP-368, 1987 WL 18205 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Oct. 6, 1987).

A landlord’s failure to make such repairs can be a valid basis for terminating the 325 This prerequisite for a security deposit is consistent with Ohio common law on the legal status of security deposits. See Section XVI.A, above. But the R.C. 5321.01(D) definition of security deposit, on its face, does not include it. Query whether R.C. 5321.01(D) supplants Ohio common law on this issue.

491 tenancy on or before its commencement date. Mayer v. McGreevy , No. 99AP -707, 2000 WL 329043 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 30, 2000) (R.C. 5321.04 violation made the premises uninhabitable and, therefore, voided the tenant’s contractual obligations under the leas e); Harpel v. Pierce , Nos. 17163,17168, 1999 WL 55700 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 5, 1999) (defective conditions may constitute an anticipatory repudiation of the lease by the landlord); Shoemaker v. Whitt, 129 Ohio App.3d 591, 718 N.E.2d 932 (2nd Dist. 1998) (a termination of the lease under R.C. 5321.07(B)(3) may be effective the first day of the lease term);Riegel v. Huber Inv. Corp., No. 87AP- 368, 1987 WL 18205 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Oct. 6, 1987) (tenant’s agreement to lease the house was contingent on the landlord’s completion of specified repairs and replacements by the lease’s commencement date).

When a prospective tenant cancels the rental agreement before the tenant has commenced occupancy of the premises, and the landlord has complied with all of the landlord’s relevant duties, generally the R.C. 5321.16(B) requirements are still applicable to the tenant’s security deposit. Christe v. GMS Mgmt. Co. , 124 Ohio App.3d 84, 705 N.E.2d 691 (9th Dist. 1997); Simone v. Hill’n Dale Homes, No. 89-B- 9, 1989 WL 158232 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Dec. 29, 1989); Dearwester v. Lagos, 33 Ohio App.3d 199, 514 N.E.2d 1136 (3rd Dist. 1986).

However, in these circumstances, the case law is split on whether the R.C.

5321.16(B) requirements apply to the tenant’s securitydeposit if the prospective tenant never received possession ( e.g., keys) of the premises. Compare Christe v. GMS
Mgmt. Co., 124 Ohio App.3d 84, 705 N.E.2d 691 (9th Dist. 1997) (applicable without possession), and Simone v. Hill’n Dale Homes, No. 89-B-9, 1989 WL 158232 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Dec. 29, 1989) with Kanistros v. Holeman , No. 20528, 2005 WL 388178 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 18, 2005) (not applicable without possession),Cox
v. Parish, 66 Ohio App.3d 138, 583 N.E.2d 1039 (8th Dist. 1990),Sand v. Prokos, No.

95-CA-1664, 1996 WL 159687 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 27, 1996), and Geis v.

Zylka, 70 Ohio Misc.2d 28, 650 N.E.2d 211 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., 1995).

In Cox, Sand, and Geis, the inapplicability of R.C. 5321.16(B) to the tenant’s security deposit deprived the tenants of the right to recover statutory damages and reasonable attorney’s fees under R.C. 5321.16(C). The courts still awarded the tenants judgments for the amount of their security deposits. In Kanistos, the court construed the deposit as earnest money that the owner was entitled to retain.

### D. Amount of the Security Deposit

Ohio law does not impose any limit on the amount of the security deposit that a landlord may require a tenant to pay.326 However, the amount of the security deposit determines whether the deposit must bear interest payable to the tenant. See Section XVI.E, below.

### E. Interest on the Security Deposit Payable

to the Tenant A landlord is obligated to make interest payments to the tenant if the amount of the security deposit exceeds one month’s rent or fifty dollars, whichever is greater, and the deposit is held at least six months. R.C. 5321.16(A). See, e.g., Leak v. Hale, No.

2006-CVG-33367 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 10, 2008) ($63.60 interest, for 24 months).

In such cases, the landlord must pay annually to the tenant interest at the rate of five percent per year on the amount of the security deposit that exceeds one month’s rent (or fifty dollars). R.C. 5321.16(A). See also Hart v. Per van, No. 79915, 2002 WL 31528735 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 14, 2002); Williams v. Glaser III , No. C- 000539, 2001 WL 395165 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Apr. 20, 2001);Yancey v. Haehn, No.

99-G-2210, 2000 WL 263757 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 3, 2000);Mentor Lagoons,
Inc. v. Mayor , No. 10 -180, 1985 WL 7807 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 1, 1985);

Malcom v. Tate , No. 99 -CVH-21689 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., June 29, 2001).

If the amount of a security deposit exceeds fifty dollars but not one month’s rent, the tenant is not entitled to receive interest on the security deposit. Bonesteel v. Nash, 65 N.E.3d 326, 2016-Ohio-3114 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., 2016); Murry v. Heather Hill Apts., No. L-91-155, 1991 WL 280128 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 27, 1991).

For assisted housing tenants,327 the R.C. 5321.16(A) term “one month’s periodic rent” means the monthly contract rent, not the tenant’s portion of the contract rent.

326 In some of the assisted housing programs, federal law limits the amount of the security
deposit. For a discussion on those limitations, see National Housing Law Project, HUD Housing Programs: Tenants’Rights § 6.2 (5th ed. 2018).

327 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, above.

493 Harris v. Hardnick, No. 07-CVI-00705 (Ohio Mun., East Cleveland, July 23, 2007);

H-1 Mgmt. LLC v . Perryman, No. 2006 -CVG-28200 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., July 10, 2007).

### F. Maintenance/Use of the Security Deposit,

During the Tenancy R.C. Chapter 5321 does not impose any restrictions or requirements on a landlord’s maintenance or use of a secur ity deposit during the tenancy. Mustic v. Mustic , No.

87AP-318, 1987 WL 32174 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 29, 1987).

For example, it does not require a landlord to maintain a separate account for the security deposit. Shoregate Towers Partners L.L.C. v. Antebi, No. 109817, 2021 WL 3416980, ¶ 128 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 5, 2021) (“We therefore agree with appellants that there is no legal duty to segregate tenants’ security deposits by themselves in a separate account”); Mustic v. Mustic, No. 87AP-318, 1987 WL 32174 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 29, 1987) . And it does not require a landlord to use a tenant’s security deposit to pay the tenant’s rent arrearage so as to avoid the tenant’s default on the lease. Small v. Williams, No. 44418, 1982 WL 5247 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 25, 1982).

### G. Use/Return of the Security Deposit,

After the Tenancy

#### 1. General Rule

At the end of the tenancy, the landlord may apply the security deposit “to the payment of any past due rent and to the payment of the amount of damages that the landlord has suffered by reason of the tenant’s noncompliance with Section 5321.05 of the Revised Code or the rental agreement.”328 R.C. 5321.16(B).

“[W]ithin thirty days after termination of the rental agreement and delivery of possession,” the landlord is required to return the security deposit to the tenant and/or provide the tenant with a written itemization of any portion of the security deposit that 328 For a discussion on landlord claims for past due rent, see Chapter XVII, below. For a discussion on landlord claims under R.C. 5321.05(C) for property damage, see Chapter XVIII, below.

the landlord retains. Id.

The tenant is required to provide the landlord with a forwarding address to which the security deposit and/or written itemization may be sent. Id.

#### 2. Landlord Option on Applying the Deposit

R.C. 5321.16(B) provides a landlord with the right to apply the security deposit (in whole or in part) to the payment of past due rent and to the payment of damages caused by the tenant’s violation of R.C. 5321.05 or the rental agreement. See Vardeman v.

Llewellyn, 17 Ohio St.3d 24, 476 N.E.2d 1038 (1985).

The election to exercise this right is a discretionary decision of the landlord. R.C.

5321.16(B) permits a landlord, but does not require a landlord, to apply the tenant’s security deposit to the tenant’s rent or damage liability. Papadakes v. Woodall, No.

47848, 1984 WL 195552 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 29, 1984);Small v. Williams, No.

44418, 1982 WL 5247 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 25, 1982).

For example, even though the amount of a tenant’s security deposit is larger than the amount of the tenant’s unpaid rent, the landlord may bring an action for a money judgment for the unpaid rent.329 Papadakes, 1984 WL 195552, at *1-2. In such cases, the burden is on the tenant to assert the security deposit as a set -off against the rent
claim. See Salem v. Roberts, No. 94-CA-28, 1996 WL 131160 (Ohio App., 4th Dist.,
Mar. 18, 1996).

However, in these circumstances, t he landlord is still required under R.C.

5321.16(B) to deliver to the tenant the security deposit (less any permissible
deductions) and the requisite written itemization (if applicable) within thirty days after the termination of the rental agreement and delivery of possession. See Campus Vill.

Toledo Univ. Park, LLC v. Mowrer, 68 N.E.3d 219, 2016-Ohio-4754 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 2016); Papadakes, 1984 WL 195552, at *2.

#### 3. Written Itemization

“[A]ny security deposit deduction must be itemized.” Nolan v. Sutton , 97 Ohio App.3d 616, 619, 647 N.E.2d 218 (1st Dist. 1994).

329 However, if the amount of the landlord’s R.C. 5321.05(C) damage claim is less than the amount of the tenant’s security deposit, and the tenant has forfeited the security deposit, the landlord has not suffered any “actual damages” under R.C. 5321.05(C) (see Section XVIII.E.4,
below) and, therefore, is not entitled to recover R.C. 5321.05(C) attorney’s fees (see Section XVIII.D, below).

495 The purpose of the itemization requirement “is to make landlords give reasons to tenants why their security deposit is being withheld so that tenants may decide whether to pursue an action for recovery of that security deposit.” McNeill v. Wasserbauer ,
No. 72846, 1998 WL 323592, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 18, 1998). Accord Senz v. Onstott , 71 Ohio Misc.2d 67, 69, 654 N.E.2d 460 (Ohio Mun., Wadsw orth, 1994). See also Vardeman v. Llewellyn, 17 Ohio St.3d 24, 28, 476 N.E.2d 1038 (1985).

However, “there is nothing in the R.C. Chapter 5321 that states that the landlord upon the request of the former tenant must let the tenant re-enter the premises to verify the damages claimed by the landlord.” Scott v. Rice, No. 9-92-48, 1993 WL 46480, at *3 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Feb. 11, 1993).

Three court decisions, which are discussed below, illustrate the accuracy and specificity required for the itemization.

One court held that, in preparing an R.C. 5321.16(B) itemization of the cost to repair the premises, a landlord may rely on estimates from local advertisements, provided such advertisements are used in good faith. Senz v. Onstott, 71 Ohio Misc.2d 67, 69, 654 N.E.2d 460 (Ohio Mun., Wadsworth, 1994).

A second court held that the statement “$40 -cleaning” does not meet the itemization requirement under R.C. 5321.16(B) because it fails to specify the reason for the cleaning charge. Nolan v. Sutton, 97 Ohio App.3d 616, 619, 647 N.E.2d 218 (1st Dist. 1994).

A third court held that the required specificity was not met by a notice that stated:

2-Times Mowing Yard $ 40.00 Wal Mart 112.59 Rug Shampooer 29.66 Cleaning Lady 75.00 257.25 Schaedler v. Shinkle, No. CA99-09-025, 2000 WL 1283776, at *1 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Sept. 11, 2000). This notice was deficient because it “did not explain what he [landlord] had bought or why the items were necessary.” Id.

A landlord’s failure to comply with th e R.C. 5321.16(B) written itemization requirement does not preclude the landlord from bringing an action against the tenant under R.C. 5321.05. See Section XVII.C.8, below.

Likewise, it is irrelevant to the wrongful withholding determination under R.C.

5321.16(C). See Section XVI.H.2, below.

#### 4. Thirty-Day Period

A landlord’s thirty-day period to return the security deposit and/or provide notice and an itemization of deductions from the security deposit is not relieved or suspended merely because the landlord has a pending monetary claim against the tenant. Davis
v. Wesolowski, 146 N.E.3d 633, 2020 -Ohio-677, ¶ 22 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2020).

The requirement to abide by R.C. 5321.16(B) is “separate and distinct from a landlord’s claim for any unpaid rent orpremises damaged.” Sherwin v. Cabana Club Apts., 70 Ohio App.2d 11, 18, 433 N.E.2d 932 (8th Dist. 1980). Accord Davis v.

Wesolowski, 146 N.E.3d 633, ¶ 22.

When the date on which the tenant returns the keys to the landlord is subsequent to the date on w hich the tenant vacates the premises, “delivery of possession” occurs when the keys are returned. Katzin v. Murad, No. 46553, 1984 WL 7150 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 5, 1984). Cf. Griffin v. Currie, No. 4065, 1988 WL 126385 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Nov. 25, 1988) (tenant liable to landlord for rent for the month after tenant had vacated because tenant returned the keys on the second day of that month).

When a month-to-month tenant vacates the premises without serving the landlord with an R.C. 5321.17(B) thirty-day notice of termination, the R.C. 5321.16(B) thirty - day period begins to run on the date that the tenant’s obligation to pay rent ends.

Hashemi v. Jim Owen & Co., No. 76AP-879, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 8510 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 21, 1977).

A landlord satisfies the R.C. 5321.16(B) thirty-day requirement if, on or before the thirtieth day, the landlord mails the security deposit and/or written itemization to the tenant’s forwarding address with proper postage. Goldstein v. Spitz, 30 Ohio App.3d 246, 507 N.E.2d 433 (1st Dist. 1986); Paxton v. McGranahan, No. 49645, 1985 WL 6444 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1985). When a landlord timely mails the security deposit and/or written itemization, R.C. 5321.16(B) does not require that the tenant actually receive them within the thirty-day period. Id.

#### 5. Forwarding Address

R.C. 5321.16(B) states that the tenant shall provide the landlord with the forwarding address “in writing.” “Although the statute requires a tenant to provide the landlord in writing a forwarding address, for equity’s sake, the courts have avoided a hypertechnical application of the statute.” Jensen v. Boulevard Invs., Ltd. , No. 103 658, 2016 WL 4242244, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 11, 2016).

497 “Under the existing liberal construction of thestatute, the courts have held that the forwarding address requirement is met and the court must give effect to the penalties section of R.C. 5321.16(C) where the landlord has actual knowledge of the tenant’s new address such that compliance with the landl ord’s duties pursuant to R.C.

5321.16(B) is possible.” 330 Jensen v. Boulevard Invs., Ltd. , No. 103 658, 2016 WL 4242244, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 11, 2016).

In these circumstances, the court s have not required a written notice of the forwarding address. E.g., K&D Mgmt., L.L.C. v. Jones , No. 110262, 2021 WL 5834382 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2021);Amon v. Keagy, No. 2008-T-0033, 2009 WL 2356831 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 31, 2009); Adams v. Davenport, No. 2005- CA-108, 2006 WL 2578852 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 8, 2006); Brown v. Dobbins,
No. C-050089 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Nov. 30, 2005); Smitson v. Zeches , No. 92AP- 1773, 1993 WL 317243 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Aug. 17, 1993);331 Mahoney v. Abood,
No. L-92-230, 1992 WL 337589 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 20, 1992);Cristal v. DRC Int’l, Inc., 74 Ohio App.3d 493, 599 N.E.2d 706 (8th Dist. 1991); Baron v. Buckner,
No. CA90-10-071, 1991 WL 69355 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Apr. 29, 1991); Prescott
v. Makowski, 9 Ohio App.3d 155, 458 N.E.2d 1281 (8th Dist. 1983). But see Fowler
v. Hellman, No. 1-85-45, 1986 WL 15048 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Dec. 30, 1986);Carr
v. Ed Stein Realtors, 10 Ohio App.3d 242, 461 N.E.2d 930 (9th Dist. 1983).

Courts have also held that the forwarding address requirement is met and the R.C.

5321.16(C) penalties are applicable if the landlord has contact information for the tenant’s lawyer or other reasonable avenues to contact the former tenant. Clarke v.

Royal, No. 20AP -366, 2021 WL 2443751 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 15, 2021);

Jensen v. Boulevard Invs., Ltd., No. 103 658, 2016 WL 4242244 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 330 One appellate court was unwilling to extend this rule beyond the circumstances in which the landlord has actual knowledge of the tenant’s forwarding address. Waldenmyer Enters. v.

Bolek, No. 86AP-100070, 1987 WL 11872 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 28, 1987). InBolek, the landlord had reasonable grounds to believe, but not actual knowledge, that the tenant could be contacted through a local business.

Another appellate court held that the landlord did not have the requisite knowledge of the forwarding address via the tenant “simply leaving behind, on the kitchen counter [in the premises], a piece of c ardboard with an address scrawled upon it.” Seymour v. Ernsthausen ,
No. F-85-7, 1986 WL 1887, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 14, 1986).

331 On this issue,Smitson implicitly overrules the decision inGreen v. Northwood Terrace Apts.,
No. 78AP-580, 1979 Ohio App. LEXIS 12352 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 20, 1979).
Aug. 11, 2016); Oldendick v. Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016-Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); Wehrley v. Sunchase Am., Ltd., No. CA99-11-191, 2001 WL 88202 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 29, 2001).

Court decisions that illustrate some of those acceptable methods are summarized below.
(1) Two weeks before the tenant’s delivery of possession to the landlord, the tenant’s counsel had filed with the trial court and had served on the landlord a written notice of appearance with his mailing address.Clarke
v. Royal, No. 20AP-366, 2021 WL2443751 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 15, 2021).
(2) The landlord knew that the tenant was represented by counsel, the landlord knew the counsel’s contact information, and the landlord had utilized that contact information with respect to that representation .

Jensen v. Boulevard Invs., Ltd. , No. 103658, 2006 WL 4242244 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 11, 2016).
(3) The tenant’s counsel, in a letter to the landlord, directed the landlord to send the security deposit to the tenant in care of her attorney.Oldendick
v. Crocker , 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016 -Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016).
(4) Providing the landlord with writt en notice that states the name and address of the tenant’s lawyer and that future communications from the landlord to the tenant should be directed to the tenant’s lawyer.Wehrley
v. Sunchase Am., Ltd., No. CA99-11-191, 2001 WL 88202 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 29, 2001).
(5) Mailing the forwarding address to the landlord by certified mail, notwithstanding the landlord’s failure to claim the certified mail. Ely v.

Schroder, No. C-990426, 2000 WL 145334 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Feb.

11, 2000).
(6) Providing a writ ten notice from the tenant’s lawyer to the landlord’s lawyer that states that the tenant may be contacted in care of the lawyer’s
office. Begue v. Henson, No. 16975, 1995 WL 338427 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 7, 1995).
(7) Providing written notice of the forw arding address to the landlord’s roommate, who had apparent authority to act for the landlord for purposes of receiving tenant notices. Ridenour v. Neufer , 64 Ohio App.3d 453, 581 N.E.2d 1152 (9th Dist. 1989).
(8) Recording the tenant’s address in the initialwritten lease, when it is not 499 the address of the rental unit because the tenant entered into the lease to enable another person ( e.g., the tenant’s relative) to occupy the rental
unit. Pietrykowski v. Hamblin , No. WD -84-86, 1985 WL 7092 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 12, 1985).
(9) Providing the landlord with the tenant’s business card. Pearson v.

Huber Inv. Corp., No. 84AP-526, 1985 WL 9913 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Mar. 21, 1985).
(10) Providing the manager with an oral statement of the tenant’s forwarding address, which the manager then writes on the landlord’s office rolodex.

Bennett v. Huber Inv. Corp., No. 84AP-268, 1984 WL 5990 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 15, 1984), rev’d on other grounds, 17 Ohio St.3d 33, 476 N.E.2d 1045 (1985).
(11) Recording the address of the tenant’s guarantor in the initial written lease, if the tenant does or would receive mail sent to that address after the tenant’s vacation of the premises. Boldan v. Suburban Apt. Mgmt.

Co., No. 47089, 1984 WL 4503 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 2, 1984).
(12) Providing the landlord with a notice of the address of the tenant’s attorney as a result of ongoing litigation between the tenant and the
landlord. Peake v. Thrower , No. 88 -CVG-2325 (Ohio Mun., Akron,
Mar. 2, 1989).

R.C. 5321.16(B) does not specify a time p eriod within which the tenant must provide the landlord with the forwarding address.

One appellate court has held that the time period is a reasonable time and, under this standard, the landlord must be given adequate time to deliver the security deposit and/or written itemization within the R.C. 5321.16(B) thirty -day period. Janos v.

Justice, No. 80AP-31, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 10870 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 19, 1980). See also Grove v. Turman , No. 96 -CA-1728, 1997 WL 173874 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 28, 1997) (untimely where served eight months after tenant had vacated); McGlaughlin v. Wallace F. Ackley Co. , No. 79AP -817, 1980 Ohio App.

LEXIS 13428 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 13, 1980) (untimely where served after the R.C. 5321.16(B) thirty-day period had expired).

The tenant’s compliance (or noncompliance) with the R.C. 5321.16(B) requirement to provide a forwarding address determines whether the tenant may recover statutory damages and reasonable attorney’s fees under R.C. 5321.16(C), but not whether the tenant may recover the security deposit. See Section XVI.H.1, below.

### H. Tenant Claim for the Security Deposit

and Related Relief

#### 1. Overview

“R.C. 5321.16 is a remedial statute intended to compensate the tenant for a wrongfully withheld deposit at no expense to the tenant.” Klein v. Moutz, 118 Ohio St.3d 256, 888 N.E.2d 404, ¶ 13 (2008). “[A]n important objective of the statute” is “to ensure that the tenant incurs no expense when seeking return of the deposit wrongfully withheld.” Id., ¶ 17. Accord Christe v. GMS Mgmt. Co. , 88 Ohio St.3d 376, 378, 726 N.E.2d 497 (2000).

If the landlord retains the tenant’s security deposit (in whole or in part) in violation of R.C. 5321.16(B) and the tenant has provided the landlord with a forwarding address in co mpliance with R.C. 5321.16(B), the tenant may bring an action against the landlord for: (1) the portion of the security deposit that has been wrongfully withheld,
(2) statutory damages in an amount equal to the portion of the security deposit that has been wrongfully withheld, and (3) reasonable attorney’s fees. R.C. 5321.16(C).

If the landlord retains the tenant’s security deposit (in whole or in part) in violation of R.C. 5321.16(B) and the tenant has not provided the landlord with a forwarding address in compliance with R.C. 5321.16(B), the tenant may bring an action against the landlord for the portion of the security deposit that has been wrongfully withheld, but not for statutory damages or reasonable attorney’s fees under R.C. 5321.16(C).

Vardeman v. Llewellyn, 17 Ohio St.3d 24, 27, 476 N.E.2d 1038 (1985).332 332 See also Oldendick v. Crocker , 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016 -Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); Burnside v. Ickes , No. 02 -COA-015, 2003 WL 42455 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 3, 2003); Kelley v. Johnston , No. 01 -CA-5, 2001 WL 1479243 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Nov. 14, 2001); Nelson v. Tipton , No. 99AP -277, 1999 WL 1041154 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 18, 1999); Boice v. Emshoff, No. 13-98-23, 1998 WL 833686 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Dec. 3, 1998);

Grove v. Turman, No. 96-CA-1728, 1997 WL 173874 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 28, 1997);

Lewis v. Urquhart, No. CA95-07-118, 1996 WL 103770 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Mar. 11, 1996);

Josselyn v. Arthur, No. 12175, 1991 WL 60637 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 10, 1991); Fowler
v. Hellman, No. 1-85-45, 1986 WL 15048 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Dec. 30, 1986); McGlaughlin
v. Wallace F. Ackley Co., No. 79AP-817, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 13428 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Nov. 13, 1980).

501

#### 2. Wrongful Withholding

In an R.C. 5321.16(C) claim, the determination on whether the withheld portion of the security deposit is wrongfully withheld is based solely on whether the landlord has a substantive right to retain it (i.e., whether the tenant owes the landlord for unpaid rent and/or damages in an amount equal to or greater than the withheld portion of the security deposit).

By virtue of the rulings in Smith v. Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987), and Vardeman v. Llewellyn , 17 Ohio St.3d 24, 476 N.E.2d 1038 (1985), whether the landlord has complied with the R.C. 5321.16(B) written itemization requirement is irrelevant to the R.C. 5321.16(C) wrongful withholding determination.

In Padgett, the court held that, when a landlord has provided a tenant with a written itemization of the withheld portion of the security deposit and has provided the itemization timely and in good faith, it is still a wrongful withholding if the court ultimately finds that the landlord did not have a substantive right to retain it.333 In Vardeman, the court held that, when a landlord has a substantive right to retain the withheld portion of the security deposit, the landlord’s failure to provide thetenant with a written itemization does not cause the withholding to be a wrongful withholding.334 333 See also Warner v. Evans , No. 27536, 2015 WL 3398400 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 27, 2015); Spring Hill Townhomes v. Pounds, No. 25887, 2014 WL 1887690 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 9, 2014); Budler v. Empire Mgmt. Group, No. 22376, 2005 WL 1026697 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 4, 2005); Williams v. Glaser III, No. C-000539, 2001 WL 395165 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Apr. 20, 2001); Christe v. GMS Mgmt. Co. , 124 Ohio App.3d 84, 705 N.E.2d 691 (9th
Dist. 1997); Gainer v. Hunker, No. 17153, 1995 WL 553199 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 20, 1995); Feldmann v. Ponziani, No. 14857, 1995 WL 232476 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 21, 1995); Fitz v. Dienes, No. 56172, 1989 WL 139572 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 16, 1989).

334 See also Amba Invs., LLC v. Clark, No. CA2021-02-016, 2022 WL 94540 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 10, 2022); Williams v. McMillian, No. 107570, 2019 WL 3855356 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 15, 2019); Nieto v. Marcellino, No. 2017-G-0146, 2018 WL 6445474 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 10, 2018); DePaz v. Bahramian , Nos. C-130128,130317, 2013 WL 6672913 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Dec. 18, 2013);Moore-El v. Petrella, No. 94749, 2011 WL 553877 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 17, 2011); Knipp v. Sadler, No. 6-09-04, 2009 WL 2710053 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 31, 2009); Schaedler v. Shinkle , No. CA99-09-025, 2000 WL 12 83776 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Sept. 11, 2000); Korom v. Pesek, No. 57559, 1990 WL 130909 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 13, 1990).

#### 3. Statutory Damages

If any portion of the security deposit is wrongfully withheld, the landlord’s liability for statutory damages under R.C. 5321.16(C) is mandatory. Smith v. Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987).335 A tenant’s failure to demand the statutory damages in the pleadings does not waive the tenant’s right to recover them. Ohio R. Civ. P. 54(C). 336 E.g., Leszczynski v.

Brewer, No. 12523, 1991 WL 285435 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 27, 1991). Cf.

Morales v. Windsor Realty & Mgmt., Inc. , No. 2000 -CVH-002037 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 21, 2000) (R.C. 5321.16(C) attorney’s fees).

The landlord’s good faith in withholding the security deposit is not a defense to the tenant’s claim for statutory damages. Smith v. Padgett , 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 513 335 See also Klemas v. Flynn, 66 Ohio St.3d 249, 611 N.E.2d 810 (1993); Clarke v. Royal, No.

20AP-366, 2021 WL 2443751 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 15, 2021); Alcoroso v. Correll, 159 N.E.3d 924, 2020 -Ohio-4752 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2020); Filby v. Stocker Dev., LLC , No.

2017-AP06-0020, 2017 WL 6371312 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 11, 2017); Oldendick v.

Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016 -Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); Spring Hill Townhomes v. Pounds , No. 25887, 2014 WL 1887690 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 9, 2014);

Manninen v. Alvarez, No. CA2013-06-106, 2014 WL 107852 (Ohio App., 12th Dis t., Jan. 13, 2014); Bacon v. Atlas Home Corp., No. 22471, 2005 WL 3557129 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Dec.

30, 2005); Budler v. Empire Mgmt., No. 22376, 2005 WL 1026697 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 4, 2005); Compton v. Spinks, No. CA2003-08-019, 2004 WL 1543201 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 12, 2004); Munsie v. Welkener , No. CA96 -08-080, 1997 WL 374739 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 7, 1997); Buck v. Georgian Manor Invs., No. 67170, 1995 WL 143813 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 30, 1995); Ulle-Mance v. Berardinelli, No. 92-L-210, 1993 WL 407262 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993); Trepka v. Blake, No. 61633, 1993 WL 12255 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 21, 1993); Chaney v. Dominque, No. 91-WD-099, 1992 WL 163903 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., July 17, 1992);Calhoun v. Yeager, No. 91-CA-12, 1992 WL 56793 (Ohio App., 4th Dist.,
Mar. 26, 1992); Simone v. Hill’n Dale Homes, No. 89-B-9, 1989 WL 158232 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Dec. 29, 1989); Pappenhagen v. Payne, 48 Ohio App.3d 176, 549 N.E.2d 208 (8th Dist.

1988).

336 The 1994 amendment to Civil Rule 54(C) renders obsolete the contrary decisions in Simon
v. Durham , 98 Ohio App.3d 828, 649 N.E.2d 1240 (8th Dist. 1994), and Pietrykowski v.

Hamblin, No. WD-84-86, 1985 WL 7092 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 12, 1985). Both decisions relied on pre-1994 Civil Rule 54(C), which limited money judgment awards to the amount demanded in the pleadings.

503 N.E.2d 737 (1987).337 The duration of the landlord’s wrongful withholding, beyond the R.C. 5321.16(B) thirty-day period, is imma terial. Munsie v. Welkener , No. CA96 -08-080, 1997 WL 374739 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 7, 1997) (one day after the thirty-day period). See also Forquer v. Colony Club, 26 Ohio App.3d 178, 499 N.E.2d 7 (10th Dist. 1985).

“[The statutory] damages recove rable pursuant to R.C. 5321.16(C) are not ‘punitive damages’ under former R.C. 1925.02(A)(2)(c) and [thus] are not excluded from the jurisdiction of small claims divisions of municipal and county courts.”Klemas
v. Flynn, 66 Ohio St.3d 249, 253, 611 N.E.2d810 (1993). This rule remains good law for purposes of modern R.C. 1925.02(A)(2)(a)(iii). Bonesteel v. Nash, 65 N.E.3d 326, 2016-Ohio-3144 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., 2016).

#### 4. Reasonable Attorney’s Fees

If any portion of the security deposit is wrongfully withheld, the landlord’s liability for reasonable attorney’s fees under R.C. 5321.16(C) is mandatory. Smith v. Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987).338 A tenant’s failure to demand the attorney’s fees in the pleadings does not waive the tenant’s right to recover them. 339 Ohio R. Civ. P. 54(C) (“Except as to a party against whom a judgment is entered by default, every final judgment should grant the relief to which the party in whose favor it is rendered is entitled, even if the party has not demanded the relief in the pleadings”).340 E.g., Leszczynski v. Brewer, No. 12523, 337 See also Alcoroso v. Correll, 159 N.E.3d 924, 2020-Ohio-4752 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2020);

Oldendick v. Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016 -Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); Spring Hill Townhomes v. Pounds, No. 25887, 2014 WL 1887690 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 9, 2014);

Compton v. Spinks , No. CA2003-08-019, 2004 WL 1543201 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 12, 2004); Williams v. Glaser III, No. C-000539, 2001 WL 395165 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Apr. 20, 2001); Munsie v. Welkener, No. CA96-08-080, 1997 WL 374739 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 7, 1997); Buck v. Georgian Manor Invs. , No. 67170, 1995 WL 143813 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Mar. 30, 1995); Ulle-Mance v. Berardinelli, No. 92-L-210, 1993 WL 407262 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993); Calhoun v. Yeager , No. 91 -CA-12, 1992 WL 56793 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 26, 1992);Perry v. Guttman, Nos. C-870530,870568, 1988 WL 101216 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept. 28, 19 88); Pappenhagen v. Payne , 48 Ohio App.3d 176, 549 N.E.2d 208 (8th
Dist. 1988).

338 The court decisions cited in note 335, above, support this proposition as well.

339 These attorney’s fees are costs, not damages. See Section XIX.C, below.

340 See note 355, below.

1991 WL 285435 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 27, 1991); Morales v. Windsor Realty & Mgmt., Inc., No. 2000-CVH-002037 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 21, 2000). Cf. Zimmerman v. Elmore, No. 47724, 1984 WL 5119 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 28, 1984) (R.C. 5321.05(C) attorney’s fees). But see Biasella v. Costakis , No.

71281, 1997 WL 253157 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 15, 1997).

The landlord’s good faith in withholding the s ecurity deposit is not a defense to the tenant’s claim for reasonable attorney’s fees.Smith v. Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987).341 The determination on the amount of attorney’s fees to be awarded lies within the sound discretion of the t rial court. Clarke v. Royal , 20AP-366, 2021 WL 2443751 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 15, 2021); Christen v. Cont’l Enters., Ltd., 154 N.E.3d 1192, 2020 -Ohio-3665 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2020); Mandzuch v. Affordable Reasonable Rental, LLC , 2018-G-0179, 2019 WL 2269297 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., May 28, 2019); Timoneri v. Northsteppe Realty , 71 N.E.3d 630, 2016 -Ohio-5901 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2016); LaCare v. Dearing, 73 Ohio App.3d 238, 596 N.E.2d 1097 (11th Dist. 1991);McGregor v. Armeni, No. 89AP-1500, 1990 WL 179981 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 20, 1990); Heath v. Burgess , No. CA86 -12-019, 1987 WL 12730 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 15, 1987); Forquer v. Colony Cl ub, 26 Ohio App.3d 178, 499 N.E.2d 7 (10th Dist. 1985).

The computation of the amount of attorney’s fees to be awarded is based solely on the time spent on the R.C. 5321.16(C) claim. Smith v. Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987).342 This time includes any time spent in defending against a landlord’s objections to a magistrate’s decision in favor of the tenant. Alexander v. LJF Mgmt. Inc. , No. C - 100618, 2011 WL 2112623 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., May 27, 2011).

This time also includes any time spe nt in defending against a counterclaim to the R.C. 5321.16(C) claim, if the security deposit claim and the counterclaim are virtually
indivisible. Alcorso v. Correll, No. 110218, 2021 WL 4317396, (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 341 The court decisions cited in note 337, above, support this proposition as well.

342 See also Congdon v. Senters, No. 99-CA-43, 1999 WL 1071768 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov.

16, 1999); Nelson v. Tipton , No. 99AP -277, 1999 WL 1041154 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov.

18, 1999); Buck v. Georgian Manor Invs., No. 67170, 1995 WL 143813 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Mar. 30, 1995); Oakwood Mgmt. Co. v. Young, No. 92AP-207, 1992 WL 324338 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Oct. 27, 1992).

505
Sept. 23, 2021) ; Christen v. Cont’l Enters., Ltd., 154 N.E.3d 1192, 2020 -Ohio-3665 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2020); Jerels v. Begue , No. 24700, 2010 WL 1780140 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 5, 2010);Whitestone Co. v. Stittsworth, No. 06AP-371, 2007 WL 155299 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 23, 2007); Nelson v. Tipton, No. 99AP-277, 1999 WL 1041154 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 18, 1999); LaCare v. Dearing , 73 Ohio App.3d 238, 596 N.E.2d 1097 (11th Dist. 1991);Opsomer v. Gray, No. 7-84-20, 1986 WL 6434 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., May 29, 1986).

The typical counterclaim that meets this standard is a counterclaim for unpaid rent or damage to the premises. E.g., Clarke v. Royal, 20AP-366, 2021 WL 2443751 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 15, 2021); Whitestone Co. v. Stittsworth, No. 06AP-371, 2007 WL 155299 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 23, 2007); Congdon v. Senters, No. 99-CA- 43, 1999 WL 1071768 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 16, 1999); LaCare v. Dearing, 73 Ohio App.3d 238, 596 N.E.2d 1097 (11th Dist. 1991); Cook v. Downey , No. 87AP - 1075, 1988 WL 70446 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 30, 1988); Opsomer v. Gray, No.

7-84-20, 1986 WL 6434 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., May 29, 1986). See also Nelson v.

Tipton, No. 99AP-277, 1999 WL 1041154 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 18, 1999).

The court may consider the amount awarded on the security deposit claim in determining the reasonable amount of attorney’s fees. Nelson v. Tipton , No. 99AP- 277, 1999 WL 1041154 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 18, 1999); Cook v. Downey, No.

87AP-1075, 1988 WL 70446 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 30, 1988);Fant v. DiSabato,
No. 87AP-265, 1987 WL 32190 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 29, 1987).

However, the attorney fee award is not limited to the amount of the security
deposit. Congdon v. Senters , No. 98 -CA-55, 1999 WL 4058 (Ohio App., 5th Dist.,
Dec. 23, 1998).

“Simply because the attorney fees incurred in this case were greatly disproportionate to the damages recovered on [tenant’s] security deposit claim does not mean they were unreasonable. As stated above, R.C. 5321.16 is a remedial statute intended to en sure that a tenant incurs no expense when seeking a refund of a wrongfully withheld security deposit. Consistent with th at purpose, [the tenant] was entitled to recover reasonable attorney fees [the landlord’s] actions forced her to incur to enforce her statutory rights.” Alcorso v. Correll, No. 110218, 2021 WL 4317396, ¶ 49 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 23, 2021) ($26,825 fee award, $1,200 judgment).

When the attorney fee award exceeds the security deposit award, a clear statement of the method by which the trial court arrived at the attorney fee award is helpful to avoid a remand. See Clarke v. Royal, 20AP-366, 2021 WL 2443751 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 15, 2021) (award of $950 security deposit, $950 statutory damages, $200 refund, and $5,415 attorney’s fees);Christen v. Cont’l Enters., Ltd., 154 N.E.3d 1192, 2020-Ohio-3665 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2020) (award of $850 for portion of security deposit wrongfully withheld, $850 statutory damages, and $23,500 attorney’s fees);

Schultz v. Wurdlow , No. 11AP -62, 2012 WL 2859914, ¶¶ 1,25,26 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 12, 2012) (award of $350 security deposit, $350 statutory damages, $14,782.50 attorney’s fees);343 Whitestone Co. v. Stittsworth, No. 06AP-371, 2007 WL 155299, ¶ 62 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 23, 2007) (award of $700 security deposit, $700 statutory damages, $5,989.76 attorney’s fees);Ridenour v. Dunn, No. 03AP-611, 2004 WL 1445270, ¶¶ 11,12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 29, 2004) (award of $225 security deposit, $225 statutory damages, $1,000 attorney’s fees). In these circumstances, appellate courts have invoked the rule: “where the amount recovered is small compared to the attorney fees as sessed, the court must give adequate reasoning as to how it arrived at the specific amount of the award.” Dunn, 2004 WL 1445270, ¶ 10.

Any downward adjustment of the attorney fee award, based on the amount of the security deposit judgment, may not reduce the attorney fee award to an amount that is unreasonable and inconsistent with the purposes of such fees.Congdon v. Senters, No.

99-CA-43, 1999 WL 1071768 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 16, 1999).

The Congdon trial court awarded the tenant a judgment of $550 for the security deposit, $550 for R.C. 5321.16(C) statutory damages, and $600 for attorney’s fees.

With respect to the attorney fee award, it found that the attorney’s time (36.7 hours) and rate ($140 per hour) were reasonable but made a downward adjustment to take into account the amount of the security deposit judgment.

The Congdon appellate court reversed the trial court on the attorney fee award, on the ground that the amount was too low:

For the trial court to concede that the amount of time ex pended was reasonable in light of the case presented and then to award a fee tantamount to a charge of $16.35 per hour for trial counsel’s endeavor, is to in fact condone an abuse of the court system by litigants. It sends a clear message that condones th e action of withholding a security 343 An example of the type of reasoning that is adequate is contained inSchultz v. Wurdlow, No.

2008-CVI-031737, slip op. at 5 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 30, 2010), aff’d, No. 11AP- 62, 2012 WL 2859914, ¶¶ 25,26 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 12, 2012).

507 deposit because the cost of recovery is too great. It invites an abuse of the legal process and punishes litigants from seeking their day in
court. It telegraphs a clear message that the prosecution of small claims is too expensive.

Congdon, 1999 WL 1071768, at *2.

A pro se tenant who is an attorney may recover attorney’s fees under R.C.

5321.16(C). Lytle v. K & D Group, Inc. , No. 84889, 2005 WL 1994938 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 18, 2005).

For further discussion onissues related to calculating and moving for R.C. Chapter 5321 attorney’s fees, see Chapter XIX, below.

#### 5. Practice Issues


##### a. Compulsory Counterclaims

A landlord’s claim for unpaid rent, or for damage caused by the tenant’s violation of R.C. 5321.05 or the rent al agreement, is a compulsory counterclaim (as well as a
defense) to the tenant’s security deposit claim. Cochran v. Larson , No. 7311, 1982 WL 3656 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 8, 1982); Thirion v. Tutoki, 94 Ohio Misc.2d 77, 703 N.E.2d 378 (Ohio Mun., War ren, 1998). Cf. Pruitt v. Mauger Mgmt. Co. , No.

77AP-367, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 7605 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 29, 1977) (R.C.

5321.09 release of rent deposit action and R.C. 5321.16 security deposit action a rose out of the same transaction for Civil Rule 13(A) purposes).

Since Civil Rule 13(A) applies to proceedings in a municipal court, small claims division, these landlord claims are compulsory counterclaims even if the tenant files the security deposit claim in a small claims court. Thirion v. Tutoki, 94 Ohio Misc.2d 77, 703 N.E.2d 378 (Ohio Mun., Warren, 1998).

A landlord’s right to file these counterclaims is not waived by the landlord’s noncompliance with the R.C. 5321.16(B) written itemization requirement. See Section XVII.C.8, below.

A tenant’s claim for refund of a security deposit likewise may be a compulsory counterclaim to a landlord’s claim for unpaid rent. GMS Mgmt. Co. v. Seminaro, No.

63007, 1993 WL 277073 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 22, 1993).

##### b. Burden of Proof

The tenant bears the burd en of proving the existence and then termination of the rental agreement, the amount of the security deposit, and the amount of the security deposit not returned. Schultz v. Wurdlow, No. 2008-CVI-031737 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 30, 2010), aff’d onother grounds, No. 11AP-62, 2012 WL 2859914 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 12, 2012).

“The burden falls upon the landlord to establish the lawfulness of any deductions from the security deposit.” Zeallear v. F & W Props. , No. 99AP -1215, 2000 WL 1015345, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 25, 2000). Accord Oldendick v. Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016 -Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); Paxton v.

McGranahan, No. 49645, 1985 WL 6444 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 31, 1985);Schultz
v. Wurdlow, No. 2008-CVI-031737 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 30, 2010).

Two court decisions conflict on whether the landlord (or tenant) bears the burden of proving noncompliance (or compliance) with the forwarding address requirement.

Compare Dowell v. Krebs, No. 54517, 1988 WL 112857, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Oct. 20, 1988) (landlord’s burden) with Schultz v. Wurdlow , No. 2008 -CVI-031737 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 30, 2010) (tenant’s burden),aff’d on other grounds,
No. 11AP-62, 2012 WL 2859914 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 12, 2012).

##### c. Accord and Satisfaction

When a landlord withholds a portion of the security deposit and provides the tenant with a check for the remainder of the deposit, and the tenant cashes the check, it does not necessarily create an accord and satisfaction. Tourville v. Terzuoli , No. 22802, 2009 WL 1636605 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 5, 2009).

In Tourville, the landlord “returned a check for the amount of the security deposit that he believed was due.” Id., ¶ 13. “[T]here was no evidence that the check was the product of a negotiation between the [landlord and tenant] regarding the amount of the security deposit that should be refunded.” Id. The court thereupon held that “there is no evidence that an accord was reached.” Id.

##### d. Improper Parties, R.C. 5321.16(C)

An R.C. 5321.16(C) claim may not be brought against persons who are landlords of the premises under the R.C. 5321.01(B) definition of landl ord, but who neither received nor retain the security deposit (e.g., a clerk in the rental office who physically receives the rent checks on behalf of the clerk’s employer). Ford v. Club S. Inv. Co.,
No. 86AP-559, 1986 WL 14482 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 16, 1986).

An R.C. 5321.16(C) claim may not be brought by a non-tenant, such as a guarantor under a lease to whom the tenant has assigned the security deposit rights. Beatley v.

509 Schwartz, No. 03AP -911, 2004 WL 1244360 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 8, 200 4).

However, such guarantors may recover the assigned deposit under common law. Id.

##### e. R.C. 5321.07 Deposits Constitute Rent Paid

Any “rent money deposited with the clerk of court pursuant to R.C. 5321.07 constitutes rent.” Davis v. Wesolowski, 146 N.E.3d 633, 2020 -Ohio-677, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2020). Therefore, a landlord may not consider these deposits unpaid rent for R.C. 5321.16 purposes. Id., ¶¶ 17-20.

##### f. Agreement Between Tenants Cannot Waive Landlord’s

Duty “No agreement can relieve a landlord of the mandatory duties imposed by R.C.

5321.16(B).” Timoneri v. Northsteppe Realty, 71 N.E.3d 630, 2016-Ohio-5901, ¶ 32 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2016). Consequently, an agreement between replacement tenants (i.e., one tenant is replacing another tenant under a lease) cannot relieve the landlord of the mandatory R.C. 5321.16(B) obligations relative to the replaced tenant.
Id. For example, if the new tenant fails to pay to the replaced tenant a specified security deposit amount in violation of the replacement agreement, the landlord is still obligated to return the replaced tenant’s security deposit to the replaced tenant in accordance with R.C. 5321.16(B). Id., ¶¶ 32-35.

#### 6. Third-Party Purchaser of the Premises

When a third party purchases the premises during the tenancy, an issue may arise as to whether the original landlord or the new landlord is liable to the tenant for the security deposit.

Because a security deposit is a pledge (see Section XVI.A, above), the original landlord, as pledgee, always remains liable to the tenant for returning the security deposit in accordance with the terms of the rental agreement. Dovi Interests, Ltd. v.

Somerset Point Ltd. P’ship, No. 82788, 2004 WL 254211 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb.

12, 2004); Castlebrook, Ltd. v. Dayton Props. Ltd. P’ship, 78 Ohio App.3d 340, 604 N.E.2d 808 (2nd Dist. 1992); UNF Corp. v. Deffet Rentals, Inc., No. 81AP-337, 1981 WL 3661 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 10, 1981); Tuteur v. P. & F. Enters., Inc. , 21 Ohio App.2d 122, 255 N.E.2d 284 (8th Dist. 1970).

The new landlord is liable only if, in the purchase of the premises, the new landlord agreed to assume such liability or received a credit for the security deposit against the sale price. Castlebrook, Ltd., 78 Ohio App.3d at 348; UNF Corp., 1981 WL 3661, at *1,2; Tuteur, 21 Ohio App.2d at 133. E.g., Jordan v. McShan , No. 91 -CVG-20057 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 9, 1992) (purchase agreement provision providing for the transfer of the tenant’s security deposit made the new landlord liable for the deposit).

The new landlord’s agreement to assume such liability may be established by the lease between the tenant and the original landlord, if the lease contains a provision imposing such liability on any purchaser of the premises. Smith v. Applegate Prop.

Mgmt., No. 2012-CVF-2488 (Ohio Mun., Massillon, July 31, 2013). This conclusion is based, in part, on t he common law rule that, when a third -party purchases rental property, generally the third-party purchases the property subject to the existing leases.
Id. For a discussion on that common law, see Section XXII.B, below.

#### 7. Conflict Between R.C. 5321.16 and the Rental

Agreement Any liquidated damage provision in a rental agreement that conflicts with R.C.

5321.16(C) is unenforceable. R.C. 5321.13(A). E.g., Oldendick v. Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016-Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); KGM Capital, LLC v . Jackson,
No. C-130438, 2014 WL 2566286 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 6, 2014); Albregt v.

Chen, 17 Ohio App.3d 79, 477 N.E.2d 1150 (6th Dist. 1983); Riding Club Apts. v.

Sargent, 2 Ohio App.3d 146, 440 N.E.2d 1368 (10th Dist. 1981);Ankney v. Dame, No.

L-76-307, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 10154 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 29, 1977). See also Chaney v. Breton Builder Co. , 130 Ohio App.3d 602, 720 N.E.2d 941 (6th Dist.

1998); Cook v. Heritage Glen Apts., No. CA96-03-055, 1996 WL 535238 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Sept. 23, 1996).

Likewise, any provision in a rental agreement that provides for an automatic forfeiture of the security deposit is a violation of R.C. 5321.16 and, therefore,
unenforceable. R.C. 5321.13(A). E.g., KGM Capital, LLC v. Jackson, No. C-130438, 2014 WL 2566286 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 6, 2014) (lease provided for an automatic forfeiture of the security deposit, plus the amount of one -month’s rent, if tenant vacated the premises before the expiration of the lease term); C & L Ventures, LLC v. Heistand, No. 00-CVG-01995 (Ohio Mun., Fairfield Cty., Nov. 14, 2001) (lease designated the security deposit a “non -refundable security deposit”); Littlejohn v.

Dehostos, No. 91 -CVG-14377 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 9, 1992) (lease provided for an automatic forfeiture of the security deposit if tenant vacated the premises within a specified time period).

511

## XVII. Landlord Claim for Rent


### A. End Date of Rental Obligation


#### 1. Month-to-Month Tenant

Generally, three factors determine the end date of the time period for which a month-to-month tenant is obligated to pay rent: (1) the date on which the tenancy is terminated; (2) the date on which the tenant delivers possession of the premises to the landlord; and (3) any time period the landlord does not lease the prem ises as a result of the tenant’s violation of the rental agreement.

As to the third factor, the tenant’s obligation to pay rent for that period is subject to the landlord’s duty to mitigate damages. See Section XVII.B, below.

When a month-to-month tenant vacates the premises without giving the landlord a notice of termination, and the landlord has neither terminated the tenancy nor requested the tenant to leave, the tenant generally is obligated to pay rent for the month in which the tenant vacated (“vaca tion month”) and for the month after the vacation month.

Snyder v. Waldron, No. 12-CA-9, 2013 WL 3988775, ¶¶ 25,26 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 26, 2013) (citing this book, 3d edition, section XVII.A.1). The tenancy is not terminated until the end of the month after the vacation month because the tenant’s vacation of the premises is, in effect, the tenant’s R.C. 5321.17(B) 30 -day notice of
termination. The tenant’s obligation to pay rent for the month after the vacation month is based on the tenant’s obligation to pay rent during the term of the tenancy.

When a month -to-month tenant gives the landlord a notice of termination that specifies a termination date that is less than 30 days before the periodic rental date, and the tenant vacates the premises pursuant to that notice, the tenant is generally obligated to pay rent for the vacation month and for the month after the vacation month. E.g., Bowman v. Cmty. Mgmt. Corp., 14 Ohio App.3d 31, 469 N.E.2d 1038 (1st Dist. 1984) (notice Sept. 21, vacated Sept. 30 , security deposit awarded for Oct. rent). See also Adams v. Relmax , 111 N.E.3d 758, 2018 -Ohio-1751 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2018);

Barran v. Kinas, No. 85085, 2005 WL 991690 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 28, 2005);

Fenell v. Mayflower Manor Apts., No. 17224, 1995 WL 699919 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,
Nov. 29, 1995); Wilson v. Thomas , No. L -87-405, 1988 WL 69153 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 30, 1988).

When a month-to-month tenant properly terminates the tenancy and timely vacates the premises, but returns the keys post-vacation, the tenant is generally obligated to pay rent for a post -vacation period: either up to the date the keys are returned or for an entire month post-vacation. McGreevy v. Bassler, No. 09AP-381, 2010 WL 177255 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 19, 2010) (liability up to the date the keys are returned);

Knipp v. Sadler, No. 609-04, 2009 WL 2710053 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug. 31, 2009) (liability for an entire month post-vacation).

When a month-to-month tenant gives the landlord a 30-day notice of termination, and before the expiration of the 30-day period the landlord locks the tenant out of the premises, the tenant is not liable for any rent that accrues subsequent to the lockout.

Gordon v. Morris, No. 2000-CA-69, 2001 WL 85797 (Ohio App., 2nd D ist., Feb. 2, 2001). For a discussion on the tenant’s claim for the lockout, see Chapter XII, above.

When a month-to-month tenant vacates the premises in response to the landlord’s notice to vacate, and the reason for the notice is not a tenant violation of the rental agreement, the tenant is obligated to pay rent only for the period up to the date on which the tenant delivers possession of the premises to the landlord. E.g., VanHoose
v. McGee , No. CA97 -04-008, 1997 WL 598127 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Sept. 29, 1997). The landlord has terminated the tenancy with the service of the notice to vacate.

For the period between the termination of the tenancy and the tenant’s delivery of possession, the tenant is a holdover tenant and thus liable for t he rent. See Section XVII.A.4, below. For the period after the tenant’s delivery of possession, there is neither a tenancy nor any other basis on which to hold the tenant liable for rent.

When a month-to-month tenant vacates the premises in response to the landlord’s notice to vacate, and the reason for the notice is the tenant’s violation of the rental agreement, the tenant is generally obligated to pay rent for the vacation month, but not for the month after the vacation month. E.g., Simoudis v. Diehl, 116 Ohio App.3d 296, 688 N.E.2d 16 (11th Dist. 1996) (notice to vacate for nonpayment of rent; tenant vacates eleventh day of the month); Raymond v. Blanchard Inv. Co., No. 84AP-1000, 1985 WL 9931 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 28, 1985) (notice to vacate f or causing damage to the premises; tenant vacates twenty-seventh day of the month); Hashemi v.

Jim Owen & Co., No. 76AP-879, 1977 Ohio App. LEXIS 8510 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Apr. 21, 1977) (notice to vacate for nonpayment of rent; tenant vacates third dayof the month); Moran v. Foster , No. 97 -CVG-300 (Ohio Mun., Lakewood, Dec. 30, 1997) (notice to vacate for nonpayment of rent; tenant vacates third day of the month).

However, in these circumstances, if the tenant returns the keys for the premises in the month after the vacation month, the tenant is generally obligated to pay rent for the 513 vacation month and for all or part of the month after the vacation month. E.g., Griffin
v. Currie, No. 4065, 1988 WL 126385 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Nov. 25, 1988) (liable for the entire month after the vacation month);Jordan v. McShan, No. 91-CVG-20057 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 9, 1992) (liable up to the date the keys are returned in the month after the vacation month). See also Peters v. Durroh , 28 Ohio App.2d 245, 277 N.E.2d 69 (10th Dist. 1971).

When a landlord’s notice (under R.C. 1923.04 or R.C. 5321.11) terminates a month-to-month tenancy for a tenant’s violation of the rental agreement, the tenant’s rental obligation post-termination is an element of the damages that the landlord has suffered as a result of the tenant’s violation.

#### 2. Remainder of Lease Term, Post-Service of the

Notice to Vacate “[T]he issuance of a three-day notice to vacate pursuant to R.C. 1923.04 does not terminate the obligation of the tenant to the landlord to pay rent for the remainder of the term or until a new tenant is secured in the exercise of reasonable diligence.” Dennis v. Morgan, 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 418, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000).

“If the lessor has acted reasonably in attempting to secure a new tenant, the lessee is liable for the rent up to the point of the lessor’s finding a new tenant or the expiration of the lease, whichever is earlier.” Id. at 419. See also Pinnacle Mgmt. v. Bell , No.

CA2011-08-145, 2012 WL 1202678 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Apr. 9, 2012); Budler v.

Empire Mgmt. Group, No. 22376, 2005 WL 1026697 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 4, 2005).
