The Dennis decision resolved a conflict among the lower courts on whether, in the absence of a continuing liability provision in the lease, the landlord’s service of a notice to vacate relieved the tenant of future rental obligations under the lease.

Notwithstanding Dennis, if a tenant vacates the premises in response to the landlord’s notice to vacate, and the landlord served the notice to vacate in violation of the tenant’s rights under the lease and applicable law, the tenant is not liable for any rent that accrues subse quent to the tenant’s vacation of the premises. Wehrley v.

Sunchase Am., Ltd., No. CA99-11-191, 2001 WL 88202 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan.

29, 2001). In these circumstances, the tenant also has a claim against the landlord for wrongful eviction. See Section XIV.B, above.

#### 3. Remainder of Lease Term, Post-Death of the

Tenant “As a general rule, a lease for a term of years does not terminate by operation of law upon the death of the lessee but passes to his personal representative, who becomes bound as assignee for the remaining term.” Sherman v. Carlin, 46 Ohio App.3d 149, 150, 546 N.E.2d 433 (8th Dist. 1988).

“However, a lease may be so personal in its terms as to manifest the parties’ intention that the lease terminates upon the tenant’s death.”Id.

The assisted housing lease 344 is sufficiently personal in its terms that the courts should find that the lease terminates upon the tenant’s death, absent a remaining household member. The tenant’s initial eligibility to lease the dwelling unit is based, in part, on the tenant’s low income. See Section XXIV.B.2, below. The amount of the tenant’s rental obligation under the lease is primarily based on the tenant’s income.

See Section XXIV.B.4, below. The project -based subsidized housing lease, 345 ¶ 13, prohibits altogether any assignment of the lease, and the lease does not include a provision stating that the lease is binding on the tenant’s heirs, successors, representatives, or assigns.

#### 4. Holdover Tenant

A tenant who remains in occupancy of the premises aftera lease expires is a tenant at sufferance (“holdover tenant”).K&D Mgmt., L.L.C. v. Jones, No. 110262, 2021 WL 5834382 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2021); Deutsche Bank Nat’l Trust Co. v.

Lovette, No. 106546, 2018 WL 6266863 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov.29, 2018); Adams
v. Relmax, 111 N.E.3d 758, 2018-Ohio-1751 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2018);3637 Green Road Co., Ltd. v. Specialized Component Sales Co., Inc., 69 N.E.3d 1083, 2016-Ohio- 5324 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); Vlcek v. Brogee , No. 25499, 2013 WL 5 436930 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 27,2013); Capella III, L.L.C. v. Wilcox, 190 Ohio App.3d 133, 940 N.E.2d 1026 (10th Dist. 2010); Wengerd v. Martin, No. 99-CA-0004, 2000 WL 354148 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 5, 2000); City of Cleveland v. A.J. Rose Mfg.

Co., 89 Ohio App.3d 267, 624 N.E.2d 245 (8th Dist. 1993); Steiner v. Minkowski, 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist. 1991);Craig Wrecking Co. v. Loewendick 344 For the definition of “assisted housing,” see note 163, above.

345 For the definition of “subsidized housing lease,” see note 174, above.

515 & Sons, Inc., 38 Ohio App.3d 79, 526 N.E.2d 321 (10th Dist. 1987).

“[T]he law construes the tenant’s continued occupancy as a proposal to lease the premises for another term.” Capella III, L.L.C. v. Wilcox, 190 Ohio App.3d 133, 940 N.E.2d 1026, ¶ 18 (10th Dist. 2010). Accord Bill Swad Chevrolet, Inc. v. Ricart Jeep Eagle, Inc., 129 Ohio App.3d 501, 718 N.E.2d 470 (10th Dist. 1998).

In response to a holdover tenancy, the landlord may create a new tenancy or treat the tenant as a trespasser and evict the tenant. K&D Mgmt., L.L.C. v. Jones , No.

110262, 2021 WL 58 34382 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2021); 3637 Green Road Co., Ltd. v. Specialized Component Sales Co., Inc., 69 N.E.3d 1083, 2016-Ohio-5324 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); Vlcek v. Brogee, No. 25499, 2013 WL 5436930 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 27, 2013); Capella III, L.L.C. v. Wilcox, 190 Ohio App.3d 133, 940 N.E.2d 1026 (10th Dist. 2010); Wengerd v. Martin, No. 99-CA-0004, 2000 WL 354148 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 5, 2000); City of Cleveland v. A.J. Rose Mfg. Co., 89 Ohio App.3d 267, 624 N.E.2d 245 (8th Dist. 1993); Steiner v. Minkowski, 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist. 1991); Craig Wrecking Co. v. Loewendick & Sons, Inc., 38 Ohio App.3d 79, 526 N.E.2d 321 (10th Dist. 1987).

“In such circumstances, the conduct of the parties determines whether an implied contract arises.” Steiner v. Minkowski, 72 Ohio App.3d 754,762, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th
Dist. 1991). Accord K&D Mgmt., L.L.C. v. Jones , No. 110262, 2021 WL 5834382 ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2021).

For example, where a tenant notifi ed the landlord of the tenant’s intent to vacate at the end of the lease term and, approximately seven to ten days after the lease term ended, the landlord gave the tenant a demand notice to vacate, an implied contract did not arise. K&D Mgmt., L.L.C. v. Jones, No. 110262, 2021 WL 5834382 ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2021).

A new tenancy is not created if the landlord proposes a rent increase for the new tenancy and the tenant expresses dissent to the rent increase.346 Steiner v. Minkowski, 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist. 1991); Fredricks v. Stromberg, No.

42601 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 2, 1981); Lane v. Green , 21 Ohio App. 62, 152 N.E.2d 790 (4th Dist. 1926).

A new tenancy is not created if the landlord does not accept rent paymen ts from 346 The landlord may unilaterally increase the rent for the new tenancy only if the tenant fails to express dissent to the rent increase. Steiner v. Minkowski , 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist. 1991).

the tenant and does not otherwise create the new tenancy. City of Cleveland v. A.J.

Rose Mfg. Co., 89 Ohio App.3d 267, 624 N.E.2d 245 (8th Dist. 1993).

“The landlord may consent to the proposed tenancy through the acceptance of rent or other con duct signaling acquiescence.” Capella III, L.L.C. v. Wilcox , 190 Ohio App.3d 133, 940 N.E.2d 1026, ¶ 18 (10th Dist. 2010).Accord Wengerd v. Martin, No.

99-CA-0004, 2000 WL 354148 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 5, 2000); Bill Swad Chevrolet, Inc. v. Ricart Jeep Eagle, Inc., 129 Ohio App.3d 501, 718 N.E.2d 470 (10th
Dist. 1998); Steiner v. Minkowski , 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist.

1991).

After the expiration of a fixed term ( e.g., one-year) lease, absent an agreement of the parties on a new lease term, the landlord’s continued acceptance of monthly rent converts the holdover tenancy to a month -to-month tenancy. Adams v. Relmax, 111 N.E.3d 758, 2018 -Ohio-1751, ¶¶ 10,11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2018); McCain v.

Brewer, No. 2014-CA-8, 2015 WL 302829, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 23, 2015);

Vlcek v. Brogee, No. 25499, 2013 WL 5436930, ¶ 42 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 27, 2013); Snyder v. Wal dron, No. 12 -CA-9, 2013 WL 3988775, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 26, 2013); Capella III, L.L.C. v. Wilcox, 190 Ohio App.3d 133, 940 N.E.2d 1026, ¶ 18 (10th Dist. 2010).

Under this month -to-month tenancy, the tenant “subjects himself or herself to a renewal or continuance of the liability to pay rent as fixed by the terms and conditions of the prior lease.” Fennell v. DeMichiei, No. 106966, 2019 WL 350874, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 24, 2019).

Also, “generally the [other] terms and conditions o f the original lease govern the holdover lease term.” Capella III, L.L.C. , 190 Ohio App.3d 133, ¶ 18. Accord Wengerd v. Martin, No. 99-CA-0004, 2000 WL 354148 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 5, 2000); Bill Swad Chevrolet, Inc. v. Ricart Jeep Eagle, Inc., 129 Ohio App.3d 501, 718 N.E.2d 470 (10th Dist. 1998); Craig Wrecking Co. v. Loewendick & Sons, Inc. , 38 Ohio App.3d 79, 526 N.E.2d 321 (10th Dist. 1987).

When the parties do not enter into a new tenancy, the holdover tenant is liable for the reasonable value of the use and occupation of the premises during the period of the 517 holdover.347 Steiner v. Minkowski , 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist.

1991); Fredricks v. Stromberg, No. 42601, 1981 Ohio App. LEXIS 10469 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 2, 1981); Tuteur v. P. & F. Enters., Inc. , 21 Ohio App.2d 122, 255 N.E.2d 284 (8th Dist. 1970); Lane v. Green , 21 Ohio App. 62, 152 N.E.2d 790 (4th
Dist. 1926).

This means that a holdover tenant is generally required to pay rent only for the number of days that the tenant has held over. K&D Mgmt., L.L.C. v. Jones , No.

110262, 2021 WL 5834382, ¶¶ 30,31 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 2021);Ozdemir v.

Boldt, No. L-18-1022, 2018 WL 6601185, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 14, 2018);
Vill. Station Assocs. v. Geauga Co., 84 Ohio App.3d 448, 451, 616 N.E.2d 1201 (11th
Dist. 1992). But see Johnson v. Brown , No. 2000 -CA-76, 2003 WL 1193795, at *4 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 14, 2003).

A liquidated damage provision for a holdover tenant is enforceable only if it is reasonably related to actual damages. Ozdemir v. Boldt , No. L -18-1022, 2018 WL 6601185, ¶ 22 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 14, 2018) (for a month-to-month tenant who held over five days, if the landlord’s position that the tenant is “liable for the entire month … been accepted, the cost would become an illegal penalty in violation of R.C.

5321.06”); Brunswick Ltd. P’ship v. Feudo , 171 Ohio App.3d 369, 870 N.E.2d 804 (11th Dist. 2007) (liquidated damage provision of double the pro -rated portion of the rent for the holdover period is reasonably related and thus enforceable); Vill. Station
Assocs. v. Geauga Co. , 84 Ohio App.3d 448, 616 N.E.2d 1201 (11th Dist. 1992) (liquidated damage provision of double the entire monthly rent, where tenant held over for nine days, is not reasonably related and thus unenforceable).

#### 5. Rent Accrual During an Eviction Action

While a landlord may not accept future rent after serving a notice to vacate (see Section II.C, above), “a tenant in occupancy during an eviction action remains liable for rent [that accrues] during the pendency of the action.” Julian Invs., Inc. v. Dudley,
No. 98-CA-85, 1999 WL 64227, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 12, 1999). Accord Four Stars Serv., Inc. v. City of Akron, No. 19124, 1999 WL 980558 (Ohio App., 9th 347 The holdover tenant is also liable “for damages caused by the delay in returning possession of the premises to the landlord.” Steiner v. Minkowski , 72 Ohio App.3d 754, 762 -63, 596 N.E.2d 492 (6th Dist. 1991). Accord Tuteur v. P. & F. Enters., Inc., 21 Ohio App.2d 122, 130, 255 N.E.2d 284 (8th Dist. 1970). “However, those damages do not include the loss of profits.” Tuteur, 21 Ohio App.2d at 128.

Dist., Oct. 27, 1999);Fin. Land Corp. v. Visible Difference, Inc., No. 93AP-1044, 1994 WL 195024 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 19, 1994); Sheridan Manor Apts. v. Carter,
No. 92-CA-4, 1992 WL 388539 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Dec. 22, 1992); Graham v.

Pavarini, 9 Ohio App.3d 89, 458 N.E.2d 421 (8th Dist. 1983);Presidential Park Apts.
v. Colston, 17 Ohio Op.3d 220 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 1980).

However, a tenant is entitled to a reasonable period of time, after the conclusion of the eviction action, to pay that rent. See Section II.M, above.

#### 6. Landlord Divested of Ownership

The tenant’s rental obligation to a landlord ends on the date that the landlord is divested of ownership of the premises. Tillimon v. Moore, Nos. L-18-1040,1041, 2018 WL 3814842 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 10 , 2018). For example, when a sheriff’s sale in a foreclosure action divests a landlord of ownership of the premises, the tenant’s rental obligation to the landlord ends as of the date of that sale. Id.

### B. Landlord Duty to Mitigate

“[L]andlords have a duty, as all parties to contracts do, to mitigate their damages caused by a breach.” Dennis v. Morgan , 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 419, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000). Accord Frenchtown Square P’ship v. Lemstone , 99 Ohio St.3d 254, 791 N.E.2d 417 (2003).

The duty to mitigate exists where, in response to a notice to vacate, a tenant vacates the premises before the lease or tenancy has expired. E.g., Dennis v. Morgan, 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000) (notice to vacate for disturbances);Gerber v. Lewis,
No. 70955, 1996 WL 715504 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 12, 1996) (notice to vacate for nonpayment of rent);Hashemi v. Jim Owen & Co., No. 76AP-879, 1977 Ohio App.

LEXIS 8510 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Apr. 21, 1977) (notice to vacate for nonpayment of rent).

The duty to mitigate also exists where, absent any notice to vacate, a tenant vacates the premises before the lease or tenancy has expired. E.g., Oldendick v. Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016-Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); Snyder v. Waldron, No.

12-CA-9, 2013 WL 3988775 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 26, 2013); Zunshine v.

Wallace F. Ackley Co., No. 99AP-531, 2000 WL 329008 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar.

30, 2000); Hines v. Riley , 129 Ohio App.3d 379, 717 N.E.2d 1133 (4th Dist. 1998);

Angelo v. Parker, No. 13140, 1987 WL 25776 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 25, 1987);

Lyon v. Howard, No. 5-86-22, 1987 WL 20290 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Nov. 10, 1987);

519 Shenk v. Green, No. C-860415, 1987 WL 11204 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., May 13, 1987).

When a tenant vacates the premises before the lease or tenancy has expired, the “[l]andlord[] mitigate[s] by attempting to rerent the property.” Dennis v. Morgan, 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 419, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000).

The duty to mitigate requires a landlord to make reasonable efforts to obtain a new tenant for the property. Frenchtown Square P’ship v. Lemstone, 99 Ohio St.3d 254, 791 N.E.2d 417, ¶ 19 (2003); Dennis v. Morgan, 89 Ohio St. 3d 417, 419, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000).348 “What constitutes ‘reasonable efforts’ is a fact-specific determination….There is no bright-line test for determining reasonableness.” Tillimon v. Myles, No. L-17-1032, 2018 WL 679455, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Feb. 2, 2018).

“Whether a landlord made reasonable efforts to mitigate damages is a question of fact to be resolved by the trier of fact.” Beatley v. Schwartz, No. 03AP-911, 2004 WL 1244360, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 8, 2004). Accord Oldendick v. Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016-Ohio-5621, ¶ 62 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016).

The duty to mitigate “does not require a landlord to make extraordinary efforts to find a new tenant or attempt the unreasonable or impracticable.” Zunshine v. Wallace

### F. Ackley Co. , No. 99AP-531, 2000 WL 329008, at *5 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar.

30, 2000). Accord Oldendick v. Crocker , 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016 -Ohio-5621 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016); Beatley v. Schwartz, No. 03AP-911, 2004 WL 1244360 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 8, 2004); Applecrest Vill. Ltd. P’ship v. Yaple, No. 01-CA-185, 2003 WL 346301 (Ohio App., 7th Di st., Feb. 11, 2003); Hines v. Riley , 129 Ohio App.3d 379, 717 N.E.2d 1133 (4th Dist. 1998);Gerber v. Lewis, No. 70955, 1996 WL 715504 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 12, 1996);Lyon v. Howard, No. 5-86-22, 1987 WL 20290 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Nov. 10, 1987).

It also does not require a landlord to “unquestionably accept any and all potential 348 See also May v. Petrick , No. 2009 -CA-00031, 2010 WL 1452604 (Ohio App., 5th Dist.,
Apr. 8, 2010); Applecrest Vill. Ltd. P’ship v. Yaple, No. 01-CA-185, 2003 WL 346301 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 11, 2003); Zunshine v. Wallace F. Ackley Co. , No. 99AP-531, 2000 WL 329008 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 30, 2000); Hines v. Riley , 129 Ohio App.3d 379, 717 N.E.2d 1133 (4th Dist. 1998); Gerber v. Lewis, No. 70955, 1996 WL 715504 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 12, 1996); Bella Vista Apts. v. Arthon , No. CA90-10-106, 1991 WL 274313 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 23, 1991); Lyon v. Howard, No. 5-86-22, 1987 WL 20290 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Nov. 10, 1987); Briggs v. MacSwain , 31 Ohio App.3d 85, 508 N.E.2d 1028 (10th
Dist. 1986).

tenants who present themselves in order to reduce his damages.” Cuzzort v. Rose, No.

CA-9791, 1986 WL 12632, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 6, 1986). The landlord “must be mindful of the nature of a possible tenant and the impact on other existing tenants.” Id.

It also does not require a landlord to modify its policy of leasing available units in the order in which they become available. See Applecrest Vill. Ltd. P’ship v. Yaple,
No. 01-CA-185, 2003 WL 346301, ¶¶ 22,23 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 11, 2003);

Mentor Lagoons, Inc. v. Chalker , No. 86 -CVF-195 (Ohio Mun., Mentor, June 25, 1986).

“The failure to mitigate damages is an affirmative defense.” Hines v. Riley, 129 Ohio App.3d 379, 383, 717 N.E.2d 1133 (4th Dist. 1998). Accord Oldendick v.

Crocker, 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016-Ohio-5621, ¶ 62 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016);Beatley
v. Schwartz, No. 03AP-911, 2004 WL 1244360, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 8, 2004); Applecrest Vill. Ltd. P’ship v. Yaple , No. 01 -CA-185, 2003 WL 346301, ¶¶ 20,21 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Feb. 11, 2003);Zunshine v. Wallace F. Ackley Co., No.

99AP-531, 2000 WL 329008, at *5 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 30, 2000). “Thus, the burden is on the tenant to prove that the landlord failed to mitigate damages.” Hines
v. Riley, 129 Ohio App.3d at 383. Accord Oldendick v. Crocker , 70 N.E.3d 1033, 2016-Ohio-5621, ¶ 62 (Ohio Ap p., 8th Dist., 2016); Tecumseh Landing LLC v.

Bonetzky, 38 N.E.3d 1149, 2015-Ohio-2741, ¶ 35 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., July 6, 2015).

When a landlord mitigates damages, the tenant is liable for the rent under the lease until the end of the lease term or until the premises are relet, whichever occurs first.

Dennis v. Morgan, 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 419, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000).

If a landlord relets the premises before the end of the lease term for a higher rent than the rent under the first lease, an issue exists as to whether this difference in rent amounts may reduce the first tenant’s rental obligation under the first lease. Compare Strip Delaware, LLC v. Landry’s Rest., Inc.,No. 2010-CA-00316, 2011 WL 3587455, ¶¶ 22-29 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 15, 2011) (not reduced) with Way Int’l v. Ohio Ctr., 3 Ohio App.3d 451, 452, 445 N.E.2d 1158 (10th Dist. 1982) (reduced).

When the landlord mitigates damages, the tenant is also liable for the costs of the landlord’s reasonable efforts to re-rent the property. For exa mple, the landlord may recover the reasonable cost of newspaper advertisements that announce the availability of the rental unit. Carr v. Ed Stein Realtors, 10 Ohio App.3d 242, 461 N.E.2d 930 (9th
Dist. 1983); Alba v. Lockhart, No. 94-CVI-140 (Ohio County Ct., Jefferson Cty., Mar.

15, 1995).

521 A lease provision stipulating the amount of these re -renting expenses is an unenforceable penalty. Beatley v. Schwartz, No. 03AP-911, 2004 WL 1244360 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 8, 2004); Crest Mgmt., Inc. v. Kanonju , No. 15848, 1993 WL 107839 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr. 14, 1993). “[I]n order to be a valid liquidated damage clause rather than an invalid penalty, the damages for breach need to be ‘uncertain as to amount and difficult of proof.’” Kanonju, 1993 WL 107 839, at *1.

These re-renting expenses are neither uncertain as to amount nor difficult of proof.

Beatley, 2004 WL 1244360, ¶ 26; Kanonju, 1993 WL 107839, at *1,2.

One appellate court has held that the landlord’s duty to mitigate damages also applies where a tenant remains in occupancy of the rental premises without making rent payments for an extended period. May v. Petrick, No. 2009-CA-00031, 2010 WL 1452604 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 8, 2010). In May, the tenant failed to make rent payments for 30 months. The court held that the landlord had a duty to commence an eviction action to mitigate damages. It thereupon upheld the trial court’s decision that awarded the landlord a money judgment for unpaid rent for only six of those months, based on the landlord’s breach of the duty to mitigate damages.

### C. Defense Issues


#### 1. Collateral Estoppel

Because the doctrine of collateral estoppel applies to an eviction action (see Section I.M.8, above), if a court finds nonpayment of rent when ruling on an eviction claim, this finding is binding on the parties when the court later rules on the landlord’s monetary claim for nonpayment of rent. Great Lakes Mall, Inc. v. Deli Table, Inc. ,
No. 93 -L-154, 1994 WL 587559 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 16, 1994); Chan v.

Birch, No. 15249, 1992 WL 64190 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 25, 1992).

#### 2. Compulsory Counterclaim/Res Judicata

When a tenant brings a security deposit claim, the landlord’s claim for unpaid rent due at that time is a compulsory counterclaim to the tenant’s claim. See Section XVI.H.5.a, above.

Likewise, when a tenant brings a claim under R.C. 5321.04 or 5321.15, the landlord’s claim for unpaid rent due at that time is a compulsory counterclaim to the tenant’s claim. E.g., Lujan v. Smith, No. L -83-197, 1983 WL 6990 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 11, 1983).

Therefore, if a tenant brings and prevails on one of these cl aims, and the landlord does not file a counterclaim for unpaid rent due at that time, under the doctrine of res judicata, the landlord may not thereafter bring an action against the tenant for the unpaid rent. Lujan v. Smith, No. L-83-197, 1983 WL 6990 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov.

11, 1983) (R.C. 5321.04 claim and R.C. 5321.15 claim); Thirion v. Tutoki , 94 Ohio Misc.2d 77, 703 N.E.2d 378 (Ohio Mun., Warren, 1998) (security deposit claim).

#### 3. Security Deposit as Set-Off

One court has held that a tenant must fi le a counterclaim for the return of the security deposit in order to use the security deposit as a set -off against the landlord’s unpaid rent claim. Salem v. Roberts , No. 94 -CA-28, 1996 WL 131160 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 18, 1996). But this holding seems to conflict with Ohio common law.

See Section II.Q, above.

#### 4. Untimely Rent Refused

A landlord’s prior refusal to accept an untimely tender of rent does not waive the landlord’s right to assert a claim for that rent. Korom v. Pesek, No. 57559, 1990 WL 130909 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 13, 1990); Real Prop. Servs. Corp. v. Bonen, No.

93-CVG-7804 (Ohio Mun., Marion Cty., May 26, 1993).

#### 5. Unaccrued Rent

“Generally, a landlord may not recover unaccrued rent because such recovery ignores the possibility that the landlord could recover rent from a new tenant.” Schulz
v. Grimes, No. 91-CA-3, 1992 WL 24883, at *7 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 11, 1992).

Accord Gen. Dev. Corp. v. Wilbur-Rogers Atlanta Corp., 28 Ohio App.2d 35, 38, 273 N.E.2d 908 (1st Dist. 1971).

In addition, “termination [of the lease or tenancy] does not operate as a windfall for the landlord– the remaining rents under the agreement do not come due all at once.” Dennis v. Morgan, 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 419, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000).

This rule does not deprive landlords of an adequate remedy to recover the rents that accrue after an eviction judgment (“future rent”). An eviction judgment in favor of the landlord does not bar the landlord from bringing a separate action against the tenant for the future rent, if and when it becomes due and owing. Dennis v. Morgan, 89 Ohio St.3d 417, 419, 732 N.E.2d 391 (2000); Gen. Dev. Corp. v. Wilbur -Rogers Atlanta Corp., 28 Ohio App.2d 35, 37, 273 N.E.2d 908 (1st Dist. 1971). For additional 523 discussion on issues related to a separate action for future rent, see Sections I.E.2.e and I.M.8, above.

#### 6. Preclusion of Additional Claim for Unpaid

Security Deposit When a tenant ha s violated an obligation to pay a security deposit, at the end of the tenancy, the landlord may not recover both the unpaid security deposit and the full amount of any unpaid rent (or property damage) claim. Wayne Mgmt. Co. v. McGill,
No. 81-CA-57, 1982 WL 3741 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 2, 1982); Hall v. Leonard,
No. 94-CG-667 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Mar. 7, 1995).

#### 7. Preclusion of Additional Claim for Late Payment

Fee When a landlord is awarded a judgment for unpaid rent for a month, the landlord cannot be awarded a judgment for a late payment fee for that month. 200 W. Apts. v.

Foreman, No. 66107, 1994 WL 505271 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 15, 1994); Siara
Mgmt. v. Nedley, No. 61433, 1992 WL 292248 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 15, 1992);

Luca v. Volunteer s of Am., Cleveland, Ohio Post , No. 3756, 1985 WL 10731 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 19, 1985); Carr v. Ed Stein Realtors , 10 Ohio App.3d 242, 461 N.E.2d 930 (9th Dist. 1983).

In these circumstances the late payment fee is a penalty and thus unenforceable .

Id.

One trial court decision, which denied a late payment fee award in these circumstances, opted not to decide whether the late fee was an unenforceable penalty.

Allman v. Thorne, No. 2006-CVI-000629 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 10, 2006).

Instead, it concluded:

Late fees are designed to compensate the landlord for the loss of use of the rent they were due for whatever period they were deprived of it.

In this case, however, the recovery of the full amount due, plus interest from the date it was due, will adequately accomplish those ends. The court does not, therefore, separately award a judgment for late fees.

A second trial court decision used similar reasoning in its denial of a late payment fee award in these circumstances. Jordan v. McShan, No. 91-CVG-20057 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Apr. 9, 1992). It concluded:

Late charges, however, are, as the name implies, charges assessed when a landlord chooses to accept late rent, presumably to compensate the landlord for the inconvenience of processing late payments. In this case, however, rent was simply unpaid. To assess a late charge of Thirty-Five Dollars ($35.00) for each of those months that rent was unpaid would be tantamount to granting plaintiff a rent increase of Thirty-Five Dollars ($35.00) for each of those months. The court is unwilling to do so. Accordingly, plaintiff’s claim for unpaid late charges is denied.

A third trial court decision denied a late payment fee award in these circumstances because the landlord failed to prove his actual damages or foreseeable losses arising from the nonpayment. Sloan v. Greathouse , No. 11 -CVG-04935 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 17, 2012). The court denied the late fee award on two independent grounds. First, absent proof of theselosses, the court could “not conclude that the fee was in reasonable proportion to the loss which might be sustained.” Id., ¶ 16. Second, absent proof of these damages, the court could not “find that the actual damages plaintiff likely would suffer would be uncertain as to amount or difficult to prove.” Id., ¶ 17.

#### 8. Noncompliance with R.C. 5321.16(B) Itemization

Rule A landlord’s failure to comply with the R.C. 5321.16(B) written itemization requirement does not waive a landlord’s right to bring a monetar y claim (or
counterclaim) for unpaid rent or property damage. Trimble v. Rossi, No. 108683, 2020 WL 4218808 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 23, 2020) (property damage);Vlcek v. Brogee,
No. 25499, 2013 WL 5436930 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 27, 2013) (proper ty damage); Knipp v. Sadler, No. 6-09-04, 2009 WL 2710053 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Aug.

31, 2009) (rent and property damage); Adams v. Davenport, No. 2005-CA-108, 2006 WL 2578852 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 8, 2006) (rent and property damage);Kelley
v. Johnston, No. 01-CA-5, 2001 WL 1479243 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Nov. 14, 2001) (property damage); Quinn v. Humphrey, No. 92-L-141, 1993 WL 262582 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 30, 1993) (property damage); Scott v. Rice , No. 9 -92-48, 1993 WL 46480 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Feb. 11, 1993) (property damage); Sherwin v. Cabana Club Apts., 70 Ohio App.2d 11, 433 N.E.2d 932 (8th Dist. 1980) (property damage).

525

#### 9. Preclusion of Award of Both Liquidated and

Actual Damages When a lease contains a liquidated damages provision for a breach of the lease, a court may not award both liquidated damages and actual damages. Mentor Lagoons,
Inc. v. Laity, No. 10-184, 1985 WL 9999 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., May 24, 1985). “If a valid stipulation is made in the contract for payment of liquid ated damages, the amount stipulated becomes the measure of damages. No further recovery may be had by the complaining party.” Id., 1985 WL 9999, at *1. Accord Carr v. Ed Stein Realtors, 10 Ohio App.3d 242, 243, 461 N.E.2d 930 (9th Dist. 1983) (“the forf eiture of the deposit … imposed in addition to the actual damage suffered is clearly an unlawful penalty”).

#### 10. Fictitious Name (Landlord)

If the name of the landlord in a lease is a fictitious name, and if the landlord failed to register the fictitious name with the Secretary of State under R.C. 1329.01, the landlord may not bring an action under the lease for unpaid rent. Ebner v. Caudill, 93 Ohio App.3d 785, 639 N.E.2d 1231 (10th Dist. 1994); Frate v. Al-Sol, Inc., 131 Ohio App.3d 283, 722 N.E.2d 185 (8th Dist. 1999).

The case law is split on whether, in these circumstances, the landlord may maintain the action in the fictitious name if the landlord has registered the fictitious name after the filing of the action but before the entry of the final judgment . See Section II.D, below.

### D. Practice and Procedure Issues


#### 1. Default Judgment, Complaint for Eviction Claim

and Monetary Claim

##### a. Application of Civil Rule 55

When a landlord’s complaint includes an eviction claim and a monetary claim for unpaid rent (or property damage), the tenant’s presence at the eviction hearing constitutes an appearance in the action for Civil Rule 55(A) purposes. Koob v. Rue, 73 Ohio App.3d 418, 597 N.E.2d 550 (2nd Dist. 1992); Newlon v. Tallman, No. CA- 2520, 1988 WL 17835 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 22, 1988); Rader v. Swanson, No.

80AP-954, 1981 WL 3191 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 14, 1981);Harris v. Hampton,
No. 2005-CVG-43533 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Mar. 24, 2006).

Therefore, in such cases, if the landlord files an application for default judgment on the monetary claim, the tenant must be served with written notice of the application at least seven days prior to the hearing on the application. Ohio R. Civ. P. 55(A). See Koob, 73 Ohio App.3d at 420 -21; Newlon, 1988 WL 17835, at *1; Rader, 1981 WL 3191, at *1-2.

“Without the requisite notice and hearing under Civ.R. 55(A), a default judgment is void and shall be vacated upon appeal.” Hartmann v. Ohio Crime Victims Reparations Fund, 138 Ohio App.3d 235, 238, 741 N.E.2d 149 (10th Dist. 2000).

Accord AMCA Int’l Corp. v. Carlton, 10 Ohio St.3d 88, 461 N.E.2d 1282 (1984). See also Rader v. Swanson , No. 80AP-954, 1981 WL 3191 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 14, 1981) (reversed denial of Civil Rule 60(B) motion).

##### b. Application of Civil Rule 54(C)

When a landlord’s complaint includes an eviction claim and a monetary claim for unpaid rent (or property damage), Civil Rule 54(C) applies to the monetary c laim.

Masny v. Vallo, No. 84983, 2005 WL 1048237 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 5, 2005);

Buckley v. Lucas , No. 98 -CA-14, 1999 WL 436838 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 8, 1999); Borges v. Everdry Waterproofing of Wapakoneta, Inc. , 95 Ohio App.3d 175, 642 N.E.2d 16 (3rd Dist. 1994); Glazier v. Hall , No. 1076, 1994 WL 477994 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 18, 1994); 349 Wright v. Miller, No. 752, 1991 WL 37926 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Mar. 11, 1991); Tribby v. Gudzinas, No. 90-T-4403, 1991 WL 18673 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Feb. 15, 1991); Tolle v. Morris , 90AP -040034, 1990 WL 163807 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct 18, 1990); Doan Gardens, Ltd. v. Beverly , No.

56551, 1989 WL 65646 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 15, 1989);Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Coley , No. 2001 -CVG-19335 (Ohi o Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 2, 2003, and Jan. 29, 2004);Forte v. Bellan, No. 91-CVG-18631 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Oct. 28, 1991).

When the complaint demands both a specified amount for past due rent and “further rental amounts that may a ccrue during the pendency of this action,” the amount of the default judgment for the landlord may include the past due rent specified plus the “rent pro-rated to the date of restitution.” Buckley v. Lucas, No. 98-CA-14, 349 The Glazier decision expressly overrules the cou rt’s prior holding on this issue in Pietro v.

Hershberger, No. 90AP-110065, 1991 WL 76473 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 2, 1991).

527 1999 WL 436838, at *2 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 8, 1999).

When the complaint demands both a specified amount for past due rent and a specified amount per month for ongoing rent, the amount of the default judgment for the landlord may include the respective amounts specified for past du e rent and ongoing rent up to the date of restitution. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Coley, No.

2001-CVG-19335 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., May 2, 2003, and Jan. 29, 2004). It may not include, for any month during the pendency of the action, a ren tal amount more than the specified ongoing rent. Id.

When the complaint demands only a specified amount for past due rent and late payment fees and the filing fee, the amount of the default judgment for the landlord may not include any rent that accrues d uring the pendency of the action. Masny v.

Vallo, No. 84983, 2005 WL 1048237 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 5, 2005).

A tenant may file a Civil Rule 60(B)(5) motion to enforce these Civil Rule 54(C)
limits. Masny v. Vallo , No. 84983, 2005 WL 1048237, ¶ 20 ( Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 5, 2005). A “trial court’s failure to comply with Civ.R. 54(C) constitutes a sufficient ground under Civ.R. 60(B)(5) justifying relief from judgment.” Id.

#### 2. Joinder of Parties, Civil Rule 19(A)


##### a. Co-Tenant Owners

If the premises are owned by two or more tenants in common, under Civil Rule 19(A), all of the owners are necessary parties in an action against a tenant for unpaid
rent. Peters v. Durroh , 28 Ohio App.2d 245, 277 N.E.2d 69 (10th Dist. 1971). Cf.

Oda v. Davis , 81 Ohio App.3 d 555, 611 N.E.2d 933 (2nd Dist. 1992) (managing partner and partnership are co -owners of unpaid rent claim; under Civil Rules 17(A) and 19(A), managing partner may not bring the claim without joinder of the partnership or all other partners).

A failure to join a co-tenant owner (“absent owner”) creates a risk that the absent owner will later assert the absent owner’s own claim against the tenant for unpaid rent.

Peters v. Durroh, 28 Ohio App.2d 245, 277 N.E.2d 69 (10th Dist. 1971). Cf. Carter
v. Davis, No. 87-CVG-1420-29 (Ohio Mun., East Cleveland, Aug. 22, 1987) (right of a co-tenant to demand the rent when another co-tenant fails to demand and collect the rent).

##### b. Guarantor of Lease

A guarantor of a tenant’s lease obligation to pay rent is not an indispensable party, under Civil Rule 19(A), to the landlord’s claim for rent against the tenant. Fed.

Knitting Mills Ltd. v. Harper , 157 Ohio Misc.2d 42, 930 N.E.2d 879 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, 2010).

The tenant and the guarantor are jointly and severally liable. Id. A landlord’s judgment against a tenant on a rent claim is not res judicata against a guarantor, when the landlord has not made the guarantor a party to the action. Id.

However, the guarantor has a due process right to notice and hearing on the underlying unpaid rent claim, before being held liable for any unpaid rent. Id. A lease provision that waives the guarantor’s right to notice of the tenant’s rent delinquency does not waive this due process notice and hearing right. Id.

#### 3. Interest Award on Judgments

The appellate districts are split on whether a residential lease may establish a contractual interest rate for judgments that is higher than the statutory interest rate under R.C. 1343.03. Compare Meadowbrook Dev. Corp. v. Roberts, No. 79747, 2001 WL 1654540 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 27, 2001) (rejects an 18% interest rate and applies the statutory rate) with Buchenroth v. Adkins , 7 N.E.3d 621, 2014 -Ohio-257 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014) (upholds an 18% interest rate).

R.C. Chapter 5321 does not expressly address the permissible interest rate for judgments or whether a residential lease may establish a specific interest rate for
judgments. See Meadowbrook Dev. Corp. v. Roberts, No. 79747, 2001 WL 1654540, at *4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 27, 2001).

529

## XVIII. Landlord Claim for Property

Damage, R.C. 5321.05(C)

### A. Introduction

When a tenant fails to maintain or use the premises in compliance with the tenant’s R.C. 5321.05 obligations,350 the landlord may bring an action against the tenant under R.C. 5321.05(C) for actual damages, reasonable attorney’s fees, and, where applicable, injunctive relief to access the premises.

The landlord has “the burden to prove [tenant’s] negligent or intentional conduct by a preponderance of the evidence….[The tenant does] not have to prove that she did not violate the statute or the terms of the lease.”Douglas v. Campost Props., LLC, No.

2016-CV-102094, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 21, 2020).

### B. Normal Wear and Tear

In an R.C. 5321.05(C) claim, the tenant is not liable for normal wear and tear to the premises. Hensel v. Childress, 145 N.E.3d 1159, 2019-Ohio-3934 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., 2019); Warner v. Evans, No. 27536, 2015 WL 3398400 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 27, 2015); Snyder v. Waldron, No. 12-CA-9, 2013 WL 3988775 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 26, 2013); Whitestone Co. v. Stittsworth, No. 06AP-371, 2007 WL 155299 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 23, 2007);Bibler v. Nash, No. 5-05-09, 2005 WL 2335191 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 26, 2005); Johnson v. Brown, No. 2002-CA-76, 2003 WL 1193795 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 14, 2003);Kelley v. Johnston, No. 01-CA-5, 2001 WL 1479243 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Nov. 14, 2001); Cuzzort v. Rose, No. CA-9791, 1986 WL 12632 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 6, 1986);Mentor Lagoons, Inc. v. Mayor,
No. 10-180, 1985 WL 7807 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 1, 1985);Konig v. Bella Dora Mgmt., Inc., 9 Ohio Misc.2d 7, 458 N.E.2d 477 (Ohio Mun., Akron, 1983).

For example, “[w]hen a tenant vacates a property after a tenancy of eleven (11) years, normal wear and tear typically requires the carpeting to be replaced and the interior walls to be painted.”Elsayed v. Miamisburg by the Mall, No. 28918, 2021 WL 2028661, ¶ 23 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 21, 2021) (adopting trial court’s statement).

350 For a discussion on the tenant’s R.C. 5321.05(A) obligations, see Section VII.D, above. For a discussion on the tenant’s R.C.5321.05(B) obligations, see Section VII.E, above.

And “[g]eneral cleaning costs may not be recovered as damages unless they are over and above ordinary wear and tear.”Schaedler v. Shinkle, No. CA99-09-025, 2000 WL 1283776, at *3 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Sept. 11, 2000). Accord May v. Metcalf,
No. 11-CA-8, 2011 WL 5625805, ¶ 33 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 15, 2011);McNeill
v. Wasserbauer, No. 72846, 1998 WL 323592, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Ju ne 18, 1998). Also, “[i]t is doubtful that any court would permit a landlord to impose a standard of ‘professional’ cleaning on a tenant in a residential rental property.” Douglas v. Campost Props., LLC , No. 2016 -CV-102094, slip op. at 4 (Ohio Mun., Elyria, July 21, 2020).

Normal wear and tear inclu des “injuries to the building due to the gradual deterioration which results from use, lapse of time, and the operation of the elements” and excludes “injuries from the tenant’s improper or negligent use of the premises.” 49 Am. Jur. 2d Landlord and Tenant § 973 at 946 (1970).

“[O]ne of the critical questions when determining if items are in need of repair due to normal wear and tear, or due to tenant conduct, is whether the items were originally installed or r epaired in a workmanlike way.” Brown v. Williams , No. 2012 -CVG- 17491, slip op. at 4 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 11, 2014, and May 2, 2014). In Brown, the court held that “Plaintiff [landlord] had not met her burden of proving that it was Defendant’s [tenant’s] conduct, and not the poor quality of repairs, that caused the problem.” Id.

Some courts have used the following guideline: “If damage is not of the type specified in R.C. 5321.05 or the lease, it will normally be considered ordinary w ear and tear.” Kelley v. Johnston, No. 01-CA-5, 2001 WL 1479243, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Nov. 14, 2001). Accord Elsayed v. Miamisburg by the Mall , No. 28918, 2021 WL 2028661, ¶ 33 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 21, 2021); Levine v. Kellogg , 153 N.E.3d 663, 2020-Ohio-1246, ¶ 40 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020); Estie Inv. Co. v.

Braff, No. 2017-L-172, 2018 WL 5433223.¶ 25 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 29, 2018).

But n o general rule categorically defines the limits of normal wear and tear.

“When determining ordinary wear and tear, the court must look to the circumstances of each case.” Hensel v. Childress , 145 N.E.3d 1159, 2019 -Ohio-3934, ¶ 26 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., 2019).

The court decisions summarized below provide some guidance on th e scope of normal wear and tear.

531 Elsayed v. Miamisburg by the Mall , No. 28918, 2021 WL 2028661 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 21, 2021) Condition/repair: sealing concrete beneath carpet due to pet stain, flea treatments, and extra painting due to cat urine an d scratches. Court decision (affirms): each of these items constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Tillimon v. Tate, No. L-18-1237, 2019 WL 3383890 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., July 12, 2019) Condition/repair: changing and re-keying the locks. Court decision (affirms): the cost for these items is part of the normal cost of doing business and do not constitute damage beyond normal wear and tear.

Wead v. Wade, No. 27204, 2017 WL 1806820 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 5, 2017) Condition/repair: dogs and cats urinated on the carpet, requiring replacement of the carpet and pad . Court decision ( reverses): the carpet was only two years old and in good condition at the start of the tenancy, and thus would not normally be replaced at the end of this one-month tenancy.

Tillimon v. Richardson-Long, No. L-16-1055, 2017 WL 169267 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Jan. 13, 2017) Condition/repair: cleaned carpet and trash removal. Court decision: affirms denial of carpet cleaning cost, since noabnormal dirt or stains and need for carpet cleaning after two years was result of normal wear and tear; reverses denial of trash removal cost, since two truckloads of garbage does not constitute normal wear and tear.

Riggle v. McFadden, No. 08-CA-0007, 2008 WL 4766737 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov.

3, 2008) Condition/repair: cleaned the apartment and replaced the carpet due to extensive damage by cigarette smoke, including a pervasive odor, yellow stains on the walls and registers, and burn marks on the car pet. Court decision (affirms): these items constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Schaedler v. Shinkle , No. CA99 -09-025, 2000 WL 1283776 (Ohio App., 12th Dist.,
Sept. 11, 2000) Condition/repair: cleaned trailor, shampooed carpet, and replaced torn paper window
shade. Court decision (affirms): condition of the trailor, including the carpet, did not constitute damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

VanHoose v. McGee , No. CA97 -04-008, 1997 WL 598127 (Ohio App., 12th Dist .,
Sept. 29, 1997) Condition/repair: replaced toilet paper holder, shampooed carpet, and tested gas
fixtures. Court decision (affirms): only the need to replace the toilet paper holder constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Fornof-Lippencott v. Powell, No. 94-CA-11, 1995 WL 19127 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Jan. 18, 1995) Condition/repair: (1) damage to the carpet, woodwork, and doors caused by the tenant’s dogs, (2) clogged drain in the dishwasher, (3) damaged toilet seat, and (4) some missing ductwork that served as a vent for the dryer in the basement. Court decision (reverses in part): damage to the carpet, woodwork, and doors constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear; damage to the dishwasher, toilet seat, and ductwork did not constitute damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Quinn v. Humphrey, No. 92-L-141, 1993 WL 262582 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 30, 1993) Condition/repair: trash throughout unit, filthy stove, hole in plaster wall and screen door, torn wallpaper, and broken water faucet. Court decision (reverses): each of these items constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Wilhelm v. Ison, No. 92-CA-97, 1993 WL 76961 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 15, 1993) Condition/repair: cleaned carpet. Court decision (reverses): condition of the carpet did not constitute damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Scott v. Rice, No. 9-92-48, 1993 WL 46480 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Feb. 11, 1993) Condition/repair: painted two walls due to crayon marks on the walls. Court decision (affirms): crayon marks on the walls constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Swartz v. Luker, No. CA91-07-051, 1991 WL 278243 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 30, 1991) Condition/repair: cleaned and painted walls and baseboards. Court decision (affirms):

none of these items constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Saam v. Mosser, No. S-86-25, 1986 WL 15255 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 24, 1986) Condition/repair: defrosted refrigerator, painted the apartment, and replaced three light
bulbs. Court decision (affirms): none of these items constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Brown v. Williams, No. 2012-CVG-17491, slip op. at 4 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous.

Div., Mar. 11, 2014, and May 2, 2014) Condition/repair: painted interior walls and hardwood floors in high traffic areas where paint had come off the floor, secured ceiling tiles that were sagging due to age, resealed 533 two toilets that had come loose over time, and re -attached ceramic soap dish that had come loose over time. Court decision: none of these items constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Gonzalez v. Gonzalez, No. 2010-CVG-9940 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug.

22, 2011) Condition/repair: carpet dirty, stove dirty and burners replaced. Court decision: none of these items constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Johnson v. Morelli Props. LLC , No. 2008 -CVF-023275 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty.,
Oct. 7, 2009) Condition/repair: burned out light bulbs and broken steps. Court decision: both items constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

Williams v. Wright, No. 2000-CVG-01040 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Aug. 15, 2001) Condition/repair: removed trash, painted and wallpapered due to marks on the walls, and replaced carpet due to “burning holes” in the carpet. Court decision: all of these items constituted damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear.

### C. Measure of Damages


#### 1. Real Property

In a landlord’s claim against a tenant for damage to the premises, the measure of damages, where restoration is feasible, is the reasonable cost of restoration, plus reasonable compensation for the loss of the use of the property. Martin v. Design
Constr. Servs., Inc., 121 Ohio St.3d 66, 902 N.E.2d 10 (2009). See also Booth v. Duffy Homes, Inc. , 185 Ohio App.3d 260, 923 N.E.2d 1175 (10th Dist. 2009) (applies Martin).

The landlord “need not prove a diminution in the market value of the property in order to recover the reasonable cost of restoration, but either party may offer evidence of diminution of market value of the property as a factor bearing on the reasonableness of the cost of restoration.”351 Martin, 121 Ohio St.3d 66, ¶ 24.

Under this rule, “restoration costs and loss of use of the property are separate and distinct types of damages ... and the absence of one does not preclude recovery for the 351 Under the owner opinion rule, the owner (see Section XVIII.F.3, below) and possibly the tenant (see Section IX.A.11.b, above) may give opinion testimony on the market value of the premises pre-damage and post-damage.

other.” Henderson v. Spring Run Allotment , 99 Ohio App.3d 633, 641, 651 N.E.2d 489 (9th Dist. 1994).

If an owner elects not to restore the real property, the owner can “still show entitlement to damages based on proof that the property damage(beyond normal wear and tear) resulted in a reduction in the market value of the property.”Levine v. Kellogg, 153 N.E.3d 663, 2020 -Ohio-1246, ¶ 49 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020). Accord Cubbedge-Parker v. Dillard, No. L-15-1245, 2016 WL 3219274, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 2016).

#### 2. Personal Property

As a general rule, the measure of damages for damage to personal property is the difference between its market value immediately before and immediately after the
damage. Falter v. City of Toledo , 169 Ohio St. 238, 158 N.E.2d 893 (1959); Gibson
v. Shephard , 87 N.E.3d 846, 2017 -Ohio-1157 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2 017) (tenant claim against landlord); Warner v. Zuccola, No. 53070, 1987 WL 30379 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 1987) (landlord claim against tenant); Cuzzort v. Rose , No. CA - 9791, 1986 WL 12632 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 6, 1986) (landlord claim agains t tenant); Cooper v. Feeney , 34 Ohio App.3d 282, 518 N.E.2d 46 (12th Dist. 1986) (tenant claim against landlord); Woodard v. Walker, No. 78AP-495, 1979 Ohio App.

LEXIS 12212 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 27, 1979) (tenant claim against landlord).

If the party who asserts the claim does not prove the difference between the value of the personal property pre -damage and post -damage, the party has failed to prove damages under the general rule.352 E.g., Warner, 1987 WL 30379, at *2;Cuzzort, 1986 WL 12632, at *2-3; Woodard, 1979 Ohio App. LEXIS, at *7.

When personal property is totally destroyed, the measure of damages is the reasonable market value of the property immediately before its destruction, less any salvage value. Falter v. City of Toledo, 169 Ohio St. 238, 158 N.E.2d 893 (1959);Oak Hill Inv. Co. v. Jablonski , 78 Ohio App.3d 643, 605 N.E.2d 998 (6th Dist. 1992) (landlord claim against tenant); Cooper v. Feeney, 34 Ohio App.3d 282, 518 N.E.2d 46 (12th Dist. 1986) (tenant claim against landlord).

352 A landlord may give opinion testimony on the value of the landlord’s personal property pre- damage and post -damage under the owner opinion rule. See Section XVIII.F.3, below.

Likewise, a tenant may give opinion testimony on the value of the tenant’s personal property pre-damage and post-damage under the owner opinion rule. See Section IX.A.11.b, above.

535 When personal property does not have a market value or its market value clearly does not reflect the injury suffered by the owner, the court may use a standard of value to the owner. Bishop v. East Ohio Gas Co., 143 Ohio St. 541, 56 N.E.2d 164 (1944);

Cooper v. Feeney, 34 Ohio App.3d 282, 518 N.E.2d 46 (12th Dist. 1986) (tenant claim against landlord); Craft v. Oney, No. 8766, 1984 WL 4060 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct.

17, 1984) (tenant claim against landlord). “This value [to the owner] is determined via consideration of a number of factors including value to the owner, original cost, replacement cost, salvage value, if any, and fair market value at the time of loss.” Cooper, 34 Ohio App.3d at 284.

Also, in some circumstances, the measure of dam ages for damage to personal property may be the cost of restoring the property to its condition before the damage
occurred. Adcock v. Rollins Protective Servs. Co., 1 Ohio App.3d 160, 440 N.E.2d 548 (1st Dist. 1981). E.g., Kenwood Courts Apts. v. Williams, No. K90-CVG-1043 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Oct. 25, 1991) (tenant claim against landlord).

### D. Reasonable Attorney’s Fees

In an R.C. 5321.05(C) claim, a landlord generally may recover reasonable attorney’s fees if the tenant violated an R.C. 5321.05 oblig ation. E.g., Hensel v.

Childress, 145 N.E.3d 1159, 2019-Ohio-3934 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., 2019). Snyder v.

Waldron, No. 12 -CA-9, 2013 WL 3988775 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 26, 2013);

Zimmerman v. Elmore , No. 47724, 1984 WL 5119 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 28, 1984); Katchmer v. Bishop, No. 90-CVG-34263 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Oct. 3, 1991).

But the case law is split on whether the tenant’s liability for attorney’s fees under R.C. 5321.05(C) is mandatory or lies within the discretion of the trial court. Compare Kinn v. Showe Mgmt. Corp. , No. 5 -01-46, 2002 WL 462859 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist.,
Mar. 27, 2002) (mandatory) with Fiffick v. Showe Mgmt., No. 2005-P-0011, 2005 WL 3610442 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 29, 2005) (discretionary).

For post-judgment work, the landlord’s entitlement to attorney’s fees under R.C.

5321.05(C) lies within the sound discretion of the trial court. Chapman v. Smith, No.

L-81-344, 1982 WL 6883 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 16, 1982).

Also, in two circumstances, the landlordcannot recover attorney’s fees under R.C.

5321.05(C), notwithstanding the tenant’s R.C. 5321.05 violation.

One is where: (1) an action includes the landlord’s R.C. 5321.05(C) claim and the tenant’s R.C. 5321.16(C) security deposit claim, (2) the amountof the R.C. 5321.05(C) judgment for the landlord is less than the portion of the security deposit that the landlord retained, and (3) the tenant is thus awarded an R.C. 5321.16(C) judgment.

Danello v. Holyko, No. 50339, 1986 WL 3471 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 20, 1986).

The second is where: (1) the amount of the landlord’s R.C. 5321.05(C) claim (and
judgment) is less than the tenant’s security deposit, and (2) the tenant forfeited the security deposit without dispute. Hines v. Riley , 129 Ohio App.3d 379, 717 N.E.2d 1133 (4th Dist. 1998); Snapp v. Rossiter, No. 14-CVF-00579 (Ohio Mun., Cambridge,
Aug. 27, 2015) (applying Hines).

The determination on the amount of the attorney’s fees to be awarded under R.C.

5321.05(C) lies within the sound discretion of th e trial court. Kinn v. Showe Mgmt.

Corp., No. 5-01-46, 2002 WL 462859 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Mar. 27, 2002).

When an action includes an R.C. 5321.05(C) claim along with other landlord claims (e.g., unpaid rent claim), the computation of the R.C. 5321.05 (C) attorney fee award is based solely on the time spent on the R.C. 5321.05(C) claim. Hines v. Riley, 129 Ohio App.3d 379, 717 N.E.2d 1133 (4th Dist. 1998); Svoboda v. Hayes, No. 90- CVG-35237 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Dec. 26, 1991);Schuab v. Roper, No.

90-CVG-26859 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 6, 1991).

For a discussion on issues relative to calculating and moving for R.C. Chapter 5321 attorney’s fees, see Chapter XIX, below.

### E. Defense Issues


#### 1. Collateral Estoppel

Because the doctrine o f collateral estoppel applies to an eviction action (see Section I.M.8, above), if a court finds an R.C. 5321.05 violation when ruling on an eviction claim,353 this finding is binding on the parties when the court later rules on the landlord’s monetary claim under R.C. 5321.05(C). Cf. Great Lakes Mall, Inc. v. Deli Table, Inc., No. 93-L-154, 1994 WL 587559 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 16, 1994) (nonpayment of rent); Chan v. Birch , No. 15249, 1992 WL 64190 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 25, 1992) (nonpayment of rent).

353 Under R.C. 5321.11 and 1923.02(A)(8), a tenant’s R.C. 5321.05 violation may be grounds to terminate the tenancy and evict the tenant. See Section I.D, above.

537

#### 2. Required Link Between the Damage and an R.C.

5321.05(A) Violation “A landlord bears the burden of submitting sufficient evidence linking any alleged damage to the tenant’s failure to fulfill obligations under R.C. 5321.05 or a lease agreement.” Whitestone Co. v. Stittsworth , No. 06AP-371, 2007 WL 2334795, ¶ 26 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 23, 2007). Accord Levine v. Kellogg , 153 N.E.3d 663, 2020-Ohio-1246, ¶ 40 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2020); Hensel v. Childress, 145 N.E.3d 1159, 2019-Ohio-3934, ¶ 28 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., 2019);Warner v. Evans, No. 27536, 2015 WL 3398400, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 27, 2015); Snyder v. Waldron, No.

12-CA-9, 2013 WL 3988775, ¶ 28 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 26, 2013); Cincinnati
Ins. Co. v. Evans , No. WD -09-012, 2010 WL 2334795, ¶ 46 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 11, 2010); Dupal v. Reindle, No. 79413, 2002 WL 450128, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 14, 2002);Kelley v. Johnston, No. 01-CA-5, 2001 WL 1479243, at *3 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Nov. 14, 2001).

“To establish the necessary link, the landlord must generally present evidenc e regarding the condition of the premises both before the tenant moved in and after he moves out.” Estie Inv. Co. v. Braff, No. 2017-L-172, 2018 WL 5433223. ¶ 26 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 29, 2018). Accord Dupal v. Reindle , No. 79413, 2002 WL 450128, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 14, 2002).

Also, if a landlord fails to prove that the damage occurred during the tenancy, the landlord has not met this burden of proof. Vlcek v. Brogee , No. 25499, 2013 WL 5436930, ¶¶ 47,48 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 27, 2013); Kelley v. Johnston, No. 01- CA-5, 2001 WL 1479243, at *4 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Nov. 14, 2001).See also Dupal
v. Reindle, No. 79413, 2002 WL 450128, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 14, 2002).

And, if the tenant did not violate R.C. 5321.05 (A), it necessarily follows that the need to clean the premises is not the result of an R.C. 5321.05(A) violation. Kovac v.

Whay Corp., No. 65469, 1994 WL 581528, at *6 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 20, 1994).

In these circumstances, the cost of cleaning i s simply part of the landlord’s “normal cost of doing business.” Id.

In addition, if a landlord’s general practice is to make certain improvements to the dwelling unit for each new tenant, whether or not the underlying condition constitutes damage to the premises beyond normal wear and tear, the improvement is not a cost incurred as a result of an R.C. 5321.05(A) violation. E.g., Cuzzort v. Rose, No. CA- 9791, 1986 WL 12632 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 6, 1986) (replaces toilet seats);

Snapp v. Rossiter , No. 14 -CVF-00579 (Ohio Mun., Cambridge, Aug. 27, 2015) (replaces throw rugs); Johnson v. Morelli Props. LLC , No. 2008-CVF-023275 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 7, 2009) (thoroughly cleans; re-keys locks); Berkeley Park, LLC v. Pile , No. 2005 -CVG-046214 (O hio Mun., Franklin Cty., Apr. 11, 2007) (“tune[s]” units, with painters and cleaners); Cliffview Manor Apts. v. Jefferson , No.

91-CVG-11203 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 27, 1992) (cleans carpets).

Two other examples of a landlord not meeting t his burden of proof are: Jones v.

Simondis, No. 97 -T-0073, 1998 WL 156871 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 27, 1998) (door blown off hinges by wind, an act of God);Gonzalez v. Gonzalez, No. 2010-CVG- 9940 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Aug. 22, 2011) (bro ken windows – no evidence on causation).

#### 3. Proof of Damage Amount

When a landlord claims damages to the premises, the landlord must prove the damages or cost of restoration with reasonable certainty. Estie Inv. Co. v. Braff , No.

2017-L-172, 2018 WL 5433223 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 29, 2018); Gibson v.

Shephard, 87 N.E.3d 846, 2017 -Ohio-1157 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2017); Baker v.

Wade, No. 2002-CVG-15892 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 2, 2002). See generally Jam es H. Washington Ins. Agency v. Nationwide Mut. Ins. Co. , 95 Ohio App.3d 577, 643 N.E.2d 143 (8th Dist. 1993). This standard is not met where the landlord’s evidence is conjecture and speculation. Id.

This standard is illustrated by the followingfour cases in which the landlord failed to meet it.

In one case, this standard was not met where a landlord testified, relative to the cost of repairing broken windows, that he “thinks the cost will be around $1,000.00,” but he “failed to produce estimates, receipts or testimony to justify how he arrived at the above amount.” Baker v. Wade, No. 2002-CVG-15892, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 2, 2002).

In a second case, this standard was not met where the landlord introduced only photographs of the premises. Moore-El v. Petrella , No. 94749, 2011 WL 553877 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 17, 2011). The court held that “those photographs alone cannot supplant evidence of the actual costs in the form of bills or invoices for the alleged repairs, taking into consideration normal wear and tear during Moore -El’s tenancy.” Id., ¶ 11.

In a third case, this standard was not met where the landlord’s estimate to repair 539 and replace damaged drywall was not limited to the area of the hole that the tenant had
made. Vlcek v. Brogee, No. 25499, 2013 WL 5436930, ¶ 53 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Sept. 27, 2013). Rather, it was an estimate to repair and replace the drywall throughout the entire house. Id., ¶ 52.

In a fourth case, this standard was not met where: (1) the landlord produced evidence of the total cost of repairing eleven damage items, without a breakdown of the cost of each item; and (2) the court upheld the landlord’s damage claim for only seven of those items. Estie Inv. Co. v. Braff, No. 2017-L-172, 2018 WL 5433223, ¶ 44 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 29, 2018).

#### 4. Damage Claim Less Than the Security Deposit

When the amount of the landlord’s R.C. 5321.05(C) damage claim is less than the amount of the tenant’s security deposit, and the tenant has forfeited the security deposit, the landlord has not suffered any “actual damages” as a result of the tenant’s R.C. 5321.05(A) violation. Hines v. Riley , 129 Ohio App.3d 379, 717 N.E.2d 1133 (4th Dist. 1998).

#### 5. Preclusion of Additional Claim for Unpaid

Security Deposit When a tenant has violated an obligation to pay a security deposit, at the end of the tenancy, the landlord may not recover both the unpaid security deposit and the full amount of any property damage (or unpaid rent) claim. Wayne Mgmt. Co. v. McGill,
No. 81-CA-57, 1982 WL 3741 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 2, 1982); Hall v. Leonard,
No. 94-CG-667 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Mar. 7, 1995).

#### 6. Noncompliance with R.C. 5321.16(B) Itemization

Rule A landlord’s failure to comply with the R.C. 5321.16(B) written itemiz ation requirement does not waive a landlord’s right to bring a monetary claim for property damage (or unpaid rent). For a discussion on this issue, see Section XVII.C.8, above.

#### 7. Joint and Several Liability

A joint and several liability provision in a resi dential lease is enforceable. Vlcek
v. Brogee, No. 25499, 2013 WL 5436930, ¶ 56 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 27, 2013);

Wayne Mut. Ins. Co. v. Parks , No. 20945, 2002 WL 1800377, ¶¶ 15 -17 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Aug. 7, 2002). R.C. Chapter 5321 does not prohibit these provisions. Id.

When a lease contains a joint and several liability provision, a tenant is liable for any breach of the lease by any of the tenants. Id.

For a discussion on the effect of a joint and several liability provision, in the context of an alleged violation of R.C. 5321.05(A)(6) by a co -tenant or guest, see Section VII.D.3.b, above.

### F. Practice and Procedure Issues


#### 1. Default Judgment, Complaint for Eviction Claim

and Monetary Claim

##### a. Application of Civil Rule 55

When a landlord’s complaint includes an eviction claim and a monetary claim for property damage (or unpaid rent), the tenant’s presence at the eviction hearing constitutes an appearance in the action for Civil Rule 55(A) purposes. For a discussion on this issue, see Section XVII.D.1.a, above.

##### b. Application of Civil Rule 54(C)

When a landlord’s complaint includes an eviction claim and a monetary claim for property damage (or unpaid rent), Civil Rule 54(C) applies to the monetary claim. For a discussion on this issue, see Section XVII.D.1.b, above.

#### 2. Joinder of Parties, Civil Rule 19(A)

If the premises are owned by two or more tenants in common, under Civil Rule 19(A), all of the owners are necessary parties in an action against a tenant for property damage (or unpaidrent). For a discussion on this issue, see Section XVII.D.2.a, above.

#### 3. Owner Opinion Rule

Under the owner opinion rule, a landlord may give opinion testimony on the value of the premises pre-damage and post-damage and on the cost of repairing the premises.

Rowe v. Sylvester, Nos. 65014,65022, 1994 WL 118371 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 7, 1994); Kearney v. Crown, No. 93-T-4945, 1994 WL 587382 (Ohio App., 11th Dist.,
Aug. 12, 1994). See generally Tokles & Son, Inc. v. Midwestern Indem. Co., 65 Ohio St.3d 621, 605 N.E.2d 936 (1992); Bishop v. East Ohio Gas Co., 143 Ohio St. 541, 56 N.E.2d 164 (1944).

541 Likewise, when a landlord’s personal property has been damaged, the landlord may give opinion testimony on the value of the property pre-damage and post-damage.

See generally Tokles & Son, Inc. v. Midwestern Indem. Co. , 65 Ohio St.3d 621, 605 N.E.2d 936 (1992); Bishop v. East Ohio Gas Co. , 143 Ohio St. 541, 56 N.E.2d 164 (1944).

#### 4. Admissibility of Estimates

An estimate of the cost of repairing the premises is not admissible as a business record under Evidence Rule 803(6) without the testimony of the person who actually prepared the estimate. Amjoy Co. v. Braggs, No. 64708, 1994 WL 43886 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 10, 1994); Mong v. Dotson, No. 1173CA, 1987 WL 8029 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 17, 1987).

As a result, a landlord’s written repair estimates from specified stores – Home Depot and Lowe’s– for replacing a carpet were not admissible because the “source of that information is wholly unidentified. In order to be credible, evidence must be elicited from a competent source.” Bailey v. Fairchild , No. 10 -CA-10, 2010 WL 4867532, ¶¶ 26,27 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 24, 2010).

#### 5. Admissibility of Photographs

In an R.C. 5321.05(C) claim, the landlord may introduce into evidence relevant
photographs. Hensel v. Childress, 145 N.E.3d 1159, 2019-Ohio-3934 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., 2019). “Authentication of photographs simply requires that a witness, based on his familiar ity with the subject matter of the photographs, establish that the photographs depict what the proponent claims they represent.” Id., ¶ 7. For a discussion on the tenant’s analogous right to introduce into evidence relevant photographs in support of an R.C. 5321.04 claim, see Section IX.A.11.e, above.

## XIX. Attorney’s Fees


### A. Authority Under R.C. Chapter 5321

The R.C. Chapter 5321 provisions that authorize a tenant to recover attorney’s fees are:
(1) R.C. 5321.02(B) (retaliation, see Chapter XI, above);
(2) R.C. 5321.04(B) (entry upon the premises, see Chapter X, above);
(3) R.C. 5321.15(C) (utility termination, lockout, seizure of personal property, see Chapters XII and XIII, above); and
(4) R.C. 5321.16(C) (security deposit, see Chapter XVI, above).

These provisions “serve[ ] as encouragement to the private bar to provide representation to tenants who normally could not afford to hire an attorney.” Drake v.

Menczer, 67 Ohio App.2d 122, 123, 425 N.E.2d 961 (8th Dist. 1980). They permit tenants to enforce their statutory right s without incurring attorney fee expense s that such enforcement requires and that exists solely because of their landlord’s action.See Alcorso v. Correll, No. 110218, 2021 WL 4317396, ¶ 49 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept.

23, 2021).

The R.C. Chapter 5321 provisions that authorize a landlord to recover attorney’s fees are:
(1) R.C. 5321.05(C) (maintenance/use of the premises, see Chapter XVIII, above); and
(2) R.C. 5321.09(D) (bad faith rent depositing, see Section VIII.E, above).

### B. Authority Under Common Law

The general rule in Ohio is t hat, absent statutory authorization, a prevailing party may not recover attorney’s fees. Wilborn v. Bank One Corp., 121 Ohio St.3d 546, 906 N.E.2d 396, ¶ 7 (2009);Sorin v. Bd. of Educ., Etc., 46 Ohio St.2d 177, 179, 347 N.E.2d 527 (1976); State ex rel. Grosser v. Boy , 46 Ohio St.2d 184, 185, 347 N.E.2d 539 (1976). See generally Alyeska Pipeline Serv. Co. v. Wilderness Soc’y , 421 U.S. 240
(1975) (discussion of American rule on attorney’s fees); Fleischman Distilling Corp.
v. Maier Brewing Co., 386 U.S. 714 (1967) (same).

However, there are numerous exceptions to this general rule.

543 One exception is that attorney’s fees are allowed when the defendant acted “in bad faith, vexatiously, wantonly, or for oppressive reasons.”Sorin v. Bd. of Educ., Etc., 46 Ohio St.2d 177, 181, 347 N.E.2d 527 (1976). Accord Wilborn v. Bank One Corp., 121 Ohio St.3d 546, 906 N.E.2d 396, ¶ 7 (2009);Taylor v. McCullough-Hyde Mem’l Hosp., 116 Ohio App.3d 595, 600, 688 N.E.2d 1078 (12th Dist. 1996); Sutherland v.

Nationwide Gen. Ins. Co. , 102 Ohio App.3d 297, 301, 657 N.E.2d 281 (10th Dist.

1995); Sladoje v. Slettebak , 44 Ohio App.3d 206, 207, 542 N.E.2d 701 (10th Dist.

1988).

A second exception is that attorney’s fees are allowed when punitive damages are
awarded. Villella v. Waikem Motors, Inc. , 45 Ohio St.3d 36, 41, 543 N.E.2d 464 (1989); Columbus Fins. Inc. v. Howard , 42 Ohio St.2d 178, 183, 327 N.E.2d 654 (1975); Davis v. Tunison, 168 Ohio St. 471, 477, 155 N.E.2d 904 (1959); Roberts v.

Mason, 10 Ohio St. 277, 282 (1859) (the seminal opinion); Sutherland v. Nationwide
Gen. Ins. Co., 102 Ohio App.3d 297, 301, 657 N.E.2d 281 (10th Dist. 1995).

Under this second exception, attorney’ s fees are also allowed when punitive damages properly could have been awarded but were not awarded. Atram v. Star Tool & Die Co., 64 Ohio App.3d 388, 581 N.E.2d 1110 (8th Dist. 1989).

However, when attorney’s fees are awarded in connection with punitivedamages, the prevailing party might not be entitled to attorney’s fees for time spent defending the judgment on appeal. See LaFarciola v. Elbert , No. 08 -CA-009471, 2009 WL 2858059 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 8, 2009).

A third exception is that attorney’s fees are allowed when an enforceable contract specifically provides that the losing party shall pay the prevailing party’s attorney’s
fees. Wilborn v. Bank One Corp. , 121 Ohio St.3d 546, 906 N.E.2d 396, ¶ 7 (2009);

Nottingdale Homeowners’ Ass’n, Inc. v. Darby, 33 Ohio St.3d 32, 34, 514 N.E.2d 702 (1987). These provisions are not allowed in residential leases. R.C. 5321.13(C).

A fourth exception is that attorney’s fees are allowed in an action on the breach of a settlement agreement. Scott v. Reedy, 5 Ohio Dec. Reprint 388 (1876).

A fifth exception is that attorney’s fees are allowed in a civil contempt proceeding, if the defendant is found guilty of civil contempt. State ex rel. Fraternal Order of Police v. City of Dayton, 49 Ohio St.2d 219, 230, 361 N.E.2d 428 (1977).

A sixth exception is that attorney’s fees are allowed “when the litigation has created or protected a common fund.” Rocca v. Wilke , 53 Ohio App.2d 8, 18, 371 N.E.2d 223 (1st Dist. 1977). Accord Sutherland v. Nationwide Gen. Ins. Co., 102 Ohio App.3d 297, 300-01, 657 N.E.2d 281 (10th Dist. 1995); City of Seven Hills v. City of Cleveland, 47 Ohio App.3d 159, 163 -64, 547 N.E.2d 1024 (8th Dist. 1988). See generally Trs. of Internal Improvement Fund v. Greenough , 105 U.S. 527 (1881) (announcing the “common fund” doctrine).

Equity alone is not a basis for awarding attorney’s fees. In Sorin v. Bd. of Educ., Etc., 46 Ohio St.2d 177, 182 n.4, 347 N.E.2d 527 (1976), the Supreme Court reversed an equitable award of attorney’s fees and stated that it was “unpersuaded by the ‘reasoning’ of the Court of Appeals inChannell ....” The Channell decision awarded attorney’s fees on purely equitable grounds. Channell v. N.C.R. Employee’s Indep.

Union, 28 Ohio App.2d 260, 263-64, 277 N.E.2d 85 (2nd Dist. 1971).

## C. Attorney’s Fees Taxed as Costs

Attorney’s fees under R.C. Chapter 5321 are taxed as costs; they are not awarded as damages. Christe v. GMS Mgmt. Co. , 88 Ohio St.3d 376, 726 N.E.2d 497 (2000) (R.C. 5321.16(C)); Alcoroso v. Correll, 159 N.E.3d 924, 2020-Ohio-4752 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2020) (R.C. 5321.16(C)) Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 4859073 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 12, 2012) (R.C. 5321.15(C)); Schultz v. Wurdlow , No.

11AP-62, 2012 WL 2859914 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 12, 2012) (R.C. 5321.16(C));

Jerels v. Begue, No. 24700, 2010 WL 1780140 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 5, 2010) (R.C. 5321.16(C)); Kinn v. Showe Mgmt. Corp., No. 5-01-46, 2002 WL 462859 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Mar. 27, 2002) (R.C. 5321.05(C)); Croxford v. Litchfield, No. 1491, 1992 WL 20818 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Jan. 27, 1992) (R.C. 5321.02(B)); Yarber v.

Cooper, No. H -87-36, 1988 WL 61003 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 1988) (R.C.

5321.02(B) and 5321.04(B)); Rand v. Washington , No. 7822, 1983 WL 2448 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 26, 1983) (R.C. 5321.15(C));Drake v. Menczer, 67 Ohio App.2d 122, 425 N.E.2d 961 (8th Dist. 1980) (R.C. 5321.02(B)). But see Chaney v. Davis ,
No. 17593, 1996 WL 525851 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 18, 1996) (R.C. 5321.02(B));

Fay Gardens Mobi le Home Park v. Newman , 14 Ohio App.3d 144, 470 N.E.2d 164 (12th Dist. 1983) (construing R.C. 3733.10(B) (now R.C. 4781.38(B)) – the manufactured home park provision that is essentially the same as R.C. 5321.04(B)).

Christe v. GMS Mgmt. Co., 88 Ohio St.3d 376, 726 N.E.2d 497 (2000), only holds that attorney’s fees under R.C. 5321.16(C) are to be taxed as costs. The Supreme Court declined the opportunity in Christe to extend its ruling to the other provisions for attorney’s fees in R.C. Chapter 5321.

Nonetheless, the Supreme Court’s reasoning in Christe compels the conclusion that attorney’s fees under any section of R.C. Chapter 5321 are to be taxed as costs.

545 See Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 4859073, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct.

12, 2012) (“We see no logical reason for attorney fees awarded in actions under R.C.

5321.15 to be handled differently than those awarded under R.C. 5321.16”);Staffilino Chevrolet, Inc. v. Balk , 158 Ohio App.3d 1, 813 N.E.2d 940, ¶ 14 (7th Dist. 2004) (“Although the court limited its analysis to that statutory section [R.C. 5321.16(C)], the rationale can reasonably be extended to all similar statutes”).

The reasoning in Christe is four-fold. First, “[u]nder common law, attorney fees are in the nature of costs.” 88 OhioSt. 3d at 378. Second, “we [Supreme Court] have repeatedly held that when a statute authorizes the awarding of attorney fees, it does so by allowing the fees to be taxed as costs rather than awarded as damages.” Id. Third, “the legislature could have expressly stated in R.C. 5321.16(C) that attorney fees are recoverable damages.” Id. Fourth, “in the absence of such express language, we are unwilling to depart from our long -standing practice of treating statutorily authorized attorney fees as costs.” Id.

Likewise, the legislature could have expressly stated in the other R.C. Chapter 5321 provisions for attorney’s fees that the fees are recoverable damages, but it failed to do so. Therefore, for these other provisions as well, the Christe reasoning dictates adherence to the longstanding practice of courts of treating statutorily authorized attorney’s fees as costs. Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 4859073, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 12, 2012); Drake v. Menczer, 67 Ohio App.2d 122, 425 N.E.2d 961 (8th Dist. 1980). See also Yarber v. Cooper, No. H-87-36, 1988 WL 61003 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 10, 1988) (follows Drake); Fant v. DiSabato , No. 87AP -265, 1987 WL 32190 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 29, 1987) (follows Drake).354

### D. Absence of Attorney Fee Demand in

the Pleadings In an R.C. Chapter 5321 claim for attorney’s fees, a tenant’s (or landlord’s) failure 354 The Drake holding concerns R.C. 5321.02(B), but the decision addressed this issue for all of the R.C. Chapter 5321 attorney fee provisions. The reasoning in Drake is similar to the reasoning in Christe. It noted and applied the trad itional view that, “when a statute authorizes the award of attorneys’ fees, it does so by allowing the fees to be taxed as costs.” 67 Ohio App.2d at 124. It found that the legislature did not express an intent to deviate from the traditional view because, if it had intended to do so, the relevant provision in R.C. 5321.02(B) would have stated “actual damages, including reasonable attorneys’ fees.” Id.

to demand the fees in the pleadings does not waive the tenant’s (or landlord’s) right to recover them. Leszczynski v. Brewer, No. 12523, 1991 WL 285435 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 27, 1991) (R.C. 5321.16(C)); Zimmerman v. Elmore, No. 47724, 1984 WL 5119 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 28, 1 984) (R.C. 5321.05(C)); Morales v. Windsor Realty & Mgmt., Inc. , No. 2000 -CVH-002037 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div.,
Nov. 21, 2000) (R.C. 5321.16(C));Zemaitis v. Waugh, No. 91-CVG-5137 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 11, 1991) (R.C. 5321.05(C)). See generally Ohio R. Civ.
P. 54(C) (“Except as to a party against whom a judgment is entered by default, every final judgment shall grant the relief to which the party in whose favor it is rendered is entitled, even if the party has not demanded the re lief in the pleadings”).355 But see Biasella v. Costakis , No. 71281, 1997 WL 253157 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 15,
1997) (R.C. 5321.16(C)).

### E. Time Period for Filing a Motion for

Attorney’s Fees Since attorney’s fees under R.C. Chapter 5321 are taxed as costs (see Section XIX.C, above), the time period for filing a motion for attorney’s fees should be the time period for filing a Civil Rule 54(D) motion to tax costs.

Civil Rule 54(D) does not provide a specific time period for filing a motion to tax
costs. Bookatz v. Kupps , 39 Ohio App.3d 36, 528 N.E.2d 961 (8th Dist. 1987).

Therefore, a motion to tax costs must be filed within a reasonable period of time. Id.

In Bookatz, the court held that a m otion to tax costs had been filed within the requisite reasonable time, even though it had been filed two months after the final judgment and one month after the time to perfect an appeal had expired.

Other court decisions have provided different time periods for filing a motion for attorney’s fees. See, e.g., McGinnis v. Donatelli, 36 Ohio App.3d 120, 521 N.E.2d 513 (8th Dist. 1987) (a motion for attorney’s fees under R.C. 5321.16(C) must be filed 355 The 1994 amendment to Civil Rule 54(C) “makes the division identical to Fed. R. Civ. P.

54(c).” Staff Notes, Ohio R. Civ. P. 54. Under Fed. R. Civ. P. 54(c), the failure to demand attorney’s fees in the pleadings does not waive the right of a party to recover attorney’s fees.

Capital Asset Research Corp. v. Finnegan, 210 F.3d 1268 (11th Cir. 2000).

547 before the time to perfect an appeal has expired). 356 Cf. Fair Hous. Advocates Ass’n,
Inc. v. James, 114 Ohio App.3d 104, 682 N.E.2d 1045 (9th Dist. 1996) (time for filing a motion for attorney’s fees under 42 U.S.C. § 3613(c)(2) is governed by state law, which is before the entry of the final judgment).

Given these conflicting holdings, if the tenant (or landlord) has requested attorney’s fees in a pleading, and if the court has not established a time period for filing the motion for attorney’s fees, the tenant (or landlord) should file the motion shortly after the entry of the court’s order that finds that the tenant (or landlord) is entitled to an attorney fee award.

### F. Standards for Computing the Amount of

an Attorney Fee Award

#### 1. General Standard

In an R.C. Chapter 5321 claim for attorney’s fees, the court determines the amount of the attorney fee award based on the guidelines set forth now in Rule 1.5(a) of the Ohio Rules of Professional Conduct (and pre -2007 in DR 2 -106(B) of the Code of Professional Responsibility). Christe v. GMS Mgmt. Co. , 88 Ohio St.3d 376, 726 N.E.2d 497 (2000); Alcorso v. Correll, No. 110218, 2021 WL 4317396 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 23, 2021); Clarke v. Royal, No. 20AP-366, 2021 WL 2443751 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 15, 2021);Timoneri v. Northsteppe Realty, 71 N.E.3d 630, 2016- Ohio-5901 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2016); Whitestone Co. v. Stittsworth , No. 06AP - 371, 2007 WL 155299 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 23, 2007); Ridenour v. Dunn, No.
