03AP-611, 2004 WL 1445270 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 29, 2004); Meacham v.

Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992); Yarber v. Cooper , 61 Ohio App.3d 609, 573 N.E.2d 713 (6th Dist. 1988); Nobis v. E.A. Zicka Co., 31 Ohio App.3d 104, 508 N.E.2d 980 (1st Dist. 1986);Drake v. Menczer, 67 Ohio App.2d 122, 356 When a motion for attorney’s fees under R.C. Chapter 5321 is pending in the trial court, the judgment on the underlying claim is not a final appealable order. Ingram v. Glaser, No. CA- 11511, 1989 WL 130801 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 1, 1989); Peterson v. Supinger, No. 90- CV-H-T-279 (Ohio Mun., Miami Cty., Sept. 11, 1991).

425 N.E.2d 961 (8th Dist. 1980). 357 See generally Bittner v. Tri-County Toyota, Inc., 58 Ohio St.3d 143, 569 N.E.2d 464 (1991). The Rule 1.5(a) guidelines are:
(1) The time and labor required, the novelty and difficulty of the questions involved, and the skill requisite to perform the legal services properly;
(2) The likelihood, if apparent to the client, that the acceptance of the employment will preclude other employment by the lawyer;
(3) The fee customarily charged in the locality for similar legal services;
(4) The amount involved and the results obtained;
(5) The time limitations imposed by the client or by the circumstances;
(6) The nature and length of the professional relationship with the client;
(7) The experience, reputation, and ability of the lawyer or lawyers performing the services; and
(8) Whether the fee is fixed or contingent.

“The factors specified in divisions (a)(1) through (8) are not exclusive. Nor will each factor be relevant in each instance.” Official Comment, Rule 1.5, Ohio Rules of Professional Conduct. See also Bittner v. Tri-County Toyota, Inc., 58 Ohio St.3d 143, 146, 569 N.E.2d 464 (1991) (“All factors may not be applicable in all cases and the trial court has the discretion to determine which factors to apply, and in what manner that application will affect the initial calculation”).

“[T]he trial court should first calculate the ‘lodestar amount,’ which is the number of hours reasonably expended on the case multiplied by an hourly fee. Then the court may modify that calculation by application of the factors listed in DR 2 -106(B) [now Rule 1.5(a)].” Latio v. Rapien, No. C-970887, 1998 WL 655482, at *1 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept. 25, 1998). Accord Nolan v. Sutton, 97 Ohio App.3d 616, 647 N.E.2d 218 (1st Dist. 1994). See generally Bittner v. Tri-County Toyota, Inc., 58 Ohio St.3d 143, 569 N.E.2d 464 (1991).

357 See also Stark v. Weimer , No. H-98-031, 1999 WL 253451 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Apr. 30, 1999); Davis v. Reed , Nos. 68699,68700, 1996 WL 347814 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 20, 1996); Breault v. Williamsburg Estates, No. L-86-116, 1986 WL 13169 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Nov. 21, 1986); Hoerner v. GMS Mgmt. Co., No. 46736, 1983 WL 2892 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Dec. 15, 1983); Lewis v. Romans, No. 43594, 1981 WL 4713 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec . 24, 1981); Blue v. Castlerock Props., Inc. , 77 Ohio Misc.2d 3, 665 N.E.2d 296 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1996).

549 If the court modifies the lodestar amount, it is required to state the reasons for the modification, based on the evidence presented. Parks v. Kunani, No. 02AP-916, 2003 WL 1701499 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 31, 2003).

#### 2. Inclusion of Time Spent Litigating the Attorney

Fee Award In an R.C. Chapter 5321 claim for attorney’s fees, the moving party may recover attorney’s fees for the time spent litigating the fee request, as well as for time spent litigating and enforcing the underlying judgment. Hall v. Lacheta, No. 92AP-020013, 1992 WL 362546 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 18, 1992). Cf. Westbourne Dev. Co. v.

Sigma Sys. Health Mgmt. Specialists, Inc. , No. 91AP -856, 1992 WL 11197 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 16, 1992) (att orney’s fees under commercial lease). But see Alexander v. LJF Mgmt. Inc., No. C-100618, 2011 WL 2112623 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., May 27, 2011) (R.C. 5321.16(C)).

#### 3. Inclusion of Time of Legal Intern

“[T]he efforts of a legal intern on behalf of a client isproperly compensable as part of an award of attorney fees.”Jackson v. Brown, 83 Ohio App.3d 230, 232, 614 N.E.2d 847 (8th Dist. 1992). Accord Non-Employees of Chateau Estates Resident Ass’n v.

Chateau Estates, Ltd., Nos. 2004-CA-19,20, 2004 WL 1587234, ¶ 33 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 9, 2004).

#### 4. Exclusion of Time Spent on Other Claims

When an action includes one or more claims for which attorney’s fees may be awarded under R.C. Chapter 5321, and one or more other claims for which attorney’s fees cannot be awarded under R.C. Chapter 5321, the computation of the attorney fee award under R.C. Chapter 5321 is based solely on the time spent on the R.C. Chapter 5321 claims for which attorney’s fees may be awarded. See Section X.B, above (R.C.

5321.04(B) claim); Section XII.B.2, above (R.C. 5321.15(C) claim); Section XVI.H.4, above (R.C. 5321.16(C) claim); Section XVIII.D, above (R.C. 5321.05(C) claim).

#### 5. Limitation Based on the Amount of the

Underlying Judgment In an R.C. Chapter 5321 action, because the amount of the money judgment is often relatively small, the amount of the attorney fee award may be limited by Rule 1.5(a)(4) of the Ohio Rules of Professional Conduct , which requires that attorney’s fees be reasonable in relation to the amount involved an d the results obtained. E.g., Nolan v. Sutton , 97 Ohio App.3d 616, 647 N.E.2d 218 (1st Dist. 1994) ($4,170 fee requested, $500 fee awarded); Cook v. Downey , No. 87AP -1075, 1988 WL 70446 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 30, 1988) ($4,322.76 fee requested, $650 fee awarded);

Lewis v. Romans , No. 43594, 1981 WL 4713 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 1981) ($8,800 fee requested, $350 fee awarded). See also Smith v. Padgett , 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 350, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987) (Wright, J., concurring).

However, as one cou rt observed, in applying this factor, it is important to be mindful that “limiting the attorney fees available might discourage attorneys from taking these cases”358 and thereby undermine an important purpose of R.C. Chapter 5321 – to discourage violations of the Act. Johnson v. Morelli Props. LLC, No. 2008- CVF-023275, slip op. at 5 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Oct. 7, 2009) ($3,350 fee award, $960 judgment). E.g., Timoneri v. Northsteppe Realty , 71 N.E.3d 630, 2016 -Ohio- 5901 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2016) (a ffirms $10,505 fee award, $695 judgment);

Spring Hill Townhomes v. Pounds , No. 25887, 2014 WL 1887690 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 9, 2014) ( affirms $4,637.50 fee award, $539 judgment) ; Liston v. Jurek ,
No. 59758, 1992 WL 41858 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 5, 1992) (affirms $3,972 fee award, $313.70 judgment).

Another court similarly observed: “Simply because the attorney fees incurred in this case were greatly disproportionate to the damages recovered on [the tenant’s] security deposit claim does not mean t hey were unreasonable. As stated above, R.C.

5321.16 is a remedial statute intended to ensure that a tenant incurs no expense when seeking a refund of a wrongfully withheld security deposit. Consistent with that purpose, [the tenant] was entitled to reco ver reasonable attorney fees [the landlord’s] actions forced her to incur to enforce her statutory rights.” Alcorso v. Correll , No.

110218, 2021 WL 4317396, ¶ 49 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 23, 2021) ($26,825 fee award, $1,200 judgment).

Appellate court decisions that found an abuse of discretion and reversed the trial court’s attorney fee award because the fee award was significantly less than the lodestar amount include: Congdon v. Senters, No. 99-CA-43, 1999 WL 1071768 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 16, 1999);Hall v. Lacheta, No. 92AP-020013, 1992 WL 362546 358 For a statement on the purpose of these fees, see Section XIX.A, above.

551 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 18, 1992).

Congdon concerned a tenant’s R.C. 5321.16(C) claim for attorney’s fees. The trial court awarded the tenant a judgment of $550 for the security deposit, $550 for statutory damages, and $600 for attorney’s fees. Based on the trial court’s findings on reasonable hours (36.7) and reasonable rate ($140 per hour), the lod estar amount was $5,138.359 Hall concerned a tenant’s R.C. 5321.15(C) claim for attorney’s fees. The trial court awarded the tenant a judgment of $500 for the R.C. 5321.15 claim, $2,500 for an invasion of privacy claim, and $3,632 for attorney’s fees. Thetrial court’s finding on reasonable hours (86.8) was not disputed. But despite the uncontested testimony on reasonable rate ($75 per hour), the trial court used hourly rates of $50 (in -court) and $40 (out-of-court).

Conversely, a ppellate court decisio ns that found an abuse of discretion and reversed the trial court’s attorney fee award because the amount of the fee award was too high in relation to the amount of the judgment include:Nelson v. Tipton, No. 99AP- 277, 1999 WL 1041154 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 18, 1999) ($26,472.39 fee award, $3,200 judgment); McGregor v. Armeni , No. 89AP -1500, 1990 WL 179981 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Nov. 20, 1990) ($5,809.76 fee award, $465 judgment); Fant v.

DiSabato, No. 87AP -265, 1987 WL 32190 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 29, 1987) ($3,712.50 fee award, $255 net judgment); Burroughs v. Douglas , No. 46030, 1983 WL 2865 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 8, 1983) ($11,727.50 fee award, $6,500 judgment)..

This Rule 1.5(a)(4) issue often arises in security deposit cases and, wit hin that context, it is discussed in Section XVI.H.4, above.

#### 6. Limitation Based on the Absence of Difficulty

In some R.C. Chapter 5321 actions, courts have found that, because the case was neither difficult nor novel, the amount of the attorney fee award sho uld be limited under Rule 1.5(a)(1). E.g., Latio v. Rapien, No. C-970887, 1998 WL 655482 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Sept. 25, 1998) ($2,478.75 fee requested, $750 fee awarded);Liston v.

Jurek, No. 59758, 1992 WL 41858 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 5, 1992) ($1 1,750 fee requested, $3,972 fee awarded); Posey v. Mentor Lagoons , No. 58868, 1991 WL 117972 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 20, 1991) ($2,870 fee requested, $750 fee 359 For further discussion on the attorney fee ruling in Congdon, see Section XVI.H.4, above.

awarded); Lewis v. Romans, No. 43594, 1981 WL 4713 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24,
1981) ($8,800 fee requested, $350 fee awarded).

#### 7. No Limitation Based on Contingency Agreement

When a contingency agreement exists, the amount of the attorney fee award under R.C. Chapter 5321 is not limited to the amount specified in the contingency agreement.

Bynum v. Huffman, No. 57730, 1990 WL 210225 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 20, 1990).

The reasonable attorney’s fees that the court awards may exceed the contingency
amount. Id.

#### 8. No Limitation Based on Free Legal Services

Attorneys who provide clients with lega l services at minimal or no cost may recover attorney’s fees under R.C. Chapter 5321 to the same extent as other attorneys.

Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 4859073 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 12, 2012);

Chaney v. Breton Builder Co., 130 Ohio App.3d 602, 720 N.E.2d 941 (6th Dist. 1998);

Lewis v. Romans, 70 Ohio App.2d 7, 433 N.E.2d 622 (8th Dist. 1980).

As stated in Lewis, 70 Ohio App.2d at 9, the reason for this conclusion is:

The Landlord and Tenant Act enunciates a clear policy of awarding attorney’s fees to a tenant who must seek legal redress in the courts because of acts committed by a landlord in violation of the act. No exceptions are made by the language of R.C. 5321.15(C) for individuals represented at no charge.

A similar reason is stated in Chaney, 130 Ohio App.3d at 607-08:

[T]he language of R.C. 5321.16 does not restrict the trial court’s ability to award attorney fees in cases where tenants have obtained legal representation at little or no cost. Such a limitation would allow landlords to arbitrarily benefit from the “fortuitous circumstance” of a tenant’s inability to afford a private attorney.

“When a tenant has actually incurred no out -of-pocket attorney fees, the amount of fees determined to be proper should be awarded directly to th e attorney or organization that provided the legal services.” Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 4859073, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 12, 2012). Accord Lewis v. Romans , 70 Ohio App.2d 7, 9, 433 N.E.2d 622 (8th Dist. 1980).

553

### G. Evidence in Support of the Motion for

Attorney’s Fees

#### 1. Time for Presenting the Evidence

Attorney’s fees under R.C. Chapter 5321 are taxed as costs. See Section XIX.C, above.

“Because attorney fees [under R.C. Chapter 5321] are assessed as costs rather than as damages, a tenant is not required to offer evidence of the amount of those attorney fees at trial.” Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 4859073, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 12, 2012). Accord Croxford v. Litchfield, No. 1491, 1992 WL 20818, at *1 (Ohio App., 4th Dis t., Jan. 27, 1992); Fant v. DiSabato , No. 87AP -265, 1987 WL 32190, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 29, 1987); Motz v. Liptak, No. CA-10310, 1987 WL 14269, at *3 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 9, 1987); Berlinger v. Suburban
Apt. Mgmt. Co. , 7 Ohio App.3d 12 2, 126, 454 N.E.2d 1367 (8th Dist. 1982). See generally Int’l Bd.of Elec. Workers, Local Union No. 8 v. Vaughn Indus., L.L.C., 116 Ohio St.3d 335, 879 N.E.2d 187, ¶¶ 14,15 (2007).

“An evidentiary hearing may be held to determine the reasonable amount of attorney fees to be awarded following a judgment finding that the tenant is entitled to attorney fees.” Gaitawe v. Mays, No. 25083, 2012 WL 4859073, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 12, 2012).

#### 2. Presentation of the Evidence Via Affidavits or a

Hearing In an R.C. Chapter 5321 claim for attorney’s fees, evidence in support of (or in opposition to) the fee request may initially be presented to the court through affidavits.

Manninen v. Alvarez, No. CA2016-01-017, 2016 WL 4426451, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Aug. 22, 2016); Manninen v. Alvarez, No. CA2013-CA-106, 2014 WL 107852, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 13, 2014);Oakwood Mgmt. Co. v. Young, No. 92AP- 207, 1992 WL 324338, at *4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Oct. 27, 1992); Breault v.

Williamsburg Estates, No. L -86-116, 1986 WL 13169, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Nov. 21, 1986); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Watkins, 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 25, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984);Drake v. Menczer, 67 Ohio App.2d 122, 126, 425 N.E.2d 961 (8th Dist. 1980) (Krenzler, J., concurring). Cf. Westbourne Dev. Co. v. Sigma Sys.

Health Mgmt. Specialists, Inc., No. 91AP-856, 1992 WL 11197, at *3 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 16, 1992) (attorney’s fees under commercial lease; “if the parties to an action agree that attorney’s fees should be determined via a non -oral hearing based upon affidavits, the trial court could proceed in that fashion”).

However, if there is conflicting evidence or the non -moving par ty otherwise challenges the fee request, the court must hold an evidentiary hearing to resolve the
issue. Meacham v. Miller , 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992);

Oakwood Mgmt. Co. v. Young, No. 92AP207, 1992 WL 324338 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Oct. 27, 1992); Mazer v. Fish, No. C-890345, 1990 WL 88752 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 27, 1990). See also Davis v. Reed , Nos. 68699,68700, 1996 WL 347814 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 20, 1996). Cf. Westbourne Dev. Co. v. Sigma Sys. Health Mgmt.

Specialists, Inc., No. 91AP-856, 1992 WL 11197 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 16, 1992) (commercial lease).

The moving party’s lawyer has a right to testify to the court with respect to the amount of attorney services provided. Nobis v. E.A. Zicka Co., 31 Ohio App.3d 104, 508 N.E.2d 980 (1st Dist. 1986). This right becomes a requirement if a timely objection is made to the attorney’s affidavit of attorney hours, since the affidavit evidence is hearsay. See Fay Gardens Mobile Home Park v. Newman, 14 Ohio App.3d 144, 470 N.E.2d 164 (12th Dist. 1983).

The non -moving party has a right to confront and cross -examine the moving party’s attorney with respect to the amount of attorney services provided and to the other factors relevant to the reasonableness of the fee request. Meacham v. Miller, 79 Ohio App.3d 35, 606 N.E.2d 996 (4th Dist. 1992);Mazer v. Fish, No. C-890345, 1990 WL 88752 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 27, 1990). See, e.g., Hoerner v. GMS Mgmt.

Co., No. 46736, 1983 WL 2892 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 15, 1983).

Evidence of the reasonableness of the attorney’s hours and hourly rate is usually presented through the testimony of an attorney who is familiar with the type of case involved and the hourly rate customarily charged in the locality for similar legal
services. See, e.g., Hall v. Lacheta, No. 92AP-020013, 1992 WL 362546 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Nov. 18, 1992); Budac v. Nahmi, No. 14901, 1991 WL 115908 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 19, 1991); Schuab v. Roper , No. 90 -CVG-26859 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Nov. 6, 1991).

#### 3. Burden of Proof

In an R.C. Chapter 5321 claim for attorney’s fees, the moving party has the burden of proving the attorney services rendered, the reasonableness of the attorney’s hours, 555 the attorney’s hourly rate, and the attorney fee requ ested. Alcorso v. Correll , No.

110218, 2021 WL 4317396 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 23, 2021);Helfrich v. Mellon,
No. 06-CA-69, 2007 WL 1881537 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 27, 2007); Breault v.

Williamsburg Estates, No. L-86-116, 1986 WL 13169 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 21, 1986); Choi v. Katz, No. 44819, 1982 WL 2607 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 18, 1982);

Myers v. Loudy, No. 80-CA-8, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 11895 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Oct. 21, 1980). Cf. Enyart v. Columbus Metro. Area Cmty. Action Org. , 115 Ohio App.3d 348, 685 N.E.2d 550 (10th Dist. 1996) (R.C. 4123.90 claim).

The moving party must present “evidence going to the factors set forth in DR 2 - 106(B) [now Rule 1.5(a)].”Davis v. Reed, Nos. 68699,68700, 1996 WL 347814, at *7 (Ohio App., 8th Dis t., June 20, 1996). Most importantly, the moving party must present evidence of the number of attorney hours spent on the case, the type of services performed, and the fees customarily charged in the locality for similar services.Zamos
v. Dunn, No. 2000-P-0120, 2001 WL 1149844 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 28, 2001);

Myers v. Loudy, No. 80-CA-8, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 11895 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Oct. 21, 1980). See also Drake v. Menczer, 67 Ohio App.2d 122, 126, 425 N.E.2d 961 (8th Dist. 1980) (Krenzler, J., concurring).

A moving party’s failure to present evidence of the amount and nature of the attorney services provided and the reasonable value of that service is grounds for denying an attorney fee request. Myers v. Loudy , No. 80 -CA-8, 1980 Ohio App.

LEXIS 11895 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 21, 1980). See also Warner v. Zuccola, No.

53070, 1987 WL 30379 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 1987).

It is also grounds for reversing an attorney fee award.360 Choi v. Katz, No. 44819, 1982 WL 2607 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 18, 1982).See also Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.
Auth. v. Watkins, 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984).

The moving party may not rely on the trial judge’s presence in the case, experience, and knowledge of prevailing fees in the locality. Myers v. Loudy, No. 80-CA-8, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 11895 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 21, 1980).See also Nobis v. E.A.

Zicka Co., 31 Ohio App.3d 104, 508 N.E.2d 980 (1st Dist. 1986). But cf. Enyart v.

Columbus Metro. Area Cmty. Action Org., 115 Ohio App.3d 348, 358, 685 N.E.2d 550 (10th Dist. 1996) (R.C. 4123.90 claim; “[w]hile the better practice is to receive 360 In an appeal of an attorney’s fees award under R.C. Chapter 5321, the standard of review is abuse of discretion. Smith v. Padgett, 32 Ohio St.3d 344, 513 N.E.2d 737 (1987). See generally Bittner v. Tri-County Toyota, Inc., 58 Ohio St.3d 143, 569 N.E.2d 464 (1991).

testimony regarding the reasonableness of fees, a trial court under some circumstances is permitte d to use its own knowledge in reviewing the record to determine the reasonableness and necessity of the services rendered”).

Likewise, the moving party may not rely solely on the amount of attorney’s fees
paid. Warner v. Zuccola, No. 53070, 1987 WL 30379 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 1987).

### H. Attorney’s Fees for Time Spent

Litigating an Appeal In an R.C. 5321 claim for attorney’s fees, if a party is awarded fees and then successfully defends an appeal of the judgment, the party is entitled to recover reasonable attorney’s fees for the time spent on the appeal (“appellate attorney’s fees”).

Klein v. Moutz, 118 Ohio St.3d 256, 888 N.E.2d 404 (2008);Cristal v. DRC Int’l, Inc., 74 Ohio App.3d 493, 599 N.E.2d 706 (8th Dist. 1991); Breault v. Williamsburg Estates, No. L-86-116, 1986 WL 13169 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 21, 1986). See also Christe v. GMS Mgmt. Co. , 88 Ohio St.3d 376, 379, 726 N.E.2d 497 (2000) (Lundberg Stratton, J., concurring in judgment only). Cf. Parker v. I & F Insulation Co., No. C -980502, 1999 WL 420066 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., June 25, 1999) (R.C.

1345.09(F) claim, relies in part on Breault). But see Zeallear v. F & W Props. , No.

99AP-1215, 2000 WL 1015345 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., July 25, 2000) (declines to award appellate attorney’s fees because the appeal is not frivolous under Appellate Rule 23); Nolan v. Sutton , 97 Ohio App.3d 616, 647 N.E.2d 218 (1st Dist. 1994) (same).

Either the appellate court or the trial court may determine and award the appellate attorney’s fees. Klein v. Moutz, 118 Ohio St.3d 256, 888 N.E.2d 404 (2008).

The appellate court may determine and award the appellate attorney’s fees under Appellate Rule 24. Kreps v. Pesina, No. L-95-377, 1996 WL 430846 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 2, 1996); Breault v. Williamsburg Estates, No. L-86-116, 1986 WL 13169 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 21, 1986). See also Christe v. GMS Mgmt. Co. , 88 Ohio St.3d 376, 379, 726 N.E.2d 497 (2000) (Lundberg Stratton, J., concurring in judgment only).

The trial court may determine and award the appellate attorney’s fees on a remand to determine and award costs. Alcorso v. Correll , No. 110218, 2021 WL 4317396, (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 23, 2021);Cristal v. DRC Int’l, Inc., 74 Ohio App.3d 493, 557 599 N.E.2d 706 (8th Dist. 1991);Breault v. Williamsburg Estates, No. L-86-116, 1986 WL 13169 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Nov. 21, 1986). A party may request the appellate attorney’s fees in the trial court via a motion for supplemental attorney’s fees. E.g., Breault, 1986 WL 13169, at *3-4.

Whether a remand of the fee issue is cost effective and promotes judicial efficiency may depend on whether, aside from the fee issue, the appellate court is remanding the case for further proceedings. Klein v. Moutz , 118 Ohio St.3d 256, 888 N.E.2d 404, ¶ 17 (2008).

Another reason for appellate courts to remand the fee issue is: “‘The trial court is in a better position to determine a fee award, for it may hold a hearing, take testimony, create a record, and otherwise evaluate the numerous factors associated with calculating an attorney fee award.’” Klein v. Moutz, No. 23473, 2008 WL 2745960, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 16, 2008) (quotingKlein v. Moutz, 118 Ohio St.3d 256, 888 N.E.2d 404, ¶ 13 (2008)).

### I. Jury Trial

Since attorney’s fees under R.C. Chapter 53 21 are taxed as costs (see Section XIX.C, above), in a trial by jury, the award of attorney’s fees is a matter for the court, not the jury. Christe v. GMS Mgmt. Co., 88 Ohio St.3d 376, 726 N.E.2d 497 (2000);

Fant v. DiSabato , No. 87AP -265, 1987 WL 32190 ( Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 29, 1987); Drake v. Menczer, 67 Ohio App.2d 122, 425 N.E.2d 961 (8th Dist. 1980).

## XX. Jurisdiction


### A. Introduction

This chapter briefly reviews the jurisdiction of county courts, municipal courts, and courts of common pleas, relative to the landlord -tenant claims that are discussed in Chapters I-XIX, above. For a discussion on jurisdictional issues in eviction actions, see Section I.L, above.

### B. Monetary Jurisdiction


#### 1. County Court

County courts have exclusive original jurisdiction in civil actions for the recovery of sums not exceeding five hundred dollars, and original jurisdiction in civil actions for the recovery of sums not exceeding fifteen thousand dollars. R.C. 1907.03.

“[I]t is the amount sought to be recovered as set f orth in the complaint that determines jurisdiction, not the amount the court decides to award after the case has gone to trial.” Staffilino Chevrolet, Inc. v. Balk, 158 Ohio App.3d 1, 813 N.E.2d 940, ¶ 11 (7th Dist. 2004). Also, a party may reduce the am ount of the recovery to stay within the court’s monetary jurisdiction. Id., ¶ 24.

The monetary jurisdictional limit is exclusive of costs. Staffilino Chevrolet, Inc.
v. Balk, 158 Ohio App.3d 1, 813 N.E.2d 940 (7th Dist. 2004); Fay Gardens Mobile Home Park v. Newman, 14 Ohio App.3d 144, 470 N.E.2d 164 (12th Dist. 1983).

Therefore, it does not apply to attorney’s fees under R.C. Chapter 5321, since they are taxed as costs (see Section XIX.C, above). Cf. Staffilino Chevrolet, Inc. v. Balk , 158 Ohio Ap p.3d 1, 813 N.E.2d 940, ¶ 22 (7th Dist. 2004) (attorney’s fees under Ohio’s bad faith exception).

#### 2. Municipal Court

Municipal courts (exclusive of housing and environmental divisions) have original jurisdiction in civil actions in which the amount claimedby any party, or the appraised value of the personal property sought to be recovered, does not exceed fifteen thousand 559 dollars.361 R.C. 1901.17.

The monetary jurisdictional limit is exclusive of costs. R.C. 1901.17. Therefore, it does not apply to attorney’s fees under R.C. Chapter 5321, since they are taxed as costs (see Section XIX.C, above).

When a municipal court is enforcing the equitable rights of a party to a contract, the value of the contract may exceed the fifteen-thousand-dollar limit. Behrle v. Beam, 6 Ohio St.3d 41, 451 N.E.2d 237 (1983). For example, a municipal court has jurisdiction to order specific performance of a lease or land contract whose value exceeds fifteen thousand dollars, so long as none of the parties asserts a monetary claim for more than fifteen thousand dollars. Id.

Likewise, a municipal court has jurisdiction to hear an eviction action that concerns a land installment contract, even though the value of the property exceeds fifteen thousand dollars. Shaw v. Kadey, No. CA-7048, 1981 WL 2839 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 29, 1981).

#### 3. Court of Common Pleas

Courts of common pleas have original jurisdiction in civil actions in which the sums or matter in dispute exceed the exclusive original jurisdiction of county courts, currently five hundred dollars. R.C. 2305.01.

A court of common pleas may transfer to a municipal court any civil action in which the amount sought by the plaintiff does not exceed one thousand dollars, provided the municipal court has jurisdiction over t he subject-matter and the parties, and provided the municipal court concurs in the transfer. Id.

### C. Equity Jurisdiction


#### 1. County Court

County courts do not have equity jurisdiction. Gallagher v. Billmaier , 79 Ohio Law Abs. 417, 154 N.E.2d 472 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 1958).

361 Two municipal courts have a housing division, Cleveland Municipal Court and Toledo Municipal Court. R.C. 1901.011. One municipal court has an environmental division, Franklin County Municipal Court. Id. The housing and environmental divisions are not subject to the R.C. 1901.17 monetary jurisdictional limit. See Section XX.E, below.

#### 2. Municipal Court

Municipal courts (exclusive of municipal courts within Cuyahoga County and exclusive of housing and environmental divisions 362) have equity jurisdiction in landlord-tenant actions only when it is an action at law based on contr act. In such actions, under R.C. 1901.18(A)(3), a municipal court may enforce the equitable rights of a party to the contract and may grant any equitable relief necessary or proper for a complete determination of the rights of the parties.

Municipal courts have equity jurisdiction in eviction actions because an eviction action is an action at law upon a contract. See Sections I.A.4 and II.BB, above.

An action that seeks only equitable relief generally is not an action at law and, as a result, does not fa ll within the purview of R.C. 1901.18(A)(3). E.g., Dudkeiwicz v.

United Mobile Homes, No. 9-95-23, 1995 WL 577817 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Sept. 19,
1995) (complaint sought only to enjoin defendant from unlawfully removing plaintiff and her trailer from the premises); Johnson v. Middleton , 66 Ohio App.3d 783, 586 N.E.2d 1093 (3rd Dist. 1989) (action for specific performance of a land installment contract).

In any action brought in a municipal court within Cuyahoga County, and in any action brought in a housi ng or an environmental division of a municipal court: the court or division “has jurisdiction to ... hear and determine all legal and equitable remedies necessary or proper for a complete determination of the rights of the parties.” R.C. 1901.13(B) (municipal courts within Cuyahoga County); R.C. 1901.131 (housing and environmental divisions). For further discussion on the equity jurisdiction of housing divisions, see Section XX.E, below.

Excluding cases in Cuyahoga County or in a housing or an environmental division, the equity jurisdiction of municipal courts is often an issue in R.C. 5321.15 actions.

See Chapters XII and XIII, above. The tenant typically seeks monetary and injunctive relief in response to a lockout, a utility termination, or a seizure of the tenant’s personal
property. Id. The resulting issue is whether municipal courts have equity jurisdiction under R.C. 1901.18(A)(3) to grant the injunctive relief. The better reasoned conclusion is that municipal courts have this equity jurisdiction because the R.C. 5321.15(C) claim 362 See note 361, above.

561 for monetary relief is an action at law based on contract (i.e., the rental agreement).363 Spaulding v. Sign of the Cross, No. 01-CV-19578 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., Sept. 4, 2001); Colquett v. Byrd, 59 Ohio Misc. 45, 392 N.E.2d 1328 (Ohio Mun., Mansfield, 1979). E.g., Parker v. Hiles, No. 02-CVH-362 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Jan. 6, 2003) (enjoined landlord’s termination of water service).

Under the same reasoning, in a tenant’s R.C. 5321.04 contract claim for monetary and injunctive relief (see Sections IX.A.7,8, above), a municipal court has equity jurisdiction to enjoin the landlord from failing to remedy the R.C. 5321.04(A)
violations. E.g., Woods v. Coleman, No. 01-CVG-444 (Ohio Mun., Jackson Cty., Apr.

26, 2001) (preliminary injunction).

#### 3. Court of Common Pleas

Courts of common pleas have general equity jurisdiction. Beechler v. Winkel, 59 Ohio App.2d 65, 392 N.E.2d 889 (6th Dist. 1978); Carter v. Birnbaum, 68 Ohio Law
Abs. 97, 113 N.E.2d 102 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 1953).

### D. Subject-Matter Jurisdiction


#### 1. County Court

County courts have limited subject -matter jurisdiction in civil actions. See Gallagher v. Billmaier, 79 Ohio Law Abs. 417, 154 N.E.2d 472(Ohio App., 6th Dist., 1958). Its scope is defined principally by R.C. Chapter 1907.

Relevant to the landlord-tenant claims that are discussed in Chapters I-XIX, above, the subject-matter jurisdiction of county courts includes:
(1) civil actions for the recov ery of sums not exceeding fifteen thousand dollars, R.C. 1907.03, 1907.031;
(2) actions for trespass on real estate in which the damages demanded do not exceed fifteen thousand dollars, R.C. 1907.05(A);
(3) replevin actions in which the value of the personal prop erty that is sought to be recovered does not exceed fifteen thousand dollars, R.C.

1907.06; and 363 A tenant may avoid this jurisdictional issue by filing the R.C. 5321.15 action in the court of common pleas. See Section XX.C.3., below.
(4) forcible entry and detainer actions, R.C. 1923.01(A), 1907.031(A)(6).

A county court has subject -matter jurisdiction in an eviction action that concerns a land installment contract. Shelton v. Smith, No. CA-323, 1984 WL 7469 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 30, 1984).

A county court has subject-matter jurisdiction to enter a judgment that declares a cancellation and forfeiture of a land installment contract. Williams v. Bjork, 112 Ohio Misc.2d 57, 753 N.E.2d 995 (Ohio County Ct., Ashtabula Cty., 2001).

A county court has subject -matter jurisdiction in an eviction action that concerns whether the occupant exercised an option to purchase and thereby has an equitable interest in the title to the property. Sears v. Fechuck, No. 1999AP-080048, 2000 WL 874727 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 6, 2000).

A county court does not have subject-matter jurisdiction in an eviction action if a dispute exists as to which party holds present record title. See Section I.L.3, above.

#### 2. Municipal Court

Municipal courts have limited subject -matter jurisdiction in civil actions, but it encompasses virtually all of the landlord -tenant claims that are discussed in Chapters I-XIX, above. The scop e of municipal court subject -matter jurisdiction is defined principally by R.C. 1901.18.

One court held that, u nder R.C. 1901.18(A)(2) ,(3), it had subject-matter jurisdiction over the tenant’s 42 U.S.C.§ 1983 monetary counterclaim against a public housing authority. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Payne, No. 2021-CVG-02216 (Ohio Mun., Lorain, Feb. 8, 2022). But cf. White v. Chambers, Nos. 1-01-114,115,116, 2002 WL 1299781 (Ohio App., 3rdDist., May 29, 2002) (lack of subject-matter jurisdiction over plaintiff’s42 U.S.C. § 1983 claims alleged in a complaint).

Municipal courts do not have subject-matter jurisdiction in an eviction action if a dispute exists as to which party holds present record title. See Section I.L.3, above.

#### 3. Court of Common Pleas

Courts of common pleas are courts of general jurisdiction. State ex rel. Shoop v.

Mitrovich, 4 Ohio St.3d 220, 448 N.E.2d 800 (1983). This means that they have jurisdiction in all actions except those excluded sp ecifically by statute. State ex rel.

Mastracci v. Rose , 79 Ohio App. 556, 72 N.E.2d 582 (10th Dist. 1947); Fellers v.

Belau, 87 Ohio Law Abs. 54, 178 N.E.2d 530 (Ohio C.P., Paulding Cty., 1961).

With one exception, and subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of housing divisions 563 (see Section XX.E, below),the landlord-tenant claims that are discussed in Chapters I- XIX, above, fall within the subject-matter jurisdiction of courts of common pleas. The one exception is an R.C. 5321.07(B) claim, which must be f iled in the applicable municipal or county court. See Chapter VIII, above.

Generally, courts of common pleas have subject -matter jurisdiction in eviction actions, and it is concurrent with the jurisdiction of the applicable municipal or county
court. Seventh Urban, Inc. v. Univ. Circle Prop. Dev., Inc., 67 Ohio St.2d 19, 24, 423 N.E.2d 1070 (1981). See R.C. 1923.01(A), 2305.01.

However, a court of common pleas does not have subject-matter jurisdiction in any eviction action that falls within the jurisd iction of a housing division. 364 Maduka v.

Parries, 14 Ohio App.3d 191, 192 n.3, 470 N.E.2d 464 (8th Dist. 1984). A housing division has exclusive jurisdiction, within the territory of the municipal court, in eviction actions. R.C. 1901.181. See also Maduka, 14 Ohio App.3d at 192 n.3.

### E. Housing Division, Cleveland and Toledo

Municipal Courts Two municipal courts in Ohio have a housing division, Cleveland Municipal Court and Toledo Municipal Court. R.C. 1901.011.

A housing division has exclusive jurisdiction, within the territory of the municipal court, in civil actions commenced under R.C. Chapter 1923 or R.C. Chapter 5321. R.C.

1901.181(A). See also State ex rel. J.K. & E. Auto Wrecking v. Trumbo, 64 Ohio St.3d 73, 591 N.E.2d 1238 (1992); Edmunds v. Little , 103 Ohio App.3d 488, 659 N.E.2d 1291 (6th Dist. 1995); Higginbottom v. Rehoboth Temple Church, Etc. , No. 67909, 1995 WL 546935 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 14, 1995); Maduka v. Parries, 14 Ohio
App. 3d 191, 470 N.E.2d 464 (8th Dist. 1984).

A housing division also has exclusive jurisdiction, within the territory of the municipal court, in civil actions commenced under R.C. Chapter 4781 (former R.C.

Chapter 3733). Anderle v. Ideal Mobile Home Park, Inc., 101 Ohio App.3d 122, 655 N.E.2d 203 (8th Dist. 1995).

The R.C. 1901.17 monetary jurisdictional limit does not apply to a complaint, 364 See note 361, above.

counterclaim, or cross-claim filed in a housing division.365 R.C. 1901.17.

Whenever an action is properly brought in a housing division, the housing division has jurisdiction:

to determine, preserve, and enforce all rights involved in the action or proceeding, to hear and determine all legal and equitable remedies necessary or proper for a complete determination of the rights of the parties, including, but not limited to, the granting of temporary restraining orders and temporary and permanent injunctions, to render personal judgment irrespective of amount in favor of any party, and to render any judgments and make any findings and orders in the same manner and to the same extent that the court of common pleas can render a judgment or make a finding or order in a similar action or proceeding.

R.C. 1901.131.

“Thus, subject matter jurisdiction [under R.C. 1901.131] is a two-part test. First, the case must be ‘properly brought’ in the housing division. Second, the Housing Court’s jurisdiction cannot be greater than that of common pleas court.” Cuyahoga
Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jenkins , No. 2005 -CVG-22841, slip op. at 1 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb. 7, 2006).

“Once the housing division has jurisdiction over a claim, its incidental jurisdiction [under R.C. 1901.131] over related claims is broadly defined.” Skerl v. Sheet Metal
Prods. Co., No. 43743, 1982 WL 2453, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., July 1, 1982).

The R.C. 1901.131 incidental jurisdiction permits a plaintiff to include related claims in the complaint. E.g., Cleveland Hous. Renewal Project v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., 188 Ohio App.3d 36, 934 N.E.2d 372 (8th Dist. 2010) (complaint for R.C.

3767.41 claims under R.C. 1901.181 jurisdiction, and for a related common law nuisance claim under R.C. 1901.131 jurisdiction).

The R.C. 1901.131 incidental jurisdiction also permits a defendan t to assert counterclaims related to the complaint. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jenkins, No.

365 Before January 27, 1997, under R.C. 1901.17 and 1901.181, the R.C. 1901.17 monetary jurisdictional limit applied to a complaint filed in a housing division, but not to a counterclaim or cross -claim filed in a housing division. See 1996 Ohio Legis. Serv. L -3373 to L -3376
(Baldwin) (H 350 § 1). See also City of Cleveland v. A.J. Rose Mfg. Co., 89 Ohio App.3d 267, 624 N.E.2d 245 (8th Dist. 1993); City of Cleveland v. Sundorph Aeronautical Corp., No. 59739, 1991 WL 34719 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 14, 1991); Davis v. Freeman , No. 57245, 1990 WL 130919 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 13, 1990).

565 2005-CVG-22841 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb. 7, 2006) (complaint for eviction claim; 42 U.S.C. § 1983 counterclaim, related to the eviction claim, permitted under the court’s R.C. 1901.131 jurisdiction).

Under R.C. 1901.131, a housing division has subject -matter jurisdiction over related counterclaims brought under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, provided the complaint was properly brought in the housing division. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jenkins ,
No. 2005-CVG-22841 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb. 7, 2006). See also Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Humphries , No. 93 -CVG-06480 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 3, 1996). Since courts of common pleas have jurisdiction to hear § 1983 claims ( State ex rel. Carter v. Schotten , 70 Ohio St.3d 89, 92 n.2, 637 N.E.2d 306 (1994)), § 1983 counterclaims fall within the purview of R.C. 1901.131.

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Jenkins , No. 2005 -CVG-22841 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb. 7, 2006).

One appellate court erroneously stated that “[t]here is no authority for maintaining a 1983 action or an aviation case, as proposed, in the [housing division of] municipal courts because they are not courts of general jurisdiction.” City of Cleveland v. A.J.

Rose Mfg. Co., 89 Ohio App.3d 267, 273, 624 N.E.2d 245 (8th Dist. 1991). The court’s statement is erroneous because, as discussed above, R.C. 1901.131 confers upon housing divisions incidental subject-matter jurisdiction that is the same as the subject- matter jurisdiction of courts of common pleas. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Jenkins, No. 2005-CVG-22841, slip op. at 2 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Feb.

7, 2006).366 366 A.J. Rose Mfg. Co. is also distinguishable from Jenkins and Humphreys in that the § 1983 claim in A.J. Rose Mfg. Co. was a permissive counterclaim, whereas the § 1983 claims in Jenkins and Humphreys were compulsory counterclaims. See Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v.

Humphries, No. 93-CVG-06480 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Jan. 3, 1996).

## XXI. Landlord-Tenant Law for

Manufactured Home Parks

### A. Introduction

The Ohio landlord -tenant law for manufactured home parks (“mh parks”) is set forth in R.C. 4781.01, 4781.36 -.52 (former R.C. 3733.01, 3733.09 -.20). The legislature relocated the Ohio landlord -tenant law for mh parks, from R.C. Chapter 3733 to R.C. Chapter 4781, effective September 10, 2012. Am. Sub. H.B. No. 487, 129th General Assembly.

In 2017, the legislature dissolved the Manufactured Homes Commission and transferred the state regulation of ma nufactured home parks to the Department of Commerce, effective January 21, 2018. Am. Sub. H.B. No. 49, 132nd General
Assembly. The Director of Commerce promulgates rules, in Ohio Admin istrative Code Chapter 4781 -12, that supplement R.C. Chapter 4781. The Department of Commerce investigates complaints concerning violations of Ohio’s Manufactured Homes Law. Id.

The Ohio landlord-tenant law for mh parks is remedial legislation and, therefore, must be liberally construed in order to promote its object an d assist the parties in attaining justice. Schwartz v. McAtee, 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986).

The Ohio landlord -tenant law for mh parks establishes, in the mh park context, virtually all of the rights and obligations that are established in R.C. Chapter 5321 for residential landlords and tenants. Schwartz v. McAtee, 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 17 -18, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986). “The two chapters are essentially identical but for the omission of a parallel statute in R.C. Chapter 3733 [now R.C. Chapter 4781] p roviding for termination of periodic tenancies as embodied in R.C. 5321.17.”367 Courts v. Buckeye Lake Estates Mobile Home Park , No. 01 -CA-00121, 2002 WL 1299838, ¶ 16 (Ohio 367 “The reason for this omission is the recognition by the legislature that today’s ‘mobile’ homes have, by nature, become ‘immobile.’” Courts v. Buckeye Lake Estates Mobile Home Park, No. 01 -CA-00121, 2002 WL 1299838, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 4, 2002). For further discussion on the immobility of mobile homes, see Pojman v. Columbia -Brookpark Mgmt., LLC, No. 88666, 2007 WL 2269682, ¶ 3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 9, 2007). For a discussion on the resulting goo d cause requirement for terminating a MH park tenancy, see Section IV.C.1, above.

567 App., 5th Dist., June 4, 2002).

Therefore, with respect to Chapters I -XX, above, each discussion of an R.C.

Chapter 5321 provision is generally applicable to the corresponding R.C. Chapter 4781 (former R.C. Chapter 3733) provision.

### B. Parallel Provisions – R.C. Chapters

4781, Former 3733, and 5321 The corresponding provisions betw een R.C. Chapter 4781, former R.C. Chapter 3733, and R.C. Chapter 5321 are set forth below.

Subject Chapter 4781 Chapter 3733 Chapter 5321 Retaliation 4781.36,.37 3733.09,.091 5321.02,.03 Obligations of park operator or landlord 4781.38 3733.10 5321.04 Obligations of resident 4781.39 3733.101 5321.05 Rental agreement provisions 4781.40(L) 3733.11(L) 5321.06 Rent deposit and related remedies 4781.41- 4781.44 3733.12- 3733.123 5321.07- 5321.10 Termination notice for resident’s statutory violation 4781.45 3733.13368 5321.11 Action for damages 4781.46 3733.14 5321.12 Unlawful rental agreement provisions 4781.47 3733.15 5321.13 Unconscionability 4781.48 3733.16 5321.14 368 While R.C. 4781.45 (former R.C. 3733.13) is similar to R.C. 5321.11, it includes more substantive and procedural requirements than R.C. 5321.11.

Self-help evictions and seizure of resident’s property 4781.49 3733.17 5321.15 Security deposits 4781.50 3733.18 5321.16 Notice of name and address of owner 4781.51 3733.19 5321.18 Conflicts with ordinances 4781.52 3733.20 5321.19

### C. Additional Rights and Obligations, and

Court Decisions, R.C. Chapter 4781

#### 1. Introduction

The Ohio landlord-tenant law for mh parks also creates rights and obligations for which there are no corresponding R.C. Chapter 5321 provisions. The y are discussed in Chapter IV, above, with regard to eviction actions, and in Section XXI.C.3, below, with regard to R.C. 4781.40. Summaries of R.C. Chapter 4781 court decisions are set forth in Section XXI.C.4, below. Definitions of the relevant parties in Ohio’s landlord law for mh parks are set forth in Section XXI.C.2, below.

#### 2. Definitions of Relevant Parties

The relevant parties in Ohio’s landlord-tenant law for mhparks are defined in R.C.

4781.01. A “manufactured home park operator” or “park operator” is “the person who has responsible charge of a manufactured home park and who is licensed under sections 4781.26 to 4781.35 of the Revised Code.” R.C. 4781.01(M). A “tenant” is “a person who is entitled under a rental agreement with a manufactured home park operator to occupy a manufactured home park lot and who does not own the home occupying the lot.” R.C. 4781.01(T). An “owner” is “a person who is entitled under a rental agreement with a manufactured home park operator to occupy a manufactured home park lot and who owns the home occupying the lot.” R.C. 4781.01(U). A “resident” is “a person entitled under a rental agreement to the use and occupancy of residential premises to the exclusion of others.” R.C. 4781.01(V). Resident includes both tenants and owners. Id. For further discussion on these definitions, see Section IV.A., above.

569

#### 3. Summary of R.C. 4781.40 Rights and Obligations

Before the installation of an owner’s manufactured or mobile home in a mh park, the park operator must offer the owner a written rental agreement for a term of one year or more. R.C. 4781.40(A)(1). The park operator must deliver this offer to the owner in person or by certified mail, return receipt requested. R.C. 4781.40(A)(2).

At the end of each successive rental agreement, the park operator must offer the owner another rental agreement “for a term that is mutually agreed upon, and that contains terms essentially the same asthe alternative month-to-month agreement.” Id.

The park operator must deliver subsequent rental offers by ordinary mail or personal
delivery. Id.

Before a resident executes a rental agreement and assumes occupancy in a mh park, the park operator must disclose in writing all fees, charges, and assessments, including rental fees. R.C. 4781.40(B). Those fees, charges, and assessments may include a fee for moving a manufactured or mobile home into or out of the mh park, but only for the actual costs and e xpenses that are incurred by the park operator in the move. R.C.

4781.40(H)(5).

If a park operator fails to disclose a fee, charge, or assessment: (1) the park operator may not collect the fee, charge, or assessment, and (2) the park operator may not evict the resident for nonpayment of the fee, charge, or assessment. R.C. 4781.40(B), 4781.40(H)(4).

A park operator may not increase a disclosed fee, charge, or assessment during the term of the resident’s rental agreement. R.C. 4781.40(B). Inaddition, a park operator may not increase a disclosed fee, charge, or assessment without providing the resident with at least 30 days advance written notice of the increase and its effective date. R.C.

4781.40(B).

Before a resident executes a rental agreement and assumes occupancy in a mh park, the park operator must disclose in writing the park rules and must provide a copy of the park rules to the resident. R.C. 4781.40(B),(C). The park operator may not change those rules without providing the resident with at least 30 days advance written notice of the change and its effective date. R.C. 4781.40(B).

The park operator may not adopt any park rule that is unreasonable, arbitrary, or
capricious. R.C. 4781.40(C). And the park operator may not adopt any rule (or include any provision in a rental agreement) that restricts a resident’s right to display the flag of the United States or certain other specified flags, provided the flag is displayed in accordance with certain specified requirements. R.C. 4781.40.

A park operator may not require an owner: (1) to purchase from the park operator any personal property, (2) to purchase a manufactured or mobile home from the park operator or any other specific person as a prerequisite to entering into a rental agreement, or (3) to use the services of the park operator or any other specific person for the performance of any service. R.C. 4781.40(D),(F),(G).

A park operator may not assess a fee for the installation of an owner’s electric or gas appliance, unless the installation is performed by the park operator at the owner’s
request. R.C. 4781.40(E).

R.C. 4781.40 establishes various rights and obligations regarding the sale of a manufactured or mobile home within a mh park. The park operator may not deny the owner the right to sell the home within the park, if the owner gives the park operator ten days advance notice of the owner’s intent to sell the home. R.C. 4781.40(H)(1).

The park operator may not require the owner to remove the home from the park solely because of the sale. R.C. 4781.40(H)(2). The park operator may not unreasonably refuse to enter into a rental agreement with the purchaser of the home. R.C.

4781.40(H)(3). The park operator may not assess the owner a fee because of the transfer of ownership. R.C. 4781.40(H)(5). A rental agreement may not require the owner to sell the home to any specific person or through any specific agent. R.C.

4781.40(J). A rental agreement may not require the owner to pay a fee to the park operator if the owner does not use the park operator or some other designated person as the owner’s agent in the sale of the home. R.C. 4781.40(K).

#### 4. Summary of Court Decisions, R.C. 4781.36-.52

(Former R.C. 3733.09-.17)

##### a. R.C. 4781.36 (Former R.C. 3733.09)

Webb v. C & J Props., LLC, No. CA2010-01-016, 2010 WL 3213020 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Aug. 16, 2010) “While there is a temporal proximity between the complaint and the termination, ‘such a circumstance does not create a presumption of retaliation under Ohio law.’ Without any other evidence regarding C & J’s termination of the rental agreement, Webb failed to show by a preponderance of the evidence that C & J retaliated against him.” 571 Euclid Beach Ltd. v. Brockett , No. 75047, 1999 WL 1129086 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Dec. 9, 1999) The park operator violated R.C. 3733.09 when it terminated the water service to the home and thereby forced the resident to vacate the home.

Pierce v. Derickson, No. CA-3105, 1985 Ohio App LEXIS 8010 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 10, 1985) A court may awardattorney’s fees under R.C. 3733.09 only if the resident has suffered actual damages as a result of an R.C. 3733.09 violation.

Braff Mobile Home Park Co. v. McFarland , No. 8-265, 1982 WL 5815 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 23, 1982) The court upheld the denial of a retaliation defense. No direct evidence showed that:
(1) the resident complained to the park operator regarding the water problem, (2) the park operator knew that the resident had taken water samples to a governmental agency, or (3) the governmental agency found anything wrong with the water.

Voyager Vill., Ltd. v. Sudduth , No. 81 -CA-63, 1982 Ohio App. LEXIS 15638 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Apr. 15, 1982) The court upheld the denial of a retaliation defense. The alleged retaliation was based on the r esident’s complaint to the park operator regarding standing water. The complaint could not be the basis for a retaliation defense because the standing water was not a violation of R.C. 3733.10.

Troy v. Casale, No. 2976, 1982 WL 5894 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 31, 1982) The court upheld the denial of a retaliation defense. The resident’s complaints to the park operator involved a personal conflict between the resident and the park operator, and not any of the grounds set forth in R.C. 3733.09.

Country Club Vill. Mobile Home Park v. Gall, No. 958, 1980 WL 154220 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 20, 1980) The court upheld the award of retaliation damages to the resident. The park operator filed an eviction action in response to the resident’s complaints to the park operator, which concerned low water pressure and an inoperative outdoor light.

##### b. R.C. 4781.37 (Former R.C. 3733.091)

Fay Gardens Mobile Home Park v. Newman , 14 Ohio App.3d 144, 470 N.E.2d 164 (12th Dist. 1983) A resident who is in default in the payment of rent may not assert a retaliation defense under R.C. 3733.09 and may not recover damages for the park operator’s retaliation.

Voyager Vill., Ltd. v. Williams, 3 Ohio App.3d 288, 444 N.E.2d 1337 (2nd Dist. 1982) R.C. 3733.091(A)(3) does not apply when a park operator serves a month -to-month resident with a notice of termination because of the resident’s R.C. 3733.09(A) activities.

##### c. R.C. 4781.38 (Former R.C. 3733.10)

General Issues Wood v. Crestwood Assocs., LLC, No. 1-09-37, 2010 WL 1175208 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Mar. 29, 2010) “While acknowledging that claims against park operators are governed under R.C. Chapter 3733 and not R.C. Chapter 5321 (governing landlords), the Court of Appeals has, nonetheless, applied analogous landlord-tenant case law wh en analyzing claims of negligence arising under R.C. 3733.10(A).” Lewis v. Wall , No. 2007 -A-0048, 2008 WL 2633473 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 3, 2008) A violation of R.C. 3733.10, like a violation of R.C. 5321.04, constitutes negligence per se. 369 The R.C. 3733.10(A)(1),(2),(3) standards are virtually identical to the R.C. 5321.04(A)(1),(2),(3) standards.

Arndt v. P & M Ltd. , 163 Ohio App.3d 179, 837 N.E.2d 398 (11th Dist.

2005) Use (i.e., exhaustion) of the R.C. 3733.12 rent deposit remedy is not a prerequisite for filing an R.C. 3733.10(B) claim for injunctive relief and compensatory damages.

Sterling v. Stevens , No. 02 -JE-47, 2003 WL 22232008 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Sept. 25, 2003) The R.C. 3733.10(A)(2),(3) standards are virtually identical to the R.C.

5321.04(A)(2),(3) standards.

Eastgate Mobile Home, Etc. v. Klekamp , No. CA92 -05-060, 1994 WL 29876 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Jan. 31, 1994) If an occupant of a home is not a tenant, owner, or resident of the home, as those terms are defined in R.C. 37 33.01, the occupant does not have standing to commence an action against the park operator for alleged 369 For a discussion on the R.C. 5321.04 negligence per se claim, see Section IX.B, above.

573 violations of R.C. 3733.10.

Water or sewer service Non-Employees of Chateau Estates Resident Ass’n v. Chateau Estates, Ltd., No. 2006-CA-25, 2007 WL 196467 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Jan. 26, 2007) Court of appeals upheld trial court’s schedule for testing the water filtration system, which had been installed under previous orders in this case.

Non-Employees of Chateau Estates Resident Ass’n v. Chateau Estates , Ltd., 162 Ohio App.3d 453, 833 N.E.2d 1259 (2nd Dist. 2005) Court of appeals upheld trial court’s remedies for park operator’s violations of its R.C. 3733.10(A)(4) obligation to maintain the water system in good and safe working order. The water contain ed elevated levels of iron and arsenic. The remedy upheld included immediate relief ($13 per person per month reduction in rent to purch ase bottled water), intermediate relief (periodic testing of the water quality and flushing of the water system), and permanent relief (installation of a filtration system to remove the iron and arsenic from the water).

Non-Employees of Chateau Estates Resident Ass’n v. Chateau Estates, Ltd., No. 2002 -CA-68, 2003 WL 21121992 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 16, 2003) Court of appeals upheld trial court’s holding that the park operator violated its R.C. 3733.10(A)(4) obligation to maintain the water system in good and safe working order. The water contained elevated levels of iron and
arsenic. Court of appeals remanded the remedy issue for the trial court to enter an order “specifying the terms for testing the water, for remedying the underlying problem, and for providing short-term, immediate relief to the residents.” Anderle v. Ideal Mobile Home Park, Inc. , 114 Oh io App.3d 385, 683 N.E.2d 348 (8th Dist. 1996) “[T]he duty R.C. 3733.10(A)(4) places on park operators to maintain water systems in good and safe working order and condition does not place the responsibility for paying for meter reading and billing expense s on the park operator.” Wickham v. Woodlyn Acres, No. H-96-015, 1996 WL 612314 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 25, 1996) The park operator violated R.C. 3733.10(A)(4) in furnishing water to the home that was not drinkable because of its iron content.

Menges v. Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. , No. 94AP -100068, 1995 WL 498820 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., July 12, 1995) The park operator violated R.C. 3733.10 in failing to provide a water service connection and thereby failing to provide water service. The park operator also violated R.C. 3733.10 in failing to remedy a backup from the main sewer line, which caused sewage to overflow into the home.

Rex Hill, Inc. v. Cherry, No. 2588, 1991 WL 95243 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 29, 1991) The park rule required residents to maintain lateral sewer lines. The park rule was “an improper abdication of a park operator’s legal obligation to maintain good and safe septic systems pursuant to R.C. 3733.10(A)(4).” “R.C. 3733.10(B) permits reasonable attorney fees to a tenant for an action involving a park operator’s failure to comply with its statutory obligations.” Benson v. Oaks Mobile Home Park, No. 1156, 1983 WL 6313 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 15, 1983) The park operator violated R.C. 3733.10 in failing to repair a sewer system, which backed up and discharged over the resident’s lot whenever the toilet was flushed.

Miller v. Hamm, No. 15-CV-0580 (Ohio C.P., Marion Cty., June 22, 2016) Park operator violated R.C. 4781.38(A)(4) when the water service to the park was shut-off because of the park operator’s failure to pay the water
bill. Under R.C. 4781.38(B), actual damages may include emotional distress injury.

Shouhayib v. Yarger , No. E -9700004, 1998 WL 911706 (Ohio Mun., Perrysburg, Jan. 28, 1998) The park operator violated R.C. 3733.10(A)(4) in furnishing water to the home that was not drinkable, had iron levels as high as forty-one times the EPA’s Secondary Maximum Contaminant Level for iron, had a heavy chlorine odor, stained clothing during the laundry process,stained fixtures, destroyed heating elements, and damaged small appliances.

575 Electrical service Eastgate Mobile Home, Etc. v. Klekamp , No. 87 -CV-000280, 1988 WL 583172 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty., Apr. 6, 1988) R.C. 3733.10 does not require the park operatorto repair the electrical line that connects the home to the electrical box of the park.

Nichols v. Malone , No. 00 -CVG-1022 (Ohio Mun., Marietta, Nov. 14, 2001) The park operator was liable for the defective electrical wiring from the electric meter to the home and for the defective supply line from the electric pole to the electric meter.

Concrete pad Jones v. Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. , No. 93 -CVH-1-R, 1994 WL 906706 (Ohio County Ct., Trumbull Cty., Jan. 24, 1994) The park operator is responsible for ma intaining the concrete pad that it rents to an owner, unless there is a written agreement to the contrary between the park operator and the owner.

Flooding Arndt v. P & M Ltd. , Nos. 2007-P-0038,0039, 2008 WL 2042831 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., May 9, 2008) “[P]ursuant to R.C. 3733.10(A)(1) and Ohio Adm. Code 3701 -27-07(A), a manufactured home park operator has a duty to ‘ensure that a manufactured home park ... is well drained and not subject to recurrent flooding.” Under this duty, a park operator is required “to do what is reasonably necessary to prevent recurrent flooding.” The park operator must “use ‘reasonable diligence and care’ to comply with the statutory obligation and that, if compliance is impossible despite such diligence and care, the actor [park operator] is relieved of liability.... Conversely, where an actor [park operator] fails to exercise reasonable diligence and care to comply with the statutory duty, they may be found liable.” “Thus, holding defendants [park operators] liable for failing to prevent recurrent flooding does not impose strict liability on park operators for any or all flooding that may occur.” A claim also exists for violations of R.C. 3733.10(A)(2) and 3733.10(A)(3) where flood waters render homes and common areas unfit for habitation for weeks at a time.370 Willow Rest Trailer Park, Inc. v. Willow Rest Tenants’ Ass’n, Inc., No.

17338, 1996 WL 137402 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Mar. 27, 1996) The park owner violated R.C. 3733.10 in failing to prevent or correct the periodic flooding from a ditch, which runs through the park.

Outside Grounds Lewis v. Wall , No. 2007 -A-0048, 2008 WL 2633473 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 3, 2008) The defective condition was erosion around a retaining wall, which was located on the resident’sproperty. It did not violate any code and thus did not violate R.C. 3733.10(A)(1). While it may have made mowing and yard work more difficult to perform, it did not violate the R.C. 3733.10(A)(2) fit and habitable standard. It was not located in a commo n area and thus did not violate the R.C. 3733.10(A)(3) safe and sanitary standard.

Sterling v. Stevens , No. 02 -JE-47, 2003 WL 22232008 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Sept. 25, 2003) The defective condition was an indentation in the grounds of theresident’s
property. It did not violate the R.C. 3733.10(A)(2) fit and habitable
standard. It was not located in a common area and thus did not violate the R.C. 3733.10(A)(3) safe and sanitary standard.

Pets Thompson v. Irwin, No. CA97-05-101, 1997 WL 666079 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 27, 1997) R.C. 3733.10(A)(3) does not require a park operator to police the animals or pets of the residents. Therefore, if a resident’s dog breaks its chain and escapes in violation of a park rule, the park operator has not violated R.C.

3733.10(A)(3).

Bundy v. Sky Meadows Trailer Park, No. CA89-01-002, 1989 WL 125379 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 23, 1989) R.C. 3733.10(A)(3) does not require a park operator to police the animals or pets of the residents. Therefore, if a resident’s dog is roaming the 370 The Court reiterated and applied this reasonable diligence standard in a subsequent appeal in the case. Arndt v. P & M Ltd., No. 2013-P-0027, 2014 WL 3420450, ¶ 117 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 14, 2014).

577 common area in violation of a park rule, the park operator has not violated R.C. 3733.10(A)(3).

Entry upon premises Easely v. Thompson, No. 96-P-0105, 1996 WL 648734 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 18, 1996) The park operator’s entries upon the property, without prior notice to the owner, did not violate R.C. 3733.10. The owner did not have a telephone, the entries were not into the home, and the entries were for valid purposes (e.g., reading the water meter).

##### d. R.C. 4781.40 (Former R.C. 3733.11)

General issues LeMay v. Seckler , No. OT -04-032, 2005 WL 1414449 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 17, 2005) R.C. 3733.11(I) attorney’s fees may be awarded based on the issuance of a declaratory judgment. An award of damages is not a prerequisit e to an award of attorney’s fees under R.C. 3733.11(I).

Sale of home Kantner v. Gibson, No. 2-07-17, 2007 WL 414177 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist.,
Nov. 26, 2007) A “potential tenant’s credit history, rental history, and income are reasonable factors for a landlord to consider when deciding whether a lease should be extended” to such purchasers. Court of appeals upheld the trial court’s finding that the park operator’s application of these criteria was unreasonable for four reasons. First, the park operator failed to inform the resident of its reasons for denying the rental application and thus denied the resident an opportunity to cure. Second, the park operator failed to give the resident a copy of the park guidelines that were allegedly violated.

Third, the resident had remedied many of the violations and was making a good faith effort to comply with the park operator’s requests. Fourth, the park operator initially requested the resident to vacate even before giving the resident a rental application to complete.

Bennington v. Austin Square, Inc., No. 2005-CA-00095, 2005 WL 52245 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 9, 2006) The enforcement of an upgrade rule, at the time of sale, violated R.C.

3733.11(H)(1),(2). Because it was a selective enforcement of the upgrade rule, it also violated R.C. 3733.11(C).

Euclid Beach Ltd. v. Brockett, No. 75047, 1999 WL 1129086 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 9, 1999) The park operator violated R.C. 3733.11(H) because it “effectively prevented Brockett [resident] from selling her home by refusing to accept prospective buyers, as evidenced by the two real estate contracts that never resulted in the sale of the home.” Anderle v. Ideal Mobile Home Park, Inc. , 101 Ohio App.3d 122, 655 N.E.2d 203 (8th Dist. 1995) The park operator violated R.C. 3733.11(H)(5) in charging a $300 transfer fee to any resident who sold a home to a person wishing to remain in the park.

Santilla v. Sahara Mobile Home Park & Sales , No. 93-L-028, 1993 WL 407233 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Sept. 30, 1993) The park operator did not violate R.C. 3733.11(H)(3) when it refused to enter into a rental agreement with a purchaser of a home. The refusal “was based on concerns over lack of employment and lack of established credit.” Trailer Mart, Inc. v. Semenach, No. 42629, 1981 WL169157 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 26, 1981) When a park operator violates R.C. 3733.11(H)(3), the resident may recover actual damages under R.C. 3733.11(C) and the resident may recover damages (including nominal damages) under R.C. 3733.14.

White v. Leichtman, No. 838, 1980 Ohio App. LEXIS 13362 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 31, 1980) The court upheld a park rule that required new owners (and not current
owners) to install a cement pad under their home. A current owner challenged the park rul e under R.C. 3733.11(H)(1),(2),(3). She claimed that she had lost a prospective sale of her home because of the park rule.

The court held that R.C. 3733.11(H)(3) did not support the claim because it confers rights on the purchaser, not the seller. The c ourt held that R.C.

3733.11(H)(1),(2) are irrelevant to the claim.

Jameson v. Sommer’s Mobile Home Sales, Inc. , No. 83769 (Ohio C.P., Lorain Cty., May 13, 1980) R.C. 3733.11(F),(K) prohibit a park operator from requiring the purchaser of a home to move the home from the park, if the requirement is imposed 579 because the park operator was not the agent for the sale.
Cmty. Gardens Park & Sales, Inc. v. Roe, No. M8109-CVG-027176 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Nov. 6, 1981) “O.R.C. 3733.11(H) prohibits a park operatorfrom denying [a] sale of the trailor on the site and requires a one year lease to the new occupant if reasonable.” Mentor Trailer Park, Inc. v. Clark, No. 79-CVG-492 (Ohio Mun., Mentor, June 7, 1979) A park operator may not require all purchasers of homes within the park to move, without regard to the age and condition of the home. However, R.C. 3733.11(H) does not prohibit the eviction of an owner for noncompliance with park rules merely because, after a writ of restitution had been granted, the owner sought to sell the home.

Age of home LeMay v. Seckler , No. OT -04-032, 2005 WL 1414449 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 17, 2005) A park rule that requires pre -1977 mobile homes to be moved from the park upon sale is unreasonable, arbitrary, or capricious in violation of R.C.

3733.11(C).

Czerwonko v. Sahara Mobile Home Park & Sales, Inc., 137 Ohio App.3d 266, 738 N.E.2d 461 (11th Dist. 2000) The park rule concerned homes that were twenty -one years old or older.

It required the removal of such homes from the park if the owner moved out of the home or sold the home. The park rule violated R.C. 3733.11(C) because it was unreasonable. The park rule violated R.C. 3733.11(H)(3) because it was an unreasonable refusal to enter into a rental agreement with the purchaser of the home.

Freyermuth v. Navarre Vill. Homes Austin Square, Inc. , No. 1999 -CA- 00187 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 24, 2000) The park rule concerned homes that were over twelve years old. It did not permit such homes to remain in the park without the wri tten consent of
management. The park rule violated R.C. 3733.11(C) because it was unreasonably enforced. The park rule violated R.C. 3733.11(A)(2) because it deprived an owner of the right to renew a tenancy. In this case, the park rule also violated R.C. 3733.11(H)(1) and 3733.11(H)(2) because it denied the owner the right to sell the home and required removal of the home on the basis of the sale.

White v. Superior Mobile Homes, Inc., No. 98-T-0011, 1999 WL 1320188 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 10, 1999) The park rule concerned homes that were over twenty years old at the time of a change in ownership. Under the rule, the park operator would not enter into a rental agreement with any new owner of such homes. The park rule violated R.C. 3733.11(C) because it was unreasonable. The park rule violated R.C. 3733.11(H)(3) because it was an unreasonable refusal to enter into a rental agreement with the purchaser of the home.

Disclosure of fee/rent May v. Petrick, No. 2009-CA-00031, 2010 WL 1452604 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 8, 2010) Court of appeals upheld trial court’s finding that the park operator failed to offer the resident a written lease and, therefore, failed to provide the resident with written disclosure of a late fee and moving fee. Therefore, R.C. 3733.11(B) and 3733.11(H)(4) barred a damage award for the fees.

Simco Mgmt. Corp. v. Snyder , No. 98 -CA-210, 2000 WL 309396 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar. 20, 2000) R.C. 3733.11(B) does not protect a resident from liability or eviction for nonpayment of a rental fee, if the rental fee was disclosed orally to the resident and the resident agreed to pay the disclosed amount. The R.C.

3733.11(H)(4) requirement for written disclosure of the rental fee was met by a rental slip stating the amount of the rent, ev en though the resident filled in the amount.

Dearwester’s Gem City Estates v. Nelson, No. 14320, 1994 WL 681104 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 7, 1994) In an eviction action for nonpayment of rent, the R.C. 3733.11(B) requirement to disclose the rental fee is not an element of the park operator’s claim. Any failure to meet the disclosure requirement is a defense for which the resident has the burden of proof.

Callahan v. Croxford , No. 2006 -CVF-00621, 2007 WL 4359907 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., July 31, 2007) $200 per month rent under verbal agreement, but no written agreement or written disclosure. During a 17 -month occupancy, the resident paid the lot rent only for three months. “R.C. 3733.11(H)(4) prohibits a park operator from charging ‘any fee, charge, or assessment, including a rental 581 fee’ unless the terms are set forth in writing.” Therefore, the court granted judgment to the resident on the park operator’s counterclaim for lot rent.

Reck v. Lucas , No. 2006 -CVG-00235, 2006 WL 4399761 (Ohio Mun., Auglaize Cty., Aug. 25, 2006) Because the park operator failed to offer the residents a written lease, the park operator’s attempt to raise the rent, due to late payment of rent, was not permitted.

Bennett v. Croxford, No. 96-CVH-000488, 1997 WL 810044 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., Apr. 18, 1997) The park operator violated R.C. 3733.11(B) in failing “to disclose fully the rates, charges and assessments for water prior to the commencement of the Plaintiff’s [resident’s] occupancy.” The court awarded the resident a judgment in the amount of the resident’s water payments and held that the resident was not liable for the unpaid or unbilled water charges.

Apel v. Wolf , No. CVG-960027, 1996 WL 740085 (Ohio Mun., Newton Falls, Sept. 10, 1996) The park operator violated R.C. 3733.11(B) in assessing a $15 per month surcharge for taxes and insurance without the required disclosure. The court awarded the resident a judgment in the amount of the resident’s surcharge payments.

Estep v. Tisdale, No. 93 -CVG-700, 1993 WL 837913 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Nov. 29, 1993) The park operator failed to provide the resident with a written disclosure of the rental fee or the electric service fee before the execution of the rental
agreement. Therefore, R.C. 3733.11(H)(4) barred the park operator from evicting the resident for nonpayment of either of these fees.
Props. Ltd. v. Coffee, No. 93-CVG-8597 (Ohio Mun., Marion Cty., Nov.

16, 1993) R.C. 3733.11(B) barred the park operator’s claim for a $15 per month surcharge for water service because the park operator had not disclosed the fee in writing before the execution of the rental agreement.

Miller Mobile Homes, Inc. v. Reeves , No. 92 -CVG-15700, 1993 WL 837914 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Sept. 23, 1992) R.C. 3733.11(B) and 3733.11(H)(4) barred the park operator’s claim for various fees (cost of moving resident’s home, storing the home, and cleaning the lot) because the fees had not been disclosed in writing before the execution of the rental agreeement. A $200 entrance fee violated R.C.

3733.11(H)(5) because it was a transfer of ownership fee. The court granted the resident credit for the entrance fee.

Pets Jowers v. Eastgate Vill., Ltd., No. CA98-10-095, 1999 WL 374131 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 7, 1999) The park rule required registration of dogs and prohibited dogs over twenty pounds. The park operator was evicting a third -party resident for violating this park rule. Pending the eviction, the third-party’s dog bit the daughter of a residen t (plaintiff) in the third -party’s home. The park operator was not liable under R.C. 955 (strict liability) or common law because they limit a landlord’s liability to cases in which the landlord permits the dog in the common area and the dog bite here didnot occur in the common area. The court found its decision to be consistent withBundy
(below) and Heitman (below) because, in those cases, the dog bite occurred in the common area and the landlord permitted the dogs in common area by not enforcing the relevant park rules.

Burgess v. Tackas, 125 Ohio App.3d 294, 708 N.E.2d 285 (8th Dist. 1998) The park rule limited residents to one dog. A resident had two dogs and, at the resident’s door, one of the two dogs bit a visitor. The park operator was not liable under R.C. 955 (strict liability) or common law because they limit a landlord’s liability to cases in which the landlord permits the dog in the common area and the dog bite here did not occur in the common area.

Thompson v. Irwin, No. CA97-05-101, 1997 WL 666079 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 27, 1997) The park rule required that pets be on a leash at all times. A resident’s dog broke a chain and escaped in violation of the park rule. The dog then bit a third-party in the common area. The park operator was not liable under R.C. 955 (strict liability) or common law because they limit a landlord’s liability to cases in which the landlord permits the dog in the common area and here the landlord neither permitted nor acquiesced in the dog being kept in the common area.

### W. Ridge Green Co. v. Ortiz, No. 94CA-005819, 1994 WL 432162 (Ohio

App., 9th Dist., Aug. 10, 1994) The park rule prohibited residents from permitting their dogs to run at large and from tying their dogs outside their homes for more than thirt y 583 minutes at a time. The park rule was reasonable under R.C. 3733.11(D).

Bundy v. Sky Meadows Trailer Park, No. CA89-01-002, 1989 WL 125379 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 23, 1989) A park operator is under a duty to enforce the rules and regulations of the park that have been established under R.C. 3733.11(C). If a park operator is on notice of a third-party resident’s noncompliance with a park rule that prohibits residents from allowing dogs to run at large and if the park operator is on notice of the prop ensity of the dog to attack children, the park operator may be liable to a resident in a negligence claim for injuries resulting from the park operator’s failure to require the third-party resident to comply with the park rule.

Heitman v. Sky Meadows Trail er Park , No. CA89 -05-067, 1989 WL 125381 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Oct. 23, 1989) A park operator is under a duty to enforce the rules and regulations of the park that have been established under R.C. 3733.11(C). If a park operator is on notice of a third-party resident’s noncompliance with a park rule that prohibits residents from allowing dogs to run at large and if the park operator is on notice of the propensity of the dog to attack children, the park operator may be liable to a resident in a negligenceclaim for injuries resulting from the park operator’s failure to require the third-party resident to comply with the park rule.

Leichtman v. Fike, No. 1106, 1984 WL 7338 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Apr.

27, 1984) The park rule that prohibited pets was unreasonable and arbitrary [and thus prohibited by R.C. 3733.11(C)].

Hamlet Mobile Home Park v. Rose , No. R93 -CV-1786 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Mar. 14, 1994) The park rule allowed one small dog in the home, if the resident obtained written permission from the park operator. The park rule was reasonable under R.C. 3733.11(D), provided noncompliance constituted a violation only if it affected the health and safety of the park residents.

Miller Mobile Homes Inc. v. Denardy , No. 92 -CVG-15699 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Oct. 23, 1992) The park rule prohibited pets. The park rule was unreasonable under R.C.

3733.11(C), when applied to indoor cats.

Visit of family member MHC5, LLC v. Martin, No. 2011-CVG-0318 (Ohio Mun., Clermont Cty.,
Oct. 26, 2011) The park operator commenced an eviction against a resident for permitting his son’s family to stay with him. Court found that the park operator failed to prove that the family’s stay violated a ny park rule and, therefore, granted summary judgment for the resident.

Buckeye Lake Estates Mobile Home Park v. Abel , No. 01 -CVG-0177, 2002 WL 32001271 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., 2002) Resident permitted his daughter and granddaughter to remain in his manufactured home for more than 14 consecutive days, in violation of a park rule. The park operator had knowledge of this stay. Court held that the violation was not a material violation and, therefore, not grounds to evict.

Term of rental agreement Gitler v. Gerity, No. L-94-260, 1995 WL 458690 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Aug. 4, 1995) R.C. 3733.11(A) requires a one -year term only for the first year of the tenancy.

Reck v. Lucas , No. 2006 -CVG-00235, 2006 WL 4399761 (Ohio Mun., Auglaize Cty., Aug. 25, 2006) Because the park operator failed to offer the residents a written lease with a one-year term, “the Court finds that the defendants have been under a series of one year leases.” Ramm v.
