Shreves, No. 2005-CVG-0028, 2005 WL 5314591 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., May 4, 2005) Until the park operator offers a tenant a one-year lease in accordance with R.C. 3733.11, the tenancy remains a month -to-month tenancy, the park operator remains obligated to offer a one-year lease, and the required one- year lease period does not commence until the park operator offers a one- year lease in accordance with R.C. 3733.11.

Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. v. Russell, No. 7-89-CVG-336 (Ohio Mun., New Philadelphia, Jan. 12, 1990) If a park operator fails to offer an owner a written rental agreement with a term of at least one year, the owner’s rental agreement is deemed to be for 585 a term of one year.

Exterior alteration or addition to the home Troy Oaks Homes & Residential Club, Inc. v. Sokolowski, 78 N.E.3d 365, 2016-Ohio-8427 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., 2016) The park rule required prior approval of any alterations or changes to the exterior of the home. The purpose of the rule is to insure compliance with the park’s requirements. The court held that the rule is reasonable and that the residents violated the rule when they installed a metal roof.

Friendly Vill. v. Duty , 75 Ohio App.3d 555, 599 N.E.2d 878 (6th Dist.

1992) The park rule required prior approval of the park operator for exterior home additions, so as to insure that the additions met the park’s
requirements. R.C. 3733.11(C) does not require that park rules be related to matters of health and safety. Therefore, the park rule did not violate R.C. 3733.11(C).

Water and sewer service Rex Hill, Inc. v. Cherry, No. 2588, 1991 WL 95243 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 29, 1991) The park rule required residents to maintain lateral sewer lines. The park rule violated R.C. 3733.10(A)(4) and, therefore, was unreasonable, arbitrary, and capricious under R.C. 3733.11(C).

Utility fee Huntsberry v. Co untryside Assocs. , No. CA85 -08-088, 1986 WL 7105 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., June 23, 1986) A rental agreement provided for a utility fee of a specific amount. The park operator’s attempt to assess a surcharge on a 30-day notice to cover unexpectedly high uti lity costs was unlawful for two reasons. First, the surcharge was not an increase in an existing fee because it was not intended to be permanent in nature but rather was a one -time event.

Therefore, the surcharge was not an increase in an existing fee th at could be effected under R.C. 3733.11(B). Second, the surcharge violated R.C.

3733.11(C). It was unreasonable and arbitrary because it would result in major increases in utility charges upon just 45 days advance notice.

##### e. R.C. 4781.41-.43 (Former R.C. 3733.12-.122)

Superior Mobile Homes, Inc. v. White, Nos. 98-T-0072,0081, 1999 WL 974136 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., July 23, 1999) The resident attempted to deposit rent with the court of common pleas. R.C. 3733.12 required the resident, if opting to rent deposit, to deposit the rent with the county court.

As a result, the court upheld an eviction for nonpayment of rent.

Wickham v. Woodlyn Acres , No. H-96-015, 1996 WL 612314 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Oct. 25, 1996) The court permitted the resident to contin ue rent depositing until the park operator furnished the home with drinkable water and thereby remedied the R.C. 3733.10(A)(4)
violation. The park operator was furnishing water to the home that was undrinkable due to its iron content.

Gettysburg Homeowners Ass’n v. Ellenburg Capital Corp., 80 Ohio App.3d 555, 609 N.E.2d 1315 (12th Dist. 1992) The R.C. 3733.12 notice must be “sufficiently specific to enable the park operator to discern the nature of the alleged problem and to remedy the problem within a reasonable period of time.” A notice fails to meet this standard if it merely cites to sections of the Ohio Administrative Code as asserted violations and contains no specific instances of acts or omissions that allegedly constitute violations.

Rex Hill, Inc . v. Cherry , No. 2588, 1991 WL 95243 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., May 29, 1991) R.C. 3733.12 does not permit a resident to rent deposit when the resident remedies the park operator’s violation of R.C. 3733.10 and the park operator refuses to reimburse the resident for the resident’s costs in remedying the violation.

Woodlake Assocs. v. Heringhausen , No. WD-89-27, 1991 WL 3810 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 18, 1991) The R.C. 3733.12 notice must contain specific instances of violations of the park operator’s obligations under the statute or the rental agreement. Under R.C. 3733.122, the court shall order the release of the rent to the park operator if there is no violation of the park operator’s obligations under R.C. 3733.10, the rental agreement, or the applicable codes. The park operator’s refusal to pay certain expenses related to utilities, and its refusal to permit the residents to choose their own supplier for propane gas, did not constitute a violation of the park operator’s obligations under R.C.

3733.10, the rental agreement, or the applicable codes.

Woodside Terrace Mobile Home Owners Ass’n v. Woodside Terrace Co. Ltd., 53 Ohio App.3d 20, 557 N.E.2d 804 (6th Dist. 1988) 587 When a park operator files an action for release of rent deposits under R.C. 3733.122, the park operator must file a complaint, naming the resident as a defendant, and the complaint must be served in accordance with Civil Rule 4.1. In such actions, the tenant must be given 28 days to file an answer in accordance with Civil Rule 12(A)(1). The court must conduct a trial on the rent release claim to determine whether grounds exist for releasing the rent deposit under R.C. 3733.122. In R.C. 3733.122 actions, the court may not assess costs against the resident unless the R.C. 3733.122(D) requirements are met.

Smith v. Vilk, No. 46759, 1983 WL 2902 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 15, 1983) In an action for release of rent deposits under R.C. 3733.12, the court may award the park operator costs under Civil Rule 54. Such costs are not limited to theamount (one percent of rent deposit) that the clerk of court retains under R.C. 3733.121 for processing and maintaining the rent deposit account. With respect to whether costs may be assessed against the resident only if the requirements of R.C. 3733.12(D ) are met, the court found that “from the record before us, we are unable to determine how costs were computed” and thus it presumed regularity.

Stoots v. Huber Mobile Home Park, No. 1180, 1981 WL 2863 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 27, 1981) A resident does not lose the right to rent deposit under R.C. 3733.12 simply because the resident, in completing a printed form at the time of the rent deposit, states the code section as R.C. 5321.07 instead of R.C. 3733.12. To commence an action under R.C.

3733.122, the park operator must file a complaint and the complaint must be served upon the resident in accordance with Civil Rule 4.1.

Shouhayib v. Yarger, No. E-9700004, 1998 WL 911706 (Ohio Mun., Perrysburg, Jan.

28, 1998) The court denied the park operator’s request for release of the rent deposit. The park operator was still furnishing the home with defective water and, therefore, was still violating R.C. 3733.10(A)(4). The water was not drinkable, had iron levels as high as 41 times the EPA’s Secondary Maximum Contaminant Level for iron, had a heavy chlorine odor, stained clothing during the laundry process, stained fixtures, destroyed heating elements, and damaged small appliances.

Quinlan v. Mobile Manor Mobile Home Park, No. 40, 1983 WL 191087 (Ohio Mun., Cuyahoga Falls, Jan. 11, 1983) The park operator’s violations included contaminated well water, inadequate water pressure, drainage problems on certain lots, and an unsafe playground area. As of the hearing date, except for the unsafe playground area, the pa rk operator had corrected the violations. The court denied the park operator’s claim of bad faith rent depositing, released part of the rent deposit, and ordered the release of the remainder of the rent deposit upon the park operator’s installation of a playground fence.

Jakacki v. Raineri, No. 179-193, 1981 WL 167768 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., Sept. 9, 1981) The park operator’s violations included unsafe road surface conditions in the park, non- existent or non -functional street lighting on several streets in the park, and lack of maintenance and mowing of common areas in the park. The court held that the rent deposit shall not be released until the park operator remedies these violations.

Swogger v. Merkel, No. 4139, 1989 WL 572092 (Ohio County Ct., Carroll Cty., Nov.

14, 1989) The resident’s R.C. 3733.12(A) notice to the park operator is not required to state that the rent may be deposited.

##### f. R.C. 4781.45 (Former R.C. 3733.13)371

Schwartz v. McAtee, 22 Ohio St.3d 14, 488 N.E.2d 479 (1986) A park operator may terminate a tenancy under R.C. 3733.13 only if: (1) the resident fails to fulfill an obligation imposed by R.C. 3733.101 that materially affects health and safety, (2) the park operator gives the resident a notice of noncompliance in accordance with R.C. 3733.13, and (3) the resident fails to remedy the noncompliance by the date specified in the notice, which shall not be less than 30 days from the date of service of the notice.

Thompson v. KMV II, Ltd., No. 2001-P-0125, 2003 WL 943871 (Ohio App., 11th Dist.,
Mar. 7, 2003) A park operator may evict a resident for one material violation of a park rule, if the park operator serves the resident with an R.C. 3733.11 notice and the resident fails to remedy the violation within the required 30-day time period.

Hamlet Mobile Home Park v. Sigmund, 118 Ohio App.3d 29, 691 N.E.2d 1081 (11th
Dist. 1997) A park operator may evict a resident for two material violations of the park rules within a six-month period. As a result of the 1987 revision to R.C. 3733 .17, it is immaterial whether the violations materially affect the health and safety of other residents.372 All Seasons Mobile Home Park v. Tymico , No. 96 -P-0201, 1996 WL 761225 (Ohio 371 The legislature revised R.C. 3733.13 [now R.C. 4781.45], effective February 25, 1987. See note 252, above.

372 See note 252, above.

589 App., 11th Dist., Dec. 31, 1996) “There is no requirement under R.C. 3733 .13 that the notice specifically cite which rule was violated. Instead, the rule simply requires ‘a description of the violation.’” This requirement is met if the notice provides a sufficient description of the objectionable conduct to put the tenant on notice as to which park rule has been
violated. The objectionable conduct was not cured because it recurred twice after the service of the notice.

Moonlight Mobile Home Parks, Inc. v. Eichner, No. L-93-028, 1993 WL 434584 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Oct. 29, 1993) The resident’s use of aluminum skirting instead of vinyl skirting violated a park rule but was not a material violation. Therefore, it was not grounds to evict under R.C.

3733.13.

M.L.R. Props., Inc. v. Baer, No. L-85-330, 1986 WL 6703 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., June 13, 1986) R.C. 3733.13 applies when a park operator seeks to evict a resident for violation of the park rules. R.C. 3733.13 provides that, when a resident violates R.C. 3733.101 and the violation materially affects health and safety, the pa rk operator may serve the resident with an R.C. 3733.13 notice of termination. If the resident fails to remedy the condition specified in the notice within the time specified in the notice, the rental agreement will be terminated. The R.C. 3733.13 notice must state the date on which the rental agreement will be terminated.

Leichtman v. Fike, No. 1106, 1984 WL 7338 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Apr. 27, 1984) If a resident cures the breach of the park rule within the relevant 30 -day period, the resident cannot be evicted for the rule violation.

Cont’l Inv. P’ship v. Hukill, No. 06-CV-131 (Ohio Mun., Delaware, Mar. 8, 2006) For a termination of a rental agreement for an alleged material violation of park rules, R.C. 3733.11 requires a notice of termination that in cludes: a description of the violation(s); a statement of the specific termination date, if the resident does not timely cure; and a statement that, if a second material violation of park rules occurs within six months, the rental agreement will terminate immediately. This means that R.C.

3733.11 requires two notices, each with a description of the violation(s). The park operator served three notices and only one contained a description of the violation(s).

None of the notices specified a termination dat e. Their statements that the rental agreement “shall terminate within 30 days after receipt by you of this notice” does not meet the specified date requirement. Based on these violations, court held it lacked jurisdiction over the eviction action and granted judgment for the resident.

Stites Enters., Inc. v. Dixon, No. 04-CVG-1659 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Jan. 4, 2005) Before any termination of a tenancy for park rule violations, R.C. 1923.02(A)(1) and 3733.13 require a 30 -day notice and opportunity to cu re. A park operator may not circumvent this “jurisdictional requirement ... by claiming a lease violation and proceeding under O.R.C. 1923.02(A)(10), by merely incorporating the rules and regulations by reference into the lease.” Buckeye Lake Estates Mobi le Home Park v. Abel , No. 01 -CVG-01777, 2002 WL 32001271 (Ohio Mun., Licking Cty., 2002) The park rule prohibited additional residents for more than 14 consecutive days, without management approval. The resident violated this rule when she permitted her daughter and granddaughter to reside in her home for more than 14 consecutive days.

The violation constituted a de minimis violation (not a material violation) of the park
rules. Therefore, it was not a basis for terminating the tenancy.

Summit Mobile Home Park v. Brode, No. R97-CVG-1046 (Ohio Mun., Portage Cty., June 27, 1997), aff’d on other grounds, No. 97 -P-0080, 1998 WL 34002598 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 16, 1998) The court entered an eviction judgment in favor of the resident because the resident’s violation of the park rule, regarding the style of the required utility shed, was neither willful nor committed in bad faith.

United Mobile Home v. Scruggs, No. 93-CVG-3-8 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., Apr. 16, 1993) The park operator served the resident with an R.C. 3733.13 notice of termination but failed to provide the resident with the requisite 30 days to remedy the alleged
violations. The park operator served the resident with an R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate before the 30 -day period had expired. As a result, the court entered an eviction judgment in favor of the resident.

Highland Estates Mobile Home Park v. Miller, No. 92-CVG-1054, 1993 WL 837917 (Ohio Mun., Findlay, Jan. 22, 1993) The resident’s failure to install new skirting violated a park rule,but the violation was neither willful nor committed in bad faith. Therefore, it was not grounds to evict under R.C. 3733.13. The resident’s failure to remove or conceal a hitch violated a park rule, but the violation was not grounds to evict under R.C. 3733.13 because the park operator had not been enforcing the rule uniformly against other residents.

Martin v. Woods, No. 92-CVG-714 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Sept. 10, 1992) The purported notice of termination did not terminate the tenancy because it failed to describe the resident’s violations and it failed to include other provisions that R.C.

3733.13 requires.

591
Props. Ltd. v. Paquette, No. M8908-CVG-29204 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Sept. 8, 1989) A park operator must comply strictly with the R.C. 3733.13 notice requirement. This standard was not met where: (1) the R.C. 3733.13 notice contained the phrase “the two material violations were unreasonable” instead of the phrase “that the park rule is unreasonable” and (2) the notice did not contain a refe rence to any provision of a statute, code, or rule that the resident allegedly violated.

Peterson v. Woods, No. 08028 (Ohio County Ct., Adams Cty., June 1, 1988) The court dismissed the eviction complaint because the park operator sought to evict the resident for violating a park rule without serving the resident with an R.C. 3733.13 notice.

Tea v. Wilhelm, No. 86-CVG-42 (Ohio County Ct., Sandusky Cty., July 18, 1986) The court dismissed the eviction complaint because the park operator sought to evict the resident for violating a park rule without serving the resident with an R.C. 3733.13 notice.

##### g. R.C. 4781.46 (Former R.C. 3733.14)

Trailer Mart, Inc. v. Semenach , No. 42629, 1981 WL 169157 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Mar. 26, 1981) If a park operator violates R.C. 3733.11(H), the resident may recover damages under R.C. 3733.14.

##### h. R.C. 4781.47 (Former R.C. 3733.15)

Arndt v. P & M Ltd. , Nos. 2007 -P-0038,0039 2008 WL 2042831 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., May 9, 2008) “[A] manufactured home park operator may not avoid the statutory duties imposed by R.C. 3733.10(A) by the inclusion of an ‘as is’ in the lease agreement. ‘No provision of sections 3733.09 to 3733.20 of the Revised Code may be modified or waived by any oral or written agreement,’ except that the ‘[t]he park operato r may agree to assume responsibility for fulfilling any duty or obligation imposed on a resident by section 3733.101 [3733.10.1] of the Revised Code.”

##### i. R.C. 4781.48 (Former R.C. 3733.16)

Pojman v. Columbia -Brookpark Mgmt., LLC , No. 88666, 2007 WL 2269682 (O hio App., 8th Dist., Aug. 9, 2007) The court sets forth a two -part test – procedural ( e.g., bargaining power) and substantive (e.g., unfairness) – for determining whether a rental agreement provision is unconscionable in violation of R.C. 3733.16. For ren t increases, the relevant issue is whether the new rent charged (not the amount of the increase) is unconscionable.

The R.C. 3733.16 clause “a reasonable opportunity to present evidence” does not preclude a summary judgment ruling without a hearing.
Props. Ltd. v. Coffee, No. 93-CVG-8597 (Ohio Mun., Marion Cty., Nov. 16, 1993) The rental agreement imposed a fee for late payment of rent that permitted fees up to 61% of the basic rent charge. The late payment fee was unconscionable and thus violated R.C. 3733.16. The court limited the fee to a maximum of 25% of the $115 per month basic rent ($28.75 per month).

##### j. R.C. 4781.49 (Former R.C. 3733.17)

Eller v. Cont’l Inv. P’ship, 151 Ohio App.3d 729, 785 N.E.2d 802 (6th Dist. 2003) The park operator removed the re sident’s home from the park after the court had entered an eviction judgment against the resident, but before it had issued a writ of
restitution. The court of appeals reversed the summary judgment for the park operator, held that the park operator violat ed R.C. 3733.17, and remanded the case for a determination of damages.

Keesey v. Superior Mobile Homes, Inc., No. 2000-AP-080057, 2001 WL 293253 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Mar. 20, 2001) The court entered an eviction judgment against the resident and, under th e writ of restitution, the resident’s home was removed from the park. Thereafter, the court granted the resident’s Civil Rule 60(B) motion. The resident then filed an R.C. 3733.17 action against the park operator. “Because appellee [park operator] proceeded under a lawfully issued writ of restitution, the municipal court’s subsequent decision to vacate the judgment does not operate to retroactively transform a lawful act into a[n] unlawful one.” Therefore, on the R.C. 3733.17 claim, the court upheld thejudgment for the park operator.

Childers v. Russell, Nos. 01-4142,4145, 2002 WL 31950741 (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., Feb. 7, 2002) The park operator terminated water service to two manufactured homes, after providing a 30-day notice of the park’s closing. The court held that the park operator violated R.C. 3733.17(A) and awarded damages to the residents.

Stein v. Fairview Cmty. Park, Co., No. 33061, 1981 WL 167766 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Sept. 23, 1981) The park operator threatened to move a residen t’s home from the park. The court enjoined the park operator from moving or otherwise transporting the home and from seizing the resident’s personal possessions.

593 Callahan v. Croxford, No. 2006-CVF-00621, 2007 WL 4359907 (Ohio Mun., Athens Cty., July 31, 2007) The park operator removed the water meter (and thereby terminated the water service) for the resident’s manufactured home for two weeks. The court awarded the resident $100 compensatory damages and $500 punitive damages.

Shaw v. Mays Mobile Home Court , No. 87-CVE-233, 1987 WL 419803 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Dec. 10, 1987) The court found that the park operator padlocked the resident’s home and seized the resident’s personal property in violation of R.C. 3733.17(A),(B) and 3733.10(A)(6).

The court found that the park operator’s actions were wilful, wanton, and malicious.

## XXII. Miscellaneous Landlord-Tenant

Issues

### A. Statute of Conveyances, Statute of

Frauds, and Related Matters

#### 1. Statute of Conveyances – Defectively Executed

Leases The statute of conveyances generally requires all leases to be in writing, signed by the landlord, and acknowledged before a notary public (or other designated official).

R.C. 5301.01.373 Certain leases are exempt from these formal requirements, the most noteworthy being leases that are for a term of three years or less. R.C. 5301.08.

However, a perpetual lease is subject to the statute of conveyances, even if its initial term is less than three years. Cont’l Enters., Ltd. v. Hunt, No. 102200, 2015 WL 9460300 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 2015); Zunshine v. Wallace F. Ackley Co. ,
No. 99AP-531, 2000 WL 329008 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 30, 2000); Friedrich v.

Matrka, No. 37178, 1978 Ohio App. LEXIS 9975 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 23, 1978); Frank v. Flynn, 120 Ohio App. 361, 197 N.E.2d 657 (8th Dist. 1964); Morales
v. Windsor R ealty & Mgmt., Inc. , No. 2000 -CVH-002037 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Nov. 21, 2000). See also Note, Residential Renewal Leases and the Ohio Statute of Conveyances: Invalidation and Subsequent Treatment, 27 Clev. St. L. Rev.

231 (1978).

A lease is a perpetual lease if its term automatically renews unless and until the tenant provides the landlord with a timely notice of nonrenewal.Id.; Verhovsek v. Mut.

Props., No. 51600, 1987 WL 7435 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 5, 1987); Hoffman v.

Foster & Kleise r, Inc., No. 44134, 1982 WL 5385 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 27, 1982); President & Trs. of Ohio Univ. v. Athens Livestock Sales, Inc. , 115 Ohio App.

21, 179 N.E.2d 382 (4th Dist. 1961).

373 The revision of R.C. 5301.01, effective February 1, 2002, eliminated the requirement for two witnesses. It also creates a presumption of validity for leases that were executed prior to February 1, 2001, without the signature of two witnesses.

595 A perpetual lease may permit the landlord as well to discontinue the automatic renewals via a timely notice of nonrenewal or a timely notice of a change in the rent (or a lease provision). Zunshine v. Wallace F. Ackley Co. , No. 99AP-531, 2000 WL 329008 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 30, 2000) (landlord notice of nonrenewal) ;

Friedrich v. Matrka, No. 37178, 1978 Ohio App. LEXIS 9975 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Mar. 23, 1978) (landlord notice of rent/lease change); Frank v. Flynn, 120 Ohio App.

361, 197 N.E.2d 657 (8th Dist. 1964) (landlord notice of rent/lease change).

“‘[W]herea purported lessee takes possession under a defectively executed lease and pays rent, a tenancy will be implied and is subject to all of the terms of the purported lease except duration.’” Ruben v. S.M. & N. Corp., 83 Ohio App.3d 80, 83, 613 N.E.2d 1101 ( 8th Dist. 1993). Accord Zunshine v. Wallace F. Ackley Co. , No.

99AP-531, 2000 WL 329008 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 30, 2000); Schultz v.

Wurdlow, No. 94 -APG07-1033, 1995 WL 41648 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 31, 1995); Manifold v. Schuster, 67 Ohio App.3d 251, 586 N.E.2d 1142 (4th Dist. 1990).

“‘The duration of the term [of the implied tenancy] is determinable by the provisions for payment of rent, so a lease providing for annual rent creates a tenancy from year to year, whereas a lease providing for mon thly rent creates a tenancy from month to month.’” Ruben v. S.M. & N. Corp. , 83 Ohio App.3d 80, 83, 613 N.E.2d 1101 (8th Dist. 1993). Accord Zunshine v. Wallace F. Ackley Co. , No. 99AP -531, 2000 WL 329008, at *3-4 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 30, 2000).E.g., Delfino v. Paul Davies Chevrolet, Inc., 2 Ohio St.2d 282, 209 N.E.2d 194, syllabus no. 1 (1965) (“A defectively executed lease for a term of five years upon monthly rental creates a tenancy in the lessee from month to month”);9900 Timbers Dr. Inv. LLC v. Li, No. C- 190224, 2020 WL 1870257 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Apr. 15, 2020) (a defectively executed lease with a five -year term and monthly rent payments created a month -to- month tenancy); Tower Realty v. Zalenski , No. 07 -JE-24, 2008 WL 2583131, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 23, 2008) (“a defective [three-year] lease that provides for monthly rent creates a tenancy from month-to-month”);R & S Props. v. Weiszner, No.

68637, 1995 WL 628342 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 26, 1995) (month -to-month tenancy created where a defectively executed lease had a term of five years, with a five year renewal option, and specified a rent amount to be paid monthly); Yelanosky v.

White, No. 67479, 1995 WL 32869, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 26, 1995) (“A month-to-month tenancy is created where a [defectively executed] lease provides for monthly payments”); Katzin v. Murad, No. 46553, 1984 WL 7150, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 5, 1984) (“When the lease is … defectively executed, a month-to-month tenancy is created , where there is a monthly rental payment”); Friedrich v. Matrka ,
No. 37178, 1978 Ohio App. LEXIS 9975 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 23, 1978) (when the invalid lease has an initial term of one year and an automatic renewal provision, and specifies a rent am ount to be paid monthly, the implied tenancy is month -to- month). But see Ruben v. S.M. & N. Corp., 83 Ohio App.3d 80, 83, 613 N.E.2d 1101 (8th Dist. 1993) (“‘year to year tenancy will be implied where the rent is reserved annually, although payable in monthly installments’”).

For a discussion on the equitable doctrine of partial performance taking an oral rental agreement out of the statute of conveyances, see Section XXII.A.3, below.

#### 2. Statute of Frauds – Oral Rental Agreements

R.C. 1335.04 and 1335.05 are the statute of frauds for real estate transactions, including rental agreements. Schloss v. Sachs, 63 Ohio Misc.2d 457, 459, 631 N.E.2d 212 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1993).

R.C. 1335.04,.05 provide that, in order to create a valid rental agreement or tobring an action upon a rental agreement, the rental agreement must be in writing and signed by the “party ... granting it” (R.C. 1335.04) and by “the party to be charged therewith” (R.C. 1335.05). Therefore, the rental agreement must be signed by the land lord (i.e., the grantor) and, when the tenant is “to be charged therewith,” by the tenant.

R.C. 1335.04,.05 require a rental agreement to be in writing even if it is for a term of less than three years and thus not subject to the statute of conveyances (see Section XXII.A.1, above). Findlay v. Baker Rental Co. , No. 77AP -874, 1978 Ohio App.

LEXIS 10554, at *5 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 9, 1978); Schloss v. Sachs, 63 Ohio Misc.2d 457, 460, 631 N.E.2d 212 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1993).

When a party takes possession of premises under an oral rental agreement that is invalid under the statute of frauds, a tenancy at will is created. Reid v. Plainsboro Partners, III, Nos. 09AP -442,446, 2010 WL 3610931, ¶ 33 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Sept. 16, 2010); Peoples v. Holley, 181 Ohio App.3d 203, 908 N.E.2d 517, ¶ 22 (2nd
Dist. 2009); Mark v. Long, 180 Ohio App.3d 832, 907 N.E.2d 759, ¶¶ 9,10 (4th Dist.

2009); Weishaar v. Strimbu , 76 Ohio App.3d 276, 284, 601 N.E.2d 587 (8th Dist.

1991); Manifold v. Schuster , 67 Ohio A pp.3d 251, 255, 586 N.E.2d 1142 (4th Dist.

1990).

The tenancy at will converts to a periodic tenancy upon the payment and acceptance of rent. Id. The period of the periodic tenancy depends on the rental agreement’s provision for payment of rent. Id. If it provides for monthly rent 597 payments, the periodic tenancy is a month-to-month tenancy. Id.

In addition, a landlord and tenant may create a month -to-month tenancy under an express oral agreement (or by implication from monthly rent payments). May v.

Petrick, No. 2009-CA-00031, 2010 WL 1452604, ¶ 22 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 8, 2010); Amick v. Sickles, 177 Ohio App.3d 337, 894 N.E.2d 733, ¶ 19 (4th Dist. 2008);

Swartz v. Schutte, No. 14717, 1991 WL 2022, at *2 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Jan. 9, 1991).

See also Reid v. Plainsboro Partners, III, Nos. 09AP-442,456, 2010 WL 3610931, ¶33 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 16, 2010).

However, a landlord and tenant may not establish the terms or conditions of their tenancy in an oral agreement. “[A]n orallease can create nothing more than a periodic tenancy.”374 Neubauer v. Patzkowsky, No. 91AP-1236, 1992 WL 125269, at *2 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 2, 1992).

For a discussion on the equitable doctrine of partial performance taking an oral rental agreement out of the statute of frauds, see Section XXII.A.3, below.

#### 3. Partial Performance Exception

The equitable doctrine of partial performance applies to the statute of frauds and to the statute of conveyances. Delfino v. Paul Davies Chevrolet, Inc. , 2 Ohio St.2 d 282, 286, 209 N.E.2d 194 (1965); Cuvier Press Club v. Fourth & Race St. Assocs., Ltd., 1 Ohio App.3d 30, 35-36, 439 N.E.2d 443 (1st Dist. 1981); Schloss v. Sachs, 63 Ohio Misc.2d 457, 463, 631 N.E.2d 212 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1993).

Therefore, this doctrine may take an oral rental agreement out of the statute of frauds, and out of the statute of conveyances, and thereby render the agreement valid and enforceable.

The standard for determining whether this doctrine applies in a particular case is the same for both statutes. Delfino v. Paul Davies Chevrolet, Inc. , 2 Ohio St.2d 282, 286, 209 N.E.2d 194 (1965).

The standard is essentially comprised of three elements. See Delfino v. Paul Davies Chevrolet, Inc. , 2 Ohio St.2d 282, 287, 209 N.E.2d 194 (1965) ; Weishaar v.

Strimbu, 76 Ohio App.3d 276, 283 -84, 601 N.E.2d 587 (8th Dist. 1991); Manifold v.

374 For example, an oral agreement may not establish a fee for late payment of rent. Neubauer
v. Patzkowsky, No. 91AP-1236, 1992 WL 125269, at *2 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., June 2, 1992).

When an oral agreement establishes a periodic tenancy, the terms and conditions of the tenancy are those established in R.C. Chapter 5321 (see Section VII.B, above) and in any other relevant laws.

Schuster, 67 Ohio App.3d 251, 254 -55, 586 N.E.2d 1142 (4th Dist. 1990); Loveland
Props. v. Ten Jays, Inc., 57 Ohio App.3d 79, 82-83, 567 N.E.2d 270 (1st Dist. 1988);

Schloss v. Sachs, 63 Ohio Misc.2d 457, 463-64, 631 N.E.2d 212 (Ohio Mun., Hamilton Cty., 1993). See also Clements v. Mayhew , No. 2006 -CA-126, 2007 WL 2070362 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., July 20, 2007) (99-year lease); Brown v. Brown, 04-CA-000018, 2005 WL 914490 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Apr. 14, 2005) (life estate);Kaiser v. Caskey,
No. L-01-1487, 2002 WL 1823007 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 9, 2002) (life estate).

The first and second elements are taking possession of the premises and payment of rent, wher e the possession and rent payments are referable to the oral rental agreement and not to any other source. 375 Id. The third element is one of two alternative circumstances. Id.

The third element may be detrimental reliance by the party arguing partial performance, where the detrimental reliance makes it impossible or impractical to place the parties in status quo. 376 Id. See also Brannan v. Fowler , 100 Ohio App.3d 577, 584, 654 N.E.2d 434 (4th Dist. 1995).

Alternatively, the third element may be an unconscionable advantage obtained by the party raising the statute of frauds (or statute of conveyances). Brannan v. Fowler, 100 Ohio App.3d 577, 584, 654 N.E.2d 434 (4th Dist. 1995);Manifold v. Schuster, 67 Ohio App.3d 251, 254-55, 586 N.E.2d 1142 (4th Dist. 1990).

When the equitable doctrine of partial performance takes an oral rental agreement out of the statute of frauds (and the statute of conveyances), the permissible actions under the rental agreement include actions at law and actions in equity. Hughes v.

Oberholtzer, 162 Ohio St. 330, 123 N.E.2d 393, syllabus no. 3 (1954); Aircraft Specialty Co. v. Ctr. Woodland Realty Co. , 40 Ohio App. 125, 178 N.E.2d 213 (8th
Dist. 1931). But see Beggin v. Ft. Worth Mortgage Corp. , 93 Ohio App.3d 333, 638 N.E.2d 604 (3rd Dist. 1994) (misconstrues Hughes).

375 For example, the first element was not met where the possession of the premises began under a prior month -to-month tenancy. Roos v. John Shillito Co. , 52 Ohio App. 262, 3 N.E.2d 666 (1st Dist. 1935).

376 For example, this alternative of the third element was met where a tenant had conveyed title of the property to a third party (her son).Egner v. Egner, 24 Ohio App.3d 171, 173, 493 N.E.2d 999 (11th Dist. 1985).

599

#### 4. Oral Condition Precedent, Written Rental

Agreement Ohio courts have long recognized the exception to the parole evidence rule that permits oral evidence to prove a condition precedent to the existence of a contr act.

Beatley v. Knisley , 183 Ohio App.3d 356, 917 N.E.2d 280, ¶ 15 (10th Dist. 2009).

“Courts admit extrinsic evidence of a condition precedent because satisfaction of such a condition must occur before a contract comes into existence.” Id., ¶ 16.

The only exception to this rule is that “a party may not introduce extrinsic evidence of an oral condition precedent when the written contract addresses the subject matter of the condition and the contractual terms are inconsistent with the condition precedent.” Id., ¶ 19.

#### 5. Oral Modification, Written Rental Agreement

The statute of frauds (see Section XXII.A.2, above), as a general rule, prohibits an oral modification of a written rental agreement. Miami Valley United Methodist Mission Soc’y v. White-Dawson, No. 17873, 2000 WL 234712 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Mar. 3, 2000); 200 W. Apts. v. Foreman, No. 66107, 1994 WL 505271 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 15, 1994).

However, there is an exception to this general rule “when the parties to the written agreement act upon the terms of the oral agreement.” 200 W. Apts. v. Foreman , No.

66107, 1994 WL 505271, at *2 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 15, 1994). “[W]hen an agreement [in writing] as orally modified has been acted upon, as in the instant case, the rights of the parties are to be determined by the modified agreement.”377 Id. Accord 3637 Green Road Co., Ltd. v. Specialized Component Sales Co., Inc., 69 N.E.3d 1083, 2016-Ohio-5342 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2016) (commercial lease).

377 In Foreman, the written lease contained a thirteen -month term and specified a $450 per month r ent. Concurrent with its execution, the tenant and landlord orally agreed that the tenant’s rental obligation would be only $225 per month because he would soon become the property manager and, in the interim, he would perform certain computer consultingwork. For the initial seven months, the tenant paid and the landlord accepted $225 per month rent. The landlord then notified the tenant that the property manager position was no longer available and the tenant must begin paying $450 per month rent. How ever, the landlord’s claim requested $450 per month rent for the entire thirteen-month term of the written lease. The court held that, in these circumstances, the oral modification of the rental obligation established the rights of the parties for the initial seven months of the lease.

### B. Purchase of Premises Subject to Existing

Tenancy A purchaser of real estate, which is in the actual possession of a tenant, is charged with knowledge of, and acquires title subject to, the tenant’s rights under the existing
tenancy. Four Howards, Ltd. v. J & F Wenz Rd. Inv., L.L.C. , 179 Ohio App.3d 399, 902 N.E.2d 63 (6th Dist. 2008); Endersby v. Schneppe , 73 Ohio App.3d 212, 596 N.E.2d 1081 (3rd Dist. 1991); Kemp v. Feldman, 84 Ohio App. 154, 81 N.E.2d 319 (2nd Dist. 1948); Dunkel v. Hedges , 15 Ohio App. 259 (4th Dist. 1921); Smith v.

Gallagher, No. M9109 -CVG-034190 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 4, 1991);

Cassidy v. Vaccariello, No. M8907-CVG-026434 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 28, 1989).

For a discussion on the application of this law to tenancies under the Housing Choice Voucher program, see Section III.H.4, above.

Under this law, the purchaser acquires title subject to, among other things, the tenant’s right topay rent in accordance with the established course of dealings between the tenant and the previous owner (see Section II.H, above). Smith v. Gallagher, No.

M9109-CVG-034190 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Dec. 4, 1991); Cassidy v.

Vaccariello, No. M8907 -CVG-026434 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Aug. 28, 1989);

Classic Real Estate v. Bowen, No. M79-CVG-01404 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty., Mar.

12, 1979).

When a third party purchases a tenant -occupied property, three other issues that may arise, among the seller, buyer , and tenant, concern: the tenant’s rental payment obligations (see Section VII.F.10, above); negligence per se liability for defective conditions that existed at the property at the time of the sale (see Section IX.B.6, above); and security deposit liabi lity for deposits paid to the seller (see Section XVI.H.6, above).

### C. Foreclosure of a Mortgage on Tenant-

Occupied Property

#### 1. Introduction

When a foreclosure action concerns tenant-occupied property and the tenancy was created after the mortgage was executed, three issues often arise. The first is whether, 601 pending the foreclosure action ( i.e., after commencement of the action and before confirmation of the foreclosure sale), the landlord-tenant relationship continues and, if so, are the rights and du ties of the landlord or tenant modified in any respect. The second is whether the confirmation of the foreclosure sale terminates the tenancy. The third is whether, in the foreclosure action, the court may issue an order ( i.e., writ of
possession) for the forcible removal of the tenant from the premises.

Ohio common law on these issues is discussed in Section XXII.C.2, below. The Protecting Tenants at Foreclosure Act (“PTFA”)378 – a federal law that preempts Ohio common law on these issues – is discussed in Section XXII.C.3, below. Rules governing FHA-insured mortgages on these issues are discussed in Section XXII.C.4, below.

#### 2. Ohio Common Law


##### a. Pending Foreclosure Action, Effect on Tenancy

During the pendency of a foreclosure action ( i.e., after commencem ent of the action and before confirmation of the foreclosure sale), generally the landlord -tenant relationship remains unchanged. The tenancy ( e.g., one-year lease, month -to-month
tenancy) continues. The landlord and tenant have the same rights and duties as before the filing of the foreclosure action.

The tenant still has a legal duty to pay the rent to the landlord. A tenant’s failure to pay the rent may permit the landlord to terminate the tenancy and file an eviction action.

Likewise, the landlord is still required to maintain the property and, if the rental agreement requires the landlord to pay for a utility service, the landlord is still required to make those utility payments. If the landlord fails to maintain the property or pay for those utilit y services, the tenant may exercise the remedies that are discussed in 378 The PTFA was enacted in 2009. Pub. L. No. 111 -12, tit. VII, §§ 701 -704, 123 Stat. 1632, 1660-62 (2010). On December 31, 2014, the PTFA expired under the sunset provision of§ 704 of the PTFA. In 2018, Congress enacted a permanent extension of the PTFA, which, effective June 23, 2018, restored and revived §§ 701-703 of the PTFA, and repealed the sunset provision in § 704 of the PTFA. Pub. L. No. 115 -174, tit. III, § 304, 132 Stat. 1339 (2018). The PTFA is not codified (except for § 703), but it is contained in the U.S.C.A. notes of various sections:

§ 701 – 12 U.S.C.A. § 5201 notes; § 702 – 12 U.S.C.A. § 5220 notes; § 703 – 42 U.S.C.A.

§§ 1437(o)(7)(C) and (F); § 704 – 12 U.S.C.A. § 5201 notes and § 5220 notes, and 42 U. S.C.

§ 1437f notes.

Chapters VIII and IX, above.

Generally, a foreclosure filing, alone, may not be a basis for rent depositing (see Chapter VIII, above). However, it may be a basis for rent depositingif the foreclosure filing and related facts support a claim for breach of the implied covenant of quiet enjoyment (see Section VII.C.9, above).

Three exceptions to these general rules, which (when applicable) modify the landlord-tenant relationship pendi ng the foreclosure action, are: (1) the court’s appointment of a receiver; (2) the court’s approval of a mortgagee-in-possession; and
(3) the mortgagee’s exercise of an assignment of rent provision. Generally, a receiver has whatever powers the court dele gates, a mortgagee -in-possession has the general duties of a landlord, and an assignee of rent does not have the general duties of a landlord.

##### b. Foreclosure Sale, Effect on Tenancy

When the tenant is a party in the foreclosure action, the confirmation of t he foreclosure sale terminates the tenancy. See Prudential Ins. Co. of Am. v. Bull Market, Inc., 66 Ohio Misc. 9, 420 N.E.2d 140 (C.P., Montgomery Cty., 1979); Davis v.

Boyajian, 11 Ohio Misc. 97, 229 N.E.2d 116 (C.P., Stark Cty., 1967).

In these circumst ances, the foreclosure court may issue an order for the forcible removal of the tenant from the premises. See Ohio Att’y Gen. Op. No. 78-015 (Apr.

14, 1978).

When the tenant is not a party in the foreclosure action, recent appellate court decisions hold t hat the confirmation of the foreclosure sale still terminates the tenancy.379 Victoria Mortgage Corp. v. Williams, No. 68012, 1996 WL 200160 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 25, 1996);Hembree v. Mid-Am. Fed. Sav. & Loan Ass’n, 64 Ohio App.3d 144, 580 N.E.2d 1103 (2nd Dist. 1989). See also Prudential Ins. Co. of Am. v.

Bull Market, Inc., 66 Ohio Misc. 9, 420 N.E.2d 140 (C.P., Montgomery Cty., 1979).

These decisions rely on New York Life Ins. Co. v. Simplex Prods. Corp., 135 Ohio St.

501, 21 N.E.2d 585 (1939).

379 In these circumstances, even absent the PTFA (see note 378, above), the foreclosure sale arguably would not terminate a Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”) lease. The federal law that prohibits an owner from terminating a HCVP lease without good cause arguably preempts any state law under which a foreclosure sale would terminate the HCVP lease. For a discussion on this issue, see Section III.H.5, above.

603 Before the enactment of the PTFA, 380 the Williams and Hembree rule seemed to be followed in most municipal courts. Therefore, in practice, those decisions overruled an earlier appellate court decision and an earlier common pleas court decision – First
Fed. Sav. & Loan, Etc. v. Rig Oil Co. , No. CA83-11, 1983 WL 7013 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 2, 1983) (foreclosure sale does not terminate the tenancy, if tenant seeks to affirm it); Davis v. Boyajian , 11 Ohio Misc. 97, 229 N.E.2d 116 (C.P., Stark Cty., 1967).

However, when the tenant is not a party in the foreclosure action, the foreclosure court may not issue an order for the forcible removal of the tenant from the premises.

Ohio Att’y Gen. Op. No. 78-015 (Apr. 14, 1978); Norman v. McFaul, No. C78-1698 (N.D. Ohio Dec. 20, 1978, and Apr. 25, 1979); Scott v. O’Grady, 760 F. Supp. 1288 (N.D. Ill. 1991), aff’d on other grounds, 975 F.2d 366 (7th Cir. 1992); Hite v. Field, 38 Conn. Supp. 70, 462 A.2d 393 (1982). See, e.g., NRG-Nevada Note Servs., Inc. v.

Barrett, No. 04 -CV-0839 (Ohio C.P., Clark Cty., June 4, 2008) (vacates writ of possession because the tenant had not been served in the foreclosure action).

In these circumstances, the purchaser of the property at the foreclosure sale may forcibly remove the tenant – more precisely, the former tenant but continued occupant under Ohio common law – in a separate eviction action, on a three -day notice under R.C. 1923.04.

A common foreclosure practice is to name a John Doe tenant in the complaint.

The John Doe te nant is not a party in the foreclosure action, unless and until the relevant Civil Rule 15(D) requirements are met. See LaNeve v. Atlas Recycling, Inc., 119 Ohio St.2d 324, 894 N.E.2d 25 (2008); Ludwigsen v. Lakeside Plaza, LLC , No.

CA2014-03-008, 2014 WL 7014717 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 15, 2014).

#### 3. Protecting Tenants at Foreclosure Act


##### a. Preemption of Ohio Common Law

The PTFA 381 preempts any state or local law that provides tenants with less protection than the PTFA. Therefore, the PTFA, when it ap plies, preempts Ohio common law on the effect of a foreclosure sale on residential tenancies (see Section XXII.C.2, above).

380 See note 378, above.

381 See note 378, above.

##### b. Applicable Tenancies

The PTFA applies to bona fide residential rental agreements – both written leases and oral periodic tenancies – that exist at a property when “complete title” of the property is transferred by foreclosure.

In Ohio, “complete title” is transferred upon the confir mation of the foreclosure
sale. See Hausman v. City of Dayton , 73 Ohio St.3d 671, 653 N.E.2d 1190 (1995);

Bates v. Postulate Invs. , 176 Ohio App.3d 523, 892 N.E.2d 937 (8th Dist. 2008).

Therefore, the PTFA applies to any bona fide residential rental agreement that has been entered into before the confirmation of the foreclosure sale, even if it was entered into after the commencement of the foreclosure action.

A bona fide residential rental agreement means: (1) the tenant is not the child, spouse, or parent of the mortgagor (former landlord); (2) the lease or tenancy was the result of an arms-length transaction; and (3) the rent is not substantially less than the fair market rent (or the rent is reduced or subsidized due to a federal, state, or local subsidy).

The owner to which the PTFA applies is the immediate successor in interest to the property pursuant to the foreclosure (“new owner”). In Ohio, this means the purchaser of the property at the foreclosure sale.

##### c. PTFA – General Requirements

When the PTFA applies, the new owner acquires title subject to the rights of any bona fide tenants under their respective rental agreements. The new owner becomes the landlord under the rental agreements, like a purchaser of tenant -occupied real property under Ohio common law. See Section XXII.B, above.

However, the new owner’s right to terminate the rental agreement is subject to certain PTFA requirements.

With respect to written leases, absent a lease violation, the lease continues until the end of the lease te rm, with two exceptions. First, the new owner may, with a 90 -day notice of termination, terminate the lease in order to use the property as the new owner’s personal residence. Second, if the lease has less than 90 days remaining when the new owner acquir es title, the lease continues until either: (1) the new owner provides the tenant with a 90 -day notice of termination, or (2) the tenant vacates the property at the end of the lease term or thereafter.

With respect to month -to-month tenancies, absent ca use for termination, the tenancy continues until either: (1) the new owner provides the tenant with a 90 -day 605 notice of termination and the notice period expires, or (2) the tenant provides the new owner with a 30-day notice of termination under R.C. 5321.17(B) and the notice period expires.

With respect to Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”) tenancies, the HCVP lease is treated the same as other written leases. In addition, the Housing Assistance Payments (“HAP”) contract continues, with the new ownersubject to the terms of the HAP contract. The PTFA also bars the new owner from terminating the HCVP lease on the “other good cause” business ground that it will assist in the sale of the property.

The new owner (or the new owner’s agent) is the only part y who may serve the 90-day notice of termination. See HUD Notice, 74 Fed. Reg. 30106, 30107 (June 24, 2009).

The service of the 90-day notice of termination may not precede the confirmation of the foreclosure sale because, before the confirmation: (1) t he determination as to which entity (or person) will be the new owner has not been made; (2) none of the potential new owners (such as the mortgagee) is the new owner at that time; and (3) since none of the potential new owners (aside from the mortgagor) possess title to the property, the potential new owners are without standing to serve a notice of termination.

Since the 90-day notice of termination is analogous to the R.C. 5321.17(B) notice of termination, two conclusions reasonably follow. First, gener ally the tenant is obligated to pay rent during the 90 -day notice period. See Section I.D.4, above.

Second, the bar against serving the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate prior to the expiration of the tenancy should apply to the 90-day notice period. See Section I.C.3.e., above.

##### d. PTFA – Rent Payment Issues

The rent payment issues arising from the confirmation of the foreclosure sale are the same as those arising from any other sale of occupied rental premises. Those issues are discussed in Section VII.F.10, above.

##### e. PTFA – Eviction Issues

The PTFA makes eviction actions the exclusive remedy for evicting bona fide
tenants. The foreclosure court is without grounds to enter an eviction order against the tenant because the tenancy survives the foreclosure sale.

Noncompliance with the PTFA is an eviction action defense. 382 “The PTFA was intended to allow tenants who are victims of theforeclosure crisis a protection that can be used in the state courts to combat unlawful evictions.Nativi v. Deutsche Bank Nat’l Trust Co. , No. 09 -06090, 2010 WL 2179885, at *3 (N.D. Cal. May 26, 2010).

Moreover, in Ohio, a landlord’s noncompliance with a federal notice of termination requirement is a legal defense to an eviction action. See Sections III.A-E, above A trial court may raise, sua sponte, any noncompliance with the PTFA 90 -day notice requirement because it constitutes a jurisdictional is sue. Proper service of the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate is a jurisdictional requirement for commencement of an eviction action. See Section I.C.1.b, above. An R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate may not be served before the expiration of the tenancy. See Section I.C.3.e, above. Therefore, if the new owner violates the PTFA 90-day notice requirement, the R.C. 1923.04 notice to vacate has been served prematurely (i.e., before the expiration of the tenancy) and, therefore, the court is without subject-matter jurisdiction to hear the eviction action.

If the eviction action is for nonpayment of rent, the PTFA 90 -day notice of termination requirement does not apply. Like the analogous R.C. 5321.17(B) notice, it is a notice for nonrenewal (or to enable the new owner to reside in the property), not for a tenant’s breach of the rental agreement.

##### f. PTFA – Deed in Lieu of Foreclosure

The PTFA does not apply to a deed in lieu of foreclosure because the title has not been transferred by foreclosure. However, a deed inlieu of foreclosure is governed by Ohio common law on the sale of tenant -occupied real property. See Section XXII.B, above.

##### g. PTFA – Sale by Initial Successor in Interest to Third

Party When the initial successor in interest sells the property to a third party, the sale is 382 E.g., Fed. Nat’l Mortgage A ssoc. v. Moore , No. 11 -CVG-15320 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Jan. 4, 2012) (lack of 90 -day notice); Fed. Nat’l Mortgage Assoc. v. Norman, No.

10-CVG-02142 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 8, 2010) (lack of 90 -day notice;

plaintiff’s burden toprove not bona fide tenant); Fed. Nat’l Mortgage Assoc. v. Taylor, No. 10- CVG-02141 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 2, 2010) (same); Fed. Nat’l Mortgage
Assoc. v. Dobson , No. 10 -CVG-02140 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland, Hous. Div., Mar. 2, 2010) (same).

607 governed by Ohio common law on the sale of tenant -occupied real property. See Section XXII.B, above. The third -party purchaser thus acquires title subject to the existing tenancy. Id. Since the PTFA tenant protections become a part of the tenancy between the tenant and the initial successor in interest, the third -party purchases the property subject to the PTFA tenant protections.

#### 4. FHA-Insured Mortgages – PTFA and the

Occupied Conveyance Rule When an FHA -insured mortgage on a single -family ( i.e., one to four unit) residential property is foreclosed, the mortgagee “conveys the property to HUD in exchange for an FHA mortgage insurance claim.” HUD Notice, 75 Fed. Reg. 66385, 66386 (Oct. 28, 2010). “HUD generally requires the mortgagee to convey the property unoccupied, but in certain circumstances, as described in HUD’s occupied conveyance regulations at 24 CFR 203.670-203.681, HUD will accept the property occupied.” Id.

When HUD requires an unoccupied conveyance, the mortgagee is still required to comply with PTFA (see Section XXII.C.3, above), for any bona fide rental agreement at the property. Id.; HUD Notice, 77 Fed. Reg. 15379 (Mar. 15, 2012).

In addition, pending HUD’s review of a tenant’s request for continued occupancy under 24 C.F.R. §§ 203.670-203.681, any eviction action against the tenant should be stayed on preemption and/or ripeness grounds. Bank United v. Vogel, No. 99-SP-2369
(Mass. Hous. Ct., Hampden Div., Nov. 5, 1999).

If the mortgagee fails to serve the tenant with the required notice of these continued occupancy rights ( see 24 C.F.R. § 203.675), it should be a defense to any eviction action against the tenant, at least until the ten ant is provided with the notice and an opportunity to exercise these continued occupancy rights. See BancPlus Mortgage
Corp. v. Smith , No. 57641, 1990 WL 15294 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 22, 1990) (under Appellate Rule 18(C), court finds that “appellant’s brief reasonably appears to sustain a reversal of the judgment upon the facts and issues presented”).

### D. Utility Termination for Landlord

Nonpayment, Tenant Rights

#### 1. PUCO Regulated Utility Company

When a tenant’s utility services are included in the rental payments and are furnished by a PUCO regulated utility company, PUCO rules provide the tenant with certain safeguards in any termination of service for the landlord’s nonpayment.383 Ohio
Admin. Code § 4901:1-18-08.

The safeguards include the utility comp any’s duty to provide the tenant with ten days advance notice of the proposed termination of service. Id. In the notice, the company must “summarize the remedies tenants may choose to prevent disconnection or to have service reconnected.” Id.

The safeguards also include the tenant’s right to maintain (or reinstate) the utility service by paying the amount due for the current month’s service for the building (plus any reconnection charge). Id.

The safeguards also include, for mastermetered premises, the tenant’s right to maintain (or reinstate) the utility service by timely invoking the R.C. 5321.07 rent deposit procedures, provided at least fifty percent of the tenants of the occupied dwelling units in the building participate. Id. Under this remedy, after rent depositing, the tenants apply to the court for an order to use the rent deposit to maintain or reinstate the utility service. Id.

#### 2. Governmental Utility Supplier – Equal Protection

and Due Process When a utility supplier is a governmental entity ( e.g., municipal water or electric company), its procedures for termination of service are subject to the requirements of equal protection and due process. See Memphis Light, Gas & Water Div. v. Craft, 436 U.S. 1 (1978); Myers v. City of Alcoa, 752 F.2d 196 (6th Cir. 1985);Davis v. Weir, 497 F.2d 139 (5th Cir. 1974).

Therefore, when a tenant’s utility services are included in the rental payments and are furnished by a governmental entity, equal protection and due process requirements may provide the tena nt with certain safeguards in any termination of service for the landlord’s nonpayment.384 E.g., Turpen v. City of Corvallis , 26 F.3d 978 (9th Cir.

1994); DiMassimo v. City of Clearwater , 805 F.2d 1536 (11th Cir. 1986); Sterling v.
Vill. of Maywood, 579 F.2d 1350 (7th Cir. 1978);Koger v. Guarino, 412 F. Supp. 1375 383 For a discussion on tenant claims against a landlord where a landlord proposes to terminate or terminates utility services in order to recover possession of the premises, see Chapter XII, above.

384 See note 383, above.

609 (E.D. Pa. 1976), aff’d, 549 F.2d 795 (3d Cir. 1977); Davis v. Weir, 497 F.2d 139 (5th
Cir. 1974); Pilchen v. City of Auburn, New York, 728 F. Supp.2d 192 (N.D.N.Y. 2010).

With respect to these terminations, court decisions have held that equal protection provides the tenant with the right to become the utility customer for the premises without paying the landlord’s delinquent utility bill, which enables the tenant to maintain or reinstate the utility service. Golden v. City of Columbus, 404 F.3d 950 (6th
Cir. 2005); Sterling v. Vill. of Maywood , 579 F.2d 1350 (7th Cir. 1978); Craft v.

Memphis Light, Gas & Water Div. , 534 F.2d 684 (6th Cir. 1976), aff’d on other grounds, 436 U.S. 1 (1978); Davis v. Weir, 497 F.2d 139 (5th Cir. 1974); Pilchen v.

City of Auburn, New York, 728 F. Supp. 2d 192 (N.D.N.Y. 2010). Cf. O’Neal v. City of Seattle, 66 F.3d 1064 (9th Cir. 1995) (prior customer’s delinquency); Lake v. City of Youngstown , No. 4:93CV2559 (N.D. O hio July 14, 1994) (prior customer’s delinquency). But cf. Ransom v. Marrazzo , 848 F.2d 398 (3d Cir. 1988) (non -tenant occupant and prior customer’s delinquency; declined to addreess the constitutional rights of non-delinquent tenants).

With respect to these terminations, court decisions also have held that due process requires the utility company to provide the tenant with an adequate pre -termination
notice. Turpen v. City of Corvallis, 26 F.3d 978 (9th Cir. 1994); DiMassimo v. City of Clearwater, 805 F. 2d 1536 (11th Cir. 1986); Davis v. Weir , 497 F.2d 139 (5th Cir.

1974); Pilchen v. City of Auburn, New York, 728 F. Supp.2d 192 (N.D.N.Y. 2010).But see Sterling v. Vill. of Maywood, 579 F.2d 1350 (7th Cir. 1978). But cf. James v. City of St. Petersburg , 33 F.3d 1304 (11th Cir. 1994) (en banc) (tenant without property interest if neither landlord nor tenant has contractual relationship with the utility company).

The case law is split on whether, for these terminations, due process requires the utility comp any to provide the tenant with a pre -termination hearing opportunity.

Compare Turpen v. City of Corvallis, 26 F.3d 978 (9th Cir. 1994) (not required), and DiMassimo v. City of Clearwater, 805 F.2d 1536 (11th Cir. 1986) with Davis v. Weir, 497 F.2d 139 (5th Cir. 1974) (required), and Pilchen v. City of Auburn, New York, 728

### F. Supp.2d 192 (N.D.N.Y. 2010).

The Sixth Circuit has held, in two cases, that the tenants failed to demonstrate a due process property interest for these terminations: Golden v. City of Columbus, 404 F.3d 950 (6th Cir. 2005), and Midkiff v. Adams County Reg’l Water Dist. , 409 F.3d 758 (6th Cir. 2005).

In Golden, the tenant’s due process property interest argument was “the bare assertion that because water is ‘an absolute necessity of life,’ the City’s termination of her service was unconstitutional.” 404 F.3d at 958. Because the ten ant did not rely upon any provision of the Ohio landlord-tenant law, the court opted not to express any opinion on whether that law creates a due process property interest for these
terminations. Id.

In Midkiff, the tenant’s due process property interestargument was based on R.C.

5321.04(A)(6) and 5321.15. The court rejected this argument on the ground that neither of these sections provides tenants with a right to continued water service. The court’s analysis of R.C. 5321.04(A)(6) is correct. The court’s analysis of R.C. 5321.15 is incorrect because, for the reasons discussed in Section XII.B.3, above, a tenant may secure injunctive relief to maintain or restore water service, in response to a landlord’s violation of R.C. 5321.15. More importantly, these analyses are irrelevant because the tenant’s due process property interest, for these terminations, does not arise from R.C.

5321.04(A)(6) or R.C. 5321.15.

In Ohio, the tenant’s due process property interest for these terminations arises from R.C. 5321.04(A)(2) and, in many cases, R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) as well. The R.C.

5321.04(A)(2) requirement “to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition” necessarily includes a requirement to furnish water service, if the rental agreement requires the landlord to pay for the water service. Absent the water service, the premises are not habitable. Similarly, where a local code requires water service for rental premises, and where the rental agreement requires the landlord to pay for the water service, the R.C. 5321.04(A)(1) requirement “to comply with the requirements of all applicable building, housing, health, and safety codes that materially affect health and safety” necessarily includes a requirement to furnish water service.

If the due process pro perty interest depends on whether a tenant may obtain injunctive relief to enforce these R.C. 5321.04(A)(1),(2) rights to continued water service, as Midkiff suggests, R.C. 5321.07(B)(2) expressly provides tenants with that injunctive remedy. R.C. 3767.41 likewise provides tenants with an injunctive remedy if their rental premises are “no longer fit and habitable” (e.g., without water service).

See Section IX.G.1, above.

For further discussion on these constitutional rights, see National Consumer Law Center, Access to Utility Service (5th ed. 2011).

The following court decisions and consent orders establish due process notice and hearing procedures for municipal water suppliers in Ohio.

611
(1) Canter v. Vill. of Smithfield, No. C2-01-0138 (S.D. Ohio June 15, 2001) (consent decree, Civil Rule 71 relief)

### E. Utility Submeter Issues

Some residential landlords and manufactured home park operators (“mh park operators”) install a utility submeter system for each of their rental units or lots and then charge the tenant or manufactured home park owner (“mh park owner”) for the service, at the public utility rate (or higher) plus an administrative fee. See Wingo v.

Nationwide Energy Partners, 163 Ohio St.3d 208, 169 N.E.3d 617 (2020); Pledger v.
Pub. Utils. Comm’n of Ohio, 109 Ohio St.3d 463, 849 N.E.2d 14 (2006); Anderle v.

Ideal Mobile Home Park, Inc., 114 Ohio App.3d 385, 683 N.E.2d 348 (8th Dist. 1996);

Sliwinski v. Capital Props. Mgmt. Ltd. , No. CV2006 -02-0884, 2007 WL 5843 246 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Dec. 11, 2007).

In these circumstances, the landlord or mh park operator often uses a submeter company to manage the billing system.

A potential issue, relative to these submeter systems, is whether the landlord, mh park operator, or submeter company is operating as a public utility (and then subject to a determination on whether it is doing so unlawfully). PUCO makes this determination under the R.C. 4905.05 definitions of a public utility, as implemented in 613 the PUCO -established Shroyer test for submeter systems. 385 Wingo v. Nationwide Energy Partners, 163 Ohio St.3d 208, 169 N.E.3d 617 (2020); Pledger v. Pub. Utils.

Comm’n of Ohio, 109 Ohio St.3d 463, 849 N.E.2d 14 (2006) . In Pledger, the court upheld the Shroyer test. In Wingo, the court reaffirmed its support of the original Shroyer test but held unlawful PUCO’s 2016 and 2017 modifications of the Shroyer test.386 Another potential issue, relative to these submeter systems, is whether, under R.C.

Chapter 1327, these meters are subject to inspection and certification by the Weights and Measures Division of the Ohio Department of Agriculture (“ODA”). The ODA has stated that these meters are not subject to such inspection and certification because they are used for residential (and not commercial) purposes. Letter from William A.

Hopper, Jr., Chief Legal Counsel, ODA, to John Wood, Esq. (July 27, 2006).

However, in Sliwinski v. Capital Props. Mgmt. Ltd. , No. CV2006-020884, 2007 WL 5843246 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Dec. 11, 2007), the court denied the landlord’s motion to dismiss the tenant’s claim that was premised on this inspection and certification requirement.387 Another potential issue, relative to these submeter companies, is whether the Ohio Consumer Sales Practices Act applies to th eir third-party billing system. For a discussion on this issue, see Section XXII.F, below.

385 PUCO adopted the Shroyer test in Matter of the Complaints of Inscho, Etc. v. Shroyer’s Mobile Homes, Pub. Utils. Comm’n of Ohio, Nos. 90 -182-WS-CSS, Etc., 1992 WL 937210 (Opinion and Order, filed Feb. 27, 1992). It is the following three-part test: (1) Has the landlord manifested an intent to be a public utility by availing itself of special benefits available to public utilities such as accepting a grant of a franchised territory, a certificate of public convenience and necessity, or use of the public right of way for utility purposes? ( 2) Is the utility service available to the general public rather than just to tenants? (3) Is the provision of utility service ancillary to the landlord’s primary business?

386 PUCO adopted these modifications of the Shroyer test in Matter of the Comm’n’s Investigation of Submetering in the State of Ohio , Pub. Utils. Comm’n of Ohio, No. 15-1594- AU-COI (Finding and Order, filed Dec. 7, 2016, and Second Entry on Rehearing, filed June 21, 2017). The modifications focused the test on whether the utility charge s exceeded the amount that the tenant or manufactured home park owner would have been charged through the local public utility’s default service tariffs.

387 The tenant thereafter dismissed the action with prejudice as to all defendants. Sliwinski v.

Hunt Club, No. CV2006-02-0884 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Nov. 13, 2012).

### F. Ohio Consumer Sales Practices Act

The Ohio Consumer Sales Practices Act (“CSPA”), R.C. Chapter 1345, does not apply to residential lease transactions. Heritage Hills, Ltd. v. Deacon, 49 Ohio St.3d 80, 551 N.E.2d 125 (1990). See also Mesnick v. De Ville Apts., No. 71517, 1997 WL 599376 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Sept. 25, 1997); Hartman v. Garden Woods Apts., No.

15228, 1995 WL 628021 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct. 25, 1995).

However, the CSPA may apply to the service, between a submeter company and a tenant, for furnishing a rental unit with utility service under a submeter system. See Sliwinski v. Capital Props. Mgmt. Ltd. , No. CV2006 -02-0884, 2007 WL 5843246 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Dec. 11, 2007) (in denying motion to dismiss CSPA claim, “Court finds first that the sale of water and sewage may be an action unrelated to the landlord-tenant relationship, and therefore is not necessarily preempted by the landlord-tenant act”).388 Cf. Nelson v. Am. Power & Light, No. 2:08-cv-549, 2010 WL 3219498 (S.D. Ohio Aug. 12, 2010) (motion to dismiss CSPA claims denied; per lease, tenant maintained electric service with utility company, which had agreement with landlord for commencing eviction upon any tenant delinquency of 60 days on the utility account).

Under these third-party billing systems, typically, the submeter company installs and maintains the submeter, reads the submeter, and bills and collects payments from the tenant for the utility service. The tenant remits payments for the utility service for the rental unit to the submeter company, not the landlord or utility company.

The tenant ’s residential lease incorporates the service – the delivery/payment system between the submeter company and the tenant – and permits the landlord to evict the tenant for any nonpayment of the tenant’s payment obligation to the submeter
company. A separat e contract is executed between the submeter company and the landlord.

While the CSPA does not apply to “pure” real estate transactions, it applies to “mixed” transactions, which involve a transfer of both real property and either a service or personal property. Brown v. Liberty Clubs, Inc., 45 Ohio St.3d 191, 543 N.E.2d 783 (1989). While the Heritage Hills decision was issued after the Brown decision, Ohio appellate courts have continued to follow the holding in Brown, often in cases closer to a pure real estate transaction than the incorporation of the third-party billing system 388 See note 387, above.

615 into the residential lease.

For example, courts consistently have held that the construction of a new home constitutes a “service” within the meaning of the CSPA, even though the CSPA does not apply to the accompanying purchase of real property. See, e.g., Fine v. U.S. Erie Islands Co., No. OT-07-048, 2009 WL 826526 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 31, 2009);

Morrison v. Skestos, No. 04AP-244, 2004 WL 2944159 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., De c.

21, 2004); Kieber v. Spicer Constr. Co., 85 Ohio App.3d 391, 619 N.E.2d 1105 (2nd
Dist. 1993).

A residential lease that incorporates a third-party billing system for a utility service is arguably a “mixed” transaction, similar to those discussed in Kieber and Fine, and not a pure real estate transaction similar to the one discussed in Heritage Hills. The relationship between the submeter company and the tenant is reasonably classified as a service agreement for a utility service.

This service is separat e and distinct from the real estate transaction between the landlord and tenant, i.e., the transfer of the landlord’s interest in the real property to the tenant for the lease period, which includes the landlord’s obligation to maintain the property and the tenant’s obligation to pay rent. In Heritage Hills, the dispute arose solely from the terms of the lease that concern the transfer of the landlord’s interest in the real property to the tenant – the amount due to the landlord for repairs made to the real property.

### G. Statute of Limitations, R.C. Chapter

5321 Claims The statute of limitations for a statutory claim, under a remedial statute that does not contain a statute of limitations, is six years under R.C. 2305.07. Cleveland Mobile Radio Sales, Inc. v. Verizon Wireless , 113 Ohio St.3d 394, 865 N.E.2d 1275, ¶ 7 (2007). R.C. Chapter 5321 is a remedial statute. See Section V.A, above.

The test for determining whether a claim is a statutory claim, for R.C. 2305.07 purposes, is set forth in McAlliffe v. W. States Import Co. , 72 Ohio St.3d 534, 651 N.E.2d 957, syllabus no. 1 (1995):

In order for a statutory cause of action to be “an action ... upon a liability created by statute” under R.C. 2305.07, that cause of action must be one that would not exist bu t for the statute. Any statutory “modification, alteration or conditioning” of a common-law cause of action which falls short of creating a previously unavailable cause of action is not “an action ... upon a liability created by statute.” Applying McAllife, courts have held that the statute of limitations for a tenant’s claim for bodily injury or injury to personal property, arising from a landlord’s R.C.

5321.04(A) violation, is two years under R.C. 2305.10 (and not six years under R.C.

2305.07). E.g., Shorter v. Neapolitan, 179 Ohio App.3d 608, 902 N.E.2d 1061 (7th
Dist. 2008); Segal v. Zehman-Wolf Mgmt., Inc., 139 Ohio App.3d 146, 743 N.E.2d 425 (8th Dist. 2000); Daniels v. Thompson , No. 49015, 1985 WL 7992 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 25, 1985); Craver v. Doogan, No. 2003 -CVC-00982, 2005 WL 4985282 (Ohio C.P., Clermont Cty., May 27, 2005). Cf. Pummill v. Carnes, No. 02-CA-2659, 2003 WL 896349 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Feb. 4, 2003) (R.C. 3733.10 (now R.C.

4781.38) violation, although court refers to R.C. 5321.04).

The R.C. 2305.10 statute of limitation applies to these claims “regardless of whether it is framed as a tort claim, a contract claim, or a warranty -of-habitability claim.” Shorter v. Neapolitan, 179 Ohio App.3d 608, 902 N.E.2d 1061, ¶ 18 (7th Dist.

2008).

The statute of limitations for a tenant’s R.C. 5321.04 contract claim for other damages (such as general damages) is eight years under R.C. 2305.06 for written rental agreements, and six years under R.C. 2305.07 for oral rental agreements. 389 Rental agreements are treated as contracts. See Section VII.A, above.

For written rental agreements, the statute of limitations for a landlord’s claim for damage to the rental premises is governed by R.C. 2305.06. Young v. McCleese, No.

97-CA-2351, 1998 WL 399861 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., July 13, 1998).

The statute of limitations for each of the remaining claims under R.C. Chapter 5321 is still to be determined. The remaining claims are those under R.C. 5321.02(B), 5321.05(C), 5321.07(B)(2), 5321.12, 5321.15(C), and 5321.16(C).

When an R.C. Chapter 5321 claim is ongoing, in month-to-month tenancies, each month constitutes a new contract from which the statute of limitations period runs.

Mozingo v. 2007 Gaslight Ohio, LLC, No. 27759, 2016 WL 3608751,¶ 22 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., July 6, 2016).

389 Generally, an oral rental agreement will be valid and enforceable only if it is taken out of the statute of frauds under the equitable doctrine of partial performance. See Section XXII.A.3, above.

617

### H. Recoupment, R.C. Chapter 5321 Claims

The “supreme court has held that a claim that would be barred by the statute of limitations if brought in an action for affirmative relief is nonetheless available as a defense or under a common law theory of recoupment when it arises out of the same transaction as the plaintiff’s claim for relief and is offered to reduce the plaintiff’s right to relief. Riley v. Montgomery (1984), 11 Ohio St.3d 75, 78, 463 N.E.2d 1246.” Johnson v. Brown, No. 2002-CA-76, 2003 WL 1193795, ¶ 27 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist.,
Mar. 14, 2003).

In Johnson, the landlord asserted a claim for tenant damage to the rental property.

The court held that the tenant’s claim for sewer repair costs, incurred as a result of the landlord’s failure to remedy a sewage backup problem, arose from thesame transaction for recoupment purposes.

### I. PHA Sovereign Immunity, R.C.

Chapter 2744

#### 1. Overview

In Moore v. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. , 121 Ohio St.3d 455, 905 N.E.2d 606 (2009), the Supreme Court clarifies the R.C. Chapter 2744 sovereign immunity of public housing authorities (“PHAs) relative to tenant claims.

The Moore decision contains two significant conclusions of law.

First, it holds that “the operation of a public housing authority is a governmental function under R.C. 2744.01(C)(2).” Moore, 121 Ohio St.3d 455, ¶ 19. Therefore, as to tenant claims, PHAs are not liable under the R.C. 2744.02(B)(2) exception “for injury, death, or loss to person or property caused by the negligent performance of acts by their employees with respect to proprietary functions.” Id., ¶ 20 n.5.

Second, it holds that “a unit of public housing is a building ‘used in connection with the performance of a governmental function’ within the meaning of R.C.

2744.01(C)(2).” Id., ¶ 24. Therefore, as to tenant claims, PHAs are li able under the R.C. 2744.02(B)(4) exception for injury, death, or loss to person or property that: (1) is caused by the negligence of a PHA employee, (2) occurs within or on the rental premises, and (3) is due to a physical defect within or on the rental premises. Id.

But if the tenant’s claim arises from a physical defect in Housing Choice Voucher program rental premises, the PHA does have immunity from liability under R.C.

2744.02. Holimon v. Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. , 180 N.E.3d 1226, 2021-Ohio- 3840 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., 2021). The R.C. 2744.02(B)(4) exception is inapplicable in these cases because, unlike the Public Housing program, the rental premises are privately owned (not governmentally owned) buildings. Id.

R.C. Chapter 2744 does not de fine the term “physical defect” for R.C.

2744.01(C)(2) purposes. However, the appellate courts have adopted a definition, namely: “if the instrumentality that caused the plaintiff’s injury did not operate as intended due to a perceivable condition– or, to put it in the words of theHamrick court, if the instrumentality contained a perceivable imperfection that impaired its worth or utility.” Leasure v. Adena Local Sch. Dist. , 973 N.E.2d 810, 2012 -Ohio-3071, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., 2012) (improperly s et up bleachers – physical defect). Accord Johnson v. Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth., No. C-210240, 2022 WL 68345 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Jan. 7, 2022) (loose floor mat on top step of stairs– genuine issue on whether it was physical defect and thus summaryjudgment denied);Moncrief v. Bohn, 9 N.E.3d 508, 2014 -Ohio-837, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2014) (lack of adequate security services – no physical defect); Jones v. Delaware City Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ. , 995 N.E.2d 1252, 2013-Ohio-3907, ¶ 22 (Ohio App.,5th Dist., 2013) (orchestra pit without reflective tape and lights – physical defect); Gibbs v. Columbus Metro. Hous. Auth. ,
No. 11AP-711, 2012 WL 1852086, ¶¶ 13 -16 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 22, 2012) (trash chute in which body found – no physical defect); Duncan v. Cuyahoga Cmty.

Coll., 970 N.E.2d 1092, 2012-Ohio-1949, ¶¶ 26,27 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2012) (lack of floor mats in self-defense class – no physical defect); Hamrick v. Bryan City School Dist., No. WM-10-014, 2011 WL 2090038, ¶¶ 27,28 (Ohio A pp., 6th Dist., May 27,
2011) (unmarked service pit in bus garage – no physical defect).

PHA employees, as employees of a political subdivision, may rely on their R.C.

2744.03(A)(6) immunity from liability “for injury, death, or loss to person or property allegedly caused by an act or omission in connection with a governmental or proprietary function.” However, this immunity is subject to three exceptions. It does not apply if the “employee’s acts or omissions were manifestly outside the scope of the employee’s employment or official responsibilities.” R.C. 2744.03(A)(6)(a). It does not apply if the “employee’s acts or omissions were with malicious purpose, in 619 bad faith, or in a wanton or reckless manner.”390 R.C. 2744.03(A)(6)(b). And it does not apply if the “[c]ivil liability is expressly imposed upon the employee by a section of the Revised Code.” R.C. 2744.03(A)(6)(c).

The Moore decision does not address the R.C. Chapter 2744 sovereign immunity of PHAs with respect to intentional tort claims. But “courts have generally held that because R.C. 2744.02(B) includes no specific exemption for intentional torts, political subdivisions are immune from intentional tort claims.” Griffits v. Vill. of Newburgh Heights, No. 91428, 2009 WL 280376, ¶ 26 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 5, 2009).

Accord Zieber v. Heffelfinger, No. 08-CA-0042, 2009 WL 695533, ¶ 27 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Mar. 17, 2009) (“Ohio courts have consistently held that political subdivisions are immune under R.C. 2744.02 from intentional tort claims”).E.g., Rid- All Exterminating Co. v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 98174, 2012 WL 5370978, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 1, 2012) (contractor’s fraud claim barred); Kelly v.

Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 10-00430 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Apr. 5, 2011) (tenant’s trespass and conversion claims barred under R.C. 2744.02,.05 immunity).

The R.C. Chapter 2744 sovereign immunity of PHAs does not apply to contract
claims. R.C. 2744.09(A). See also Bozeman v. Cleveland Metro. Hous. Auth. , Nos.

92435,92436, 2009 WL 3321402, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 15, 2009);Carter v.
