Complete Gen. Constr. Co., No. 08AP-309, 2008 WL 5104787, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Dec. 4, 2008).

The R.C. Chapter 2744 sovereign immunity of PHAs also does not apply to federal claims (statutory or constitutional). R.C. 2744.09(E).

#### 2. Negligence Per Se Claims

An R.C. 5321.04(A) violation may give rise to a tenant’s negligence per se claim against a landlord. See Section IX.B, above. The claim often arises from a defective condition at the rental premises.

Under Moore, tenants may assert these claims against PHAs under the R.C.

2744.02(B)(4) exception. Appellate court decisions on these tenant claims against PHAs are summarized below.

390 For a discussion on the meaning of the terms willful, wa nton, and reckless, including their differences, see Anderson v. City of Massillon, 134 Ohio St.3d 380, 983 N.E.2d 266 (2012). For a discussion directly on the elements of this exception, see Spears v. Akron Police Dep’t, No.

24847, 2010 WL 625822, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Feb. 24, 2010).

Three courts upheld denials of the PHA’s motion for judgment on the pleadings, with remands for a determination on whether the defective condition was a “physical defect” under R.C. 2744.02(B)(4): Diaz v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 92907, 2010 WL 27868 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 7, 2010) (defective window); Williams v.

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 92964, 2009 WL 4857623 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Dec. 17, 2009) (defective pool table); Bozeman v. Cleveland Metro. Hous. Auth., Nos.

92435,92436, 2009 WL 3321402 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 15, 2009) (lead paint).

One court denied the tenant’s claim against the PHA on the ground that the defective physical condition did not cause the injury. Canidate v. Cuyahoga Metro.
Hous. Auth., No. 101753, 2015 WL 1121444 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Mar. 12, 2015) (2- 1 decision). The tenant slipped as a result of water on the floor of the first -floor common area. The water came from an overflowing sink in a third -floor apartment.

The defective condition was a missing ceiling tile in the first-floor common area.

One court held that an alleged lack of adequate security service did not constitute a physical defect under R.C. 2744.02(B)(4). Moncrief v. Bohn, 9 N.E.3d 508, 2014 - Ohio-837, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., 2014).

One court held that the laundry chute, in which the tenant’s body was found, was not defective and thus not a physical defect under R.C. 2744.02(B)(4). Gibbs v.

Columbus Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 11AP -711, 2012 WL 1852086 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., May 22, 2012).

One court upheld a denial of summary judgment because a material issue of fact existed on whether the PHA had notice – actual or constructive – of the defective laundry pole. Dickerson v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., Nos. 100650,100943, 2014 WL 5408456 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 23, 2014).

#### 3. Contract Claims

An R.C. 5321.04 violation may give rise to a tenant’s contract claim against a
landlord. See Section IX.A, above. Similarly, a rental agreement violation may give rise to a ten ant’s contract claim against a landlord. “The breach of a contract for the lease of property may give rise to ordinary contract remedies for the injured party.” U.S.A. Parking Sys., Inc. v. Penn -Lillis Corp., No. 66936, 1995 WL 601186, at *4 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 12, 1995).

The tenant may assert these contract claims against a PHA because the R.C.

Chapter 2744 sovereign immunity does not apply to contract claims (see Section XXII.I.l., above). E.g., Bozeman v. Cleveland Metro. Hous. Auth., Nos. 92435, 92436, 621 2009 WL 3321402, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 15, 2009); Kelly v. Chillicothe
Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 10-00430 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Apr. 5, 2011).

In addition to its express terms, a residential rental agreement incorporates by operation of law the rights and duties that are established in R.C. Chapter 5321. See Section VII.B, above. Therefore, a tenant may assert a rental agreement contract claim against a PHA for any PHA violation of R.C. Chapter 5321. 391 E.g., Kelly v.

Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 10-00430 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Apr. 5, 2011) (R.C. 5321.15 contractual liability). This contract claim includes any attorney’s fees provision in the relevant section of R.C. Chapter 5321. Id. See also Lewis v. Romans,
No. 43594, 1982 WL 4713, at *2 n.6 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 24, 1981).

Since punitive damages are not available in contract claims (see Section IX.A.8.e, above), they are not available in these contract claims against a PHA. See Kelly v.

Chillicothe Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 10-00430 (Ohio Mun., Chillicothe, Apr. 5, 2011).

#### 4. Practice Issues

A tenant is not required to plead with specificity the basis on which the PHA lacks sovereign immunity. Diaz v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 92907, 2 010 WL 27868, ¶¶ 14,15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 7, 2010); Williams v. Cuyahoga Metro.
Hous. Auth., No. 92964, 2009 WL 4857623, ¶¶ 10-12 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 17, 2009).

A sovereign immunity defense under R.C. Chapter 2744 is an affirmative defense.

Maddox v. City of East Cleveland, No. 96390, 2012 WL 20134, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Jan. 5, 2012). Consequently, “if it is not raised in a timely fashion, it is waived.” Turner v. Cent. Local Sch. Dist., 85 Ohio St.3d 95, 97, 706 N.E.2d 1261 (1999).

391 A residential rental agreement also contains an implied covenant of quiet enjoyment (see Section VII.C.9, above) and incorporates any other relevant Ohio law. “It is an elementary principle that any law relating to a contract which is in existence at the time of the relevant execution of the contract becomes a part of such contract.” E. Mach. Co. v. Peck, 161 Ohio St.

1, 6-7, 117 N.E.2d 593 (1954).

### J. Municipal Ordinances and Court Rules:

Six Examples of Local Tenant-Rights

#### 1. Introduction

Ohio municipalities have enacted ordinances, and Ohio courts have adopted court rules, that provide tenants with rights that supplement their state law rights. Six examples of those ordinances and rules are discussed in Sections XXII.J.2. -7, below.

The areas of tenant rights that they address are:a pay to stay defense in eviction actions, a limit on late payment fees, a right to counsel in eviction actions, sealing records of an eviction action, a source of income protection, and lead safe rental housing.

#### 2. Pay to Stay Defense in Eviction Actions

At least eight Ohio municipalities have enacted ordinances that establish a pay to stay affirmative legal defense in eviction actions for nonpayment of rent (“pay to stay defense”). Those ordinances (and municipalities) are: Akron OH Municipal Code tit.

15, ch. 150, § 150.5; Cincinnati Municipal Code tit. 8, ch. 871, § 871-9(c); Dayton Code of Ordinances tit. 9, ch. 93, §§ 93.71-.74; Codified Ordinances of Maple Heights
pt. 14, tit. 6, ch. 1488; Codified Ordinances of Newburgh Heights pt. 7, ch. 727; South Euclid OH Code of Ordinances pt. 14, ch. 1415; Toledo Municipal Code pt. 17, tit. 3,
ch. 1770; Yellow Springs OH Code of Ordinances pt. 8, tit. 2, ch. 868.392 The basic elements of the pay to stay defense under these ordinances are: (1) the tenant or a third-party has tendered all past due rent and reasonable late payment fees before the filing of the eviction action and the landlord has refused to accept it; or (2) the tenant or a third party has tendered all past due rent, reasonable late payment fees, and court costs prior to the entry of the eviction judgment and the landlord has refused to accept it.

One significant difference among these ordinances is whether the pay to stay defense is subject to the court’s discretion in each case. The ordinances under which the defense is subject to the court’s discretion are the Akron , Cincinnati, and Dayton 392 In addition, at least three Ohio municipalities enacted emergency o rdinances for the Covid- 19 pandemic that establish, until their respective sunset dates, the pay to stay defense. Those ordinances (and municipalities) are: Cleveland Heights Council Ordinance 79 -2021 (expires
Dec. 31, 2022); Euclid Code of Ordinances ch. 1765 (expires Dec. 31, 2022); Lakewood City Council Ordinance 12-2021 (expires Dec. 31, 2022).

623
ordinances. The ordinances under which the defense is not subject to the court’s discretion, but rather is a tenant’s right as a matter of law, are the Maple Heights, Newburgh Heights, South Euclid, Toledo, and Yellow Springs ordinances.

The following provisions, per city, illustrate other variations among these ordinances.

Akron Late payment fees are limited to the larger of twenty -five dollars or eight percent of the monthly rent amount. A tenant may assert the pay to stay defense only one time per calendar year.

Cincinnati The tenant may meet the tender requirements with the tender of a voucher issued by a social service, nonprofit, or governmental agency that guarantees the payment of these amounts to the landlord. When the tenant invokes the pay to stay defense based on a tender of funds during the eviction hearing, the amount of that tender must also include reasonable attorney’s fees (not to exceed $125).

Dayton The tenant may place the tender of past due rent, late payment fees, and court costs in escrow with the court. Under the pay to stay defense, the tenant cannot be liable for the landlord’s attorney’s fees. A tenant may assert the pay to stay defense only one time per calendar year.

Maple Heights, Newburgh Heights, South Euclid, and Yellow Springs If a tenant tenders all past due rent, reasonable late payment fees, court courts, and the cost of the writ of restitution before the writ of restitutionis executed (and after the entry of the eviction judgment), the landlord is required to accept the tender of these amounts and either dismiss the eviction action ornotify the court to vacate the eviction judgment and dismiss the eviction action.

Toledo Late payment fees are limited to the larger of twenty-five dollars or five percent of the monthly rent amount.

These ordinances codify, as an affirmative defense, a longstanding equity defense in eviction actions for nonpayment of rent. For a discussion of that equity defense, see Section II.EE, above.

Court decisions ruling in favor of the tenant based on the pay to stay defense include: Century Pacific Hous. P’ship v. Jeffries , No. 21 -CVG-4124 (Ohio Mun., Dayton, Oct.19, 2021) (tenant tendered the r ent and late fees for two months, during the second month, and landlord refused it); Buckeye Northwest Realty for Tahoe Blue, LLC v. Stigall, No. CVG-21-05045 (Ohio Mun., Toledo, Hous. Div., June 22, 2021) (tenant tendered the requisite amount during the eviction hearing); Smith v. Cade, No.

21-CVG-2160 (Ohio Mun., Akron, May 12, 2021) (tenant made the requisite tender via a rental assistance program and landlord refused it).

#### 3. Limit on Late Payment Fees

At least three municipalities have enacted ordinances that establish a limit on the amount of the fee that a landlord may assess a tenant for late payment of the monthly rent (“late payment fee”). Those ordinances (and municipalities) are: Akron OH Municipal Code tit. 15, ch. 150,§ 150.52; Cleveland Codified Ordinances pt. III.C, tit.

IX, ch. 375, § 375.02(c); Toledo Municipal Code pt. 17, tit. 3, ch. 1770, § 1770.06.

The Akron ordinance limits late payment fees to the larger of twenty -five dollars or eight percent of the monthly rent amount.

The Cleveland o rdinance limits late payment fees to the larger of twenty -five dollars or five percent of the monthly rent amount. It also contains a second limit on late payment fees that applies when the tenant is not obligated to pay the entire contract rent (e.g., the tenant receives a rent subsidy). In these circumstances, late payment fees also may not exceed twenty -five percent of the portion of the monthly contract rent that the tenant is obligated to pay under the rental agreement.

The Toledo ordinance limits late payment fees to the larger of twenty-five dollars or five percent of the monthly rent amount.

These ordinances supplement the Ohio common law and R.C. 5321.14 limitations on the amount of late payment fees. Those limitations are discussed in Section VII.F.9, above.

#### 4. Right to Counsel in Eviction Actions

Generally, Ohio law does not provide parties in a civil action (including eviction
actions) with a right to counsel. Robinson v. Mays, No. L-19-1065, 2020 WL 1528012 625 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar. 31, 2020) (no constitutional right to counsel in eviction actions; upheld trial court’s ruling that required tenant to proceed pro se when tenant’s attorney could not appear at the eviction hearing due to illness).

Two Ohio municipalities have enacted ordinances that establish a tenant’s right to counsel in eviction actions. Those ordinances (and municipalities) are Cleveland Codified Ordinances pt. III.C, tit. IX, ch. 375, § 375.12, and Toledo Municipal Code
pt. 17, tit. 3, ch. 1768.

The Cleveland ordinance, implemented July 2020, entitles covered individuals to full legal representation in eviction actions filed in the Cleveland Municipal Court, Housing Division. Covered individuals are tenants who have at least one child in the household and whose inco me does not exceed one hundred percent of the federal poverty guidelines.

The Toledo ordinance, implemented January 2022, entitles covered individuals to full legal representation in eviction actions filed in the Toledo Municipal Court, Housing Division. Covered individuals are tenants whose annual gross income does not exceed two hundred percent of the federal poverty guidelines.

#### 5. Sealing Records of an Eviction Action


##### a. Introduction

In certain circumstances, courts will enter an order that seals the reco rds of an eviction action. Courts have entered those orders under Rule 45 (E) of the Rules of Superintendence for the Courts of Ohio, under local court rules, and under the court’s inherent authority . These bases for sealing the records of an eviction act ion are discussed in Sections XXII.J.5.b-d, below.

##### b. Rule 45(E) of the Rules of Superintendence for the

Courts of Ohio The records of an eviction action may be sealed under Rule 45(E) of the Rules of Superintendence for the Courts of Ohio (“Rule 45(E)”).

Under Rule 45(E), there is a presumption in favor of allowing public access to court records. In determining whether to seal the records of an eviction action, the court determines whether clear and convincing evidence of a higher public interest outweighs this presumption. Rule 45(E).

The court considers three factors for these purposes, namely: “(a) whether public policy is served by restricting public access; (b) whether any state, federal, or common law exempts the document or information from public access; (c) whether factors that support restriction of public access exist, including risk of injury to persons, individual privacy rights and interests, proprietary business information, public safety, and fairness of the adjudicatory process.” Rule 45(E).

Three court decisions in which the court granted a tenant’s Rule 45(E) motion to seal the records of an eviction action are: (1) Wayne Metro. Hous. Auth. v. L.F. , No.

CVG-12-02-0021 (Ohio Mun., Wayne Cty., Oct. 28, 2020) (where the landlord voluntarily dismissed the eviction action, motion granted because the records of the eviction action adversely affect ed the tenant with one potential landlord and two employers); (2) Evans v. B.S., No. 19-CVG-02190 (Ohio Mun., Akron, June 11, 2019) (where the eviction action was mistakenly filed against the tenant and then dismissed, motion granted because the records of the eviction action will adversely affect the tenant’s future ability to secure housing and public access to these records does not serve any lawful, meaningful public purpose); an d (3) Real Solutions Realty Grove City, LLC v. D.G. , No. 2018 -CVG-29089 (Ohio Mun., Franklin Cty, Feb. 7, 2019) (where the tenant vacated more than two months before the eviction action filing per a mutual rescission agreement, motion granted because the records of the eviction action adversely affect the tenant’s future ability to secure housing , are misleading and prejudicial to the tenant, and violate public policy by promulgating inaccurate information).

Also, in S.C. v. T.H. , 154 N.E.3d 345, 2020 -Ohio-2698 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., 2020), the trial court summarily denied the tenant’s Rule 45(E) motion to seal the records of the eviction action and, as a result, the court of appeals remanded the case for the trial court to explain its reasoning for denying the motion.

##### c. Local Court Rules

Some courts have adopted a local court rule on the sealing of records of an eviction
action. Two examples are Cleveland Municipal Court, Housing Division, Local Rule 6.13 and Marion Municipal Court, Local Rule 209.1.

Both of the se local rules provide that the records of an eviction action may be sealed, pursuant to the tenant’s motion, when the interests of justice in sealing the records outweighs the interests of the government and the public in maintaining a public record of the action. They also contain examples of circumstances that weigh in favor of sealing the records, which include: the dismissal of the eviction action either by the court or by the landlord; the landlord’s stipulation in support of sealing the 627 records; and, notwithstanding the eviction judgment for the landlord, extenuating circumstances led to the eviction action, five years have elapsed since the entry of the eviction judgment, and during t hat five-year period no other eviction judgment has been entered against the tenant.

Marion Municipal Court, Local Rule 209 also permits the court, on its own motion, to seal the records of an eviction action if the eviction judgment was for the tenant o r the landlord dismissed the eviction action.

The Franklin County Municipal Court has addressed this issue with a procedure not for sealing the records but, instead, for removing the records from the Court’s electronic records. The Court’s website doesnot allow electronic access to the records of an eviction action when the action is more than three years old. These records are still available to the public at the Clerk’s office. The Court also has adopted and placed on its website a standard form for a tenant’s motion to seal the records of an eviction action, which may be filed at any appropriate time.

##### d. Court’s Inherent Authority

A court has inherent authority to enter an order that seals the records of an eviction
action. Lokal Real Estate v. G.R ., No. 2017 -CVG-004609 (Ohio Mun., Cleveland,
Hous. Div., Aug. 20, 2018) (relying onSchussheim v. Schussheim, 137 Ohio St.3d 133, 998 N.E.2d 446 (2013).

In exercising this inherent authority, the court weighs its legitimate need to maintain public records against the tenant’s interest in not being denied housing based on the past filing of an eviction action. Id.

Courts consider a variety of factors in this weighing process. Id. Those factors include whether the landlord dismissed the eviction action, whether the landlord agrees to the sealing of the records or does not oppose it, and whether the tenant has been denied housing because of the unsealed records. Id.

Courts also consider whether unusual and exceptional circumstances exist. Id.

Those circumstances may be the tenant’s age, physical disability, low income, or limited English proficiency, or the disability of the tenant’s child. Id.

#### 6. Source of Income Protection

Municipalities across Ohio have broadened their fair housing ordinances to include source of income (“SOI”)as a protected class. Those ordinances (and municipalities) include: Akron OH Municipal Code tit. 3, ch. 38; Athens City Code tit. 3, § 3.07.61;

Bexley Codified Ordinances pt. 6, ch. 637; Cincinnati Codified Ordinances tit. 7, ch.

740; Cleveland Heights Codified Ordinances pt. 7, ch. 749; Columbus Code of Ordinances tit. 45, ch. 4551, § 4551.03; Linndale Codified Ordinances pt. 5, ch. 515;

Reynoldsburg Codified Ordinances pt. 5, ch. 503; South Euclid Codified Ordinances
pt. 14, ch. 1408; Toledo Municipal Code pt. 5, ch. 554; University Heights Codified Ordinances pt. 8, tit. 2, ch. 820; Warrensville Heights Codified Ordinances pt. 1, tit. 1,
ch. 113; Westerville Codified Ordinancespt. 5, ch. 511; Wickliffe Codified Ordinances
pt. 11, ch. 1103; Worthington Codified Ordinances pt. 5, ch. 539; Yellow Springs OH Codified Ordinances pt. 6, ch. 626.

These SOI ordinances generally prohibit landlords from discriminating against tenants based on the tenant’s source of lawful income. Examples of those protected sources of income include: welfare assistance, child support, financial aid from any person or from any federal, state, or local government program, and social security disability payments.

Most of these SOI ordinances explicitly include rental assistance in their examples of protected sources of income. They are the ordinances enacted in Akron (“housing assistance, housing vouchers, emergency rental assistance”), Athens (“rent vouchers”), Bexley (“rent vouchers”), Cleveland Heights (“housing vouchers, emergency rental assistance”), Columbus (“rent vouchers”), Reynoldsburg (“rent vouchers”), Toledo (“Section 8 housing assistance payments or any other rent subsidy or rent assistance program”), University Heights (“rent vouchers”), Westerville (“emergency rental assistance”), Worthington (“emergency rental assistance”), Yellow Springs (“rental assistance”).

The other SOI ordinances implicitly include rental assistance in their protecte d sources of income. Rental assistance falls within the general examples of protected sources of income that most of these ordinances use, such as all forms of federal, state, or local government assistance payments or subsidies. Also, rental assistance is, in effect, a source of income.

These SOI ordinances vary in their procedures for asserting a claim for an alleged violation of this prohibition. Some permit the claim to be filed only in their administrative complaint procedure. E.g., Akron, Athens, Bexley, Cleveland Heights, Columbus, Linndale, Reynoldsburg, Warrensville Heights, Westerville, Wickliffe, and Worthington ordinances. Some establish a private right of action. E.g., Cincinnati
ordinance. And some authorize both an administrative comp laint and a private right of action. E.g., University Heights and Yellow Springs ordinances.

629 These SOI ordinances also vary in the penalties that they impose for a violation of this prohibition. The penalties range among injunctive relief, damages, civil penalties, and/or attorney’s fees.

For a nationwide list of the states, counties, and cities that have enacted SOI laws, see the National Multifamily Housing Council website, at www.nmhc.org/research- insight/analysis-and-guidance/source-of-income-laws-by-state-county-and-city.

#### 7. Lead Safe Rental Housing

Ohio’s state lead abatement law does not require inspections of rental units until after a child has been poisoned by lead. R.C. Chapter 3742; Ohio Admin . Code Chapter 3701-32.

Two Ohio municipalities have enacted ordinances that requirethe owners of rental units that were built before 1978 (“pre-1978 rental units”) to certify that their rental units are lead safe. Those ordinances (and municipalities) are Cleveland Codified Ordinances pt. III.C, tit. IX, ch. 365, enacted in 2019, and Toledo Municipal Code pt.

17, tit. 3, ch. 1760, enacted in 2020.

Both of these ordinances set forth a presumption thatpre-1978 rental units contain lead paint.

Under the Cleveland ordinance, implementation of its inspection and certification process is phased in by zip code, with full implementation by March 1, 2023. It requires all pre-1978 rental units to pass an inspection by a certified lead clearance inspector and the owner of those rental unit to then apply for a Lead Safe Certificate.

Under the Toledo ordinance, implementation of its inspection and certification process is phased in over five years. It requires the owners of pre-1978 rental units in properties that have four or fewer units, and the owners of family child-care homes that were built before 1978, to submit a Lead Safe Report and then a pply for a Lead Safe
Certificate. The Lead Saf e Certificate expires in five years. See Mack v. City of Toledo, 151 N.E.3d 151, 2019 -Ohio-5427 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., 2019) (upholds the four or fewer unit scope of the ordinance).

## XXIII. Land Installment Contracts,

R.C. Chapter 5313

### A. Introduction


#### 1. Definition of Land Installment Contract

The definition of a land installment contract is set forth in R.C. 5313.01(A).Shrock
v. Spognardi, 46 N.E.3d 1115, 2015-Ohio-4555, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., 2015).

The definition is comprised of three elements: (1) an executory contract that is not required to be fully performed by one or more of the parties within one year; (2) the vendor’s agreement to convey title to real property and the vendee’s agreement to purchase the property and to pay the purchase price in installments; and (3) the vendor’s retention of the title as security for performance. See Am. Servicing Corp. v.

Wannemacher, 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014-Ohio-3984, ¶ 28 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014).

“[A] land installment contract conveys a present ownership interest in realty.” A & J Homes, Inc. v. Green, No. 101416, 2015 WL 1511020, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 8th Dist.,
Apr. 2, 2015). Accord Shrock v. Spognardi, 46 N.E.3d 1115, 2015-Ohio-4555, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., 2015).

“A land installment contract is an executory agreement whereby the purchaser
(vendee) agrees to pay the purchase price and is vested with equitable ownership, while the seller (vendor) retains bare legal title in the property to secure payment of the purchase price.” Baraby v. Swords, 166 Ohio App.3d 527, 851 N.E.2d 559, ¶ 14 (3rd
Dist. 2006). Accord Voska v. Coffman, No. S-13-008, 2013 WL 6579576, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 13, 2013); Judson v. Lyendecker , No. 12AP -615, 2013 WL 1187618, ¶ 8 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 21, 2013).

#### 2. Purpose and Scope of R.C. Chapter 5313

“R.C. Chapter 5313 is essentially a ‘consumer protection law’ designed to ‘prevent a windfall to a vendor who has previously collected substantial sums under a land contract and/or has actually recovered the property.’” Voska v. Coffman , No. S -13- 008, 2013 WL 6579576, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 13, 2013) (quoting Howard v.

Temple, 172 Ohio App.3d 21, 872 N.E.2d 1260, ¶ 9 (4th Dist. 2007)).

R.C. Chapter 5313 regulates land installment contracts for the purchase of residential dwellings only. R.C. 5313.01(B). See Shepard’s Mobile Home Park, Ltd.

631
v. Sigmon, No. 14-CA-61, 2014 WL 4923972 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Sept. 29, 2014);

P.M.D. Land Co. v. Warner Realty, Inc. , No. 2009-T-0021, 2009 WL 489554 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 18, 2009); Smith v. Jewett, No. 04-CA-96, 2005 WL 1840025 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 2, 2005); Johnson v. Maxwell, 51 Ohio App.3d 137, 554 N.E.2d 1370 (9th Dist. 1988); DiYorio v. Porter, No. 81-CA-5, 1981 WL 4734 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 24, 1981).

R.C. Chapter 5313 applies irrespective of whether the residential dwelli ng is a single-family dwelling or a multifamily dwelling. Akron First Seventh Day Adventist Church v. Smith, No. 11577, 1984 WL 3956 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 27, 1984).

R.C. Chapter 5313 applies to real property on which a house trailer or mobile home is parked at the time of contracting. Ohio Att’y Gen. Op. No. 73-042 (May 4, 1973).

But it does not apply to an agreement to purchase only a mobile home because the purchase fails to convey title to real property.Army v. Dunlap, 101 N.E.3d 1085, 2017- Ohio-9084, ¶ 29 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2017); Shepard’s Mobile Home Park, Ltd. v.

Sigmon, No. 14-CA-61, 2014 WL 4923972, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Sept. 29, 2014).

R.C. Chapter 5313 does not apply to real property on which a recreational vehicle is parked at the time of contracting because the vehicle is not a dwelling. Simes v.

Beaver Valley Resorts, Inc., No. 2925, 1992 WL 274656 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Oct.

6, 1992).

R.C. Chapter 5313 does not apply to commercial real property. Retirement Mgmt.
Co. v. Nsong, 94 N.E.3d 1077, 2017-Ohio-5869, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., 2017).

#### 3. Distinction Between a Land Installment Contract

and a Lease with an Option to Purchase “A lease is a conveyance of an estate in real property for a limited term, with conditions attached, in consideration of rent.” Judson v. Lyendecker, No. 12AP-615, 2013 WL 1187618, ¶ 9 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 21, 2013). Accord Shrock v.

Spognardi, 46 N.E.3d 1115, 2015 -Ohio-4555, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., 2015);

Fadelsak v. Hagley, No. 02-CA-41, 2003 WL 21489613, ¶ 11 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 25, 2003); Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Watkins, 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 23, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984) “An option contract for the purchase of real property is an agreement wherein the legal t itleholder of the property grants another person the privilege, without the obligation, to purchase the real property at a set price within a set time.” Judson v.

Lyendecker, No. 12AP-615, 2013 WL 1187618, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 21, 2013). Accord Shrock v. Spognardi , 46 N.E.3d 1115, 2015-Ohio-4555, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., 2015); A & J Homes, Inc. v. Green, No. 101416, 2015 WL 1511020, ¶ 14 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 2, 2015).

A lease with an option to purchase is not a land installment con tract. R.C.

5313.01(A). See Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Watkins , 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 23, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984) (“the statutory definition of land installment contract expressly excludes option contracts for the purchase of real property”).

“To determine whether an agreement is a land installment contract or a lease with an option to purchase, a court must analyze the intent of the parties at the time they executed the agreement.” Judson v. Lyendecker, No. 12AP-615, 2013 WL 1187618, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 21, 2013). Accord Shrock v. Spognardi, 46 N.E.3d 1115, 2015 -Ohio-4555, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., 2015); Am. Servicing Corp. v.

Wannemacher, 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014 -Ohio-3984, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014);

Vorhees v. Jovingo, Nos. 04-CA-16,17,18, 2005 WL 2292796, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 19, 2005); Fadelsak v. Hagley, No. 02-CA-41, 2003 WL 21489613, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 25, 2003).

When the language of a written contract is unambiguous, a court is requi red to determine the intent of the parties from the plain language of the contract. Judson v.

Lyendecker, No. 12AP-615, 2013 WL 1187618, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 21, 2013). See also Am. Servicing Corp. v. Wannemacher , 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014 -Ohio- 3984, ¶¶ 25,26,31 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014). When the language of a written contract is ambiguous, “a court may consider extrinsic evidence to ascertain the parties’ intent.” Judson v. Lyendecker, No. 12AP-615, 2013 WL 1187618, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 21, 2013).

If the plain language of the contract establishes that the three elements of the R.C.

5313.01(A) definition are met, the contract is a land installment contract. Am.

Servicing Corp. v. Wannemacher , 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014 -Ohio-3984, ¶¶ 28 -30 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014). If the plain language of the contract establishes that one of these elements is not met, the contract is not a land installment contract. Lovejoy v.

Diel, No. 2020CA-06-067, 2021 WL 1246295, ¶¶ 23-26 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Apr.

5, 2021) (contract did not contain any of the three elements of the R.C. 5313.01(A) definition); Judson v. Lyendecker, No. 12AP-615, 2013 WL 1187618, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Mar. 21, 2013) (contract lacked requirement to purchase).

In this de termination, the title of the contract is not dispositive. Am. Servicing
Corp. v. Wannemacher, 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014-Ohio-3984, ¶ 27 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 633 2014). “The fact that an agreement is titled ‘Option to Purchase,’ however, does not necessarily make it so.” Anderson v. Ballard, No. L-10-1007, 2010 WL 3292685, ¶42 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Aug. 20, 2010).

The determination is based primarily on the language employed in the body of the contract, not its title. See, e.g. Am. Servicing Corp. v. Wannemacher, 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014-Ohio-3984, ¶¶ 27,28 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014) (contract titled “Lease Agreement with Option to Purchase;” based on body of contract, it is land installment contract); Fadelsak v. Hagley, No. 02-CA-41, 2003 WL 21489613, ¶ 12 (Ohi o App., 4th Dist., June 25, 2003) (same).

A split among the appellate districts exists on when extrinsic evidence of the intent of the parties may be considered in these determinations. “[S]ome courts apply a six- factor test supplied by the Fourth Distric t to ascertain the intent of the parties” in all
cases. Am. Servicing Corp. v. Wannemacher , 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014 -Ohio-3984, ¶ 25 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014).

Those courts hold that, “[i]n determining intent, the court should consider factors such as: (1) the characterization of the document; (2) the lessee’s rights at the end of the lease term; (3) the application of rent to the purchase price; (4) the responsibility for payments for repairs, utilities, and taxes; (5) the nonexistence of a financing statement; and (6) whether an option to purchase existed. If necessary, the court may also consider the factual circumstances surrounding the parties’ agreement.”Fadelsak
v. Hagley, No. 02 -CA-41, 2003 WL 21489613, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 25,
2003) (citations omitted). Accord; Vorhees v. Jovingo , Nos. 04 -CA-16,17,18, 2005 WL 2292796, ¶ 24 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 19, 2005).

The Third District, inAm. Servicing Corp. v. Wannemacher, 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014- Ohio-3984, ¶¶ 25,31 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014), expressly rejected the Fourth District’s approach. With its statement of contract law and analysis based solely on the unambiguous language of the contract, the Tenth District, inJudson v. Lyendecker,
No. 12AP -615, 2013 WL 1187618, ¶ 9 (Ohio App. , 10th Dist., Mar. 21, 2013), implicitly rejected the Fourth District’s approach. Likewise, the Eighth District, in A & J Homes, Inc. v. Green , No. 101416, 2015 WL 1511020, ¶¶ 10,15,16 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Apr. 2, 2015) (follows Judson), implicitly reje cted the Fourth District’s approach.

### B. Contract Requirements and Related

Matters

#### 1. Statutory Requirements

R.C. 5313.02(A) specifies sixteen provisions that must be included in a land installment contract. They are:
(1) The full names and current mailing address es of all parties to the contract;
(2) The date when the contract was signed by each party;
(3) A legal description of the property conveyed;
(4) The contract price of the property conveyed;
(5) Any charges or fees for services that are includable in the contract separate from the contract price;
(6) The amount of the vendee’s down payment;
(7) The principal balance owed, i.e., sum of items (4) and (5) less item (6);
(8) The amount and due date of each installment payment;
(9) The interest rate on the unpaid balance and the method of computing the rate;
(10) A statement of any encumbrances against the property;
(11) A statement requiring the vendor to deliver a general warranty deed on completion of the contract, or another deed that is available when the vendor is legally unable to deliver a general warranty deed;
(12) A provision that the vendor shall provide evidence of title in accordance with the prevailing custom in the area in which the property is located;
(13) A provision that, if the vendor defaults on any mortgage on the property, the vendee can pay on that mortgage and receive credit on the land installment contract;
(14) A provision that the vendor shall cause a copy of the contract to be recorded;
(15) A requirement that the vendee shall be responsible for the payment of taxes, assessments, and other charges against the property from the date of the contract, unless agreed to the contrary;

635
(16) A statement of any pending order of any public agency against the property.

#### 2. Substantial Compliance

A contract for the sale of a residential dwelling may be a land installment contract even if it fails to include all of the provisions required by R.C. 5313.02(A). Elkins v.

Colburn, No. 18-CA-893, 2019 WL 2754096 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 21, 2019);

Phillips v. May, No. 2003-G-2520, 2004 WL 2526409 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Nov. 9, 2004); Gollihue v. Russo, 152 Ohio App.3d 710, 789 N.E.2d 1151 (10th Dist. 2003);

Real Flo Props. v. Kelly , No. L -99-1099, 1999 WL 1203751 (Ohio App., 6th Dist.,
Dec. 17, 199 9); Shimko v. Marks , 91 Ohio App.3d 458, 632 N.E.2d 990 (5th Dist.

1993); Young v. Hodapp, No. CA85-08-094, 1986 WL 14965 (Ohio App., 12th Dist.,
Dec. 29, 1986). But see Lovejoy v. Diel , No. 2020CA -06-067, 2021 WL 1246295 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Apr. 5, 2 021) (an agreement does not constitute a land installment contract if it omits an R.C. 5313.02(A) provision); Standard Fed. v.

Asencio, No. 98 -CA-007011, 1999 WL 61425 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Feb. 9, 1999) (omission of an R.C. 5313.02(A) element renders thecontract voidable by the parties).

“The purpose of R.C. Chapter 5313.02 is to protect the buyer by making specific information available in a land installment contract.” Real Flo Props. v. Kelly, No. L- 99-1099, 1999 WL 1203751, at *2 (Ohio App., 6th Dist ., Dec. 17, 1999). Accord Elkins v. Colburn, No. 18-CA-893, 2019 WL 2754096 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 21, 2019); Phillips v. May, No. 2003-G-2520, 2004 WL 2526409 (Ohio App., 11th Dist.,
Nov. 9, 2004); Young v. Hodapp , No. CA -85-08-094, 1986 WL 14965 (O hio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 29, 1986); Akron First Seventh Day Adventist Church v. Smith , No.

11577, 1984 WL 3956 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 27, 1984).

“The statute was not designed as a way for sellers to escape from contracts they wished to void.” Young v. Hodapp, No. CA85-08-094, 1986 WL 14965, at *3 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 29, 1986). Accord Elkins v. Colburn, No. 18-CA-893, 2019 WL 2754096 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 21, 2019); Phillips v. May , No. 2003 -G-2520, 2004 WL 2526409 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Nov. 9, 2004); Real Flo Props. v. Kelly ,
No. L-99-1099, 1999 WL 1203751 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 17, 1999).

Therefore, a contract may be a land installment contract if it substantially complies with the R.C. 5313.02(A) requirements. Elkins v. Colburn, No. 18-CA-893, 2019 WL 2754096 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., June 21, 2019); Phillips v. May , No. 2003 -G-2520, 2004 WL 2526409 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Nov. 9, 2004);Gollihue v. Russo, 152 Ohio App.3d 710, 789 N.E.2d 1151 (10th Dist. 2003); Real Flo Props. v. Kelly, No. L-99- 1099, 1999 WL 1203751 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 17, 1999); Whitacre v. Starks ,
No. 1998-CA-0307, 1999 WL 547943 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 29, 1999); Shimko
v. Marks, 91 Ohio App.3d 458, 632 N.E.2d 990 (5th Dist. 1993).

“[T]he proper inquiry for determining whether the land installment contract [is] valid is to determine if all the essential elements of a contract [are] present.” Young v.

Hodapp, No. CA85-08-094, 1986 WL 14965, at *3 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., Dec. 29, 1986). See, e.g., Hudson v. Bates, No. 93-CVG-7406 (Ohio Mun., Marion Cty., Jan.

26, 1993) (“the ‘Land Contract’ does contain sufficient elements to fit within the [R.C.

5313.01] definition of a land installment contract”).

“Ohio law does not require a metes and bounds legal description of property in order to create a binding agreement to sell that property.” Phillips v. May, No. 2003- G-2520, 2004 WL 2526409, ¶ 36 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Nov. 9, 2004). But the contract must contain a description of the particular land to be conveyed or must furnish the means of identifying it with reasonable certainty and specificity. Id. See also Nofzinger v. Blood, No. H-02-014, 2003 WL 1464519 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Mar.

14, 2003).

Also, if a contract provision declares that the c ontract is a statement of intent to enter into a formal land installment contract in the future, the declaration can be a factor for finding that the contract is not a land installment contract, even though the contract contains some of the R.C. 5313.02 el ements. E.g., Grexa v. Hollenbaugh, No. 2014- T-0039, 2014 WL 5421222 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 27, 2014).

#### 3. Recording of the Contract

R.C. 5313.02(C) requires the vendor to record the land installment contract, as provided in R.C. 5301.25, within twe nty days after the contract has been signed by both the vendor and the vendee. “[T]he purpose of R.C. 5313.02(C) is to protect subsequent bona fide purchasers of property.” Bradford v. B & P Wrecking Co., 171 Ohio App.3d 616, 872 N.E.2d 331, ¶ 32 (6th Dist. 2007). Accord McGinnis v. Fuller,
No. 3-04-29, 2005 WL 1205117, ¶ 20 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., May 23, 2005).

Therefore, a vendor’s failure to record the contract does not render it unenforceable as between the vendee and the vendor. Bradford v. B & P Wrecking Co., 171 Ohio App.3d 616, 872 N.E.2d 331, ¶ 32 (6th Dist. 2007); Burkett v. Strickland, No. 02-CV- 7400, (Ohio C.P., Montgomery Cty., Sept. 29, 2003). See also Merivale Invs. v.

Tuggle, No. L-08-1439, 2009 WL 4727760, ¶¶ 23 -31 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 11, 637 2009).

The failure to record a land installment contract also does not prevent a vendee from enforcing the vendee’s equitable interest against the vendor’s judgment creditors.

McGinnis v. Fuller, No. 3-04-29, 2005 WL 1205117 (Ohio App., 3r d Dist., May 23, 2005); State Fid. Fed. Sav. & Loan Ass’n v. Wehrby, 25 Ohio Misc. 221, 263 N.E.2d 801 (C.P., Montgomery Cty., 1970). “The law is clear that judgment creditors are not bona fide purchasers for value.” McGinnis, 2005 WL 1205117, ¶ 20.

#### 4. Oral Agreement, Partial Performance

“[P]art performance of an oral land installment contract for the sale of real estate can be sufficient to remove the contract from the operation of the statute [of frauds, R.C. 1335.05].” Shimko v. Marks , 91 Ohio App.3d 458, 461, 632 N.E.2d 990 (5th
Dist. 1993). E.g., Gay v. Parr , No. CVG -00-141 (Ohio County Ct., Harrison Cty.,
Dec. 21, 2000) (substantial repairs to the premises and installment payments on the down payment). See also Monea v. Lanci , No. 2009 -CA-0083, 2009 WL 4690214 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 7, 2009) (summary judgment reversed due to genuine issue of material facts; occupant alleges payment of two -thirds of purchase price and substantial improvements to the property).

“Ohio courts generally consider the following factors to be relevant in determining the applicability of the part performance doctrine: (1) evidence of a change in possession; (2) payment of all or part of the consideration for the land; and (3) improvements, alterations or repairs upon the land by the possessor.” Monea v. Lanci,
No. 2009-CA-0083, 2009 WL 4690214, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 7, 2009).

For a discussion on the statute of conveyances, the statute of frauds, and the partial performance exception, see Section XXII.A, above.

#### 5. Enforcement of R.C. 5313.02(A) Requirements

If a vendor fails to comply with an R.C. 5313.02(A) requirement, the vendee may file an action to enforce it. R.C. 5313.04. Upon finding such noncompliance, “the court shall grant appropriate relief.” Id. E.g., Labbe v. Smith, No. 11078, 1983 WL 3899 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Nov. 2, 1983) (in eviction for nonpayment, court enforced vendee’s R.C. 5313.02(A)(13) rights by granting vendee credit for his payments to vendor’s mortgagee).

However, “[t]here is no provision in the statute [R.C. Chapter 5313] for a remedy in damages for noncompliance.” 393 Akron First Seventh Day Adventist Church v.

Smith, No. 11577, 1984 WL 3956, at *2 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 27, 1984).

#### 6. Prepayment of Contract

A vendee does not hav e a right to prepay the outstanding principal and accrued interest on a land installment contract, unless the contract so provides. DiMarco v.

Shay, 154 Ohio App.3d 141, 796 N.E.2d 572 (10th Dist. 2003).E.g., Lowry v. Buroker,
No. 2017 -CA-25, 2017 WL 66 28496 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 29, 2017) (land installment contract expressly provided for prepayment, and based on that provision the court granted the vendee specific performance).

### C. Vendee Rights Against Third-Party

Purchaser When a vendor sells th e contracted real property to a third party, the third party purchases the property subject to the rights of the vendee. Oberholtz v. Oberholtz, 79 Ohio App. 540, 74 N.E.2d 574 (9th Dist. 1947); State Fid. Fed. Sav. & Loan Ass’n v.

Wehrby, 25 Ohio Misc. 221, 263 N.E.2d 801 (C.P., Montgomery Cty., 1970).

The general rule in Ohio is that “the purchaser of land in the actual possession of a third person is chargeable with constructive notice of the occupant’s title and equities even though the fact of such possession is not known to the purchaser.” Sinclair Ref.
Co. v. Chaney, 114 Ohio App. 538, 549, 184 N.E.2d 214 (3rd Dist. 1961). See also State Fid. Fed. Sav. & Loan Ass’n v. Wehrby , 25 Ohio Misc. 221, 263 N.E.2d 801 (C.P., Montgomery Cty., 1970).

But one appellate court has held that a vendee in possession under an oral (and thus unrecorded) land contract does not have any enforceable rights “as against a subsequent purchaser who buys the property from the vendor with knowledge of the vendee’s possession but without knowledge of the oral land contract.” Bishop v. Rice,
No. 21247, 2006 WL 574396, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 10, 2006).

393 In a fraud action arising out of an alleged noncompliance with R.C. 5313.02(A), proof of that noncompliance eliminates the need to prove reliance and material ity. Akron First Seventh Day Adventist Church v. Smith , No. 11577, 1984 WL 3956 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Sept. 27, 1984). However, intent to defraud and damages resulting from the misrepresentation still must be proven. Id.

639

### D. Vendor Breach of the Land Installment

Contract If a vendor defaults upon the land installment contract ( e.g., fails to convey title upon vendee’s complete performance), the vendee may: (1) affirm the contract and bring an action for specific performance, (2) affirm the contract and bring an action for damages, or (3) disaffirm the contract and recover the paymen ts that the vendee has
made. Tyler v. Martin, 11 Ohio Law Abs. 660 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., 1932).

If the vendee affirms the contract and brings an action for damages, “the measure of damages is the difference between the value of the real estate at the t ime when conveyance should have been made and the balance due upon the contract of sale ....”
Id. at 661.

When a vendor’s breach of the land installment contract results in a foreclosure sale, the vendee may have an additional claim for unjust enrichment. In these circumstances, the vendee’s improvements to the real property “that added value to the property are compensable under the doctrine of unjust enrichment.” Zumbrink v.

Hercules, No. 1392, 1996 WL 339947, at *8 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., June 21, 1996).

### E. Vendee Breach of the Land Installment

Contract

#### 1. Introduction

If a vendee breaches the land installment contract, generally the vendor may bring an action for possession (see Section XXIII.E.2, below) and certain damages (see Section XXIII.E.3, below) or the vendor may bring an action for specific performance (see Section XXIII.E.4, below).

#### 2. Vendor Action for Possession


##### a. Standard for Selection Between Eviction and

Foreclosure R.C. 5313.07 and 5313.08 govern the action for possession that a vendor may bring if a vendee breaches the contract. While R.C. 5313.07 and 5321.08 limit the remedies available to the trial court in an action for breach of a land installment contract, they are not jurisdictional statutes. Davis v. Johnson , No. L -19-1268, 2021 WL 1415 04, ¶ 42 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Jan. 15, 2021) (“Initially, we note that R.C. 5313.07 is not a jurisdictional statute, but rather is a limit on the remedies available to the trial court”).

R.C. 5313.07 and 5313.08 divide land installment contracts into t wo groups relative to a vendor’s action for possession.

The first group is comprised of land installment contracts that have been in effect for less than five years and upon which less than twenty percent of the purchase price has been paid.

The second group is comprised of land installment contracts that have been in effect for five or more years or upon which twenty percent or more of the purchase price has been paid.

The determination on whether twenty percent of the purchase price has been paid is b ased on the face amount of the contract price, which excludes payments for insurance premiums, real estate taxes, or interest on unpaid principal, unless the contract stipulates otherwise. Stiffler v. F.I.O.P. Assocs., LLC, No. 28501, 2020 WL 1082366 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 6, 2020); Lindhorst v. Elkadi, No. 80162, 2002 WL 999315 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., May 16, 2002); Whitacre v. Starks, No. 1998-CA- 0307, 1999 WL 547943 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 29, 1999); Ramsey v. Farris, No.

64179, 1993 WL 497059 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 2, 1993); Smith v. Blackburn, 31 Ohio App.3d 251, 511 N.E.2d 132 (4th Dist. 1987); Burdge v. Welsh, No. 82AP-846, 1983 WL 3367 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Feb. 22, 1983).

When a vendor loans money to the vendee subsequentto the execution of the land installment contract, and the parties agree to add the loan amount to the principal of the land installment contract, the loan amount is nonetheless excluded from the purchase price for the determination on whether twenty perc ent of the purchase price has been paid. Waters v. Williams, No. 06-CA-88, 2007 WL 172032 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Jan. 22, 2007) (five years after execution of the contract, $20,000 loan).

When a land installment contract is renegotiated and the final contract declares that the earlier contract(s) are null and void, the five -year period for R.C. 5321.07,.08 purposes begins to run as of the execution date of the final contract.Smith v. Williams,
No. 2012-CA-00117, 2013 WL 1282002 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Ma r. 28, 2013). Cf.

Mohn v. Ousley, No. 2015-CA-00073, 2015 WL 9393954 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec.

21, 2015) (termination agreement for first contract waived application of its time and payments in computation of time and payments under second contract).

With respect to land installment contracts in the second group, the vendor may recover possession of the property only through a foreclosure action and a judicial sale.

641 R.C. 5313.07,.08. See also Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. v. Watkins, 23 Ohio App.3d 20, 491 N.E.2d 701 (8th Dist. 1984);Hudson v. Bates, No. 93-CVG-7406 (Ohio Mun., Marion Cty., Jan. 26, 1993).

With respect to land installment contracts in the first group, the vendor may recover possession of the property either through an eviction action or through a foreclosure action and a judicial sale. R.C. 5313.07,.08. See also Casto v. Prokop ,
No. 93-T-4853, 1995 WL 407217 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 9, 1995).

Two appellate districts are split on whether, in the eviction action, the burden of proof, relative to the twenty -percent standard, is on the vendor or the vendee. The Second District has held that this burden of proof is on the vendor.394 White v. Daniel,
No. 12319, 1991 WL 262675 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 10, 1991). The Fifth District has held that the twenty-percent standard is an affirmative defense and, therefore, the burden of proof is on the vendee. Sanders v. Crawford , No. 2006 -CA-00194, 2007 WL 1413218 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 14, 2007).

If the burden of proof is on the vendor , “[w]hen [the vendor] cannot state the balance due on the contract, he cannot meet his burden of proving that less than twenty per cent has been paid on the contract.” White, 1991 WL 262675, at *4.

##### b. Eviction Action – Notices, Right to Cure, and Equity

When the vendee’s default is nonpayment, a forfeiture of the vendee’s interest under the land installment contract “may be enforced only after the expiration of thirty days from the date of the default.” R.C. 5313.05. If the land installment contract provides a thirty-day grace period and does not specify when the R.C. 5313.05 thirty- day notice commences relative to the grace period, the R.C. 5313.05 thirty-day notice does not commence until the expiration of the thirty-day grace period. Garee v. Wolfe,
Nos. 2020-CA-00009,00038, 2021-WL 628622 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Feb. 16, 2021).

If the vendee cures the default ( i.e., makes all payments currently due under the
contract) within this thirty -day period, the vendor may not evict the vendee. R.C.

5313.05. See Garee v. Wolfe, Nos. 2020-CA-00009,00038, 2021-WL 628622 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Feb. 16, 2021);Addair v. Mitchell, No. 03-CA-19, 2003 WL 22951899 394 Placing the burden of proof o n the vendor is consistent with the vendor’s R.C. 5313.03
obligation. R.C. 5313.03 requires a vendor, at least once a year, to furnish a statement to the vendee showing the amount credited to principal and interest and the balance due. It thus requires the vendor to keep accurate records of the elements of the twenty -percent standard.

(Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 15, 2003); Keene v. Schnetz , 13 Ohio App.3d 87, 468 N.E.2d 125 (9th Dist. 1983).

Before filing any eviction action against the vendee, the vendor must serve the vendee with a written notice that:
(1) Reasonably identifies the contract and describes the property covered by it;
(2) Specifies the terms and conditions of the contract with which the vendee has not complied;
(3) Notifies the vendee that the contract will stand forfeited unless the vendee performs the terms and conditions of the contract within ten days of the completed service of notice; and
(4) Notifies the vendee to leave the premises.

R.C. 5313.06.395 In these eviction actions, the R.C. 5313.06 notice supplants the R.C. 1923.04(A) notice (see Section I.C.1, above). R.C. 1923.04(B).

The permissible methods of service for an R.C. 5313.06 notice are: (1) certified mail, (2) registered mail, (3) personal service, (4) leaving it at the vendee’s usual place of abode, or (5) leaving it at the property that is the subject of the contract. R.C.

5313.06. E.g., Estate of Chasteen v. Cartee, No. 1993, 1992 WL 238918 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 25, 1992) (upheld service by registered mail).

For R.C. 5313.06 purposes, a vendee “performs the terms and conditions of the contract” so as to avoid forfeiture only if the vendee makes full payment of the entire contract price before the end of the ten-day period. Addair v. Mitchell, No. 03-CA-19, 2003 WL 22951899 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 15, 2003); Keene v. Schnetz, 13 Ohio App.3d 87, 468 N.E.2d 125 (9th Dist. 1983). A vendee’s oral offer to make full payment does not meet this requirement, if the vendee fails to tender the payment .

Ramsey v. Farris, No. 64179, 1993 WL 497059 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 2, 1993).

A vendor’s failure to comply with the R.C. 5313.06 notice requirement is a defense to the eviction action. Examples of court decisions finding the content of an R.C.

5313.06 notice defective, and thereupon entering an eviction judgment for the vendee, 395 The R.C. 5313.06 notice does not apply when the vendor files a foreclosure action instead of an eviction action. Chemical Bank v. Sullivan, 121 Ohio App.3d 111, 699 N.E.2 d 105 (6th
Dist. 1997).

643 are set forth below.
(1) Am. Servicing Corp. v. Wannemacher, 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014-Ohio-3984 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014) (notice failed to notify the vendee of the ten-day period for performing the terms of the contract to avoid forfeiture).
(2) Austin v. Sullivan , No. 93 -CA-43, 1994 WL 650228 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Nov. 17, 1994) (notice failed to identify the property and failed to notify the vendee of the ten -day period for performing the terms of the contract to avoid forfeiture).
(3) Northrop v. Mangus , No. CA -81-07, 1981 WL 6607 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Dec. 28, 1981) (notice failed to describe the property and failed to notify the vendee of the ten -day period for performing the terms of the contract to avoid forfeiture).
(4) Krivins v. Smyers, No. 9935, 1981 WL 3945 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., Apr.

22, 1981) (notice misstated the amount due and unpaid).

One appellate district has held that “R.C. 5313.06, like R.C. 1923.04, is jurisdictional in nature and a prerequisite to land contract forfeiture proceedings.” Austin v. Sullivan, No. 93-CA-43, 1994 WL 650228, at *2 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Nov.

17, 1994). It thereupon held that a vendor’s “failure to comply with the provisions of R.C. 531 3.06, as it sets forth notice requirements, deprive[s] the trial court of jurisdiction over the subject matter of the land contract.” Id.

Two other appellate districts have stated that the jurisdictional issue is not settled, since only the Seventh District has ruled on it. Am. Servicing Corp. v. Wannemacher, 19 N.E.3d 566, 2014 -Ohio-3984, ¶ 44 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., 2014); State ex rel.

Invesco Mgmt. Co. LLC v. Geauga County Court of Common Pleas, No. 2012-G-3085, 2012 WL 4788965, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Oct. 9, 2012). But neither court ruled on the issue. In Wannemacher, the court did not need to (and opted not to) decide the jurisdictional issue “because the trial court ultimately dismissed the complaint, which was the proper disposition of the c ase in light of the trial court’s conclusion that Amerian Servicing failed to satisfy R.C. 5313.06.”Wannemacher, 2014-Ohio-3984, ¶
44. In Invesco, the court did not decide the jurisdictional issue because the trial court had not yet determined (and woulddetermine on remand) whether the notice complied with R.C. 5313.06. Invesco, 2012 WL 4788965, ¶ 19 In three cases, where the R.C. 5313.06 notice instructed the vendee to vacate before the required ten days and the vendor commenced the eviction action after the required ten days, the trial courts refused to dismiss the eviction actions and the appellate courts found error but not prejudicial error. Goble v. Grosswiler, No. 2018-CA-0102, 2019 WL 5595795 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Oct. 29, 2019) (notice s pecified three days and eviction complaint filed 48 days after service of the notice); Estate of Chasteen v.

Cartee, No. 1993, 1992 WL 238918 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 25, 1992) (notice specified seven days and eviction complaint filed four months after service of the notice); Caltrider v. Reitler, No. CA-2208, 1984 WL 7506 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., May 21, 1984) (notice specified three days and eviction complaint filed thirteen days after service of the notice).

When a vendee cannot avoid forfeiture via th e rights to cure under the R.C.

5313.05 and 5313.06, the vendee still has a right to assert an equity defense. Because “forfeitures are highly disfavored by the law ..., the forfeiture clause of a land contract will be disregarded upon the vendee’s failure to perform on time, unless the failure is intentional or it results in a loss to the vendor which cannot be compensated by interest.” Krivins v. Smyers, No. 9935, 1981 WL 3945, at *2 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,
Apr. 22, 1981). “Accordingly, courts have decl ined to enforce forfeitures of land contracts when the buyer later made an adequate tender of the amounts due.” Id. See also Blenheim Homes, Inc. v. Mathews, 119 Ohio App. 44, 196 N.E.2d 612 (10th Dist.

1963).

##### c. Vendee Claim for Payments in Excess of Fair Rental

Value When a vendor declares the land installment contract void because of the vendee’s nonpayment, the vendee is generally entitled to recover any sums paid on the purchase price in excess of the fair rental value of the property, during the period of the vendee’s
possession. Brown v. Johnston, 95 Ohio App. 136, 108 N.E.2d 298 (2nd Dist. 1952).

But cf. Doty v. Potteiger, No. 2017-T-0029, 2017 WL 5998569 (Ohio App., 11th Dist.,
Dec. 4, 2017) (vendorin a land installment contract which had beenforfeited is entitled to keep the initial deposit that is in excess of the statutory damages allowed by R.C.

5313.10). For a discussion on fair rental value, see Section XXIII.E.3.b, below.

#### 3. Vendor Action for Damages


##### a. Overview

The termination of a land installment contract under R.C. 5313.07 or 5313.08 is an exclusive remedy, which generally bars further action on the contract. R.C. 5313.10.

645 “Following foreclosure and judicial sale, R.C. 5313.07 allows a vendor to recover the proceeds from the judicial sale ‘up to and including the unpaid balance due on the land installment contract.’” Casto v. Prokop, No. 93-T-4853, 1995 WL 407217, at *2 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 9, 1995). Accord Castro v. Prokop, No. 89-T-4238, 1991 WL 35640 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Ma r. 15, 1991); Dalton v. Acker , 5 Ohio App.3d 150, 450 N.E.2d 288 (5th Dist. 1981).

The vendor may not recover a deficiency judgment against the vendee. 396 R.C.

5313.10. See Howard v. Temple , 172 Ohio App.3d 21, 872 N.E.2d 1260 (4th Dist.

2007); Castro v. Prokop, No. 89-T-4238, 1991 WL 35640 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar.

15, 1991); Good Shepherd Baptist Church v. City of Columbus, 20 Ohio App.3d 228, 485 N.E.2d 725 (10th Dist. 1984); Shone v. Griffis, No. 8252, 1984 WL 4376 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 198 4); Dalton v. Acker, 5 Ohio App.3d 150, 450 N.E.2d 288 (5th Dist. 1981).

But the vendor may recover damages: (1) for the difference, if any, between the amount paid by the vendee on the contract and the fair rental value of the property, and
(2) for any deterioration or destruction of the property caused by the vendee. R.C.

5313.10. See Anna Holdings, LLC v. McClanahan, No. 148 N.E.3d 1255, 2019-Ohio- 4697 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 2019); Voska v. Coffman , No. S -13-008, 2013 WL 6579576 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 13, 2013); Howard v. Temple, 172 Ohio App.3d 21, 872 N.E.2d 1260 (4th Dist. 2007); Casto v. Prokop , No. 93 -T-4853, 1995 WL 407217 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 9, 1995); White v. Daniel, No. 12319, 1991 WL 262675 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Dec. 10,1991); Castro v. Prokop, No. 89-T-4238, 1991 WL 35640 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 15, 1991); Frey v. Hibbard, 62 Ohio App.3d 781, 577 N.E.2d 669 (1st Dist. 1989); Tanner v. Fulk, No. CA-2297, 1985 WL 6462 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., Aug. 1, 1985); Good Shepher d Baptist Church v. City of Columbus, 20 Ohio App.3d 228, 485 N.E.2d 725 (10th Dist. 1984); Butler v. Michel, 14 Ohio App.3d 116, 470 N.E.2d 217 (9th Dist. 1984).

For these purposes, the amount paid by the vendee includes the amount of the vendee’s initial deposit (if any). Doty v. Potteiger , No. 2017 -T-0029, 2017 WL 5998569 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 4, 2017).

396 If a court does order a deficiency judgment, it is voidab initio and subject to collateral attack.

Shone v. Griffis, No. 8252, 1984 WL 4376, at *4 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Feb. 2, 1984).

##### b. Fair Rental Value Claim

The recovery for fair rental value plus deterioration or destruction is an exception to the otherwise exclusive remedy of filing an action under R.C. 5313.07 or R.C.

5313.08. Kennedy v. Kunze, No. 28978, 2019 WL 469767, ¶ 21 (Ohio App., 9th Dist.,
Feb. 6, 2019).

“[A] ‘fair renta l value claim’ is intended to compensate the vendor for any additional amount he could have earned during the period in which he was receiving payments under the contract .” Belknap v. Shears , No. 2012 -P-0007, 2012 WL 5995095, ¶ 29 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 3, 2012).

“Accordingly, it follows that the vendor must establish the fair rental value of the property during the period in which the payments were made. Generally, evidence only on the fair rental value of the property for a subsequent period will not meet this standard.” Id.

“The fair rental value is the amount a willing landlord rents the property for and what a reasonable tenant is willing to pay.” Voska v. Coffman , No. S -13-008, 2013 WL 6579576, ¶ 13 (Ohio App., 6th Dist., Dec. 13, 2013). Accord Taylor v. Nickston Invs., No. 94 -APE02-259, 1995 WL 41638, at *2 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Jan. 26, 1995).

“The installment amount set forth in a land installment contract is evidence of its fair rental value.” Id. “Fair rental value may also includeunpaid taxes, insurance, and water charges.” Id.

##### c. Practice Issues

When the vendor asserts these damage claims in a foreclosure action, the court should conduct an evidentiary hearing on the claim after the judicial sale. Flora v.

Pullins, No. 96-CA-13, 1996 WL 648353 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Nov. 8, 1996).

When the vendor brings an eviction action, the vendor may assert the damage claim in the eviction action or in a separate and subsequent action. Marvin v. Stemen, 68 Ohio App.2d 26, 426 N.E.2d 205 (6th Dist. 1980). Res judicata is not a defense to the subsequent action. Id.

##### d. Bar on a Deficiency Judgment – Applicability Issues

When a vendor serves the vendee with an R.C. 5313.06 notice and, thereupon, the vendee vacates the property, the R.C. 5313.10 bar against a deficiency judgment applies, even though the vendor did not file an action for forfeiture. Koehler v.

647 Panizgua, No. 5-02-64, 2003 WL 1903737 (Ohio App., 3rd Dist., Apr. 18, 2003).

When a vendor has a note and mortgage with the vendee, in a ddition to the land installment contract, and the vendor commences a foreclosure action under the note and mortgage (and not under R.C. 5313.07), the R.C. 5313.10 bar against a deficiency judgment does not apply. Bradford v. B & P Wrecking Co., 171 Ohio App.3d 616, 872 N.E.2d 331 (6th Dist. 2007).

When a vendor is seeking to collect payment under a guarantee agreement, the R.C. 5313.10 bar against a deficiency judgment does not apply to the guarantor.

O’Brien v. Ravenswood Apts., Ltd., 169 Ohio App.3d 233, 862 N.E.2d 549 (1st Dist.

2006).

#### 4. Vendor Action for Specific Performance for the

Purchase Price When a vendee breaches a land installment contract, the vendor may elect “to exercise the option of accelerating the payments under the Land Contract and bringing a suit against [the vendee] to compel the performance of the contract.” Arnett v.

Bardonaro, No. 25371, 2013 WL 1189009, ¶ 37 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Mar. 22, 2013).

In the action for specific performance, the vendor is electing to receive the purcha se price in exchange for the property. Id.

The action for specific performance is an alternative option to the vendor’s right to bring an action for possession under R.C. 5313.07,.08. Id., ¶ 35.

## XXIV. HUD-Assisted Rental Housing

Programs

### A. Introduction

Section XXIV.B, below, is an index of HUD regulations that govern the primary HUD-assisted rental housing programs. The index identifies, per program, the HUD regulations on: (1) overall program rul es, (2) selection of tenants, (3) leases, (4) rent formula, (5) interim rent reduction, and (6) termination of tenancy and eviction.

Section XXIV.C, below, discusses R.C. Chapter 2506 appeals of a public housing authority’s decision to terminate a famil y’s participation in the Housing Choice Voucher program (“HCVP”).

For a discussion on eviction actions, relative to the HUD -assisted rental housing programs, see Chapter III, above. For a detailed outline of the HUD regulations on terminating a tenancy for the criminal activity of a tenant, household member, or guest, see Section III.G.8, above.

For a comprehensive discussion on the HUD-assisted rental housing programs, see National Housing Law Project, HUD Housing Programs: Tenants’ Rights ( 5th ed.

2018).
