653

### C. Appeal from PHA Decision Terminating

HCVP Participation, R.C. Chapter 2506

#### 1. Jurisdiction

R.C. 2506.01 authorizes an appeal to the court of common pleas from “every final order, adjudication, or decision of any officer, tribunal, authority, board, bureau, commission, department, or other division of any political subdivision of the state.” “In order for a decision of an administrative agency to be appealable pursuant to R.C. Chapter 2506, it m ust be a final resolution rendered in a quasi -judicial proceeding.”404 Lakota Local Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ. v. Brickner , 108 Ohio App.3d 637, 644, 671 N.E.2d 578 (6th Dist. 1996). Accord Milhoan v. E. Local Sch. Dist. Bd.

of Educ. , 157 Ohio App.3d 716, 81 3 N.E.2d 692, ¶ 13 (4th Dist. 2004); Sebest v.

Campbell City Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ., No. 00-CA-272, 2002 WL 1483917, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 28, 2002); State ex rel. Barno v. Crestwood Bd. of Educ. , 134 Ohio App.3d 494, 500, 731 N.E.2d 701 (11th Dist. 1998).

However, “[w]here the administrative agency should have given notice, conducted a hearing, and afforded the parties an opportunity to be heard and to introduce evidence, the order is the result of an adjudication even if the agency fails to a fford such notice and hearing.” In re Howard, 73 Ohio App.3d 717, 719, 598 N.E.2d 165 (10th Dist. 1991). Accord Milhoan v. E. Local Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ. , 157 Ohio App.3d 716, 813 N.E.2d 692, ¶ 13 (4th Dist. 2004);Sebest v. Campbell City Sch. Dist.
Bd. of Educ., No. 00-CA-272, 2002 WL 1483917, ¶¶ 6,7 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 28, 2002); State ex rel. Barno v. Crestwood Bd. of Educ., 134 Ohio App.3d 494, 500, 731 N.E.2d 701 (11th Dist. 1998); Lakota Local Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ. v. Brickner , 108 Ohio App.3d 637, 644-45, 671 N.E.2d 578 (6th Dist. 1996).

Under Ohio law, a public housing authority (“PHA”) is a political subdivision of the State. Moore v. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth., 121 Ohio St.3d 455, 457, 905 N.E.2d 606 (2009); Dudukovich v. Lorain Metro . Hous. Auth., 58 Ohio St. 2d 202, 203 n.2, 404 “The earmarks of a quasi -judicial proceeding include a requirement, imposed by law, of notice, a hearing and an opportunity to introduce evidence.” Lakota Local Sch. Dist. Bd. of
Educ. v. Brickner , 10 8 Ohio App.3d 637, 644, 671 N.E.2d 578 (6th Dist. 1996). Accord Milhoan v. E. Local Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ. , 157 Ohio App.3d 716, 813 N.E.2d 692, ¶ 13 (4th
Dist. 2004); Sebest v. Campbell City Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ. , No. 00 -CA-272, 2002 WL 1483917, ¶ 6 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., June 28, 2002).

389 N.E.2d 1113 (1979).

Therefore, final PHA administrative hearing decisions may be appealed under R.C.

Chapter 2506 to the court of common pleas.Dudukovich v. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth., 58 Ohio St.2d 202, 389 N.E.2d 1113 (1979).

Those decisions include final PHA administrative hearing decisions that terminate a family’s participation in the HCVP.405 Downie v. Lake Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 2014- L-060, 2015 WL 1000211, ¶ 18 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 9, 2015). For a summary of court decisions in R.C. Chapter 2506 appeals from PHA HCVP hearing decisions, see Section XXIV.C.8, below.

#### 2. Notice of Appeal


##### a. Method for Perfecting the Appeal

An R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal is perfected with the filing of a notice of ap peal.

R.C. 2505.04 (incorporated by R.C. 2506.01). See Jacobs v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.

Auth., No. 102248, 2015 WL 3647561, ¶ 15 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 11, 2015).

The notice of appeal must be filed with the court and the PHA, with one receiving the original of the notice and the other receiving a copy of the notice. Welsh Dev. Co.
v. Warren County Reg’l Planning Comm’n , 128 Ohio St.3d 471, 946 N.E.2d 215 (2011); Dudukovich v. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth., 58 Ohio St.2d 202, 389 N.E.2d 1112 (1979). The original of the notice may be filed with either the court or the PHA. Id.

“R.C. 2505.04 is silent as to the permissible method for filing and serving the notice of appeal.” Jacobs v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 102248, 2015 WL 3647561, ¶ 17 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 11, 2015).

However, the Supreme Court has held that the “appellant may use any method reasonably certain to accomplish delivery to the agency within the required 30 days, which is filing that satisfies the jurisdictional requirement for an administrative appeal.” Welsh Dev. Co. v. Warren County Reg’l Planning Comm’n, 128 Ohio St.3d 471, 946 N.E.2d 215, ¶ 40 (2011).

405 Other final PHA administrative hearing decisions that may be appealed under R.C. Chapter 2506 include: (1) decisions denying HCVP admission, e.g., Peters v. Lake Metro. Hous. , No.

94-CV-000481 (Ohio C.P., Lake Cty., Oct. 12, 1994); (2) decisions terminating a public housing tenancy, e.g., Perry v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. CV -05-01-0290 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Feb. 28, 2005); and (3) decisions denying public housing admission, e.g., Riley
v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous . Auth. , No. CV -06-602405 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Mar. 12, 2007).

655 The permissible methods of service include those that are enumerated inJacobs v.

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 102248, 2015 WL 3647561, ¶ 27 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 11, 2015):

the Ohio Supreme Court as well as this court has consistently recognized that a notice of appeal in an R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal may be served b y hand -delivery, certified mail, regular U.S. mail, facsimile, or by the clerk of courts. Dudukovich, 28 Ohio St.2d at 204, 389 N.E.2d 1113 (no particular method of delivery is prescribed by the statute and therefore ‘any method productive of certainty of accomplishment’ is acceptable, including certified mail);In re Jones- Smith, 8th Dist. Cuyahoga No. 93276, 2009 -Ohio-6470, at ¶ 12, 14 (service via regular U.S. mail); Berea Music v. Berea , 8th Dist.

Cuyahoga No. 80897, 2002 -Ohio-6639, ¶ 14 (service via hand - delivery of copy with the administrative agency); Hanson v. Shaker Heights, 152 Ohio App.3d 1, 2003 -Ohio-749, 786 N.E.2d 487, ¶ 12 (8th Dist.) (service via facsimile).

If the appellant uses regular U.S. mail service, there is a presumption “that the service was effective when the certificate of service was entered on the record and the envelope was not returned for failure of delivery.”In re Jones-Smith, No. 93276, 2009 WL 4695438, ¶ 14 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 10, 2009). Accord Jacobs v. Cuyahoga
Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 102248, 2015 WL 3647561, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 11, 2015). Also, the burden of proving the deficiency in this service is on the administrative agency – the party moving to dismiss the appeal. Jacobs, 2015 WL 3647561, ¶ 20.

Civil Rules 4 to 4.6 do not apply to R.C. Chapter 2506 appeals. Jacobs v.

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 102248, 2015 WL 3647561, ¶ 26 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 11, 2015). Theref ore, “service through the clerk of court is not required;

instead, it is simply one method to accomplish the filing requirement of R.C.

2505.04….” Id., ¶ 27.

##### b. Time Period for Perfecting the Appeal

The time period for perfecting the appeal is thirty days from the date of the PHA’s final decision. R.C. 2505.07 (incorporated by R.C. 2506.01). See Jacobs v. Cuyahoga
Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 102248, 2015 WL 3647561, ¶ 16 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 11, 2015).

The thirty-day period begins to run on the datethat the PHA mails the final, written decision to the HCVP participant (or the HCVP participant’s lawyer).406 Cornacchione
v. Akron Bd. of Zoning Appeals , 118 Ohio App.3d 388, 692 N.E.2d 1083 (9th Dist.

1997); New Haven Missionary Baptist Church v. City of Norwood, No. C -961046, 1997 WL 602867 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Aug. 22, 1997); Am. Aggregates Corp. v.

Clayton Twp., Ohio Appeals Bd., No. 16311, 1997 WL 282334 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., May 30, 1997).

If the PHA uses certified mail for this service, the thirty-day period does not begin to run until the date of the participant’s receipt of the decision. Sturdivant v. Toledo
Bd. of Educ., 157 Ohio App.3d 401, 811 N.E.2d 581 (6th Dist. 2004).

The PHA must actually receive the notice of appeal within this thirty -day period.

Welsh Dev. Co. v. Warren County Reg’l Planning Comm’n, 128 Ohio St.3d 471, 946 N.E.2d 215 (2011); Dudukovich v. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth., 58 Ohio St.2d 202, 389 N.E.2d 1112 (1979).

Therefore, an appeal is not perfected timely when: (1) a HCVP participant, before the expiration of the thirty-day period, files the notice of appeal only with the clerk of court; and (2) the PHA receivesthe clerk of court’s service of the notice of appeal after the expiration of the thirty-day period. Henson v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No.

104274, 2016 WL 7296572 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 15, 2016).

#### 3. Bond and Stay Issues


##### a. R.C. 2505.06 Bond to Perfect Appeal, Inapplicable

In an appeal from a PHA decision that terminates a family’s participation in the HCVP, an issue that may arise is whether the family is required to post a bond, under R.C. 2505.06, to perfect the appeal. The proper resolution of this issue is that the family is not required to post the bond.

An R.C. 2505.06 bond is not required when there is neither a monetary judgment against the appellant nor any appellee interest that needs to be secured by a bond pending the appeal. Cleavinger v. Bd. of County Comm’rs of Hamilton County , 72 406 Inconsistent with this general rule, one appellate court held that the thirty-day period begins to run on the effective date of the termination of the family’s participation in the HCVP .

Downie v. Lake Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 2014 -L-060, 2015 WL 1000211, ¶ 19 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Mar. 9, 2015). The effective date of termination is often subsequent to the date on which the notice of the hearing decision is issued and mailed. For exa mple, in Downie, the PHA issued and mailed the hearing decision on August 22, 2013, and the effective date of the termination was September 30, 2014.

657 Ohio App.3d 187, 594 N.E.2d 135 (1st Dist. 1991). See also Houghtaling v. City of Medina Bd. of Zoning Appeals, 134 Ohio App.3d 541, 731 N.E.2d 733 (9th Dist. 1999) (bond not required absent monetary damage award); Mahoney v. City of Berea , 33 Ohio App.3d 94, 514 N.E.2d 889 (8th Dist. 1986) (bond not required absentmonetary damage award).

In an appeal from a PHA decision that terminates a family’s participation in the HCVP, the PHA does not have a monetary judgment (or claim) against the family or any interest that needs to be secured by a bond pending the appeal. If the PHA continues to make HCVP payments on behalf of the family pending the appeal, the PHA incurs no damages because: (1) those HCVP payments will be made in accordance with the program’s rent subsidy requirements, and (2) if the termination decision is upheld on appeal, federal law does not require the HCVP landlord or the HCVP tenant to refund those HCVP payments.

The circumstances are analogous to a landlord’s eviction action against a HCVP
tenant. In those actions, the PHA is required to make HCVP payments on behalf of the tenant during the pendency of the eviction action, including any appeal. 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.311(b).

##### b. Stay Without R.C. 2505.09 Bond

In an appeal from a PHA decision that terminates a family’s participation in the HCVP, an issue that may arise is whether the family is required to post a bond, under R.C. 2505.09, to secure a stay pending the appeal.

When an appellant seeks a stay pending an R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal, R.C.

2505.09 requires the appellant to post a bond “in such sum, notless than, if applicable, the amount of the final order, judgment, or decree and interest involved.” Under R.C.

2505.09, “the trial court or appellate court may direct that sufficient sureties in certain cases means no sureties.” Lomas & Nettleton Co. v. Warren, No. 89 -G-1519, 1990 WL 93138 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., June 29, 1990).

As discussed in Section XXIV.C.3.a, above, when the appeal is from a PHA decision that terminates a family’s participation in the HCVP, the PHA does not have a monetary judgment (or claim) against the family, and it does not incur any damages in continuing to make HCVP payments on behalf of the family during the appeal.

Therefore, in these appeals, courts have granted stays of the termination decision without a requirement to post bond under R.C. 2505.09. E.g., Comston v. Licking
Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 11 -CV-0246 (Ohio C.P., Licking Cty., Feb. 28, 2011);

Huffman v. Coshocton Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 10 -CI-0380 (Ohio C.P., Coshocton Cty., June 16, 2010); Osman v. Columbus Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 08-CVF-07-10542 (Ohio C.P., Franklin Cty., Aug. 26, 2008); Owens v. Parma Pub. Hous. Agency , No.

CV-08-656626 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Apr. 29, 2008); Hammond v. Cleveland
Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV-07-616036 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Feb . 28, 2007);

Boone v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV -06-587744 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., May 25, 2006);Mays v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV-02-466090 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., May 2, 2002); Taylor v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. , No.

353992 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., July 2, 1998); Smith v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth.,
No. CV93-11-3893 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Dec. 1, 1993); Gray v. Akron Metro.
Hous. Auth. , No. CV93 -11-3889 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Nov. 30, 1993);

Yarborough v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 259548 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty.,
Nov. 26, 1993). See also Smith v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV94-09-3277 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Sept. 30, 1994) (nominal bond of $50).

If the family does not secure a stay pending the appeal, the family may consider filing a separate complaint for monetary relief against the PHA. In an R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal, the court may order the family’s reinstatement to the HCVP, but it cannot award monetary relief.

Typically, the family’s claim for monetary relief, brought under 42 U.S.C. §1983, would assert that the PHA violated the family’s rights under federal statutes and
regulations. The family’s monetary damages would include, but not be limited to, the amount of the HCVP payments that the PHA failed to make on behalf of the family during the appeal.

#### 4. Appeal Record


##### a. Administrative Hearing Record

The HCVP participant is obligated to file a praecipe requesting the PHA to prepare and file with the court the requisite transcript of the admin istrative hearing. Woerner
v. Mentor Exempted Vill. Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ., 84 Ohio App.3d 844, 619 N.E.2d 34 (11th Dist. 1993).

The PHA is then obligated “to prepare and file in the court ... a complete transcript of all the original papers, testimony, and evidence offered, heard, and taken into consideration in issuing the final order.” R.C. 2506.02. The PHA is required to file the administrative record within forty days of the filing of the notice of appeal. Id.

659 “The failure to timely file a transcript pursuant to R.C. 2506.02 does not, on that basis alone, entitle a party to a judgment against the agency, unlike an appeal unde r R.C. 119.12.” Martin v. City of Cleveland Bd. of Bldg. Standards & Bldg. Appeals ,
No. 86039, 2005 WL 3120211, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 23, 2005). In order to enforce compliance with R.C. 2506.02, a trial court may invoke its contempt powers.
Id. Alternatively, a trial court may reverse the PHA’s decision based on its failure to present to the court any reason for its late filing. E.g., Crim v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.

Auth., No. CV -17-875960 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Apr. 20, 2017). “[T]he trial court may exercise discretion in determining the appropriate sanction when a party fails to comply with R.C. 2506.02.”Martin v. City of Cleveland Bd. of Bldg. Standards & Bldg. Appeals, No. 86039, 2005 WL 3120211, ¶ 10 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Nov. 23, 2005).

##### b. Additional Evidentiary Hearing, R.C. 2506.03

The HCVP participant may supplement the evidentiary record via a hearing in the court of common pleas, if one or more of the criteria under R.C. 2506.03(A)(1) -(5)
apply. R.C. 2506.03. An R.C. 2506.03 hearing is mandatory, if the appellant shows that one or more of those criteria apply. City of Cleveland v. Posner, 193 Ohio App.3d 211, 951 N.E.2d 476, ¶¶ 37-39 (8th Dist. 2011).

While R.C. 2506.03(A)(1) expressly refers to an incomplete transcript, it also applies to cases in which the administrative agency fails to file any transcript.Woerner
v. Mentor Exempted Vill. Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ., 84 Ohio App.3d 844, 619 N.E.2d 34 (11th Dist. 1993); Powell v. Meigs Local Sch. Dist., No. 502, 1993 WL 379085 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 8, 1993); Xetron Corp. v. Butler County Bd. of Zoning Appeals,
No. 87-12-163, 1988 WL 76814 (Ohio App., 12th Dist., July 25, 1988). In these cases, the court must hold an R.C. 2506.03 hearing in order to establish a record on which it may render a decision.407 Id.

In appeals from PHA decisions that terminate a family’s participation in the HCVP, the basis for the R.C. 2506.03 hearing is often:

407 Alternatively, if the appellant does not request an R.C. 2506.03 hearing, the court may reverse the administrative decision and remand the case to the administrative agency for a new hearing and determination. See State ex rel. Vill. of Chagrin Falls v. Geauga County Bd. of Comm’rs, 96 Ohio St.3d 400, 775 N.E.2d 512, ¶¶ 8 -13 (2002); In re Rocky Point Plaza Corp., 86 Ohio App.3d 486, 496, 621 N.E.2d 566 (10th Dist. 1993).
(1) R.C. 2506.03(A)(1)(PHA failed to file transcript, or complete transcript, of administrative hearing): E.g., Daughery v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.

Auth., No. CV-07-625529 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Aug. 11, 2010);

Hammond v. Cleveland Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. CV-07-616036 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., June 26, 2008); Holman v. Cuyahoga Metro.
Hous. Auth., No. CV -01-449663 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., May 30, 2002); Godfrey v. Miami Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 96 -202 (Ohio C.P., Miami Cty., Oct. 24, 1996); Tapley v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. , No.

CV88-4-1215 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Sept. 30, 1988). Cf. Conner v.

Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 254421 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty.,
Nov. 19, 1993) (reversed HCVP termination because PHA failed to prepare and file a complete transcript of the administrative proceeding).
(2) R.C. 2506.03(A)(3) (administrative he aring testimony not adduced under oath): E.g., Daughery v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV- 07-625529 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Aug. 11, 2010).

#### 5. Standard of Review

In an R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal, “the function of the Court of Common Pleas ...

differs substantially from that of appellate courts in other contexts.” Cincinnati Bell,
Inc. v. Vill. of Glendale , 42 Ohio St.2d 368, 370, 328 N.E.2d 808 (1975). Accord Dudukovich v. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth. , 58 Ohio St.2d 202, 207, 389 N.E.2d 1113 (1979).

It is a “hybrid form of review.” Harvey v. Civil Serv. Comm’n of Cincinnati, 27 Ohio App.3d 304, 306, 501 N.E.2d 39 (1st Dist. 1985). “[A]lthough a hearing before the Court of Common Pleas pursuant to R.C. 2506.01 is not de novo, it often in fact resembles a de novo proceeding.” Cincinnati Bell, Inc., 42 Ohio St.2d at 370. Accord Dudukovich, 58 Ohio St.2d at 206-07.

The court of common pleas “proceed[s] as in the trial of a civil action.” R.C.

2506.03(A). “[T]he court of common pleas must give ‘due d eference’ to the administrative agency’s resolution of evidentiary conflicts.”Harvey, 27 Ohio App.3d at 306. But the court “acts as a finder of fact in regard to the new evidence” under R.C.

2506.03. Id. The R.C. 2506.03 hearing is “a hearing de novo.” Woerner v. Mentor Exempted Vill. Sch. Dist. Bd. of Educ., 84 Ohio App.3d 844, 847, 619 N.E.2d 34 (11th
Dist. 1993).

The court applies the law to the evidence that was submitted to the administrative agency and to the court’s factual findings on the ne w evidence. Henley v. City of Youngstown Bd. of Zoning Appeals, 90 Ohio St.3d 142, 147, 735 N.E.2d 433 (2000);

661 Harvey, 27 Ohio App.3d at 306. The court “determines whether the administrative order is unconstitutional, illegal, arbitrary, capricious, unre asonable, or unsupported by the preponderance of substantial, reliable, and probative evidence.” Henley, 90 Ohio St.3d at 147.

“The standard of review to be applied by the court of appeals in an R.C. 2506.04 appeal is ‘more limited in scope.’”Henley, 90 Ohio St.3d at 147. The court of appeals “review[s] the judgment of the common pleas court only on ‘questions of law,’ which does not include the same extensive power to weigh ‘the preponderance of substantial, reliable and probative evidence,’ as is gran ted to the common pleas court.” Kisil v.

City of Sandusky, 12 Ohio St 3d 30, 34 n.4, 465 N.E.2d 848 (1984). Accord Henley v.

City of Youngstown Bd. of Zoning Appeals , 90 Ohio St.3d 142, 147, 735 N.E.2d 433 (2000).

“On evidentiary matters, appellate court s are limited to reviewing to determine whether the trial court’s decision is unsupported by a preponderance of reliable, probative, and substantial evidence. In this context, a reversal‘as a matter of law’can occur only when, having viewed the evidencemost favorably to the decision, there are no facts to support the trial court’s decision. However, if the trial court considers evidence outside the administrative record by allowing additional evidence under the statutory scheme, the review of those determinations fall under the abuse of discretion standard of review.’” Austin v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 107247, 2019 WL 856776, ¶ 23 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 21, 2019).

#### 6. Evidentiary Issues


##### a. Burden of Proof

The PHA has the burden of proof on its alleged grounds for terminating the family’s participation in the HCVP. Gooden v. Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth., No. A- 0710779, slip op. at 7 (Ohio C.P., Hamilton Cty., July 25, 2008, and Aug. 29, 2008).

See also Williams v. Hous. Auth. of City of Raleigh, 595 F. Supp.2d 627, 633 (E.D.N.C.

2008); Hassan v. Dakota County Cmty. Dev. Agency , No. A08 -0373, 2009 WL 437775, at *1 (Minn. App. Feb. 24, 2009); Meyer v. Dakota County Cmty. Dev.

Agency, No. A06-1290, 2007 WL 2703005, at *3 (Minn. App. Sept. 18, 2007).

Generally, the PHA must prove the allegations against the HCVP participant by a preponderance of the evidence. 24 C.F.R. § 982.555(e)(6); R.C. 2506.04. See also Edgecomb v. Hous. Auth. of Town of Vernon, 824 F. Supp. 312, 315 (D. Conn. 1993).

##### b. Hearsay Evidence

In an administrative hearing on the termination of a family’s participation in the HCVP, “evidence may be considered without regard to admissibility under the rules of evidence applicable to judicial proceedings.” 24 C.F.R. § 982.5 55(e)(5). Therefore, the hearing officer may consider hearsay evidence.

However, there are limits under Ohio law on the type of hearsay evidence that is admissible in these hearings.

R.C. 2506.04 requires substantial, reliable, and probative evidence. “( 1) ‘Substantial’ evidence is evidence with some weight; it must have importance and
value. (2) ‘Reliable’ evidence is dependable; that is, it can be confidently trusted. In order to be reliable, there must be a reasonable probability that the evidence is true.
(3) ‘Probative’ evidence is evidence that tends to prove the issue in question; it must be relevant in determining the issue.”Our Place, Inc. v. Ohio Liquor Control Comm’n, 63 Ohio St.3d 570, 571, 589 N.E.2d 1303 (1992). Accord City of Cleveland v. Posner, 193 Ohio App.3d 211, 951 N.E.2d 476, ¶ 27 (8th Dist. 2011).

For example, Ohio law permits reliance on hearsay evidence where it has anindicia of reliability and other evidence supports it. E.g., Doersam v. City of Gahanna , No.

96-APF12-1766, 1997 WL 607473, at *9,10 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Sept. 30, 1997);

Habe v. S. Euclid Civil Serv. Comm’n, No. 61786, 1993 WL 27682, at *3 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 4, 1993). See generally Consol. Edison Co. v. NLRB, 305 U.S. 197, 230
(1938) (“Mere uncorro borated hearsay or rumor does not constitute substantial evidence”).

Evidence is inherently unreliable where it “consists of subjective judgments of third parties, not present at the hearing.”Dudukovich v. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth., 58 Ohio St.2d 202, 208, 389 N.E.2d 1113 (1979).

Two Ohio court decisions that reversed PHA HCVP termination decisions (concerning alleged unauthorized occupants ) because the PHA decisions relied on unreliable hearsay evidence are: Gooden v. Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. A - 0710779 (Ohio C.P., Hamilton Cty., July 25, 2008, and Aug. 29, 2008) (court documents in domestic violence criminal proceeding), andRodriguez v. Lorain Metro.
Hous. Auth., No. 05-CV-140942 (Ohio C.P., Lorain Cty., Oct. 23, 2006) (newspaper articles).

A related issue is whether, under due process, a PHA may rely solely on hearsay evidence, when contrary, non-hearsay evidence raises issues of credibility.

663 Some court decisions hold that, in these circumstances, due process bars the PHA from relying solely on hearsay evidence. E.g., Stevenson v. Willis, 579 F. Supp.2d 913, 919-20 (N.D. Ohio 2008); Edgecomb v. Hous. Auth. of Town of Vernon, 824 F. Supp.

312, 316 (D. Conn. 1993); Young v. Maryville Hous. Auth., No. 3:09-cv-37, 2009 WL 2043891, at *8 (E.D. Tenn. July 2, 2009) (preliminary injunction). See also Davis v.

Mansfield Metro. Hous. Auth., 751 F.2d 180, 185 (6th Cir. 1984) (due process requires the right to confront and cross -examine all witnesses upon whose testimony or information the PHA relies and a written decision based solely on the evidence presented at the hearing).

In Woods v. Willis, 515 F. App’x 471, 282-83, 2013 WL 611141 (6th Cir. 2013), the court reviews the split in the court decisions on this issue. The court does not need to (and el ects not to) render a decision on the issue. “We need not decide whether Defendant may rely solely on reliable hearsay testimony, or if such reliance violates due process or the regulations absent the right to question the declarant, because the evidence here fails under either rule.” Id., 515 F. App’x at 483.

##### c. Criminal Conviction or Arrest Evidence

“A record of a criminal conviction is only some evidence, not conclusive evidence, of the facts attempted to be proved in a subsequent civil proceeding.” Vancamp v.

Austintown Twp., No. 01-CA-17, 2002 WL 925197, ¶ 60 (Ohio App., 7th Dist., Mar.

19, 2002) (R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal). Accord McAlister v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth.,
No. CV-2007-08-5813 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Apr. 14, 2008) (R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal, HCVP).

“[A] guilty plea is not an admission in the technical sense of Civ.R. 36, but rather, is an admission in the sense of a confession or a statement against interest.”Vancamp, 2002 WL 925197, ¶ 64. Accord Swigart v. Swigart, 65 Ohio Law Abs. 582, 591, 115 N.E.2d 871 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., 1953); McAlister v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth., No.

CV-2007-08-5813 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Apr. 14, 2008) (R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal, HCVP).

“One of the primary reasons for allowing a party in a civil case to rebut the facts underlying a prior guilty plea is that ‘in many cases a plea of guilty may, in fact, amount to a compromise by the defendant to avoid an expensive or undesirable trial.’” Vancamp, 2002 WL 925197, ¶ 65. See also Costa v. Fall River Hous. Auth., 71 Mass.
App. Ct. 269, 283, 881 N.E.2d 800 (2008) (“a plea may result from extrinsic considerations ... by the accused (elimination of the risk of a more serious conviction and greater punishment)”). E.g., McAlister v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. CV- 2007-08-5813 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Apr. 14, 2008) (“Appellant asserts that she pled guilty to the charges in order to enter into the felony drug court program and that she understood that by entering into the Drug Court Program that the charges wo uld be dismissed if she successfully completed the program”).

A party in a civil case “‘may explain the prior conviction and may offer any evidence rebutting the fact sought to be proved by the opponent.’”Phillips v. Rayburn, 113 Ohio App.3d 374, 381, 68 0 N.E.2d 1279 (4th Dist. 1996) (quoting Hickman v.

Kraft, No. 84-CA-2, 1985 WL 11160 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 26, 1985)).

“Thus, the conviction may be admitted into evidence and accorded whatever weight the factfinder deems appropriate. It does not, however, preclude additional litigation involving the facts and legal issues underlying the conviction.” Id. at 381.

Also, an arrest, by itself, does not meet the PHA’s burden of proof in the termination of a family’s participation in the HCVP program. The PHA must prove the underlying allegations against the HCVP participant by a preponderance of the
evidence. See Section XXIV.C.6.a., above. “The standard for effecting a valid arrest is probable cause to believe that the person arrested has committeda criminal offense.” State v. Swonger , No. 09AP -1166, 2010 WL 4011201, ¶ 12 (Ohio App., 10th Dist.,
Oct. 14, 2010). “Probable cause is a lesser standard of proof than that required ... by a preponderance of the evidence.” State v. Smith, No. 09-CA-29, 2010 WL 3722257, ¶ 84 (Ohio App., 4th Dist., Sept. 20, 2010). See also Nashua Hous. Auth. v. Wilson, 162 N.H. 358, 33 A.3d 1163 (2011) (Public Housing eviction).

##### d. Evidence Outside the Hearing

In the administrative hearing decision on whether to terminate a family’s participation in the HCVP, the factual determinations must be based on the evidence presented at the hearing. 24 C.F.R. § 982.555(e)(6).

For example, the hearing officer may not investigate and secure evidence after the hearing, and then rely on that evidence in the hearing decision. Lyons v. Tuscarawas
Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 2007-AP-080051, 2009 WL 2781802 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 24, 2008).408 Cf. Jackson v. Greene Metro Hous. Auth., No. 2000-CV-0544 (Ohio C.P., Greene Cty., Jan. 25, 2001) (reversed denial of HCVP admission; hearing officer 408 The Westlaw publication of this decision incorrectly states the date of the decision as June 24, 2009.

665 secured information after the hearing and relied on it, in violation of due process).

#### 7. Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure, Applicability

An R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal is a special statutory proceeding under Civ il Rule 1(C)(7). Quick v. Wolfe, No. 81AP-561, 1981 WL 3422 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Aug.

20, 1981). Therefore, “each rule must be considered independently to determine its
applicability. Id., 1981 WL 3422, at *1.

Court decisions on the applicability of a Civil Rule to R.C. Chapter 2506 proceedings include the following decisions.

Civil Rules 4 to 4.6: Not Applicable Jacobs v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 102248, 2015 WL 3647561 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., June 11, 2015).

Civil Rule 41(B)(1): Applicable Parker v. Lake Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 2012 -L-054, 2012 WL 5995062 (Ohio App., 11th Dist., Dec. 3, 2012).

Civil Rule 52: Applicable, with limitation per Jandecka Jandecka v. Petre , No. 46623, 1983 WL 2695 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Oct. 27,
1983) (applicable, if the court takes new evidence, or makes new findings from the administrative record, or accepts the administrative findings; not applicable, “if the court simply reviews the administrative record, and bases its decision on the legal conclusions”).

Civil Rule 59: Applicable Quick v. Wolfe , No. 81AP -561, 1981 WL 3422 (Ohio App., 10th Dist., Aug. 20, 1981)

#### 8. Summary of HCVP Court Decisions, R.C.

Chapter 2506 Appeals

##### a. PHA Termination Decision Reversed, Substantive

Grounds Austin v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 107247, 2019 WL 856776 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Feb. 21, 2019) PHA termination for failing to report timely (reported fifty days late) a change in household composition (move -out of two adult children). “At the time in q uestion, Austin was a single mother in the late stages of pregnancy, who then gave birth by cesarean section. She had two other minor children residing with her and two older children that she was trying to remove from the lease because they were not following household rules. Austin was in and out of the hospital and the birth of the baby left her medically incapacitated during the time CMHA alleged she failed to report the change in household circumstances.” Austin, ¶ 39. The trial court reversed the PHA termination decision on the ground that it was unreasonable, and the court of appeals affirmed the trial court judgment.

McClarty v. Greene Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 2011-CA-30, 2011 WL 3890295 (Ohio App., 2nd Dist., Sept. 2, 2011) PHA termination for failing to report child -support income. Court of appeals held fraud is a required element of this ground. It found participant did not commit fraud, because her failure to report the start of child -support income was an unintentional mistake – she thought that she or ODJFS had reported it.

Smith v. Hamilton County, Ohio , No. C-060315, 2007 WL 1095680 (Ohio App., 1st Dist., Apr. 13, 2007) PHA termination for continuing to list sons as household members, after temporarily losing custody of them. Court of appeals held fraud is an element of this ground. It found participant did not commit fraud, because her continued listing of her sons as household members was an unintentional mistake – she thought that she was required to list them because of their visits.

Holman v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.Auth., No. 81475, 2002 WL 31771307 (Ohio App., 8th Dist., Dec. 12, 2002) PHA termination for absence of HAP payments for six months, applying 24 C.F.R.

§ 982.455. Court reversed PHA decision because evidence showed HAP p ayments made, not absence of HAP payments, during the relevant six months.

Chalk v. Stark Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 2018-CV-00693 (Ohio C.P., Stark Cty., Jan.

31, 2019) PHA termination for unauthorized occupant. Court found that the PHA did not establish that the alleged unauthorized occupant stayed at the premises for more than fifteen consecutive days or for more than ninety cumulative days in a twelve -month
period. T herefore, under the relevant PHA definition, the PHA did not establish an unauthorized occupancy. In reaching its findings, the court found unpersuasive the PHA’s reliance on the delivery of mail to the premises to the alleged unauthorized occupant, given the participant’s testimony on her unsuccessful efforts to stop those deliveries.

667 Coleman v. Mansfield Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 2011-CV-1071H (Ohio C.P., Richland Cty., July 10, 2012) PHA termination for drug-related criminal activity. Court reversed the PHA decision on two grounds. First, the participant’s conduct– one-time use of marijuana – did not rise to the level of criminal activity because, under Ohio law, it could have led only to a minor misdemeanor charge, for which a conviction would not constitute a criminal
record. Second, the termination decision is unreasonable – which is an R.C. 2506.04 ground for reversing the decision – based on five mitigating factors: one -time drug- use; occurred off the premises; no evidence of ongoing drug -use; model tenant and neighbor; and termination would deny the family the ability to secure safe, reliable housing.

Johnson v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 2010 -08-5472 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty.,
Feb. 23, 2011) PHA termination for unauthorized occupant. Court held residency means a permanent, fixed location with specific intent to stay, and not mere physical presence in the unit for any period of time during the day (as PHA argued). Even under PHA’s mere physical presence standard, PHA failed to prove violat ion of PHA’s guest limitation, four consecutive days or 15 days per 12 months.

Robertson v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous.Auth., No. CV-07-635765 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Aug. 31, 2010) PHA termination for listing children, who did not reside in the unit but vi sited, as household members. According to participant’s brief: she did not commit fraud; she listed her children as household members because the larger accommodation was required by the terms of her reunification plan with the County; and PHA knew or should have known of her children’s residency via recertification information. Court decision simply states that the PHA decision was illegal, arbitrary, and unsupported by the preponderance of the evidence.

Gooden v. Cincinnati Metro. Hous. Auth., No. A-0710779 (Ohio C.P., Hamilton Cty., July 25, 2008, and Aug. 29, 2008) PHA termination for unauthorized occupant. Court held PHA failed to meet its burden of proof. PHA’s evidence was unreliable and not credible hearsay– court documents in a criminal domestic violence proceeding. Participant presented competent, credible evidence that the guest did not reside there.

Hammond v. Cleveland Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV-07-616036 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., June 26, 2008) PHA termination for unauthorized occupant and side rental payments. Court held the PHA decision was unsupported by a preponderance of substantial, reliable, and probative evidence. Evidence on unauthorized occupant claim: (1) “mailings consist of junk mail addressed to [ex -husband], and not impo rtant mail such as utility statements, bank statements, or credit card statements;” and (2) participant’s direct testimony that her ex-husband, and other men (uncles, brothers, boyfriend), spent time at the unit, but none stayed overnight. Evidence on sid e rental payments claim: (1) three months of rent receipts that do not show participant was responsible for making the payments; (2) evidence of hostility between former landlord and participant; and
(3) absence of testimony by former landlord.

Jones v. C incinnati Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. A -0709492 (Ohio C.P., Hamilton Cty.,
Apr. 15, 2008) PHA termination for violent criminal activity (robbery). Court held the PHA decision violated HUD regulations because, under 24 C.F.R. § 982.551(l), violent criminal activity is grounds to terminate participation only if it occurs in the immediate vicinity of the rental unit. Court found that 2/3 of a mile – distance between the unit and the site of the criminal activity – did not meet the immediate vicinity test.

May v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV-07-632536 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty.,
Nov. 2, 2007) PHA termination for listing children, who did not reside in the unit but visited, as household members. Court held the PHA decision was arbitrary, unreasonable and capricious, because PHA failed to demonstrate participant committed fraud and PHA failed to consider relevant mitigating circumstances.

Young v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. CV-06-593687 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Mar. 7, 2007) PHA termination for two -fold reason: for vacating the rental unit for an extended period (two months), and for vacating the rental unit without notifying the PHA.

Participant vacated the rental unit due to a threat of domestic violence. Upon returning to the unit after two months, she found the locks changed and her belongings missing.

She then informed the PHA of her two -month absence and the reason for it. Court rejected the PHA’s argument that the termination was not due to the threat of domestic violence, but rather the extended absence without notifying PHA. Court held that “Ms.

Young submitted sufficient evidence to show that she was a victim of domestic violence and subject to the protections provided under the Violence Against Women’s Act.” Boone v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV -06-587744 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Feb. 2, 2007) PHA termination for causing injury to PHA employee. According to Appellant’s Proposed Jounal Entry: after a PHA inspector left the rental unit, participant cl osed the door to the unit; unknown to participant, the inspector was also closing the door 669 from the outside; this caused the inspector’s finger to be scraped; and participant
apologized. Court held this incident did not warrant a termination of the family ’s participation in the HCVP.

Mihaly v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV-2005-11-6946 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty.,
Dec. 14, 2006) PHA termination for drug -related criminal activity. Participant arrested for drug - related activity, when on the HCVP waiting list . She pled guilty to the drug -related criminal activity after she had been issued a voucher, but before commencement of her initial HCVP tenancy. Applying HUD regulations, court held that, at the time of her arrest and conviction, she was not a HCVP participant and, therefore, the PHA cannot terminate her participation in the HCVP for those activities.

Rodriguez v. Lorain Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 05-CV-140942 (Ohio C.P., Lorain Cty.,
Oct. 23, 2006) PHA termination for violent criminal activity that occur red in front of participant’s rental unit. Issue whether perpetrator of the crime was a member of participant’s
household. PHA’s evidence: two newspaper articles; a police report; and a court docket in an old criminal proceeding. Participant’s evidence: family’s direct testimony and perpetrator’s parole release address. Court found the newspaper articles unreliable hearsay, the police report established only the site of the crime, and the court docket not probative. Court held the PHA decision was unr easonable and not supported by reliable, substantial and probative evidence.

Blackmond v. Cuyahoga Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. CV-541895 (Ohio C.P., Cuyahoga Cty., Oct. 21, 2004) PHA termination for serious lease violation. PHA terminated the HAP contract for landlord’s HQS violations and thereafter landlord secured eviction judgment against
participant. PHA relied solely on the eviction judgment, which did not contain any factual findings. Court held the eviction judgment alone did not establish a serious lease violation and, therefore, the PHA decision was capricious, arbitrary, and unreasonable.

Chambers v. Stark Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 1997 -CV-02490 (Ohio C.P., Stark Cty.,
Aug. 3, 1998) PHA termination for violent criminal activity. Court held PHA’s evidence of violent criminal activity – arrest for which grand jury issued “no-bill” – was insufficient.

Robertson v. Mansfield Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 87-426-C (Ohio C.P., Richland Cty.,
Mar. 28, 1989) PHA termination for failing to report a change in household composition. Court found family reported the change in household composition at its next annual recertification, and PHA failed to present any evidence that the PHA required or requested an earlier reporting of it.

Tapley v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV88-4-1215 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Sept.

30, 1988) PHA termination for unauthorized occupant. Court found PHA did not present any evidence of an unauthorized occupant.

##### b. PHA Termination Decision Reversed, Procedural

Grounds Lyons v. Tuscarawas Me tro. Hous. Auth. , No. 2007 -AP-080051, 2009 WL 2781802 (Ohio App., 5th Dist., June 24, 2008)409 PHA hearing officer secured evidence after the hearing and relied on the post-hearing evidence in the hearing decision. In addition, the PHA failed to make available to the participant exculpatory evidence.

McAlister v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. CV -2007-08-5813 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty., Apr. 14, 2008) PHA termination for illegal drug activity, based on participant’s guilty plea to charges of cocaine, marijuana and obstructing justice. Participant contends she pled guilty in order to enter a felony drug court program. Court held the guilty plea is not dispositive of the issue, and participant is “entitled to present evidence of the circumstances surrounding the guilty plea and then the Hearing Officer can determine as to whether there is a preponderance of the evidence that the household member engaged in the activity.” Court remanded the case for these purposes.

Skipper v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth., No. CV-2007-07-5114 (Ohio C.P., Summit Cty.,
Dec. 27, 2007) The ground for termination in the PHA notice was participant’s failure to appear (and furnish requested information) at 2007 annual re-exam. The ground for termination in the PHA decision was the fai lure to appear at the 2007 annual re -examination “is a repeat pattern from the 2006 reexamination appointment.” Court held that, in relying on the failure to attend 2006 re -exam and “the repeat pattern,” the PHA decision was based on grounds not stated in the PHA notice and, therefore, violated due process.

409 See note 408, above.

671

##### c. PHA Termination Decision Upheld, Substantive

Grounds King v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 25664, 2011 WL 2420232 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 15, 2011) Court of appeals affirmed trial court’s decision that upheld PHA termination for unauthorized occupant, based on evidence that included: admission of guest at the hearing that he stayed two nights per week; and admission of guest to the police that he “lived at her apartment, kept clothing there, and received his mail there.” Hammond v. Akron Metro. Hous. Auth. , No. 25425, 2011 WL 2175801 (Ohio App., 9th Dist., June 1, 2011) Court of appeals affirmed trial court’s decision that upheld PHA termination for unauthorized occupant, based on evidence that included: complaint of guest’s mother that he lived there; admission of participant that guest stayed overnight one or two nights per week, for several months; and guest’s receipt of mail there.

Miller v. Lake Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 11-CV-000613 (Ohio C.P., Lake Cty., Nov. 17, 2011) Court upheld PHA termination for unauthorized occupant, based on the admission of guest that he stayed up to three nights per week, which resulted in violation of PHA’s guest limitation, 90 days per 12 months.

#### 9. Federal Court Action

A HCVP participant may be entitled to contest the PHA termination decision by filing an action in federal court, under 42 U.S.C § 1983, alleging a violation of applicable federal statutes and regulations (and, if applicable, due process).

The participant may elect to file the federal court action instead of, or in addition to, the R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal.

However, if the participant files an R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal in state court and a § 1983 action in federal court, under the doctrine of collateral estoppel, the state court may decide the federal court’s liability issue. If entered first, the state court’s R.C.

Chapter 2506 decision, relative to the PHA’s alleged noncompliance with federal law, may be binding on the fed eral court action. See Osborn v. Ashland County Bd. of Alcohol, Etc., 979 F.2d 1131 (6th Cir. 1992) (state court ruling on due process claims in R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal precluded federal court action on those claims). See also Copeland v. Hous. Auth. of Hollywood, 300 Fed. App’x 843, 2008 WL 4997184 (11th
Cir. 2008) (res judicata effect of state court judgment).

If the participant failed to perfect timely (i.e., within the 30-day period)410 an R.C.

Chapter 2506 appeal, the federal court action may be the participant’s only avenue for
appeal. In Ohio, the statute of limitations for the § 1983 action is two years.Browning
v. Pendleton, 869 F.2d 989, 992 (6th Cir. 1989) (en banc).

In the federal court action, “[t]he level of deference shown to the factfindings of a hearing officer is not absolute but it is significant.” Clark v. Alexander, 85 F.3d 146, 151 (4th Cir. 1996). Accord Gammons v. Mass. Dep’t of Hous. & Com. Dev., 502 F.

Supp.2d 161 (D. Mass. 2007); Litsey v. Hous. Auth. of Bardstown , No. 3:99CV-114- H, 1999 WL 33604017 (W.D. Ky. Apr. 1, 1999). “[T]he reviewing court must be satisfied that the hearing officer’s conclusions are supported by substantial evidence.” Clark, 85 F.3d at 152. See also Ellis v. Ritchie , 803 F. Supp. 1097, 1101 (E.D. Va.
1992) (court determines whether “a rational basis exists for the agency’s decision”).

410 For a discussion on the 30 -day period for perfecting an R.C. Chapter 2506 appeal, see Section XXIV.C.2.b, above.
